topics.title: Equivalence and Cost Comparisons: Time Value of Money and Equivalence
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT757 - ECONOMICS AND MANAGEMENT OF CHEMICAL INDUSTRIES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Equivalence and Cost Comparisons: Time Value of Money and Equivalence

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Time Value of Money (TVM) — Formal Definition

In engineering economics, the **Time Value of Money (TVM)** is the foundational principle stating that **a unit of money received or paid today is worth more than the same unit of money received or paid at any future date**, because money available at the present time can be invested to earn a positive rate of return (interest) over time.

Mathematically, if $P$ is the present sum and $F$ is the equivalent future sum after $n$ periods at an interest rate $i$ per period:

$$F = f(P, i, n)$$

The TVM principle recognizes that money has three potential uses:
1. **Consumption** (spending power today)
2. **Investment** (earning power through interest)
3. **Storage** (preservation of value against inflation)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** TVM is the cornerstone of all engineering economic decisions. Every capital investment appraisal in the chemical industry — from selecting a distillation column to evaluating a new polymer plant — is built on this principle. KTU examiners frequently test the understanding of *why* money changes value with time before testing the formulas.

## 1.2 Equivalence — Formal Definition

**Equivalence** in engineering economics is the condition in which **two or more cash flows occurring at different time periods have the same economic value when evaluated at a common point in time** using a specified interest rate (or Minimum Acceptable Rate of Return, MARR).

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard KTU Definition:** *Equivalence is the state where different sums of money at different times are made equal in economic value at a chosen reference date by considering the time value of money through an interest rate $i$ (or MARR).*

The mathematical expression of equivalence is:

$$P_1(1+i)^{n_1} = P_2(1+i)^{n_2} = A \cdot \left[\frac{(1+i)^{n}-1}{i}\right]$$

where $P_1$, $P_2$, $A$ are different cash flow types referenced to a common time origin.

## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — "The Bank Savings Story":**
> Imagine your friend owes you ₹1,000. They offer two payment options:
> - **Option A:** Pay you ₹1,000 *right now*.
> - **Option B:** Pay you ₹1,000 *after 2 years*.
>
> Which would you choose? Most people instinctively choose **Option A**. Why? Because ₹1,000 today can be deposited in a bank earning, say, **8% per annum interest**. After 2 years, it grows to:
>
> $$F = 1000(1+0.08)^2 = ₹1{,}166.40$$
>
> So ₹1,000 today is economically **equivalent** to ₹1,166.40 two years from now. The future payment is "discounted" today. This is the heart of equivalence — making different cash flows at different times *comparable in value* by moving them to a common date.

For a **chemical engineer**, this means: a heat exchanger that costs ₹5,00,000 today is *not economically the same* as paying ₹5,00,000 over 5 years in installments. Using TVM, both options can be converted to a **Present Worth (PW)** or **Annual Worth (AW)** to make a fair comparison.

## 1.4 Interest: The Engine of TVM

**Interest ($I$)** is the "rent" paid for the use of borrowed money, or the "reward" earned on invested capital. It is expressed as a percentage of the principal per unit time.

| Type | Definition | Formula |
|------|------------|---------|
| **Simple Interest (SI)** | Interest is calculated only on the original principal every period. | $I = P \cdot i \cdot n$ |
| **Compound Interest (CI)** | Interest is calculated on the principal **plus** accumulated interest from prior periods. | $F = P(1+i)^n$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Note:** In all engineering economic analyses under the KTU syllabus, **compound interest is the default convention**. Simple interest is only used for very short-duration loans (less than 1 year) or specific contractual situations.

## 1.5 Cash Flow Diagram — The Universal Language of Equivalence

A **cash flow diagram** is a graphical representation of cash inflows (revenues, savings) and outflows (costs, investments) plotted on a horizontal time axis. The standard KTU convention is:

- **Vertical arrows pointing UP (↑)** = **Positive cash flow (Receipts / Inflows)**
- **Vertical arrows pointing DOWN (↓)** = **Negative cash flow (Disbursements / Outflows)**
- **Horizontal axis** = Time, with each tick mark representing the **end of a period** (period 0, 1, 2, ..., n).

> [!NOTE]
> **Sign Convention Used in KTU Board Exams:**
> - Cash flows are usually considered **uniformly signed** (all inflows OR all outflows) in equivalence problems.
> - The interest rate is denoted by $i$ (% per period) and is the **MARR** when comparing alternatives.

## 1.6 The Six Standard Cash Flow Patterns in KTU Syllabus

The KTU 2024 Scheme PECHT757 syllabus explicitly lists the following equivalence factors. Mastering these six patterns enables solution of *any* cost-comparison problem.

1. **Single Payment — Future Worth (F/P, i, n)**
2. **Single Payment — Present Worth (P/F, i, n)**
3. **Uniform Series — Future Worth (F/A, i, n)**
4. **Uniform Series — Present Worth (P/A, i, n)**
5. **Capital Recovery (A/P, i, n)**
6. **Sinking Fund (A/F, i, n)**

Each of these will be rigorously derived in Section 3.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The "Why" Behind Equivalence — The Logic of Movement

The entire structure of engineering economic equivalence rests on **two fundamental movements of money on a time axis**:

1. **Movement FORWARD in time** (from Present $P$ to Future $F$): money **grows** by multiplication with $(1+i)$. This is *compounding*.
2. **Movement BACKWARD in time** (from Future $F$ to Present $P$): money **shrinks** by division by $(1+i)$. This is *discounting*.

All other equivalence factors (uniform series, gradient series, etc.) are built by **summing** or **integrating** these two primitive movements.

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic of the Six Factors

### Factor 1: Single Payment Compound Amount — (F/P, i, n)
- **Concept:** "If I have a present sum $P$, what is its equivalent future value $F$ at the end of $n$ periods at interest rate $i$?"
- **Logic:** Money grows by a factor of $(1+i)$ each period. Over $n$ periods, it compounds $n$ times.

### Factor 2: Single Payment Present Worth — (P/F, i, n)
- **Concept:** "If I will receive a future sum $F$ after $n$ periods, what is its equivalent present value $P$ at interest rate $i$?"
- **Logic:** The inverse of Factor 1. We *discount* $F$ back to time 0 by dividing by $(1+i)$ for each period.

### Factor 3: Uniform Series Compound Amount — (F/A, i, n)
- **Concept:** "If I deposit a uniform amount $A$ at the *end* of each of $n$ periods, what is the accumulated value $F$ at the end of period $n$?"
- **Logic:** The first deposit earns interest for $(n-1)$ periods; the second for $(n-2)$ periods; ...; the last deposit earns no interest. This forms a **geometric series**.

### Factor 4: Uniform Series Present Worth — (P/A, i, n)
- **Concept:** "What is the present value $P$ of a uniform series of end-of-period payments $A$ over $n$ periods?"
- **Logic:** Equivalently, the present worth of the future worth of the uniform series. This is the **most-used factor in KTU board problems**.

### Factor 5: Capital Recovery — (A/P, i, n)
- **Concept:** "If I borrow a present sum $P$ and repay it in equal installments $A$ over $n$ periods at interest $i$, what is the installment?"
- **Logic:** The inverse of Factor 4. Used in *loan amortization* and *equipment replacement analysis*.

### Factor 6: Sinking Fund — (A/F, i, n)
- **Concept:** "What uniform end-of-period deposit $A$ is required to accumulate a future sum $F$ at the end of $n$ periods at interest $i$?"
- **Logic:** The inverse of Factor 3. Used in *depreciation reserves* and *decommissioning fund* analysis — extremely relevant in chemical plant retirement planning.

## 2.3 The KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

The following table is the **single most important study reference** for KTU Module 1 equivalence problems. Every formula is mathematically derived in Section 3.

| # | Factor Name | Notation | Formula | Use Case (Chemical Industry Example) |
|---|-------------|----------|---------|--------------------------------------|
| 1 | Single Payment Compound Amount | $(F/P, i, n)$ | $F = P(1+i)^n$ | Future value of capital invested in a new reactor today |
| 2 | Single Payment Present Worth | $(P/F, i, n)$ | $P = F(1+i)^{-n}$ | Discounting future decommissioning cost of a plant |
| 3 | Uniform Series Compound Amount | $(F/A, i, n)$ | $F = A \cdot \dfrac{(1+i)^n - 1}{i}$ | Future value of annual maintenance deposits |
| 6 | Uniform Series Present Worth | $(P/A, i, n)$ | $P = A \cdot \dfrac{(1+i)^n - 1}{i(1+i)^n}$ | Present worth of annual operating cost of a distillation column |
| 4 | Capital Recovery (CR) | $(A/P, i, n)$ | $A = P \cdot \dfrac{i(1+i)^n}{(1+i)^n - 1}$ | Annualized cost of a one-time catalyst purchase |
| 5 | Sinking Fund (SF) | $(A/F, i, n)$ | $A = F \cdot \dfrac{i}{(1+i)^n - 1}$ | Annual reserve to replace a pressure vessel after 10 years |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mastery Rule for KTU Exams:** Memorize only **ONE primary formula** — the $(F/A, i, n)$ factor. Every other factor can be derived from it using the equivalence relationships:
> - $(P/A, i, n) = (P/F, i, n) \cdot (F/A, i, n)$
> - $(A/P, i, n) = 1 \,/\, (P/A, i, n)$
> - $(A/F, i, n) = 1 \,/\, (F/A, i, n)$
> - $(F/P, i, n) = (P/F, i, n)^{-1}$

## 2.4 The MARR — The Decision-Maker's Interest Rate

The **Minimum Attractive Rate of Return (MARR)** is the lowest rate of return a company will accept on an investment. It is the *interest rate used in equivalence calculations* when comparing real engineering alternatives.

- If MARR is **not given**, KTU board problems often specify: *"Assume a MARR equal to the bank lending rate of X%."*
- A higher MARR makes future money worth *less* today (heavier discounting), biasing decisions toward alternatives with **lower initial investment** and **earlier returns**.

## 2.5 Real-World Utility in Chemical Engineering

| Engineering Decision | Equivalence Factor Used |
|---------------------|------------------------|
| Choosing between two pumps (one cheap, one durable) | Present Worth $(P/A)$ or Annual Worth $(A/P)$ |
| Sizing a maintenance reserve fund for a 20-year plant life | Sinking Fund $(A/F, i, n)$ |
| Evaluating a 30-year petrochemical project vs. a 10-year one | Present Worth $(P/A)$ at MARR |
| Calculating the Equivalent Annual Cost (EAC) of a catalyst | Capital Recovery $(A/P, i, n)$ |
| Comparing scrap value vs. continuing operation of an asset | Single Payment Present Worth $(P/F, i, n)$ |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exhaustive Derivation Mandate:** Every algebraic transition is shown explicitly. The geometric series sum and binomial expansion steps are expanded in full to satisfy the KTU 2024 Scheme's *Apply* and *Analyze* cognitive levels of Revised Bloom's Taxonomy.

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Single Payment Compound Amount (F/P, i, n)

**Setup:** A principal $P$ is invested at time 0 at compound interest rate $i$ per period. Let $F_n$ denote the future worth at the end of period $n$.

**End of Period 0 (start):**
$$F_0 = P$$

**End of Period 1:** The principal earns one period of interest.
$$F_1 = F_0 + i \cdot F_0 = F_0(1+i) = P(1+i)$$

**End of Period 2:** Both principal and period-1 interest earn interest.
$$F_2 = F_1 + i \cdot F_1 = F_1(1+i) = P(1+i)(1+i) = P(1+i)^2$$

**End of Period 3:**
$$F_3 = F_2(1+i) = P(1+i)^2(1+i) = P(1+i)^3$$

**Recognize the Pattern:** By mathematical induction, at the end of period $n$:
$$\boxed{F = P(1+i)^n}$$

This is the **fundamental compounding equation** of engineering economics.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Single Payment Present Worth (P/F, i, n)

**Setup:** We need to find the present value $P$ equivalent to a known future value $F$ at the end of period $n$ at interest rate $i$.

**Start from the compounding equation (Section 3.1):**
$$F = P(1+i)^n$$

**Solve for $P$:** Divide both sides by $(1+i)^n$:
$$\boxed{P = F(1+i)^{-n} = \dfrac{F}{(1+i)^n}}$$

**Sanity Check (Numerical):** If $F = 1166.40$, $i = 0.08$, $n = 2$:
$$P = \frac{1166.40}{(1.08)^2} = \frac{1166.40}{1.1664} = 1000.00 \checkmark$$

This confirms our bank-savings analogy from Section 1.3.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Uniform Series Compound Amount (F/A, i, n)

**Setup:** A uniform end-of-period deposit $A$ is made for $n$ consecutive periods. We want the accumulated sum $F$ at the end of period $n$.

**Visualize the Cash Flow:**

- Period 1 deposit of $A$ earns interest for $(n-1)$ periods → grows to $A(1+i)^{n-1}$
- Period 2 deposit of $A$ earns interest for $(n-2)$ periods → grows to $A(1+i)^{n-2}$
- Period 3 deposit of $A$ earns interest for $(n-3)$ periods → grows to $A(1+i)^{n-3}$
- ... continuing ...
- Period $(n-1)$ deposit → grows to $A(1+i)^{1}$
- Period $n$ deposit → earns no interest → remains $A(1+i)^0 = A$

**Sum the Geometric Series:**
$$F = A(1+i)^{n-1} + A(1+i)^{n-2} + A(1+i)^{n-3} + \ldots + A(1+i)^{1} + A(1+i)^{0}$$

Factor out $A$:
$$F = A \cdot \left[(1+i)^{n-1} + (1+i)^{n-2} + \ldots + (1+i) + 1\right]$$

The bracketed sum is a **finite geometric series** with first term $u_1 = 1$, common ratio $r = (1+i)$, and $n$ terms.

**Standard Geometric Series Sum Formula:**
$$S_n = u_1 \cdot \frac{1 - r^n}{1 - r} = 1 \cdot \frac{1 - (1+i)^n}{1 - (1+i)} = \frac{1 - (1+i)^n}{-i} = \frac{(1+i)^n - 1}{i}$$

**Substitute back:**
$$\boxed{F = A \cdot \dfrac{(1+i)^n - 1}{i}}$$

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Uniform Series Present Worth (P/A, i, n)

**Setup:** Find the present value $P$ of a uniform end-of-period series $A$ over $n$ periods.

**Method — Two-step equivalence:**
1. First, find the future worth of the series at the end of period $n$ using Factor 3.
2. Then, discount that future worth back to time 0 using Factor 2.

**Step 1: Future worth of the series (from Section 3.3):**
$$F = A \cdot \frac{(1+i)^n - 1}{i}$$

**Step 2: Discount to time 0 (from Section 3.2):**
$$P = F \cdot (1+i)^{-n} = A \cdot \frac{(1+i)^n - 1}{i} \cdot (1+i)^{-n}$$

**Simplify the algebra:**
$$P = A \cdot \frac{(1+i)^n - 1}{i \cdot (1+i)^n}$$

Split the numerator as a difference of two terms:
$$P = A \cdot \frac{(1+i)^n}{i(1+i)^n} - A \cdot \frac{1}{i(1+i)^n}$$

$$P = A \cdot \frac{1}{i} - \frac{A}{i(1+i)^n}$$

Factor out $\dfrac{A}{i}$:
$$\boxed{P = \dfrac{A}{i} \cdot \left[1 - (1+i)^{-n}\right]}$$

This is the **standard closed-form expression** of the $(P/A, i, n)$ factor used in KTU textbooks and reference tables.

## 3.5 Derivation 5: Capital Recovery (A/P, i, n)

**Setup:** Given a present loan $P$ repaid as a uniform series $A$ over $n$ periods at interest $i$, find $A$.

**Invert the result of Section 3.4:**
$$P = A \cdot \frac{(1+i)^n - 1}{i(1+i)^n}$$

Solve for $A$:
$$\boxed{A = P \cdot \dfrac{i(1+i)^n}{(1+i)^n - 1}}$$

**Alternate Form (Equivalent, often used in KTU numericals):**
Using the identity $(1+i)^n - 1 = (1+i)^n - 1$, we can also write:
$$A = P \cdot \left[i + \frac{i}{(1+i)^n - 1}\right] = P \cdot \left[i + (A/F, i, n)\right]$$

This is the **"i plus sinking fund" interpretation**: the annual payment equals the interest on the principal plus the sinking fund deposit that retires the principal.

## 3.6 Derivation 6: Sinking Fund (A/F, i, n)

**Setup:** Find the uniform end-of-period deposit $A$ required to accumulate a future sum $F$ at the end of period $n$ at interest $i$.

**Invert the result of Section 3.3:**
$$F = A \cdot \frac{(1+i)^n - 1}{i}$$

Solve for $A$:
$$\boxed{A = F \cdot \dfrac{i}{(1+i)^n - 1}}$$

## 3.7 The Master Algebraic Relationship

Combining all six derivations, the following **cyclic relationship** holds and is the basis of solving any KTU equivalence problem:

$$(F/A, i, n) \cdot (A/F, i, n) = 1$$
$$(P/A, i, n) \cdot (A/P, i, n) = 1$$
$$(F/P, i, n) \cdot (P/F, i, n) = 1$$
$$(F/A, i, n) = (F/P, i, n) \cdot (P/A, i, n)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** If you remember only the $(F/A, i, n)$ formula and the identity above, you can derive every other factor during the exam. This is the *fastest* path to scoring full marks in the equivalence section.

## 3.8 Analytical Matrix — Cost Comparison Methods

In engineering economic analysis of chemical projects, once equivalence is established, **cost comparison** is performed using one of the following methods. The KTU syllabus lists these as the standard tools:

| Method | Decision Criterion | Best Suited For | Limitation |
|--------|--------------------|------------------|------------|
| **Present Worth (PW)** | Choose alternative with highest $PW$ (or lowest $PW$ of costs) | Comparing alternatives with **same service life** | Sensitive to choice of study period |
| **Future Worth (FW)** | Choose alternative with highest $FW$ (or lowest $FW$ of costs) | Long-horizon comparisons | Numerically large values; not intuitive |
| **Annual Worth (AW)** | Choose alternative with highest $AW$ (or lowest $AW$ of costs) | **Unequal service lives** | Requires repeating-cycle assumption |
| **Rate of Return (ROR / IRR)** | Choose if ROR > MARR | Selecting from many mutually exclusive options | May have multiple roots; iterative solution required |
| **Benefit-Cost Ratio (BCR)** | Accept if BCR > 1 | Public-sector / environmental projects | Subjective benefit estimation |
| **Payback Period (PP)** | Choose alternative with **shorter** PP | Quick screening of risky projects | Ignores time value beyond payback |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Module 1 Scope:** The course explicitly emphasizes **Present Worth (PW)**, **Future Worth (FW)**, and **Annual Worth (AW)** methods, with brief exposure to **Rate of Return**. BCR and Payback Period are mentioned as supplementary screening tools but are not the primary focus of Module 1.

### Equivalence Across Methods

The PW, FW, and AW methods are **mathematically equivalent** — they will always yield the same ranking of alternatives. The choice of method is purely for **computational convenience**:

- If your cash flow is given as a single future amount, use **PW** by converting to $P = F(1+i)^{-n}$.
- If your cash flow is a uniform series, use **AW** directly or convert to PW first.
- If your cash flow is a present amount, use **AW** via $A = P \cdot (A/P, i, n)$.

---

### Worked Example (Illustrative, complements Section 5)

A chemical plant requires an initial investment of ₹20,00,000. It generates a uniform annual revenue of ₹4,00,000 for 5 years and has a salvage value of ₹2,00,000 at the end of year 5. The MARR is 10%. Compute the Present Worth and determine if the investment is acceptable (PW > 0 ⇒ acceptable).

**Step 1:** Compute the Present Worth of the uniform revenue series.
$$P_{revenue} = 4{,}00{,}000 \cdot (P/A, 10\%, 5)$$

$$(P/A, 10\%, 5) = \frac{(1.10)^5 - 1}{0.10 \cdot (1.10)^5} = \frac{1.61051 - 1}{0.161051} = \frac{0.61051}{0.161051} \approx 3.7908$$

$$P_{revenue} = 4{,}00{,}000 \times 3.7908 = ₹15{,}16{,}320$$

**Step 2:** Compute the Present Worth of the salvage value.
$$P_{salvage} = 2{,}00{,}000 \cdot (P/F, 10\%, 5)$$

$$(P/F, 10\%, 5) = (1.10)^{-5} = \frac{1}{1.61051} \approx 0.6209$$

$$P_{salvage} = 2{,}00{,}000 \times 0.6209 = ₹1{,}24{,}180$$

**Step 3:** Compute the Net Present Worth.
$$NPW = -20{,}00{,}000 + 15{,}16{,}320 + 1{,}24{,}180 = -3{,}59{,}500$$

**Conclusion:** NPW is **negative** (₹-3,59,500). The investment is **NOT acceptable** at a 10% MARR. The chemical plant will not earn the minimum required return.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Compilation Safeguards Applied:** All node IDs are alphanumeric, labels are clean uppercase text, no markdown formatting inside double-quoted node labels, and no reserved keywords are used as standalone node names.

## 4.1 Cash Flow Pattern Diagram — The Six Equivalence Factors

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0["CASH FLOW AT TIME 0"] --> A1["Single Payment P"]
    A0 --> A2["Uniform Series A over n periods"]
    A0 --> A3["Gradient Series G increasing each period"]
    
    A1 --> B1["Compound Forward: F = P times 1+i raised to n"]
    A1 --> B2["Discount Backward: P = F times 1+i raised to minus n"]
    
    A2 --> C1["Future Worth Factor: F = A times F by A factor"]
    A2 --> C2["Present Worth Factor: P = A times P by A factor"]
    A2 --> C3["Capital Recovery: A = P times A by P factor"]
    A2 --> C4["Sinking Fund: A = F times A by F factor"]
    
    A3 --> D1["Convert gradient G to equivalent A using P by G and A by G factors"]
    
    C2 --> E1["EQUIVALENT VALUE AT TIME 0"]
    B2 --> E1
    C1 --> E2["EQUIVALENT VALUE AT TIME N"]
    B1 --> E2
    C3 --> E3["EQUIVALENT UNIFORM ANNUAL VALUE"]
    C4 --> E3
    D1 --> E3
```

## 4.2 Equivalence Master Relationship — The Cyclic Wheel

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    N0["PRESENT P at time 0"] -- "F/P compound forward" --> NN["FUTURE F at time n"]
    NN -- "P/F discount backward" --> N0
    
    N0 -- "A/P capital recovery" --> NA["UNIFORM A over n periods"]
    NA -- "P/A present worth" --> N0
    
    NN -- "A/F sinking fund" --> NA
    NA -- "F/A future worth" --> NN
    
    style N0 fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style NN fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style NA fill:#98FB98,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

## 4.3 Cost Comparison Decision Flow — Block-Level Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1["STEP 1: Define Alternatives and Cash Flows"] --> S2["STEP 2: Establish Common Time Horizon and MARR"]
    S2 --> S3{"STEP 3: Identify Service Life Equal?"}
    
    S3 -- "YES, equal lives" --> S4["STEP 4A: Apply Present Worth Method"]
    S3 -- "NO, unequal lives" --> S5["STEP 4B: Apply Annual Worth Method with repeat assumption"]
    
    S4 --> S6["STEP 5: Compute Equivalent Worth of Each Alternative"]
    S5 --> S6
    
    S6 --> S7["STEP 6: Select Alternative with Lowest Cost or Highest Net Worth"]
    S7 --> S8["STEP 7: Perform Sensitivity and Risk Check"]
    S8 --> S9["FINAL RECOMMENDATION"]
    
    style S3 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style S9 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:3px
```

## 4.4 Cash Flow Diagram — Standard Conventions (Text Schematic)

```
          ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────► Time
          │
       0  │      1      2      3    ...   n
          │      │      │      │           │
          │      ▼      ▼      ▼           ▼
          │      A      A      A           A    (Uniform end-of-period)
          │
          │      ┌──────────┐
          │      │          │  Single Payment P at time 0
       P  ├─────►│          │
          │      │          │
          │      └──────────┘
          │
          │                                ┌─────┐
          │                                │  F  │  Future Single Payment
          │                                └─────┘
          │
          ▼
```

**Legend:**
- ↓ Down-arrow = Cash Outflow (Cost / Investment)
- ↑ Up-arrow = Cash Inflow (Revenue / Salvage)
- Time 0 = Present; Period n = End of project

## 4.5 Block Diagram — TVM to Cost Comparison Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Raw Cash Flow Data: Inflows, Outflows, Salvage, Periods"] --> B["TVM Engine: Apply Interest i or MARR"]
    B --> C["Convert to Common Date using F/P, P/F, F/A, P/A, A/P, A/F"]
    C --> D["Equivalence Established: PW, FW, or AW Computed"]
    D --> E["Cost Comparison Method Selected"]
    E --> F1["Present Worth Method"]
    E --> F2["Future Worth Method"]
    E --> F3["Annual Worth Method"]
    E --> F4["Rate of Return Method"]
    F1 --> G["Rank Alternatives: Select Best"]
    F2 --> G
    F3 --> G
    F4 --> G
    G --> H["Engineering Economic Decision"]
    
    style B fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style C fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style D fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px

```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1: [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Question]
**Define the Time Value of Money. Why is a rupee received today worth more than a rupee received in the future?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Definition (1 Mark):** The Time Value of Money (TVM) is the economic principle that a unit of currency available at the present time is worth more than the same unit of currency available at a future date, because the present unit can be invested to earn interest over time.
- **Reason 1 (1 Mark):** Earning potential — money available today can be invested at a positive interest rate (MARR) and grow to a larger amount in the future.
- **Reason 2 (1 Mark):** Inflation effect — the purchasing power of money erodes over time due to general price-level increases, so future money buys fewer goods and services. Combined with risk and opportunity cost, this makes present money more valuable.

### Question A2: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Question]
**State and explain the equivalence relationship between the Uniform Series Present Worth factor and the Capital Recovery factor.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Statement (2 Marks):** The Uniform Series Present Worth factor $(P/A, i, n)$ and the Capital Recovery factor $(A/P, i, n)$ are mathematical reciprocals of each other:
$$(A/P, i, n) = \frac{1}{(P/A, i, n)} = \frac{i(1+i)^n}{(1+i)^n - 1}$$
- **Explanation (1 Mark):** $P/A$ converts a uniform series $A$ into a single present sum $P$, while $A/P$ converts a present sum $P$ into an equivalent uniform series $A$. Since both represent the *same* economic value at the *same* time reference, they must be exact inverses.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> - **Common Mistake 1:** Students frequently forget the *sign convention* — initial investments are negative (outflow), revenues are positive (inflow). Mixing signs leads to incorrect PW/ AW values and full loss of 7 marks in sub-question (a).
> - **Common Mistake 2:** Students apply the **wrong factor**. If the cash flow is uniform, use $(P/A)$ or $(F/A)$. If it is a single future amount, use $(P/F)$ or $(F/P)$. Always identify the cash flow pattern *first* before selecting the factor.
> - **Common Mistake 3:** Students forget to **convert the interest rate** to match the period. If $i$ is given annually and periods are in months, divide $i$ by 12.
> - **Common Mistake 4:** Students write the formula but do **not show the numerical substitution** step. The KTU board examiner requires the *substitution step* for full credit.

### Question Choice A: [KTU University Exam - Sample Module-End Question, 14 Marks]

**A chemical engineering firm is evaluating two alternative reactors, Reactor-X and Reactor-Y, for producing a specialty chemical. The relevant data are given below. Use the Present Worth method at a MARR of 12% per annum to determine which reactor should be selected. Assume end-of-year cash flows.**

| Parameter | Reactor-X | Reactor-Y |
|-----------|-----------|-----------|
| Initial Investment | ₹8,00,000 | ₹12,00,000 |
| Annual Operating Cost | ₹1,50,000 | ₹90,000 |
| Salvage Value | ₹1,00,000 | ₹2,00,000 |
| Service Life | 6 years | 6 years |

**Sub-part (a) [7 Marks]:** Compute the Present Worth of Cost (PWC) for both reactors.

**Sub-part (b) [7 Marks]:** Select the better reactor and justify your answer.

---

**Model Solution for Question Choice A:**

**Solution to (a):**

**Step 1:** Identify the cash flows for each reactor.

For Reactor-X (costs are negative to the firm, but we compute *cost* and minimize it):
- Initial Investment at $t=0$: ₹8,00,000 (outflow)
- Annual Operating Cost: ₹1,50,000 (end of years 1 through 6)
- Salvage Value: ₹1,00,000 (inflow at end of year 6)

For Reactor-Y:
- Initial Investment: ₹12,00,000
- Annual Operating Cost: ₹90,000
- Salvage Value: ₹2,00,000

**Step 2:** Write the Present Worth of Cost (PWC) equation.

Since both reactors have the same service life (6 years), we can directly compute PWC at MARR $i = 12\%$.

For Reactor-X:
$$PWC_X = P_{initial} + A_{op} \cdot (P/A, 12\%, 6) - S \cdot (P/F, 12\%, 6)$$

For Reactor-Y:
$$PWC_Y = P_{initial} + A_{op} \cdot (P/A, 12\%, 6) - S \cdot (P/F, 12\%, 6)$$

**Step 3:** Compute the required factors at $i = 12\%$, $n = 6$.

First, $(1+i)^n = (1.12)^6$:
$$(1.12)^2 = 1.2544$$
$$(1.12)^3 = 1.404928$$
$$(1.12)^6 = (1.404928)^2 = 1.973823$$

Now compute the factors:

$$(P/A, 12\%, 6) = \frac{(1.12)^6 - 1}{0.12 \cdot (1.12)^6} = \frac{1.973823 - 1}{0.12 \times 1.973823} = \frac{0.973823}{0.236859} = 4.1114$$

$$(P/F, 12\%, 6) = \frac{1}{(1.12)^6} = \frac{1}{1.973823} = 0.5066$$

**Step 4:** Compute $PWC_X$.

$$PWC_X = 8{,}00{,}000 + 1{,}50{,}000 \times 4.1114 - 1{,}00{,}000 \times 0.5066$$

$$PWC_X = 8{,}00{,}000 + 6{,}16{,}710 - 50{,}660$$

$$\boxed{PWC_X = ₹13{,}66{,}050}$$

**Step 5:** Compute $PWC_Y$.

$$PWC_Y = 12{,}00{,}000 + 90{,}000 \times 4.1114 - 2{,}00{,}000 \times 0.5066$$

$$PWC_Y = 12{,}00{,}000 + 3{,}70{,}026 - 1{,}01{,}320$$

$$\boxed{PWC_Y = ₹14{,}68{,}706}$$

**Solution to (b):**

**Comparison and Decision:**
- $PWC_X = ₹13{,}66{,}050$
- $PWC_Y = ₹14{,}68{,}706$

Since both alternatives have the same service life, we select the one with the **lower Present Worth of Cost**. The decision rule is: **Choose the alternative with minimum PWC.**

$$PWC_X < PWC_Y$$

$$13{,}66{,}050 < 14{,}68{,}706$$

**Conclusion:** **Reactor-X should be selected** because its present worth of cost is ₹1,02,656 lower than that of Reactor-Y. Although Reactor-Y has a lower annual operating cost, the higher initial investment and the time value of those costs make Reactor-X economically superior at 12% MARR.

> [!NOTE]
> **Important Insight:** This problem demonstrates a key principle — *lower annual cost does not always mean lower total cost* when the initial investments differ. The TVM equivalence through the $(P/A)$ factor captures this trade-off mathematically.

---

### Question Choice B: [KTU University Exam - Sample Module-End Question, 14 Marks]

**A process plant requires a one-time capital investment of ₹15,00,000 today. The plant is expected to generate a uniform net revenue of ₹4,50,000 per year for 8 years. At the end of year 8, the plant will be decommissioned and the salvage value is estimated as ₹3,00,000. The Minimum Attractive Rate of Return (MARR) is 9% per annum.**

**Sub-part (a) [7 Marks]:** Compute the Net Present Worth (NPW) of the investment and determine whether the investment is acceptable.

**Sub-part (b) [7 Marks]:** Compute the Equivalent Annual Worth (AW) of the investment and verify that the decision matches the NPW result.

---

**Model Solution for Question Choice B:**

**Solution to (a):**

**Step 1:** Identify the cash flows.

- Initial Investment at $t=0$: $P = -₹15,00,000$ (outflow, negative sign)
- Annual Net Revenue (years 1 through 8): $A = +₹4,50,000$ (inflow)
- Salvage Value at end of year 8: $S = +₹3,00,000$ (inflow)

**Step 2:** State the NPW equation.

$$NPW = -P + A \cdot (P/A, 9\%, 8) + S \cdot (P/F, 9\%, 8)$$

**Step 3:** Compute the required factors at $i = 9\%$, $n = 8$.

Compute $(1.09)^8$ step by step:
$$(1.09)^2 = 1.1881$$
$$(1.09)^4 = (1.1881)^2 = 1.41158$$
$$(1.09)^8 = (1.41158)^2 = 1.99256$$

Now:
$$(P/A, 9\%, 8) = \frac{1.99256 - 1}{0.09 \times 1.99256} = \frac{0.99256}{0.17933} = 5.5348$$

$$(P/F, 9\%, 8) = \frac{1}{1.99256} = 0.5019$$

**Step 4:** Substitute and compute NPW.

$$NPW = -15{,}00{,}000 + 4{,}50{,}000 \times 5.5348 + 3{,}00{,}000 \times 0.5019$$

$$NPW = -15{,}00{,}000 + 24{,}90{,}660 + 1{,}50{,}570$$

$$\boxed{NPW = ₹11{,}41{,}230}$$

**Step 5:** Decision Rule.

Since $NPW = +₹11,41,230 > 0$, the investment **earns more than 9% MARR** and is therefore **ACCEPTABLE** (Profitable).

**Solution to (b):**

**Step 1:** Convert the NPW to an equivalent uniform annual series using the Capital Recovery factor $(A/P, 9\%, 8)$.

$$(A/P, 9\%, 8) = \frac{0.09 \times 1.99256}{1.99256 - 1} = \frac{0.17933}{0.99256} = 0.18067$$

**Step 2:** Compute the Equivalent Annual Worth.

The NPW of ₹11,41,230, when distributed as an equivalent annual worth over 8 years at 9% MARR, is:

$$AW = NPW \cdot (A/P, 9\%, 8) = 11{,}41{,}230 \times 0.18067$$

$$\boxed{AW = ₹2{,}06{,}175 \text{ per year}}$$

**Alternative direct calculation (verification):**
$$AW = -P \cdot (A/P, 9\%, 8) + A + S \cdot (A/F, 9\%, 8)$$

Compute $(A/F, 9\%, 8)$:
$$(A/F, 9\%, 8) = \frac{0.09}{1.99256 - 1} = \frac{0.09}{0.99256} = 0.09067$$

$$AW = -15{,}00{,}000 \times 0.18067 + 4{,}50{,}000 + 3{,}00{,}000 \times 0.09067$$

$$AW = -2{,}71{,}005 + 4{,}50{,}000 + 27{,}201 = ₹2{,}06{,}196$$

The small difference (₹21) is due to rounding of intermediate factors — this is acceptable in KTU valuation.

**Step 3:** Decision Verification.

Since $AW = +₹2,06,175 > 0$, the investment is **ACCEPTABLE**, confirming the NPW result.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Takeaway for Students:** The NPW and AW methods are mathematically *equivalent* — they will always produce the same accept/reject decision. AW is particularly useful when comparing alternatives with *unequal service lives* because it does not require a common study period.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Module 1 Equivalence:**

### Conceptual Foundation
- ✅ **TVM** = money today is worth more than the same money in the future (interest earning + inflation + risk).
- ✅ **Equivalence** = two cash flows at different times have the same economic value at a common date at a given interest rate $i$ (MARR).
- ✅ **Compound interest** is the default in KTU problems; simple interest is rare.
- ✅ **MARR** is the interest rate used for all equivalence calculations.

### The Six Master Formulas
- ✅ $(F/P, i, n) = (1+i)^n$ — single payment compound amount
- ✅ $(P/F, i, n) = (1+i)^{-n}$ — single payment present worth
- ✅ $(F/A, i, n) = \dfrac{(1+i)^n - 1}{i}$ — uniform series future worth
- ✅ $(P/A, i, n) = \dfrac{(1+i)^n - 1}{i(1+i)^n}$ — uniform series present worth
- ✅ $(A/P, i, n) = \dfrac{i(1+i)^n}{(1+i)^n - 1}$ — capital recovery
- ✅ $(A/F, i, n) = \dfrac{i}{(1+i)^n - 1}$ — sinking fund

### The Reciprocal Relationships
- ✅ $(F/P)$ and $(P/F)$ are reciprocals
- ✅ $(F/A)$ and $(A/F)$ are reciprocals
- ✅ $(P/A)$ and $(A/P)$ are reciprocals
- ✅ $(P/A) = (P/F) \times (F/A)$ — the master linkage

### Cash Flow Sign Convention
- ✅ Inflows (revenues, salvage) = **Positive (+)** ↑ arrows
- ✅ Outflows (investment, costs) = **Negative (−)** ↓ arrows
- ✅ Time 0 = **present**; end of period = end-of-year cash flow
- ✅ All KTU problems assume **end-of-period** cash flows unless stated otherwise

### Cost Comparison Decision Rules
- ✅ **PW method:** Choose the alternative with the **minimum PWC** (cost minimization) or **maximum NPW** (revenue maximization).
- ✅ **AW method:** Choose the alternative with the **minimum AWC** or **maximum NAW**; especially useful for *unequal service lives*.
- ✅ **FW method:** Choose the alternative with the **minimum FWC** or **maximum NFW**; equivalent to PW.
- ✅ **Rate of Return method:** Accept if ROR ≥ MARR.

### Common Exam Pitfalls to Avoid
- ❌ Forgetting the sign of the initial investment (must be negative for NPW)
- ❌ Confusing the $(P/A)$ factor with the $(A/P)$ factor (they are reciprocals, NOT equal)
- ❌ Applying a factor with the wrong interest rate (annual vs. monthly mismatch)
- ❌ Computing $(F/A)$ as $\dfrac{(1+i)^n + 1}{i}$ instead of $-1$ in the numerator
- ❌ Comparing PW of two alternatives with **different service lives** without applying the repeat-cycle or AW method
- ❌ Not showing the **substitution step** in numerical problems (KTU examiner deducts 1 mark per missing step)

### Final Exam Strategy
- ✅ **First** draw a cash flow diagram for each alternative.
- ✅ **Second** identify the cash flow pattern (single, uniform, gradient).
- ✅ **Third** select the appropriate factor from the six standard ones.
- ✅ **Fourth** compute the factor values at the given MARR and $n$.
- ✅ **Fifth** apply the formula and substitute numerical values.
- ✅ **Sixth** state the decision rule and select the alternative.

> [!WARNING]
> **Final KTU Examiner's Warning:** In KTU board valuation, *correct final answer without showing substitution steps = 50% marks only*. Always show the equation, the factor lookup or computation, and the final substitution. Marks are awarded at each step, not just the answer.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Equations Used in Economic Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT757 - ECONOMICS AND MANAGEMENT OF CHEMICAL INDUSTRIES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Equations Used in Economic Analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

**Equations Used in Economic Analysis** are the mathematical relationships that quantify the **time value of money (TVM)**, allowing engineers to compare cash flows occurring at different points in time. In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology for Process Economics, these equations form the **analytical foundation of Engineering Economy** — the discipline of formulating, estimating, and evaluating the economic outcomes of engineering alternatives in chemical process industries.

In a chemical plant project, money is spent today (capital investment) to earn revenue in the future (product sales). Because **a rupee today is worth more than a rupee tomorrow**, we must mathematically move all cash flows to a common point on the time axis before comparing them. The equations that perform this "shifting" of money are collectively called the **Equations Used in Economic Analysis**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:**
> These equations are the *gateway* to all subsequent modules — depreciation, profitability analysis (DCFRR, NPV, Payback), replacement analysis, and optimum design of chemical plants are all built on top of these basic TVM equations.

## 1.2 Variables & Symbols (The Engineering "Alphabet")

Before diving in, let us fix the meaning of every symbol so that all subsequent equations are unambiguous.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Engineering Economy Notation**

| Symbol | Meaning | Typical Units |
|:---:|:---|:---:|
| $P$ | Present Worth / Present Value | ₹ (Rupees) |
| $F$ | Future Worth / Future Value | ₹ (Rupees) |
| $A$ | Uniform Annual Amount (Annuity) | ₹ / year |
| $i$ | Interest Rate per Period (effective) | dimensionless / period |
| $n$ | Number of Compounding Periods | years / periods |
| $t$ | Time | years |
| $r$ | Nominal (stated) Interest Rate | dimensionless / year |
| $m$ | Number of Compounding Periods per Year | integer |
| $i_e$ | Effective Annual Interest Rate | dimensionless / year |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **The "Money-Time Machine" Analogy**

Imagine you have a **time machine for money**. If you place ₹1,000 today into a savings account paying 10% per year, after 1 year the bank gives you back ₹1,100. The bank essentially *moved* your ₹1,000 *forward in time* and added a "rental fee" of ₹100 for using your money.

- **Future Worth (F):** How much will my money *become* if I invest it? (Time Machine: Present → Future)
- **Present Worth (P):** How much is a *future* amount worth *today*? (Time Machine: Future → Present)
- **Annuity (A):** A series of *equal* payments every year, like a monthly salary.

Every equation in this module is just a different "gear setting" of this time machine.

## 1.4 Why This Topic Matters in Chemical Engineering

In a real chemical plant, the **Capital Expenditure (CAPEX)** of a ₹500 crore plant is incurred *now*, but profits trickle in over 20–30 years. The Economic Analysis equations allow a process engineer to:

1. **Compare two reactor designs** with different cost and revenue profiles.
2. **Decide whether to replace** an old distillation column.
3. **Choose between leasing vs. buying** a compressor.
4. **Estimate the minimum selling price** of a chemical product.

Without these equations, capital investment decisions in petrochemicals, fertilizers, and bulk drug industries would be pure guesswork.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The "Why" — Logic Behind the Time Value of Money

Money has time value because of three fundamental reasons:

- **Opportunity Cost:** A rupee invested today earns a return; a rupee received tomorrow cannot earn until it is received.
- **Inflation:** The purchasing power of money decreases over time.
- **Risk:** Future money is uncertain; a bird in hand is worth two in the bush.

To make rational economic decisions, the engineer must account for all three by mathematically "discounting" or "compounding" every cash flow.

## 2.2 The Two Fundamental Operations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Every TVM equation is a combination of just two operations:**
> 1. **Single-Payment Compound-Amount Factor (SPCAF / F/P):** Future value of a present sum.
> 2. **Single-Payment Present-Worth Factor (SPPWF / P/F):** Present value of a future sum.

All other equations (annuities, perpetuities, sinking funds) are **repeated applications** of these two core operations.

## 2.3 Complete KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> **All 12 KTU-Mandated TVM Equations — Master This Table**

| # | Name | Equation | Find Given | KTU Tag |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---:|:---:|
| 1 | Simple Interest | $I = P \, n \, i$ | Interest from P, n, i | Basic |
| 2 | Single-Payment Compound Amount | $F = P(1+i)^{n}$ | F given P | Core |
| 3 | Single-Payment Present Worth | $P = F(1+i)^{-n}$ | P given F | Core |
| 4 | Equal Payment Series Compound Amount | $F = A \left[ \dfrac{(1+i)^{n} - 1}{i} \right]$ | F given A | Annuity |
| 5 | Sinking Fund Factor | $A = F \left[ \dfrac{i}{(1+i)^{n} - 1} \right]$ | A given F | Annuity |
| 6 | Capital Recovery Factor | $A = P \left[ \dfrac{i(1+i)^{n}}{(1+i)^{n} - 1} \right]$ | A given P | Annuity |
| 7 | Equal Payment Series Present Worth | $P = A \left[ \dfrac{(1+i)^{n} - 1}{i(1+i)^{n}} \right]$ | P given A | Annuity |
| 8 | Uniform Gradient Present Worth | $P = G \left[ \dfrac{(1+i)^{n} - i n - 1}{i^{2}(1+i)^{n}} \right]$ | P given G | Gradient |
| 9 | Uniform Gradient Future Worth | $F = G \left[ \dfrac{(1+i)^{n} - i n - 1}{i^{2}} \right]$ | F given G | Gradient |
| 10 | Uniform Gradient to Annuity | $A = G \left[ \dfrac{1}{i} - \dfrac{n}{(1+i)^{n} - 1} \right]$ | A given G | Gradient |
| 11 | Continuous Compounding | $F = P \, e^{i n}$ | F given P (continuous) | Special |
| 12 | Effective Annual Rate | $i_{e} = \left(1 + \dfrac{r}{m}\right)^{m} - 1$ | $i_e$ from nominal $r$ | Special |

**Depreciation Equations (Module 1 Extension in KTU):**

| # | Method | Equation | Notes |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---|
| 13 | Straight-Line | $D_{k} = \dfrac{C_{0} - S}{N}$ | Constant each year |
| 14 | Declining Balance (DB) | $D_{k} = C_{k-1} \, d$ | $d$ = depreciation fraction |
| 15 | Sum-of-Years' Digits (SYD) | $D_{k} = \dfrac{N - k + 1}{\sum_{j=1}^{N} j} (C_{0} - S)$ | Accelerated |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| TVM Concept | Application in Chemical Industry |
|:---|:---|
| Present Worth (P) | Comparing capital cost of two reactor designs *today* |
| Future Worth (F) | Estimating replacement cost of a heat exchanger 10 years hence |
| Annuity (A) | Annual operating cost of a cooling tower |
| Capital Recovery (CR) | Equivalent annual cost of a ₹100 crore plant over 20 years |
| Sinking Fund (SF) | Money set aside yearly to replace a turbine after 15 years |
| Gradient (G) | Annual maintenance cost that *increases* by ₹50,000/year |
| Continuous Compounding | High-frequency dividend / spot-price discounting |
| Effective Rate | Converting "12% compounded monthly" to true annual % |

## 2.5 Cash Flow Diagrams — The Universal Pictorial Language

A **cash flow diagram** is an engineer's *free-body diagram* for money. Convention:

- **Horizontal axis** = Time (left = present, right = future).
- **Upward arrow** = Cash inflow (revenue, salvage).
- **Downward arrow** = Cash outflow (investment, cost).

> [!TIP]
> KTU examiners love asking students to **draw a cash flow diagram** before solving a problem. Always do it — it earns easy marks and prevents sign errors.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section provides **every algebraic step** for the KTU-mandated derivations. The KTU 2024 Scheme expects students to know *how* the key formulas are derived, not just memorize them.

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Single-Payment Compound-Amount Factor (SPCAF)

**Starting point:** A sum $P$ is invested at interest rate $i$ per period. We want the value $F$ after $n$ periods.

**Step 1 — Define the growth per period.**
At the end of period 1, the amount equals principal plus one period's interest:

$$
F_{1} = P + P \cdot i = P(1 + i)
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the same logic to period 2.**
The new principal is $F_1$. So:

$$
F_{2} = F_{1}(1 + i) = P(1+i)(1+i) = P(1+i)^{2}
$$

**Step 3 — Inductively generalize.**
By the principle of mathematical induction, after $n$ periods:

$$
F = P(1+i)^{n}
$$

**Step 4 — Define the standard factor notation.**

$$
\boxed{F = P \, (F/P, i, n) \quad \text{where} \quad (F/P, i, n) = (1+i)^{n}}
$$

This is the **fundamental building block** of all other TVM equations.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Single-Payment Present-Worth Factor (SPPWF)

**Starting point:** We know $F$ will be received $n$ periods from now. What is its equivalent value *today*?

**Step 1 — Start from the SPCAF equation.**

$$
F = P(1+i)^{n}
$$

**Step 2 — Algebraically isolate $P$.**
Divide both sides by $(1+i)^{n}$:

$$
P = \dfrac{F}{(1+i)^{n}}
$$

**Step 3 — Write in standard notation.**

$$
\boxed{P = F \, (P/F, i, n) \quad \text{where} \quad (P/F, i, n) = (1+i)^{-n}}
$$

**Step 4 — Verify the inverse relationship.**

$$
(P/F, i, n) = \dfrac{1}{(F/P, i, n)} \quad \checkmark
$$

This invertibility is a powerful property used to convert between present and future worth in *one* step.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Equal Payment Series Compound-Amount (Annuity → F)

**Setup:** A uniform amount $A$ is deposited at the *end* of each of $n$ periods. Find the total value $F$ at the end of period $n$.

**Step 1 — Lay out the cash flow timing.**

| Period $t$ | Deposit | Periods Remaining to Grow |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 1 | $A$ | $n - 1$ |
| 2 | $A$ | $n - 2$ |
| $\vdots$ | $\vdots$ | $\vdots$ |
| $n$ | $A$ | $0$ |

**Step 2 — Compound each deposit to the end of period $n$.**

$$
F = A(1+i)^{n-1} + A(1+i)^{n-2} + \cdots + A(1+i)^{1} + A(1+i)^{0}
$$

**Step 3 — Recognize this as a geometric series.**
Let $x = (1+i)$. The series is $A(x^{n-1} + x^{n-2} + \cdots + x + 1)$.

The sum of a finite geometric series is $S = a \, \dfrac{r^{N} - 1}{r - 1}$.

With $a = A$, $r = (1+i)$, $N = n$:

$$
F = A \cdot \dfrac{(1+i)^{n} - 1}{(1+i) - 1}
$$

**Step 4 — Simplify the denominator.**

$$
\boxed{F = A \left[ \dfrac{(1+i)^{n} - 1}{i} \right]}
$$

The bracketed term is the **Sinking Fund Factor (SFF) inverse**, called the **Equal Payment Series Compound-Amount Factor**, denoted $(F/A, i, n)$.

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Capital Recovery Factor (P → A)

**Setup:** A sum $P$ is borrowed today. It must be paid back in $n$ equal annual installments $A$ at interest rate $i$. Find $A$.

**Step 1 — Convert $P$ to $F$ at the end of year $n$ using SPCAF.**

$$
F = P(1+i)^{n}
$$

**Step 2 — Convert $F$ to an equivalent series of payments $A$ using the annuity equation from §3.3.**

$$
F = A \left[ \dfrac{(1+i)^{n} - 1}{i} \right]
$$

**Step 3 — Equate the two expressions for $F$ and solve for $A$.**

$$
P(1+i)^{n} = A \left[ \dfrac{(1+i)^{n} - 1}{i} \right]
$$

$$
A = P \cdot \dfrac{i(1+i)^{n}}{(1+i)^{n} - 1}
$$

**Step 4 — Final boxed result.**

$$
\boxed{A = P \left[ \dfrac{i(1+i)^{n}}{(1+i)^{n} - 1} \right]}
$$

The bracketed term is the **Capital Recovery Factor (CRF)**, denoted $(A/P, i, n)$.

## 3.5 Derivation 5: Equal Payment Series Present Worth (A → P)

**Setup:** A uniform annual amount $A$ is received at the end of each of $n$ years. Find present worth $P$ at interest rate $i$.

**Step 1 — Discount each $A$ to present using SPPWF.**

$$
P = \dfrac{A}{(1+i)^{1}} + \dfrac{A}{(1+i)^{2}} + \cdots + \dfrac{A}{(1+i)^{n}}
$$

**Step 2 — Recognize as a geometric series with $r = \dfrac{1}{1+i}$.**

$$
P = A \cdot \dfrac{\dfrac{1}{1+i} \left[1 - \left(\dfrac{1}{1+i}\right)^{n}\right]}{1 - \dfrac{1}{1+i}}
$$

**Step 3 — Algebraically simplify.**

The denominator becomes $\dfrac{(1+i) - 1}{1+i} = \dfrac{i}{1+i}$.

Multiplying numerator and denominator by $(1+i)$:

$$
P = A \cdot \dfrac{1 - (1+i)^{-n}}{i}
$$

**Step 4 — Multiply top and bottom by $(1+i)^{n}$ to obtain the standard form.**

$$
P = A \cdot \dfrac{(1+i)^{n} - 1}{i(1+i)^{n}}
$$

**Step 5 — Final boxed result.**

$$
\boxed{P = A \left[ \dfrac{(1+i)^{n} - 1}{i(1+i)^{n}} \right]}
$$

The bracketed factor is the **Uniform Series Present Worth Factor (USPWF)**, denoted $(P/A, i, n)$.

## 3.6 Derivation 6: Continuous Compounding

**Setup:** Compounding happens continuously, i.e., $m \to \infty$.

**Step 1 — Start from the effective annual rate formula:**

$$
i_{e} = \left(1 + \dfrac{r}{m}\right)^{m} - 1
$$

**Step 2 — Take the limit as $m \to \infty$.**

$$
\lim_{m \to \infty} \left(1 + \dfrac{r}{m}\right)^{m} = e^{r}
$$

(This is the standard definition of $e$.)

**Step 3 — Therefore:**

$$
F = P \, e^{r n}
$$

where $r$ is the nominal annual rate compounded continuously.

**Step 4 — Verify with $r = 0.10$, $n = 1$:**

$$
F = P \, e^{0.10} = P \times 1.10517
$$

Compare with annual compounding $(1.10)^{1} = 1.1000$. Continuous compounding gives a slightly higher return (1.0517% more), as expected.

## 3.7 Derivation 7: Effective Annual Interest Rate

**Setup:** A bank quotes a nominal rate $r$ compounded $m$ times per year. Find the *true* effective annual rate $i_e$.

**Step 1 — Per-period rate.**

$$
i_{\text{period}} = \dfrac{r}{m}
$$

**Step 2 — After $m$ periods in 1 year, $P$ grows to:**

$$
F = P \left(1 + \dfrac{r}{m}\right)^{m}
$$

**Step 3 — Define effective rate as the growth factor minus 1.**

$$
\boxed{i_{e} = \left(1 + \dfrac{r}{m}\right)^{m} - 1}
$$

**Worked example:** $r = 12\%$, $m = 12$ (monthly).

$$
i_{e} = \left(1 + \dfrac{0.12}{12}\right)^{12} - 1 = (1.01)^{12} - 1 = 0.12683 = 12.683\%
$$

So monthly compounding boosts the effective rate from **12%** to **12.683%** — a difference of **68.3 basis points**.

## 3.8 Derivation 8: Straight-Line Depreciation

**Setup:** An asset costs $C_0$, has salvage value $S$ after $N$ years.

**Step 1 — Total depreciable base.**

$$
\text{Depreciable Base} = C_0 - S
$$

**Step 2 — Spread uniformly over $N$ years.**

$$
\boxed{D_{k} = \dfrac{C_0 - S}{N}}
$$

where $D_k$ is the depreciation in year $k$ (constant for all $k = 1, 2, \dots, N$).

**Step 3 — Book value at end of year $k$:**

$$
BV_{k} = C_0 - k \cdot D_{k} = C_0 - k \cdot \dfrac{C_0 - S}{N}
$$

## 3.9 Derivation 9: Declining-Balance Depreciation

**Setup:** Same asset. Depreciation rate is a fixed fraction $d$ of the *current* book value.

**Step 1 — Year-1 depreciation.**

$$
D_{1} = C_0 \cdot d
$$

**Step 2 — Book value at end of year 1.**

$$
BV_{1} = C_0 - D_{1} = C_0(1 - d)
$$

**Step 3 — Inductive generalization.**

$$
D_{k} = C_0 (1 - d)^{k - 1} \, d
$$

**Step 4 — Book value at end of year $k$:**

$$
\boxed{BV_{k} = C_0 (1 - d)^{k}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Double-Declining Balance (DDB)** is a special case where $d = 2/N$.

## 3.10 Numerical Example — Worked Out in Full

> [!TIP]
> **KTU-Style Illustrative Problem**

A chemical plant requires an initial investment of **₹20,00,000**. It generates a uniform annual revenue of **₹4,50,000** for 5 years. The salvage value at the end of year 5 is **₹2,00,000**. The Minimum Acceptable Rate of Return (MARR) is **12% per year**. Find the Net Present Worth (NPW).

**Step 1 — Draw the cash flow diagram mentally:**
- Year 0: outflow of ₹20,00,000
- Years 1–5: inflow of ₹4,50,000 each
- Year 5: additional inflow of ₹2,00,000

**Step 2 — Write the NPW equation.**

$$
NPW = -P + A \cdot (P/A, 0.12, 5) + S \cdot (P/F, 0.12, 5)
$$

**Step 3 — Look up or compute the factors.**

$$
(P/A, 0.12, 5) = \dfrac{(1.12)^{5} - 1}{0.12(1.12)^{5}}
$$

Compute $(1.12)^{5}$:

$$
(1.12)^{5} = 1.12 \times 1.12 \times 1.12 \times 1.12 \times 1.12
$$

Let us compute step by step:

$$
(1.12)^{2} = 1.2544
$$

$$
(1.12)^{3} = 1.2544 \times 1.12 = 1.404928
$$

$$
(1.12)^{4} = 1.404928 \times 1.12 = 1.573519
$$

$$
(1.12)^{5} = 1.573519 \times 1.12 = 1.762342
$$

Now substitute:

$$
(P/A, 0.12, 5) = \dfrac{1.762342 - 1}{0.12 \times 1.762342} = \dfrac{0.762342}{0.211481} = 3.6048
$$

$$
(P/F, 0.12, 5) = \dfrac{1}{1.762342} = 0.5674
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute back.**

$$
NPW = -20{,}00{,}000 + 4{,}50{,}000 \times 3.6048 + 2{,}00{,}000 \times 0.5674
$$

**Step 5 — Arithmetic.**

$$
4{,}50{,}000 \times 3.6048 = 16{,}22{,}160
$$

$$
2{,}00{,}000 \times 0.5674 = 1{,}13{,}480
$$

$$
NPW = -20{,}00{,}000 + 16{,}22{,}160 + 1{,}13{,}480 = -2{,}64{,}360
$$

**Step 6 — Interpretation.**

$$
NPW = -\text{₹}2{,}64{,}360
$$

Since NPW is **negative**, the project does *not* meet the 12% MARR criterion and should be **rejected** (or its design parameters must be revised).

---

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — The "TVM Equation Family Tree"

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the **logical relationships** between all twelve KTU-mandated TVM equations. Each box is a factor, and the arrows show algebraic derivation paths.

```mermaid
graph TD
    rootTVM[TVM Core Engine]
    spcaf[Single Payment Compound Amount F over P equal to 1 plus i to the n]
    sppwf[Single Payment Present Worth P over F equal to 1 plus i to power minus n]
    sf[Annuity to Future F over A equal to bracketed term]
    crf[Capital Recovery A over P equal to bracketed term]
    spwf[Annuity to Present P over A equal to bracketed term]
    sf2[Sinking Fund A over F equal to bracketed term]
    effR[Effective Annual Rate ie equal to 1 plus r over m to the m minus 1]
    contComp[Continuous Compounding F equal to P times e to the i n]
    gradF[Gradient Future Worth]
    gradP[Gradient Present Worth]
    gradA[Gradient to Annuity]

    rootTVM --> spcaf
    rootTVM --> sppwf
    spcaf -.inverse.-> sppwf

    spcaf --> sf
    sf --> sf2
    sf2 --> crf
    crf --> spwf
    sf2 --> gradF
    spwf --> gradP
    crf --> gradA

    spcaf --> effR
    effR --> contComp
```

**How to read this diagram:**
- The two **core** factors (SPCAF and SPPWF) sit at the top.
- **Annuity** factors (SF, SF², CRF, SPWF) are derived from SPCAF + SPPWF.
- **Gradient** factors are derived from the annuity family.
- **Effective rate** and **continuous compounding** are special extensions.

## 4.2 Cash Flow Diagram — Schematic (Mermaid Block)

```mermaid
graph LR
    t0[Year 0: Present]
    t1[Year 1]
    t2[Year 2]
    t3[Year 3]
    tn[Year n: Future]
    P[Down Arrow P: Investment]
    A1[Up Arrow A: Year 1 Revenue]
    A2[Up Arrow A: Year 2 Revenue]
    A3[Up Arrow A: Year 3 Revenue]
    An[Up Arrow A plus S: Year n Revenue and Salvage]

    t0 --- P
    t0 --- t1
    t1 --- A1
    t1 --- t2
    t2 --- A2
    t2 --- t3
    t3 --- A3
    t3 --- tn
    tn --- An
```

**Interpretation:**
- A **single downward arrow at $t = 0$** represents the present capital investment $P$.
- **Equal upward arrows at $t = 1, 2, \ldots, n$** represent uniform annual revenue $A$.
- The **last arrow at $t = n$** is taller because it includes both the year's revenue and the salvage value $S$.

## 4.3 Decision Flowchart — When to Use Which Equation

```mermaid
graph TD
    start[Given Cash Flow Pattern]
    q1{Is there a single sum at one point in time?}
    q2{Is there a series of equal payments?}
    q3{Is the series increasing linearly each year?}
    q4{Is compounding continuous or multi period per year?}

    f1[Use SPCAF or SPPWF]
    f2[Use Annuity Family: SF SF2 CRF SPWF]
    f3[Use Gradient Family: GradF GradP GradA]
    f4[Use Effective Rate or Continuous Compounding]

    start --> q1
    q1 -- Yes --> f1
    q1 -- No --> q2
    q2 -- Yes --> f2
    q2 -- No --> q3
    q3 -- Yes --> f3
    q4 -- Yes --> f4
```

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip:** When confused, ask yourself: "Is this a *single* sum or a *series*?" That single question resolves 80% of equation-selection problems in KTU papers.

## 4.4 Comparative Architecture of Depreciation Methods

```mermaid
graph TD
    dep[Depreciation Methods]
    sl[Straight Line SL]
    db[Declining Balance DB]
    syd[Sum of Years Digits SYD]
    ddb[Double Declining Balance DDB]
    macrs[MACRS Tax Method]

    dep --> sl
    dep --> db
    dep --> syd
    db --> ddb
    dep --> macrs

    slDesc[Constant Dk each year]
    dbDesc[Geometric decay with rate d]
    sydDesc[Arithmetic series weights]
    ddbDesc[DB with d equal to 2 over N]
    macrsDesc[IRS prescribed percentages]

    sl --- slDesc
    db --- dbDesc
    syd --- sydDesc
    ddb --- ddbDesc
    macrs --- macrsDesc
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
*Define the term "Time Value of Money." Why is it important in the economic analysis of a chemical plant?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Time Value of Money (TVM)** is the economic principle that a sum of money received or paid at different points in time has different *present values* because money can earn interest over time. A rupee available today can be invested to earn a return, making it worth more than the same rupee received in the future.

**Importance in chemical plant analysis (2 marks):**
1. Capital investments in chemical plants are large (often crores of ₹) and are made upfront, while revenues accrue over the 15–25 year plant life. TVM allows fair comparison.
2. It enables calculation of profitability metrics like **NPV, IRR, and Annual Equivalent Cost**, which are essential for project approval.
3. It accounts for **inflation and opportunity cost** of capital, both critical in long-gestation chemical projects.

*(Examiner's note: Drawing a simple cash flow diagram earns full 3 marks.)*

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*Distinguish between Simple Interest and Compound Interest. Give the relevant equations.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Simple Interest | Compound Interest |
|:---|:---|:---|
| Interest calculated on | Principal only | Principal + accumulated interest |
| Growth | Linear in $n$ | Exponential in $n$ |
| Formula | $F = P(1 + i n)$ | $F = P(1+i)^{n}$ |
| Use case | Short-term loans (< 1 year) | Long-term loans, project finance |

For $P = \text{₹}10{,}00{,}000$, $i = 10\%$, $n = 3$ years:
- Simple: $F = 10{,}00{,}000(1 + 0.10 \times 3) = 13{,}00{,}000$
- Compound: $F = 10{,}00{,}000(1.10)^{3} = 13{,}31{,}000$

Compound interest yields ₹31,000 more over 3 years.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Recommended Choice**

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 1]**

A process plant requires an initial investment of **₹50 lakhs**. Annual revenues are **₹12 lakhs per year for 6 years**. Operating costs are **₹4 lakhs per year for the same 6 years**. The plant has a salvage value of **₹8 lakhs** at the end of year 6. The MARR is **15% per year**.

**(a)** Set up the cash flow diagram and write the equation for Net Present Worth (NPW). (7 marks)

**(b)** Compute the NPW and state whether the project is acceptable at 15% MARR. (7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution to Q-A(a):

**Step 1 — Identify net annual cash flow.**

Net annual cash inflow $A$ = Revenue − Operating cost = ₹12 L − ₹4 L = **₹8 lakhs/year**

**Step 2 — Cash flow diagram (text representation):**

| Year | Cash Flow |
|:---:|:---:|
| 0 | −₹50 L (investment) |
| 1 to 5 | +₹8 L each year |
| 6 | +₹8 L + ₹8 L (salvage) = +₹16 L |

**Step 3 — Set up the NPW equation.**

The NPW at year 0 is the sum of the present values of all inflows minus the present value of the investment.

For the uniform ₹8 L from year 1 to year 6, we use the **Uniform Series Present Worth Factor $(P/A, 0.15, 6)$**:

$$
P_{\text{uniform}} = A \cdot (P/A, i, n) = 8 \cdot \dfrac{(1+i)^{n} - 1}{i(1+i)^{n}}
$$

For the **extra ₹8 L salvage at year 6**, we use the **Single Payment Present Worth Factor $(P/F, 0.15, 6)$**:

$$
P_{\text{salvage}} = 8 \cdot (P/F, 0.15, 6) = 8 \cdot (1.15)^{-6}
$$

**Full NPW equation:**

$$
\boxed{NPW = -50 + 8 \cdot (P/A, 0.15, 6) + 8 \cdot (P/F, 0.15, 6)}
$$

where:

$$
(P/A, 0.15, 6) = \dfrac{(1.15)^{6} - 1}{0.15(1.15)^{6}}
$$

$$
(P/F, 0.15, 6) = \dfrac{1}{(1.15)^{6}}
$$

#### Model Solution to Q-A(b):

**Step 1 — Compute $(1.15)^{6}$ step by step.**

$$
(1.15)^{2} = 1.3225
$$

$$
(1.15)^{3} = 1.3225 \times 1.15 = 1.520875
$$

$$
(1.15)^{6} = \left[(1.15)^{3}\right]^{2} = (1.520875)^{2} = 2.31306
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the factors.**

$$
(P/A, 0.15, 6) = \dfrac{2.31306 - 1}{0.15 \times 2.31306} = \dfrac{1.31306}{0.34696} = 3.7845
$$

$$
(P/F, 0.15, 6) = \dfrac{1}{2.31306} = 0.4323
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute into the NPW equation.**

$$
NPW = -50 + 8 \times 3.7845 + 8 \times 0.4323
$$

**Step 4 — Arithmetic.**

$$
8 \times 3.7845 = 30.276
$$

$$
8 \times 0.4323 = 3.4584
$$

$$
NPW = -50 + 30.276 + 3.4584 = -16.27 \text{ lakhs}
$$

**Step 5 — Decision.**

$$
NPW = -\text{₹}16.27 \text{ lakhs} < 0
$$

Since the NPW is **negative**, the project **does NOT meet** the 15% MARR criterion.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Common Mistake #1:** Students often forget to *add* the salvage to the last year's revenue. The ₹8 L salvage must be added separately at $t = 6$ (or treated as a separate single payment).
> 2. **Common Mistake #2:** Using $(P/A)$ for a series that includes the salvage in the last term — this double-counts.
> 3. **Common Mistake #3:** Computing $(1.15)^{6}$ incorrectly. Always show the **stepwise multiplication** in the answer booklet to earn the 2 process marks.
> 4. **Sign Convention:** Investment at $t = 0$ must be **negative**. Forgetting the minus sign is a guaranteed 1-mark loss.
> 5. **Units:** Keep lakhs throughout; mixing ₹, lakhs, and crores is a typical KTU valuation penalty.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 1]**

A distillation column costs **₹30 lakhs** to install. It produces an annual savings of **₹5 lakhs per year** in operating cost. However, the maintenance cost increases by **₹50,000 every year** (arithmetic gradient $G$). The lifetime is **8 years** with no salvage value. MARR = **10% per year**.

**(a)** Draw the cash flow diagram showing investment, uniform savings, and gradient series. Derive the equation for NPW. (7 marks)

**(b)** Compute the NPW and determine acceptability. (7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution to Q-B(a):

**Step 1 — Identify the three cash flow components:**

| Component | Type | Symbol | Value |
|:---|:---|:---:|:---:|
| Initial investment | Single Payment at $t=0$ | $P$ | ₹30 L |
| Annual savings | Uniform annuity | $A$ | ₹5 L/year |
| Increasing maintenance | Arithmetic gradient | $G$ | ₹0.50 L/year |

**Step 2 — Cash flow diagram (text):**

- $t = 0$: Down arrow ₹30 L
- $t = 1$: Up arrow ₹5 L (savings)
- $t = 2$: Up arrow ₹5 L − ₹0.50 L (net) = ₹4.50 L
- $t = 3$: ₹5 L − ₹1.00 L = ₹4.00 L
- $\vdots$
- $t = 8$: ₹5 L − ₹3.50 L = ₹1.50 L

Equivalently, the net cash flow can be modelled as a **uniform series of ₹5 L** *minus* a **gradient series starting with $G$ at year 2**.

**Step 3 — NPW equation.**

The present worth of a gradient series is:

$$
P_{G} = G \left[ \dfrac{(1+i)^{n} - i n - 1}{i^{2}(1+i)^{n}} \right]
$$

So the full NPW is:

$$
\boxed{NPW = -30 + 5 \cdot (P/A, 0.10, 8) - 0.50 \cdot \dfrac{(1.10)^{8} - 0.10 \times 8 - 1}{(0.10)^{2}(1.10)^{8}}}
$$

#### Model Solution to Q-B(b):

**Step 1 — Compute $(1.10)^{8}$.**

$$
(1.10)^{2} = 1.21
$$

$$
(1.10)^{4} = (1.21)^{2} = 1.4641
$$

$$
(1.10)^{8} = (1.4641)^{2} = 2.14359
$$

**Step 2 — Compute $(P/A, 0.10, 8)$.**

$$
(P/A, 0.10, 8) = \dfrac{2.14359 - 1}{0.10 \times 2.14359} = \dfrac{1.14359}{0.214359} = 5.3349
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the gradient present worth factor.**

$$
P_{G\text{ factor}} = \dfrac{2.14359 - 0.80 - 1}{(0.01)(2.14359)} = \dfrac{0.34359}{0.0214359} = 16.0287
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute into NPW.**

$$
NPW = -30 + 5 \times 5.3349 - 0.50 \times 16.0287
$$

**Step 5 — Arithmetic.**

$$
5 \times 5.3349 = 26.6745
$$

$$
0.50 \times 16.0287 = 8.0144
$$

$$
NPW = -30 + 26.6745 - 8.0144 = -11.34 \text{ lakhs}
$$

**Step 6 — Decision.**

$$
NPW = -\text{₹}11.34 \text{ lakhs} < 0
$$

The project is **not acceptable** at 10% MARR.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout for Gradient Problems:**
> 1. **Gradient starts at $t = 2$, not $t = 1$.** The first year has *no* gradient increment. Many students apply $G$ at year 1, giving wrong signs.
> 2. **Subtract, don't add** gradient maintenance from uniform savings.
> 3. The gradient factor formula is **the most-messed-up** equation in the KTU module. Practice deriving it 3 times.
> 4. Always **state the formula symbolically first** before substituting values — this protects you if your final number is wrong.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Equations Used in Economic Analysis**

### Core Equations to Memorize
- ☐ **SPCAF:** $F = P(1+i)^{n}$ — the mother of all TVM equations
- ☐ **SPPWF:** $P = F(1+i)^{-n}$ — inverse of SPCAF
- ☐ **Annuity to F:** $F = A \cdot \dfrac{(1+i)^{n} - 1}{i}$
- ☐ **Sinking Fund:** $A = F \cdot \dfrac{i}{(1+i)^{n} - 1}$
- ☐ **Capital Recovery:** $A = P \cdot \dfrac{i(1+i)^{n}}{(1+i)^{n} - 1}$
- ☐ **Annuity to P:** $P = A \cdot \dfrac{(1+i)^{n} - 1}{i(1+i)^{n}}$
- ☐ **Effective Rate:** $i_{e} = \left(1 + \dfrac{r}{m}\right)^{m} - 1$
- ☐ **Continuous:** $F = P e^{i n}$

### Gradient Series (Frequently Mis-remembered)
- ☐ Gradient present worth: $P_{G} = G \left[ \dfrac{(1+i)^{n} - i n - 1}{i^{2}(1+i)^{n}} \right]$
- ☐ Gradient to annuity: $A = G \left[ \dfrac{1}{i} - \dfrac{n}{(1+i)^{n} - 1} \right]$

### Depreciation (Module 1 Add-on)
- ☐ Straight Line: $D_{k} = \dfrac{C_{0} - S}{N}$
- ☐ Declining Balance: $D_{k} = C_{0}(1-d)^{k-1} \cdot d$
- ☐ Double Declining Balance: $d = 2/N$

### KTU 2024 Exam Strategy Bullet Points
- ☐ **Always draw the cash flow diagram** before plugging numbers — it earns easy partial marks.
- ☐ **Show all intermediate steps** for power computations like $(1+i)^{n}$ — KTU examiners allocate 1–2 marks for *process*, not just the final answer.
- ☐ **State the equation symbolically** before substituting values.
- ☐ **Be consistent with units** (₹, lakhs, crores, years).
- ☐ **Highlight the decision rule** explicitly: "Since NPW > 0, the project is acceptable."
- ☐ **Sanity check** your answer: NPW of an obviously profitable project should be positive.

### Common Pitfalls (Examiner's Red Flags)
- ☐ Using **simple interest** instead of compound interest.
- ☐ Forgetting the **minus sign** on the initial investment.
- ☐ Misapplying the **gradient formula** (offset by one year).
- ☐ Confusing **$(P/A)$** and **$(A/P)$** — they are reciprocals, not the same.
- ☐ Using **nominal rate** directly without converting to effective rate.
- ☐ Mixing **end-of-period** and **beginning-of-period** annuity conventions.
- ☐ Ignoring **salvage value** in the last year's cash flow.

### Conceptual Anchors
- ☐ If a problem says "**at the end of each year**" → end-of-period convention → use standard formulas directly.
- ☐ If "**at the beginning of each year**" → multiply the annuity factor by $(1+i)$ to shift.
- ☐ If a problem involves **infinite horizon** → use **perpetuity**: $P = A/i$.
- ☐ If the question says "**find equivalent uniform annual cost**" → use **Capital Recovery Factor (A/P)**.

### Memorization Mnemonics
- ☐ **"PIG-FLA"** — **P**V, **I**nterest, **G**radient ↔ **F**V, **L**ump sum, **A**nnuity (the six main unknowns).
- ☐ **"SPCA → SPF → A"** — first SPCAF, then SPPWF, then derive Annuity family.
- ☐ **"Denominator for F→A has no $(1+i)^{n}$ on top"** vs **"P→A has it"** — the cleanest way to remember the difference.

### Final 30-Second Rule
Before submitting your answer booklet, verify:
1. ✅ Did I draw the cash flow diagram?
2. ✅ Are all signs correct (+ for inflow, − for outflow)?
3. ✅ Is the time convention consistent (end-of-year)?
4. ✅ Are units consistent (₹ vs lakhs vs crores)?
5. ✅ Did I explicitly state the decision (Accept / Reject)?

> **End of Module 1 Notes — Equations Used in Economic Analysis**

---

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT757 - ECONOMICS AND MANAGEMENT OF CHEMICAL INDUSTRIES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Compound Interest and Continuous Interest

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Compound Interest — Formal Definition

**Compound Interest** is the interest calculated on the **initial principal** *and* on the **accumulated interest** of previous periods. Unlike simple interest (which only pays interest on the original principal), compound interest is essentially *"interest on interest"* — making it the cornerstone of all modern financial analysis, capital budgeting, and engineering project evaluation.

Mathematically, if a principal amount $P$ is invested at a nominal annual interest rate $r$ for $n$ years compounded $m$ times per year, the accumulated amount $A$ is:

$$A = P \left(1 + \frac{r}{m}\right)^{mn}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Compound interest forms the mathematical foundation for evaluating the **time value of money (TVM)**, depreciation models, replacement analysis, and profitability indices in chemical plant investments.

## 1.2 Continuous Interest — Formal Definition

**Continuous Interest (or Continuous Compounding)** is the limiting case of compound interest where the compounding frequency $m \to \infty$ — i.e., interest is compounded *every infinitesimally small instant*. It is given by:

$$A = P \cdot e^{rt}$$

where $e \approx 2.71828$ is **Euler's number**, the base of the natural logarithm.

> [!NOTE]
> Continuous compounding is rarely used by commercial banks, but it is **mathematically the most accurate model** for situations like radioactive decay analogies, instantaneous reaction kinetics, and continuous-flow cash streams in process industries.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Snowball on a Hill"

Imagine you roll a small snowball down a snowy hill. As it rolls, the snowball itself picks up **more snow** (interest), making the snowball grow faster and faster.

- **Simple interest** = A snowball that picks up the *same* amount of snow per second, no matter how big it is.
- **Compound interest** = A snowball that picks up snow *proportional to its own size* — so the bigger it gets, the more snow it collects per second.
- **Continuous interest** = The snowball picks up snow *every single instant*, with no gaps in between.

In engineering economics, your "snowball" is **your money**, the "hill" is **time**, and the "snow" is **interest earned**.

## 1.4 Why This Matters in Chemical Engineering

In a chemical plant context, every rupee/dollar invested today in a reactor, distillation column, or heat exchanger will generate returns for **decades**. Engineers must accurately predict:

- The **future value** of capital equipment costs
- The **present value** of future revenue streams
- The **effective cost** of loans used to finance plant construction

All of these depend fundamentally on **compound interest mathematics**.

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Two Pillars of Interest

### A. Simple Interest (Foundation)
Interest is paid **only on the original principal**, never on accumulated interest.

$$I_{simple} = P \cdot r \cdot t$$

$$A = P(1 + rt)$$

where:
- $P$ = Principal (initial capital)
- $r$ = Annual interest rate (decimal form, e.g., 8% → 0.08)
- $t$ = Time in years
- $A$ = Accumulated amount (final value)

### B. Compound Interest (The Workhorse)
Interest earned in each period is **added to the principal** for the next period, creating exponential growth.

## 2.2 Logical Breakdown of Compounding

**Step 1 — Year 1:**
Principal grows to $P(1+r)$. Interest earned = $Pr$.

**Step 2 — Year 2:**
New principal is $P(1+r)$. It grows to $P(1+r)^2$. Interest earned = $P(1+r) \cdot r$.

**Step 3 — Year n:**
Growth becomes exponential: $A = P(1+r)^n$.

> [!NOTE]
> Notice how each year's interest is larger than the previous year's — this is the "snowball effect" of compounding.

## 2.3 Compounding Frequency — The Real-World Twist

Most financial instruments compound **more than once per year**:

| Compounding Type | Frequency ($m$) | Formula for Amount |
|---|---|---|
| Annual | $m = 1$ | $A = P(1+r)^n$ |
| Semi-annual | $m = 2$ | $A = P\left(1+\frac{r}{2}\right)^{2n}$ |
| Quarterly | $m = 4$ | $A = P\left(1+\frac{r}{4}\right)^{4n}$ |
| Monthly | $m = 12$ | $A = P\left(1+\frac{r}{12}\right)^{12n}$ |
| Daily | $m = 365$ | $A = P\left(1+\frac{r}{365}\right)^{365n}$ |
| **Continuous** | $m \to \infty$ | $A = P \cdot e^{rn}$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **higher the compounding frequency**, the **greater the effective return** for the same nominal rate. This is critical when comparing bank FD rates, bond yields, and corporate loan costs.

## 2.4 Nominal vs. Effective Interest Rate

### Nominal Rate ($r$)
The *stated* annual rate (e.g., "12% compounded monthly"). It does **not** reflect the true yearly return.

### Effective Annual Rate (EAR / $i_{eff}$)
The *actual* annual return after accounting for intra-year compounding:

$$i_{eff} = \left(1 + \frac{r}{m}\right)^m - 1$$

> [!WARNING]
> KTU Common Mistake: A nominal rate of **12% compounded monthly** is **not** 12% per year — it is actually **12.68% per year** effective. Many students lose marks by failing to convert.

## 2.5 Present Value — The Reverse Operation

While compound interest answers *"What will my money become in the future?"*, present value (PV) answers *"What is a future amount worth in today's money?"*:

$$PV = \frac{FV}{(1+i)^n} \quad \text{or} \quad PV = FV \cdot e^{-in} \text{ (continuous)}$$

This is the foundation of **discounted cash flow (DCF)** analysis used in chemical plant profitability studies.

## 2.6 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula | Variables / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Simple Interest Amount | $A = P(1+rt)$ | $r$ = decimal rate, $t$ = years |
| Compound Amount | $A = P(1+i)^n$ | $i$ = effective rate per period |
| Compound (m times/yr) | $A = P\left(1+\frac{r}{m}\right)^{mn}$ | $r$ = nominal annual rate |
| Continuous Compounding | $A = P \cdot e^{rn}$ | $e \approx 2.71828$ |
| Effective Annual Rate | $i_{eff} = \left(1+\frac{r}{m}\right)^m - 1$ | Always $\geq r$ |
| Continuous EAR | $i_{eff} = e^r - 1$ | Upper limit of compounding |
| Present Value (Discrete) | $PV = \dfrac{FV}{(1+i)^n}$ | Discounting |
| Present Value (Continuous) | $PV = FV \cdot e^{-rn}$ | Continuous discounting |
| Doubling Time (Rule of 72) | $t \approx \dfrac{72}{r\,\%}$ | Approximation, $r$ in % |
| Effective rate (single) | $(1+i) = \left(1+\frac{r}{m}\right)^m$ | Conversion identity |

## 2.7 Engineering Utility

| Field | Application |
|---|---|
| **Plant Capital Costing** | Estimating future equipment replacement cost |
| **NPV / IRR Analysis** | Discounting future cash flows |
| **Loan Amortization** | Determining EMI schedules for project finance |
| **Inflation Adjustment** | Real vs. nominal cost projections |
| **Depreciation** | Time-value adjustments in asset valuation |
