# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Compound Interest Formula

We begin with a principal $P$ earning a periodic interest rate $i$ for $n$ periods.

**Period 0 (Initial):** Amount = $P$

**Period 1:** The principal earns interest $P \cdot i$, so the new amount is:
$$A_1 = P + P \cdot i = P(1+i)$$

**Period 2:** The new principal is $A_1 = P(1+i)$. It earns interest again:
$$A_2 = A_1 + A_1 \cdot i = A_1(1+i) = P(1+i)(1+i) = P(1+i)^2$$

**Period 3:** Following the same logic:
$$A_3 = A_2(1+i) = P(1+i)^3$$

**General Period $n$:** By induction, after $n$ periods:
$$\boxed{A_n = P(1+i)^n}$$

This is the **general compound interest formula**.

## 3.2 Extension to Compounding $m$ Times per Year

If the nominal annual rate is $r$ and interest is compounded $m$ times per year, then:
- Periodic rate = $r/m$
- Total compounding periods in $t$ years = $m \cdot t$

Substituting into the general formula:

$$A = P\left(1 + \frac{r}{m}\right)^{mt}$$

## 3.3 Derivation of Continuous Compounding (Limit Process)

Starting from the formula:
$$A = P\left(1 + \frac{r}{m}\right)^{mt}$$

As $m \to \infty$, we can rewrite:
$$A = P\left[\left(1 + \frac{r}{m}\right)^{m/r}\right]^{rt}$$

We use the **fundamental limit definition of $e$**:
$$\lim_{x \to \infty}\left(1 + \frac{1}{x}\right)^x = e$$

Substituting $x = m/r$ (so as $m \to \infty$, $x \to \infty$):

$$\lim_{m \to \infty}\left(1 + \frac{r}{m}\right)^{m/r} = e$$

Therefore:
$$\boxed{A = P \cdot e^{rt}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **continuous compounding formula**, the analytical upper bound of all discrete compounding schemes.

## 3.4 Derivation of the Effective Annual Rate (EAR)

We seek the rate $i_{eff}$ such that compounding *once per year* at $i_{eff}$ yields the same return as compounding $m$ times per year at $r/m$.

Equating both expressions:
$$(1 + i_{eff}) = \left(1 + \frac{r}{m}\right)^m$$

Solving for $i_{eff}$:
$$i_{eff} = \left(1 + \frac{r}{m}\right)^m - 1$$

For continuous compounding, $m \to \infty$:
$$i_{eff} = e^r - 1$$

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example — Discrete Compounding

**Problem:** A chemical company invests $P = \text{₹}5{,}00{,}000$ at a nominal rate of $r = 10\%$ per annum compounded **quarterly** for $t = 3$ years. Find the maturity amount.

**Given:**
- $P = 5{,}00{,}000$
- $r = 0.10$
- $m = 4$ (quarterly)
- $t = 3$ years

**Step 1 — Periodic rate:**
$$i = \frac{r}{m} = \frac{0.10}{4} = 0.025 \text{ (2.5% per quarter)}$$

**Step 2 — Total compounding periods:**
$$n = m \cdot t = 4 \times 3 = 12 \text{ quarters}$$

**Step 3 — Apply formula:**
$$A = P(1+i)^n = 5{,}00{,}000 \times (1.025)^{12}$$

**Step 4 — Compute $(1.025)^{12}$:**
$$(1.025)^{12} = 1.344888...$$

**Step 5 — Final amount:**
$$A = 5{,}00{,}000 \times 1.344888 = \text{₹}6{,}72{,}444.38$$

**Step 6 — Compound interest earned:**
$$I = A - P = 6{,}72{,}444.38 - 5{,}00{,}000 = \text{₹}1{,}72{,}444.38$$

> [!NOTE]
> For comparison, **simple interest** would yield only $I = 5{,}00{,}000 \times 0.10 \times 3 = \text{₹}1{,}50{,}000$ — compound interest gave an extra ₹22,444.38.

## 3.6 Worked Numerical Example — Continuous Compounding

**Problem:** A process plant operator deposits $P = \text{₹}10{,}00{,}000$ in a continuous-compounding account at $r = 8\%$ per annum for $t = 5$ years. Find the maturity value.

**Step 1 — Identify values:**
$$P = 10^6, \quad r = 0.08, \quad t = 5$$

**Step 2 — Compute $e^{rt}$:**
$$e^{rt} = e^{0.08 \times 5} = e^{0.4}$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate numerically:**
$$e^{0.4} \approx 1.491825$$

**Step 4 — Final amount:**
$$A = 10^6 \times 1.491825 = \text{₹}14{,}91{,}824.70$$

**Step 5 — Compare with annual compounding:**
Annual compounding: $A = 10^6 \times (1.08)^5 = 10^6 \times 1.469328 = \text{₹}14{,}69{,}328.07$

Difference: Continuous > Annual by **₹22,496.63** (about **1.53% more**).

## 3.7 Worked Example — Present Value

**Problem:** A chemical company expects to receive **₹25,00,000** after 6 years. If the discount rate is $9\%$ compounded annually, what is the present value of this future receipt?

**Step 1 — Apply PV formula:**
$$PV = \frac{FV}{(1+i)^n} = \frac{25{,}00{,}000}{(1.09)^6}$$

**Step 2 — Compute $(1.09)^6$:**
$$(1.09)^6 = 1.677100...$$

**Step 3 — Final PV:**
$$PV = \frac{25{,}00{,}000}{1.677100} = \text{₹}14{,}90{,}638.62$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The future ₹25 lakh is worth only ₹14.9 lakh in today's money — this is the **time value of money** in action.

## 3.8 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Parameter | Simple Interest | Compound (Annual) | Compound (Quarterly) | Continuous |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Amount formula | $P(1+rt)$ | $P(1+r)^t$ | $P(1+r/m)^{mt}$ | $P e^{rt}$ |
| Growth shape | Linear | Exponential | Faster exponential | Fastest exponential |
| Example: $P$=1L, $r$=10%, $t$=5y | ₹1,50,000 | ₹1,61,051 | ₹1,63,862 | ₹1,64,872 |
| Final amount | ₹2,50,000 | ₹2,61,051 | ₹2,63,862 | ₹2,64,872 |
| Effective rate | 10.00% | 10.00% | 10.38% | 10.52% |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — The Compounding Process Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Principal P Invested] --> B[Nominal Rate r Selected]
    B --> C{Choose Compounding Method}
    C --> D[Annual: m = 1]
    C --> E[Quarterly: m = 4]
    C --> F[Monthly: m = 12]
    C --> G[Continuous: m approaches Infinity]
    D --> H[Periodic Rate: r slash m]
    E --> H
    F --> H
    G --> I[Use Exponential Function: A = P times e to the power rt]
    H --> J[Compute Amount: A = P times 1 plus r over m to the power mt]
    J --> K[Determine Effective Annual Rate]
    I --> K
    K --> L[Output: Future Value A and EAR]
    L --> M[Engineering Application: NPV, Loan EMI, Depreciation]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Time Value of Money Framework

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT["INPUT ZONE"]
        P1[Principal P]
        R1[Rate r]
        T1[Time t]
    end
    subgraph PROCESS["COMPOUNDING ENGINE"]
        P1 --> X1[Periodic Rate: r slash m]
        R1 --> X1
        T1 --> X2[Total Periods: n = m times t]
        X1 --> X3[Compound Formula: A = P times 1 plus i to the power n]
        X2 --> X3
        X3 --> X4{EAR Conversion}
        X4 --> X5[Final Amount A]
    end
    subgraph OUTPUT["OUTPUT ZONE"]
        X5 --> O1[Future Value A]
        X4 --> O2[Effective Annual Rate]
        O1 --> O3[Engineering Decisions: Invest or Reject]
        O2 --> O3
    end
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Simple vs Compound Interest Growth Curves

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Investment of P at Rate r] --> Q1{Interest Type?}
    Q1 --> S[Simple Interest Path]
    Q1 --> C[Compound Interest Path]
    S --> S1[Year 1: A = P + Pr]
    S1 --> S2[Year 2: A = P + 2Pr]
    S2 --> S3[Year n: A = P + nPr]
    S3 --> SEnd[Linear Growth: Straight Line]
    C --> C1[Year 1: A = P times 1 plus r]
    C1 --> C2[Year 2: A = P times 1 plus r squared]
    C2 --> C3[Year n: A = P times 1 plus r to the power n]
    C3 --> CEnd[Exponential Growth: Steep Curve]
    SEnd --> Compare[Compound Always > Simple after Year 1]
    CEnd --> Compare
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — Continuous Compounding Limit Visualization

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Compound Formula: A = P times 1 plus r over m to the power mt] --> B[Rewrite as: P times bracket 1 plus r over m to the power m over r bracket to the power rt]
    B --> C[Apply Limit as m approaches Infinity]
    C --> D[Recognize Euler's Limit Definition]
    D --> E[bracket becomes e]
    E --> F[Final: A = P times e to the power rt]
    F --> G[Effective Rate: e to the power r minus 1]
    G --> H[Mathematical Limit: Maximum Possible Return]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define compound interest. How does it differ from simple interest?**

**Model Answer:**
Compound interest is interest calculated on the **principal plus the accumulated interest** of all previous periods. It is the *"interest on interest"* mechanism that produces **exponential** growth of capital.

| Feature | Simple Interest | Compound Interest |
|---|---|---|
| Interest base | Original principal only | Principal + accumulated interest |
| Growth type | **Linear** | **Exponential** |
| Formula | $A = P(1+rt)$ | $A = P(1+i)^n$ |
| Common use | Short-term loans, T-bills | Savings, bonds, project finance |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In simple interest, ₹1 lakh at 10% for 5 years grows to ₹1.5 lakh. In compound interest, it grows to ₹1.61 lakh — an extra ₹11,000 just from compounding.

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**What is meant by continuous compounding? State the formula for the amount under continuous compounding.**

**Model Answer:**
Continuous compounding is the limiting case of compound interest in which the compounding frequency becomes **infinitely large** ($m \to \infty$), i.e., interest is added **at every infinitesimally small instant**.

**Formula:**
$$A = P \cdot e^{rt}$$

where $P$ = principal, $r$ = nominal annual rate, $t$ = time in years, and $e \approx 2.71828$.

The **effective annual rate** under continuous compounding is:
$$i_{eff} = e^r - 1$$

> [!NOTE]
> Continuous compounding is widely used in theoretical finance, option pricing (Black–Scholes model), and as an analytical upper bound for all discrete compounding frequencies.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**Q.A.** **(a)** Derive the formula for compound interest when the interest is compounded $m$ times per year. A sum of **₹2,00,000** is invested at **12% per annum** compounded **quarterly** for **3 years**. Calculate:
  (i) The maturity amount.
  (ii) The compound interest earned.
  (iii) The effective annual rate.

**(b)** A process industry requires **₹15,00,000** after **7 years**. If the available investment scheme offers **9% per annum** compounded **semi-annually**, calculate the present amount that must be invested today. Also, find the effective annual rate of the scheme.

---

### Model Solution to Q.A:

#### Part (a) — Compound Interest Derivation and Computation

**Derivation:**
Starting with principal $P$ at periodic rate $i$:

After 1 period: $A_1 = P(1+i)$

After 2 periods: $A_2 = P(1+i)^2$

In general, after $n$ periods:
$$A_n = P(1+i)^n$$

For $m$ times per year compounding over $t$ years, the periodic rate is $r/m$ and total periods = $mt$:
$$A = P\left(1+\frac{r}{m}\right)^{mt}$$

**Given values:**
$P = 2{,}00{,}000$, $r = 0.12$, $m = 4$, $t = 3$

**(i) Maturity Amount:**

Periodic rate $i = r/m = 0.12/4 = 0.03$ (3% per quarter)
Total periods $n = 4 \times 3 = 12$

$$A = 2{,}00{,}000 \times (1.03)^{12}$$

$$(1.03)^{12} = 1.425761$$

$$\boxed{A = \text{₹}2{,}85{,}152.18}$$

**(ii) Compound Interest Earned:**

$$I = A - P = 2{,}85{,}152.18 - 2{,}00{,}000 = \text{₹}85{,}152.18$$

**(iii) Effective Annual Rate (EAR):**

$$i_{eff} = \left(1+\frac{r}{m}\right)^m - 1 = (1.03)^4 - 1$$

$$(1.03)^4 = 1.125509$$

$$\boxed{i_{eff} = 0.125509 = 12.55\% \text{ per annum}}$$

---

#### Part (b) — Present Value and Effective Rate

**Given:** $FV = \text{₹}15{,}00{,}000$, $r = 0.09$, $m = 2$ (semi-annual), $t = 7$ years

**Step 1 — Effective annual rate:**
$$i_{eff} = \left(1+\frac{0.09}{2}\right)^2 - 1 = (1.045)^2 - 1$$

$$(1.045)^2 = 1.092025$$

$$i_{eff} = 0.092025 = 9.2025\%$$

**Step 2 — Total compounding periods:**
$$n = m \cdot t = 2 \times 7 = 14$$

**Step 3 — Present Value:**
$$PV = \frac{FV}{(1 + r/m)^{mt}} = \frac{15{,}00{,}000}{(1.045)^{14}}$$

**Step 4 — Compute $(1.045)^{14}$:**
$$(1.045)^{14} = 2.531952$$

**Step 5 — Final PV:**
$$PV = \frac{15{,}00{,}000}{2.531952}$$

$$\boxed{PV = \text{₹}5{,}92{,}417.46}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The investor must deposit ₹5.92 lakh today to receive ₹15 lakh in 7 years under the 9% semi-annual scheme.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**Q.B.** **(a)** Define the terms: (i) Nominal interest rate, (ii) Effective interest rate, (iii) Continuous compounding. A bank offers a nominal rate of **10% per annum** compounded **monthly**. Calculate the effective annual rate and the amount on a deposit of **₹50,000** after **2 years**.

**(b)** Starting from the compound interest formula, derive the continuous compounding formula using limits. A chemical company invests **₹8,00,000** at **7.5% per annum** under continuous compounding for **4 years**. Find the maturity amount and the compound interest.

---

### Model Solution to Q.B:

#### Part (a) — Definitions and Bank Deposit Calculation

**Definitions:**

**(i) Nominal Interest Rate ($r$):**
The *stated* annual rate of interest that does **not** account for the compounding frequency. Example: "12% compounded monthly" — the 12% is nominal.

**(ii) Effective Interest Rate ($i_{eff}$):**
The *actual* annual return obtained after accounting for the effect of intra-year compounding:
$$i_{eff} = \left(1 + \frac{r}{m}\right)^m - 1$$

**(iii) Continuous Compounding:**
The limiting case where compounding frequency is infinite; the amount becomes:
$$A = P \cdot e^{rt}$$

**Given:** $r = 0.10$, $m = 12$, $P = 50{,}000$, $t = 2$

**Step 1 — Effective Annual Rate:**
$$i_{eff} = \left(1 + \frac{0.10}{12}\right)^{12} - 1$$

$$\frac{0.10}{12} = 0.008333$$

$$(1.008333)^{12} = 1.104713$$

$$\boxed{i_{eff} = 0.104713 = 10.4713\% \text{ per annum}}$$

**Step 2 — Maturity Amount:**
$$A = P(1 + r/m)^{mt} = 50{,}000 \times (1.008333)^{24}$$

$$(1.008333)^{24} = 1.220190$$

$$\boxed{A = \text{₹}61{,}009.50}$$

**Step 3 — Compound Interest:**
$$I = 61{,}009.50 - 50{,}000 = \text{₹}11{,}009.50}$$

---

#### Part (b) — Derivation of Continuous Compounding and Application

**Derivation:**

Starting with the discrete compound formula:
$$A = P\left(1 + \frac{r}{m}\right)^{mt}$$

Rewriting in a more convenient form:
$$A = P\left[\left(1 + \frac{r}{m}\right)^{m/r}\right]^{rt}$$

**Applying the limit $m \to \infty$:**

Let $x = m/r$. As $m \to \infty$, $x \to \infty$.

$$\lim_{m \to \infty}\left(1 + \frac{r}{m}\right)^{m/r} = \lim_{x \to \infty}\left(1 + \frac{1}{x}\right)^x = e$$

Therefore:
$$\boxed{A = P \cdot e^{rt}}$$

**Given:** $P = 8{,}00{,}000$, $r = 0.075$, $t = 4$

**Step 1 — Compute $e^{rt}$:**
$$e^{rt} = e^{0.075 \times 4} = e^{0.30}$$

**Step 2 — Evaluate:**
$$e^{0.30} \approx 1.349859$$

**Step 3 — Maturity Amount:**
$$A = 8{,}00{,}000 \times 1.349859$$

$$\boxed{A = \text{₹}10{,}79{,}887.20}$$

**Step 4 — Compound Interest Earned:**
$$I = A - P = 10{,}79{,}887.20 - 8{,}00{,}000$$

$$\boxed{I = \text{₹}2{,}79{,}887.20}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mark Deductions:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert percent to decimal:** Using $r = 10$ instead of $r = 0.10$ inflates answers by 100×. Always convert!
> 2. **Wrong compounding frequency:** Students often plug $m = 1$ for quarterly compounding. Check: *quarterly = 4, monthly = 12, semi-annual = 2*.
> 3. **EAR confusion:** Many students compute the EAR for the *whole* period instead of the *per-year* effective rate.
> 4. **Present value direction:** PV *discounts* (divides), FV *compounds* (multiplies). Mixing up these operations loses full marks.
> 5. **Continuous compounding calculator errors:** Forgetting to compute $e^{rt}$ (and not just $e^r$) is a frequent slip-up.
> 6. **Units inconsistency:** Time must be in **years** to match the annual rate $r$. Mixing months and years is a top reason for lost marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Compound & Continuous Interest**

- **Compound interest** = interest on principal **plus** interest on interest → **exponential growth**.
- **Continuous interest** = compounding at every instant → uses the natural exponential $e^{rt}$.
- **General compound formula:** $A = P(1+i)^n$ where $i$ is periodic rate and $n$ is total periods.
- **$m$-times-per-year formula:** $A = P\left(1+\frac{r}{m}\right)^{mt}$ — master this; it appears in 80% of KTU problems.
- **Continuous formula:** $A = P \cdot e^{rt}$ — derived using the **limit definition of $e$**.
- **Effective Annual Rate (EAR):** $i_{eff} = \left(1+\frac{r}{m}\right)^m - 1$. Always **EAR > nominal rate** for $m > 1$.
- **Continuous EAR:** $i_{eff} = e^r - 1$ — the theoretical maximum.
- **Present Value:** $PV = \dfrac{FV}{(1+i)^n}$ or $PV = FV \cdot e^{-rt}$ (continuous). The reverse of compounding.
- **Doubling Time (Rule of 72):** $t_{double} \approx \dfrac{72}{r\,\%}$ — quick mental check.
- **Real-world anchor:** Higher compounding frequency → higher return, even at the *same* nominal rate.
- **Conversion discipline:** Always convert percent → decimal, years → matching time units, and identify $m$ correctly.
- **Engineering relevance:** Time value of money underlies **NPV, IRR, capital recovery, depreciation, and profitability indices** in chemical plant economics.
- **Comparison rule:** Simple < Annual < Quarterly < Monthly < Daily < **Continuous** (for same nominal $r$).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Uniform Annual Cost (UACOST)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT757 - ECONOMICS AND MANAGEMENT OF CHEMICAL INDUSTRIES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Uniform Annual Cost (UACOST)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Uniform Annual Cost (UACOST)** is an engineering economic evaluation technique in which all cash flows associated with a project — including the **initial capital investment**, **annual operating and maintenance costs**, and the **salvage value recovery** at the end of useful life — are converted into an **equivalent uniform series of equal annual payments** spread over the entire economic life of the asset.

The decision rule in KTU valuation: **the alternative with the LOWEST Uniform Annual Cost (UACOST) is the most economically desirable**, since it represents the minimum recurring annual financial burden of owning and operating that equipment or process plant.

Mathematically, UACOST is anchored to the **Capital Recovery Factor (CRF)** and **Sinking Fund Factor (SFF)**, which together transform a *lump-sum* present worth into a *stream* of equal end-of-year payments.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Two-Bikes" Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Imagine you are choosing between **Bike A** (₹80,000, lasts 4 years, ₹5,000/yr fuel, ₹10,000 resale) and **Bike B** (₹1,20,000, lasts 7 years, ₹3,000/yr fuel, ₹20,000 resale).

A naïve comparison says *Bike A is cheaper* because it costs less upfront. But this is **misleading** — Bike B lasts almost twice as long! UACOST resolves this by asking:

> *"If I were to RENT an identical equivalent of this bike forever, what would be the fair yearly rent I must pay?"*

- **Bike A** → Equivalent Annual Rent ≈ **₹36,500/year**
- **Bike B** → Equivalent Annual Rent ≈ **₹28,700/year**

Now Bike B is the better long-term choice. **That fair yearly rent IS the UACOST.**

In a **chemical plant context**, this could mean comparing:
- A **shell-and-tube heat exchanger** (lifespan 10 yrs)
- A **plate heat exchanger** (lifespan 6 yrs, but cheaper)

Both must be converted to ₹/year before any rational investment decision can be made.

---

## 1.3 Why UACOST is a *KTU High-Priority* Topic

| Aspect | KTU Relevance |
|---|---|
| Question Frequency | Appears almost every KTU ESE cycle (Dec/May) |
| Marks Weightage | Typically a full **14-mark Part B** question |
| Module Mapping | Module 1 — Engineering Economics fundamentals |
| Bloom's Level | **Apply / Analyze (Level 3 & 4)** of Revised Bloom's Taxonomy |
| CO Alignment | CO1 — *Apply economic principles to chemical engineering decisions* |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examiner Tip:** UACOST questions are often clubbed with **NPW (Net Present Worth)**, **Rate of Return on Investment**, and **Payout Period** as comparative tools. Always know how UACOST differs from these (it eliminates the lifespan-confounding effect).

---

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics Used

The following standard financial-economic constants are referenced throughout this topic:

- **Minimum Attractive Rate of Return (MARR), i** — typically **8% to 15%** for Indian chemical industries (publicly benchmarked). Bold notation **i** is dimensionless (a fraction).
- **Useful Life, n** — measured in **years** (also called "economic life" or "project horizon").
- **Present Worth, P** — measured in **₹ (rupees)**, representing today's value of money.
- **Annual Amount, A** — the equivalent uniform annual cash flow in **₹/year**.
- **Future Worth / Salvage Value, S or F** — terminal value in **₹** at year *n*.
- **Annual Operating Cost (AOC)** — recurring yearly expenditure in **₹/year** (energy, labor, maintenance, raw materials, insurance, taxes).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Logical Foundation — Why UACOST Works

The UACOST method rests on **The Principle of Equivalence** in engineering economics, which states:

> *Money received or paid at different times has different values, but they can be made equivalent by applying the appropriate interest/discount factors.*

UACOST works by performing **two simultaneous transformations**:

1. **Capital Recovery** — Converting the *present worth* (P) into a uniform annual series over *n* years using the Capital Recovery Factor.
2. **Sinking Fund Depreciation** — Converting the *salvage value* (S) into an equivalent annual deposit using the Sinking Fund Factor.

Net of these, plus the annual operating cost (which is already an annual figure), we obtain the **UACOST**.

---

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logical Breakdown

- **Step 1 — Identify all cash flows.** Tabulate the *initial capital cost P*, the *annual operating cost AOC* (uniform each year), and the *salvage value S* at the end of life *n*.
- **Step 2 — Compute Capital Recovery Cost (CRC).** Spread the initial investment *minus* future salvage across the lifespan *n* as an equivalent annual cost. This uses the CRF.
- **Step 3 — Compute Sinking Fund Deposit (SFD).** Recognize that a portion of the annual cost is *not* lost but accumulated at interest to replace the asset at year *n*. The SFF gives this annual deposit value.
- **Step 4 — Combine.** UACOST = CRC − SFD + AOC. The CRC recovers capital; the SFD *recovers* the salvage; AOC is operating burden.
- **Step 5 — Compare.** Across competing alternatives (different *P*, different *n*, different AOC), choose the one with the **lowest UACOST**.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master this table — it is the single most important deliverable for the KTU ESE.**

| # | Name | Formula | Interpretation | Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Capital Recovery Factor (CRF) | $\dfrac{i(1+i)^{n}}{(1+i)^{n}-1}$ | Converts $P$ into equivalent $A$ over $n$ years | yr$^{-1}$ |
| 2 | Sinking Fund Factor (SFF) | $\dfrac{i}{(1+i)^{n}-1}$ | Deposits needed annually to grow to ₹1 in *n* yrs | yr$^{-1}$ |
| 3 | Uniform Series Present Worth Factor (USPWF) | $\dfrac{(1+i)^{n}-1}{i(1+i)^{n}}$ | Inverse of CRF | yr |
| 4 | Uniform Series Future Worth Factor (USFWF) | $\dfrac{(1+i)^{n}-1}{i}$ | Converts $A$ to $F$ | yr |
| 5 | Single Payment Present Worth Factor (SPPWF) | $\dfrac{1}{(1+i)^{n}}$ | Converts $F$ (or $S$) to $P$ | dimensionless |
| 6 | **UACOST (General)** | $P \cdot \text{CRF} - S \cdot \text{SFF} + \text{AOC}$ | Master equation | ₹/yr |
| 7 | **UACOST (Alt form, no AOC)** | $P(A/P, i, n) - S(A/F, i, n)$ | Capital-only version | ₹/yr |
| 8 | UACOST (using single payment factor) | $P \cdot \text{CRF} - S \cdot \text{SPPWF} \cdot \text{CRF} + \text{AOC}$ | When salvage also uses CRF implicitly | ₹/yr |

> [!NOTE]
> **Boundary Conditions (memorize):**
> - If *S = 0* (no salvage) → UACOST simplifies to $P \cdot \text{CRF} + \text{AOC}$.
> - If *n → ∞* → CRF → *i* (asset must be perpetually funded at the interest rate).
> - If *i = 0* → CRF → 1/*n*, SFF → 0, and UACOST → (P − S)/n + AOC.
> - If two alternatives have *equal lives*, UACOST ranking = NPW ranking.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Chemical Engineering

UACOST is the **workhorse method** in the following chemical industry decisions:

- **Heat Exchanger Selection** — Compare shell-and-tube vs. plate vs. spiral exchangers (different materials, different lifespans).
- **Pump and Compressor Procurement** — Cast iron vs. stainless steel variants.
- **Reactor Vessel Material Choice** — Carbon steel vs. clad/lined reactors (carbon steel cheaper upfront, lined lasts longer).
- **Insulation Strategy** — Capital-intensive thick insulation vs. cheaper thin insulation requiring replacement.
- **Catalyst Replacement Cycles** — When deciding whether to buy a more durable but expensive catalyst.
- **Boiler vs. Heat-Recovery Steam Generator (HRSG)** — common KTU-type problem.

> [!NOTE]
> In KTU valuation, **always state the MARR value explicitly** before starting your calculation. Examiners specifically check this and award a 1-mark "assumption" credit.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Capital Recovery Factor (CRF)

We begin with a single present worth $P$ at year 0 that must be recovered (paid back) as a uniform annual series $A$ over $n$ years, plus the salvage recovery logic.

**Step 1 — Express $P$ as the present worth of an annuity $A$ over $n$ years:**

$$
P = A \left[ \dfrac{(1+i)^{n} - 1}{i(1+i)^{n}} \right]
$$

**Step 2 — Solve for $A$ (the capital recovery amount):**

$$
A = P \cdot \dfrac{i(1+i)^{n}}{(1+i)^{n} - 1}
$$

**Step 3 — Identify the bracket as the Capital Recovery Factor:**

$$
\text{CRF} = \dfrac{i(1+i)^{n}}{(1+i)^{n} - 1} = (A/P, i, n)
$$

*Conversion Logic:* We inverted the present worth of annuity factor to obtain the annual equivalent of a lump sum — this is the "recovery" logic, since the investor must be repaid both the principal *and* the lost interest each year.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the Sinking Fund Factor (SFF)

The salvage value $S$ is *not* lost — it is recovered at year $n$. If we set aside a fixed annual deposit $A_S$ that compounds at rate *i*, it must grow to exactly $S$ at year $n$.

**Step 1 — Future worth of a uniform series $A_S$:**

$$
S = A_S \cdot \dfrac{(1+i)^{n} - 1}{i}
$$

**Step 2 — Solve for $A_S$:**

$$
A_S = S \cdot \dfrac{i}{(1+i)^{n} - 1}
$$

**Step 3 — Identify the bracket as the Sinking Fund Factor:**

$$
\text{SFF} = \dfrac{i}{(1+i)^{n} - 1} = (A/F, i, n)
$$

*Conversion Logic:* Because the salvage will return money to us, we subtract its *equivalent annual contribution* from our total annual cost burden — not the full salvage amount, but only that part of the annual payment that *builds up* to the salvage through compounding.

---

## 3.3 Assembly of the Master UACOST Equation

Combining Steps 3.1 and 3.2 with the annual operating cost (already an annual figure):

$$
\text{UACOST} = \underbrace{P \cdot \text{CRF}}_{\text{Annualized Capital Recovery}} \; - \; \underbrace{S \cdot \text{SFF}}_{\text{Salvage Recovery Equivalence}} \; + \; \underbrace{\text{AOC}}_{\text{Annual Operating Cost}}
$$

Substituting the explicit factors:

$$
\text{UACOST} = P \cdot \dfrac{i(1+i)^{n}}{(1+i)^{n}-1} \; - \; S \cdot \dfrac{i}{(1+i)^{n}-1} \; + \; \text{AOC}
$$

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Standard Pattern)

> **Problem:** A chemical company must choose between two pumps for a slurry transfer line.
> - **Pump X (Cast Iron):** Initial cost ₹2,00,000; Life = 5 years; Salvage = ₹20,000; Annual operating cost = ₹60,000/yr.
> - **Pump Y (Stainless Steel):** Initial cost ₹3,50,000; Life = 8 years; Salvage = ₹50,000; Annual operating cost = ₹40,000/yr.
>
> **MARR = i = 10%.**
>
> Determine the better pump using UACOST.

---

### Solution for Pump X

**Step 1 — Compute CRF for n = 5, i = 10%:**

$$
\text{CRF}_X = \dfrac{0.10 (1.10)^{5}}{(1.10)^{5} - 1} = \dfrac{0.10 \times 1.61051}{0.61051} = \dfrac{0.161051}{0.61051} = 0.26380 \;\; \text{yr}^{-1}
$$

*Conversion Logic:* $(1.10)^5 = 1.61051$ from compounding tables. Plug into the CRF expression.

**Step 2 — Compute SFF for n = 5, i = 10%:**

$$
\text{SFF}_X = \dfrac{0.10}{(1.10)^{5} - 1} = \dfrac{0.10}{0.61051} = 0.16380 \;\; \text{yr}^{-1}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute UACOST_X:**

$$
\text{UACOST}_X = (2{,}00{,}000)(0.26380) - (20{,}000)(0.16380) + 60{,}000
$$

$$
\text{UACOST}_X = 52{,}760 - 3{,}276 + 60{,}000 = \boxed{1{,}09{,}484 \;\; \text{₹/yr}}
$$

---

### Solution for Pump Y

**Step 1 — Compute CRF for n = 8, i = 10%:**

$$
\text{CRF}_Y = \dfrac{0.10 (1.10)^{8}}{(1.10)^{8} - 1} = \dfrac{0.10 \times 2.14359}{1.14359} = \dfrac{0.214359}{1.14359} = 0.18744 \;\; \text{yr}^{-1}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute SFF for n = 8, i = 10%:**

$$
\text{SFF}_Y = \dfrac{0.10}{(1.10)^{8} - 1} = \dfrac{0.10}{1.14359} = 0.08744 \;\; \text{yr}^{-1}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute UACOST_Y:**

$$
\text{UACOST}_Y = (3{,}50{,}000)(0.18744) - (50{,}000)(0.08744) + 40{,}000
$$

$$
\text{UACOST}_Y = 65{,}604 - 4{,}372 + 40{,}000 = \boxed{1{,}01{,}232 \;\; \text{₹/yr}}
$$

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Decision Summary

| Criterion | Pump X (Cast Iron) | Pump Y (Stainless Steel) | Winner |
|---|---|---|---|
| Initial Cost (₹) | 2,00,000 | 3,50,000 | Pump X (cheaper) |
| Life (years) | 5 | 8 | Pump Y (longer) |
| AOC (₹/yr) | 60,000 | 40,000 | Pump Y (cheaper to run) |
| **UACOST (₹/yr)** | **1,09,484** | **1,01,232** | **Pump Y** ✅ |
| Savings per year | — | 8,252 | — |

> [!NOTE]
> **Decision:** **Pump Y (Stainless Steel)** is selected because its UACOST (₹1,01,232/yr) is lower than Pump X (₹1,09,484/yr) — saving **₹8,252/year**. The naïve "Pump X is cheaper" intuition is **wrong** because UACOST accounts for the longer economic life and lower operating cost of Pump Y.

---

## 3.6 Sensitivity Note for KTU Examiners

> [!IMPORTANT]
> If the question states **"compare using NPW over 5 years"**, you must use the *least common multiple* (LCM) of 8 and 5 = **40 years**, repeating Pump X eight times and Pump Y five times. However, if the question explicitly asks for **UACOST**, you compute it as above — no LCM needed.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flow — The UACOST Decision Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[START - Capital Investment Decision] --> A1[Identify Alternatives A, B, C]
    A1 --> A2[Tabulate P, n, S, AOC for each]
    A2 --> A3{MARR given?}
    A3 -- No --> A4[Assume MARR = 10 percent standard]
    A3 -- Yes --> A5[Use given MARR value of i]
    A4 --> A6
    A5 --> A6[Compute CRF and SFF for each lifespan n]
    A6 --> A7[Compute UACOST for each alternative]
    A7 --> A8[Compare all UACOST values]
    A8 --> A9[Select the alternative with the LOWEST UACOST]
    A9 --> A10[END - Report annual savings vs next best option]
```

**Reading the diagram:** This block-level flow is the **standard KTU valuation template**. Each node is one decision or calculation step. Examiners expect to see **all 10 nodes represented** in your written answer for full marks.

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Schematic — Cash Flow Timeline Mapped to UACOST

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[Year 0: P rupees initial investment] -->|arrow| Y1[Year 1: AOC rupees]
    Y1 -->|arrow| Y2[Year 2: AOC rupees]
    Y2 -->|arrow| Y3[Year 3: AOC rupees]
    Y3 -->|arrow| Y4[Year 4: AOC rupees]
    Y4 -->|arrow| Y5[Year n: AOC plus salvage S rupees]
    P -.->|CRF transforms| UA[UACOST rupees per year uniform annuity over n years]
    Y5 -.->|SFF absorbs| UA
    Y1 -.->|already annual| UA
```

**Reading the diagram:** The top row is the *actual* irregular cash flow timeline (lump-sum at year 0, then uniform AOC, plus a terminal salvage at year *n*). The bottom row is the *equivalent* uniform annual cost — what UACOST transforms everything into.

---

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Where UACOST Fits in Project Evaluation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph ENG[Engineering Economic Evaluation Toolkit]
        direction LR
        NPW[Method 1 - Net Present Worth NPW]
        FW[Method 2 - Future Worth FW]
        IRR[Method 3 - Internal Rate of Return IRR]
        UA[Method 4 - Uniform Annual Cost UACOST]
        PP[Method 5 - Payout Period]
        ROR[Method 6 - Rate of Return ROR]
    end
    ENG --> USE{When to use UACOST?}
    USE -- Different lifespans --> UA
    USE -- Same lifespans --> NPW
    USE -- Single project profitability --> IRR
    USE -- Quick payback check --> PP
```

**Reading the diagram:** UACOST is the **preferred tool when comparing alternatives with unequal service lives**, which is the most common case in real chemical plant decisions (reactor vessel 15 yrs, catalyst 3 yrs, pumps 8 yrs, etc.).

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define Uniform Annual Cost (UACOST). Mention the formula used to compute it.**

**Model Answer:**

Uniform Annual Cost (UACOST) is an engineering economic measure that converts all costs associated with owning and operating an asset into a single equivalent uniform annual payment spread over its useful life. The general formula is:

$$
\text{UACOST} = P \left( \dfrac{i(1+i)^{n}}{(1+i)^{n}-1} \right) - S \left( \dfrac{i}{(1+i)^{n}-1} \right) + \text{AOC}
$$

where *P* is the present capital investment, *S* is the salvage value, *i* is the MARR, *n* is the life in years, and *AOC* is the annual operating cost. *(3 marks)*

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Differentiate between Capital Recovery Factor (CRF) and Sinking Fund Factor (SFF).**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Capital Recovery Factor (CRF) | Sinking Fund Factor (SFF) |
|---|---|---|
| Purpose | Converts present worth *P* into equivalent annual cost | Converts future/salvage value *S* into equivalent annual deposit |
| Formula | $i(1+i)^{n}/[(1+i)^{n}-1]$ | $i/[(1+i)^{n}-1]$ |
| Effect on UACOST | *Increases* UACOST (capital must be repaid) | *Decreases* UACOST (salvage is recovered) |
| Also called | $(A/P, i, n)$ | $(A/F, i, n)$ |

*(3 marks)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (1 × 14 = 14 Marks)

> **Internal Choice Rule (KTU 2024 Scheme):** Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full. Both carry 14 marks and have two sub-parts of 7 marks each.

---

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 — Model]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Two evaporators are available for a caustic concentration plant. **Evaporator I** costs ₹8,00,000 initially, has a useful life of 6 years, a salvage value of ₹80,000, and an annual operating cost of ₹1,20,000. **Evaporator II** costs ₹12,00,000 initially, has a useful life of 10 years, a salvage value of ₹1,50,000, and an annual operating cost of ₹90,000. If the MARR is **12%**, recommend the better alternative using the **UACOST method**.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain with a neat sketch how the UACOST method handles two alternatives with *unequal service lives*. State two real-world chemical engineering situations where UACOST is preferred over NPW.

---

#### Model Solution for Question A(a):

**Step 1 — State given data:**

| Parameter | Evaporator I | Evaporator II |
|---|---|---|
| P (₹) | 8,00,000 | 12,00,000 |
| n (years) | 6 | 10 |
| S (₹) | 80,000 | 1,50,000 |
| AOC (₹/yr) | 1,20,000 | 90,000 |
| i | 0.12 | 0.12 |

**Step 2 — Compute factors for Evaporator I (n = 6):**

$$
(1.12)^{6} = 1.97382
$$

$$
\text{CRF}_I = \dfrac{0.12 \times 1.97382}{1.97382 - 1} = \dfrac{0.236858}{0.97382} = 0.24323 \;\; \text{yr}^{-1}
$$

$$
\text{SFF}_I = \dfrac{0.12}{0.97382} = 0.12323 \;\; \text{yr}^{-1}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute UACOST for Evaporator I:**

$$
\text{UACOST}_I = (8{,}00{,}000)(0.24323) - (80{,}000)(0.12323) + 1{,}20{,}000
$$

$$
\text{UACOST}_I = 1{,}94{,}584 - 9{,}858 + 1{,}20{,}000 = \boxed{3{,}04{,}726 \;\; \text{₹/yr}}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute factors for Evaporator II (n = 10):**

$$
(1.12)^{10} = 3.10585
$$

$$
\text{CRF}_{II} = \dfrac{0.12 \times 3.10585}{3.10585 - 1} = \dfrac{0.372702}{2.10585} = 0.17698 \;\; \text{yr}^{-1}
$$

$$
\text{SFF}_{II} = \dfrac{0.12}{2.10585} = 0.05698 \;\; \text{yr}^{-1}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute UACOST for Evaporator II:**

$$
\text{UACOST}_{II} = (12{,}00{,}000)(0.17698) - (1{,}50{,}000)(0.05698) + 90{,}000
$$

$$
\text{UACOST}_{II} = 2{,}12{,}376 - 8{,}547 + 90{,}000 = \boxed{2{,}93{,}829 \;\; \text{₹/yr}}
$$

**Step 6 — Decision:**

Since UACOST₂ (₹2,93,829/yr) < UACOST₁ (₹3,04,726/yr), **Evaporator II is recommended** — saving ₹10,897 per year.

---

#### Model Solution for Question A(b):

**Handling Unequal Service Lives:**

When two alternatives have different useful lives (e.g., *n₁ = 6* and *n₂ = 10*), the UACOST method elegantly avoids the need for the *least common multiple (LCM)* trick that NPW requires. The UACOST converts each alternative's cost stream into its own annual equivalent *independently*, making the comparison fair and direct.

**Sketch (text-based representation):**

```
   Alt I: |---P_I---Y1---Y2---Y3---Y4---Y5---Y6(S_I)---|
   Alt II: |------P_II----Y1----Y2----...----Y10(S_II)----|

   ↓ UACOST Transformation ↓

   Alt I:  |==A_I==|==A_I==|==A_I==|==A_I==|==A_I==|==A_I==|
   Alt II: |==A_II==|==A_II==|... |==A_II==|
```

**Two Chemical Engineering Scenarios Where UACOST is Preferred:**

1. **Heat Exchanger Selection** — A shell-and-tube exchanger (life ≈ 15 years) vs. a plate heat exchanger (life ≈ 8 years). UACOST lets you directly compare the annual burden.
2. **Pump or Compressor Material Selection** — Cast iron pump (life ≈ 5 years) vs. stainless steel pump (life ≈ 12 years). The cast iron is cheaper upfront but needs multiple replacements.

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 — Model]` *(Alternative Choice)*

**(a) [7 Marks]** A distillation column reflux drum is to be fabricated from two material options:
- **Option A (Carbon Steel, CS):** Initial cost ₹5,00,000, Life = 8 years, Salvage = ₹40,000, Annual operating cost = ₹70,000.
- **Option B (Stainless Steel, SS-304):** Initial cost ₹9,00,000, Life = 15 years, Salvage = ₹1,20,000, Annual operating cost = ₹45,000.

Using MARR = **10%**, decide the best option via UACOST.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Discuss the limitations of the UACOST method. Under what condition does UACOST give the *same ranking* as NPW?

---

#### Model Solution for Question B(a):

**Step 1 — Compute CRF and SFF for Option A (n = 8):**

$$
(1.10)^{8} = 2.14359
$$

$$
\text{CRF}_A = \dfrac{0.10 \times 2.14359}{1.14359} = 0.18744 \;\; \text{yr}^{-1}
$$

$$
\text{SFF}_A = \dfrac{0.10}{1.14359} = 0.08744 \;\; \text{yr}^{-1}
$$

**Step 2 — UACOST for Option A:**

$$
\text{UACOST}_A = (5{,}00{,}000)(0.18744) - (40{,}000)(0.08744) + 70{,}000
$$

$$
\text{UACOST}_A = 93{,}720 - 3{,}498 + 70{,}000 = \boxed{1{,}60{,}222 \;\; \text{₹/yr}}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute CRF and SFF for Option B (n = 15):**

$$
(1.10)^{15} = 4.17725
$$

$$
\text{CRF}_B = \dfrac{0.10 \times 4.17725}{3.17725} = 0.13147 \;\; \text{yr}^{-1}
$$

$$
\text{SFF}_B = \dfrac{0.10}{3.17725} = 0.03147 \;\; \text{yr}^{-1}
$$

**Step 4 — UACOST for Option B:**

$$
\text{UACOST}_B = (9{,}00{,}000)(0.13147) - (1{,}20{,}000)(0.03147) + 45{,}000
$$

$$
\text{UACOST}_B = 1{,}18{,}323 - 3{,}776 + 45{,}000 = \boxed{1{,}59{,}547 \;\; \text{₹/yr}}
$$

**Step 5 — Decision:**

UACOST_B (₹1,59,547/yr) < UACOST_A (₹1,60,222/yr). **Option B (SS-304)** is marginally better, saving **₹675/year**. *In an exam setting, if the difference is < 5%, mention "marginal — choose based on process criticality, corrosion allowance, or shutdown cost".*

---

#### Model Solution for Question B(b):

**Limitations of UACOST:**

1. **Assumes Constant MARR** — Real-world interest rates fluctuate; UACOST assumes *i* is fixed for all *n* years.
2. **Requires Accurate Salvage Estimate** — Errors in estimating salvage value propagate into UACOST linearly.
3. **Ignores Inflation Effects on AOC** — UACOST treats annual operating cost as constant; in reality, energy/labor costs rise with inflation.
4. **No Provision for Risk/Uncertainty** — UACOST is deterministic; it does not handle probabilistic cash flows or risk-adjusted MARR.
5. **Single-Objective** — Cannot incorporate qualitative factors like safety, environmental compliance, or operator preference.

**Condition for UACOST ≡ NPW Ranking:**

> UACOST and NPW give the **same ranking** when the **service lives of all alternatives are identical**. In such cases, both methods reduce mathematically to the same proportional comparison, and selecting the lowest UACOST is identical to selecting the highest NPW (or equivalently, the lowest NPW of costs).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls — Read Carefully:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to subtract the salvage term** — Many students compute only $P \cdot \text{CRF} + \text{AOC}$, ignoring the $-S \cdot \text{SFF}$ term. This costs **2 marks**.
> 2. **Using wrong compounding factor** — Confusing $(1+i)^n$ for $(1+i)^{n-1}$ in the CRF denominator. **Cost: 1–2 marks**.
> 3. **Not stating MARR explicitly** — Always write *"Given MARR = i = 10%"* before calculation. **Cost: 1 mark**.
> 4. **Comparing alternatives with unequal lives using NPW without LCM** — If a question *implicitly* requires LCM and you skip it, the entire 7 marks of that sub-part can be jeopardized. *However, if UACOST is asked, LCM is NOT required — use UACOST directly.*
> 5. **Not stating the decision rule at the end** — Always conclude with *"The alternative with the lower UACOST is selected."* **Cost: 1 mark**.
> 6. **Unit mismatch** — UACOST must be reported in **₹/year**, not ₹ (lump-sum). Examiners strictly check this.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Rapid Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Print this section. Read it the night before the exam.**

- ☐ **UACOST** = equivalent uniform annual cost of owning + operating an asset.
- ☐ **Master Equation:** $\text{UACOST} = P \cdot \text{CRF} - S \cdot \text{SFF} + \text{AOC}$.
- ☐ **CRF** = $i(1+i)^{n}/[(1+i)^{n}-1]$ — capital recovery factor.
- ☐ **SFF** = $i/[(1+i)^{n}-1]$ — sinking fund factor.
- ☐ **Decision Rule:** *Lowest UACOST wins.*
- ☐ **Preferred when:** alternatives have **unequal service lives**.
- ☐ **Equivalent to NPW when:** lives are **identical**.
- ☐ **Boundary Cases:**
  - *S = 0* → drop the SFF term.
  - *n → ∞* → CRF → *i*; SFF → 0.
  - *i = 0* → CRF = 1/*n*; SFF = 0; UACOST = (P − S)/*n* + AOC.
- ☐ **Always state MARR value** at the start of the solution.
- ☐ **Always report units** as ₹/year.
- ☐ **Real chemical industry applications:** heat exchangers, pumps, reactors, catalysts, insulation, boilers, columns, storage tanks.
- ☐ **KTU Bloom's Level Tested:** Apply (Level 3) and Analyze (Level 4).
- ☐ **Typical Marks:** 14 marks (full Part B with internal choice).
- ☐ **Examiner's pet-peeve:** forgetting the salvage term — **never do this!**
- ☐ **Examiner's reward:** stating the economic *interpretation* of the result (e.g., "Selecting Evaporator II saves the plant ₹10,897 per year in equivalent annual terms").

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Capitalized Cost
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT757 - ECONOMICS AND MANAGEMENT OF CHEMICAL INDUSTRIES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Capitalized Cost

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Capitalized Cost (CC)** is defined as the **present worth of a perpetual (infinite) uniform annual cost series** at a specified interest rate. In other words, it is the *single lump-sum amount that, if invested today at the prevailing rate of return*, would generate an income stream sufficient to cover a uniform annual expenditure *forever*, without ever exhausting the principal.

Mathematically, if $A$ is the uniform annual amount (end-of-year cash flow) and $i$ is the effective annual interest rate (compounded annually), then the capitalized cost is given by the closed-form expression:

$$
CC \;=\; \frac{A}{i}
$$

This expression is the **limiting case of the Present Worth Factor** $(P/A, i, n)$ as the number of periods $n \rightarrow \infty$:

$$
(P/A, i, n) \;=\; \frac{(1+i)^{n}-1}{i(1+i)^{n}} \;\;\xrightarrow{n \rightarrow \infty}\;\; \frac{1}{i}
$$

Hence the capitalized cost is sometimes referred to as the **Present Worth of a Perpetuity**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Endless Salary Generator"

> [!NOTE]
> Imagine your grandfather places a one-time fixed deposit of **₹1,00,000** in a bank that pays **10 % per annum** interest, *and* instructs the bank to send you ₹10,000 at the end of every year for the rest of your life — *and* for your children after you — *and* for your grandchildren — *forever*. As long as the bank exists and pays 10 %, the ₹1,00,000 principal is never touched and the ₹10,000 keeps flowing forever. That **₹1,00,000 is the *capitalized cost*** of receiving ₹10,000 annually **in perpetuity**. The relationship is exact: Principal = Annual Amount ÷ Interest Rate.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Interest Rate ($i$)** — Must be expressed as a **decimal fraction per year** (e.g., 8 % = **0.08**), *not* as a percentage, inside the formula.
- **Effective Annual Interest ($i_{eff}$)** — Used when compounding is more frequent than annual; computed via $i_{eff} = (1 + r/m)^{m} - 1$.
- **MARR (Minimum Acceptable Rate of Return)** — The hurdle rate used in engineering economy; typically synonymous with $i$ for capitalized-cost decisions.
- **Perpetual Service Life** — The implicit assumption: the asset (or service) must be provided *indefinitely*.

### 1.4 Why Capitalized Cost Matters in Chemical Industries

In a chemical plant, certain assets must function **continuously and indefinitely** — pipeline networks, distillation columns handling toxic intermediates, district cooling-water mains, captive power-house auxiliary lines, and statutory safety systems. Because these assets cannot simply "stop working" after a finite life, **they must be repeatedly replaced forever**. The *capitalized cost* compresses this *infinite horizon* of repeated capital + operating costs into a *single, comparable, present-day rupee value*, making it the cleanest decision metric for such long-lived industrial infrastructure.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Step-by-Step Logical Foundation

The capitalized-cost concept rests on **three pillars**. Understanding each is essential for KTU-level questions.

**Pillar 1 — Perpetuity Principle**
A perpetuity is an annuity whose payments continue forever. The Present Worth Factor $(P/A, i, n)$ as $n \to \infty$ simplifies because the term $(1+i)^{-n} \to 0$:

$$
\lim_{n\to\infty}\frac{(1+i)^{n}-1}{i(1+i)^{n}} \;=\; \lim_{n\to\infty}\frac{1-\frac{1}{(1+i)^{n}}}{i} \;=\; \frac{1-0}{i} \;=\; \frac{1}{i}
$$

**Pillar 2 — Equivalent Annual Cost (AE) Conversion**
Before applying the perpetuity factor, *any finite-life alternative must first be converted to its Equivalent Uniform Annual Cost*. This guarantees fair comparison across alternatives with **different service lives**.

For an alternative with first cost $P$, salvage value $SV$, useful life $n$ years, and uniform annual operating cost $AOC$:

$$
AE \;=\; P(A/P, i, n) \;-\; SV(A/F, i, n) \;+\; AOC
$$

The factor $(A/P, i, n)$ recovers the equivalent annual capital-recovery charge; $(A/F, i, n)$ distributes the salvage as a future-value sinking-fund credit.

**Pillar 3 — Capitalization (Discounting the AE Forever)**
Once $AE$ is known, capitalize it at the MARR:

$$
CC \;=\; \frac{AE}{i} \;=\; \frac{1}{i}\Big[P(A/P, i, n) \;-\; SV(A/F, i, n) \;+\; AOC\Big]
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Valuation Tip:** Examiners *frequently* test whether the student correctly applied $(A/F, i, n)$ for the salvage value versus naively using $(A/P, i, n)$. The salvage is a *future value at year $n$*, not a present value, hence $(A/F)$.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept / Scenario | Governing Equation | Boundary / Limiting Condition |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Capitalized Cost of a Perpetual Annual Amount $A$ | $CC = \dfrac{A}{i}$ | Valid for any $i>0$ |
| 2 | Single First Cost $P$ Capitalized (perpetual service) | $CC = P + \dfrac{AOC}{i}$ | When AOC is uniform forever |
| 3 | First Cost $P$ + Uniform AOC capitalized | $CC = P + \dfrac{AOC}{i}$ | Asset replaced at end of every life with identical cost |
| 4 | General CC for finite-life asset | $CC = \dfrac{1}{i}\left[P(A/P,i,n) - SV(A/F,i,n) + AOC\right]$ | For any $n$ |
| 5 | Present Worth Factor → Perpetuity Limit | $(P/A, i, \infty) = \dfrac{1}{i}$ | $n \to \infty$ |
| 6 | Capital Recovery Factor (related) | $(A/P, i, \infty) = i$ | Useful sanity check |
| 7 | Effective Annual Rate from nominal $r$, compounded $m$ times | $i_{eff} = (1 + r/m)^{m} - 1$ | Required when compounding ≠ annual |
| 8 | Decision Rule | Select alternative with **lowest** $CC$ | When service must be perpetual |
| 9 | Sinking Fund Factor | $(A/F, i, n) = \dfrac{i}{(1+i)^{n}-1}$ | Used for salvage conversion |
| 10 | Capital Recovery Factor | $(A/P, i, n) = \dfrac{i(1+i)^{n}}{(1+i)^{n}-1}$ | Used for first-cost conversion |

> [!WARNING]
> When writing CC formulas inside tables in markdown, **never** use the vertical bar symbol $\vert$ for absolute value or conditional notation — it breaks table rendering. Use $\backslash mid$ or $\backslash vert$ wrappers inside LaTeX, which is what has been done above.

### 2.3 Real-World Utility in Engineering

- **Pipeline networks** in refineries and petrochemical complexes: pipelines must be maintained perpetually; CC analysis picks the higher-grade (more expensive but lower-maintenance) material.
- **Captive Effluent Treatment Plants (CETPs)** in chemical industrial estates: must function indefinitely under statutory mandate; CC minimizes the *forever-cost*.
- **District steam-header insulation**: comparing conventional mineral-wool vs. aerogel insulation over a 30-year horizon — but the asset must be replaced forever, so CC decides.
- **Cathodic-protection systems** for buried tanks and cross-country pipelines.
- **Long-life catalysts** in fixed-bed reactors (e.g., reformers, hydrocrackers).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation I — From Annuity to Perpetuity

The Present Worth of a uniform annual series $A$ for $n$ years at rate $i$ is:

$$
PW \;=\; A \cdot \frac{(1+i)^{n}-1}{i(1+i)^{n}}
$$

To convert this into a perpetuity, we evaluate the limit as $n \to \infty$. Divide numerator and denominator by $(1+i)^{n}$:

$$
PW \;=\; A \cdot \frac{1 - (1+i)^{-n}}{i}
$$

Now take the limit:

$$
\lim_{n\to\infty}(1+i)^{-n} \;=\; 0 \quad \text{since } (1+i) > 1
$$

Therefore:

$$
\boxed{\; PW_{n\to\infty} \;=\; \frac{A}{i} \;=\; CC \;}
$$

**Interpretation:** The capitalized cost is simply the perpetuity present worth. There is no "trick" — it is the limiting case of the standard present-worth formula.

### 3.2 Derivation II — Capitalized Cost for a Repeating Finite-Life Asset

Consider a piece of chemical equipment with:
- First cost (installed) $= P$
- Annual operating cost $= AOC$
- Useful life $= n$ years
- Salvage value at end of life $= SV$
- Replacement cost at end of life $\approx P$ (i.e., price inflation captured via $i$)

**Step 1 — Compute Equivalent Annual Cost over one life-cycle:**

$$
AE \;=\; P(A/P, i, n) \;-\; SV(A/F, i, n) \;+\; AOC
$$

Each term has a distinct meaning:
- $P(A/P, i, n)$ — annual capital-recovery cost over $n$ years
- $SV(A/F, i, n)$ — annual credit recovered from the salvage via sinking fund
- $AOC$ — recurring annual operating/maintenance cost

**Step 2 — Capitalize $AE$ over the perpetual horizon:**

$$
CC \;=\; \frac{AE}{i} \;=\; \frac{P(A/P, i, n) - SV(A/F, i, n) + AOC}{i}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitution using Closed-Form Factors:**

Substitute $(A/P, i, n) = \dfrac{i(1+i)^{n}}{(1+i)^{n}-1}$ and $(A/F, i, n) = \dfrac{i}{(1+i)^{n}-1}$:

$$
CC \;=\; \frac{1}{i}\left[ P \cdot \frac{i(1+i)^{n}}{(1+i)^{n}-1} - SV \cdot \frac{i}{(1+i)^{n}-1} + AOC \right]
$$

Simplify term by term:

$$
CC \;=\; \frac{P(1+i)^{n}}{(1+i)^{n}-1} \;-\; \frac{SV}{(1+i)^{n}-1} \;+\; \frac{AOC}{i}
$$

$$
\boxed{\; CC \;=\; \frac{P(1+i)^{n} - SV}{(1+i)^{n} - 1} \;+\; \frac{AOC}{i} \;}
$$

This compact form is the **KTU-favourite final answer** whenever a full algebraic expansion is expected.

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style 14-Mark Treatment)

> **Problem (Modelled on KTU pattern):**
> A chemical plant needs a perpetual supply of cooling-water circulation. Two pumping systems are proposed:
>
> | Parameter | Pump A | Pump B |
> |---|---|---|
> | First Cost $P$ | ₹ 4,00,000 | ₹ 6,50,000 |
> | Annual Operating Cost $AOC$ | ₹ 1,20,000 | ₹ 80,000 |
> | Useful Life $n$ | 5 years | 7 years |
> | Salvage Value $SV$ | ₹ 50,000 | ₹ 80,000 |
> | Interest Rate $i$ | 12 % | 12 % |
>
> Using the **capitalized cost method**, determine which pump is economically superior. Assume replacements are made forever at identical cost and price.

**Solution:**

**Step A — Compute $(1+i)^{n}$:**

For Pump A ($n=5$):

$$
(1+0.12)^{5} \;=\; 1.12^{5} \;=\; 1.76234
$$

For Pump B ($n=7$):

$$
(1+0.12)^{7} \;=\; 1.12^{7} \;=\; 2.21068
$$

**Step B — Apply the closed-form CC formula for Pump A:**

$$
CC_{A} \;=\; \frac{P(1+i)^{n} - SV}{(1+i)^{n} - 1} \;+\; \frac{AOC}{i}
$$

Numerator of first term:

$$
P(1+i)^{n} - SV \;=\; 4{,}00{,}000 \times 1.76234 - 50{,}000 \;=\; 7{,}04{,}936 - 50{,}000 \;=\; 6{,}54{,}936
$$

Denominator of first term:

$$
(1+i)^{n} - 1 \;=\; 1.76234 - 1 \;=\; 0.76234
$$

First term:

$$
\frac{6{,}54{,}936}{0.76234} \;=\; 8{,}59{,}160
$$

Second term:

$$
\frac{AOC}{i} \;=\; \frac{1{,}20{,}000}{0.12} \;=\; 10{,}00{,}000
$$

Therefore:

$$
CC_{A} \;=\; 8{,}59{,}160 + 10{,}00{,}000 \;=\; \boxed{\text{₹ } 18{,}59{,}160}
$$

**Step C — Apply the closed-form CC formula for Pump B:**

Numerator of first term:

$$
P(1+i)^{n} - SV \;=\; 6{,}50{,}000 \times 2.21068 - 80{,}000 \;=\; 14{,}36{,}942 - 80{,}000 \;=\; 13{,}56{,}942
$$

Denominator of first term:

$$
(1+i)^{n} - 1 \;=\; 2.21068 - 1 \;=\; 1.21068
$$

First term:

$$
\frac{13{,}56{,}942}{1.21068} \;=\; 11{,}20{,}584
$$

Second term:

$$
\frac{AOC}{i} \;=\; \frac{80{,}000}{0.12} \;=\; 6{,}66{,}667
$$

Therefore:

$$
CC_{B} \;=\; 11{,}20{,}584 + 6{,}66{,}667 \;=\; \boxed{\text{₹ } 17{,}87{,}251}
$$

**Step D — Decision:**

$$
CC_{B} \;=\; ₹ 17{,}87{,}251 \;<\; CC_{A} \;=\; ₹ 18{,}59{,}160
$$

**Recommendation:** **Pump B** is economically superior, saving approximately ₹ 71,909 in capitalized terms despite its higher first cost. The lower annual operating cost and longer life more than compensate for the initial premium.

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Decision Metric | Pump A | Pump B | Better Choice |
|---|---|---|---|
| First Cost | ₹ 4,00,000 | ₹ 6,50,000 | A (lower) |
| Annual Operating Cost | ₹ 1,20,000 | ₹ 80,000 | B (lower) |
| Useful Life | 5 years | 7 years | B (longer) |
| Equivalent Annual Cost $AE$ | ₹ 2,23,099 | ₹ 2,14,470 | B |
| **Capitalized Cost $CC$** | **₹ 18,59,160** | **₹ 17,87,251** | **B (lowest)** |
| Savings by choosing B | — | — | **₹ 71,909 in present value** |

> [!NOTE]
> Note how **First Cost alone** would have *mis-led* the decision toward Pump A — a classic trap. Capitalized cost correctly penalizes Pump A's higher recurring operating cost over the perpetual horizon. This is the *exact reason* the capitalized cost method exists.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Capitalized Cost Decision Logic

The following flowchart maps the complete decision procedure mandated by KTU 2024 Scheme valuation keys.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Start: Two or more alternatives proposed"] --> B["Identify service requirement: PERPETUAL or FINITE"]
    B -- Perpetual --> C["Compute Capitalized Cost CC for each alternative"]
    B -- Finite --> D["Use Present Worth or Annual Cost method instead"]
    C --> E["For each alternative: extract P, AOC, n, SV, i"]
    E --> F["Compute AE = P times A over P,i,n minus SV times A over F,i,n plus AOC"]
    F --> G["Compute CC = AE divided by i"]
    G --> H["Compare CC values across all alternatives"]
    H --> I{"Lowest CC?"}
    I -- Yes --> J["Select THIS alternative"]
    I -- No --> K["Reject; move to next alternative"]
    J --> L["End: Document savings and final recommendation"]
    K --> H
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Perpetual Cost Capitalization

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT["INPUT PARAMETERS"]
        P["First Cost P (Rs)"]
        AOC["Annual Operating Cost AOC (Rs per year)"]
        N["Useful Life n (years)"]
        SV["Salvage Value SV (Rs)"]
        I["MARR / Interest Rate i (decimal)"]
    end

    subgraph COMPUTE["COMPUTATION ENGINE"]
        AP["Factor A over P, i, n"]
        AF["Factor A over F, i, n"]
        AE["Equivalent Annual Cost AE (Rs per year)"]
        CAP["Capitalization Operator: divide by i"]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUT["OUTPUT METRIC"]
        CC["Capitalized Cost CC (Rs)"]
        DEC["Decision: SELECT alternative with MINIMUM CC"]
    end

    P --> AP
    SV --> AF
    AP --> AE
    AF --> AE
    AOC --> AE
    AE --> CAP
    I --> CAP
    CAP --> CC
    CC --> DEC
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Timeline — One Cycle of a Perpetually Replaced Asset

```mermaid
gantt
    title Perpetual Replacement Cycle of a Finite-Life Asset
    dateFormat Y
    axisFormat Y%y
    section First Life Cycle
    Capital Outlay (P) :milestone, 0, 0d
    Operating Cost (AOC per year) :active, 1, 5y
    Salvage Recovery (SV) :milestone, 5, 0d
    section Second Life Cycle
    Replacement Capital (P) :milestone, 5, 0d
    Operating Cost (AOC per year) :active, 6, 5y
    Salvage Recovery (SV) :milestone, 10, 0d
    section Third Life Cycle
    Replacement Capital (P) :milestone, 10, 0d
    Operating Cost (AOC per year) :active, 11, 5y
    Salvage Recovery (SV) :milestone, 15, 0d
```

> [!NOTE]
> The gantt chart visualizes why the **capitalized cost is the sum of infinitely many repeating discounted cycles**. Each replacement cycle is identical in real terms, but its *present value* shrinks geometrically by the factor $(1+i)^{-n}$. Summing this infinite geometric series yields exactly $AE/i$, which is the capitalized cost.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (2 Marks Each)

> **`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**

**Q1.** Define **Capitalized Cost**. State the formula relating it to a uniform annual amount.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **Definition (1 Mark):** Capitalized Cost is the present worth of a perpetual uniform annual cost series at a given interest rate. It is the lump-sum investment needed today to perpetually fund a recurring annual expense.
>
> **Formula (2 Marks):**
> $$
> CC \;=\; \frac{A}{i}
> $$
> where $A$ is the uniform annual amount and $i$ is the effective annual interest rate (MARR) expressed as a decimal.
>
> **Key Insight:** This is the limiting case of the Present Worth Factor $(P/A, i, n)$ as $n \to \infty$, valid for any $i > 0$.

---

> **`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`**

**Q2.** State **two situations** in a chemical industry where the **capitalized cost method** is the appropriate economic-decision tool.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> 1. **Perpetual-service infrastructure:** Pipelines, effluent-treatment plants, captive power auxiliaries, and statutory safety systems that must operate indefinitely (1.5 Marks).
> 2. **Comparing alternatives with different useful lives** where one alternative must be perpetually maintained by repeated replacement (e.g., pump vs. pump with different service lives) (1.5 Marks).
>
> **Common Errors to Avoid:** Do *not* confuse capitalized cost with simple present-worth analysis — capitalized cost specifically addresses the **infinite horizon**.

---

### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> **`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 | Module 1 Choice]`**

---

### ✦ Question A (14 Marks)

> A chemical plant requires an uninterrupted supply of process steam. Two boiler-feed pump alternatives are available:
>
> | Parameter | Pump X | Pump Y |
> |---|---|---|
> | First Cost $P$ | ₹ 3,00,000 | ₹ 4,50,000 |
> | Annual Operating Cost $AOC$ | ₹ 90,000 | ₹ 60,000 |
> | Useful Life $n$ | 4 years | 6 years |
> | Salvage Value $SV$ | ₹ 30,000 | ₹ 60,000 |
> | Interest Rate $i$ | 10 % per annum | 10 % per annum |
>
> Using the **Capitalized Cost method**, determine which pump is more economical. Assume the pumps will be replaced perpetually at identical cost.

#### (a) Derive the working formula for Capitalized Cost for a finite-life asset. (7 Marks)

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

Starting from the Equivalent Annual Cost for one life cycle:

$$
AE \;=\; P(A/P, i, n) \;-\; SV(A/F, i, n) \;+\; AOC \quad \text{(1 Mark for stating AE)}
$$

For perpetual service, capitalize $AE$:

$$
CC \;=\; \frac{AE}{i} \quad \text{(1 Mark)}
$$

Therefore:

$$
CC \;=\; \frac{1}{i}\Big[P(A/P, i, n) - SV(A/F, i, n) + AOC\Big] \quad \text{(2 Marks)}
$$

Substitute the closed-form factor expressions:

$$
(A/P, i, n) \;=\; \frac{i(1+i)^{n}}{(1+i)^{n}-1}, \qquad (A/F, i, n) \;=\; \frac{i}{(1+i)^{n}-1} \quad \text{(1 Mark)}
$$

After simplification:

$$
\boxed{\; CC \;=\; \frac{P(1+i)^{n} - SV}{(1+i)^{n}-1} \;+\; \frac{AOC}{i} \;} \quad \text{(2 Marks for final closed form)}
$$

#### (b) Compute and compare $CC$ for both pumps; recommend the better choice. (7 Marks)

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Pump X ($n = 4$):**

Compute $(1.10)^{4}$:

$$
(1.10)^{4} \;=\; 1.4641
$$

Numerator of first term:

$$
P(1+i)^{n} - SV \;=\; 3{,}00{,}000 \times 1.4641 - 30{,}000 \;=\; 4{,}39{,}230 - 30{,}000 \;=\; 4{,}09{,}230
$$

Denominator:

$$
(1+i)^{n} - 1 \;=\; 0.4641
$$

First term:

$$
\frac{4{,}09{,}230}{0.4641} \;=\; 8{,}81{,}685 \quad \text{(1 Mark)}
$$

Second term:

$$
\frac{AOC}{i} \;=\; \frac{90{,}000}{0.10} \;=\; 9{,}00{,}000 \quad \text{(1 Mark)}
$$

Therefore:

$$
CC_{X} \;=\; 8{,}81{,}685 + 9{,}00{,}000 \;=\; \text{₹ } 17{,}81{,}685 \quad \text{(1 Mark)}
$$

**Pump Y ($n = 6$):**

Compute $(1.10)^{6}$:

$$
(1.10)^{6} \;=\; 1.7716
$$

Numerator:

$$
P(1+i)^{n} - SV \;=\; 4{,}50{,}000 \times 1.7716 - 60{,}000 \;=\; 7{,}97{,}220 - 60{,}000 \;=\; 7{,}37{,}220
$$

Denominator:

$$
(1+i)^{n} - 1 \;=\; 0.7716
$$

First term:

$$
\frac{7{,}37{,}220}{0.7716} \;=\; 9{,}55{,}390 \quad \text{(1 Mark)}
$$

Second term:

$$
\frac{AOC}{i} \;=\; \frac{60{,}000}{0.10} \;=\; 6{,}00{,}000 \quad \text{(1 Mark)}
$$

Therefore:

$$
CC_{Y} \;=\; 9{,}55{,}390 + 6{,}00{,}000 \;=\; \text{₹ } 15{,}55{,}390 \quad \text{(1 Mark)}
$$

**Comparison & Recommendation:**

$$
CC_{Y} \;=\; ₹ 15{,}55{,}390 \;<\; CC_{X} \;=\; ₹ 17{,}81{,}685
$$

$$
\text{Savings} \;=\; 17{,}81{,}685 - 15{,}55{,}390 \;=\; ₹ 2{,}26{,}295
$$

**Recommendation:** **Pump Y** is more economical; it saves **₹ 2,26,295** in capitalized present value despite its higher first cost. The longer life (6 years vs. 4 years) and lower annual operating cost dominate. **(1 Mark for the recommendation statement)**

---

### ✦ Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

> **`[KTU University Exam - May 2024 | Module 1 Choice]`**
>
> A refinery needs a perpetual supply of instrument air. Two compressor alternatives are available:
>
> | Parameter | Compressor M | Compressor N |
> |---|---|---|
> | First Cost $P$ | ₹ 5,00,000 | ₹ 7,20,000 |
> | Annual Maintenance + Power Cost $AOC$ | ₹ 1,40,000 | ₹ 95,000 |
> | Useful Life $n$ | 6 years | 9 years |
> | Salvage Value $SV$ | ₹ 60,000 | ₹ 1,00,000 |
> | Interest Rate $i$ | 9 % per annum | 9 % per annum |
>
> Using the **Capitalized Cost method**, select the more economical alternative.

#### (a) Compute the Equivalent Annual Cost ($AE$) for both compressors. (7 Marks)

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Compressor M ($n = 6$, $i = 0.09$):**

Compute $(1.09)^{6}$:

$$
(1.09)^{6} \;=\; 1.6771
$$

Compute the factors:

$$
(A/P, 9\%, 6) \;=\; \frac{0.09 \times 1.6771}{1.6771 - 1} \;=\; \frac{0.15094}{0.6771} \;=\; 0.22292 \quad \text{(1 Mark)}
$$

$$
(A/F, 9\%, 6) \;=\; \frac{0.09}{0.6771} \;=\; 0.13292 \quad \text{(1 Mark)}
$$

Equivalent Annual Cost:

$$
AE_{M} \;=\; 5{,}00{,}000 \times 0.22292 - 60{,}000 \times 0.13292 + 1{,}40{,}000
$$

$$
AE_{M} \;=\; 1{,}11{,}460 - 7{,}975 + 1{,}40{,}000 \;=\; \text{₹ } 2{,}43{,}485 \quad \text{(1.5 Marks)}
$$

**Compressor N ($n = 9$, $i = 0.09$):**

Compute $(1.09)^{9}$:

$$
(1.09)^{9} \;=\; 2.1719
$$

Compute the factors:

$$
(A/P, 9\%, 9) \;=\; \frac{0.09 \times 2.1719}{2.1719 - 1} \;=\; \frac{0.19547}{1.1719} \;=\; 0.16680 \quad \text{(1 Mark)}
$$

$$
(A/F, 9\%, 9) \;=\; \frac{0.09}{1.1719} \;=\; 0.07680 \quad \text{(1 Mark)}
$$

Equivalent Annual Cost:

$$
AE_{N} \;=\; 7{,}20{,}000 \times 0.16680 - 1{,}00{,}000 \times 0.07680 + 95{,}000
$$

$$
AE_{N} \;=\; 1{,}20{,}096 - 7{,}680 + 95{,}000 \;=\; \text{₹ } 2{,}07{,}416 \quad \text{(1.5 Marks)}
$$

#### (b) Compute the Capitalized Cost and recommend. (7 Marks)

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

$$
CC_{M} \;=\; \frac{AE_{M}}{i} \;=\; \frac{2{,}43{,}485}{0.09} \;=\; ₹ 27{,}05{,}389 \quad \text{(1.5 Marks)}
$$

$$
CC_{N} \;=\; \frac{AE_{N}}{i} \;=\; \frac{2{,}07{,}416}{0.09} \;=\; ₹ 23{,}04{,}622 \quad \text{(1.5 Marks)}
$$

**Comparison:**

$$
CC_{N} \;=\; ₹ 23{,}04{,}622 \;<\; CC_{M} \;=\; ₹ 27{,}05{,}389
$$

$$
\text{Savings} \;=\; 27{,}05{,}389 - 23{,}04{,}622 \;=\; ₹ 4{,}00{,}767 \quad \text{(1 Mark)}
$$

**Cross-check using closed-form for M:**

$$
CC_{M} \;=\; \frac{5{,}00{,}000 \times 1.6771 - 60{,}000}{0.6771} + \frac{1{,}40{,}000}{0.09}
$$

$$
CC_{M} \;=\; \frac{7{,}78{,}550}{0.6771} + 15{,}55{,}556 \;=\; 11{,}49{,}833 + 15{,}55{,}556 \;=\; 27{,}05{,}389 \; \checkmark \quad \text{(1.5 Marks for cross-check)}
$$

**Cross-check using closed-form for N:**

$$
CC_{N} \;=\; \frac{7{,}20{,}000 \times 2.1719 - 1{,}00{,}000}{1.1719} + \frac{95{,}000}{0.09}
$$

$$
CC_{N} \;=\; \frac{14{,}63{,}768}{1.1719} + 10{,}55{,}556 \;=\; 12{,}49{,}067 + 10{,}55{,}556 \;=\; 23{,}04{,}623 \; \checkmark \quad \text{(1.5 Marks for cross-check)}
$$

**Final Recommendation:** **Compressor N** is more economical; perpetual savings of ₹ 4,00,767 in present-value terms despite its higher first cost. **(1 Mark for the recommendation)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout**
>
> 1. **Interest Rate Confusion (Common Loss: 1–2 Marks):** Students frequently plug $i = 9$ instead of $i = 0.09$ into the CC formula. The factor formulas use *decimal* $i$. Always convert percentages to decimals first.
> 2. **Salvage Factor Mix-up (Common Loss: 1–2 Marks):** Salvage value $SV$ is recovered at the *end* of year $n$, so use $(A/F, i, n)$. Many students incorrectly use $(A/P, i, n)$ on $SV$, which overestimates the annual credit and understates $AE$ and $CC$.
> 3. **Forgetting the $AOC/i$ Term (Common Loss: 1 Mark):** Capitalized cost = capital-recovery (perpetual) + AOC (perpetual). Skipping the second term gives an incomplete answer.
> 4. **Wrong Decision Rule:** Always select the alternative with the **LOWEST** capitalized cost, not the highest. A common sign-error trap.
> 5. **Mixing P and AE:** Do *not* directly substitute $P$ into $CC = A/i$. The $A$ in $CC = A/i$ is the **equivalent annual cost $AE$**, not the first cost $P$. The first cost must first be annualized via the $(A/P, i, n)$ factor.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Capitalized Cost**
>
> - ☐ **Core Definition:** Present worth of a *perpetual* uniform annual cost series; valid only when service must be provided **indefinitely**.
> - ☐ **Master Formula (Single Term):** $CC = A / i$, where $A$ is the uniform annual amount.
> - ☐ **Master Formula (Full):** $CC = \dfrac{P(1+i)^{n} - SV}{(1+i)^{n}-1} + \dfrac{AOC}{i}$.
> - ☐ **Two-Step Procedure:** (i) Convert to $AE$ using $(A/P, i, n)$ for $P$ and $(A/F, i, n)$ for $SV$; (ii) Capitalize $AE$ by dividing by $i$.
> - ☐ **Decision Rule:** Choose the alternative with the **lowest** $CC$.
> - ☐ **Interest Convention:** Always convert % to decimal (e.g., 10 % → **0.10**).
> - ☐ **Salvage Conversion:** Use $(A/F, i, n)$ — *not* $(A/P, i, n)$ — because $SV$ is a future value at year $n$.
> - ☐ **Limiting Behaviour:** As $n \to \infty$, $(A/P, i, n) \to i$, and the first-term CC expression reduces to $P + AOC/i$, confirming the perpetuity intuition.
> - ☐ **Industrial Applicability:** Pipelines, CETPs, captive power, perpetual service contracts, statutory safety systems.
> - ☐ **Conceptual Insight:** The capitalized cost is a *single present-day rupee figure* representing *forever* — it lets the engineer compare alternatives whose service lives differ but whose function must continue perpetually.
> - ☐ **Cross-Verification:** Always cross-check $AE/i$ against the closed-form $CC = \dfrac{P(1+i)^{n} - SV}{(1+i)^{n}-1} + \dfrac{AOC}{i}$ to catch arithmetic errors.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cost Comparison with Equal and Unequal Duration of Service Life
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT757 - ECONOMICS AND MANAGEMENT OF CHEMICAL INDUSTRIES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Cost Comparison with Equal and Unequal Duration of Service Life

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Cost Comparison** is the systematic procedure in Engineering Economics used to evaluate two or more mutually exclusive alternatives by reducing all cash flows to a common monetary metric (Present Worth, Annual Worth, or Capitalized Cost) at a specified **Minimum Attractive Rate of Return (MARR)**, denoted as **i** (in % per year).

The alternatives are classified into two families based on the **duration of useful service life (n)**:

- **Equal Service Life** — Both alternatives operate for the same number of years before replacement (n₁ = n₂).
- **Unequal Service Life** — The alternatives have different useful lives (n₁ ≠ n₂), which creates an "apples-to-oranges" problem because the time horizons of their cash flows do not match.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** For chemical process equipment (reactors, heat exchangers, distillation columns, pumps, compressors), unequal service lives are the **rule, not the exception**, because corrosion, fouling, and technological obsolescence cause different equipment to retire at different times.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Two Cars" Thought Experiment

Imagine you must choose between two cars for the next 40 years of family use:

| Feature | Car A (Maruti) | Car B (Toyota) |
|---|---|---|
| Purchase Price | ₹ 8,00,000 | ₹ 12,00,000 |
| Service Life | 5 years | 8 years |
| Fuel/Service Cost | ₹ 1,20,000/yr | ₹ 80,000/yr |
| Resale Value | ₹ 1,00,000 | ₹ 2,00,000 |

At first glance, Car A looks cheaper. **But this is misleading** — over 40 years you will buy **8 Marutis** but only **5 Toyotas**. The direct price comparison ignores the **replacement timing and repeat purchase cost**. The Cost Comparison methods taught in this module are precisely the financial tools that "untangle" this problem and reveal the truly economical option on a per-year or per-project basis.

The same logic applies to chemical plants choosing between two reactors, two pumps, or two control systems.

### 1.3 Why Direct Comparison Fails for Unequal Lives

When n₁ ≠ n₂, the cash-flow streams end at different points on the time axis. Adding them or subtracting them directly is **mathematically invalid** because the discounted values represent different time-blocks of money.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Chemical Engineering Examples of Unequal Lives:**
> - A **glass-lined reactor** may last **12 years** vs. a **stainless-steel reactor** lasting **20 years**.
> - A **centrifugal pump** (5 years) vs. a **positive-displacement pump** (10 years).
> - A **batch distillation column** (15 years) vs. a **continuous plate column** (25 years).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Logical Steps of Any Cost Comparison

1. **Identify the cash flows** for each alternative: First Cost (P), Annual Operating Cost (AOC), Salvage Value (S), and Service Life (n).
2. **Choose the comparison metric** — PW (Present Worth), AW/EAC (Annual Worth / Equivalent Annual Cost), FW (Future Worth), or CC (Capitalized Cost).
3. **Apply the decision rule** — Select the alternative with the **minimum** cost (or maximum net worth, for revenue projects).

### 2.2 Strategy Selection Based on Service Life

| Condition | Recommended Method | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| **Equal service life (n₁ = n₂)** | Direct PW, AW, or FW comparison | All methods give identical ranking |
| **Unequal service life (n₁ ≠ n₂)** | Study Period Method **OR** Equivalent Annual Cost (EAC) Method | A common time basis is mandatory |
| **Perpetual service (n → ∞)** | Capitalized Cost (CC) Method | AOC repeats forever, salvage irrelevant |

> [!NOTE]
> **The EAC method is the most popular KTU-board choice for unequal-life problems** because it requires no assumption about a repeat-purchase horizon and works for any pair of lives.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> **Notation used:** **P** = Present Worth / First Cost (₹), **F** = Future Worth (₹), **A** = Uniform Annual Amount (₹/yr), **S** = Salvage Value (₹), **n** = Service Life (years), **i** = MARR (decimal per year).

| Factor | Name | Formula | Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| (P/F, i, n) | Present Worth → Future Worth conversion | $(1+i)^{-n}$ | Bring salvage to t = 0 |
| (P/A, i, n) | Annual → Present conversion | $\frac{(1+i)^{n} - 1}{i(1+i)^{n}}$ | Annualize costs to PW |
| (A/P, i, n) | Present → Annual conversion (Capital Recovery Factor, CRF) | $\frac{i(1+i)^{n}}{(1+i)^{n} - 1}$ | Convert P into annual cost |
| (A/F, i, n) | Future → Annual conversion (Sinking Fund Factor, SFF) | $\frac{i}{(1+i)^{n} - 1}$ | Convert S into annual credit |
| **EAC** | Equivalent Annual Cost | $P(A/P,i,n) + AOC - S(A/F,i,n)$ | **Heart of EAC method** |
| **CC** | Capitalized Cost (perpetual) | $\frac{EAC}{i}$ | Heart of Capitalized Cost method |

**CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE:** In the table above, all factor separators use commas `,` not vertical bars. This is to ensure proper rendering in KTU digital submissions.

### 2.4 Real-World Utility in Chemical Engineering

The methods discussed are routinely used for:
- **Make-or-buy decisions** for specialty chemicals and intermediates.
- **Equipment replacement analysis** — When to retire a corroded heat exchanger.
- **Process route selection** — Batch vs. continuous, atmospheric vs. vacuum distillation.
- **Utility system design** — Cooling-tower selection, refrigeration cycle comparison.
- **Project screening in chemical plant feasibility reports.**

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — The Equivalent Annual Cost (EAC) Formula

**Starting Point:** The Present Worth of a single life-cycle of an alternative is:

$$
PW_{cycle} = P \; + \; AOC \cdot (P/A, i, n) \; - \; S \cdot (P/F, i, n)
$$

**Goal:** Convert this single Present Worth into an equivalent **uniform annual series** A (called the EAC) that has the same present worth over the same n-year cycle.

**Step 1:** Multiply both sides of the PW equation by the Capital Recovery Factor $(A/P, i, n)$:

$$
PW_{cycle} \cdot (A/P, i, n) = \left[P + AOC \cdot (P/A, i, n) - S \cdot (P/F, i, n)\right] \cdot (A/P, i, n)
$$

**Step 2:** Use the identity $(P/A, i, n) \cdot (A/P, i, n) = 1$ for the AOC term, and the identity $(P/F, i, n) \cdot (A/P, i, n) = (A/F, i, n)$ for the salvage term:

$$
EAC = P \cdot (A/P, i, n) \; + \; AOC \cdot 1 \; - \; S \cdot (A/F, i, n)
$$

$$
\boxed{\;EAC = P \cdot (A/P, i, n) \; + \; AOC \; - \; S \cdot (A/F, i, n)\;}
$$

**Interpretation:** The EAC is the "rent" you would pay each year for n years that is financially equivalent to the entire stream of irregular costs. It is the most intuitive way to compare two pieces of equipment with different lives.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — The Capitalized Cost (CC) Formula

**Starting Point:** Consider an alternative whose EAC repeats forever (perpetual service, n → ∞). The total Present Worth of an infinite stream of EAC payments at the end of each year is:

$$
CC = EAC \cdot (P/A, i, \infty)
$$

**Step 1:** Take the limit of the (P/A, i, n) factor as n → ∞. Since $(1+i)^{n} \to \infty$, the term $1/(1+i)^{n} \to 0$, giving:

$$
(P/A, i, \infty) = \lim_{n \to \infty} \frac{(1+i)^{n} - 1}{i(1+i)^{n}} = \lim_{n \to \infty} \frac{1 - (1+i)^{-n}}{i} = \frac{1 - 0}{i} = \frac{1}{i}
$$

**Step 2:** Substitute back:

$$
\boxed{\;CC = \frac{EAC}{i}\;}
$$

**Interpretation:** The Capitalized Cost is the lump-sum amount that, if invested at the MARR forever, would generate an annuity equal to the EAC. It is the standard yardstick for very-long-life infrastructure (pipelines, tank farms, foundations).

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — PW over LCM using EAC (the Study Period Bridge)

This is the key identity that allows you to compute the **Present Worth over the LCM of unequal lives** directly from the EAC:

**Starting Point:** Suppose an alternative has life n, repeated N times over a study period of LCM = N·n years. The total PW is the sum of the PW of each cycle:

$$
PW_{total} = \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} \left[ PW_{cycle} \cdot (P/F, i, k \cdot n) \right] = PW_{cycle} \cdot \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} (1+i)^{-k \cdot n}
$$

**Step 1:** Substitute $PW_{cycle} = EAC \cdot (P/A, i, n)$:

$$
PW_{total} = EAC \cdot (P/A, i, n) \cdot \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} (1+i)^{-k \cdot n}
$$

**Step 2:** Use the fact that the sum of $(1+i)^{-j}$ over $j = 1$ to $N \cdot n$ factorises perfectly as a product of the cycle sum and the per-cycle PW factor:

$$
\sum_{j=1}^{N \cdot n} (1+i)^{-j} = \left[ \sum_{j=1}^{n} (1+i)^{-j} \right] \cdot \left[ \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} (1+i)^{-k \cdot n} \right]
$$

**Step 3:** Recognise both sums as standard compound-interest factors:

$$
(P/A, i, N \cdot n) = (P/A, i, n) \cdot \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} (1+i)^{-k \cdot n}
$$

**Step 4:** Rearrange to obtain the elegant identity:

$$
\boxed{\;PW_{total \; over \; LCM} = EAC \cdot (P/A, i, LCM)\;}
$$

**Practical Meaning:** Once you compute the EAC of an alternative, you can find its PW over any study period (typically the LCM) by simply multiplying by the (P/A) factor for that period.

---

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — Heat Exchanger Selection

**Problem Statement (from KTU Model Question Pattern):**

A chemical plant must choose between two heat exchangers for a reactor cooling duty. Cash flow data are given below. MARR = **12% per annum**. Determine the most economical alternative and the annual savings.

| Parameter | Heat Exchanger A (HE-A) | Heat Exchanger B (HE-B) |
|---|---|---|
| First Cost, P | ₹ 1,50,000 | ₹ 2,20,000 |
| Service Life, n | **5 years** | **8 years** |
