| Annual Operating Cost, AOC | ₹ 25,000 / year | ₹ 15,000 / year |
| Salvage Value, S | ₹ 20,000 | ₹ 30,000 |

> The lives are **unequal** (5 ≠ 8), so direct PW comparison is invalid. We will use **both the EAC method and the Study Period method (LCM)** and cross-check.

---

### 3.5 Sub-Problem (a) — Solution by Equivalent Annual Cost (EAC) Method

**Step 1: Compute the required compound-interest factors at i = 12%**

For n = 5 years, $(1.12)^{5} = 1.76234$:

$$
(A/P, 12\%, 5) = \frac{0.12 \times 1.76234}{1.76234 - 1} = \frac{0.21148}{0.76234} = 0.27741
$$

$$
(A/F, 12\%, 5) = \frac{0.12}{1.76234 - 1} = \frac{0.12}{0.76234} = 0.15741
$$

For n = 8 years, $(1.12)^{8} = 2.47596$:

$$
(A/P, 12\%, 8) = \frac{0.12 \times 2.47596}{2.47596 - 1} = \frac{0.29712}{1.47596} = 0.20130
$$

$$
(A/F, 12\%, 8) = \frac{0.12}{2.47596 - 1} = \frac{0.12}{1.47596} = 0.08130
$$

**Step 2: Compute EAC of Heat Exchanger A**

$$
EAC_{A} = P_{A} \cdot (A/P, 12\%, 5) + AOC_{A} - S_{A} \cdot (A/F, 12\%, 5)
$$

$$
EAC_{A} = (1{,}50{,}000 \times 0.27741) + 25{,}000 - (20{,}000 \times 0.15741)
$$

$$
EAC_{A} = 41{,}611.5 + 25{,}000 - 3{,}148.2
$$

$$
\boxed{\;EAC_{A} = \text{₹ } 63{,}463 \; \text{per year}\;}
$$

**Step 3: Compute EAC of Heat Exchanger B**

$$
EAC_{B} = P_{B} \cdot (A/P, 12\%, 8) + AOC_{B} - S_{B} \cdot (A/F, 12\%, 8)
$$

$$
EAC_{B} = (2{,}20{,}000 \times 0.20130) + 15{,}000 - (30{,}000 \times 0.08130)
$$

$$
EAC_{B} = 44{,}286.0 + 15{,}000 - 2{,}439.0
$$

$$
\boxed{\;EAC_{B} = \text{₹ } 56{,}847 \; \text{per year}\;}
$$

**Step 4: Decision and Savings**

Since $EAC_{B} < EAC_{A}$, Heat Exchanger **B is more economical**. The annual savings are:

$$
\text{Annual Savings} = 63{,}463 - 56{,}847 = \text{₹ } 6{,}616 \; \text{per year}
$$

---

### 3.6 Sub-Problem (b) — Verification by Study Period Method (LCM)

**Step 1: Identify the Least Common Multiple of the service lives**

The lives are 5 years and 8 years.

$$
LCM(5, 8) = 40 \; \text{years}
$$

Within the 40-year study period, HE-A is replaced 7 times (cycles at years 0, 5, 10, 15, 20, 25, 30, 35) and HE-B is replaced 4 times (cycles at years 0, 8, 16, 24, 32).

**Step 2: Compute the (P/A, 12%, 40) factor**

$(1.12)^{40} = 93.0510$

$$
(P/A, 12\%, 40) = \frac{(1.12)^{40} - 1}{0.12 \times (1.12)^{40}} = \frac{92.0510}{11.1661} = 8.2438
$$

**Step 3: Use the identity from Derivation 3**

For HE-A (EAC already computed in Sub-Problem a):

$$
PW_{A} = EAC_{A} \times (P/A, 12\%, 40) = 63{,}463 \times 8.2438
$$

$$
PW_{A} = \text{₹ } 5{,}23{,}158
$$

For HE-B:

$$
PW_{B} = EAC_{B} \times (P/A, 12\%, 40) = 56{,}847 \times 8.2438
$$

$$
PW_{B} = \text{₹ } 4{,}68{,}650
$$

**Step 4: Decision and Verification**

Since $PW_{B} < PW_{A}$, Heat Exchanger **B is more economical** over the 40-year study period, in **complete agreement** with the EAC method.

Net Present Worth Savings over 40 years:

$$
PW_{A} - PW_{B} = 5{,}23{,}158 - 4{,}68{,}650 = \text{₹ } 54{,}508
$$

**Cross-check via annual savings:** $6{,}616 \times 8.2438 \approx 54{,}541$ (matches within rounding). ✓

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Both Methods Agree:** This is not coincidence. The identity $PW_{total} = EAC \times (P/A, i, LCM)$ guarantees that the EAC ranking and the LCM-based PW ranking are **mathematically identical** for any study period that is a common multiple of the individual lives.

---

### 3.7 Analytical Matrix — Method Selection Cheat Sheet

| Situation | Lives | Method to Use | Decision Rule |
|---|---|---|---|
| Two pumps, both 6-year life | Equal | Direct PW comparison | Choose lower PW |
| Reactor vs. heat exchanger, n = 12 vs. 20 | Unequal | EAC method | Choose lower EAC |
| Project horizon fixed at 10 years by management | Unequal | Study Period Method (forced horizon) | Choose lower PW over 10 years |
| Pipeline or tank farm (perpetual use) | Perpetual | Capitalized Cost | Choose lower CC = EAC / i |
| Lives are 3 and 4 | Unequal | EAC method (LCM = 12, also valid) | Choose lower EAC |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Decision Flowchart for Cost Comparison Method Selection

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Cost Comparison Problem] --> B{Service Lives Equal or Unequal}
    B -->|Equal| C[Direct PW or AW Method]
    B -->|Unequal| D{Choose Comparison Method}
    
    C --> E[Select Minimum Cost Alternative]
    
    D -->|Study Period| F[Find LCM of Service Lives]
    F --> G[Compute Total PW over LCM]
    G --> E
    
    D -->|Equivalent Annual Cost| H[Calculate EAC of Each Alternative]
    H --> I[Select Alternative with Minimum EAC]
    I --> E
    
    D -->|Capitalized Cost| J[Compute CC as EAC divided by i]
    J --> K[Select Alternative with Minimum CC]
    K --> E
```

### 4.2 Cash-Flow Timeline Architecture (Block-Level Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph T0_TO_T5["Cycle 1: Years 0 to 5"]
        A1[Initial Cost P at Year 0] --> A2[Annual Operating Cost AOC every year]
        A2 --> A3[Salvage Value S at Year 5]
    end
    
    subgraph T5_TO_T10["Cycle 2: Years 5 to 10"]
        B1[New Initial Cost P at Year 5] --> B2[Annual Operating Cost AOC every year]
        B2 --> B3[Salvage Value S at Year 10]
    end
    
    subgraph T10_TO_T15["Cycle 3: Years 10 to 15"]
        C1[New Initial Cost P at Year 10] --> C2[Annual Operating Cost AOC every year]
        C2 --> C3[Salvage Value S at Year 15]
    end
    
    A3 --> B1
    B3 --> C1
    
    D[Total PW over LCM Years] -.-> T0_TO_T5
    D -.-> T5_TO_T10
    D -.-> T10_TO_T15
```

### 4.3 Comparison Logic Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    INPUT[Inputs: P AOC S n and i] --> EACF[EAC Formula Engine]
    EACF --> CALC1[Compute CRF and SFF for Alt 1]
    EACF --> CALC2[Compute CRF and SFF for Alt 2]
    CALC1 --> EAC1[EAC value of Alternative 1]
    CALC2 --> EAC2[EAC value of Alternative 2]
    EAC1 --> COMP{Compare EAC values}
    EAC2 --> COMP
    COMP -->|EAC A less than EAC B| RES1[Choose Alternative A]
    COMP -->|EAC B less than EAC A| RES2[Choose Alternative B]
    RES1 --> OUT[Final Decision and Annual Savings]
    RES2 --> OUT
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading the diagrams:** The Mermaid flowcharts intentionally avoid markdown formatting inside the node labels to ensure clean rendering on the KTU digital learning portal. The block diagrams trace how individual cash-flow cycles are stacked to form a multi-cycle (LCM) timeline, which is the conceptual heart of the Study Period Method.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]**
*"Distinguish between cost comparison with equal service life and unequal service life of alternatives. Why can't the Present Worth method be applied directly in the unequal-life case?"*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Equal service life (1 mark):** When two alternatives have the same useful life (n₁ = n₂), their cash flows end at the same point on the time axis. Any of the three primary methods — PW, AW, or FW — will give **identical rankings**, so direct comparison using the PW method is valid.
- **Unequal service life (1 mark):** When n₁ ≠ n₂, the cash-flow streams terminate at different times. The shorter-lived alternative may be silently "renewed" in reality, and a single PW computation does not capture this repetition.
- **Why PW fails directly (1 mark):** A direct PW comparison adds cash flows occurring at **non-coincident points in time** as if they were equivalent. The PV of an alternative with a 5-year life is over 40 years is **not** the same as the PV over 5 years. A common time basis (LCM) or the EAC conversion is mathematically required to avoid an apples-to-oranges comparison.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**
*"Define the Equivalent Annual Cost (EAC) method. Write its formula and state the decision rule for selecting the better alternative."*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Definition (1 mark):** The EAC method converts all the irregular cash flows (First Cost, AOC, Salvage) of a single life-cycle of an alternative into a **single uniform annual cost** that has the same present worth over the cycle. It is the most widely used method for **unequal service-life comparisons**.
- **Formula (1.5 marks):**

$$
EAC = P \cdot (A/P, i, n) + AOC - S \cdot (A/F, i, n)
$$

- **Decision Rule (0.5 marks):** Select the alternative with the **lower EAC**. The difference in EAC values represents the equivalent annual savings (or cost penalty).

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long-Answer Question (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

> **Note:** As per the KTU 2024 scheme, Module 1 carries 14 marks with internal choice. You are required to answer **either** Question A **or** Question B.

---

#### ⭐ QUESTION A (14 Marks)

**A chemical plant must select one of two centrifugal pumps for a solvent-transfer service. The data are tabulated below. MARR = 10% per annum.**

| Parameter | Pump X | Pump Y |
|---|---|---|
| First Cost, P | ₹ 80,000 | ₹ 1,20,000 |
| Service Life, n | **6 years** | **9 years** |
| Annual Operating Cost, AOC | ₹ 18,000 / year | ₹ 12,000 / year |
| Salvage Value, S | ₹ 10,000 | ₹ 15,000 |

**(a)** Using the **Equivalent Annual Cost (EAC) method**, determine which pump is more economical and compute the annual savings. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Verify your decision using the **Study Period Method** with the LCM of the service lives. **(7 marks)**

---

##### 📝 Model Solution — Question A

### Part (a) — Solution by EAC Method (7 marks)

**Step 1: Compute compound-interest factors at i = 10%**

For n = 6 years, $(1.10)^{6} = 1.77156$:

$$
(A/P, 10\%, 6) = \frac{0.10 \times 1.77156}{1.77156 - 1} = \frac{0.17716}{0.77156} = 0.22961
$$

$$
(A/F, 10\%, 6) = \frac{0.10}{0.77156} = 0.12961
$$

For n = 9 years, $(1.10)^{9} = 2.35795$:

$$
(A/P, 10\%, 9) = \frac{0.10 \times 2.35795}{2.35795 - 1} = \frac{0.23580}{1.35795} = 0.17364
$$

$$
(A/F, 10\%, 9) = \frac{0.10}{1.35795} = 0.07364
$$

**Step 2: Compute EAC of Pump X** (1.5 marks)

$$
EAC_{X} = (80{,}000 \times 0.22961) + 18{,}000 - (10{,}000 \times 0.12961)
$$

$$
EAC_{X} = 18{,}368.8 + 18{,}000 - 1{,}296.1 = \text{₹ } 35{,}073 \; \text{per year}
$$

**Step 3: Compute EAC of Pump Y** (1.5 marks)

$$
EAC_{Y} = (1{,}20{,}000 \times 0.17364) + 12{,}000 - (15{,}000 \times 0.07364)
$$

$$
EAC_{Y} = 20{,}836.8 + 12{,}000 - 1{,}104.6 = \text{₹ } 31{,}732 \; \text{per year}
$$

**Step 4: Decision** (1 mark)

Since $EAC_{Y} \;(₹ \;31{,}732) < EAC_{X} \;(₹ \;35{,}073)$, **Pump Y is more economical**.

Annual Savings = $35{,}073 - 31{,}732 =$ **₹ 3,341 per year** (0.5 marks for the numerical savings)

---

### Part (b) — Solution by Study Period Method (7 marks)

**Step 1: Identify the LCM of service lives** (0.5 marks)

$$
LCM(6, 9) = 18 \; \text{years}
$$

- Pump X is replaced 2 times within 18 years (cycles at years 0, 6, 12) — 3 installations.
- Pump Y is replaced 1 time within 18 years (cycles at years 0, 9) — 2 installations.

**Step 2: Compute $(P/A, 10\%, 18)$** (1 mark)

$(1.10)^{18} = 5.55992$

$$
(P/A, 10\%, 18) = \frac{5.55992 - 1}{0.10 \times 5.55992} = \frac{4.55992}{0.55599} = 8.2014
$$

**Step 3: Apply the identity $PW_{LCM} = EAC \times (P/A, i, LCM)$** (3 marks)

For Pump X:

$$
PW_{X} = 35{,}073 \times 8.2014 = \text{₹ } 2{,}87{,}647
$$

For Pump Y:

$$
PW_{Y} = 31{,}732 \times 8.2014 = \text{₹ } 2{,}60{,}245
$$

**Step 4: Decision and verification** (1.5 marks)

Since $PW_{Y} < PW_{X}$, **Pump Y is more economical**, in **complete agreement** with the EAC method.

Net Present Worth Savings over 18 years = $2{,}87{,}647 - 2{,}60{,}245 =$ **₹ 27,402**.

**Cross-check:** $3{,}341 \times 8.2014 \approx 27{,}401$ (matches within rounding) ✓

---

#### ⭐ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE

**A process plant must choose between two distillation columns. MARR = 15% per annum.**

| Parameter | Column P | Column Q |
|---|---|---|
| First Cost, P | ₹ 5,00,000 | ₹ 7,50,000 |
| Service Life, n | **10 years** | **15 years** |
| Annual Operating Cost, AOC | ₹ 80,000 / year | ₹ 50,000 / year |
| Salvage Value, S | ₹ 50,000 | ₹ 80,000 |

**(a)** Using the **Capitalized Cost (CC) method**, determine which column is more economical. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Confirm the decision using the **EAC method** and report the annual savings. **(7 marks)**

---

##### 📝 Model Solution — Question B

### Part (a) — Solution by Capitalized Cost Method (7 marks)

**Step 1: Compute compound-interest factors at i = 15%**

For n = 10 years, $(1.15)^{10} = 4.04556$:

$$
(A/P, 15\%, 10) = \frac{0.15 \times 4.04556}{4.04556 - 1} = \frac{0.60683}{3.04556} = 0.19925
$$

$$
(A/F, 15\%, 10) = \frac{0.15}{3.04556} = 0.04925
$$

For n = 15 years, $(1.15)^{15} = 8.13706$:

$$
(A/P, 15\%, 15) = \frac{0.15 \times 8.13706}{8.13706 - 1} = \frac{1.22056}{7.13706} = 0.17102
$$

$$
(A/F, 15\%, 15) = \frac{0.15}{7.13706} = 0.02102
$$

**Step 2: Compute EAC of each column** (3 marks)

$$
EAC_{P} = (5{,}00{,}000 \times 0.19925) + 80{,}000 - (50{,}000 \times 0.04925) = 99{,}625 + 80{,}000 - 2{,}462.5 = \text{₹ } 1{,}77{,}163
$$

$$
EAC_{Q} = (7{,}50{,}000 \times 0.17102) + 50{,}000 - (80{,}000 \times 0.02102) = 1{,}28{,}265 + 50{,}000 - 1{,}681.6 = \text{₹ } 1{,}76{,}583
$$

**Step 3: Compute Capitalized Cost** (2 marks)

$$
CC_{P} = \frac{EAC_{P}}{i} = \frac{1{,}77{,}163}{0.15} = \text{₹ } 11{,}81{,}087
$$

$$
CC_{Q} = \frac{EAC_{Q}}{i} = \frac{1{,}76{,}583}{0.15} = \text{₹ } 11{,}77{,}220
$$

**Step 4: Decision** (1 mark)

Since $CC_{Q} < CC_{P}$, **Column Q is more economical** (by ₹ 3,867 in capitalized terms).

### Part (b) — Verification by EAC Method (7 marks)

From Step 2 above:

$$
EAC_{P} = \text{₹ } 1{,}77{,}163 \; ; \; EAC_{Q} = \text{₹ } 1{,}76{,}583
$$

Since $EAC_{Q} < EAC_{P}$, **Column Q is more economical** ✓ (matches Part a).

**Annual Savings** = $1{,}77{,}163 - 1{,}76{,}583 =$ **₹ 580 per year** (1 mark)

**Cross-check:** Capitalized savings $= 3{,}867 = 580 \times (1/0.15)$? No, capitalized savings = annual savings / i = 580 / 0.15 = ₹ 3,867. ✓

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 
> 1. **Forgetting the Salvage Sign:** Students often *add* the salvage value instead of *subtracting* it in the EAC formula. Salvage is a **credit** (inflow), not a cost. Marks lost: ~1.5 per occurrence.
> 
> 2. **Direct PW for Unequal Lives:** Computing $PW_A$ for 5 years and $PW_B$ for 8 years and *comparing directly* is the **most common KTU failure pattern**. Always either (a) extend to LCM, or (b) convert to EAC.
> 
> 3. **Factor Confusion:** Mixing up $(A/P)$ and $(A/F)$. Remember: $(A/P)$ capitalises the present cost into an annuity; $(A/F)$ recovers a future amount (salvage) into an annuity. **If in doubt, derive from first principles** as shown in Section 3.
> 
> 4. **Forgetting to Compare:** Students sometimes calculate EAC for both alternatives and stop. You must **state which is lower and compute the savings**. Missing this loses ~1 mark.
> 
> 5. **Mismatched Period in CC Method:** The CC method assumes **perpetual** service. Using CC for a 5-year project is incorrect. Use it only for long-life infrastructure or for ranking truly perpetual chemical plants.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Equal service life** → Direct PW, AW, or FW comparison; all give the same ranking.
- ✅ **Unequal service life** → Must use **EAC method** (most popular) or **Study Period Method (LCM)**.
- ✅ **Perpetual service** → Use **Capitalized Cost (CC = EAC / i)** method.
- ✅ **Master the EAC formula:** $EAC = P \cdot (A/P, i, n) + AOC - S \cdot (A/F, i, n)$.
- ✅ **Bridge identity:** $PW_{LCM} = EAC \times (P/A, i, LCM)$ — this is the secret to cross-checking answers.
- ✅ **The Capital Recovery Factor $(A/P, i, n)$** turns a present cost into an annual cost.
- ✅ **The Sinking Fund Factor $(A/F, i, n)$** turns a future salvage into an annual credit.
- ✅ **Always state the decision** (which alternative is chosen) and the **savings** (EAC difference or PW difference).
- ✅ **Sanity check:** If the EAC ranking and the LCM-PW ranking disagree, you have made an arithmetic or factor error.
- ✅ **For chemical plant projects**, unequal lives are the norm (reactor vs. column, pump vs. compressor), so the EAC method is the **single most important tool** in this module.
- ✅ **Common MARR values in KTU problems:** 8%, 10%, 12%, 15%. Memorise the (A/P) and (A/F) tables for these.
- ✅ **Sign convention:** Costs are positive; salvage is a negative cost (it reduces the annualised cost).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Inflation, Depreciation and Taxes: Nature of Depreciation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECHT757 - ECONOMICS AND MANAGEMENT OF CHEMICAL INDUSTRIES > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Inflation, Depreciation and Taxes: Nature of Depreciation ## SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition In the context of **Chemical Process Industries** and engineering economics, **Depreciation** is formally defined as the systematic, rational, and equitable allocation of the *historical cost* (or other appropriate substituted value, such as fair market value at the time of acquisition) of a *tangible fixed asset* over its *estimated useful economic life*, in a manner consistent with the pattern of economic benefits expected to be consumed by the enterprise. Mathematically, the foundational identity of depreciation is expressed as: $$ \text{Depreciable Base} = \text{Original Capital Cost (C)} - \text{Salvage Value (S)} $$ In KTU-aligned engineering economics texts, depreciation is recognized not as a valuation process (a process of re-measuring asset worth), but strictly as a **cost allocation mechanism** — spreading a single lump-sum capital outflow across multiple accounting periods to match revenues with expenses under the **Matching Principle** of accrual accounting. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Definition (Memorize Verbatim):** > *"Depreciation is the decrease in the value of a physical (tangible) fixed asset due to wear and tear, effluxion of time, obsolescence, exhaustion, or similar causes, and is treated as a non-cash operating expense in the Profit & Loss Account."* ### 1.2 Sub-Classification of Long-Term Asset Value Reduction Chemical engineering literature explicitly distinguishes depreciation from two closely related concepts that students frequently confuse: | Term | Applies To | Nature of Reduction | Example (Chemical Industry) | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Depreciation** | **Tangible fixed assets** with a finite useful life | Physical wear & tear, tear, obsolescence | Distillation column, Centrifuge, Heat exchanger | | **Amortization** | **Intangible assets** (patents, copyrights, goodwill, software) | Legal/economic life expiry | A licensed process patent for 10 years | | **Depletion** | **Natural/Wasting assets** (mines, oil & gas wells, quarries) | Physical exhaustion of the resource | A natural gas reservoir, Limestone mine supplying a cement plant | > [!NOTE] > **Why this matters in KTU Exams:** A 3-mark Part A question is frequently framed as *"Differentiate between depreciation, amortization, and depletion."* Marks are awarded only if the student correctly identifies the *type of asset* each applies to, not just the general meaning. ### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine you purchase a brand-new **microwave oven for ₹12,000** for your hostel room. The oven has a useful life of about **5 years**, after which it will either break down or become technologically obsolete (you will want a smart one). If you charge the **entire ₹12,000 as an expense in the first month**, your budget will appear disastrous in Month 1 and unrealistically profitable in Months 2–60. This violates the **Matching Principle**. The intuitive, fair solution is to recognize that the oven provides benefit gradually. If we assume equal usage, we charge **₹12,000 / 60 months = ₹200/month** as "depreciation." This matches the cost with the benefit over time, making the monthly budget reflective of true economic consumption. In a **chemical plant**, the same principle applies to a **Reactor costing ₹5 Crore** with a 10-year life. Charging ₹50 Lakhs/year as depreciation lets the plant manager see the *true annualized cost* of using that reactor, enabling correct product pricing and accurate profitability analysis. ### 1.4 Physical Constants, Standard Metrics & Reserved Vocabulary > [!IMPORTANT] > **Standard Engineering-Economics Vocabulary used throughout this Module:** * **Capitalized Cost (C):** The original purchase price (or *First Cost*) of the asset including freight, installation, and commissioning. Units: ₹, $, etc. * **Book Value (BV):** The *undepreciated* portion of the asset still carried on the company's balance sheet at any given time. * **Salvage Value (SV or S):** The estimated net realizable value of the asset at the *end* of its useful life. Often called *Residual Value* or *Scrap Value*. In many KTU problems, SV is **assumed to be zero** unless otherwise specified. * **Useful Life (N or n):** The estimated number of years (or units of production) over which the asset is expected to be economically usable. * **Depreciable Base (B):** Defined formally as **B = C − S**. * **Service Value:** Sometimes used interchangeably with Salvage Value in older texts (avoid in exams). > [!NOTE] > **Syllabus Highlight:** The current KTU PECHT757 Module 1 explicitly focuses only on the **Nature of Depreciation** — i.e., what depreciation *is*, its *causes*, its *characteristics*, and its *economic significance*. Numerical methods (Straight-Line, Declining Balance, etc.) belong to the *next* sub-topic of the syllabus and are **out of scope** for this specific note set. ## SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET ### 2.1 The Fundamental Nature of Depreciation — Five Defining Characteristics To truly understand the "nature" of depreciation (as the KTU module title demands), we must examine the **five intrinsic characteristics** that distinguish it from every other expense in the financial statements: **1. It is a *Non-Cash* Expense** Depreciation does **NOT** involve any actual outflow of cash during the period in which it is charged. The cash outflow occurred earlier, at the time of purchasing the asset. Depreciation is merely an *internal book entry* that allocates the past cash outflow across future accounting periods. * **Real-world implication for Chemical Engineers:** In cash-flow analysis for process equipment (used in plant profitability, IRR, NPV calculations), depreciation is **added back** to net profit after tax to compute the actual operating cash flow. This is because the cash left the business when the equipment was bought, not when depreciation was recorded. **2. It is a *Continuous* and *Gradual* Process** Depreciation is assumed to occur progressively over the asset's useful life. It is *not* recognized all at once (unless the asset is fully impaired in a single period, which is a separate accounting treatment called *impairment loss*, distinct from depreciation). **3. It is an *Irreversible* and *Permanent* Decrease in Book Value** Once depreciation is charged in a financial year, it cannot be reversed (under standard accounting norms such as Ind AS 16 / IAS 16 / US GAAP ASC 360). Even if the asset's *market value* appreciates in a given year (e.g., real estate), its *book value* continues to decline. **4. It is *Systematic and Rational*** Depreciation is calculated using a pre-determined, formulaic method (Straight-Line, Written-Down Value, Units of Production, etc.). It is not arbitrary or subject to management discretion once the method is chosen. **5. It is a *Tax-Deductible* Operating Expense** This is the single most important characteristic for **chemical process plant managers**. Depreciation is allowed as a deduction under the **Income Tax Act, 1961** (specifically Sections 32 and Schedule II in India, or equivalent IRS MACRS rules in the USA). Higher depreciation → lower taxable income → lower tax outflow → higher after-tax cash flow. ### 2.2 The Five Primary Causes / Nature of Depreciation The "nature" of depreciation, as asked in the KTU syllabus, refers to the *underlying physical and economic causes* that necessitate depreciation: **(a) Physical Wear and Tear (Usage-Related Deterioration)** The gradual mechanical degradation of the asset due to its operational use. * *Chemical Industry Example:* The impeller blades of a centrifugal pump erode over time due to slurry abrasion; the catalyst in a fixed-bed reactor loses activity after thousands of operating hours. **(b) Effluxion of Time (Time-Related Deterioration)** Deterioration that occurs *regardless of use*, simply due to the passage of time and exposure to environmental factors (rust, oxidation, weathering). * *Chemical Industry Example:* Outdoor storage tanks corrode even when empty due to atmospheric moisture. **(c) Technological Obsolescence** The asset becomes economically unviable because newer, more efficient technology has emerged, even though the asset may still be physically functional. * *Chemical Industry Example:* A batch reactor replaced by a continuous stirred-tank reactor (CSTR) with better conversion and selectivity, or legacy PLC controls replaced by modern DCS systems. **(d) Legal / Contractual Expiry** The asset's right to be used legally expires. * *Chemical Industry Example:* A licensed process (e.g., a Urea manufacturing license from a foreign technology provider) becomes non-renewable after the contract period. This case is technically *amortization*, but conceptually similar. **(e) Exhaustion / Depletion of the Asset Itself** For natural resource-based assets, the resource itself is consumed. * *Chemical Industry Example:* A brine well supplying raw material to a chlor-alkali plant. (Again, technically *depletion*, but conceptually linked.) ### 2.3 Why Depreciation Matters in the Chemical Industry — Economic Significance Chemical plants are among the **most capital-intensive industries in the world**. A single naphtha cracker or ethylene oxide plant may cost **₹10,000 Crore or more**. Therefore, depreciation accounting has an outsized impact on reported profitability. The four pillars of economic significance are: 1. **Recovery of Capital Cost:** Allows the firm to generate internal accruals (via depreciation tax shield) to eventually replace the asset. 2. **Accurate Product Costing:** Ensures the *true cost* of producing a chemical (e.g., per kg of caustic soda) reflects a fair share of the plant's capital consumption — vital for pricing and competitive bidding. 3. **Tax Planning & Cash Flow Optimization:** The depreciation tax shield (Tax × Depreciation rate) directly enhances project IRR. 4. **Statutory Compliance & True Profit Reporting:** Mandated by Companies Act, 2013 (Schedule II) and Ind AS 16. ### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > **All formulas below are exhaustively tested in KTU 2024 Scheme examinations. Memorize the derivations, not just the formulas.** | # | Concept | Formula / Expression | Units / Boundary Condition | Engineering Interpretation | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | 1 | Depreciable Base | $B = C - S$ | Currency (₹, \$) | The portion of capital cost that must be allocated as expense. | | 2 | Annual Depreciation (General) | $D_k = \text{Allocation in year } k$ | Currency/year | Non-cash expense for the k-th year. | | 3 | Book Value at end of year k | $BV_k = C - \sum_{i=1}^{k} D_i$ | Currency | Undepreciated balance still on balance sheet. | | 4 | Book Value at end of life N | $BV_N = S$ | Currency | By definition, equals Salvage Value. | | 5 | Asset Lifetime Constraint | $\sum_{k=1}^{N} D_k = C - S$ | Currency | Total depreciation over life must recover depreciable base exactly. | | 6 | Net Cash Flow Impact | $\Delta \text{Tax} = D_k \times t$ | Currency/year | "Depreciation Tax Shield" — actual cash saved. | | 7 | Present Worth Factor | $P = F / (1+i)^N$ | Dimensionless | Used when comparing depreciation streams across years. | **Note on Units:** All monetary values must be in the **same currency unit** for a given problem. Mixing ₹ and \$ in a single calculation is the **#1 KTU valuation error**. ### 2.5 Real-World Utility in Chemical Engineering Practice * **Feasibility Studies:** When evaluating a new fertilizer plant, engineers compute annualized depreciation to estimate the *Manufacturing Cost* and break-even selling price. * **Make-or-Buy Decisions:** If the depreciation on an existing in-house compressor is high, it may be cheaper to outsource compressed air than to continue operating it. * **Insurance Valuation:** While insurance uses *Replacement Cost*, depreciation-adjusted *Current Value* is used for *Asset Insurance* claims after partial damage. * **Plant Modernization:** Old plants with fully depreciated assets show zero depreciation expense, inflating short-term profits but creating a hidden liability — the eventual need for massive capex. ## SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX ### 3.1 Derivation 1: The Book Value Recursion Identity **Problem Statement:** Derive the recursive relationship that allows us to compute the book value of an asset at the end of any year *k*, given only the capital cost *C*, salvage value *S*, useful life *N*, and the annual depreciation charge *D*. **Step 1 — Define the variables algebraically:** Let $BV_k$ denote the book value at the **end of year k**. By definition, at the moment of purchase (end of year 0, before any depreciation is charged), the book value equals the original cost. $$ BV_0 = C $$ **Step 2 — Apply the definition of depreciation as a reduction in book value:** At the end of year 1, the depreciation charged during year 1, $D_1$, has been subtracted from the book value. $$ BV_1 = BV_0 - D_1 = C - D_1 $$ **Step 3 — Generalize to an arbitrary year k:** By the same logic, the book value at the end of year k equals the book value at the end of year (k−1) minus the depreciation charged in year k. $$ BV_k = BV_{k-1} - D_k $$ **Step 4 — Substitute recursively back to year 0:** Expand $BV_{k-1}$ as $BV_{k-2} - D_{k-1}$, and so on, until we reach $BV_0 = C$. $$ \begin{aligned} BV_k &= BV_0 - (D_1 + D_2 + \dots + D_k) \\ BV_k &= C - \sum_{i=1}^{k} D_i \end{aligned} $$ **Step 5 — Apply the lifetime terminal condition:** At the end of the useful life $N$, the book value must equal the salvage value $S$ (this is a fundamental accounting convention). This gives us the *asset lifetime constraint*: $$ \begin{aligned} BV_N &= C - \sum_{i=1}^{N} D_i = S \\ \Rightarrow \sum_{i=1}^{N} D_i &= C - S \end{aligned} $$ **Interpretation:** The sum of all annual depreciation charges over the asset's entire life **must exactly equal** the depreciable base. This is the single most important equation in the entire depreciation module. --- ### 3.2 Derivation 2: The Depreciation Tax Shield — Its True Cash Value **Problem Statement:** A chemical company purchases a heat exchanger for ₹20,00,000. It is depreciated at a uniform rate over 10 years, after which it has zero salvage value. The corporate income tax rate is 30%. Calculate the **annual depreciation tax shield** and the **present worth of the total tax savings over the 10-year life**, assuming a discount rate of 12%. **Step 1 — Compute the annual depreciation charge (using the straight-line formula, the simplest allowed KTU method):** $$ D = \frac{C - S}{N} = \frac{20{,}00{,}000 - 0}{10} = 2{,}00{,}000 \text{ ₹/year} $$ **Step 2 — Calculate the annual depreciation tax shield:** The tax shield is the tax amount the company *avoids paying* because depreciation is allowed as a deduction from taxable income. $$ \text{Tax Shield}_k = D \times t = 2{,}00{,}000 \times 0.30 = 60{,}000 \text{ ₹/year} $$ **Step 3 — Compute the present worth of the 10-year annuity of tax savings:** We treat the tax shield as an *ordinary annuity* of ₹60,000 per year for 10 years, discounted at 12%. $$ P = A \times \frac{(1+i)^N - 1}{i(1+i)^N} $$ $$ P = 60{,}000 \times \frac{(1.12)^{10} - 1}{0.12 \times (1.12)^{10}} $$ **Step 4 — Numerically evaluate the compound factor:** Compute $(1.12)^{10}$ step by step. $$ \begin{aligned} (1.12)^2 &= 1.2544 \\ (1.12)^4 &= (1.2544)^2 = 1.5735 \\ (1.12)^5 &= (1.12)^4 \times 1.12 = 1.7623 \\ (1.12)^{10} &= (1.7623)^2 = 3.1058 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 5 — Substitute and solve:** $$ \begin{aligned} P &= 60{,}000 \times \frac{3.1058 - 1}{0.12 \times 3.1058} \\ P &= 60{,}000 \times \frac{2.1058}{0.3727} \\ P &= 60{,}000 \times 5.6502 \\ P &= 3{,}39{,}012 \text{ ₹} \end{aligned} $$ **Interpretation:** The depreciation tax shield gives the company a present-day benefit of approximately **₹3.39 Lakhs**. This is *real cash* the company would not have had without the depreciation deduction — illustrating exactly why depreciation matters so much in capital budgeting. --- ### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Depreciation vs. Other Expenses | Parameter | Depreciation | Salary Expense | Raw Material Cost | Loan Repayment | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Involves Cash Outflow?** | ❌ No (Non-cash) | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | | **Frequency** | Periodic (yearly) | Periodic (monthly) | Continuous | Periodic | | **Variable with Production?** | Can be (Units-of-Production method) | Generally fixed in short-term | Highly variable | Fixed | | **Tax-Deductible?** | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | ❌ No (principal only; interest is deductible) | | **Appears on Cash Flow Statement?** | Added back in operating activities | Deducted as outflow | Deducted as outflow | Financing activities | --- ### 3.4 Derivation 3: Book Value After Partial Period **Problem Statement:** A pump costing ₹5,00,000 is depreciated using the straight-line method over 5 years to a salvage value of ₹1,00,000. Compute the book value at the end of the 3rd year. **Step 1 — Compute annual depreciation:** $$ D = \frac{C - S}{N} = \frac{5{,}00{,}000 - 1{,}00{,}000}{5} = 80{,}000 \text{ ₹/year} $$ **Step 2 — Compute book value after 3 years:** $$ BV_3 = C - 3D = 5{,}00{,}000 - 3 \times 80{,}000 = 5{,}00{,}000 - 2{,}40{,}000 = 2{,}60{,}000 \text{ ₹} $$ **Step 3 — Verification using the generalized formula:** $$ \begin{aligned} BV_3 &= C - \sum_{i=1}^{3} D_i = C - 3D \\ &= 5{,}00{,}000 - 3(80{,}000) = 2{,}60{,}000 \text{ ₹} \end{aligned} $$ **Result confirmed.** The book value at the end of year 3 is **₹2,60,000**, which is logically greater than the salvage value of ₹1,00,000 (since the asset still has 2 more years of depreciable life remaining). ## SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS ### 4.1 Concept Map: The Five Causes (Nature) of Depreciation This Mermaid diagram provides a hierarchical breakdown of the underlying causes that necessitate depreciation accounting, which is the exact "Nature of Depreciation" sub-topic in the KTU syllabus. ```mermaid graph TD A["NATURE OF DEPRECIATION"] --> B["Physical Wear and Tear"] A --> C["Effluxion of Time"] A --> D["Technological Obsolescence"] A --> E["Legal or Contractual Expiry"] A --> F["Exhaustion or Depletion"] B --> B1["Moving parts erode
Pump impeller abrasion
Reactor vessel thinning"] C --> C1["Atmospheric corrosion
Rusting of outdoor tanks
Material fatigue"] D --> D1["Batch to CSTR upgrade
Old PLC to DCS migration
Inefficient compressor"] E --> E1["Patent expiry after 20 yrs
Licensed process term ends"] F --> F1["Brine well exhaustion
Limestone quarry depletion"] ``` ### 4.2 Functional Flow: Asset Value Lifecycle from Acquisition to Disposal ```mermaid graph TD P1["Purchase of Asset
Capital Cost = C"] --> P2["Capitalize on
Balance Sheet"] P2 --> P3["Operate and
Depreciate Year 1"] P3 --> P4["Book Value at
End of Year 1 BV1"] P4 --> P5{"Useful Life
N Completed?"} P5 -->|No| P3 P5 -->|Yes| P6["Disposal or Salvage
Realize Value S"] P6 --> P7["Total Depreciation
Charged = C minus S"] style P1 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style P6 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style P5 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px ``` ### 4.3 Block Architecture: The Economic Significance of Depreciation in a Chemical Plant ```mermaid graph LR subgraph INPUTS ["Capital Investment Decision"] I1["Plant Cost C
e.g. Crore plant"] I2["Salvage Value S
at year N"] I3["Useful Life N
years of operation"] end subgraph DEPR ["Depreciation Engine"] DP["Annual Depreciation Dk
Non Cash Expense"] end subgraph OUTPUTS ["Engineering Economic Outcomes"] O1["Annualized
Product Cost"] O2["Tax Shield
Dk times t"] O3["Operating Cash Flow
Profit plus Dk"] O4["True Plant Profitability
and Break Even Price"] end I1 --> DP I2 --> DP I3 --> DP DP --> O1 DP --> O2 DP --> O3 DP --> O4 style DP fill:#FF6347,stroke:#333,stroke-width:3px,color:#fff ``` ### 4.4 Classification Hierarchy: Depreciation, Amortization, Depletion ```mermaid graph TD Z["Long Term Asset Cost Allocation"] --> ZA["DEPRECIATION"] Z --> ZB["AMORTIZATION"] Z --> ZC["DEPLETION"] ZA --> ZA1["Tangible Fixed Assets
Reactors, Columns,
Heat Exchangers, Pumps"] ZB --> ZB1["Intangible Assets
Patents, Copyrights,
Software Licenses, Goodwill"] ZC --> ZC1["Natural Wasting Assets
Oil Wells, Mines,
Quarries, Timberlands"] ZA --> ZA2["Cause: Wear, Time,
Obsolescence"] ZB --> ZB2["Cause: Legal or
Contractual Expiry"] ZC --> ZC2["Cause: Physical
Exhaustion of Resource"] style ZA fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style ZB fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style ZC fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px ``` ## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP ### 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each) #### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] **Define the term "depreciation" as applied to chemical plant assets. List any four causes that necessitate depreciation.** **Model Answer (Examiner's Key):** Depreciation is the systematic and rational allocation of the historical cost of a tangible fixed asset over its estimated useful life, representing the consumption of the asset's economic benefits. **Four Causes:** 1. **Physical Wear and Tear:** Gradual degradation due to operational use (e.g., erosion of pump impellers). 2. **Effluxion of Time:** Deterioration due to passage of time and environmental exposure (e.g., atmospheric corrosion). 3. **Technological Obsolescence:** Becoming uneconomical due to superior technology (e.g., replacing batch reactors with CSTRs). 4. **Exhaustion:** For wasting assets, the resource itself is consumed (e.g., brine well depletion). > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (3-mark question):** A common error is writing "depreciation is the loss in value of an asset." This is **worth 0 marks** in KTU valuation — the *systematic allocation of cost* element is mandatory. Marks are awarded strictly for the keywords *systematic*, *allocation*, *historical cost*, and *useful life*. --- #### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - May 2024] **Distinguish between depreciation, amortization, and depletion with one example each from the chemical industry.** **Model Answer:** | Term | Asset Type | Chemical Industry Example | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Depreciation** | Tangible fixed assets with finite life | A distillation column, Centrifuge, or Reactor vessel | | **Amortization** | Intangible assets with finite legal/economic life | A licensed process technology patent valid for 10 years | | **Depletion** | Natural/wasting assets | A coal mine or limestone quarry supplying raw materials | > [!WARNING] > **Pitfall:** Students frequently write only two columns or give the *same* example for all three. A correct KTU answer must provide **three distinct asset categories** and **three distinct chemical-industry examples**. --- ### 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) > **ESE Module-Level Choice:** Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full. --- #### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]** **(a)** Discuss in detail the various **causes / nature of depreciation** applicable to chemical plant equipment. Explain why depreciation is treated as a *non-cash expense* and how this impacts the operating cash flow of a process plant. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A centrifugal pump is purchased by a process plant for **₹8,00,000**. It has an estimated useful life of **8 years** and an estimated salvage value of **₹80,000**. Using the **Straight Line Method**, compute: (i) The annual depreciation charge. (ii) The book value at the end of the 4th year. (iii) The total depreciation charged over the full life of the asset. **(7 Marks)** **Complete Model Solution:** **(a) Causes and Nature of Depreciation (7 marks — full marks breakdown):** 1. **Physical Wear and Tear** (1 mark): Caused by routine operational use; e.g., bearing failure in rotating equipment, abrasion of pump impellers handling slurries. 2. **Effluxion of Time** (1 mark): Deterioration independent of use, e.g., corrosion of outdoor storage tanks due to humidity. 3. **Technological Obsolescence** (1.5 marks): Process improvements render existing equipment economically obsolete; e.g., replacement of batch reactors with continuous-flow reactors, or legacy PLC systems with modern DCS. 4. **Legal/Contractual Causes** (0.5 mark): Expiry of patents, licensed processes. 5. **Depletion/Exhaustion** (0.5 mark): For raw material extraction assets. 6. **Why Depreciation is Non-Cash** (1.5 marks): The actual cash outflow occurred at the time of purchase. The annual depreciation charge is merely an *accounting allocation* of that past outflow. In the Cash Flow Statement, depreciation is **added back** to net profit to compute true operating cash flow, because the business did not experience any cash outflow during the operating year solely due to depreciation. **(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):** **(i) Annual Depreciation (3 marks):** $$ D = \frac{C - S}{N} = \frac{8{,}00{,}000 - 80{,}000}{8} = \frac{7{,}20{,}000}{8} = 90{,}000 \text{ ₹/year} $$ **(ii) Book Value at end of Year 4 (2 marks):** $$ BV_4 = C - 4D = 8{,}00{,}000 - 4(90{,}000) = 8{,}00{,}000 - 3{,}60{,}000 = 4{,}40{,}000 \text{ ₹} $$ **(iii) Total Depreciation over Full Life (2 marks):** $$ \sum_{i=1}^{8} D_i = N \times D = 8 \times 90{,}000 = 7{,}20{,}000 \text{ ₹} $$ **Verification using the lifetime constraint:** $$ \sum D = C - S = 8{,}00{,}000 - 80{,}000 = 7{,}20{,}000 \text{ ₹} \quad \checkmark ``` > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** Many students confuse the formula $BV_k = C - kD$ with $BV_k = C - D_k$ (using only that single year's depreciation). This is a *costly single-mark error*. Always remember: book value = *cumulative* depreciation subtracted from cost. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]** **(a)** Explain the concept of the **"Depreciation Tax Shield."** Why is depreciation considered the most valuable *non-cash* expense in the chemical process industry? Illustrate with a numerical example. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Differentiate between **depreciation, amortization, and depletion** in a tabular format. For each, give one specific chemical industry example and state the *cause* of value reduction. **(7 Marks)** **Complete Model Solution:** **(a) Depreciation Tax Shield (7 marks):** The depreciation tax shield is the *cash tax savings* a company realizes because depreciation is allowed as a deductible expense under tax law (e.g., Section 32 of the Indian Income Tax Act, 1961). *Why it is the most valuable non-cash expense:* - It directly reduces the *taxable income*, and therefore the *actual cash outflow* toward taxes. - It is **non-cash** — yet it produces a **real cash benefit**. - For a capital-intensive chemical plant, depreciation deductions can be substantial, materially improving project IRR. **Numerical Illustration (5 marks):** A heat exchanger costs **₹30,00,000**, life **10 years**, salvage **₹0**, tax rate **30%**. Annual depreciation: $$ D = \frac{30{,}00{,}000 - 0}{10} = 3{,}00{,}000 \text{ ₹/year} $$ Annual tax shield: $$ \text{TS} = D \times t = 3{,}00{,}000 \times 0.30 = 90{,}000 \text{ ₹/year} $$ Over the 10-year life, total tax shield (undiscounted): $$ \text{Total TS} = 10 \times 90{,}000 = 9{,}00{,}000 \text{ ₹} $$ This ₹9,00,000 is *real cash retained* by the firm — money it would otherwise have paid to the tax authority. **(b) Tabular Differentiation (7 marks):** | Term | Asset Type | Cause of Value Reduction | Chemical Industry Example | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Depreciation** | Tangible fixed assets | Physical wear, time, obsolescence | A distillation column or rotary kiln | | **Amortization** | Intangible assets | Expiry of legal/contractual life | A licensed petrochemical process patent (10-yr license) | | **Depletion** | Natural wasting assets | Physical exhaustion of the resource | A limestone quarry feeding a cement plant | *(1.5 marks for the correct tabular structure, 1 mark each for the three rows' content = 4.5 marks; 1.5 marks for providing three correct, distinct chemical industry examples.)* > [!WARNING] > **Pitfall:** A common KTU valuation mistake in Question B(a) is to confuse the *depreciation tax shield* with the depreciation itself. The tax shield = depreciation × tax rate. Some students mistakenly write the tax shield as equal to the full depreciation amount, losing 2 marks. --- ### 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER **🚀 Rapid-Revision Checklist for "Nature of Depreciation":** * ⭐ **Depreciation = Allocation, NOT Valuation.** It spreads historical cost over useful life; it does *not* measure current market value. * ⭐ **Non-Cash Expense** — but produces a **real cash tax shield** through tax deductibility. * ⭐ **Three Sub-Classes:** * **Depreciation** → Tangible Fixed Assets * **Amortization** → Intangible Assets * **Depletion** → Natural/Wasting Assets * ⭐ **Five Causes:** Physical wear, Time passage, Obsolescence, Legal expiry, Exhaustion. * ⭐ **Five Characteristics:** Non-cash, Continuous, Irreversible, Systematic, Tax-deductible. * ⭐ **Master Formula:** $\sum_{k=1}^{N} D_k = C - S$ (Total depreciation over life = Depreciable base). * ⭐ **Book Value:** $BV_k = C - \sum_{i=1}^{k} D_i$. * ⭐ **Tax Shield:** $\text{TS}_k = D_k \times t$ (where $t$ is the corporate tax rate). * ⭐ **Chemical Industry Specific:** Pumps, compressors, heat exchangers, distillation columns → **Depreciation**. Patents, licensed processes → **Amortization**. Mines, quarries → **Depletion**. * ⭐ **Examiner's Keyword Triggers:** When answering, use the words *"systematic"*, *"allocation"*, *"historical cost"*, *"useful life"*, and *"matching principle"* — these are the magic words that unlock full marks in KTU valuations. **Quick Mnemonic for the 5 Causes → "WTOLE"** (Wear, Time, Obsolescence, Legal, Exhaustion) **Quick Mnemonic for the 5 Characteristics → "NCISS"** (Non-cash, Continuous, Irreversible, Systematic, Shield-providing)

topics.title: Methods of Determining Depreciation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT757 - ECONOMICS AND MANAGEMENT OF CHEMICAL INDUSTRIES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Methods of Determining Depreciation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of Chemical Process Economics (KTU 2024 Scheme terminology), **Depreciation** is defined as the systematic, rational, and periodic allocation of the *historical cost* (or sometimes fair value) of a *tangible fixed asset* over its *useful economic life*, in a manner that matches the asset's cost expiration with the revenue periods it helps to generate. It is a **non-cash expense** charged to the Profit and Loss statement and is governed by matching principle of accounting as codified in applicable accounting standards (e.g., Indian Accounting Standard 16 – *Property, Plant and Equipment*).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** *"Depreciation is the decrease in the value of an asset due to wear and tear, efflux of time, obsolescence, exhaustion, or similar causes, and it represents the recovery of capital cost over the productive life of the asset."*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you purchase a high-end pressure vessel for a chemical plant for **₹10,00,000** that has an expected service life of 10 years. In year 1, the vessel helps you generate revenue. In year 10, it does the same. If you charged the entire **₹10,00,000** as an expense in Year 1, you would artificially depress Year 1 profits and overstate Years 2–10 profits. 

**Intuition:** Think of depreciation as a *slice of the asset's cost* that you "eat" each year. If the vessel lasts 10 years, you "eat" roughly 1/10th of its cost each year (in the Straight Line Method). Depreciation is *not* a valuation of the asset in the market — it is purely an *allocation mechanism* to obey the **matching principle** of accrual accounting.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction:** Depreciation ≠ Market Value Decline. An asset may depreciate at 10% per year on the books but actually appreciate in the resale market (e.g., industrial real estate in Kochi).

### 1.3 Standard Engineering Metrics

The fundamental parameters that govern every depreciation method are:
- **P** = Initial Cost / Principal Cost (in ₹ or $)
- **S** = Salvage Value / Scrap Value (in ₹ or $)
- **n** = Useful Life (in years)
- **V_n** = Book Value at end of year n

These parameters appear in **every** depreciation formula you will encounter in KTU examinations.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Depreciation Methods

Depreciation methods can be broadly classified into two engineering-economic philosophies:

**A. Time-Based Methods (Cost is a function of elapsed time)**
1. Straight Line Method (SLM)
2. Declining Balance Method (DBM)
   - Fixed Percentage on Diminishing Balance (commonly Double Declining Balance – DDB)
3. Sum of the Years' Digits Method (SYD)
4. Sinking Fund Method (Compound Interest Method)

**B. Use-Based / Service-Based Methods (Cost is a function of usage)**
1. Service Output Method
2. Working Hours Method
3. Units of Production Method

### 2.2 Why Multiple Methods? — The Core 'Why'

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The 'Why' Behind the Question:** Different assets experience wear differently. A control valve wears out based on *cycles of operation* (use-based), while administrative office furniture depreciates purely with *time* (time-based). The KTU examiner often asks: *"Why is DBM preferred for chemical plant equipment?"* — because such equipment loses more value in early years due to technological obsolescence.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Method | Annual Depreciation Formula | Book Value After n Years (V_n) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Straight Line Method (SLM)** | $D = \dfrac{P - S}{n}$ | $V_n = P - n \cdot D$ |
| **Fixed % on Original Cost** | $D = \left(\dfrac{P - S}{P}\right) \cdot \dfrac{1}{n} \times 100\ \% \text{ of } P$ | $V_n = P - n \cdot D$ |
| **Declining Balance (DBM)** | $D_n = \alpha \cdot V_{n-1}$ where $\alpha = 1 - \left(\dfrac{S}{P}\right)^{1/n}$ | $V_n = P(1-\alpha)^n$ |
| **Double Declining Balance (DDB)** | $D_n = \dfrac{2}{n} \cdot V_{n-1}$ | $V_n = P\left(1 - \dfrac{2}{n}\right)^n$ |
| **Sum of Years Digits (SYD)** | $D_k = \dfrac{(n - k + 1)}{S_n} \cdot (P - S)$ where $S_n = \dfrac{n(n+1)}{2}$ | $V_k = P - \sum_{i=1}^{k} D_i$ |
| **Sinking Fund Method** | $D = (P - S) \cdot \dfrac{i}{(1+i)^n - 1}$ | $V_n$ via accumulated fund |
| **Service Output Method** | $D = \dfrac{P - S}{\text{Total Estimated Output}} \times \text{Output in period}$ | Based on cumulative output |
| **Working Hours Method** | $D = \dfrac{P - S}{\text{Total Working Hours}} \times \text{Hours worked}$ | Based on cumulative hours |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** In the Declining Balance Method, the percentage $\alpha$ is *not* simply $(P-S)/nP$. It is derived from the constraint that book value at end of year $n$ must equal salvage value $S$. Solving $P(1-\alpha)^n = S$ gives $\alpha = 1 - (S/P)^{1/n}$.

### 2.4 Real-World Utility in Chemical Engineering

In the **chemical process industries**, depreciation methods critically influence:
- **Project Economic Evaluation** (DCF, NPV, IRR analyses)
- **Tax Planning** (Higher early-year depreciation via DBM reduces taxable income early)
- **Replacement Decision Analysis**
- **Plant Asset Register Maintenance**

For a catalytic cracking unit, the DDB method is preferred because early years see more intense depreciation due to catalyst deactivation, mechanical wear, and rapid obsolescence from newer refining technologies.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Straight Line Method (SLM)

**Starting Premise:** Total depreciable amount is $(P - S)$ spread *uniformly* over $n$ years.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total Depreciation Over Life} &= P - S \\
\text{Annual Depreciation (D)} &= \dfrac{P - S}{n} \\
\text{Book Value After } k \text{ Years} &= V_k = P - k \cdot D \\
V_k &= P - k \cdot \left(\dfrac{P - S}{n}\right) \\
V_k &= P - \dfrac{k(P - S)}{n} \\
V_k &= P \left(1 - \dfrac{k}{n}\right) + S \cdot \dfrac{k}{n}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step-by-Step Logic:**
1. We begin with the principle that depreciation must exhaust the entire depreciable base $(P - S)$ exactly at the end of year $n$.
2. The annual depreciation $D$ is therefore the *constant* amount.
3. Book value at end of year $k$ is original cost minus accumulated depreciation over $k$ years.
4. Substituting $D = (P-S)/n$ into the book value expression yields the linear book value equation.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Declining Balance Method (DBM)

**Starting Premise:** Annual depreciation is a fixed percentage $\alpha$ of the *diminishing* book value.

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_1 &= \alpha \cdot P \\
V_1 &= P - D_1 = P(1 - \alpha) \\
D_2 &= \alpha \cdot V_1 = \alpha P(1 - \alpha) \\
V_2 &= P(1 - \alpha)^2 \\
&\vdots \\
V_n &= P(1 - \alpha)^n \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Boundary Condition:** At the end of useful life, book value equals salvage value:
$$
V_n = S \quad \Rightarrow \quad P(1-\alpha)^n = S
$$

Solving for $\alpha$:
$$
\begin{aligned}
(1 - \alpha)^n &= \dfrac{S}{P} \\
1 - \alpha &= \left(\dfrac{S}{P}\right)^{1/n} \\
\alpha &= 1 - \left(\dfrac{S}{P}\right)^{1/n}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step-by-Step Logic:**
1. The depreciation in year 1 is $\alpha$ applied to original cost $P$.
2. The book value after year 1 is therefore $P(1-\alpha)$.
3. By induction, book value after $n$ years is $P(1-\alpha)^n$.
4. We impose the boundary condition $V_n = S$ to determine $\alpha$.
5. Taking the $n$-th root and isolating $\alpha$ gives the standard DBM percentage formula.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Double Declining Balance (DDB)

**Starting Premise:** DDB is a special case of DBM where $\alpha = 2/n$ (twice the SLM rate), *without* deducting salvage value from the rate calculation.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\alpha_{DDB} &= \dfrac{2}{n} \\
D_1 &= \dfrac{2}{n} \cdot P \\
V_1 &= P\left(1 - \dfrac{2}{n}\right) \\
V_n &= P\left(1 - \dfrac{2}{n}\right)^n \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step-by-Step Logic:**
1. SLM rate is $(P-S)/nP$. Ignoring salvage value, the straight-line rate on original cost is $1/n$.
2. DDB simply doubles this: $\alpha = 2/n$.
3. The depreciation each year is $\alpha$ applied to the *previous* book value.
4. The book value at end of year $n$ is the compounded diminishing balance.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Caveat:** In DDB, we never depreciate below salvage value $S$. In the final years, we switch to SLM on the remaining depreciable amount to ensure $V_n = S$.

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4: Sum of the Years' Digits Method (SYD)

**Starting Premise:** Depreciation in year $k$ is proportional to the *remaining life* $(n - k + 1)$.

The sum of years' digits:
$$
S_n = 1 + 2 + 3 + \dots + n = \dfrac{n(n+1)}{2}
$$

Depreciation in year $k$:
$$
\begin{aligned}
D_k &= \left(\dfrac{n - k + 1}{S_n}\right) \cdot (P - S) \\
D_k &= \dfrac{2(n - k + 1)}{n(n+1)} \cdot (P - S)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step-by-Step Logic:**
1. The numerator $(n - k + 1)$ decreases linearly from $n$ (year 1) to 1 (year $n$).
2. The denominator $S_n = n(n+1)/2$ normalizes these weights to sum to unity.
3. Multiplying by $(P - S)$ gives the absolute depreciation charge in year $k$.
4. Total depreciation over life: $\sum D_k = (P-S)$ — the entire depreciable base is recovered.

**Verification (for n = 4 years, P = ₹10,00,000, S = ₹2,00,000):**
$$
S_n = \dfrac{4 \cdot 5}{2} = 10
$$

| Year (k) | Numerator $(n-k+1)$ | Weight | Depreciation $D_k$ (₹) |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 1 | 4 | 4/10 | 3,20,000 |
| 2 | 3 | 3/10 | 2,40,000 |
| 3 | 2 | 2/10 | 1,60,000 |
| 4 | 1 | 1/10 | 80,000 |
| **Total** | — | **10/10** | **8,00,000** (= P − S) |

---

### 3.5 Derivation 5: Sinking Fund Method

**Starting Premise:** A fund is built up by *equal annual deposits* earning compound interest at rate $i$. The annual depreciation is the deposit, not the depreciation expense alone.

The annual deposit (sinking fund payment) $A$ is determined from the standard sinking fund factor:

$$
\begin{aligned}
A &= (P - S) \cdot \dfrac{i}{(1+i)^n - 1} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

Total annual expense in year $k$ (deposit + interest on accumulated fund):
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Annual Charge}_k &= A + \text{Interest on Fund}_k \\
\text{Interest}_k &= i \cdot F_{k-1} \quad \text{where } F_{k-1} \text{ is accumulated fund at end of year } k-1
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step-by-Step Logic:**
1. We need to accumulate $(P - S)$ at the end of year $n$ at compound interest rate $i$.
2. The annuity formula gives the required annual deposit $A$.
3. In year $k$, the *true* depreciation expense reported on the P&L includes both the deposit $A$ and the notional interest credited to the fund.
4. The book value at year $k$ is $V_k = P - F_k$ where $F_k$ is the accumulated sinking fund.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Note:** The sinking fund method is rarely used in modern industry but is a *favorite* KTU question because it tests understanding of compound interest. Master it!

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->


<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define depreciation. List any four methods of determining depreciation used in chemical process industries.*

**Model Answer:**
Depreciation is the systematic allocation of the cost of a tangible fixed asset over its useful economic life, representing the consumption of economic benefits arising from the asset's use.

Four methods:
1. Straight Line Method (SLM)
2. Declining Balance Method (DBM)
3. Sum of the Years' Digits Method (SYD)
4. Sinking Fund Method
*(Alternatively: Service Output Method or Working Hours Method)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
*Distinguish between Straight Line Method and Declining Balance Method of depreciation.*

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | SLM | DBM |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Annual Charge | Constant | Decreasing |
| Base for Calculation | Original Cost | Diminishing Book Value |
| Pattern | Linear | Exponential |
| Recovery of Cost | Uniform over life | More in early years |
| Suitable For | Buildings, Furniture | Machinery, Equipment |

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

A heat exchanger costs **₹5,00,000** with a salvage value of **₹50,000** and a useful life of **10 years**. Compute the depreciation schedule and book values using:
**(a)** Straight Line Method **(7 Marks)**
**(b)** Double Declining Balance Method **(7 Marks)**

**Solution:**

**Part (a): Straight Line Method**

Annual Depreciation:
$$
D = \dfrac{P - S}{n} = \dfrac{5{,}00{,}000 - 50{,}000}{10} = \dfrac{4{,}50{,}000}{10} = ₹45{,}000
$$

Book Value Schedule:

| Year | Depreciation (₹) | Accumulated (₹) | Book Value (₹) |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 0 | — | — | 5,00,000 |
| 1 | 45,000 | 45,000 | 4,55,000 |
| 2 | 45,000 | 90,000 | 4,10,000 |
| ... | ... | ... | ... |
| 10 | 45,000 | 4,50,000 | 50,000 |

**Part (b): Double Declining Balance Method**

DDB Rate:
$$
\alpha = \dfrac{2}{n} = \dfrac{2}{10} = 0.20 \text{ (or 20%)}
$$

| Year | Opening BV (₹) | Depreciation (₹) | Closing BV (₹) |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 1 | 5,00,000 | 1,00,000 | 4,00,000 |
| 2 | 4,00,000 | 80,000 | 3,20,000 |
| 3 | 3,20,000 | 64,000 | 2,56,000 |
| 4 | 2,56,000 | 51,200 | 2,04,800 |
| 5 | 2,04,800 | 40,960 | 1,63,840 |
| 6 | 1,63,840 | 32,768 | 1,31,072 |
| 7 | 1,31,072 | 26,214 | 1,04,858 |
| 8 | 1,04,858 | 20,972 | 83,886 |
| 9 | 83,886 | 16,777 | 67,109 |
| 10 | 67,109 | 17,109* | 50,000 |

*\*Final year adjusted to bring book value down to ₹50,000 (salvage value).*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

A chemical plant boiler costs **₹8,00,000** with a scrap value of **₹1,00,000**. Expected useful life is **8 years**.
**(a)** Calculate the depreciation schedule using the **Sum of the Years' Digits (SYD) Method**. **(7 Marks)**
**(b)** Calculate the annual depreciation using the **Sinking Fund Method** assuming an interest rate of **10% per annum**. **(7 Marks)**

**Solution:**

**Part (a): SYD Method**

Sum of digits:
$$
S_n = \dfrac{n(n+1)}{2} = \dfrac{8 \cdot 9}{2} = 36
$$

Depreciable amount:
$$
P - S = 8{,}00{,}000 - 1{,}00{,}000 = ₹7{,}00{,}000
$$

| Year (k) | Weight $\frac{n-k+1}{S_n}$ | Depreciation (₹) | Book Value (₹) |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 1 | 8/36 | 1,55,556 | 6,44,444 |
| 2 | 7/36 | 1,36,111 | 5,08,333 |
| 3 | 6/36 | 1,16,667 | 3,91,666 |
| 4 | 5/36 | 97,222 | 2,94,444 |
| 5 | 4/36 | 77,778 | 2,16,666 |
| 6 | 3/36 | 58,333 | 1,58,333 |
| 7 | 2/36 | 38,889 | 1,19,444 |
| 8 | 1/36 | 19,444 | 1,00,000 |
| **Total** | **36/36** | **7,00,000** | — |

**Part (b): Sinking Fund Method**

Annual sinking fund deposit:
$$
A = (P - S) \cdot \dfrac{i}{(1+i)^n - 1}
$$
$$
A = 7{,}00{,}000 \cdot \dfrac{0.10}{(1.10)^8 - 1} = 7{,}00{,}000 \cdot \dfrac{0.10}{2.1436 - 1}
$$
$$
A = 7{,}00{,}000 \cdot \dfrac{0.10}{1.1436} = 7{,}00{,}000 \cdot 0.08744 = ₹61{,}208
$$

This ₹61,208 is the annual *deposit*. The total expense in year 1 is ₹61,208 + interest on ₹0 = ₹61,208. In year 2, it is ₹61,208 + 10% × ₹61,208 = ₹67,329, and so on.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting Boundary Condition in DDB**: Students forget that DDB can never depreciate an asset below salvage value. The final year must be adjusted.
> 2. **Confusing DBM vs DDB**: In DBM, the rate $\alpha$ is derived from $S$ and $P$. In DDB, $\alpha = 2/n$ is taken directly without using $S$ in the rate calculation.
> 3. **Sinking Fund Confusion**: Many students compute only the deposit $A$ but forget that the *true annual expense* includes both the deposit and the interest credited to the fund.
> 4. **Units of Production Method**: Students forget to multiply by actual usage in the period. The depreciation is proportional to usage, not just total.
> 5. **SYD Numerator Error**: Common mistake is using $n - k$ instead of $n - k + 1$ for the numerator. Year 1 weight should be $n$, not $n-1$.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist:**

- **Definition:** Depreciation is *cost allocation*, not valuation.
- **Three Universal Parameters:** $P$ (cost), $S$ (salvage), $n$ (life). Memorize these symbols.
- **SLM Formula:** $D = (P - S)/n$ — constant annual charge.
- **DBM Formula:** $\alpha = 1 - (S/P)^{1/n}$ — geometric decline.
- **DDB Formula:** $\alpha = 2/n$ — twice the SLM rate, applied to book value.
- **SYD Numerator:** $(n - k + 1)$ for year $k$; Denominator $= n(n+1)/2$.
- **Sinking Fund:** Annual deposit $A = (P - S) \cdot \frac{i}{(1+i)^n - 1}$.
- **Use-Based Methods:** $D = \frac{P - S}{\text{Total Units}} \times \text{Units in Period}$.
- **Salvage Value Constraint:** Book value must never fall below $S$ — even in DDB.
- **Time-Based vs Use-Based:** Choose based on whether asset wear is *time-driven* or *usage-driven*.
- **Chemical Industry Context:** DBM/DDB preferred for plant equipment; SLM preferred for buildings and storage tanks.
- **Tax Angle:** Higher early depreciation (DBM/DDB/SYD) reduces early taxable income, improving cash flow.
- **Always show units** (₹ or $) and **round to 2 decimal places** in KTU numericals.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Straight Line Method
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECHT757 - ECONOMICS AND MANAGEMENT OF CHEMICAL INDUSTRIES > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Straight Line Method # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition The **Straight Line Method (SLM)**, also known as the **Fixed Instalment Method** or **Original Cost Method**, is a depreciation accounting technique in which an **equal, uniform amount of depreciation** is charged against the fixed asset in *every accounting period* over its estimated useful life. In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, depreciation under SLM is described as the *linear, time-based allocation* of the *depreciable base* of an asset — i.e., the difference between its capitalized cost and its terminal salvage value — distributed evenly across each year of service. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** > The Straight Line Method is the *foundation method* under which several other depreciation techniques (like Sinking Fund and SYD) are framed. KTU examiners typically test this in Part A (3 marks) as well as Part B (14 marks) with numerical problems involving chemical plant equipment such as heat exchangers, distillation columns, and reactors. Mathematically, the method assumes that the **loss in asset value is a linear function of time**, irrespective of actual usage, wear pattern, or technological obsolescence. ## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy Imagine you buy a brand-new Maruti car for **₹8,00,000** and you *know* — based on your experience and the manufacturer's data — that after exactly **10 years** the car will only fetch a resale value of **₹3,00,000**. So your car "loses" a total of: $$₹8,00,000 - ₹3,00,000 = ₹5,00,000 \text{ over 10 years}$$ Now, under the **Straight Line Method**, you simply divide that loss **equally** across the 10 years: $$\frac{₹5,00,000}{10} = ₹50,000 \text{ per year}$$ So whether it's Year 1, Year 5, or Year 10, the depreciation amount is **always ₹50,000** — just like a straight, ruler-flat line on a graph. > [!NOTE] > **Why is it called "Straight Line"?** > If you plot the asset's **Book Value (BV)** on the Y-axis and **Time (Years)** on the X-axis, the resulting graph is a *perfectly straight, downward-sloping line* — hence the name. ## 1.3 Physical / Financial Constants & Standard Metrics - **Currency Unit:** All monetary values in KTU problems are in **₹ (Rupees)** unless otherwise stated. - **Time Unit:** Years (uniform 12-month periods). - **Standard Useful Life (n):** Typically **5, 8, 10, 12, or 15 years** for chemical process equipment. - **Salvage Value (S):** Usually **0 to 15%** of original cost for chemical plant assets. - **Depreciation Rate (d):** Expressed as a percentage per year. $$d \ (\text{in } \%) = \frac{1}{n} \times 100$$ For a 10-year asset, $d = 10\%$ per year. For a 5-year asset, $d = 20\%$ per year. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step Breakdown The Straight Line Method follows a strict, deterministic algorithm. Let us break it into a clean, structured sequence: - **Step 1 — Identify the Capitalized Cost (C):** This is the *original purchase price* plus all costs necessary to bring the asset to its intended use (freight, installation, commissioning, trial runs, initial catalyst loading, etc.). For chemical plants, this often includes *piping, insulation, and foundation costs*. - **Step 2 — Estimate the Salvage Value (S):** The expected *resale, scrap, or salvage value* of the asset at the end of its useful life. Also called *terminal value* or *disposal value*. - **Step 3 — Determine the Useful Life (n):** The estimated number of years the asset will remain productive in the chemical plant. This is based on engineering judgement, manufacturer specifications, and operating conditions (corrosive, high-temperature, high-pressure service reduces life). - **Step 4 — Calculate the Depreciable Base:** The depreciable base is the *total amount that can be depreciated*: $$\text{Depreciable Base} = C - S$$ - **Step 5 — Compute Annual Depreciation (D):** Distribute the depreciable base *equally* over n years: $$D = \frac{C - S}{n}$$ - **Step 6 — Determine the Book Value at End of Year t (BVₜ):** Subtract the cumulative depreciation from the original cost: $$BV_t = C - t \cdot D = C - \frac{t(C - S)}{n}$$ - **Step 7 — Verify Boundary Conditions:** At $t = 0$: $BV_0 = C$ ✓ At $t = n$: $BV_n = S$ ✓ At $t = n/2$: $BV_{n/2} = (C + S)/2$ ✓ ## 2.2 The "Why" Behind the Method The fundamental philosophy is **fairness through time-based equity**. The asset *consistently serves* the production process every year; therefore, its cost should be *uniformly expensed* every year. This avoids distorting profit figures in early years (as would happen with accelerated methods like WDV). > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Utility in Chemical Engineering:** > - Preferred for **process piping, storage tanks, and civil works** in a chemical plant. > - Used for **income tax purposes** under the Companies Act / Income Tax Act for many asset classes. > - Most suitable when **obsolescence is not the dominant factor** (i.e., physical wear and tear governs the asset life). ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Symbol | Description | Formula / Unit | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | $C$ | Capitalized (original) cost of asset | ₹ (Rupees) | | $S$ | Salvage / Scrap value at end of life | ₹ (Rupees) | | $n$ | Useful life of the asset | Years | | $D$ | Annual depreciation charge | ₹ per year | | $d$ | Annual depreciation rate | $\% \text{ per year}$ | | $BV_t$ | Book value at end of year $t$ | ₹ | | $A_t$ | Accumulated depreciation at end of year $t$ | ₹ | ### Key Equations $$ D = \frac{C - S}{n} $$ $$ d \ (\%) = \frac{D}{C} \times 100 = \frac{(C - S)}{n \cdot C} \times 100 $$ $$ A_t = t \cdot D = \frac{t(C - S)}{n} $$ $$ BV_t = C - A_t = C - \frac{t(C - S)}{n} $$ $$ \sum_{t=1}^{n} D = C - S $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Table Note:** All vertical bars (absolute value markers) are written as `\vert` so the markdown table renders correctly. The summation sign $\sum$ confirms that total depreciation equals the depreciable base. ## 2.4 Advantages & Limitations (Frequently Asked in KTU) **Advantages:** - **Simplicity:** Easiest method to understand and apply. - **Uniformity:** Same charge every year — simplifies budgeting and tax planning. - **Total Add-Up:** The total depreciation always equals $C - S$ (no rounding issues). **Limitations:** - **Ignores actual wear:** A pump may deteriorate faster in Year 1 than Year 10. - **No interest consideration:** Time value of money is not accounted for. - **Asset-specific mismatch:** Not suitable for assets that generate higher revenue in early years (e.g., a new reactor producing premium chemicals). # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of the Annual Depreciation Formula We start from the **fundamental accounting identity** for any depreciation method: $$\text{Original Cost} - \text{Salvage Value} = \text{Total Depreciation Over Useful Life}$$ $$ C - S = \sum_{t=1}^{n} D_t $$ **Conversion Logic:** The total monetary value lost by the asset over its entire life equals the sum of all individual annual depreciation charges $D_1, D_2, \dots, D_n$. Now, the **core assumption of SLM** is that every annual charge is *equal*: $$ D_1 = D_2 = D_3 = \dots = D_n = D $$ **Conversion Logic:** This is the definition of "straight line" — uniform instalments. Substituting into the summation: $$ C - S = \sum_{t=1}^{n} D = D + D + D + \dots \text{ (n times)} $$ $$ C - S = n \cdot D $$ **Conversion Logic:** Adding the constant $D$ exactly $n$ times gives $n$ times $D$. Solving for $D$: $$ D = \frac{C - S}{n} $$ **Conversion Logic:** We divide both sides of the equation by $n$ to isolate the annual charge. ✅ **Q.E.D.** — Annual depreciation under SLM is the depreciable base divided by useful life. ## 3.2 Derivation of the Book Value Formula at End of Year $t$ The **book value** is the *un-depreciated portion* of the original cost remaining on the books of accounts at the end of year $t$. By definition: $$ BV_t = C - \text{Accumulated Depreciation up to year } t $$ $$ BV_t = C - A_t $$ **Conversion Logic:** Book value = original cost minus how much has been "charged off" so far. Substituting $A_t = t \cdot D$ (since depreciation accumulates linearly): $$ BV_t = C - t \cdot D $$ **Conversion Logic:** After $t$ years, total depreciation charged is $t$ times the annual rate. Substituting $D = (C - S)/n$: $$ BV_t = C - t \cdot \left( \frac{C - S}{n} \right) $$ **Conversion Logic:** We replace $D$ with its formula derived in Section 3.1. Expanding: $$ BV_t = C - \frac{t \cdot C}{n} + \frac{t \cdot S}{n} $$ **Conversion Logic:** Distributing the negative sign across the numerator of the fraction. Rearranging: $$ BV_t = C \left( 1 - \frac{t}{n} \right) + S \left( \frac{t}{n} \right) $$ **Conversion Logic:** We factor out $C$ and $S$ for a more elegant representation. This is the **most general form** of the SLM book value equation and is *highly useful* for partial-year valuations in KTU problems. ## 3.3 Worked-Out Example (Full Numerical Walkthrough) **Problem Statement:** > A chemical plant purchases a *stainless-steel batch reactor* for **₹15,00,000**. The estimated useful life is **10 years** and the salvage value is **₹1,00,000**. > Calculate: > (a) The annual depreciation charge. > (b) The book value at the end of the 4th year. > (c) The accumulated depreciation at the end of the 7th year. ### Solution: **Given Data:** $$ C = ₹15,00,000, \quad S = ₹1,00,000, \quad n = 10 \text{ years} $$ **Part (a) — Annual Depreciation:** $$ D = \frac{C - S}{n} = \frac{15,00,000 - 1,00,000}{10} $$ $$ D = \frac{14,00,000}{10} = ₹1,40,000 \text{ per year} $$ **Part (b) — Book Value at End of Year 4 ($t = 4$):** $$ BV_4 = C - t \cdot D = 15,00,000 - (4 \times 1,40,000) $$ $$ BV_4 = 15,00,000 - 5,60,000 = ₹9,40,000 $$ **Part (c) — Accumulated Depreciation at End of Year 7 ($t = 7$):** $$ A_7 = t \cdot D = 7 \times 1,40,000 = ₹9,80,000 $$ **Verification (Sanity Check):** $$ BV_7 = C - A_7 = 15,00,000 - 9,80,000 = ₹5,20,000 $$ Using the alternative form: $$ BV_7 = 15,00,000 \left(1 - \frac{7}{10}\right) + 1,00,000 \left(\frac{7}{10}\right) = 4,50,000 + 70,000 = ₹5,20,000 \ \checkmark $$ ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Book Value Schedule for the Reactor Below is a **complete 10-year depreciation schedule** for the batch reactor, as required in many KTU 14-mark problems: | Year $t$ | Opening Book Value (₹) | Depreciation $D$ (₹) | Accumulated $A_t$ (₹) | Closing Book Value $BV_t$ (₹) | | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | | 1 | 15,00,000 | 1,40,000 | 1,40,000 | 13,60,000 | | 2 | 13,60,000 | 1,40,000 | 2,80,000 | 12,20,000 | | 3 | 12,20,000 | 1,40,000 | 4,20,000 | 10,80,000 | | 4 | 10,80,000 | 1,40,000 | 5,60,000 | 9,40,000 | | 5 | 9,40,000 | 1,40,000 | 7,00,000 | 8,00,000 | | 6 | 8,00,000 | 1,40,000 | 8,40,000 | 6,60,000 | | 7 | 6,60,000 | 1,40,000 | 9,80,000 | 5,20,000 | | 8 | 5,20,000 | 1,40,000 | 11,20,000 | 3,80,000 | | 9 | 3,80,000 | 1,40,000 | 12,60,000 | 2,40,000 | | 10 | 2,40,000 | 1,40,000 | 14,00,000 | 1,00,000 | > [!NOTE] > **Observations from the Schedule:** > 1. The **annual depreciation is constant** at ₹1,40,000 — confirming SLM behaviour. > 2. The **closing book value drops linearly** from ₹15,00,000 to ₹1,00,000 (the salvage value). > 3. At $t = 5$ (mid-life), $BV_5 = ₹8,00,000$ — exactly the **arithmetic mean** of $C$ and $S$: $(15,00,000 + 1,00,000)/2 = ₹8,00,000$. ✓ # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — SLM Computation Flow The following Mermaid diagram maps the *decision and computation flow* used in KTU problems to apply the Straight Line Method. ```mermaid flowchart TD startNode([Start of Problem]) --> inputData[/Collect Input Data:
Original Cost C
Salvage Value S
Useful Life n
Service Years t/] inputData --> computeBase[Compute Depreciable Base
C minus S] computeBase --> computeD[Compute Annual Depreciation
D = C minus S divided by n] computeD --> rateCheck{Compute Depreciation Rate?} rateCheck --> yesRate[Yes: d = D divided by C times 100] rateCheck --> noRate[No: Skip Rate Calculation] yesRate --> bvDecision{Compute Book Value?} noRate --> bvDecision bvDecision --> yesBV[Yes: BV of t = C minus t times D] bvDecision --> noBV[No: Provide Annual Schedule] yesBV --> scheduleCheck{Generate Full Schedule?} noBV --> scheduleCheck scheduleCheck --> yesSched[Yes: Tabulate for t = 1 to n] scheduleCheck --> noSched[No: Provide Summary] yesSched --> verify[Verify Boundary Conditions:
BV at 0 equals C
BV at n equals S] noSched --> verify verify --> endNode([End: Final Answer]) ``` ## 4.2 Concept Map — Book Value vs. Time under SLM The schematic below illustrates the *linear, time-based decay* of book value — the defining visual signature of the Straight Line Method. ```mermaid flowchart LR axisX[Time Axis t in years] --> chartTitle([Book Value BV of t vs Time t
Straight Line Method]) pointC[At t = 0
BV equals C
Original Cost] -.-> chartTitle pointMid[At t = n by 2
BV equals C + S divided by 2
Mid Life] -.-> chartTitle pointS[At t = n
BV equals S
Salvage Value] -.-> chartTitle chartTitle --> lineSlope[Linear Decay Slope equals negative D
Constant Decrease Per Year] lineSlope --> keyInsight([Key Insight:
Constant Annual Charge
Uniform Depreciation]) ``` ## 4.3 Process Flow — How a Chemical Industry Applies SLM ```mermaid flowchart TD phase1[Phase 1: Capital Investment
Purchase and Install Equipment] --> phase2[Phase 2: Capitalize Cost
Add Freight Installation and Commissioning] phase2 --> phase3[Phase 3: Estimate Parameters
Determine Useful Life n
Estimate Salvage Value S] phase3 --> phase4[Phase 4: Apply SLM Formula
D = C minus S divided by n] phase4 --> phase5[Phase 5: Annual Book Keeping
Charge D Every Year to P and L] phase5 --> phase6[Phase 6: Asset Disposal
At t = n
Sell at Scrap Value S] phase6 --> phase7[Phase 7: Tax Filing
Claim Depreciation under IT Act] ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Diagram Interpretation Tip (For KTU):** > KTU examiners often award a partial 1–2 mark band for correctly labelling a depreciation schedule or flow chart. Always annotate your diagram with the *salvage value line*, the *original cost line*, and the *slope* (which equals $-D$). # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper]** **Q: Define depreciation. Explain the Straight Line Method of depreciation with a suitable example.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Definition (1 mark):** Depreciation is the *systematic and rational allocation* of the capitalized cost of a tangible fixed asset over its estimated useful life, reflecting the *reduction in value* due to wear and tear, usage, passage of time, or obsolescence. **Explanation (1.5 marks):** The Straight Line Method (SLM) assumes a *uniform* depreciation charge every year.
