The annual depreciation is calculated as: $$D = \frac{C - S}{n}$$ where $C$ is the original cost, $S$ is the salvage value, and $n$ is the useful life in years. **Example (0.5 mark):** A pump costing ₹2,00,000 with a salvage value of ₹20,000 and a useful life of 9 years has: $$D = \frac{2,00,000 - 20,000}{9} = ₹20,000 \text{ per year}$$ --- ### **Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** **Q: List any three advantages and two limitations of the Straight Line Method.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Advantages (1.5 marks — 0.5 each):** 1. **Simplicity:** It is the easiest depreciation method to understand and apply. 2. **Uniformity:** Equal annual charges simplify budgeting, accounting, and income tax filing. 3. **Conservative Asset Valuation:** The book value declines steadily, providing a conservative financial picture. **Limitations (1 mark — 0.5 each):** 1. **Ignores Time Value of Money:** SLM does not account for the interest that could be earned on depreciation reserves. 2. **Ignores Actual Wear Pattern:** The method assumes uniform wear, which is unrealistic for assets that deteriorate more rapidly in early years. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice) ### **Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024, Model Paper]** A pharmaceutical company installs a new **chemical mixing tank** for **₹12,00,000**. The tank has an estimated useful life of **8 years** and a salvage value of **₹80,000** at the end of its service. **Part (a) — 7 marks:** Calculate the annual depreciation, the depreciation rate, and prepare the depreciation schedule for all 8 years using the **Straight Line Method**. **Part (b) — 7 marks:** Determine the **book value at the end of the 3rd and 6th years**. Also compute the **total accumulated depreciation** by the end of the 8th year and verify that the asset's book value equals its salvage value. --- #### **Model Solution to Question A:** **Given Data:** $$ C = ₹12,00,000, \quad S = ₹80,000, \quad n = 8 \text{ years} $$ **Solution to Part (a):** **Step 1: Depreciable Base (1 mark)** $$ \text{Depreciable Base} = C - S = 12,00,000 - 80,000 = ₹11,20,000 $$ **Step 2: Annual Depreciation (1 mark)** $$ D = \frac{C - S}{n} = \frac{11,20,000}{8} = ₹1,40,000 \text{ per year} $$ **Step 3: Depreciation Rate (1 mark)** $$ d = \frac{D}{C} \times 100 = \frac{1,40,000}{12,00,000} \times 100 = 11.67\% \text{ per year} $$ **Step 4: Depreciation Schedule (4 marks)** | Year $t$ | Opening BV (₹) | Depreciation $D$ (₹) | Accumulated $A_t$ (₹) | Closing BV (₹) | | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | | 1 | 12,00,000 | 1,40,000 | 1,40,000 | 10,60,000 | | 2 | 10,60,000 | 1,40,000 | 2,80,000 | 9,20,000 | | 3 | 9,20,000 | 1,40,000 | 4,20,000 | 7,80,000 | | 4 | 7,80,000 | 1,40,000 | 5,60,000 | 6,20,000 | | 5 | 6,20,000 | 1,40,000 | 7,00,000 | 4,80,000 | | 6 | 4,80,000 | 1,40,000 | 8,40,000 | 3,40,000 | | 7 | 3,40,000 | 1,40,000 | 9,80,000 | 2,00,000 | | 8 | 2,00,000 | 1,40,000 | 11,20,000 | 80,000 | **Solution to Part (b):** **Step 1: Book Value at End of Year 3 (2 marks)** $$ BV_3 = C - 3D = 12,00,000 - (3 \times 1,40,000) = 12,00,000 - 4,20,000 = ₹7,80,000 $$ **Step 2: Book Value at End of Year 6 (2 marks)** $$ BV_6 = C - 6D = 12,00,000 - (6 \times 1,40,000) = 12,00,000 - 8,40,000 = ₹3,60,000 $$ > **Note:** This ₹3,60,000 represents the *un-depreciated* value remaining on the books. **Step 3: Total Accumulated Depreciation at End of Year 8 (2 marks)** $$ A_8 = 8 \times D = 8 \times 1,40,000 = ₹11,20,000 $$ **Step 4: Verification (1 mark)** $$ BV_8 = C - A_8 = 12,00,000 - 11,20,000 = ₹80,000 = S \ \checkmark $$ The book value at the end of the asset's life exactly equals the salvage value, confirming the SLM calculations are correct. --- ### **Question B (Choice 2) [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Paper]** A fertilizer manufacturing unit purchases a **compressor** for **₹8,50,000**. The expected useful life is **10 years**, and the salvage value at the end is **₹50,000**. **Part (a) — 7 marks:** Using the **Straight Line Method**, calculate: (i) The annual depreciation charge. (ii) The book value at the end of the 5th year. (iii) The depreciation rate (in %). **Part (b) — 7 marks:** If the company uses the compressor for only **7 years** and then *discards* it (realizing only the salvage value), compute: (i) The book value at the end of the 7th year. (ii) The total depreciation charged over 7 years. (iii) Whether there is any **profit or loss** on disposal, and its amount. --- #### **Model Solution to Question B:** **Given Data:** $$ C = ₹8,50,000, \quad S = ₹50,000, \quad n = 10 \text{ years}, \quad t_{discard} = 7 \text{ years} $$ **Solution to Part (a):** **Step 1: Annual Depreciation (2 marks)** $$ D = \frac{C - S}{n} = \frac{8,50,000 - 50,000}{10} = \frac{8,00,000}{10} = ₹80,000 \text{ per year} $$ **Step 2: Book Value at End of Year 5 (3 marks)** $$ BV_5 = C - 5D = 8,50,000 - (5 \times 80,000) = 8,50,000 - 4,00,000 = ₹4,50,000 $$ **Step 3: Depreciation Rate (2 marks)** $$ d = \frac{D}{C} \times 100 = \frac{80,000}{8,50,000} \times 100 = 9.41\% \text{ per year} $$ **Solution to Part (b):** **Step 1: Book Value at End of Year 7 (2 marks)** $$ BV_7 = C - 7D = 8,50,000 - (7 \times 80,000) = 8,50,000 - 5,60,000 = ₹2,90,000 $$ **Step 2: Total Depreciation Charged Over 7 Years (2 marks)** $$ A_7 = 7 \times D = 7 \times 80,000 = ₹5,60,000 $$ **Step 3: Profit or Loss on Disposal (3 marks)** The compressor is *discarded* and the company realizes the salvage value of **₹50,000**. - **Book Value at disposal (₹2,90,000)** is **greater** than the realized value (₹50,000). - Therefore, the company incurs a **LOSS** on disposal. $$ \text{Loss on Disposal} = BV_7 - S = 2,90,000 - 50,000 = ₹2,40,000 $$ > **Note:** Even though the asset was originally planned for 10 years, premature disposal at year 7 results in a *book loss* of ₹2,40,000 — a common KTU viva question. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Forgetting the Salvage Value:** Many students write $D = C/n$ instead of $D = (C-S)/n$. KTU examiners *strictly deduct 1 mark* for this mistake. > 2. **Mixing SLM with WDV:** Some students apply the Written Down Value formula in SLM problems, which gives *declining* charges. SLM charges are *always constant*. > 3. **Rounding Errors in the Schedule:** When $D$ has a fractional value (e.g., ₹11,666.67), ensure you *carry full precision* to the last year. The final year's depreciation may need to be adjusted by ₹1–₹2 to make the closing BV exactly equal to $S$. > 4. **Sign of Loss/Profit on Disposal:** Be careful with the sign convention. If realized value > book value → *profit*; if realized value < book value → *loss*. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - ✅ **Straight Line Method (SLM)** = *Fixed Instalment Method* = *Original Cost Method* — all three names refer to the *same* depreciation technique. - ✅ The **core formula** is $D = (C - S)/n$ — charge this every year without change. - ✅ **Book value** decreases *linearly* from $C$ to $S$ over $n$ years. - ✅ **At mid-life** ($t = n/2$), the book value equals the *arithmetic mean* of $C$ and $S$: $BV = (C + S)/2$. - ✅ **Total accumulated depreciation** at the end of life always equals $C - S$. - ✅ SLM is **independent of actual usage** — it is a *time-based*, not a *use-based*, method. - ✅ The method is **silent on the time value of money** — a major conceptual limitation. - ✅ For KTU 14-mark problems, always prepare a **complete depreciation schedule** with 5 columns: Year, Opening BV, Depreciation, Accumulated Depreciation, Closing BV. - ✅ For **disposal problems**, compute the book value at the time of disposal and compare with the realized amount to determine profit/loss. - ✅ The **depreciation rate** under SLM is calculated *on the original cost* (not on diminishing book value). - ✅ For **partial year valuations** (e.g., $t = 3.5$ years), use the general formula $BV_t = C(1 - t/n) + S(t/n)$. - ✅ Preferred for **tax accounting** under the Income Tax Act for most asset classes in India. - ✅ The **straight-line graph** has slope $-D$ and intercepts $C$ (on Y-axis) and $n$ (on X-axis where BV would have hit zero if $S = 0$).

topics.title: Declining Balance Method
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT757 - ECONOMICS AND MANAGEMENT OF CHEMICAL INDUSTRIES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Declining Balance Method

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Declining Balance Method (DBM)** is an accelerated depreciation accounting technique in which a **fixed percentage (α)** is applied every year to the **remaining book value (undepreciated asset value)** of the asset, rather than to its original cost. The depreciation charge is therefore **largest in the first year** and **progressively decreases** over the asset's service life.

The method is governed by the **declining balance rate (α)**, which is a multiple of the **straight-line rate (1/N)**, where **N** is the estimated useful life of the asset in years.

### Standard Variants Used in Chemical Engineering Economics

| Variant | Declining Rate (α) | Common Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Double Declining Balance (DDB)** | α = 2/N | Most widely used in process industries |
| **150 % Declining Balance** | α = 1.5/N | Moderate acceleration |
| **200 % Declining Balance** | α = 2/N | Synonym of DDB |
| **Sum-of-Years' Digits (SOYD)** | Not DBM (related) | Hybrid accelerated method |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy 💡

> [!NOTE]
> **The Rusty Truck Analogy** 🚛
> Imagine you buy a chemical tanker truck for **₹20,00,000** that will last **5 years**. In the very first year, the truck suffers maximum wear — engine break-in, tyre wear, paint fading, exposure to corrosive chemicals. By year 5, it has already crossed the steepest "value-loss hill." 
> 
> The **Straight Line Method (SLM)** would charge equal ₹4,00,000 every year. But that feels unfair — your truck lost more value in year 1 than in year 5!
> 
> The **Declining Balance Method** recognises this by charging a **fixed % of the REMAINING value** each year. So you pay more depreciation when the asset is new, and less when it is old — just like how a new car loses 20% value the moment you drive it off the lot, but a 5-year-old car loses much less per year.

---

## 1.3 Why Chemical Industries Prefer DBM

Chemical plants contain **high-value process equipment** (reactors, distillation columns, heat exchangers, compressors) that:
- Suffer **maximum efficiency loss in early years** (fouling, scaling, catalyst deactivation),
- Generate **higher revenue in initial years** when the product is competitively priced,
- Need **faster tax shields** through accelerated depreciation (as permitted under the **Indian Companies Act, 2013** and **Income Tax Act, 1961** for plant & machinery).

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** DBM in process industries is often linked to **MACRS (Modified Accelerated Cost Recovery System)** under U.S. tax law for chemical assets classified under 5-year, 7-year, or 15-year property classes.

---

## 1.4 Standard Metrics (Bolded Constants)

- **Declining balance rate** = **α** (dimensionless, usually between **0** and **1**)
- **Useful life** = **N years**
- **Original cost (P)** measured in **₹ (INR)** or **$ (USD)**
- **Book Value (BV)** = the **undepreciated value at the start of a year**
- **Salvage Value (S)** = estimated **end-of-life scrap/residual value**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📐 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic — How DBM Works

The method is built on **three governing principles**:

1. **Fixed Percentage, Shrinking Base** — The same rate **α** is applied every year, but to a *continuously reducing* book value. This is the **"compounding decay"** nature of DBM.
2. **Asset Never Fully Depreciates Mathematically** — Under pure DBM, the book value asymptotically approaches **zero but never equals zero**. In practice, depreciation stops when BV reaches the **salvage value S**.
3. **Acceleration over SLM** — The rate **α** is always **greater than 1/N**, ensuring depreciation in the early years exceeds straight-line depreciation.

---

## 2.2 Mathematical Foundation (Why It Works)

Let:
- **P** = Original (installed) cost of the asset
- **N** = Useful life in years  
- **α** = Declining balance rate (a constant, 0 < α < 1)
- **BVₙ** = Book Value at the **end** of year n
- **Dₙ** = Depreciation expense in year n

The **recursive relationship** is:

$$
BV_{n} \;=\; BV_{n-1} \,(1 - \alpha) \quad \text{for } n = 1, 2, 3, \dots
$$

with the **initial condition**: $BV_0 = P$.

By unrolling the recursion (geometric series), we obtain the **closed-form expression**:

$$
BV_{n} \;=\; P \,(1 - \alpha)^{n}
$$

The depreciation in year n is therefore:

$$
D_{n} \;=\; BV_{n-1} \,-\, BV_{n} \;=\; P\,\alpha\,(1-\alpha)^{\,n-1}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Geometric Decay Insight:** The depreciation charges form a **geometric progression** with common ratio **(1 − α)**. The first-year depreciation is the largest, equal to **αP**.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet 📋

| # | Quantity | Formula | Remarks / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Declining balance rate (DDB) | $\alpha = \dfrac{2}{N}$ | Dimensionless; N in years |
| 2 | Declining balance rate (150% DB) | $\alpha = \dfrac{1.5}{N}$ | Slower acceleration than DDB |
| 3 | Book value at end of year n | $BV_{n} = P\,(1-\alpha)^{n}$ | ₹ or $ |
| 4 | Depreciation in year n | $D_{n} = P\,\alpha\,(1-\alpha)^{n-1}$ | ₹ or $ per year |
| 5 | Cumulative depreciation up to year n | $\sum_{k=1}^{n} D_{k} \;=\; P\Big[1-(1-\alpha)^{n}\Big]$ | ₹ or $ |
| 6 | Comparison with SLM rate | $d_{SL} = \dfrac{1}{N} \quad ; \quad \alpha = k \cdot d_{SL}$ | $k = 1.5$ or $2$ |
| 7 | Year in which BV = Salvage S | $n^{*} = \dfrac{\ln(S/P)}{\ln(1-\alpha)}$ | Switch to straight line below S |
| 8 | Total depreciation over N years | $P\big[1-(1-\alpha)^{N}\big]$ | Approaches P only as N → ∞ |

> [!WARNING]
> **CRITICAL: Use `\vert` or `\mid` for absolute values inside tables** (NOT the pipe symbol `|`) — to preserve markdown table syntax integrity as per KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 protocol.

---

## 2.4 Selection Logic — When to Use DBM

A process engineer or cost accountant chooses DBM when **at least one** of the following is true:

- The asset suffers **above-average wear and tear in early years** (e.g., **rotating equipment** like centrifugal pumps, turbines).
- Technological obsolescence is likely (e.g., **PLC control systems, specialised reactors**).
- The company wants to **maximise early tax deductions** (especially under **MACRS** or **Indian IT Act Section 32**).
- The asset generates **higher cash flows in early years** (matching principle of accounting).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🔢 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Closed-Form Book Value Equation

**Starting point:** The recursive depreciation relation is given by

$$
BV_{n} \;=\; BV_{n-1}\,(1-\alpha)
$$

**Step 1 — Substitute n = 1:**

$$
BV_{1} \;=\; BV_{0}\,(1-\alpha) \;=\; P\,(1-\alpha)
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute n = 2:**

$$
BV_{2} \;=\; BV_{1}\,(1-\alpha) \;=\; P\,(1-\alpha)\,(1-\alpha) \;=\; P\,(1-\alpha)^{2}
$$

**Step 3 — Generalise by induction.** Assume

$$
BV_{n-1} \;=\; P\,(1-\alpha)^{n-1}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply the recurrence:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
BV_{n} &\;=\; BV_{n-1}\,(1-\alpha) \\
&\;=\; P\,(1-\alpha)^{n-1}\,(1-\alpha) \\
&\;=\; P\,(1-\alpha)^{n}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** By the principle of mathematical induction,

$$
\boxed{\,BV_{n} \;=\; P\,(1-\alpha)^{n}\,}
$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Depreciation in Year n

Depreciation in any year is the **decrease in book value** during that year.

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_{n} &\;=\; BV_{n-1} \,-\, BV_{n} \\
&\;=\; P\,(1-\alpha)^{n-1} \,-\, P\,(1-\alpha)^{n} \\
&\;=\; P\,(1-\alpha)^{n-1}\,\big[\,1 - (1-\alpha)\,\big] \\
&\;=\; P\,\alpha\,(1-\alpha)^{n-1}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Verification for n = 1:** $D_1 = P \cdot \alpha \cdot (1-\alpha)^0 = \alpha P$ ✔ (matches intuition)

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Cumulative Depreciation (Sum of Geometric Series)

Sum depreciation charges from year 1 to year n:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum_{k=1}^{n} D_{k} &\;=\; \sum_{k=1}^{n} P\,\alpha\,(1-\alpha)^{k-1} \\
&\;=\; P\,\alpha \sum_{k=0}^{n-1} (1-\alpha)^{k}
\end{aligned}
$$

Using the geometric series identity

$$
\sum_{k=0}^{n-1} r^{k} \;=\; \frac{1-r^{n}}{1-r} \quad \text{for } r \neq 1
$$

with $r = (1-\alpha)$, we get:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum_{k=1}^{n} D_{k} &\;=\; P\,\alpha \cdot \frac{1 - (1-\alpha)^{n}}{1 - (1-\alpha)} \\
&\;=\; P\,\alpha \cdot \frac{1 - (1-\alpha)^{n}}{\alpha} \\
&\;=\; P\,\big[\,1 - (1-\alpha)^{n}\,\big]
\end{aligned}
$$

Hence the **book value identity check**:

$$
BV_{n} \;=\; P \;-\; \sum_{k=1}^{n} D_{k} \;=\; P\,(1-\alpha)^{n} \;\;\checkmark
$$

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — Double Declining Balance

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Example for KTU Examination Practice**
> 
> A chemical company installs a **stainless-steel batch reactor** at a cost of **P = ₹10,00,000**. The reactor has a useful life of **N = 5 years** and an estimated salvage value of **S = ₹77,760**. Compute the depreciation schedule using the **Double Declining Balance Method**.

**Step 1 — Compute the DDB rate:**

$$
\alpha \;=\; \frac{2}{N} \;=\; \frac{2}{5} \;=\; 0.40 \quad \text{or} \quad 40\% \text{ per annum}
$$

**Step 2 — Build the depreciation table.**

We apply the formulas:

- $D_n = \alpha \cdot BV_{n-1}$
- $BV_n = BV_{n-1} - D_n$

| Year (n) | BV at Start (₹) | Depreciation Rate (α) | Depreciation Dₙ (₹) | BV at End (₹) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 10,00,000 | 0.40 | 0.40 × 10,00,000 = **4,00,000** | 6,00,000 |
| 2 | 6,00,000 | 0.40 | 0.40 × 6,00,000 = **2,40,000** | 3,60,000 |
| 3 | 3,60,000 | 0.40 | 0.40 × 3,60,000 = **1,44,000** | 2,16,000 |
| 4 | 2,16,000 | 0.40 | 0.40 × 2,16,000 = **86,400** | 1,29,600 |
| 5 | 1,29,600 | 0.40 | 0.40 × 1,29,600 = **51,840** | 77,760 |
| **Total** | — | — | **9,22,240** | **77,760 (Salvage)** |

**Step 3 — Verification of the closed-form formula for n = 5:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
BV_5 &\;=\; P\,(1-\alpha)^{5} \\
&\;=\; 10{,}00{,}000 \times (1 - 0.40)^{5} \\
&\;=\; 10{,}00{,}000 \times (0.60)^{5} \\
&\;=\; 10{,}00{,}000 \times 0.07776 \\
&\;=\; 77{,}760 \;\; \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

The book value at the end of year 5 equals the **salvage value S exactly**, confirming our calculation.

---

## 3.5 Comparative Analytical Matrix — DBM vs. SLM

For the same asset (P = ₹10,00,000, N = 5 years, S = 0):

| Year | SLM Depreciation (₹) | DDB Depreciation (₹) | Cumulative SLM (₹) | Cumulative DDB (₹) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 2,00,000 | 4,00,000 | 2,00,000 | 4,00,000 |
| 2 | 2,00,000 | 2,40,000 | 4,00,000 | 6,40,000 |
| 3 | 2,00,000 | 1,44,000 | 6,00,000 | 7,84,000 |
| 4 | 2,00,000 | 86,400 | 8,00,000 | 8,70,400 |
| 5 | 2,00,000 | 51,840 | 10,00,000 | 9,22,240 |
| 6 (if extended) | 2,00,000 (if S=0) | 31,104 | 12,00,000 | 9,53,344 |
| 7 (if extended) | 2,00,000 | 18,662 | 14,00,000 | 9,72,006 |

> [!TIP]
> **Key Insight:** Under DDB, the asset **never fully depreciates** in the closed-form model — the residual book value is $P(1-\alpha)^N$. The depreciation simply **stops** once the book value reaches the salvage value S.

---

## 3.6 Derivation 4 — Finding the Year of Switch-Over to Salvage

If the asset has a non-zero salvage value S, the year **n\*** at which book value just reaches S is found by:

$$
S \;=\; P\,(1-\alpha)^{n^{*}}
$$

Solving for n*:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{S}{P} &\;=\; (1-\alpha)^{n^{*}} \\
\ln\!\left(\frac{S}{P}\right) &\;=\; n^{*} \ln(1-\alpha) \\
n^{*} &\;=\; \frac{\ln(S/P)}{\ln(1-\alpha)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Numerical Check** (using our example, S = 77,760):

$$
n^{*} \;=\; \frac{\ln(77{,}760/10{,}00{,}000)}{\ln(0.60)} \;=\; \frac{\ln(0.07776)}{\ln(0.60)} \;=\; \frac{-2.5541}{-0.5108} \;\approx\; 5.00 \;\;\checkmark
$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 🗺️ SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Declining Balance Method

> [!NOTE]
> The following Mermaid **flowchart** maps the complete computational logic of the DBM algorithm — from initial cost input to final book value tracking. This represents the **Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow** as per the KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 fallback protocol.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Phase: Asset Acquisition] --> B[Original Cost P]
    A --> C[Useful Life N years]
    A --> D[Salvage Value S]
    A --> E[Acceleration Factor k = 1.5 or 2.0]

    B --> F[Compute Declining Rate alpha]
    C --> F
    E --> F
    F --> G[alpha = k divided by N]

    G --> H[Initialise Year Counter n = 1]
    H --> I[Set BV0 = P]

    I --> J{Year Loop Start}
    J --> K[Compute Dn = alpha times BV n-1]
    K --> L[Compute BVn = BV n-1 minus Dn]

    L --> M{Check: BVn greater than S?}
    M -->|Yes| N[Record Dn and BVn in Schedule]
    M -->|No| O[Adjust Dn: Dn = BV n-1 minus S]
    O --> P[Set BVn = S, Stop Depreciation]

    N --> Q{n equals N?}
    Q -->|No| R[Increment n = n plus 1]
    R --> J
    Q -->|Yes| S[Generate Final Depreciation Schedule]

    S --> T[Output: Year-wise Dn and BVn]
    S --> U[Output: Total Depreciation]
    S --> V[Output: Tax Shield Benefit]

    T --> W[Decision: DBM vs SLM Comparison]
    U --> W
    V --> W
    W --> X[End of Depreciation Analysis]

    style A fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#1e3a8a,color:#ffffff
    style F fill:#0e7490,stroke:#0e7490,color:#ffffff
    style K fill:#7c2d12,stroke:#7c2d12,color:#ffffff
    style M fill:#854d0e,stroke:#854d0e,color:#ffffff
    style W fill:#14532d,stroke:#14532d,color:#ffffff
    style X fill:#1f2937,stroke:#1f2937,color:#ffffff
```

---

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Decision Logic for Choosing DBM

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[New Process Asset Acquired] --> B{Is asset subject to rapid obsolescence?}
    B -->|Yes| C{Does early-year tax shield matter?}
    B -->|No| D[Use Straight Line Method]

    C -->|Yes| E[Use Double Declining Balance]
    C -->|No| F{Is wear pattern moderate?}
    F -->|Yes| G[Use 150 percent Declining Balance]
    F -->|No| D

    E --> H[Document alpha = 2 over N]
    G --> I[Document alpha = 1.5 over N]
    H --> J[Compute Year-wise Depreciation]
    I --> J
    D --> K[Compute Equal Annual Depreciation]
    J --> L[Generate Audit-Ready Schedule]
    K --> L

    style A fill:#0c4a6e,stroke:#0c4a6e,color:#ffffff
    style E fill:#7f1d1d,stroke:#7f1d1d,color:#ffffff
    style G fill:#92400e,stroke:#92400e,color:#ffffff
    style D fill:#365314,stroke:#365314,color:#ffffff
    style L fill:#1e293b,stroke:#1e293b,color:#ffffff
```

---

## 4.3 Schematic — Book Value Decay Curve (DDB vs SLM)

> [!NOTE]
> The following **Gantt-style schematic** visualises how book value evolves over time under both methods. Notice the **steeper early decline** under DDB and the **linear descent** under SLM.

```mermaid
gantt
    title Book Value Decay Trajectory: DDB vs SLM (P = 10L, N = 5 yrs)
    dateFormat YYYY
    axisFormat %Y

    section Straight Line Method
    Year 1: a1, 2024, 1y
    Year 2: a2, 2025, 1y
    Year 3: a3, 2026, 1y
    Year 4: a4, 2027, 1y
    Year 5: a5, 2028, 1y

    section Double Declining Balance
    Year 1 DDB: b1, 2024, 1y
    Year 2 DDB: b2, 2025, 1y
    Year 3 DDB: b3, 2026, 1y
    Year 4 DDB: b4, 2027, 1y
    Year 5 DDB: b5, 2028, 1y
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 🎯 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define the Declining Balance Method of depreciation. How does it differ from the Straight Line Method?**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> 
> **Definition (2 marks):** The Declining Balance Method (DBM) is a depreciation technique in which a **fixed percentage (α) of the diminishing book value** is charged as depreciation every year. The book value reduces geometrically according to the relation $BV_n = P(1-\alpha)^n$.
> 
> **Distinction from SLM (1 mark):** Unlike SLM, which charges a **uniform depreciation** of P/N each year, DBM charges **larger depreciation in early years** and progressively smaller amounts in later years, making it an **accelerated** method.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**A heat exchanger costs ₹5,00,000 with a useful life of 10 years. Calculate the depreciation in the first year using the Double Declining Balance Method.**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> 
> **Step 1 (1 mark):** DDB rate: 
> 
> $$\alpha = \frac{2}{N} = \frac{2}{10} = 0.20 \;\;\text{or}\;\; 20\%$$
> 
> **Step 2 (1 mark):** First-year depreciation: 
> 
> $$D_1 = \alpha \times P = 0.20 \times 5{,}00{,}000 = 1{,}00{,}000$$
> 
> **Step 3 (1 mark):** 
> 
> $$\boxed{D_1 = ₹1{,}00{,}000}$$
> 
> Book value at end of Year 1 = ₹4,00,000.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - **Common Mistake #1:** Students often apply the rate α to the **original cost** every year (this is SLM-like, not DBM). Always apply α to the **previous book value**.
> - **Common Mistake #2:** Forgetting that under pure DBM, the asset **never mathematically reaches zero book value** — depreciation must be **stopped at salvage value**.
> - **Common Mistake #3:** Mixing up **DDB (α = 2/N)** with **150% DB (α = 1.5/N)** — read the question carefully.
> - **Mark Loss:** Failing to show the **formula, substitution, and result** separately costs **1 mark per sub-part**.

---

### ⭐ Question A (Choice 1) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**A process plant installs a centrifugal compressor at a cost of ₹8,00,000. The useful life is 6 years and the salvage value is estimated at ₹2,00,000.**

**(a)** Compute the **declining balance rate** and prepare a **complete depreciation schedule** using the **Double Declining Balance Method** until the book value equals the salvage value. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare the **total depreciation** under DDB with that under the **Straight Line Method** for the same asset. State which method provides a **greater tax shield** in the first three years. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Question A

**Part (a) — DDB Schedule (7 marks)**

> **Step 1 — Compute the DDB rate (1 mark):**
> 
> $$\alpha = \frac{2}{N} = \frac{2}{6} = 0.3333... \;\;\text{or}\;\; 33.33\%$$
> 
> **Step 2 — Apply recursively using Dₙ = α·BVₙ₋₁ (5 marks):**

| Year (n) | BV at Start (₹) | Rate α | Depreciation Dₙ (₹) | BV at End (₹) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 8,00,000 | 0.3333 | 2,66,667 | 5,33,333 |
| 2 | 5,33,333 | 0.3333 | 1,77,778 | 3,55,556 |
| 3 | 3,55,556 | 0.3333 | 1,18,519 | 2,37,037 |
| 4 | 2,37,037 | 0.3333 | 79,012 | 1,58,025 |
| 5 | 1,58,025 | 0.3333 | *(depreciation stopped at salvage)* | **2,00,000 (S)** |

> **Step 3 — Note the switch-over (1 mark):** In year 4, the calculated BV (= 1,58,025) is **below** the salvage value (2,00,000). So depreciation is adjusted in year 4 to bring BV = S exactly. The depreciation in year 4 becomes:
> 
> $$D_4 = BV_3 - S = 2{,}37{,}037 - 2{,}00{,}000 = ₹37{,}037$$
> 
> **Final BV = ₹2,00,000 (Salvage Value) ✔**

**Part (b) — Comparison with SLM (7 marks)**

> **Step 1 — SLM depreciation (2 marks):**
> 
> $$D_{SL} = \frac{P - S}{N} = \frac{8{,}00{,}000 - 2{,}00{,}000}{6} = ₹1{,}00{,}000 \text{ per year (uniform)}$$
> 
> **Step 2 — Year-by-year comparison table (3 marks):**

| Year | DDB Depreciation (₹) | SLM Depreciation (₹) | Difference (DDB − SLM) (₹) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 2,66,667 | 1,00,000 | **+1,66,667** |
| 2 | 1,77,778 | 1,00,000 | **+77,778** |
| 3 | 1,18,519 | 1,00,000 | **+18,519** |
| 4 | 37,037 (adjusted) | 1,00,000 | −62,963 |
| 5 | 0 | 1,00,000 | −1,00,000 |
| 6 | 0 | 1,00,000 | −1,00,000 |
| **Total** | **6,00,000** | **6,00,000** | **0** |

> **Step 3 — Tax shield analysis (2 marks):** 
> 
> Assuming a corporate tax rate of **t = 30%**, the tax shield in any year is **t × Dₙ**. Summing for the first 3 years:
> 
> $$\text{Tax Shield}_{DDB} = 0.30 \times (2{,}66{,}667 + 1{,}77{,}778 + 1{,}18{,}519) = ₹1{,}68{,}889$$
> 
> $$\text{Tax Shield}_{SLM} = 0.30 \times 3 \times 1{,}00{,}000 = ₹90{,}000$$
> 
> $$\text{Extra Tax Shield via DDB} = ₹1{,}68{,}889 - ₹90{,}000 = ₹78{,}889$$
> 
> **Conclusion (1 mark):** The **DDB method provides a significantly larger tax shield (₹78,889 extra)** in the first three years, making it financially advantageous for the chemical company in the early years of asset life.

---

### ⭐ Question B (Choice 2) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**A distillation column costs ₹15,00,000 with an estimated useful life of 8 years. The company wants to use the 150% Declining Balance Method.**

**(a)** Derive the **general formula** for the book value at the end of the **n-th year** and compute the book value at the end of year 4. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** If the salvage value after 8 years is ₹3,00,000, determine in which year the depreciation should be **stopped or adjusted** so that the book value does not fall below S. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Question B

**Part (a) — General Derivation and BV₄ Calculation (7 marks)**

> **Step 1 — Identify the declining rate (2 marks):**
> 
> For **150% Declining Balance**:
> 
> $$\alpha = \frac{1.5}{N} = \frac{1.5}{8} = 0.1875 \;\;\text{or}\;\; 18.75\%$$
> 
> **Step 2 — General formula (2 marks):** Using the derived closed-form:
> 
> $$\boxed{BV_n = P\,(1-\alpha)^{n} = 15{,}00{,}000 \times (0.8125)^{n}}$$
> 
> **Step 3 — Compute BV₄ explicitly (3 marks):**
> 
> $$
> \begin{aligned}
> BV_4 &\;=\; 15{,}00{,}000 \times (0.8125)^{4} \\
> &\;=\; 15{,}00{,}000 \times 0.43618 \\
> &\;=\; ₹6{,}54{,}272 \text{ (approximately)}
> \end{aligned}
> $$
> 
> **Verification via year-by-year (bonus 1 mark):**
> - D₁ = 0.1875 × 15,00,000 = 2,81,250 → BV₁ = 12,18,750
> - D₂ = 0.1875 × 12,18,750 = 2,28,516 → BV₂ = 9,90,234
> - D₃ = 0.1875 × 9,90,234 = 1,85,669 → BV₃ = 8,04,565
> - D₄ = 0.1875 × 8,04,565 = 1,50,856 → BV₄ = 6,53,709 (≈ 6,54,272, minor rounding) ✔

**Part (b) — Year of Switch-Over to Salvage (7 marks)**

> **Step 1 — Find the year n* where BV reaches salvage (3 marks):**
> 
> $$S = P\,(1-\alpha)^{n^{*}} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; 3{,}00{,}000 = 15{,}00{,}000 \times (0.8125)^{n^{*}}$$
> 
> $$0.20 = (0.8125)^{n^{*}}$$
> 
> Taking logarithms:
> 
> $$
> \begin{aligned}
> n^{*} &\;=\; \frac{\ln(0.20)}{\ln(0.8125)} \\
> &\;=\; \frac{-1.6094}{-0.2076} \\
> &\;\approx\; 7.75 \text{ years}
> \end{aligned}
> $$
> 
> **Step 2 — Interpret the result (2 marks):** 
> 
> The book value falls to the salvage value between **year 7 and year 8**. Specifically:
> - At end of year 7: 
> 
> $$BV_7 = 15{,}00{,}000 \times (0.8125)^{7} ≈ ₹3{,}70{,}484$$
> 
> - At end of year 8 (without adjustment): 
> 
> $$BV_8 = 15{,}00{,}000 \times (0.8125)^{8} ≈ ₹3{,}01{,}018$$
> 
> **Step 3 — Adjust depreciation in year 8 (2 marks):** Since BV₈ ≈ ₹3,01,018 is **slightly above** S = ₹3,00,000, the year-8 depreciation is acceptable as calculated. If it had dipped below, the depreciation would be capped as:
> 
> $$D_8 = BV_7 - S = 3{,}70{,}484 - 3{,}00{,}000 = ₹70{,}484 \text{ (final year's adjusted charge)}$$
> 
> **Final book value at end of year 8 = ₹3,00,000 (Salvage Value) ✔**

---

## 5.3 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Definition:** DBM charges a **fixed rate (α) on the diminishing book value** every year.
- ✅ **Core formula:** $BV_n = P(1-\alpha)^n$ — a **geometric decay** model.
- ✅ **Depreciation charge:** $D_n = \alpha \cdot BV_{n-1} = P \cdot \alpha \cdot (1-\alpha)^{n-1}$.
- ✅ **Double Declining Balance rate:** $\alpha = \dfrac{2}{N}$ — the **most common** variant.
- ✅ **150% Declining Balance rate:** $\alpha = \dfrac{1.5}{N}$ — moderate acceleration.
- ✅ **Salvage value handling:** Depreciation must be **stopped/adjusted** when BV reaches S — pure DBM never mathematically reaches zero.
- ✅ **Accelerated method:** Early-year depreciation is **higher** than late-year — aligns with **matching principle** in accounting.
- ✅ **Switch-over year formula:** $n^* = \dfrac{\ln(S/P)}{\ln(1-\alpha)}$.
- ✅ **Tax shield advantage:** Provides a **larger tax shield in early years** compared to SLM.
- ✅ **Industrial relevance:** Preferred for **rotating equipment, control systems, and tech-obsolete assets** in chemical plants.
- ✅ **Regulatory context:** Compliant with **Indian IT Act Section 32** (additional depreciation) and **MACRS** (U.S. tax code).
- ✅ **Common pitfall:** Never apply α to the **original cost** every year — always to the **previous book value**.
- ✅ **Geometric series check:** $\sum_{k=1}^{n} D_k = P\big[1-(1-\alpha)^n\big]$ — used to verify cumulative depreciation.
- ✅ **Comparison metric:** $\alpha_{DDB} = 2 \times \alpha_{SL}$ — DDB is exactly **twice as fast** as SLM in year 1.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Double Declining Balance Method
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT757 - ECONOMICS AND MANAGEMENT OF CHEMICAL INDUSTRIES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Double Declining Balance Method

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Double Declining Balance (DDB) Method**, also known as the **200% Declining Balance Method**, is an accelerated depreciation accounting technique widely adopted in chemical process industries for capital assets such as reactors, distillation columns, heat exchangers, and compressor units. Under this method, depreciation is computed by applying a fixed percentage rate (equal to twice the straight-line rate) to the **declining book value** of the asset at the beginning of each accounting year, rather than to its original cost.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PECHT757):**
> "Double Declining Balance Method is a depreciation technique where a constant depreciation rate, double the straight-line rate, is applied each year to the remaining book value of the asset, resulting in higher depreciation charges in earlier years and progressively lower charges in later years."

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain English Intuition

Imagine you have just purchased a brand-new chemical mixing tank for your plant. The very moment it is installed and put into service, it begins losing value — not because it physically breaks, but because it becomes slightly less "new" each year. Now consider this real-world fact: **a brand-new piece of equipment loses its value fastest in its first year of life**, and the rate of loss slows down as the equipment ages (similar to how a new car depreciates about 20% the moment you drive it off the lot, but only a few percent in its fifth year).

The **Double Declining Balance Method** captures exactly this real-world intuition. Instead of depreciating the asset uniformly over its lifetime (like Straight-Line Method does), it charges **double the normal rate** in every year — but applies it to a shrinking base (the book value, which keeps reducing each year). This creates a "double decline" effect on the book value curve, hence the name.

### Real-World Plant Example
Consider a **shell-and-tube heat exchanger** used in a sulphuric acid plant:
- **Cost:** ₹10,00,000
- **Useful Life:** 10 years
- **Salvage Value:** ₹1,00,000

In a corrosive chemical environment, this exchanger loses its functional efficiency most rapidly in the early years due to fouling, scaling, and corrosion initiation. The DDB method matches this physical reality by allocating higher depreciation (and hence higher maintenance cost recovery) in those early years.

## 1.3 Key Technical Features (Highlighted)

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Distinguishing Features of DDB Method:**
> - The **salvage value is IGNORED** during the annual depreciation calculation (unlike the Straight-Line Method). It is only used as a **floor limit** — book value can never drop below salvage value.
> - The **depreciation rate is CONSTANT** (equal to 2/n), but the **depreciation amount DECREASES every year** because it is applied to a progressively smaller book value.
> - It is classified as an **accelerated depreciation method**, providing **tax shield benefits** in the early years of an asset's life.
> - It is also called the **Constant Percentage Method** or **Mathematical Declining Balance Method** (when the rate is not exactly 200%).

## 1.4 Classification of Depreciation Methods (Context Map)

| Category | Method | Depreciation Pattern |
|---|---|---|
| Straight (Uniform) | Straight-Line Method | Equal annual depreciation |
| Accelerated | Double Declining Balance | High → Low depreciation |
| Accelerated | Sum-of-Years' Digits | High → Low depreciation |
| Accelerated | Written Down Value (WDV) | High → Low depreciation |

The DDB method falls under the **accelerated depreciation** family, alongside the **Sum-of-Years' Digits** method (which will be covered later in Module 1).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Underlying Logic — Step-by-Step Theoretical Breakdown

The DDB method operates on five foundational logical pillars. Understanding these is critical for solving KTU board exam problems.

### Pillar 1: Computation of the Constant Depreciation Rate (α)

The DDB rate is derived by **doubling** the reciprocal of the useful life of the asset. This is why the method is called "Double Declining Balance."

$$\alpha = \frac{2}{n}$$

where:
- $\alpha$ = Double Declining Balance rate (expressed as a decimal fraction, e.g., 0.20 for 20%)
- $n$ = Useful life of the asset in years

### Pillar 2: Annual Depreciation Formula

Each year, depreciation is calculated by multiplying the **beginning-of-year book value** by the rate $\alpha$.

$$D_k = \alpha \times BV_{k-1}$$

where:
- $D_k$ = Depreciation expense in year $k$
- $BV_{k-1}$ = Book value at the beginning of year $k$ (i.e., end of year $k-1$)

### Pillar 3: Book Value Recursion (The "Declining" Mechanism)

The book value reduces each year by the depreciation amount of that year:

$$BV_k = BV_{k-1} - D_k = BV_{k-1}(1 - \alpha)$$

This recursive formula can be expanded explicitly for any year $k$:

$$BV_k = C \times (1 - \alpha)^k$$

where $C$ is the original cost of the asset.

### Pillar 4: The Salvage Value Floor Rule

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Salvage Value Rule for DDB Method:**
> The salvage value is **NOT used** in computing $\alpha$ or $D_k$. However, the asset's book value **CANNOT be reduced below the salvage value**. In the final year(s), depreciation is limited so that $BV_k \geq S$, where $S$ is the salvage value.

Mathematically, the depreciation in the last year is adjusted as:

$$D_{\text{last}} = BV_{\text{before last year}} - S$$

### Pillar 5: Why "Double" and Not Other Multiples?

The choice of the multiplier "2" (giving 200%) is conventional and produces depreciation charges that are roughly twice the straight-line charges in the early years. In some jurisdictions and accounting frameworks, **150% Declining Balance** is also used (multiplier = 1.5), but KTU 2024 syllabus specifically emphasizes the **Double (200%) Declining Balance Method**.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Symbol Description | Boundary / Special Condition |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\alpha = \dfrac{2}{n}$ | $\alpha$ = DDB rate, $n$ = useful life (years) | $n \geq 1$ integer |
| 2 | $D_k = \alpha \times BV_{k-1}$ | $D_k$ = depreciation in year $k$, $BV_{k-1}$ = beginning book value | Applied for $k = 1, 2, ..., n$ |
| 3 | $BV_k = BV_{k-1} - D_k$ | Recursion for book value at end of year $k$ | $BV_0 = C$ (original cost) |
| 4 | $BV_k = C(1 - \alpha)^k$ | Closed-form book value expression | Valid when no salvage limit reached |
| 5 | $BV_k \geq S$ | Salvage value floor constraint | Triggers adjustment in final year |
| 6 | $D_{\text{final}} = BV_{\text{prev}} - S$ | Adjusted final-year depreciation | Only when BV would otherwise fall below S |
| 7 | Total depreciation $= C - S$ | Sum of all annual depreciations | Independent of method (bookkeeping identity) |
| 8 | $\sum_{k=1}^{n} D_k = C - BV_n$ | Cumulative depreciation check | Used for verification in numericals |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Valuation Pitfall:** Students frequently make the mistake of subtracting the salvage value from the cost BEFORE computing the rate. **Do NOT do this for DDB.** The correct approach is: rate uses ONLY $n$, and salvage value acts only as a **floor limit** during year-wise computation.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility in Chemical Industries

The Double Declining Balance Method is extensively used in chemical process industries for the following practical reasons:

- **Rapid Technology Obsolescence:** Chemical process equipment (e.g., membrane separation units, advanced control systems) often becomes obsolete within 5-7 years due to technological advances. DDB matches this shorter economic life.
- **Higher Maintenance in Later Years:** As equipment ages, maintenance costs typically rise. Accelerated early depreciation (via DDB) offsets these later maintenance costs, keeping the total annual cost relatively stable — an application of the **Total Cost of Ownership (TCO)** principle.
- **Tax Shield Maximization:** Since depreciation is a tax-deductible expense, accelerating depreciation in early years provides higher tax savings earlier, improving the project's Net Present Value (NPV).
- **Conservative Asset Reporting:** It results in lower book values in early years, presenting a more conservative financial position to investors and lenders.

## 2.4 Comparison: DDB vs. Straight-Line Method (SLM)

| Parameter | Straight-Line Method (SLM) | Double Declining Balance (DDB) |
|---|---|---|
| Rate basis | $(C - S) / n$ | $2/n$ (salvage ignored) |
| Annual depreciation | Constant | Decreasing |
| Book value pattern | Linear decline | Exponential decline |
| Salvage value | Used in formula | Used only as floor |
| Tax shield timing | Spread uniformly | Front-loaded |
| Best suited for | Buildings, long-life assets | Equipment, short-life assets |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Exhaustive Worked Example — KTU Numerical Pattern

> **Problem Statement (Modeled on KTU 2024 Pattern):**
> A pharmaceutical company installs a **fluidized bed dryer** for API (Active Pharmaceutical Ingredient) manufacturing. The equipment details are:
> - **Original Cost (C):** ₹8,00,000
> - **Useful Life (n):** 5 years
> - **Salvage Value (S):** ₹50,000
>
> **Required:**
> (a) Calculate the depreciation rate using the Double Declining Balance Method.
> (b) Prepare the depreciation schedule for all 5 years.
> (c) Determine the book value at the end of year 3.

### 3.1.1 Part (a): Calculation of Depreciation Rate

**Step 1 — Identify the given parameters.**

From the problem:

$$C = ₹8{,}00{,}000, \quad n = 5 \text{ years}, \quad S = ₹50{,}000$$

**Step 2 — Apply the DDB rate formula.**

$$\alpha = \frac{2}{n}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the value of $n = 5$.**

$$\alpha = \frac{2}{5} = 0.40$$

**Step 4 — Express as a percentage.**

$$\alpha = 0.40 \times 100\% = 40\%$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Result:** The Double Declining Balance depreciation rate is **40% per annum**. Note that the salvage value of ₹50,000 is **NOT** used in computing this rate.

---

### 3.1.2 Part (b): Depreciation Schedule (Year-by-Year)

We will now compute the depreciation and book value for each year using the recursive formulas.

**Step 5 — Initialize Year 1.**

For Year 1, the beginning book value is the original cost:

$$BV_0 = C = ₹8{,}00{,}000$$

Apply the depreciation formula:

$$D_1 = \alpha \times BV_0 = 0.40 \times 8{,}00{,}000$$

$$\boxed{D_1 = ₹3{,}20{,}000}$$

Compute end-of-year book value:

$$BV_1 = BV_0 - D_1 = 8{,}00{,}000 - 3{,}20{,}000$$

$$\boxed{BV_1 = ₹4{,}80{,}000}$$

**Step 6 — Compute Year 2.**

Beginning book value:

$$BV_1 = ₹4{,}80{,}000$$

Depreciation in Year 2:

$$D_2 = 0.40 \times 4{,}80{,}000 = ₹1{,}92{,}000$$

End-of-year book value:

$$BV_2 = 4{,}80{,}000 - 1{,}92{,}000 = ₹2{,}88{,}000$$

**Step 7 — Compute Year 3.**

Beginning book value:

$$BV_2 = ₹2{,}88{,}000$$

Depreciation in Year 3:

$$D_3 = 0.40 \times 2{,}88{,}000 = ₹1{,}15{,}200$$

End-of-year book value:

$$BV_3 = 2{,}88{,}000 - 1{,}15{,}200 = ₹1{,}72{,}800$$

**Step 8 — Compute Year 4.**

Beginning book value:

$$BV_3 = ₹1{,}72{,}800$$

Depreciation in Year 4:

$$D_4 = 0.40 \times 1{,}72{,}800 = ₹69{,}120$$

End-of-year book value:

$$BV_4 = 1{,}72{,}800 - 69{,}120 = ₹1{,}03{,}680$$

**Step 9 — Compute Year 5 (Final Year with Salvage Adjustment).**

If we naively apply the 40% rate:

$$D_5^{\text{naive}} = 0.40 \times 1{,}03{,}680 = ₹41{,}472$$

Resulting book value would be:

$$BV_5^{\text{naive}} = 1{,}03{,}680 - 41{,}472 = ₹62{,}208$$

But we must check: $BV_5^{\text{naive}} = ₹62{,}208 > S = ₹50{,}000$, so the salvage floor is **not** triggered. The naive calculation is acceptable.

$$\boxed{D_5 = ₹41{,}472, \quad BV_5 = ₹62{,}208}$$

**Step 10 — Verification using total depreciation identity.**

Sum of all depreciations:

$$\sum D_k = 3{,}20{,}000 + 1{,}92{,}000 + 1{,}15{,}200 + 69{,}120 + 41{,}472$$

$$\sum D_k = ₹7{,}37{,}792$$

Check:

$$C - BV_5 = 8{,}00{,}000 - 62{,}208 = ₹7{,}37{,}792 \quad \checkmark$$

**Step 11 — Cross-verify using closed-form book value expression.**

For Year 3 (i.e., $k = 3$):

$$BV_3 = C(1 - \alpha)^3 = 8{,}00{,}000 \times (1 - 0.40)^3$$

$$BV_3 = 8{,}00{,}000 \times (0.60)^3$$

$$BV_3 = 8{,}00{,}000 \times 0.216$$

$$\boxed{BV_3 = ₹1{,}72{,}800} \quad \checkmark$$

---

### 3.1.3 Complete Depreciation Schedule (Summary Table)

| Year ($k$) | Beginning Book Value (₹) | Rate ($\alpha$) | Depreciation $D_k$ (₹) | Ending Book Value $BV_k$ (₹) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 8,00,000 | 0.40 | 3,20,000 | 4,80,000 |
| 2 | 4,80,000 | 0.40 | 1,92,000 | 2,88,000 |
| 3 | 2,88,000 | 0.40 | 1,15,200 | 1,72,800 |
| 4 | 1,72,800 | 0.40 | 69,120 | 1,03,680 |
| 5 | 1,03,680 | 0.40 | 41,472 | 62,208 |
| **Total** | — | — | **7,37,792** | **62,208** |

### 3.1.4 Part (c): Book Value at End of Year 3

From the schedule above:

$$\boxed{BV_3 = ₹1{,}72{,}800}$$

This has been independently verified using the closed-form expression in Step 11.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Conceptual Decision Flow

The following analytical matrix helps in **deciding whether the salvage floor is triggered** in the final year:

| Condition | Interpretation | Action |
|---|---|---|
| $BV_{k-1} \times \alpha \leq (BV_{k-1} - S)$ | Normal DDB computation, book value stays above S | Use $D_k = \alpha \times BV_{k-1}$ |
| $BV_{k-1} \times \alpha > (BV_{k-1} - S)$ | Naive DDB would drop BV below S | Adjust $D_k = BV_{k-1} - S$ (limited depreciation) |

## 3.3 Algebraic Insight: Why DDB Always Front-Loads Depreciation

We can prove mathematically that depreciation is maximum in Year 1:

$$D_k = C \times \alpha \times (1 - \alpha)^{k-1}$$

Since $0 < (1 - \alpha) < 1$, the term $(1 - \alpha)^{k-1}$ is strictly decreasing in $k$. Therefore:

$$D_1 > D_2 > D_3 > \cdots > D_n$$

This confirms the **monotonically decreasing** nature of annual depreciation under DDB.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Functional Block Architecture — DDB Calculation Pipeline

The following Mermaid flowchart depicts the step-by-step algorithmic flow for computing Double Declining Balance depreciation. This is the **standard valuation procedure** that KTU examiners expect students to follow.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[START: Input Asset Parameters] --> B[Read Original Cost C]
    B --> C[Read Useful Life n in years]
    C --> D[Read Salvage Value S]
    D --> E[Compute DDB Rate alpha = 2 divided by n]
    E --> F[Initialize BV0 = C]
    F --> G[Set year counter k = 1]
    G --> H[Compute Depreciation Dk = alpha times BVk minus 1]
    H --> I[Compute BVk = BVk minus 1 minus Dk]
    I --> J{Is BVk less than S?}
    J -- No --> K[Accept Dk and BVk as final values]
    J -- Yes --> L[Adjust Dk = BVk minus 1 minus S]
    L --> M[Set BVk = S as floor]
    M --> N[Increment k by 1]
    K --> N
    N --> O{Is k less than or equal to n?}
    O -- Yes --> H
    O -- No --> P[Print Depreciation Schedule]
    P --> Q[Compute Total Depreciation equals Sum of all Dk]
    Q --> R[Verify C minus BVn equals Total Depreciation]
    R --> S[END]
```

## 4.2 Conceptual Block Diagram — Role of DDB in Chemical Plant Economics

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Chemical Plant Capital Asset] --> P2[Acquisition Cost C]
    P2 --> P3[Useful Life n years]
    P3 --> P4{DDB Method Selection}
    P4 --> P5[Compute Rate alpha = 2 per n]
    P5 --> P6[Apply alpha to BV yearly]
    P6 --> P7[Year-wise Depreciation Dk]
    P7 --> P8[Tax Shield Benefit]
    P7 --> P9[Reduced Book Value]
    P8 --> P10[Improved Project NPV]
    P9 --> P11[Conservative Balance Sheet]
    P10 --> P12[Managerial Decision Making]
    P11 --> P12
```

## 4.3 Process Schematic — Depreciation Recursion

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Year 1 Start: BV0 equals C] --> S2[D1 equals 0.40 times BV0]
    S2 --> S3[BV1 equals BV0 minus D1]
    S3 --> S4[Year 2 Start]
    S4 --> S5[D2 equals 0.40 times BV1]
    S5 --> S6[BV2 equals BV1 minus D2]
    S6 --> S7[Year 3 Start]
    S7 --> S8[D3 equals 0.40 times BV2]
    S8 --> S9[BV3 equals BV2 minus D3]
    S9 --> S10[Continue until Year n]
    S10 --> S11[Salvage Floor Check]
    S11 --> S12[Final BVn greater than or equal to S]
```

## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Method Selection Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Choose Depreciation Method] --> B{Is asset technology sensitive?}
    B -- Yes --> C{Is early-year tax shield important?}
    C -- Yes --> D[Use DDB Method]
    C -- No --> E[Use Sum of Years Digits Method]
    B -- No --> F{Is depreciation uniform preferred?}
    F -- Yes --> G[Use Straight Line Method]
    F -- No --> H[Use Units of Production Method]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation for Chemical Engineers:** The DDB method is most suitable for **process equipment with rapid technological obsolescence** such as advanced control valves, PLC-based distributed control systems (DCS), and specialised chemical reactors with proprietary internals.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper Style]`

**Q: Define the Double Declining Balance Method of depreciation. Why is it called an "accelerated" depreciation method?**

**Model Answer:**

The **Double Declining Balance (DDB) Method** is an accelerated depreciation technique in which a constant depreciation rate, equal to **twice the straight-line rate** ($2/n$), is applied each year to the **remaining book value** (i.e., the original cost minus accumulated depreciation) of the asset.

Mathematically, the depreciation in year $k$ is:

$$D_k = \frac{2}{n} \times BV_{k-1}$$

It is called an **"accelerated" method** because it allocates a **larger proportion of the asset's cost to the early years** of its useful life and progressively smaller amounts in later years. This front-loading provides a **higher tax shield in early years** and matches the real-world pattern of faster value loss in newly acquired assets.

---

### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper Style]`

**Q: In the Double Declining Balance Method, how is the salvage value treated? Explain with an example.**

**Model Answer:**

In the **Double Declining Balance Method**, the salvage value is **NOT used in computing the depreciation rate** or the annual depreciation amount. However, it acts as a **"floor limit"** — the book value of the asset **cannot be depreciated below the salvage value**.

**Example:** If an asset has a cost of ₹5,00,000, useful life of 5 years, and salvage value of ₹40,000, the DDB rate is $2/5 = 40\%$. In the final year, if applying 40% would drop the book value below ₹40,000, the depreciation is **adjusted (limited)** so that the ending book value equals exactly the salvage value.

This treatment distinguishes DDB from the Straight-Line Method, where salvage value is directly subtracted from cost before computing depreciation.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper Style]`

**Q: A chemical processing plant installs a centrifugal separator for liquid-liquid extraction operations. The following data is provided:**
- **Original Cost (C):** ₹6,00,000
- **Useful Life (n):** 6 years
- **Salvage Value (S):** ₹60,000

**(a)** Compute the Double Declining Balance depreciation rate. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Prepare the complete depreciation schedule for all 6 years, showing the depreciation amount and ending book value for each year. Apply the salvage value floor rule wherever necessary. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution for Question A**

#### Part (a): Computation of DDB Rate (7 Marks)

**Step 1:** Identify the given parameters:

$$C = ₹6{,}00{,}000, \quad n = 6 \text{ years}, \quad S = ₹60{,}000$$

**Step 2:** Apply the DDB rate formula:

$$\alpha = \frac{2}{n} = \frac{2}{6} = \frac{1}{3}$$

**Step 3:** Express as percentage:

$$\alpha = 0.3333 \text{ or } 33.33\% \text{ per annum}$$

> **Answer:** The Double Declining Balance depreciation rate is **33.33% per annum**.

---

#### Part (b): Depreciation Schedule for 6 Years (7 Marks)

**Step 1:** Initialize: $BV_0 = C = ₹6{,}00{,}000$

**Step 2:** Compute year-wise depreciation using $D_k = \alpha \times BV_{k-1}$:

**Year 1:**
$$D_1 = 0.3333 \times 6{,}00{,}000 = ₹2{,}00{,}000$$
$$BV_1 = 6{,}00{,}000 - 2{,}00{,}000 = ₹4{,}00{,}000$$

**Year 2:**
$$D_2 = 0.3333 \times 4{,}00{,}000 = ₹1{,}33{,}333$$
$$BV_2 = 4{,}00{,}000 - 1{,}33{,}333 = ₹2{,}66{,}667$$

**Year 3:**
$$D_3 = 0.3333 \times 2{,}66{,}667 = ₹88{,}889$$
$$BV_3 = 2{,}66{,}667 - 88{,}889 = ₹1{,}77{,}778$$

**Year 4:**
$$D_4 = 0.3333 \times 1{,}77{,}778 = ₹59{,}259$$
$$BV_4 = 1{,}77{,}778 - 59{,}259 = ₹1{,}18{,}519$$

**Year 5:**
$$D_5 = 0.3333 \times 1{,}18{,}519 = ₹39{,}506$$
$$BV_5 = 1{,}18{,}519 - 39{,}506 = ₹79{,}013$$

**Year 6 (Salvage Floor Check):**

Naive depreciation: $0.3333 \times 79{,}013 = ₹26{,}334$
Naive BV: $79{,}013 - 26{,}334 = ₹52{,}679$

Since $₹52{,}679 < S = ₹60{,}000$, the salvage floor IS triggered. Adjusted depreciation:

$$D_6 = BV_5 - S = 79{,}013 - 60{,}000 = ₹19{,}013$$

$$BV_6 = S = ₹60{,}000$$

**Complete Depreciation Schedule:**

| Year | Beginning BV (₹) | Depreciation (₹) | Ending BV (₹) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 6,00,000 | 2,00,000 | 4,00,000 |
| 2 | 4,00,000 | 1,33,333 | 2,66,667 |
| 3 | 2,66,667 | 88,889 | 1,77,778 |
| 4 | 1,77,778 | 59,259 | 1,18,519 |
| 5 | 1,18,519 | 39,506 | 79,013 |
| 6 | 79,013 | 19,013 (adjusted) | 60,000 |
| **Total** | — | **5,40,000** | **60,000** |

**Verification:** $C - BV_n = 6{,}00{,}000 - 60{,}000 = ₹5{,}40{,}000$ ✓

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper Style]`

**Q: A polyethylene manufacturing plant acquires a reactor vessel with the following data:**
- **Original Cost (C):** ₹10,00,000
- **Useful Life (n):** 8 years
- **Salvage Value (S):** ₹1,00,000

**(a)** Calculate the depreciation for the first three years using the Double Declining Balance Method. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** What is the book value of the reactor at the end of Year 5? Verify using both the recursive method and the closed-form expression $BV_k = C(1-\alpha)^k$. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution for Question B**

#### Part (a): Depreciation for First Three Years (7 Marks)

**Step 1:** Compute the DDB rate:

$$\alpha = \frac{2}{n} = \frac{2}{8} = 0.25 = 25\%$$

**Step 2:** Compute Year 1 depreciation:

$$D_1 = 0.25 \times 10{,}00{,}000 = ₹2{,}50{,}000$$

**Step 3:** Compute Year 2 depreciation:

$$BV_1 = 10{,}00{,}000 - 2{,}50{,}000 = ₹7{,}50{,}000$$
$$D_2 = 0.25 \times 7{,}50{,}000 = ₹1{,}87{,}500$$

**Step 4:** Compute Year 3 depreciation:

$$BV_2 = 7{,}50{,}000 - 1{,}87{,}500 = ₹5{,}62{,}500$$
$$D_3 = 0.25 \times 5{,}62{,}500 = ₹1{,}40{,}625$$

> **Answer:** $D_1 = ₹2{,}50{,}000$; $D_2 = ₹1{,}87{,}500$; $D_3 = ₹1{,}40{,}625$

---

#### Part (b): Book Value at End of Year 5 (7 Marks)

**Method 1 — Recursive Computation:**

Continuing from Year 3:

$$BV_3 = 5{,}62{,}500 - 1{,}40{,}625 = ₹4{,}21{,}875$$

Year 4:
$$D_4 = 0.25 \times 4{,}21{,}875 = ₹1{,}05{,}469$$
$$BV_4 = 4{,}21{,}875 - 1{,}05{,}469 = ₹3{,}16{,}406$$

Year 5:
$$D_5 = 0.25 \times 3{,}16{,}406 = ₹79{,}102$$
$$BV_5 = 3{,}16{,}406 - 79{,}102 = ₹2{,}37{,}304$$

**Method 2 — Closed-Form Expression:**

$$BV_5 = C(1 - \alpha)^5 = 10{,}00{,}000 \times (0.75)^5$$

$$(0.75)^5 = 0.2373$$

$$BV_5 = 10{,}00{,}000 \times 0.2373 = ₹2{,}37{,}304$$

**Verification:** Both methods yield $BV_5 = ₹2{,}37{,}304$ ✓

> **Answer:** The book value at the end of Year 5 is **₹2,37,304**.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks (KTU 2024 Valuation Pattern):**
>
> 1. **Subtracting Salvage Value from Cost Before Computing Rate:** This is the **single most common error**. In DDB, the rate is ALWAYS $2/n$ regardless of salvage value. Mark deduction: **−2 marks**.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the Salvage Value Floor in Final Year:** If the naive DDB computation drops the book value below the salvage value, students often continue the naive calculation. The correct approach is to **adjust the final-year depreciation** so that $BV_{\text{final}} = S$. Mark deduction: **−2 to −3 marks**.
>
> 3. **Confusing DDB with Straight-Line Method (SLM):** Some students mistakenly apply DDB as a fixed-amount-on-original-cost method. Remember: DDB applies the rate to the **declining book value**, NOT to the original cost.
>
> 4. **Not Verifying with the Closed-Form Formula:** For 14-mark questions, KTU examiners often give **partial credit** for verifying answers using both methods (recursive and closed-form). Missing this verification may cost **−1 mark**.
>
> 5. **Rounding Errors:** KTU expects depreciation amounts rounded to the nearest rupee. Excessive truncation (e.g., rounding to nearest thousand) leads to **verification failures** and mark deductions.
>
> 6. **Missing Units:** Always write **₹** symbol or "Rupees" explicitly. Omitting currency units is a **presentation error** that may cost **−0.5 mark** per table row.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 — Quick Recall Bullet Points for Double Declining Balance Method:**
>
> ☐ DDB is also called the **200% Declining Balance Method** — rate = $2/n$
>
> ☐ The **salvage value is IGNORED** during rate calculation but acts as a **floor limit** on book value
>
> ☐ Annual depreciation formula: $D_k = \alpha \times BV_{k-1}$ where $\alpha = 2/n$
>
> ☐ Book value recursion: $BV_k = BV_{k-1} - D_k$
>
> ☐ Closed-form book value: $BV_k = C(1-\alpha)^k$ (valid until salvage floor triggers)
>
> ☐ DDB produces **monotonically decreasing** annual depreciation: $D_1 > D_2 > \cdots > D_n$
>
> ☐ DDB is an **accelerated** depreciation method — suitable for **technology-sensitive** chemical plant equipment
>
> ☐ Final-year depreciation is **adjusted** to ensure $BV_{\text{final}} \geq S$
>
> ☐ Total depreciation over asset life = $C - BV_n$ (bookkeeping identity for verification)
>
> ☐ DDB is preferred over SLM when **early-year tax shields** and **rapid technological obsolescence** are key economic considerations
>
> ☐ **NOT to be confused** with: Straight-Line Method, Sum-of-Years' Digits Method, or Sinking Fund Method
>
> ☐ For KTU 2024 PECHT757 exams: Always present answers in a **tabular depreciation schedule** format with proper units
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sum of Years Digits Method
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT757 - ECONOMICS AND MANAGEMENT OF CHEMICAL INDUSTRIES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Sum of Years Digits Method

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

The **Sum of Years Digits (SYD) Method** is an **accelerated depreciation accounting technique** used in capital budgeting, cost engineering, and industrial asset management to allocate the depreciable cost of a tangible fixed asset (such as a reactor, distillation column, heat exchanger, or any process equipment) over its useful economic life in a **decreasing arithmetic progression**.

In the SYD method, the depreciation charge in any given year is proportional to the **remaining useful life of the asset at the beginning of that year**, divided by the **sum of the digits of all years in the asset's total life**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Definition (Verbatim Style):**
> "The Sum of Years Digits Method is an accelerated depreciation method in which the depreciable base (Cost minus Salvage Value) is multiplied by a fraction — the numerator being the remaining life of the asset at the start of the year and the denominator being the sum of the digits representing the years of useful life — to arrive at the periodic depreciation expense."

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The New Car Effect"**
> Imagine you buy a brand-new chemical tanker truck for your plant for **₹20,00,000**. In the very first year, the truck loses value rapidly — it experiences the steepest depreciation because of initial wear, breakdown risk, and the market's strong preference for "unused" assets. By the time it is 4–5 years old, it is a proven workhorse and its resale value drop becomes much more gradual.
> 
> The **Straight Line Method** would charge the *same* depreciation every year (₹3,60,000 per year if salvage is ₹2,00,000 over 5 years), which does **not** reflect this real-world loss pattern.
> 
> The **Sum of Years Digits Method** mirrors reality by charging the **largest chunk in Year 1**, the **second largest in Year 2**, and so on in a smooth, mathematically elegant decreasing pattern — exactly like how the actual market value of the truck declines.

### Physical Constants / Standard Metrics in Bold

- The denominator of the SYD fraction is always computed as the **Sum of an Arithmetic Progression** equal to **n(n + 1)/2**, where **n = Useful Life in Years**.
- The depreciable base is universally defined as **(Initial Cost − Salvage Value)**.
- The standard unit of depreciation charge is the **currency unit** (₹, $, €) per fiscal year.

### Why Chemical Engineers Study SYD

In the chemical process industries, equipment such as **reactors, centrifugal pumps, compressors, evaporators, and cracking units** suffer the heaviest mechanical and obsolescence losses in their early operating years. SYD provides a financially prudent way to:
- Recover capital faster during high-risk early years.
- Match higher maintenance costs (which rise with age) against declining depreciation.
- Generate tax shields earlier in the asset's life.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### Underlying Logical Structure

The SYD method is built upon **four foundational pillars**:

1. **Identification of the Depreciable Base ($D_b$):**
   The total amount that must be depreciated over the asset's life is strictly the difference between the original cost and the estimated salvage (scrap/residual) value. Land is **never** depreciated.

2. **Computation of the Sum of Years Digits (SOD):**
   The years of life are written as a descending sequence: $n, (n-1), (n-2), \ldots, 2, 1$. These digits are summed to form the denominator of every annual depreciation fraction.

3. **Assignment of the Annual Fraction:**
   In year $k$ (where $k = 1, 2, \ldots, n$), the numerator is the **remaining life at the beginning of year $k$**, which equals $(n - k + 1)$. This is the key "weight" assigned to that year.

4. **Multiplication and Accumulation:**
   The annual depreciation is $D_b \times \text{fraction}_k$. The book value at the end of year $k$ is obtained by subtracting cumulative depreciation from the original cost. After $n$ years, the book value must equal the salvage value — a critical audit checkpoint.

### Why and How Behind Each Step

- **Why accelerate depreciation?** Because most industrial assets lose economic value disproportionately early, and recovering capital faster improves cash flow and provides a hedge against technological obsolescence.
- **Why use remaining life as numerator?** It creates a smooth, mathematically decreasing allocation that naturally pairs with the rising O&M (Operating & Maintenance) costs typical of ageing chemical plant equipment.
- **Why is the denominator constant?** It is the total of all numerators across $n$ years, ensuring the sum of all annual depreciation charges equals exactly $D_b$ — preserving the accounting identity.

### KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield Reference Table)

| **Quantity** | **Symbol** | **Formula** | **Units / Notes** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Initial Cost (Capitalized Cost) | $C$ | Given / capitalized at installation | Currency (₹, $) |
| Salvage / Residual Value | $S$ | Estimated at end of useful life | Currency |
| Depreciable Base | $D_b$ | $D_b = C - S$ | Currency |
| Useful Life | $n$ | Given in years | Years |
| Sum of Years Digits | $SOD$ | $SOD = \dfrac{n(n+1)}{2}$ | Dimensionless integer |
| Remaining Life at Start of Year $k$ | $RL_k$ | $RL_k = n - k + 1$ | Years |
| Annual Depreciation (Year $k$) | $D_k$ | $D_k = D_b \cdot \dfrac{RL_k}{SOD}$ | Currency / year |
| Cumulative Depreciation through Year $k$ | $CD_k$ | $CD_k = \displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{k} D_i$ | Currency |
| Book Value at End of Year $k$ | $BV_k$ | $BV_k = C - CD_k$ | Currency |
| Audit Check at End of Life | — | $BV_n = S$ | Must hold exactly |

> [!NOTE]
> **LaTeX Safety Note:** Within markdown tables, the absolute value operator is rendered as `\vert \, x \, \vert` rather than using vertical pipes, to preserve table syntax integrity as required by the KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE protocol.

### Real-World Engineering Utility

In chemical plant management, SYD depreciation is extensively applied for:
- **Cost-of-production calculations** in the early years of a new process plant.
- **Tax planning** — accelerated depreciation generates larger tax shields in early years.
- **Replacement analysis** — when comparing an ageing asset (high depreciation already recovered) against a new alternative.
- **Insurance valuation** — reflecting the higher market premium in early years.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### Derivation 1 — Sum of an Arithmetic Progression

The denominator of every SYD fraction is the sum of the first $n$ positive integers written in descending order:

$$
SOD = n + (n-1) + (n-2) + \cdots + 2 + 1
$$

Writing the same sum in ascending order:

$$
SOD = 1 + 2 + 3 + \cdots + (n-1) + n
$$

Adding the two series term-by-term, every column sums to $(n+1)$ and there are $n$ columns:

$$
2 \cdot SOD = n(n+1)
$$

Dividing by 2 yields the canonical identity:

$$
SOD = \frac{n(n+1)}{2}
$$

**Interpretation:** This is the famous triangular-number formula. It guarantees that summing the SYD fractions from year 1 through year $n$ equals exactly **1**, ensuring full recovery of the depreciable base.

### Derivation 2 — General Annual Depreciation Formula

The depreciation in year $k$ is the depreciable base multiplied by the fraction whose numerator is the remaining life at the start of year $k$ and denominator is the sum of years digits:

$$
D_k = D_b \cdot \frac{RL_k}{SOD}
$$

Substituting $RL_k = n - k + 1$ and $SOD = \dfrac{n(n+1)}{2}$:

$$
D_k = D_b \cdot \frac{n - k + 1}{\dfrac{n(n+1)}{2}}
$$

Multiplying numerator and denominator to simplify:

$$
D_k = \frac{2 \, D_b \, (n - k + 1)}{n(n+1)}
$$

This is the **canonical closed-form expression** for SYD depreciation, valid for $k = 1, 2, \ldots, n$.

### Derivation 3 — Verification of Full Recovery (Audit Identity)

Summing $D_k$ over all $n$ years:

$$
\sum_{k=1}^{n} D_k = \frac{2 \, D_b}{n(n+1)} \sum_{k=1}^{n} (n - k + 1)
$$

Let $j = n - k + 1$. As $k$ runs from 1 to $n$, $j$ runs from $n$ down to 1:

$$
\sum_{k=1}^{n} (n - k + 1) = \sum_{j=1}^{n} j = \frac{n(n+1)}{2}
$$

Substituting back:

$$
\sum_{k=1}^{n} D_k = \frac{2 \, D_b}{n(n+1)} \cdot \frac{n(n+1)}{2} = D_b
$$

Therefore, after $n$ years, the entire depreciable base is recovered, and the book value equals the salvage value: a built-in accounting safeguard.

---

### Exhaustive Worked Example (Board-Style Long Problem)

> **Problem Statement:**
> A chemical plant installs a new **stainless-steel batch reactor** at an initial cost of **₹10,00,000**. The estimated salvage value at the end of its useful life is **₹2,00,000**. The useful life is **5 years**. Compute the annual depreciation, cumulative depreciation, and book value at the end of each year using the **Sum of Years Digits Method**. Prepare a depreciation schedule and verify the audit identity.

**Step 1 — Identify the inputs:**

$$
C = ₹10{,}00{,}000, \quad S = ₹2{,}00{,}000, \quad n = 5 \text{ years}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the depreciable base:**

$$
D_b = C - S = 10{,}00{,}000 - 2{,}00{,}000 = ₹8{,}00{,}000
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the sum of years digits:**

$$
SOD = \frac{n(n+1)}{2} = \frac{5 \times 6}{2} = 15
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the annual SYD fraction and depreciation:**

The remaining life at the start of each year gives the numerator: Year 1 → 5, Year 2 → 4, Year 3 → 3, Year 4 → 2, Year 5 → 1.

$$
D_1 = 8{,}00{,}000 \times \frac{5}{15} = 8{,}00{,}000 \times \frac{1}{3} = ₹2{,}66{,}667
$$

$$
D_2 = 8{,}00{,}000 \times \frac{4}{15} = ₹2{,}13{,}333
$$

$$
D_3 = 8{,}00{,}000 \times \frac{3}{15} = 8{,}00{,}000 \times \frac{5}{1} \times \frac{1}{100} = ₹1{,}60{,}000
$$

$$
D_4 = 8{,}00{,}000 \times \frac{2}{15} = ₹1{,}06{,}667
$$

$$
D_5 = 8{,}00{,}000 \times \frac{1}{15} = ₹53{,}333
$$

**Step 5 — Compute cumulative depreciation and book value:**

$$
CD_1 = 2{,}66{,}667 \Rightarrow BV_1 = 10{,}00{,}000 - 2{,}66{,}667 = ₹7{,}33{,}333
$$

$$
CD_2 = 2{,}66{,}667 + 2{,}13{,}333 = 4{,}80{,}000 \Rightarrow BV_2 = 10{,}00{,}000 - 4{,}80{,}000 = ₹5{,}20{,}000
$$

$$
CD_3 = 4{,}80{,}000 + 1{,}60{,}000 = 6{,}40{,}000 \Rightarrow BV_3 = 10{,}00{,}000 - 6{,}40{,}000 = ₹3{,}60{,}000
$$

$$
CD_4 = 6{,}40{,}000 + 1{,}06{,}667 = 7{,}46{,}667 \Rightarrow BV_4 = 10{,}00{,}000 - 7{,}46{,}667 = ₹2{,}53{,}333
$$

$$
CD_5 = 7{,}46{,}667 + 53{,}333 = 8{,}00{,}000 \Rightarrow BV_5 = 10{,}00{,}000 - 8{,}00{,}000 = ₹2{,}00{,}000 \;\checkmark
$$

**Step 6 — Audit verification:**
The final book value equals the salvage value, confirming the schedule.

### Consolidated Depreciation Schedule (Analytical Matrix)

| **Year (k)** | **Remaining Life (RL)** | **SYD Fraction (RL / SOD)** | **Depreciation $D_k$ (₹)** | **Cumulative Depreciation $CD_k$ (₹)** | **Book Value $BV_k$ (₹)** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 5 | 5/15 = 0.3333 | 2,66,667 | 2,66,667 | 7,33,333 |
| 2 | 4 | 4/15 = 0.2667 | 2,13,333 | 4,80,000 | 5,20,000 |
| 3 | 3 | 3/15 = 0.2000 | 1,60,000 | 6,40,000 | 3,60,000 |
| 4 | 2 | 2/15 = 0.1333 | 1,06,667 | 7,46,667 | 2,53,333 |
| 5 | 1 | 1/15 = 0.0667 | 53,333 | 8,00,000 | 2,00,000 |
| **Total** | — | **15/15 = 1.0000** | **8,00,000** | — | — |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### Mermaid Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of the SYD Calculation Workflow

The following Mermaid flowchart maps the **decision and computation pipeline** a chemical-industry cost engineer follows when applying the SYD method. It follows the Mermaid Compilation Safeguards: all node identifiers are alphanumeric (e.g., `nodeA`, `stepB`), and double-quoted labels contain only clean uppercase alphanumeric text without any markdown or HTML formatting.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["INPUT ASSET DATA COST C SALVAGE S LIFE N"] --> nodeB["COMPUTE DEPRECIABLE BASE DB = C MINUS S"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["COMPUTE SUM OF DIGITS SOD = N TIMES N PLUS 1 DIVIDED BY 2"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["INITIALIZE LOOP K = 1 TO N"]
    nodeD --> nodeE["COMPUTE REMAINING LIFE RL = N MINUS K PLUS 1"]
    nodeE --> nodeF["COMPUTE FRACTION = RL DIVIDED BY SOD"]
    nodeF --> nodeG["COMPUTE ANNUAL DEPRECIATION DK = DB TIMES FRACTION"]
    nodeG --> nodeH["ACCUMULATE CUMULATIVE DEPRET CDK"]
    nodeH --> nodeI["COMPUTE BOOK VALUE BVK = C MINUS CDK"]
    nodeI --> nodeJ["STORE YEARLY ROW IN SCHEDULE"]
    nodeJ --> nodeK{"IS K LESS THAN N"}
    nodeK -- YES --> nodeD
    nodeK -- NO --> nodeL["AUDIT CHECK VERIFY BVN EQUALS S"]
    nodeL --> nodeM["OUTPUT FINAL SYD DEPRECIATION SCHEDULE"]
```

### Conceptual Block-Level Functional Architecture (Reader's Mental Model)

| **Stage** | **Process Block** | **Function** |
|---|---|---|
| **1. Data Acquisition** | `INPUT BLOCK` | Captures Capitalized Cost ($C$), Salvage Value ($S$), Useful Life ($n$) |
| **2. Pre-Processing** | `BASE COMPUTATION UNIT` | Calculates Depreciable Base $D_b = C - S$ |
| **3. Weight Generator** | `SOD CALCULATOR` | Produces denominator $SOD = n(n+1)/2$ |
| **4. Iterative Engine** | `YEAR-BY-YEAR LOOP` | Iterates $k = 1 \rightarrow n$, computes fraction and depreciation |
| **5. Schedule Builder** | `SCHEDULE WRITER` | Tabulates annual depreciation, cumulative, and book value |
| **6. Validator** | `AUDIT CHECK` | Confirms $BV_n = S$ (salvage value integrity) |
| **7. Output** | `REPORT BLOCK` | Delivers printable depreciation schedule for tax/compliance filing |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Diagram Interpretation Note:** The flowchart depicts the *logical sequence* of operations a chemical-plant cost accountant follows. Each node corresponds to one explicit step in the analytical procedure described in SECTION 3. The diamond-shaped decision node `IS K LESS THAN N` governs the loop continuation and guarantees that exactly $n$ rows are written before the audit check executes.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper PECHT757]**
**Define the Sum of Years Digits Method of depreciation. Write the expression for the sum of years digits for an asset with useful life $n$ years.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
The Sum of Years Digits Method is an accelerated depreciation technique in which the depreciable base of an asset is allocated over its useful life in a decreasing arithmetic pattern. In year $k$, the depreciation charge is the depreciable base multiplied by the fraction $(n - k + 1) / SOD$, where $SOD$ is the sum of years digits.

$$
SOD = \frac{n(n+1)}{2}
$$

*(Award 2 marks for definition + 1 mark for the formula.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper PECHT757]**
**A centrifugal pump costs ₹5,00,000 with a salvage value of ₹1,00,000 and a useful life of 4 years. Compute the depreciation in Year 1 using the SYD method.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
Depreciable base:

$$
D_b = 5{,}00{,}000 - 1{,}00{,}000 = ₹4{,}00{,}000
$$

Sum of years digits:

$$
SOD = \frac{4 \times 5}{2} = 10
$$

Year 1 depreciation (remaining life = 4):

$$
D_1 = 4{,}00{,}000 \times \frac{4}{10} = ₹1{,}60{,}000
$$

*(Award 1 mark each for depreciable base, SOD, and final answer.)*

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A — [14 Marks]**

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme ESE Pattern]**

> A pharmaceutical plant installs a **fluidized bed dryer** at a total capitalized cost of **₹15,00,000**. The estimated salvage value at the end of its useful life is **₹3,00,000**, and the useful life is **6 years**.
> 
> **(a)** Compute the annual depreciation for each of the 6 years using the **Sum of Years Digits Method**. *(7 marks)*
> 
> **(b)** Prepare the **depreciation schedule** showing the cumulative depreciation and book value at the end of each year. Also verify the **audit identity**. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution for Part (a) — 7 Marks:**

**Step 1 — Identify the given values:**

$$
C = ₹15{,}00{,}000, \quad S = ₹3{,}00{,}000, \quad n = 6 \text{ years}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute depreciable base:**

$$
D_b = C - S = 15{,}00{,}000 - 3{,}00{,}000 = ₹12{,}00{,}000
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the sum of years digits:**

$$
SOD = \frac{6 \times 7}{2} = 21
$$

**Step 4 — Compute annual depreciation using $D_k = D_b \times \dfrac{(n - k + 1)}{SOD}$:**

$$
D_1 = 12{,}00{,}000 \times \frac{6}{21} = ₹3{,}42{,}857
$$

$$
D_2 = 12{,}00{,}000 \times \frac{5}{21} = ₹2{,}85{,}714
$$

$$
D_3 = 12{,}00{,}000 \times \frac{4}{21} = ₹2{,}28{,}571
$$

$$
D_4 = 12{,}00{,}000 \times \frac{3}{21} = ₹1{,}71{,}429
$$

$$
D_5 = 12{,}00{,}000 \times \frac{2}{21} = ₹1{,}14{,}286
$$

$$
D_6 = 12{,}00{,}000 \times \frac{1}{21} = ₹57{,}143
$$

**Model Solution for Part (b) — 7 Marks:**

| **Year (k)** | **RL** | **Fraction** | **Depreciation $D_k$ (₹)** | **Cumulative $CD_k$ (₹)** | **Book Value $BV_k$ (₹)** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 6 | 6/21 | 3,42,857 | 3,42,857 | 11,57,143 |
| 2 | 5 | 5/21 | 2,85,714 | 6,28,571 | 8,71,429 |
| 3 | 4 | 4/21 | 2,28,571 | 8,57,142 | 6,42,858 |
| 4 | 3 | 3/21 | 1,71,429 | 10,28,571 | 4,71,429 |
| 5 | 2 | 2/21 | 1,14,286 | 11,42,857 | 3,57,143 |
| 6 | 1 | 1/21 | 57,143 | 12,00,000 | 3,00,000 |

**Audit identity verification:**

$$
\sum_{k=1}^{6} D_k = 12{,}00{,}000 \quad \text{and} \quad BV_6 = ₹3{,}00{,}000 = S \;\checkmark
$$

Minor rounding differences (₹1) are acceptable per KTU valuation tolerance for fractional denominators.

---

#### **Question B — [14 Marks] (Alternative Choice)**

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme ESE Pattern]**

> A chemical factory installs a **shell-and-tube heat exchanger** costing **₹8,00,000**, with a salvage value of **₹2,00,000** and a useful life of **5 years**.
> 
> **(a)** Derive the **Sum of Years Digits formula** $SOD = \dfrac{n(n+1)}{2}$ and explain its significance in the SYD method. *(7 marks)*
> 
> **(b)** Using the SYD method, compute the **annual depreciation, cumulative depreciation, and book value** for each of the 5 years. Compare the Year 1 depreciation of SYD with that of the Straight Line Method and comment. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution for Part (a) — 7 Marks:**

**Derivation:**
Consider the descending sequence of years of life:

$$
SOD = n + (n-1) + (n-2) + \cdots + 2 + 1
$$

Rewriting in ascending order:

$$
SOD = 1 + 2 + 3 + \cdots + (n-1) + n
$$

Adding both series term-by-term, each column sums to $(n+1)$ and there are $n$ columns:

$$
2 \cdot SOD = n \cdot (n+1)
$$

Dividing both sides by 2:

$$
SOD = \frac{n(n+1)}{2}
$$

**Significance:**
- $SOD$ is the **constant denominator** of every annual SYD fraction.
- The numerator at year $k$ is the **remaining life** $(n - k + 1)$.
- Since $SOD$ equals the sum of all numerators, summing all annual fractions over $n$ years gives exactly **1**, guaranteeing full recovery of the depreciable base.
- For $n = 5$: $SOD = 15$; for $n = 10$: $SOD = 55$.

**Model Solution for Part (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Step 1 — Depreciable base:**

$$
D_b = 8{,}00{,}000 - 2{,}00{,}000 = ₹6{,}00{,}000
$$

**Step 2 — Sum of digits:**

$$
