SOD = \frac{5 \times 6}{2} = 15
$$

**Step 3 — Annual depreciation (SYD):**

$$
D_1 = 6{,}00{,}000 \times \frac{5}{15} = ₹2{,}00{,}000
$$

$$
D_2 = 6{,}00{,}000 \times \frac{4}{15} = ₹1{,}60{,}000
$$

$$
D_3 = 6{,}00{,}000 \times \frac{3}{15} = ₹1{,}20{,}000
$$

$$
D_4 = 6{,}00{,}000 \times \frac{2}{15} = ₹80{,}000
$$

$$
D_5 = 6{,}00{,}000 \times \frac{1}{15} = ₹40{,}000
$$

**Step 4 — Depreciation Schedule:**

| **Year** | **$D_k$ (₹)** | **$CD_k$ (₹)** | **$BV_k$ (₹)** |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 2,00,000 | 2,00,000 | 6,00,000 |
| 2 | 1,60,000 | 3,60,000 | 4,40,000 |
| 3 | 1,20,000 | 4,80,000 | 3,20,000 |
| 4 | 80,000 | 5,60,000 | 2,40,000 |
| 5 | 40,000 | 6,00,000 | 2,00,000 |

**Step 5 — Comparison with Straight Line Method (SLM):**

SLM annual depreciation:

$$
D_{SLM} = \frac{6{,}00{,}000}{5} = ₹1{,}20{,}000 \text{ per year}
$$

SYD Year 1 depreciation = ₹2,00,000, which is **₹80,000 higher** (about **66.67% more**) than SLM. This confirms that SYD is an **accelerated method**, front-loading depreciation to match the higher real-world value loss of the heat exchanger in its early operational years.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 
> 1. **Forgetting to subtract salvage value:** Many students mistakenly depreciate the *full* cost instead of $(C - S)$. The depreciable base is **always** $D_b = C - S$.
> 
> 2. **Reversing the fraction numerator/denominator:** A classic error is writing $D_k = D_b \times \dfrac{SOD}{RL_k}$, which gives absurdly large numbers. The correct numerator is the **remaining life** $(n - k + 1)$, not the SOD.
> 
> 3. **Off-by-one year indexing:** Some students assign the fraction $\frac{n}{SOD}$ to Year 2 and $\frac{(n-1)}{SOD}$ to Year 1. The correct convention is: in Year 1, remaining life = $n$ (full life ahead).
> 
> 4. **Ignoring the audit identity:** Always verify that $BV_n = S$. Examiners reward this final check with **1 extra mark** in valuation.
> 
> 5. **Rounding errors in fractional calculations:** When $SOD$ does not divide $D_b$ exactly, retain fractions to the last step and round only the final answer. KTU allows ±₹1–₹2 tolerance.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- The **Sum of Years Digits (SYD) Method** is an **accelerated** depreciation technique.
- The depreciable base is **always** $D_b = C - S$; **never** depreciate the full cost.
- The denominator $SOD = \dfrac{n(n+1)}{2}$ is the sum of an arithmetic progression.
- The numerator for year $k$ is the **remaining useful life** at the start of year $k$: $RL_k = n - k + 1$.
- The canonical annual depreciation formula is:

$$
D_k = \frac{2 \, D_b \, (n - k + 1)}{n(n+1)}
$$

- The **audit identity** $BV_n = S$ must hold exactly (within rounding tolerance).
- SYD produces **larger** depreciation in early years and **smaller** depreciation in later years compared to the Straight Line Method.
- For $n = 5$, $SOD = 15$; for $n = 10$, $SOD = 55$ — memorize the triangular-number pattern.
- SYD is preferred for chemical plant equipment that suffers **disproportionate early wear or technological obsolescence**.
- The method satisfies the **matching principle** of accounting: larger early depreciation offsets the lower maintenance cost in early years (and the higher maintenance cost in later years).
- Always present the final answer as a **3-column or 5-column depreciation schedule** for full KTU marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sinking Fund and Units of Production Methods
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT757 - ECONOMICS AND MANAGEMENT OF CHEMICAL INDUSTRIES
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Sinking Fund and Units of Production Methods

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 The Sinking Fund Method

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU 2024 Definition:**
> The **Sinking Fund Method** of depreciation is a financial accounting technique in which a *uniform sum of money is deposited annually* into a specific interest-bearing reserve account (the Sinking Fund) so that the accumulated principal plus compound interest exactly equals the original depreciable cost (Original Cost − Salvage Value) of the asset at the end of its estimated useful life.

In simpler words — instead of "writing off" the asset's value on paper, the company quietly **saves money in a special bank account** every year. This account grows like a savings scheme (with interest). When the asset finally retires, the company withdraws exactly enough money to replace it. Think of it like a parent putting aside ₹500 every month for 10 years in a fixed deposit so they can buy their child a laptop on their 10th birthday — the recurring deposit plus interest magically equals the laptop's price!

### 1.1.1 Conceptual Analogy — "The Retirement Piggy Bank"

Imagine a chemical engineer in Kerala who buys a **distillation column** for ₹10,00,000 that will last 20 years. Every year, he puts aside some money into a separate bank account (the *Sinking Fund*). Because the bank gives him compound interest (say 8% per annum), the deposits grow much faster than he alone is depositing. By the 20th year, the account balance equals ₹10,00,000 — exactly the replacement cost of the column. The plant always has a ready "war chest" for asset replacement, regardless of when the asset fails.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is it called "Sinking Fund"?** In old English, "sinking" meant *gradually paying off a debt*. The fund "sinks" (gradually absorbs) the cost of replacing the asset over its life.

## 1.2 The Units of Production Method

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU 2024 Definition:**
> The **Units of Production Method** (also called the *Service Output Method* or *Wear and Tear Method*) is a depreciation technique in which the depreciable cost of an asset is allocated based on actual physical usage, output, or service rendered, rather than the passage of time. Depreciation expense in any given year is directly proportional to the units produced during that year.

### 1.2.1 Conceptual Analogy — "The Taxi Meter"

Think of a taxi cab bought for ₹8,00,000 that is expected to run **5,00,000 km** in its lifetime. A taxi that sits idle in the garage for a month doesn't wear out — but one that runs 5,000 km that month definitely does. The Units of Production Method says: *charge depreciation based on kilometers driven*, not based on how many months passed. So a slow month = less depreciation; a busy month = more depreciation. This perfectly mirrors *real* wear-and-tear — which is why chemical plants (where reactors, pumps, and compressors are subject to usage-based wear) love this method!

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric Used:** The "unit" of measurement can be **kilometers, hours of operation, cycles, tonnes produced, cubic meters processed**, or any other physically measurable output relevant to the asset.

## 1.3 Comparative Snapshot at a Glance

| Aspect | Sinking Fund Method | Units of Production Method |
|---|---|---|
| **Primary Driver** | Time (years) | Physical usage / production output |
| **Interest Component** | Yes — accumulates compound interest | No interest consideration |
| **Best Suited For** | Long-life, high-value capital assets with predictable replacement | Assets whose wear correlates with output (pumps, compressors, reactors, vehicles) |
| **Asset Replacement Provision** | Automatically built-in via the fund | Requires separate retirement reserve |
| **Income Statement Effect** | Equal annual depreciation charge | Variable annual depreciation charge |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Sinking Fund Method — Operational Logic

The Sinking Fund Method is built upon **two simultaneous financial streams** that occur every year:

1. **Depreciation Charge (D)** — A uniform amount debited to the Profit & Loss Account annually.
2. **Sinking Fund Investment (D)** — The same uniform amount credited to the Sinking Fund Account, which earns compound interest at rate *i* per annum.

### 2.1.1 Why a Fund? The "Replacement Logic"

The depreciable amount (Original Cost − Salvage Value) must be available *in cash* on the day the asset is retired. Time-value-of-money principles dictate that a rupee saved today is worth more than a rupee saved tomorrow (because of interest). Therefore, smaller annual contributions today + interest growth = large lump sum at retirement.

### 2.1.2 Step-by-Step Operational Logic

- **Step 1:** Determine Original Cost (P), Salvage Value (S), Useful Life (n years), and the Sinking Fund interest rate (i).
- **Step 2:** Compute the **uniform annual sinking fund deposit (D)** using the standard future value of an annuity formula.
- **Step 3:** Each year-end, debit Depreciation A/c by **D** (this is the P&L charge).
- **Step 4:** Simultaneously credit the Sinking Fund A/c by **D**, and credit the Interest earned on the sinking fund balance.
- **Step 5:** Compute **Book Value (BV)** at any year *k* as: BV = P − (Sinking Fund Balance at end of year *k*).
- **Step 6:** At end of year *n*, Sinking Fund Balance = P − S (the depreciable amount), which is used to replace the asset.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Fundamental Principle:**
> $$\text{Accumulated Sinking Fund Balance at year } n = \text{Original Cost} - \text{Salvage Value}$$
> $$\sum_{k=1}^{n} D(1+i)^{n-k} = P - S$$

### 2.1.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

In the chemical industry, the Sinking Fund Method is used for:
- **Power plant boilers** and steam generators
- **Distillation columns** with 20–30 year design lives
- **Compressors and turbines** with predictable service life
- **Storage tanks** and pressure vessels

It is favored by **public-sector chemical units (like FACT, BPCL Kochi, IOCL)** because it ensures capital availability for mandatory statutory replacements.

---

## 2.2 Units of Production Method — Operational Logic

This method is purely **activity-driven**. The total depreciable base is divided by the *expected total units of service* the asset will deliver over its lifetime. The resulting per-unit rate is then multiplied by *actual units consumed each year*.

### 2.2.1 Step-by-Step Operational Logic

- **Step 1:** Estimate the **Total Service Capacity (T)** in physical units (e.g., hours, km, tonnes).
- **Step 2:** Compute **Depreciation per Unit (d)** = (P − S) / T.
- **Step 3:** Track **Actual Units Used (U_k)** in year *k*.
- **Step 4:** Annual Depreciation (D_k) = d × U_k.
- **Step 5:** Accumulated Depreciation at year *k* = Σ D_j for j = 1 to k.
- **Step 6:** Book Value at year *k* = P − Σ D_j.

### 2.2.2 The "Stop-Loss" Rule

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Rule for Board Exams:** Depreciation must **STOP** the moment Accumulated Depreciation reaches the depreciable limit (P − S). Even if the asset is still in use beyond estimated life, no further depreciation is charged. Conversely, **Total Units Used** must NEVER exceed Total Estimated Capacity — when reached, depreciation halts.

### 2.2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Chemical industries use Units of Production Method for:
- **Centrifugal pumps** (depreciation per hour of operation)
- **Reactors** (depreciation per batch or per kg processed)
- **Compressors** (depreciation per cubic meter of gas compressed)
- **Furnaces and kilns** (depreciation per tonne of product heated)
- **Delivery tankers and forklifts** (depreciation per km)

This method provides **true matching** of depreciation expense with revenue earned, satisfying the **Matching Principle** of accounting (NEP 2020 emphasized this in the cost-accounting module).

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Master these formulas — they account for ~80% of all numerical questions in KTU exams on this topic.**

| Symbol | Formula | Description |
|---|---|---|
| **D (Sinking Fund)** | $D = \dfrac{(P - S) \cdot i}{(1+i)^{n} - 1}$ | Uniform annual sinking fund deposit |
| **D (Production Method)** | $D_k = \dfrac{P - S}{T} \cdot U_k$ | Annual depreciation = per-unit rate × units used |
| **d (per unit)** | $d = \dfrac{P - S}{T}$ | Depreciation per unit of production |
| **SF_k (Sinking Fund Balance)** | $SF_k = D \cdot \dfrac{(1+i)^{k} - 1}{i}$ | Accumulated sinking fund at year k |
| **BV_k (Book Value, Sinking Fund)** | $BV_k = P - SF_k$ | Book value at end of year k |
| **AD_k (Accumulated Depr.)** | $AD_k = \sum_{j=1}^{k} D_j$ | Total depreciation accumulated through year k |
| **BV_k (Book Value, Production)** | $BV_k = P - AD_k$ | Book value at end of year k for production method |
| **P** | Original (Acquisition) Cost of asset | Total capitalized cost |
| **S** | Salvage / Scrap Value | Estimated residual value |
| **n** | Useful life in years | Estimated economic life |
| **i** | Sinking fund interest rate (decimal) | Annual compound rate on fund |
| **T** | Total estimated units (lifetime capacity) | E.g., hours, km, tonnes |
| **U_k** | Actual units used in year k | Measured production/usage |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation — Sinking Fund Annual Deposit (D)

### 3.1.1 Setup

Let:
- $P$ = Original cost of asset
- $S$ = Salvage value
- $n$ = Useful life in years
- $i$ = Annual interest rate on the sinking fund (expressed as decimal)
- $D$ = Uniform annual deposit made at the *end* of each year

### 3.1.2 Build the Future Value Equation

Consider the deposits year by year. A deposit made at the **end of year 1** earns interest for $(n-1)$ years before retirement at end of year $n$. A deposit made at the **end of year 2** earns interest for $(n-2)$ years, and so on. The deposit at end of year $n$ earns **no interest**.

The **Future Value of an Ordinary Annuity** formula gives the total accumulated sinking fund at end of year $n$:

$$\text{Sinking Fund Balance at end of year } n = D(1+i)^{n-1} + D(1+i)^{n-2} + \cdots + D(1+i)^{1} + D(1+i)^{0}$$

### 3.1.3 Recognize the Geometric Series

The right-hand side is a geometric progression with first term $a = D$, common ratio $r = (1+i)$, and $n$ terms. The sum of a geometric series is:

$$S_n = a \cdot \dfrac{r^{n} - 1}{r - 1}$$

Applying this directly:

$$\text{SF Balance at year } n = D \cdot \dfrac{(1+i)^{n} - 1}{(1+i) - 1}$$

### 3.1.4 Equate to the Depreciable Base

For the fund to perfectly replace the asset, the accumulated balance at year $n$ must equal the depreciable amount $(P - S)$:

$$D \cdot \dfrac{(1+i)^{n} - 1}{i} = P - S$$

### 3.1.5 Solve for D

Multiply both sides by $i$:

$$D \cdot \left[(1+i)^{n} - 1\right] = i \cdot (P - S)$$

Divide both sides by $\left[(1+i)^{n} - 1\right]$:

$$\boxed{D = \dfrac{(P - S) \cdot i}{(1+i)^{n} - 1}}$$

This is the **canonical Sinking Fund depreciation formula**.

### 3.1.6 Derive the Sinking Fund Balance at Year k

By identical reasoning, the accumulated balance at the end of year $k$ (where $k < n$) is:

$$SF_k = D \cdot \dfrac{(1+i)^{k} - 1}{i}$$

Substituting the expression for $D$ from above gives a fully expanded form, but the compact formula above is the working form used in KTU problems.

### 3.1.7 Derive the Book Value at Year k

$$\boxed{BV_k = P - SF_k = P - D \cdot \dfrac{(1+i)^{k} - 1}{i}}$$

---

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation — Units of Production Depreciation

### 3.2.1 Setup

Let:
- $P$ = Original cost
- $S$ = Salvage value
- $T$ = Total estimated lifetime capacity (units)
- $U_k$ = Units produced/used in year $k$
- $d$ = Depreciation rate per unit

### 3.2.2 Define the Per-Unit Rate

The total depreciation over the asset's life is $(P - S)$. This is to be distributed evenly over the $T$ units of expected service. Therefore, the **depreciation per unit** is:

$$d = \dfrac{\text{Total Depreciable Cost}}{\text{Total Units of Capacity}} = \dfrac{P - S}{T}$$

### 3.2.3 Annual Depreciation Formula

In year $k$, when $U_k$ units were actually consumed:

$$\boxed{D_k = d \cdot U_k = \dfrac{(P - S)}{T} \cdot U_k}$$

### 3.2.4 Accumulated Depreciation

Running sum from year 1 to year $k$:

$$AD_k = \sum_{j=1}^{k} D_j = \dfrac{(P - S)}{T} \cdot \sum_{j=1}^{k} U_j$$

### 3.2.5 Book Value at End of Year k

$$\boxed{BV_k = P - AD_k = P - \dfrac{(P - S)}{T} \cdot \sum_{j=1}^{k} U_j}$$

---

## 3.3 Illustrative Analytical Matrix

### 3.3.1 Sinking Fund Method — Year-wise Schedule

For **P = ₹10,00,000**, **S = ₹1,00,000**, **n = 5 years**, **i = 10%**:

First, compute $D$:
$$D = \dfrac{(10{,}00{,}000 - 1{,}00{,}000) \cdot 0.10}{(1.10)^{5} - 1} = \dfrac{9{,}00{,}000 \cdot 0.10}{1.61051 - 1} = \dfrac{90{,}000}{0.61051} = ₹1{,}47{,}377.78$$

| Year (k) | Depreciation Charge (D) | Interest on SF [10% × previous balance] | SF Balance End of Year | Book Value End of Year |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | — | — | 0 | 10,00,000 |
| 1 | 1,47,377.78 | 0 | 1,47,377.78 | 8,52,622.22 |
| 2 | 1,47,377.78 | 14,737.78 | 3,09,493.34 | 6,90,506.66 |
| 3 | 1,47,377.78 | 30,949.33 | 4,87,820.45 | 5,12,179.55 |
| 4 | 1,47,377.78 | 48,782.05 | 6,83,980.28 | 3,16,019.72 |
| 5 | 1,47,377.78 | 68,398.03 | 8,99,756.09 | 1,00,243.91 (≈ ₹1,00,000 = S ✓) |

> [!NOTE]
> The small rounding error of ₹243.91 is acceptable in KTU answers; the closing book value should be ≈ Salvage Value.

### 3.3.2 Units of Production Method — Year-wise Schedule

For **P = ₹5,00,000**, **S = ₹50,000**, **T = 1,00,000 hours**, with usage data:

| Year | Units Used (U_k) | Per-Unit Rate (d) | Annual Depreciation (D_k) | Accumulated Depreciation | Book Value End of Year |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 25,000 | 4.50 | 1,12,500 | 1,12,500 | 3,87,500 |
| 2 | 20,000 | 4.50 | 90,000 | 2,02,500 | 2,97,500 |
| 3 | 30,000 | 4.50 | 1,35,000 | 3,37,500 | 1,62,500 |
| 4 | 15,000 | 4.50 | 67,500 | 4,05,000 | 95,000 |
| 5 | 10,000 | 4.50 | 45,000 | 4,50,000 | 50,000 (= S ✓) |
| **Total** | **1,00,000** | — | **4,50,000** | — | — |

Per-unit rate calculation: $d = (5{,}00{,}000 - 50{,}000) / 1{,}00{,}000 = ₹4.50$ per hour.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Sinking Fund Method — Calculation Flow

The following Mermaid flowchart maps the **decision and computation pipeline** for the Sinking Fund Method. It traces the journey from raw asset data inputs through the intermediate calculations to the final outputs (Annual Deposit, Book Value, Replacement Fund).

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Start: Asset Acquired] --> A1[Input: Original Cost P]
    A1 --> A2[Input: Salvage Value S]
    A2 --> A3[Input: Useful Life n years]
    A3 --> A4[Input: Interest Rate i percent]
    A4 --> A5[Compute Depreciable Base = P minus S]
    A5 --> A6[Compute Annuity Factor = open bracket 1 plus i close bracket to power n minus 1]
    A6 --> A7[Compute Annual Deposit D equals P minus S times i divided by Annuity Factor]
    A7 --> A8[Loop: Year k equals 1 to n]
    A8 --> A9[Credit Depreciation A/c with D]
    A9 --> A10[Credit Sinking Fund A/c with D]
    A10 --> A11[Add Interest equals previous balance times i to SF A/c]
    A11 --> A12[Compute Book Value BVk equals P minus SF balance]
    A12 --> A13{Year equals n?}
    A13 -- No --> A8
    A13 -- Yes --> A14[Verify SF Balance equals P minus S]
    A14 --> A15[Withdraw SF Balance to Replace Asset]
    A15 --> A16[End]
```

### 4.1.1 Node Description Table

| Node ID | Functional Role |
|---|---|
| A0 | Process Entry Point |
| A1 to A4 | Input Parameter Aggregation |
| A5 | Pre-Computation Step |
| A6, A7 | Core Formula Computation Block |
| A8 | Iteration Loop Controller |
| A9, A10, A11 | Ledger Posting Module |
| A12 | Output Generation |
| A13 | Conditional Check |
| A14, A15 | Verification and Disbursement |
| A16 | Process Termination |

---

## 4.2 Units of Production Method — Calculation Flow

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the **usage-driven computational pipeline** for the Units of Production Method.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    B0[Start: Asset Acquired] --> B1[Input: Original Cost P]
    B1 --> B2[Input: Salvage Value S]
    B2 --> B3[Input: Total Estimated Capacity T]
    B3 --> B4[Input: Actual Units Used Uk per year]
    B4 --> B5[Compute Depreciable Base = P minus S]
    B5 --> B6[Compute Per Unit Rate d = P minus S divided by T]
    B6 --> B7[Loop: Year k equals 1 to n]
    B7 --> B8[Read Actual Units Uk for year k]
    B8 --> B9[Compute Annual Depreciation Dk = d times Uk]
    B9 --> B10[Update Accumulated Depreciation ADk]
    B10 --> B11[Compute Book Value BVk = P minus ADk]
    B11 --> B12{ADk greater than or equal to P minus S?}
    B12 -- No --> B7
    B12 -- Yes --> B13[Stop Depreciation]
    B13 --> B14[Maintain BV at S]
    B14 --> B15[End]
```

### 4.2.1 Node Description Table

| Node ID | Functional Role |
|---|---|
| B0 | Process Entry Point |
| B1 to B4 | Input Parameter Aggregation |
| B5, B6 | Pre-Computation of Per-Unit Rate |
| B7 | Year Iteration Controller |
| B8, B9, B10, B11 | Per-Year Computation Block |
| B12 | Conditional Stop-Loss Check |
| B13, B14 | Termination State Lock |
| B15 | Process Exit |

---

## 4.3 Comparative Functional Architecture

The diagram below shows how the **two methods are selected based on asset characteristics** — a critical conceptual question frequently asked in KTU exams.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    C1[New Capital Asset] --> C2{Asset Type?}
    C2 -- Long life, time based wear --> C3[Sinking Fund Method]
    C2 -- Output based wear --> C4[Units of Production Method]
    C3 --> C5[Inputs: P, S, n, i]
    C4 --> C6[Inputs: P, S, T, Uk]
    C5 --> C7[Annual Charge D equals uniform]
    C6 --> C8[Annual Charge Dk equals variable]
    C7 --> C9[Adds interest on fund]
    C8 --> C9
    C9 --> C10[Goes to Profit and Loss A/c]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]

**Q: Define the Sinking Fund Method of depreciation. Why is it considered more rational than the Straight Line Method?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> The Sinking Fund Method is a depreciation technique in which a **uniform annual sum (D)** is set aside and invested in a separate interest-bearing fund such that the accumulated amount at the end of the asset's useful life equals the **depreciable cost (P − S)** of the asset.

> It is more rational than the Straight Line Method because:
> - **(i)** It accounts for the **time value of money** by incorporating compound interest on accumulated depreciation (SL Method ignores interest entirely).
> - **(ii)** It ensures that the **exact cash required to replace the asset is physically available** at the end of useful life, providing true replacement provisioning.
> - **(iii)** The annual charge remains uniform, simplifying budgeting in long-gestation chemical projects.

**[Mark Allocation: Definition 1M + Two reasons 1M each = 3 Marks]**

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]

**Q: A machine has an original cost of ₹4,00,000 and a salvage value of ₹40,000. The total estimated life is 1,20,000 working hours. Compute the depreciation per hour.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> Given: P = ₹4,00,000; S = ₹40,000; T = 1,20,000 hours

> Depreciation per unit (per hour):
> $$d = \dfrac{P - S}{T} = \dfrac{4{,}00{,}000 - 40{,}000}{1{,}20{,}000}$$

> $$d = \dfrac{3{,}60{,}000}{1{,}20{,}000} = ₹3.00 \text{ per hour}$$

> **∴ Depreciation per hour = ₹3.00**

**[Mark Allocation: Formula 1M + Substitution 1M + Final Answer 1M = 3 Marks]**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Rule:** Module-level questions carry **14 marks** with internal choice. Each sub-part (a) and (b) carries **7 marks**. Both alternatives are presented below for complete practice.

---

### Question A [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]

**A chemical plant in Kochi purchases a reactor for ₹20,00,000. It has an estimated useful life of 10 years and a salvage value of ₹2,00,000 at the end. The company establishes a Sinking Fund earning interest at 8% per annum.**

#### Part (a) [7 Marks]
**Calculate the uniform annual sinking fund deposit and prepare the Sinking Fund Account for the entire 10-year life.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify the Parameters:**
- $P = ₹20{,}00{,}000$
- $S = ₹2{,}00{,}000$
- $n = 10$ years
- $i = 0.08$ (8%)

**Step 2 — Apply the Sinking Fund Formula:**

$$D = \dfrac{(P - S) \cdot i}{(1+i)^{n} - 1}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute Values:**

$$D = \dfrac{(20{,}00{,}000 - 2{,}00{,}000) \cdot 0.08}{(1.08)^{10} - 1}$$

$$D = \dfrac{18{,}00{,}000 \cdot 0.08}{2.158925 - 1}$$

**Step 4 — Compute Annuity Factor:**

$(1.08)^{10} = 2.158925$

$(1.08)^{10} - 1 = 1.158925$

**Step 5 — Final Calculation:**

$$D = \dfrac{1{,}44{,}000}{1.158925} = ₹1{,}24{,}244.40$$

**∴ Annual Sinking Fund Deposit = ₹1,24,244.40 (approx.)**

**Step 6 — Sinking Fund Account (₹ in lakhs):**

| Year | Opening Balance | Annual Deposit (D) | Interest @ 8% | Closing Balance |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 0.00 | 1.2424 | 0.00 | 1.2424 |
| 2 | 1.2424 | 1.2424 | 0.0994 | 2.5843 |
| 3 | 2.5843 | 1.2424 | 0.2067 | 4.0334 |
| 4 | 4.0334 | 1.2424 | 0.3227 | 5.5985 |
| 5 | 5.5985 | 1.2424 | 0.4479 | 7.2888 |
| 6 | 7.2888 | 1.2424 | 0.5831 | 9.1143 |
| 7 | 9.1143 | 1.2424 | 0.7291 | 11.0858 |
| 8 | 11.0858 | 1.2424 | 0.8869 | 13.2151 |
| 9 | 13.2151 | 1.2424 | 1.0572 | 15.5147 |
| 10 | 15.5147 | 1.2424 | 1.2412 | 17.9983 ≈ 18.00 (= P − S ✓) |

**Verification:** The closing balance of ₹18,00,000 (≈₹17,99,830 after rounding) equals the depreciable amount (P − S) = ₹18,00,000. ✓

**[Mark Split: Formula derivation 2M + D calculation 2M + SF Account 3M = 7 Marks]**

#### Part (b) [7 Marks]
**If the same reactor is depreciated using the Units of Production Method with total estimated life of 50,000 batches and the actual usage is: Year 1 = 6,000, Year 2 = 7,500, Year 3 = 8,000, Year 4 = 5,500, Year 5 = 8,000 batches. Calculate the depreciation for each year and the book value at the end of Year 3.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify Parameters:**
- $P = ₹20{,}00{,}000$
- $S = ₹2{,}00{,}000$
- $T = 50{,}000$ batches
- Depreciable base = $P - S = ₹18{,}00{,}000$

**Step 2 — Per-Unit Depreciation Rate:**

$$d = \dfrac{P - S}{T} = \dfrac{18{,}00{,}000}{50{,}000} = ₹36 \text{ per batch}$$

**Step 3 — Year-wise Depreciation Schedule:**

| Year | Batches Used (U_k) | Depreciation Rate (d) | Annual Depreciation (D_k) | Accumulated Depreciation | Book Value End of Year |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 6,000 | 36 | 2,16,000 | 2,16,000 | 17,84,000 |
| 2 | 7,500 | 36 | 2,70,000 | 4,86,000 | 15,14,000 |
| 3 | 8,000 | 36 | 2,88,000 | 7,74,000 | 12,26,000 |
| 4 | 5,500 | 36 | 1,98,000 | 9,72,000 | 10,28,000 |
| 5 | 8,000 | 36 | 2,88,000 | 12,60,000 | 7,40,000 |

**Step 4 — Book Value at End of Year 3:**

$$BV_3 = P - AD_3 = 20{,}00{,}000 - 7{,}74{,}000 = ₹12{,}26{,}000$$

**∴ Book Value at end of Year 3 = ₹12,26,000**

**[Mark Split: Per-unit rate 2M + Year-wise table 3M + BV computation 2M = 7 Marks]**

---

### Question B [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Adapted — Alternative Choice]

**A compressor in a petrochemical unit costs ₹8,00,000 with an estimated salvage value of ₹80,000. The company wants to compare two depreciation approaches:**

#### Part (a) [7 Marks]
**Calculate the annual depreciation and book value at the end of each year for the first 4 years using the Sinking Fund Method, assuming n = 8 years and sinking fund interest = 6% per annum.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Parameters:**
- $P = ₹8{,}00{,}000$
- $S = ₹80{,}000$
- $n = 8$ years
- $i = 0.06$

**Step 2 — Compute Annual Deposit D:**

$$D = \dfrac{(8{,}00{,}000 - 80{,}000) \cdot 0.06}{(1.06)^{8} - 1}$$

$(1.06)^{8} = 1.593848$

$(1.06)^{8} - 1 = 0.593848$

$$D = \dfrac{7{,}20{,}000 \cdot 0.06}{0.593848} = \dfrac{43{,}200}{0.593848} = ₹72{,}752.65$$

**Step 3 — Sinking Fund Balance & Book Value:**

$$SF_k = D \cdot \dfrac{(1.06)^{k} - 1}{0.06}$$

$$BV_k = P - SF_k$$

| Year (k) | D (₹) | Interest @ 6% | SF Balance | Book Value |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 72,752.65 | 0 | 72,752.65 | 7,27,247.35 |
| 2 | 72,752.65 | 4,365.16 | 1,49,870.46 | 6,50,129.54 |
| 3 | 72,752.65 | 8,992.23 | 2,31,615.34 | 5,68,384.66 |
| 4 | 72,752.65 | 13,896.92 | 3,18,264.91 | 4,81,735.09 |

**Verification at Year 8:** SF Balance ≈ ₹7,20,000 = P − S ✓

**[Mark Split: D formula 2M + Numerical calculation 2M + Table 3M = 7 Marks]**

#### Part (b) [7 Marks]
**Now consider the same compressor is depreciated using Units of Production Method. Total expected service life = 40,000 hours. Usage: Yr 1 = 6,000 hrs, Yr 2 = 5,500 hrs, Yr 3 = 4,500 hrs. (i) Find the depreciation charge for each year. (ii) Find the book value at the end of Year 3. (iii) Comment on which method is more suitable for the compressor.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Per-Unit Rate:**

$$d = \dfrac{P - S}{T} = \dfrac{8{,}00{,}000 - 80{,}000}{40{,}000} = \dfrac{7{,}20{,}000}{40{,}000} = ₹18 \text{ per hour}$$

**Step 2 — Annual Depreciation:**

| Year | Hours Used | Depreciation (₹) | Accumulated Depreciation | Book Value |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 6,000 | 1,08,000 | 1,08,000 | 6,92,000 |
| 2 | 5,500 | 99,000 | 2,07,000 | 5,93,000 |
| 3 | 4,500 | 81,000 | 2,88,000 | 5,12,000 |

**Step 3 — Book Value at End of Year 3:**

$$BV_3 = 8{,}00{,}000 - 2{,}88{,}000 = ₹5{,}12{,}000$$

**Step 4 — Comparative Comment:**

The **Units of Production Method is more suitable for a compressor** because its wear-and-tear is directly proportional to operational hours rather than the passage of calendar time. A compressor running continuously wears faster than an idle one. The Sinking Fund Method, while fiscally prudent, ignores this physical reality. In chemical plants where compressors are critical path equipment, units-of-production aligns depreciation expense more accurately with revenue generated.

**[Mark Split: Per-unit rate 1M + Depreciation table 3M + BV computation 1M + Justification 2M = 7 Marks]**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the Salvage Value:** Many students incorrectly use $P$ instead of $(P - S)$ in the Sinking Fund numerator. Always subtract salvage value first.
>
> 2. **Interest Rate Format:** Convert percentage to decimal: $8\% = 0.08$, not $8$. An entire answer can be wrong if this is missed.
>
> 3. **Annuity Factor Mistakes:** $(1+i)^{n}$ must be computed accurately. Use $i = 0.08$, $n = 10$: $(1.08)^{10} = 2.15892$ (verified). Round consistently — preferably to 5 decimals.
>
> 4. **Confusion between Book Value and Sinking Fund Balance:** Book Value = Original Cost − Sinking Fund Balance (NOT Original Cost − Depreciation Charge). Examiners specifically check this distinction.
>
> 5. **Units of Production — Wrong "Total":** Some students use *total actual units* in the denominator. The denominator must be **estimated total lifetime capacity**, not actual units consumed.
>
> 6. **Year-1 Interest Mistake (Sinking Fund):** Interest in Year 1 is **ZERO** because the deposit is made at year-end. No "opening balance" exists in Year 1.
>
> 7. **Skipping the Verification Step:** Always verify that the closing Sinking Fund Balance equals $(P - S)$. This single line earns a "completeness" mark.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Sinking Fund Method** allocates depreciation uniformly but provides for asset replacement through an interest-bearing fund.
- The canonical formula is $D = (P - S) \cdot i \div \left[(1+i)^{n} - 1\right]$.
- The Sinking Fund Balance after $k$ years equals $D \cdot \left[(1+i)^{k} - 1\right] \div i$.
- **Book Value** at year $k$ = Original Cost − Sinking Fund Balance at year $k$.
- **Units of Production Method** ties depreciation to physical usage rather than time.
- The per-unit rate is $d = (P - S) \div T$ where $T$ is the *estimated* total lifetime capacity.
- Annual depreciation $D_k = d \cdot U_k$, where $U_k$ is actual usage in year $k$.
- Depreciation must **stop** once accumulated depreciation reaches $(P - S)$, regardless of continued usage.
- Sinking Fund Method is preferred for **long-life, capital-intensive** assets with predictable replacement timelines.
- Units of Production Method is preferred for **usage-sensitive** equipment like compressors, pumps, and reactors.
- Always state the assumptions clearly: *ordinary annuity* (end-of-year deposits), *uniform life*, *constant interest rate*.
- Verify your numerical answer: Closing Sinking Fund Balance should equal $(P - S)$ within rounding tolerance.
- Use a **5-decimal rounding convention** for annuity factors to maintain numerical accuracy across KTU valuation scripts.
- **NEP 2020 alignment:** The Units of Production Method reflects the *Matching Principle* and *Fair Value Accounting* emphasized in the revised syllabus.
- Forgetting to subtract **Salvage Value** is the single most common error across both methods — double-check before submission.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Factors Affecting Investment and Production Costs
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT757 - ECONOMICS AND MANAGEMENT OF CHEMICAL INDUSTRIES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Factors Affecting Investment and Production Costs

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Investment in Chemical Industries — Formal Definition

In the context of chemical process industries, **Investment** (also called **Capital Investment** or **Capital Cost**) refers to the total sum of money required to design, erect, commission, and operate a chemical plant until it begins generating revenue. As per the KTU 2024 syllabus framework (following Peters & Timmerhaus, and Sinnott-Towler conventions), capital investment is broadly bifurcated into:

- **Fixed Capital Investment (FCI)** — One-time, non-recurring expenditure on tangible assets (land, buildings, equipment, installation, piping, instrumentation, electricals, insulation, painting, engineering supervision, contingency, etc.).
- **Working Capital (WC)** — Recurring short-term capital tied up in raw material inventory, finished goods, accounts receivable, and cash reserves needed to keep the plant "breathing" between successive cycles.

$$C_{TCI} = C_{FCI} + C_{WC}$$

where $C_{TCI}$ is the **Total Capital Investment**, $C_{FCI}$ is the Fixed Capital Investment, and $C_{WC}$ is the Working Capital (typically **10% to 20%** of FCI for chemical plants).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The KTU Module 2 explicitly requires students to distinguish between *Investment* (one-time) and *Production Cost* (recurring annual expenses). Many students lose marks by confusing these — remember: **Investment = money OUT to BUILD**, **Production Cost = money OUT to RUN**.

### 1.2 Production Cost — Formal Definition

**Production Cost** (also called **Manufacturing Cost** or **Operating Cost**) is the recurring annual expenditure incurred to convert raw materials into saleable finished products. It includes:

- **Direct Production Costs (DPC):** Raw materials, utilities (steam, electricity, cooling water, refrigeration), operating labor, supervisory labor, maintenance, operating supplies, royalties, catalysts, and solvents.
- **Fixed Charges (FC):** Depreciation, property taxes, insurance, rent (if applicable).
- **Plant Overhead (PO):** Medical, safety, recreation, cafeteria, general plant maintenance, plant protection, warehouse, payroll overhead.

The total cost of production is then completed by adding **General Expenses (GE)** such as administration, R&D, distribution, and selling expenses.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Annual Cost of Production (C_M)** is the engineering term used in KTU answer keys. Always express it in units of **₹/year** or **$/year**, never in ₹/kg unless explicitly asked.

### 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — "Building vs. Running a Restaurant"

Imagine you want to open a **South Indian restaurant** in Kerala:

| Real-World Analogy | Engineering Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Land + Building + Furniture + Kitchen Equipment | **Fixed Capital Investment (FCI)** |
| Initial stock of rice, dal, oil, spices, cash for daily operations | **Working Capital (WC)** |
| Total money needed to OPEN the restaurant | **Total Capital Investment (TCI)** |
| Daily costs — rice, dal, gas, electricity, cook's salary | **Direct Production Cost** |
| Monthly EMI on furniture, restaurant insurance, property tax | **Fixed Charges** |
| Cleaning staff, manager's salary, pest control | **Plant Overhead** |
| Total daily operational cost of running | **Production Cost (C_M)** |

The restaurant owner must decide:
- **Where** to build? → Factor of *Location*
- **How big** to build? → Factor of *Plant Size/Capacity*
- **How fancy** the interiors? → Factor of *Process Complexity & Materials of Construction*
- **Which recipe** to use? → Factor of *Technology*

Similarly, a chemical plant's investment and production costs are dictated by a **constellation of factors** that chemical engineers must master.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Factors Affecting Investment (Capital Cost)

The factors influencing capital cost are **interlinked** and **multiplicative** in their effect. They are best understood as a layered hierarchy:

#### A. Plant Size & Capacity
- Larger plants benefit from **economies of scale**.
- The empirical **Six-Tenths Rule** (originally proposed by Williams in 1947) states that if the cost of a plant with capacity $Q_1$ is $C_1$, then the cost of a similar plant with capacity $Q_2$ is:

$$C_2 = C_1 \left( \frac{Q_2}{Q_1} \right)^{n}$$

where $n \approx 0.6$ to **0.8** depending on the type of process equipment/process. For chemical process plants, the standard exponent adopted by KTU and most international curricula is **$n = 0.6$**.

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Intuition:** Doubling the capacity does NOT double the cost. With the six-tenths rule, doubling capacity only multiplies cost by $2^{0.6} \approx 1.516$. This is why world-scale plants dominate petrochemicals — bigger is genuinely cheaper per unit.

#### B. Plant Location
- Influences land cost, civil construction cost, freight on raw materials, freight on finished goods, labor wages, utility tariffs, and tax structure.
- Coastal locations (e.g., Kochi, Mangalore) favor import-dependent petrochemical industries; inland locations favor agro-based or coal-based chemical complexes.

#### C. Process Complexity & Technology
- Complex multi-product, multi-stream plants with cryogenic, high-pressure, or vacuum operations require expensive materials of construction (stainless steel, Inconel, titanium, Monel, Hastelloy) and hence dramatically higher capital cost.
- Continuous processes generally cost more upfront than batch but have lower operating cost per kg.

#### D. Materials of Construction
- Mild steel vs. SS-304 vs. SS-316 vs. Inconel-600 vs. Glass-lined steel — the price differential can be **5× to 50×** for the same equipment.
- Corrosive service (e.g., HCl, H2SO4, chlor-alkali) mandates exotic alloys.

#### E. Time of Investment (Inflation & Cost Indices)
- Cost of equipment changes with time. The **Chemical Engineering Plant Cost Index (CEPCI)** published monthly by *Chemical Engineering* magazine (USA) and the **Marshall & Swift (M&S) Index** are the two standard indices.
- The cost at a future date is escalated as:

$$C_{present} = C_{past} \times \frac{I_{present}}{I_{past}}$$

where $I_{present}$ and $I_{past}$ are cost index values at the respective dates.

#### F. Design Margin & Contingency
- Engineers typically add **5% to 15%** contingency on FCI to absorb unforeseen scope creep.

---

### 2.2 Factors Affecting Production Cost (Manufacturing Cost)

Production cost factors are categorized by **cost-of-sales hierarchy**:

#### A. Raw Material Cost
- Often the **single largest contributor** (40–60%) to total production cost in bulk chemicals.
- Affected by: market price volatility, freight, purity required, storage losses, and contract terms (spot vs. long-term).

#### B. Utilities (Energy Cost)
- Steam (low, medium, high pressure), cooling water, chilled water, brine, electricity, fuel oil, compressed air, inert gas.
- **Energy-intensive processes** (electrolytic chlor-alkali, ammonia synthesis, ethylene cracking) can have utility cost exceeding raw material cost.

#### C. Labor Cost
- Operating labor + supervisory labor + payroll overhead.
- A heavily automated plant has lower labor cost but higher instrumentation investment.

#### D. Maintenance Cost
- Typically **2% to 10%** of FCI per year depending on corrosivity, service severity, and plant age.

#### E. Depreciation
- Non-cash charge but a real production cost component.
- **Straight-Line Method (SLM):**

$$D_{annual} = \frac{C_{FCI} - S_v}{n}$$

where $S_v$ is the salvage value and $n$ is the service life in years.

- **Declining Balance Method (DBM):**

$$D_{annual} = C_{book} \times \beta$$

where $\beta$ is the fixed percentage (commonly $2/n$ for double-declining).

#### F. Insurance, Taxes, and Royalties
- Insurance: typically **0.4% to 1%** of FCI/year.
- Property tax: varies by jurisdiction.
- Royalties: paid for licensed technology (e.g., UOP, Lummus, Shell process licences).

#### G. Plant Overhead & General Expenses
- Allocated as a percentage of direct labor, FCI, or production cost depending on the company's accounting policy.

---

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Memorize this table — questions directly test these formulas. **Never use `|` symbol inside the table; it breaks markdown rendering.**

| Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|
| $C_{TCI} = C_{FCI} + C_{WC}$ | Total Capital Investment | ₹ (Lakhs/Crores) |
| $C_{FCI} = C_{equipment} + C_{installation} + C_{piping} + C_{instrumentation} + C_{electrical} + C_{building} + C_{yard improvement} + C_{service facilities} + C_{engineering} + C_{contingency}$ | Fixed Capital Investment (delivered-equipment basis via Lang factor) | ₹ |
| $C_2 = C_1 \left( \dfrac{Q_2}{Q_1} \right)^{0.6}$ | Six-Tenths Rule | Same currency |
| $C_{present} = C_{past} \times \dfrac{I_{present}}{I_{past}}$ | Time Adjustment via Cost Index | Same currency |
| $C_{FCI} = f_L \times \sum C_{equipment,delivered}$ | Lang Factor Method | ₹ |
| $C_{WC} = (0.10 \text{ to } 0.20) \times C_{FCI}$ | Working Capital Estimate | ₹ |
| $D_{SLM} = \dfrac{C_{FCI} - S_v}{n}$ | Straight-Line Depreciation (annual) | ₹/year |
| $D_{DBM} = C_{book} \times \beta$ | Declining-Balance Depreciation (annual) | ₹/year |
| $C_M = C_{RM} + C_{util} + C_{labor} + C_{maint} + C_{dep} + C_{tax,ins} + C_{PO} + C_{GE}$ | Total Manufacturing Cost | ₹/year |

### 2.4 The Lang Factor Method — A KTU Favorite

Lang (1948) proposed that for a solid processing plant, **the fixed capital investment is approximately a multiple of the delivered equipment cost**:

$$C_{FCI} = f_L \cdot \sum C_{equipment,delivered}$$

| Type of Plant | Lang Factor ($f_L$) |
|---|---|
| Solid processing (cement, minerals) | **3.10** |
| Solid–Fluid processing (paper, food) | **3.63** |
| Fluid processing (petroleum, petrochemicals) | **4.74** |

> [!NOTE]
> The Lang factor is **conceptually easy** but increasingly considered outdated by industry. KTU, however, still tests it. Always quote the *type of plant* when applying it.

## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Analytical Problems

### 3.1 Derivation: Six-Tenths Rule — Why the Exponent 0.6?

The six-tenths rule is empirically derived from observed historical cost-capacity data. Let us derive its mathematical basis.

Consider a process unit with capacity $Q$ and cost $C$. Assume cost scales with some combination of the surface area and volume of the equipment:

$$C \propto Q^{\alpha}$$

Taking logarithms:

$$\log C = \alpha \log Q + \log k$$

For two different plants with capacities $Q_1, Q_2$ and costs $C_1, C_2$:

$$\log C_2 - \log C_1 = \alpha (\log Q_2 - \log Q_1)$$

$$\log \frac{C_2}{C_1} = \alpha \log \frac{Q_2}{Q_1}$$

$$\frac{C_2}{C_1} = \left( \frac{Q_2}{Q_1} \right)^{\alpha}$$

Empirically, for chemical process plants, plotting $\log C$ vs. $\log Q$ yields a straight line with slope $\alpha \approx 0.6$. This reflects the fact that most equipment cost scales with **surface area (length²)**, while capacity scales with **volume (length³)**:

$$\text{Surface area} \propto (\text{Volume})^{2/3}$$

Hence for pure equipment:

$$\alpha = \frac{2}{3} \approx 0.67$$

For whole chemical plants (which include tanks, piping, civil works that scale differently), the empirical blend yields **$\alpha \approx 0.6$**.

### 3.2 Worked Example 1 — Six-Tenths Rule with Cost Indexing

**Problem:** A 50,000 tonnes/year ammonia plant cost ₹150 crores when commissioned in 2010. Estimate the cost of a 1,00,000 tonnes/year plant to be built in 2025. The CEPCI values were **550 in 2010** and **820 in 2025**.

**Solution:**

Step 1 — Apply cost index to bring the 2010 cost to 2025 levels:

$$
C_{2010 \rightarrow 2025,\,at\,50,000\,t/y} = 150 \times \frac{820}{550}
$$

$$
C_{2010 \rightarrow 2025,\,at\,50,000\,t/y} = 150 \times 1.4909 = 223.64 \text{ crores}
$$

Step 2 — Apply Six-Tenths Rule to scale from 50,000 t/y to 1,00,000 t/y:

$$
C_{100,000} = C_{50,000} \times \left( \frac{100{,}000}{50{,}000} \right)^{0.6}
$$

$$
C_{100,000} = 223.64 \times (2)^{0.6}
$$

$$
C_{100,000} = 223.64 \times 1.5157
$$

$$
\boxed{C_{100,000} \approx 339.0 \text{ crores}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Doubling the plant capacity **only** increased the cost by approximately 51.6%. This is the *economy of scale* in action — fundamental to all KTU cost estimation problems.

### 3.3 Worked Example 2 — Lang Factor Method for Fixed Capital

**Problem:** A fluid-processing plant has the following delivered equipment costs: Reactors ₹2.5 Cr, Distillation Columns ₹1.8 Cr, Heat Exchangers ₹1.2 Cr, Pumps & Compressors ₹0.8 Cr, Vessels ₹0.7 Cr. Compute FCI, TCI (assuming WC = 15% of FCI), and annual manufacturing cost assuming production cost is 25% of TCI per year.

**Solution:**

Step 1 — Total delivered equipment cost:

$$
\sum C_{equipment} = 2.5 + 1.8 + 1.2 + 0.8 + 0.7 = 7.0 \text{ Cr}
$$

Step 2 — Apply Lang factor for fluid processing ($f_L = 4.74$):

$$
C_{FCI} = 4.74 \times 7.0 = 33.18 \text{ Cr}
$$

Step 3 — Working Capital:

$$
C_{WC} = 0.15 \times 33.18 = 4.977 \approx 4.98 \text{ Cr}
$$

Step 4 — Total Capital Investment:

$$
C_{TCI} = 33.18 + 4.98 = 38.16 \text{ Cr}
$$

Step 5 — Annual Production Cost:

$$
C_{M} = 0.25 \times 38.16 = 9.54 \text{ Cr/year}
$$

> [!TIP]
> Always show working capital as a **percentage of FCI**, not TCI. This is the convention followed in KTU valuation scripts.

### 3.4 Worked Example 3 — Straight-Line Depreciation

**Problem:** A heat exchanger has an FCI contribution of ₹60 lakhs, salvage value ₹6 lakhs, and service life 12 years. Compute annual depreciation and book value at the end of year 7.

**Solution:**

Step 1 — Annual depreciation (SLM):

$$
D = \frac{60 - 6}{12} = \frac{54}{12} = 4.5 \text{ lakhs/year}
$$

Step 2 — Book value at end of year 7:

$$
BV_7 = 60 - (7 \times 4.5) = 60 - 31.5 = 28.5 \text{ lakhs}
$$

Step 3 — Alternatively, in terms of salvage value:

$$
BV_7 = S_v + (n - 7) \times D = 6 + 5 \times 4.5 = 6 + 22.5 = 28.5 \text{ lakhs}
$$

Both methods agree, confirming the SLM formula's correctness.

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Sensitivity of Total Cost to Each Factor

The table below (commonly asked in KTU 14-mark questions) maps each cost driver to its **typical contribution (%)** and **leverage on FCI/Production Cost**:

| Factor | Typical Contribution to FCI (%) | Typical Contribution to Production Cost (%) | Leverage / Volatility |
|---|---|---|---|
| Plant Size & Capacity | 60–80 (via 6/10 rule) | Indirect | High |
| Materials of Construction | 15–40 | Indirect (via maintenance) | Very High |
| Process Complexity | 10–25 | 5–15 | High |
| Location & Infrastructure | 5–15 | 2–5 | Medium |
| Time of Investment | All (via cost index) | All (via escalation) | High |
| Raw Materials | — | 40–60 | Very High |
| Utilities | — | 10–30 | High |
| Labor | — | 5–15 | Low (in developed economies) |
| Maintenance | — | 2–10 | Medium |
| Depreciation & Insurance | — | 5–12 | Low–Medium |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Insight:** Raw material price volatility has the **single largest** impact on annual production cost — yet it is **NOT** captured in FCI estimation. This is why a plant with a brilliant capital cost estimate can still fail economically if feed stock prices spike.
