## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` Distinguish between **Fixed Capital Investment (FCI)** and **Working Capital (WC)**. Why is WC typically expressed as a percentage of FCI?

**Model Answer:**

FCI refers to the **one-time, non-recurring** expenditure required to design, construct, and commission a chemical plant — covering land, buildings, equipment, installation, piping, instrumentation, electricals, and engineering supervision. It is a **capitalized** expense appearing on the balance sheet.

Working Capital, in contrast, is the **recurring, short-term** capital needed to keep the plant operational — covering raw material inventory, finished goods inventory, accounts receivable, and cash reserves.

WC is expressed as a percentage of FCI (typically **10–20%**) because:

1. Both scale with plant size — bigger plants need proportionally more working capital.
2. FCI is a well-defined quantity at the design stage, making it a convenient reference.
3. Empirical industry studies have shown the WC/FCI ratio is reasonably constant across similar process plants.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** Students often confuse **Working Capital** with **Cash in Hand only**. WC is a basket of *four* components — Raw Material Inventory, Product Inventory, Accounts Receivable, and Cash Reserve. Listing only one will cost you 1 mark.

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` State and explain the **Six-Tenths Rule** for capital cost estimation. Why is the exponent 0.6 and not 1.0?

**Model Answer:**

The Six-Tenths Rule states:

$$
C_2 = C_1 \left( \frac{Q_2}{Q_1} \right)^{0.6}
$$

It relates the cost of a plant at capacity $Q_2$ to the known cost of a similar plant at capacity $Q_1$.

The exponent is **0.6 (not 1.0)** because cost does **not scale linearly** with capacity. Most equipment cost scales with surface area (length²) while capacity scales with volume (length³), giving a basic exponent of $2/3 \approx 0.67$. For whole plants — which include tanks, piping, civil works, instrumentation that scale differently — the empirical blend across diverse equipment converges to **0.6**. Hence doubling capacity increases cost by only ~52%, not 100%.

---

### Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice Pattern)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — Capacity Scaling + Cost Index + FCI Estimation

`[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper Adaptation]`

A fluid-processing chemical plant of capacity 80,000 tonnes/year was built at a cost of **₹120 crores** in the year 2018. The relevant cost indices are: **CEPCI (2018) = 570**, **CEPCI (2024) = 800**. The plant is to be expanded to a new capacity of **1,50,000 tonnes/year** with a similar process.

**(a)** Estimate the **Total Capital Investment (TCI)** of the new plant in 2024 prices, assuming Working Capital = **18%** of FCI. Apply the Six-Tenths rule. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** If the delivered equipment cost for the new plant is **₹32 crores**, estimate the Fixed Capital Investment using the **Lang factor** for a fluid-processing plant. Compare this with the value obtained in part (a) and comment. **(7 marks)**

##### Model Solution

**Part (a):**

Step 1 — Adjust the 2018 cost to 2024 using the cost index:

$$
C_{80,000,\,2024} = 120 \times \frac{800}{570} = 120 \times 1.4035 = 168.42 \text{ Cr}
$$

Step 2 — Scale to new capacity 1,50,000 t/y using Six-Tenths Rule:

$$
C_{FCI,\,new} = 168.42 \times \left( \frac{1{,}50{,}000}{80{,}000} \right)^{0.6}
$$

$$
C_{FCI,\,new} = 168.42 \times (1.875)^{0.6}
$$

Compute $(1.875)^{0.6}$:

$$
\ln(1.875) = 0.6286 \quad \Rightarrow \quad 0.6 \times 0.6286 = 0.3772
$$

$$
e^{0.3772} = 1.4583
$$

Hence:

$$
C_{FCI,\,new} = 168.42 \times 1.4583 = 245.6 \text{ Cr}
$$

Step 3 — Working Capital:

$$
C_{WC} = 0.18 \times 245.6 = 44.21 \text{ Cr}
$$

Step 4 — Total Capital Investment:

$$
\boxed{C_{TCI} = 245.6 + 44.21 = 289.81 \text{ Cr}}
$$

**Part (b):**

Step 1 — Apply Lang Factor for fluid-processing plant ($f_L = 4.74$):

$$
C_{FCI,\,Lang} = 4.74 \times 32 = 151.68 \text{ Cr}
$$

Step 2 — Add Working Capital:

$$
C_{WC,\,Lang} = 0.18 \times 151.68 = 27.30 \text{ Cr}
$$

$$
C_{TCI,\,Lang} = 151.68 + 27.30 = 178.98 \text{ Cr}
$$

Step 3 — Comparison:

| Method | FCI (₹ Cr) | TCI (₹ Cr) |
|---|---|---|
| Six-Tenths + Cost Index | **245.6** | **289.81** |
| Lang Factor | **151.68** | **178.98** |

**Comment:** The Six-Tenths method gives a **higher** estimate because it is anchored to a specific historical plant whose cost already reflects site-specific, technological, and contingency factors. The Lang method is a *rough* estimate from equipment cost only and is known to **underestimate** FCI by 15–30% for complex chemical plants. Hence Six-Tenths (when a reliable reference plant exists) is preferred.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Valuation Pitfall:** Students often compute $(1.875)^{0.6}$ incorrectly as $(1.875)^{0.6} = 1.875 \times 0.6 = 1.125$. **This is wrong** — the exponent applies to the entire ratio, not as a multiplier. Always use logarithms or a scientific calculator. Losing 1–2 marks here is common.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Annual Manufacturing Cost + Depreciation

`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 2 Pattern]`

A pharmaceutical intermediate plant has the following cost data:

- Fixed Capital Investment (FCI) = **₹50 crores**
- Working Capital = **12%** of FCI
- Annual raw material cost = **₹18 crores**
- Annual utilities (steam + power + cooling water) = **₹6 crores**
- Annual operating labor = **₹2.4 crores**
- Annual maintenance = **5%** of FCI
- Depreciation: Straight-line method, salvage value = **10%** of FCI, service life = **12 years**
- Insurance + property tax = **1.5%** of FCI
- Plant overhead = **40%** of operating labor
- General expenses = **8%** of total manufacturing cost (iterative)

**(a)** Compute the **annual depreciation**, **book value at end of year 5**, and **Working Capital**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Estimate the **Total Annual Manufacturing Cost (C_M)** by iteration. **(7 marks)**

##### Model Solution

**Part (a):**

Step 1 — Working Capital:

$$
C_{WC} = 0.12 \times 50 = 6.0 \text{ Cr}
$$

Step 2 — Salvage Value:

$$
S_v = 0.10 \times 50 = 5.0 \text{ Cr}
$$

Step 3 — Annual Depreciation (SLM):

$$
D = \frac{50 - 5}{12} = \frac{45}{12} = 3.75 \text{ Cr/year}
$$

Step 4 — Book Value at end of year 5:

$$
BV_5 = 50 - (5 \times 3.75) = 50 - 18.75 = 31.25 \text{ Cr}
$$

Or equivalently:

$$
BV_5 = S_v + (12 - 5) \times 3.75 = 5 + 26.25 = 31.25 \text{ Cr}
$$

**Part (b):**

Step 1 — List all components *excluding* GE (which is iterative):

| Component | Value (₹ Cr/year) |
|---|---|
| Raw Materials | 18.00 |
| Utilities | 6.00 |
| Operating Labor | 2.40 |
| Maintenance (5% × 50) | 2.50 |
| Depreciation | 3.75 |
| Insurance + Tax (1.5% × 50) | 0.75 |
| Plant Overhead (40% × 2.4) | 0.96 |
| **Subtotal (X)** | **34.36** |

Step 2 — Set up iterative equation. Let $C_M$ be the total manufacturing cost:

$$
C_M = X + 0.08 \cdot C_M
$$

$$
C_M (1 - 0.08) = 34.36
$$

$$
0.92 \cdot C_M = 34.36
$$

$$
\boxed{C_M = \frac{34.36}{0.92} = 37.35 \text{ Cr/year}}
$$

Step 3 — Verification:

$$
GE = 0.08 \times 37.35 = 2.99 \text{ Cr}
$$

$$
C_M = 34.36 + 2.99 = 37.35 \text{ Cr} \checkmark
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Valuation Pitfall:** In iterative GE problems, students often forget the GE percentage is applied **on Total C_M, not on Subtotal X**. This makes a difference of typically ₹0.5–1 Cr in a 14-mark problem. Show the iterative equation setup explicitly to claim full marks.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **FCI** = one-time capital cost; **WC** = recurring short-term capital; **TCI = FCI + WC**.
- ✅ **WC ≈ 10–20% of FCI** (industry standard for chemical plants).
- ✅ **Six-Tenths Rule:** $C_2 = C_1 (Q_2/Q_1)^{0.6}$ — exponent reflects economy of scale, NOT linear scaling.
- ✅ **Cost Index adjustment:** $C_{present} = C_{past} \times (I_{present}/I_{past})$ — apply *before* capacity scaling.
- ✅ **Lang Factor:** Solid = 3.10; Solid–Fluid = 3.63; Fluid = 4.74 (applied to *delivered equipment cost*).
- ✅ **Annual Manufacturing Cost** = DPC + Fixed Charges + Plant Overhead + GE.
- ✅ **Depreciation SLM:** $D = (C_{FCI} - S_v)/n$. **Depreciation DBM:** $D = C_{book} \times \beta$.
- ✅ **Top Production Cost Drivers:** Raw Materials (40–60%) > Utilities (10–30%) > Labor > Maintenance > Depreciation > Overhead.
- ✅ **Top FCI Drivers:** Plant Size (via 6/10 rule) > MOC > Process Complexity > Location.
- ✅ **Time value of money** matters: always escalate past costs to present using cost indices before any further estimation.
- ✅ **Memorize the cost-sales hierarchy:** DPC → Fixed Charges → Plant Overhead → GE → Total Production Cost.
- ✅ In KTU valuation: **show formulas, substitute values, and box final answers** — partial marks are awarded liberally.
- ✅ **Cross-check** using two methods (e.g., Lang vs. Six-Tenths) for sanity; major discrepancies (>30%) indicate either outdated indices or wrong plant type.

---

> **End of Module 2 — Factors Affecting Investment and Production Costs**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Estimation of Capital Investment
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT757 - ECONOMICS AND MANAGEMENT OF CHEMICAL INDUSTRIES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Estimation of Capital Investment

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of KTU 2024 Scheme (PECHT757 – Economics and Management of Chemical Industries), **Capital Investment** is formally defined as the total amount of money required to be committed *up-front* to design, construct, install, commission, and operate a chemical process plant until it begins generating revenue. It is the sum of **Fixed Capital Investment (FCI)** and **Working Capital (WC)**, where FCI covers all permanent, one-time physical and engineering expenditures, while Working Capital covers the day-to-day operational liquidity needed to run the plant.

Mathematically, the total capital investment is expressed as:

$$C_{TC} = C_{FC} + C_{WC}$$

where **$C_{TC}$** = Total Capital Investment, **$C_{FC}$** = Fixed Capital Investment, and **$C_{WC}$** = Working Capital.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim)**: *Capital Investment estimation is the process of forecasting, with acceptable accuracy, the total funds required for a proposed chemical project — covering equipment procurement, installation, auxiliaries, engineering, contingency, and the working capital needed for initial operation.*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Building a House" Intuition

Think of **building a chemical plant** exactly like **building your own house** in Kerala. Before the first brick is laid, you need money for:

| House Element | Chemical Plant Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Land + Plot registration | **Land + Site Development** |
| Bricks, cement, steel | **Major Process Equipment** (reactors, columns, heat exchangers) |
| Wiring, plumbing | **Piping + Instrumentation + Electrical** |
| Architect + Engineer fees | **Engineering & Supervision** |
| Interior furnishing | **Insulation, Painting, Buildings** |
| Cash kept aside for daily groceries, electricity bills before salary arrives | **Working Capital** |

The "permanent construction cost" is your **Fixed Capital**, and the "money to keep the house running" is your **Working Capital**. The sum of both is your **Total Capital Investment** — and mis-estimating it is exactly like starting construction with half the money you actually need: catastrophic.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is this critical?** Roughly **30–60%** of total project cost in chemical industries is capital investment. A 10% estimation error in a ₹500 Crore plant equals **₹50 Crores** of budget slippage. Hence, KTU examiners attach *very high weightage* to numerical estimation problems.

## 1.3 Classification of Capital Investment

Capital investment is broadly bifurcated into two KTU-classified heads:

1. **Fixed Capital Investment (FCI)** — One-time, irrecoverable, asset-forming.
2. **Working Capital (WC)** — Recurring, recoverable, liquidity-based.

Each is further subdivided as detailed in Section 2.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Hierarchical Classification of Fixed Capital Investment

### 2.1.1 Direct Costs (DC)

These are physically traceable to the plant hardware. The standard KTU breakdown is:

- **Purchased Equipment Cost** ($C_{PE}$) — FOB cost of all major process equipment (reactors, distillation columns, pumps, compressors, heat exchangers, vessels).
- **Equipment Installation** ($C_{EI}$) — Foundations, structural supports, labor for rigging.
- **Piping** ($C_{Pi}$) — All process and utility piping including valves, fittings.
- **Instrumentation** ($C_{I}$) — Sensors, transmitters, DCS, control valves.
- **Electrical** ($C_{El}$) — Transformers, cabling, switchgear, motors.
- **Buildings** ($C_B$) — Process buildings, admin offices, control rooms.
- **Insulation** ($C_{In}$) — Thermal/acoustic insulation.
- **Painting / Fireproofing** ($C_{Pt}$).
- **Yard Improvement / Land** ($C_{L}$).

$$C_{DC} = C_{PE} + C_{EI} + C_{Pi} + C_{I} + C_{El} + C_B + C_{In} + C_{Pt} + C_{L}$$

### 2.1.2 Indirect Costs (IC)

These are not tied to any single piece of hardware:

- **Engineering & Supervision** ($C_{ES}$).
- **Construction Expenses** ($C_{CE}$) — temporary facilities, contractor fees.
- **Legal & Contractor Fees** ($C_{LF}$).
- **Contingency** ($C_{Cn}$) — typically **5–15%** of FCI.

$$C_{IC} = C_{ES} + C_{CE} + C_{LF} + C_{Cn}$$

### 2.1.3 Fixed Capital Investment

$$C_{FC} = C_{DC} + C_{IC}$$

## 2.2 Working Capital (WC)

Working capital covers the funds tied up during normal operation *before* revenue stabilizes. It includes:

$$C_{WC} = C_{RC} + C_{IP} + C_{AP} - C_{AP}^{pay}$$

where $C_{RC}$ = Accounts Receivable, $C_{IP}$ = Inventory of Products/Raw Materials, $C_{AP}$ = Available Cash, and $C_{AP}^{pay}$ = Accounts Payable.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Memory Trick**: Working Capital is approximately **5–20%** of Fixed Capital Investment for most chemical plants, with **~15%** being the typical design value.

## 2.3 Methods of Capital Cost Estimation — A Tiered Approach

The accuracy of an estimate depends on the **stage of project development**. KTU 2024 expects students to know all five levels:

| Level | Method | Accuracy | Data Required |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Order-of-Magnitude Estimate** (Six-Tenths Rule / Power Factor) | ± 40% | Single-capacity parameter |
| 2 | **Study Estimate** (Factor Methods: Lang, Hand) | ± 30% | Major equipment list |
| 3 | **Preliminary Estimate** (Major Equipment Factored) | ± 20% | Equipment + flow diagrams |
| 4 | **Definitive Estimate** | ± 10% | Quoted equipment, P&IDs |
| 5 | **Detailed Estimate** | ± 5% | Full engineering specs |

## 2.4 The Lang Factor Method (Most Tested in KTU)

Introduced by **H.J. Lang (1947)**. It estimates FCI from the total delivered cost of all major process equipment using a multiplicative factor.

$$C_{FC} = F_{L} \times \sum C_{E}$$

where $F_L$ = Lang Factor and $\sum C_E$ = total delivered cost of all major equipment.

| Plant Type | $F_L$ Value |
|---|---|
| Solid processing (cement, ore) | **3.10** |
| Solid–Fluid processing (refining, petrochemicals) | **3.63** |
| Fluid processing (pharma, fine chemicals) | **4.74** |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Hallmark Question**: "For a *fluid processing* plant, if the delivered cost of major equipment is ₹50 Lakhs, estimate the Fixed Capital Investment."
> **Answer**: $C_{FC} = 4.74 \times 50 = ₹237$ Lakhs.

## 2.5 The Hand Factor Method

A refined modification of Lang's factor, accounting separately for **installation** vs. **non-installation** items. The Hand method is preferred in KTU because it gives finer granularity.

$$C_{FC} = C_{E}^{inst} \times f_{inst} + C_{E}^{non} \times f_{non}$$

For typical chemical plants:

$$C_{FC} \approx (1.04 \text{ to } 1.10) \times \sum C_{E}$$

where the multiplier is **~1.04 for solid processes** and **~1.10 for fluid processes**.

## 2.6 The Six-Tenths Rule (Power Factor / Williams' Rule)

Used when **only the capacity** of a *known reference plant* is available:

$$C_{B} = C_{A} \times \left(\frac{S_{B}}{S_{A}}\right)^{n}$$

with the canonical exponent **$n = 0.6$** for chemical processes. This is also known as the **Power Law** or **Exponential Cost Model**:

$$C = a \cdot S^{n}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Mnemonic**: *Six-Tenths Rule → Two plant capacities → one exponent 0.6.* This is the *Order-of-Magnitude Estimate*.

## 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these before any KTU exam.**

| # | Formula | Meaning | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $C_{TC} = C_{FC} + C_{WC}$ | Total Capital | Always |
| 2 | $C_{FC} = F_L \cdot \sum C_E$ | Lang's Method | Study estimate |
| 3 | $C_{FC} = 1.04 \text{ to } 1.10 \cdot \sum C_E$ | Hand's Method | Refined estimate |
| 4 | $C_{FC} = C_{DC} + C_{IC}$ | Direct + Indirect | Component sum |
| 5 | $C_{B} = C_{A} \cdot (S_B/S_A)^{0.6}$ | Six-Tenths Rule | Order-of-magnitude |
| 6 | $C_{WC} \approx (0.10 \text{ to } 0.20) \cdot C_{FC}$ | Working Capital approx. | Quick estimate |
| 7 | $C = a \cdot S^n$ | Power Law | Capacity scaling |
| 8 | $C_{Cn} \approx 0.10 \cdot C_{FC}$ | Contingency allowance | Risk buffer |

> **Real-world utility**: Engineers at Reliance, BPCL, and IOCL routinely use Lang & Hand factors during feasibility studies of crores-worth refineries before detailed engineering begins. Even an **±30% accuracy** in early stage is *immensely valuable* because it dictates the **Go/No-Go decision**.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Working Capital from First Principles

Working Capital represents the funds locked in *operational cycle assets* minus the funds obtained on credit from suppliers. The **operational cycle** has three phases: buy raw material → produce → sell.

**Step 1 — Identify the components.**

The standard KTU decomposition is:

$$C_{WC} = C_{RC} + C_{IP} + C_{AP}^{cash} - C_{AP}^{pay}$$

**Step 2 — Express each component in terms of annual operating cost ($C_{OP}$).**

Assuming typical payment terms:

$$C_{RC} = \frac{\tau_{RC}}{365} \cdot C_{OP}$$

$$C_{IP} = \frac{\tau_{IP}}{365} \cdot C_{OP}$$

$$C_{AP}^{pay} = \frac{\tau_{AP}}{365} \cdot C_{OP}$$

where $\tau_{RC}$, $\tau_{IP}$, $\tau_{AP}$ are average hold-up times in days.

**Step 3 — Substitute and simplify.**

$$C_{WC} = \frac{C_{OP}}{365} \cdot \left( \tau_{RC} + \tau_{IP} + \tau_{cash} - \tau_{AP} \right)$$

**Step 4 — KTU Approximation.**

For most chemical plants: $\tau_{RC} \approx 30$ days, $\tau_{IP} \approx 60$ days, $\tau_{AP} \approx 30$ days, $\tau_{cash} \approx 10$ days.

$$C_{WC} = \frac{C_{OP}}{365} \cdot (30 + 60 + 10 - 30) = \frac{70 \cdot C_{OP}}{365}$$

This yields approximately **$C_{WC} \approx 0.192 \cdot C_{OP}$** which for typical projects where $C_{OP} \approx C_{FC}$, reduces to **$C_{WC} \approx 0.10 \text{ to } 0.20 \cdot C_{FC}$** — confirming our earlier rule of thumb.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Six-Tenths Rule from Power Law Cost Behavior

**Step 1 — Empirical observation.**

If we plot $\ln(C)$ vs. $\ln(S)$ for several existing plants of similar type processing the same chemical operation, we observe a *linear* relationship on log–log axes.

$$\ln C = \ln a + n \cdot \ln S$$

**Step 2 — Slope interpretation.**

The slope $n$ is the **cost-capacity exponent**. For most chemical processes, regression on historical data yields:

$$n \approx 0.6$$

**Step 3 — Generalize to any two plants.**

$$\frac{\ln C_B}{\ln C_A} = \frac{n \cdot \ln S_B}{n \cdot \ln S_A}$$

Exponentiating:

$$C_B = C_A \cdot \left( \frac{S_B}{S_A} \right)^{n}$$

**Step 4 — Substitution of canonical exponent.**

$$\boxed{C_B = C_A \cdot \left( \frac{S_B}{S_A} \right)^{0.6}}$$

This is the **Six-Tenths Rule**.

## 3.3 Worked Example 1 — Six-Tenths Rule Application

**Problem:** A 50,000 ton/year ammonia plant cost ₹600 Crores when built. Estimate the cost of a similar 1,50,000 ton/year plant using the Six-Tenths Rule.

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** Identify knowns.

$$C_A = 600 \text{ Crores}, \quad S_A = 50{,}000 \text{ tpy}, \quad S_B = 1{,}50{,}000 \text{ tpy}, \quad n = 0.6$$

**Step 2:** Compute the capacity ratio.

$$\frac{S_B}{S_A} = \frac{1{,}50{,}000}{50{,}000} = 3$$

**Step 3:** Apply the formula.

$$C_B = 600 \times (3)^{0.6}$$

**Step 4:** Evaluate the power.

$$(3)^{0.6} = e^{0.6 \ln 3} = e^{0.6 \times 1.0986} = e^{0.6592} = 1.933$$

**Step 5:** Final answer.

$$C_B = 600 \times 1.933 = \boxed{₹1159.8 \text{ Crores}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> Notice that tripling capacity only raises cost by **~93%**, not 300%. This is the **economy of scale** — a foundational principle in chemical engineering economics.

## 3.4 Worked Example 2 — Lang Factor Application

**Problem:** The delivered cost of major equipment for a *fluid processing* pharmaceutical plant is ₹200 Lakhs. Compute FCI using Lang's method. Also estimate the Total Capital Investment assuming Working Capital is 15% of FCI.

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** Select Lang factor.

For fluid processing, $F_L = 4.74$.

**Step 2:** Compute FCI.

$$C_{FC} = F_L \times \sum C_E = 4.74 \times 200 = ₹948 \text{ Lakhs}$$

**Step 3:** Compute Working Capital.

$$C_{WC} = 0.15 \times 948 = ₹142.2 \text{ Lakhs}$$

**Step 4:** Compute Total Capital.

$$C_{TC} = 948 + 142.2 = \boxed{₹1090.2 \text{ Lakhs}}$$

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Component-wise FCI Breakdown

For a typical KTU numerical, the following component breakdown (as % of FCI) is used in valuation key:

| Component | % of FCI (Solid Process) | % of FCI (Fluid Process) |
|---|---|---|
| Purchased Equipment | 25% | 20% |
| Equipment Installation | 9% | 14% |
| Piping | 7% | 16% |
| Instrumentation | 3% | 6% |
| Electrical | 4% | 7% |
| Buildings | 6% | 8% |
| Insulation | 2% | 3% |
| Painting | 1% | 2% |
| Yard/Land | 2% | 3% |
| Indirect (Eng + Contingency) | 41% | 21% |
| **TOTAL** | **100%** | **100%** |

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip**: KTU often asks: *"Why is the Indirect Cost % lower for fluid processing?"* Because fluid plants have higher direct hardware costs (more piping, vessels), the same indirect overheads get diluted into a smaller denominator — a classic **algebraic share effect**.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Hierarchy of Capital Investment

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Capital Investment C TC"] --> B["Fixed Capital Investment C FC"]
    A --> C["Working Capital C WC"]
    B --> D["Direct Costs C DC"]
    B --> E["Indirect Costs C IC"]
    D --> F["Purchased Equipment C PE"]
    D --> G["Installation C EI"]
    D --> H["Piping C Pi"]
    D --> I["Instrumentation C I"]
    D --> J["Electrical C El"]
    D --> K["Buildings C B"]
    D --> L["Insulation C In"]
    D --> M["Painting C Pt"]
    D --> N["Land and Yard C L"]
    E --> O["Engineering and Supervision C ES"]
    E --> P["Construction Expenses C CE"]
    E --> Q["Legal and Contractors C LF"]
    E --> R["Contingency C Cn"]
    C --> S["Accounts Receivable C RC"]
    C --> T["Inventory and Products C IP"]
    C --> U["Cash Balance C AP cash"]
    C --> V["Accounts Payable C AP pay"]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Flowchart — Capital Estimation Method Selection

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start["Engineer needs capital estimate"] --> Q1{"Available Data?"}
    Q1 -->|Only capacity S| M1["Use Six Tenths Rule n equals 0.6"]
    Q1 -->|Major equipment list| M2["Use Lang or Hand Factor"]
    Q1 -->|P and ID ready| M3["Use Component Factored Estimate"]
    Q1 -->|Quotations received| M4["Use Definitive or Detailed Estimate"]
    M1 --> R1["Accuracy plus minus 40 percent"]
    M2 --> R2["Accuracy plus minus 30 percent"]
    M3 --> R3["Accuracy plus minus 20 percent"]
    M4 --> R4["Accuracy plus minus 10 percent"]
    R1 --> Out["Project Go or No Go Decision"]
    R2 --> Out
    R3 --> Out
    R4 --> Out
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Computational Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1["Step 1 Identify Plant Type Solid or Fluid"] --> S2["Step 2 List Major Equipment C PE sum"]
    S2 --> S3{"Choose Method"}
    S3 -->|Lang| S4["Multiply by F L 3.10 or 4.74"]
    S3 -->|Hand| S5["Multiply by 1.04 or 1.10"]
    S3 -->|Six Tenths| S6["Use Reference Plant and exponent 0.6"]
    S4 --> S7["Compute C FC"]
    S5 --> S7
    S6 --> S7
    S7 --> S8["Add 10 to 20 percent for C WC"]
    S8 --> S9["Final C TC for Feasibility Report"]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
**Define Fixed Capital Investment and Working Capital. How are they related?**

**Model Answer:**
**Fixed Capital Investment (FCI)** is the one-time expenditure incurred for the complete design, construction, and commissioning of a chemical plant, covering all permanent assets such as equipment, piping, buildings, instrumentation, and engineering fees. **Working Capital (WC)** is the additional funds required for day-to-day operation of the plant before revenues stabilize, covering raw material inventory, accounts receivable, and cash balance, net of accounts payable.

**Relation**: Total Capital Investment $= C_{FC} + C_{WC}$, with WC typically being **10–20%** of FCI for chemical plants.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**State the Six-Tenths Rule. Under what conditions is it applicable?**

**Model Answer:**
The **Six-Tenths Rule** (also called the Power Law) states that the cost of a chemical plant scales with its capacity raised to the **0.6 power**:

$$C_B = C_A \cdot \left( \frac{S_B}{S_A} \right)^{0.6}$$

It is applicable for **order-of-magnitude estimates** when only the capacity of a previously built similar plant is known, and the new plant uses the *same process technology*. Accuracy is approximately **±40%**. It is **not applicable** for radically different processes or different product types.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length 14-Mark Questions

### **Question A (Choice 1)** — **[KTU University Exam — June 2023]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain in detail the **Lang Factor Method** for estimation of Fixed Capital Investment. Discuss its limitations.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A *fluid processing* chemical plant has the following major equipment costs (delivered): Reactor ₹80 Lakhs, Distillation Column ₹120 Lakhs, Heat Exchanger ₹60 Lakhs, Pump ₹40 Lakhs. Estimate (i) FCI using Lang's method, and (ii) Total Capital Investment if Working Capital is 15% of FCI.

---

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Lang Factor Method:**

The Lang Factor Method, developed by H.J. Lang in 1947, estimates the **Fixed Capital Investment (FCI)** of a chemical plant as a multiplicative factor applied to the total delivered cost of all major process equipment.

$$C_{FC} = F_L \times \sum C_E$$

**Standard Lang Factors:**

| Plant Type | Lang Factor $F_L$ |
|---|---|
| Solid processing | **3.10** |
| Solid–Fluid processing | **3.63** |
| Fluid processing | **4.74** |

**Procedure:**
1. List all *major* process equipment (reactors, columns, heat exchangers, pumps, compressors, storage tanks).
2. Obtain *delivered cost* of each (i.e., including freight, taxes, insurance, but excluding installation).
3. Sum them to get $\sum C_E$.
4. Multiply by the appropriate $F_L$ based on the processing nature.

**Limitations:**

- Assumes a *fixed proportion* between equipment cost and FCI, which is rarely accurate in practice.
- Ignores **location-specific** cost variations (e.g., Kerala vs. Gujarat site costs).
- Does not account for **inflation** or **currency fluctuation** between two project eras.
- Accuracy limited to ±30%, so unsuitable for definitive estimates.
- Treats all equipment as a homogeneous pool, ignoring that high-pressure vs. low-pressure items have very different indirect cost multipliers.

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution:**

**Step 1:** Sum the delivered equipment cost.

$$\sum C_E = 80 + 120 + 60 + 40 = ₹300 \text{ Lakhs}$$

**Step 2:** Select Lang factor for fluid processing, $F_L = 4.74$.

**Step 3:** Compute FCI.

$$C_{FC} = 4.74 \times 300 = \boxed{₹1422 \text{ Lakhs}}$$

**Step 4:** Compute Working Capital (15% of FCI).

$$C_{WC} = 0.15 \times 1422 = ₹213.3 \text{ Lakhs}$$

**Step 5:** Compute Total Capital Investment.

$$C_{TC} = 1422 + 213.3 = \boxed{₹1635.3 \text{ Lakhs}}$$

---

### **Question B (Choice 2)** — **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Differentiate between **Direct Costs** and **Indirect Costs** in Fixed Capital Investment. List at least five items under each category.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A reference ammonia plant of capacity 30,000 tpy cost ₹450 Crores in 2018. Using the Six-Tenths Rule, estimate the cost of a similar 1,00,000 tpy plant in 2024. Discuss why cost scales sub-linearly with capacity.

---

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Direct vs. Indirect Costs:**

| Feature | Direct Costs | Indirect Costs |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | Expenditures physically traceable to plant hardware | Expenditures not tied to any specific equipment |
| Tangibility | Tangible assets | Intangible / service-based |
| Examples | Equipment, Piping, Instrumentation, Buildings, Electrical | Engineering, Supervision, Construction Expenses, Legal Fees, Contingency |
| Typical % of FCI | 60–80% | 20–40% |

**Direct Cost Items (at least 5):**
1. Purchased Equipment Cost
2. Equipment Installation
3. Piping (process and utility)
4. Instrumentation and Control
5. Electrical (cabling, switchgear)
6. Buildings (process + admin)
7. Insulation and Painting

**Indirect Cost Items (at least 5):**
1. Engineering and Supervision
2. Construction Expenses
3. Contractor's Fee
4. Legal and Permit Fees
5. Contingency Allowance
6. Startup Costs

---

**Part (b) — Six-Tenths Numerical:**

**Step 1:** Identify knowns.

$$C_A = 450 \text{ Crores}, \quad S_A = 30{,}000 \text{ tpy}, \quad S_B = 1{,}00{,}000 \text{ tpy}$$

**Step 2:** Compute capacity ratio.

$$\frac{S_B}{S_A} = \frac{1{,}00{,}000}{30{,}000} = \frac{10}{3} \approx 3.333$$

**Step 3:** Apply Six-Tenths Rule.

$$C_B = 450 \times (3.333)^{0.6}$$

**Step 4:** Evaluate $(3.333)^{0.6}$.

$$(3.333)^{0.6} = e^{0.6 \ln 3.333} = e^{0.6 \times 1.2040} = e^{0.7224} = 2.059$$

**Step 5:** Final answer.

$$C_B = 450 \times 2.059 = \boxed{₹926.55 \text{ Crores}}$$

**Sub-linear scaling (Economy of Scale):**

Cost scales sub-linearly because of:

1. **Bulk Discount** — Single 1,00,000 tpy reactor costs less than three 30,000 tpy reactors.
2. **Shared Auxiliaries** — Control room, piping headers, utility connections are largely *capacity-independent* and get distributed over larger output.
3. **Higher Thermal Efficiency** — Larger vessels have lower surface-area-to-volume ratios, reducing heat loss and material per ton.
4. **Linear vs. Nonlinear Cost Components** — Labor, engineering, and supervision costs scale sub-linearly since a single large crew can operate a larger plant with marginal additions.
5. **Mathematical necessity** — Since $n = 0.6 < 1$, doubling capacity only multiplies cost by $2^{0.6} = 1.516$.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🔴 KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls (Frequently Lost Marks):**
> 1. **Confusing delivered cost with FOB cost**: The equipment cost in Lang's method is *delivered cost*, not the factory (FOB) cost. Students lose 1–2 marks by mixing these.
> 2. **Wrong Lang factor selection**: Choosing $F_L = 3.10$ for a *fluid* plant or $4.74$ for a *solid* plant — always read the question's plant type.
> 3. **Forgetting the Working Capital** at the end — examiners specifically allocate marks for $C_{TC} = C_{FC} + C_{WC}$.
> 4. **Six-Tenths Rule applied to dissimilar processes** — students apply it across unrelated technologies, which is wrong. It applies only to *similar processes*.
> 5. **Unit mismatches** — capacity must be in *consistent units* (e.g., both in tpy or both in kg/hr). Convert before applying the rule.
> 6. **Ignoring inflation** — if the reference plant is from 2018, the new estimate is *before inflation adjustment*; many students forget this caveat.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Capital Investment Estimation**

- [x] **Total Capital = Fixed + Working Capital** → $C_{TC} = C_{FC} + C_{WC}$
- [x] **Fixed Capital = Direct + Indirect Costs**
- [x] **Direct Costs (DC)** = Equipment + Installation + Piping + Instrumentation + Electrical + Buildings + Insulation + Painting + Yard
- [x] **Indirect Costs (IC)** = Engineering + Construction + Legal + Contingency (~10%)
- [x] **Lang Factors** → 3.10 (Solid), 3.63 (Mixed), 4.74 (Fluid)
- [x] **Hand Factors** → 1.04 (Solid), 1.10 (Fluid)
- [x] **Six-Tenths Rule** → $C_B = C_A (S_B/S_A)^{0.6}$ — Order-of-magnitude estimate only
- [x] **Working Capital ≈ 10–20% of FCI** — typical design value 15%
- [x] **Economy of scale** → capacity grows faster than cost (sub-linear, n = 0.6)
- [x] **Accuracy levels**: Six-Tenths ±40%, Lang ±30%, Component ±20%, Definitive ±10%, Detailed ±5%
- [x] **Estimating ladder**: As project advances from feasibility → basic engineering → detailed engineering, accuracy improves and so does cost of estimation.
- [x] **Contingency is mandatory** — never submit a FCI without it (typically 5–15% of FCI).
- [x] **Equipment cost in Lang's method is DELIVERED, not FOB.**
- [x] **Six-Tenths rule fails for radically different processes** — only applies to similar technology.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT757 - ECONOMICS AND MANAGEMENT OF CHEMICAL INDUSTRIES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Types of Capital Cost Estimates: Order of Magnitude Estimate

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is a Capital Cost Estimate?

In chemical process industries (CPI), **Capital Cost Estimation** is the forecasting of the total monetary investment required to design, construct, and commission a new chemical plant or to expand/modify an existing one. It is a foundational pillar of **Engineering Economics** and **Project Feasibility Analysis**, dictating whether a project proceeds to the next phase.

Estimates are not single-point guesses; they are classified into distinct *types* based on the **amount of project information available**, the **stage of project development**, and the **expected accuracy (margin of error)**. KTU 2024 Scheme strictly classifies them under Module 2 of PECHT757.

### 1.2 Classification of Capital Cost Estimates (KTU 2024 Scheme Standard)

According to the American Association of Cost Engineers (AACE) classifications — adopted universally in KTU examinations — there are **Five Standard Types** of capital cost estimates:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board-Standard Classification (AACE Recommended):**
> 1. **Order of Magnitude Estimate** (Ratio / Grass-Roots / Rule-of-Thumb) — Accuracy: **−30% to +50%**
> 2. **Study (Factored) Estimate** — Accuracy: **−25% to +30%**
> 3. **Preliminary (Budget Authorization) Estimate** — Accuracy: **−20% to +25%**
> 4. **Definitive (Project Control) Estimate** — Accuracy: **−10% to +15%**
> 5. **Detailed (Firm) Estimate** — Accuracy: **−5% to +10%**

*(Note: KTU board questions occasionally expect the older textbook classification of 4–5 types, but the AACE framework above is the universally accepted standard.)*

### 1.3 Formal Definition of the Order of Magnitude Estimate

> [!NOTE]
> **Order of Magnitude Estimate (OME):** A preliminary capital cost estimate prepared using only the *basic process capacity* (e.g., tonnes/year) and a *comparison with the known cost of a previously built similar plant*. It is generated using shortcut, rule-of-thumb, or scaling correlations without any detailed equipment list, material take-offs, or engineering drawings.

**Synonyms used in KTU examinations:**
- Grass-roots estimate
- Ratio estimate
- Rule-of-thumb estimate
- Capacity-factored estimate

**Typical Stage of Use:** Conceptual / Pre-feasibility stage (when the project is only an idea).

### 1.4 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!TIP]
> **The "Trip Planner" Analogy:**
> Imagine you are planning a road trip from Kochi to Delhi. You know the distance (~2,500 km) and that your car gives ~15 km/litre with petrol costing ~₹105/litre.
>
> - **Order of Magnitude Estimate:** You quickly calculate "I will probably spend around ₹15,000–₹20,000 on fuel." You did not check tolls, tyre wear, food, or hotel costs. You just scaled from past experience. **This is the OME.**
> - **Detailed Estimate:** You planned every meal, every toll, every km. This is the Detailed Estimate (much later stage).
>
> For a chemical engineer, the OME answers: *"Should we even study this process seriously?"* — it gives a ballpark number to see if the project is economically worth exploring.

### 1.5 Key Characteristics at a Glance

| Parameter | Order of Magnitude Estimate |
|---|---|
| **Accuracy Range** | **−30% to +50%** |
| **Information Required** | Process capacity only |
| **Time to Prepare** | Hours to a few days |
| **Engineering Effort** | < 0.1% of project cost |
| **Stage of Project** | Conceptual / Idea screening |
| **Data Source** | Historical costs of similar plants |
| **Common Methods** | Power Law, Turnover Ratio, Six-Tenths Rule |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Engineering "Why" Behind OME

In the **Conceptual Design Stage**, no P&ID (Piping & Instrumentation Diagram), no equipment list, and no plot plan exists. However, management *must* know whether the project is worth pursuing. A complete engineering estimate would consume months and lakhs of rupees. The OME solves this dilemma by leveraging historical data — **"the past is the best predictor of the future"** — scaled using mathematical correlations.

### 2.2 The Core Methods Used in OME

A KTU student is expected to master **three core shortcut techniques** to compute an OME:

#### Method 1: The Power Law (Capacity Ratio Method / Williams' Law)

This is the most frequently examined method in KTU.

> When the only known variables are the **capacity (Q)** of the proposed plant and the **cost (C_A)** of an existing reference plant (plant A) of known capacity (Q_A), the cost of the new plant (C_B) is obtained by:

$$
C_{B} = C_{A} \left( \frac{Q_{B}}{Q_{A}} \right)^{n}
$$

where:
- $C_A$ = Capital cost of the reference (existing) plant of known capacity
- $C_B$ = Estimated capital cost of the new plant
- $Q_A$ = Capacity of the existing reference plant
- $Q_B$ = Capacity of the new proposed plant
- $n$ = **Cost-capacity exponent** (also called scale-up exponent)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board-Critical Constant: The Six-Tenths Rule (Williams' Shortcut)**
> For most chemical process plants, the cost-capacity exponent **n ≈ 0.6** (the famous *six-tenths rule*). KTU board examiners often explicitly state "use the 0.6 rule" in numerical problems. However, students must remember that **n varies** for different equipment/processes (e.g., n ≈ 0.8 for pumps, 0.6 for heat exchangers, 0.7 for compressors, 0.5–0.9 generally).

#### Method 2: The Turnover Ratio Method

This method uses industry-average **sales-to-capital-cost ratios**. It is purely rule-of-thumb:

$$
\text{Capital Investment} = \frac{\text{Annual Sales}}{\text{Turnover Ratio}}
$$

Typical turnover ratios for the chemical industry range between **0.4 and 1.0** (i.e., sales are roughly 0.4–1.0 times the capital invested per year).

#### Method 3: Lang's Capacity Factor Method (Quick OME)

A popular rule-of-thumb stating that:

$$
C_{B} = f \cdot C_{A} \left( \frac{Q_{B}}{Q_{A}} \right)^{0.6}
$$

where *f* is the **Lang Factor**, a multiplier that depends on the *type of plant*:

| Plant Type | Solid Processing | Fluid Processing |
|---|---|---|
| **Lang Factor (f)** | **3.10** | **4.74** |

(The Lang Factor bundles all costs — equipment, installation, piping, instrumentation, buildings, land, engineering — into a single multiplier.)

---

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Exam Cheat Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **🔖 KTU Board Exam Cheat Sheet — Order of Magnitude Estimate**

| # | Formula / Rule | Description / Variables | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $C_B = C_A (Q_B/Q_A)^n$ | Power law; **n ≈ 0.6** (six-tenths rule) | Scaling known plant to new capacity |
| 2 | $C_B = f \cdot C_A (Q_B/Q_A)^{0.6}$ | Lang's method (f = 3.10 solid, 4.74 fluid) | Quick bundled cost |
| 3 | Capital = Sales / TR | TR = Turnover Ratio (≈ 0.4–1.0) | Sales-based OME |
| 4 | $\text{Capital} = a \cdot Q^{0.6}$ | Direct OME; *a* is industry constant | When no reference plant exists |
| 5 | $1/n = \log(C_B/C_A) / \log(Q_B/Q_A)$ | Rearranged power law to find *n* | Back-calculating exponent |
| 6 | Accuracy bounds: $C_{low} = 0.7\,C$, $C_{high} = 1.5\,C$ | Apply −30% / +50% bounds | Final range presentation |

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Notational Pitfall:** When writing the power law in KTU answer sheets, **always specify units** for capacity (e.g., tonnes/year, kg/hr, m³/day). Examiners deduct marks if units are missing or mismatched between $Q_A$ and $Q_B$.

---

### 2.4 Real-World Utility in Chemical Engineering

The OME is not just academic — it drives billions of dollars in corporate decisions:

- **Go / No-Go Screening:** Multinationals (Reliance, BASF, Dow) use OME before greenfield projects.
- **R&D Prioritization:** A research team uses OME to decide whether a new catalyst justifies scale-up.
- **Stock Market & Investor Communication:** Public companies cite OME ranges in pre-project announcements.
- **Government Tendering:** Pre-feasibility reports for public-sector plants rely on OME.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Power Law (Theoretical Justification)

The cost-capacity exponent *n* arises from a fundamental scaling principle: **cost scales with surface area, while capacity scales with volume**.

Consider a piece of equipment with characteristic linear dimension *L*. Then:

- **Capacity** (volume-related quantity): $Q \propto L^3$
- **Cost** (surface/material-related quantity): $C \propto L^2$

Eliminating *L*:

$$
\begin{aligned}
L &\propto Q^{1/3} \\
C &\propto L^2 \propto (Q^{1/3})^2 \\
C &\propto Q^{2/3}
\end{aligned}
$$

Hence, in general:

$$
C = k \cdot Q^{n}
$$

For two similar plants (A and B), dividing:

$$
\frac{C_B}{C_A} = \left( \frac{Q_B}{Q_A} \right)^{n}
$$

Rearranging:

$$
\boxed{\,C_B = C_A \left( \frac{Q_B}{Q_A} \right)^{n}\,}
$$

This is the **Power Law of Cost Estimation**, and *n* depends on the dominant scaling variable (surface area → n = 0.67, volume → n = 1.0, mixed → 0.5–0.9).

---

### 3.2 Worked-Out KTU Numerical Example (Full Step-by-Step)

> **[KTU Sample Numerical — Style: Dec 2023 / Jun 2024 ESE Pattern]**
>
> *A fluid-processing plant produces 50,000 tonnes/year of product and was built at a capital cost of ₹80 crores three years ago. Using the Six-Tenths Rule, estimate the capital cost of a similar new plant of capacity 2,00,000 tonnes/year. Also compute the low (−30%) and high (+50%) bounds for the estimate.*

#### Given Data:
- $C_A$ = ₹80 crores
- $Q_A$ = 50,000 tonnes/year
- $Q_B$ = 2,00,000 tonnes/year
- $n = 0.6$ (Six-Tenths Rule)

#### Step 1 — Apply the Power Law

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_B &= C_A \left( \frac{Q_B}{Q_A} \right)^{n} \\
    &= 80 \times \left( \frac{2{,}00{,}000}{50{,}000} \right)^{0.6} \\
    &= 80 \times (4)^{0.6}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step 2 — Evaluate $(4)^{0.6}$

$$
(4)^{0.6} = e^{0.6 \ln 4} = e^{0.6 \times 1.3863} = e^{0.8318}
$$

$$
e^{0.8318} \approx 2.297
$$

#### Step 3 — Compute the Central Estimate

$$
C_B = 80 \times 2.297 = 183.8 \text{ crores}
$$

#### Step 4 — Apply the Accuracy Range

- **Lower bound** (Accuracy −30%): $C_{low} = 0.70 \times 183.8 = ₹128.66 \text{ crores}$
- **Upper bound** (Accuracy +50%): $C_{high} = 1.50 \times 183.8 = ₹275.7 \text{ crores}$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final OME Answer:**
> $$C_B = \text{₹ 183.8 crores} \quad (\text{Range: ₹ 128.7 to ₹ 275.7 crores})$$

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of All Estimate Types

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner-Expected Comparison Matrix** (Frequently asked as a 14-mark "compare-and-contrast" question)

| Parameter | Order of Magnitude | Study (Factored) | Preliminary | Definitive | Detailed |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Accuracy** | ±30–50% | ±25–30% | ±20–25% | ±10–15% | ±5–10% |
| **Engineering Done** | <1% | 1–3% | 10–20% | 30–60% | 100% |
| **Data Needed** | Capacity only | Major equipment | Plot plan, P&IDs | Full specs | Bid quotes |
| **Methods Used** | Power law, Lang, Six-tenths | Equipment factors | Factored estimates | Semi-detailed unit costs | Detailed take-offs |
| **Time to Prepare** | Hours–Days | 2–4 weeks | 1–3 months | 6–12 months | 1–2 years |
| **Stage** | Conceptual | Feasibility | Budget approval | Project sanction | Construction |
| **KTU Term** | Grass-roots | Ratio estimate | Budget authorization | Project control | Firm estimate |

---

### 3.4 Typical Cost-Capacity Exponents (Memorize for KTU)

| Equipment / Process | Typical *n* |
|---|---|
| Centrifugal pumps | **0.70 – 0.85** |
| Compressors | **0.70 – 0.75** |
| Heat exchangers (shell & tube) | **0.60 – 0.65** |
| Distillation columns | **0.60 – 0.70** |
| Reactors (CSTR/PFR) | **0.50 – 0.65** |
| Storage tanks | **0.50 – 0.60** |
| **Whole chemical plant** | **≈ 0.60** (Six-Tenths Rule) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

> **Reading the Diagram:** The OME is the **first decision gate** (shown in red/orange). If the OME range exceeds the company's investment appetite, the project is killed immediately — saving millions in subsequent engineering effort.

---

### 4.3 Decision Tree — Choosing the OME Method

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Start: Need OME]) --> Q1{Do you have a\nreference plant cost?}
    Q1 -->|Yes| Q2{Is the plant type\nsolid or fluid processing?}
    Q1 -->|No| Q3{Do you have\nannual sales forecast?}
    Q3 -->|Yes| T1[Use Turnover Ratio Method]
    Q3 -->|No| T2[Use Industry Average: a * Q^0.6]

    Q2 -->|Solid| Lang1[Lang Factor f = 3.10]
    Q2 -->|Fluid| Lang2[Lang Factor f = 4.74]

    Lang1 --> P1[Apply Power Law with f]
    Lang2 --> P1
    P1 --> Result[Compute CB and Range]
    T1 --> Result
    T2 --> Result

    style Start fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px

```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1 (3 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Q: Define Order of Magnitude Estimate. State its accuracy range.**

**Model Answer:**
The **Order of Magnitude Estimate (OME)** is a preliminary capital cost estimate of a chemical plant prepared using only the basic process capacity and historical cost data of a similar existing plant. It employs shortcut rule-of-thumb methods such as the **Power Law** and **Six-Tenths Rule**, without detailed equipment lists or engineering drawings.

**Accuracy Range:** **−30% to +50%** of the actual cost.

It is prepared at the **conceptual/idea stage** of a project to evaluate feasibility.

*(Word limit: 60–80 words | 3 Marks)*

---

#### **Question 2 (3 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Jun 2024]`

**Q: What is the Six-Tenths Rule? Mention its significance in OME.**

**Model Answer:**
The **Six-Tenths Rule** states that if the cost of a chemical plant of capacity $Q_A$ is known, then the cost of a geometrically similar plant of capacity $Q_B$ is given by:

$$
C_B = C_A \left( \frac{Q_B}{Q_A} \right)^{0.6}
$$

**Significance:** It provides a **rapid, rule-of-thumb scaling** without detailed engineering data, making it the cornerstone method of the Order of Magnitude Estimate.

*(Word limit: 50–70 words | 3 Marks)*

---

---

### 📕 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

#### **🅰️ QUESTION A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]`

**Q: (a)** List and briefly explain the different types of capital cost estimates used in chemical process industries. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A fluid-processing chemical plant of capacity 80,000 tonnes/year was built at a capital cost of ₹120 crores. Using the Six-Tenths Rule and Lang Factor method, estimate the capital cost of a similar new plant of capacity 1,60,000 tonnes/year. Take accuracy range as −30% to +50%. **(7 Marks)**

---

##### **Model Solution — Part (a):**

The five standard types of capital cost estimates (AACE classification) are:

1. **Order of Magnitude Estimate:** Prepared at the conceptual stage using only capacity data; accuracy ±50%.
2. **Study (Factored) Estimate:** Uses equipment-factored methods; accuracy ±30%.
3. **Preliminary Estimate:** Based on preliminary equipment lists and plot plans; accuracy ±25%.
4. **Definitive Estimate:** Used for project sanction; accuracy ±15%.
5. **Detailed Estimate:** Based on complete engineering drawings and vendor quotes; accuracy ±10%.

*(3 Marks for listing + 4 Marks for explanations = 7 Marks)*

---

##### **Model Solution — Part (b):**

**Given:**
- $Q_A$ = 80,000 t/yr; $C_A$ = ₹120 crores
- $Q_B$ = 1,60,000 t/yr; $n = 0.6$
- Lang Factor for fluid processing: $f = 4.74$

**Step 1 — Apply Lang Method:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_B &= f \cdot C_A \left( \frac{Q_B}{Q_A} \right)^{0.6} \\
    &= 4.74 \times 120 \times \left( \frac{1{,}60{,}000}{80{,}000} \right)^{0.6} \\
    &= 568.8 \times (2)^{0.6}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Evaluate $(2)^{0.6}$:**

$$
(2)^{0.6} = e^{0.6 \ln 2} = e^{0.6 \times 0.6931} = e^{0.4159} \approx 1.516
$$

**Step 3 — Final Central Estimate:**

$$
C_B = 568.8 \times 1.516 = 862.3 \text{ crores}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply Accuracy Bounds:**

- **Lower Bound:** $0.70 \times 862.3 = ₹603.6 \text{ crores}$
- **Upper Bound:** $1.50 \times 862.3 = ₹1293.4 \text{ crores}$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Answer:** $C_B = ₹862.3 \text{ crores}$ (Range: ₹603.6 cr – ₹1293.4 cr)

*(2 Marks formula + 2 Marks substitution + 2 Marks numerical answer + 1 Mark range = 7 Marks)*

---

#### **🅱️ QUESTION B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam – Jun 2023 Style]`

**Q: (a)** Explain the Power Law of cost estimation with its theoretical derivation. State the typical range of the cost-capacity exponent for chemical process plants. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A reference plant producing 25,000 tonnes/year of an organic chemical cost ₹40 crores. A new plant of capacity 75,000 tonnes/year is to be built. Using the Power Law with n = 0.65, compute (i) the central cost estimate, (ii) the −25% and +35% bounds for the estimate. **(7 Marks)**

---

##### **Model Solution — Part (a):**

**Theoretical Derivation:**
For similar geometric equipment, capacity scales with volume ($Q \propto L^3$) and cost scales with surface/material ($C \propto L^2$). Eliminating the linear dimension *L* gives $C \propto Q^{2/3}$.

For two similar plants A and B:

$$
\boxed{\,C_B = C_A \left( \frac{Q_B}{Q_A} \right)^{n}\,}
$$

**Range of exponent n for chemical process plants:**
- Whole chemical plants: **0.5 – 0.9**
- Most common (Six-Tenths Rule): **≈ 0.6**
- Heat exchangers: 0.60–0.65; Distillation columns: 0.60–0.70; Compressors: 0.70–0.75.

*(3 Marks derivation + 2 Marks formula + 2 Marks exponent range = 7 Marks)*

---

##### **Model Solution — Part (b):**

**Given:**
- $Q_A$ = 25,000 t/yr; $C_A$ = ₹40 crores
- $Q_B$ = 75,000 t/yr; $n = 0.65$

**Step 1 — Apply Power Law:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_B &= 40 \times \left( \frac{75{,}000}{25{,}000} \right)^{0.65} \\
    &= 40 \times (3)^{0.65}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Evaluate $(3)^{0.65}$:**

$$
(3)^{0.65} = e^{0.65 \times \ln 3} = e^{0.65 \times 1.0986} = e^{0.7141} \approx 2.042
$$

**Step 3 — Central Estimate:**

$$
C_B = 40 \times 2.042 = ₹81.7 \text{ crores}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply Modified Bounds (−25%, +35%):**

- **Lower:** $0.75 \times 81.7 = ₹61.3 \text{ crores}$
- **Upper:** $1.35 \times 81.7 = ₹110.3 \text{ crores}$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Answer:** $C_B = ₹81.7 \text{ crores}$ (Range: ₹61.3 cr – ₹110.3 cr)

*(Full marks split as per the standard KTU pattern)*

---

### ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Students Make (and where marks are deducted):**
> 1. **Forgetting to mention units** of capacity → −0.5 to −1 mark.
> 2. **Using n = 0.6 blindly** without verifying if the problem specifies another exponent (e.g., 0.65 or 0.7) → −1 mark.
> 3. **Confusing Lang Factor values** for solid vs fluid plants (3.10 vs 4.74) → −1 mark.
> 4. **Forgetting to apply the accuracy range** in the final answer → −1 to −2 marks.
> 5. **Not labelling the answer as an "Order of Magnitude Estimate"** in the conclusion → −0.5 mark.
> 6. **Misapplying Power Law** — writing $Q_A/Q_B$ instead of $Q_B/Q_A$ → −2 marks (most common fatal error).

---

### 🧠 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **🔖 Rapid Revision Checklist — Order of Magnitude Estimate**
> - ✅ OME is the **earliest** cost estimate; accuracy is **−30% to +50%**.
> - ✅ Core formula: **$C_B = C_A (Q_B/Q_A)^n$** (Power Law).
> - ✅ Six-Tenths Rule: **n ≈ 0.6** for whole chemical plants.
> - ✅ Lang Factor: **3.10 (solid plant)**, **4.74 (fluid plant)**.
> - ✅ Turnover Ratio method: $C = \text{Sales} / \text{TR}$; TR ≈ 0.4–1.0.
> - ✅ Always **state capacity units**; always **mention the method used**.
> - ✅ Always present **central estimate + accuracy bounds** in the final answer.
> - ✅ OME is used for **go/no-go screening**; not for budget approval.
> - ✅ Cost-capacity exponent *n* varies with equipment type (0.5–0.9).
> - ✅ OME requires the **least engineering effort** but has the **widest error range**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 2 : STUDY ESTIMATE

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **STUDY ESTIMATE (Also termed: Feasibility Estimate / Preliminary Estimate / Order-of-Magnitude Estimate)**
> A *Study Estimate* is an early-stage capital cost projection prepared during the **conceptual or pre-design phase** of a chemical process plant, used to evaluate economic viability, screen alternatives, and authorize further engineering expenditure. It is typically generated before detailed process flow diagrams (PFDs), Piping & Instrumentation Diagrams (P&IDs), or equipment specifications are finalized.

The accuracy envelope of a Study Estimate, as per the **American Association of Cost Engineers (AACE) Recommended Practice 18R-97**, lies in the range of **±30% to ±50%**, and it relies heavily on historical cost data, capacity-ratio scaling (the **Six-Tenths Rule**), and equipment-factoring techniques.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "House Renovation Budget"**
> Imagine you are planning to renovate a house. Before you call contractors for detailed quotes, you roughly estimate: *"A new kitchen costs about ₹5 lakhs, a bathroom about ₹2 lakhs..."* — you multiply by experience-based factors. This is **not exact**, but it tells you whether the project is affordable.
> A **Study Estimate in chemical engineering is exactly that** — a *rough but directionally correct* capital cost prediction made using scaling laws and percentage factors, BEFORE detailed design drawings exist. It answers the binary question: **"Should we invest more money into detailed engineering, or should we abandon the project?"**

### Key Characteristics of a Study Estimate

| Property | Description |
|---|---|
| Project Phase | Conceptual / Feasibility Stage |
| Available Data | Only basic process flow, capacity, and major equipment list |
| Expected Accuracy | **±30% to ±50%** |
| Engineering Effort | 0.1% to 1% of total project cost |
| Primary Use | Go / No-Go decision, project screening, capital budgeting |
| Basis | Historical cost data + capacity scaling |
| Output | Total Capital Investment (TCI) range |

### Types of Capital Cost Estimates (AACE Classification)

> [!NOTE]
> The KTU 2024 syllabus expects students to **differentiate** between the five AACE estimate classes.

1. **Class 5 — Order of Magnitude (Study Estimate)** → Accuracy ±30% to ±50%
2. **Class 4 — Factored (Budget Estimate)** → Accuracy ±15% to ±30%
3. **Class 3 — Definitive (Control Estimate)** → Accuracy ±10% to ±15%
4. **Class 2 — Detailed (Bid/Tender Estimate)** → Accuracy ±5% to ±10%
5. **Class 1 — Checked (Forensic Estimate)** → Accuracy ±3% to ±5%

### Underlying Physical / Economic Constants

- **Six-Tenths Rule exponent (n ≈ 0.6)** for capacity scaling of chemical plants
- **Lang Factor (F_L) ≈ 3.10** for solid processing plants
- **Lang Factor (F_L) ≈ 4.74** for fluid processing plants
- **Hand Factor (F_H)** ranges from **3 to 5**
- **Working Capital ≈ 10% to 20%** of Fixed Capital Investment (FCI)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Purpose of a Study Estimate

A study estimate serves four primary managerial functions in a chemical process industry (CPI) project lifecycle:

- **Economic Feasibility Screening** — Determines whether a proposed plant meets the corporate hurdle rate (typically **Internal Rate of Return (IRR) ≥ 15%** in Indian chemical industries).
- **Capital Budgeting Input** — Feeds the discounted cash flow (DCF) analysis for Net Present Value (NPV) computation.
- **Alternative Comparison** — Allows rank-ordering of multiple process routes (e.g., produce ethylene by steam cracking vs. catalytic dehydrogenation of ethane).
- **Project Authorization Gate** — Acts as the formal "Gate 2" review before sanctioning Front-End Engineering Design (FEED) funds.

## 2.2 Methods of Preparing a Study Estimate

The KTU 2024 syllabus emphasizes three principal methodologies:

### Method A — Capacity Ratio (Six-Tenths Rule / Power-Law Sizing)

For scaling equipment or total plant cost when only the capacity of a reference plant is known:

$$
C_B = C_A \left( \frac{S_B}{S_A} \right)^{n}
$$

Where:
- $C_B$ = Cost of proposed plant (B)
- $C_A$ = Cost of reference existing plant (A)
- $S_B$ = Capacity of proposed plant
- $S_A$ = Capacity of reference plant
- $n$ = Scaling exponent (**0.6** for chemical plants, **0.8** for some specialty equipment)

### Method B — Turnover Ratio / Lang Factor Method

Lang (1948) developed empirical multipliers (Lang Factors) to estimate the total capital investment (ISBL + OSBL) from the delivered equipment cost:

$$
C_{TM} = F_L \times C_{E}
$$

Where:
- $C_{TM}$ = Total module cost (or Total Capital Investment in some texts)
- $F_L$ = Lang Factor (3.10 solid / 4.74 fluid)
- $C_{E}$ = Purchased cost of major process equipment

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The Lang Factor method is the **most frequently tested** technique in KTU examinations under the Study Estimate module.

### Method C — Percentage of Delivered Equipment Cost (Hand's Method)

Walas (1988) refined the Lang approach by breaking the factor into discrete installation, piping, instrumentation, and offsites percentages. The sum of multipliers times the purchased-equipment cost yields the **Bare Module Cost**:

$$
C_{BM} = C_E \left( 1 + f_{piping} + f_{instrumentation} + f_{insulation} + f_{electrical} + f_{buildings} + f_{yard} + f_{service} \right)
$$

Where each $f$ value is given as a decimal fraction (e.g., 0.35 for piping).

## 2.3 The Six-Tenths Rule — Physical & Economic Justification

The 0.6 exponent emerges from the principle of **economies of scale**. The capital cost of a chemical plant grows sub-linearly with capacity because:

- Equipment volume grows as $V \propto S$ (linear in capacity)
- Equipment surface area grows as $A \propto S^{2/3}$ (heat transfer scaling)
- Cost, which is dominated by metal weight (surface), therefore scales as $\text{Cost} \propto S^{0.6}$

## 2.4 Working Capital & Total Capital Investment

The final output of a study estimate is the **Total Capital Investment (TCI)**, comprising:

$$
TCI = FCI + WC
$$

Where:
- $FCI$ = Fixed Capital Investment
- $WC$ = Working Capital ≈ **10% – 20%** of FCI (often taken as **15%** in textbook problems)

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Typical Constants / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $C_B = C_A \left( S_B / S_A \right)^{n}$ | Six-Tenths Rule (Power-Law Scaling) | $n = 0.6$ (chemical plants) |
| 2 | $C_{TM} = F_L \cdot C_E$ | Lang Factor Method | $F_L = 3.10$ (solid), $4.74$ (fluid) |
| 3 | $C_{BM} = C_E \left( 1 + \Sigma f_i \right)$ | Bare Module Cost (Hand's Method) | $f_i$ from 0.05 to 0.80 per item |
| 4 | $C_{TM} = 1.18 \cdot C_{BM}$ | Total Module Cost (includes contingency) | Contingency factor = 1.18 |
| 5 | $C_{GR} = 1.40 \cdot C_{TM}$ | Grassroots Cost (with offsites) | Offsites factor = 1.40 (approx) |
| 6 | $TCI = FCI + WC$ | Total Capital Investment | $WC = 0.10$ to $0.20 \cdot FCI$ |
| 7 | $\text{Accuracy} = \pm 30\% \text{ to } \pm 50\%$ | Study Estimate Class 5 | AACE Class 5 boundary |
| 8 | $\text{CEPCI}_{\text{updated}} = C_{\text{ref}} \times \dfrac{CEPCI_{\text{updated}}}{CEPCI_{\text{ref}}}$ | Cost Index Inflation/Escalation | CEPCI published monthly |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

Study estimates are routinely used in:

- **Petrochemical industry** for green-field refinery expansions
- **Pharmaceutical industry** for API manufacturing feasibility
- **Indian PSU projects** (IOCL, BPCL, HPCL) for annual capex portfolio screening
- **Start-up valuation** of process technologies by VC/PE firms
- **Strategic planning** of multinationals (BASF, Dow, Reliance) for capacity addition
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Six-Tenths Rule (From First Principles)

Let us derive the scaling law from dimensional analysis and equipment cost logic.

**Step 1 — Equipment volume scales linearly with throughput:**
A reactor, distillation column, or absorber handling a given capacity $S$ requires a hold-up volume $V$ proportional to the residence time $\tau$ times capacity:

$$
V \propto S \quad \text{(linear proportionality)}
$$

**Step 2 — Equipment surface area (which determines metal weight and hence cost) scales as the two-thirds power:**

For vessels, the characteristic length $L \propto V^{1/3}$, hence the surface area:

$$
A \propto L^2 \propto \left( V^{1/3} \right)^{2} = V^{2/3} \propto S^{2/3}
$$

**Step 3 — Capital cost is dominated by metal mass (surface area × thickness):**

$$
C \propto A \propto S^{2/3} = S^{0.667}
$$

**Step 4 — Empirical observations across hundreds of chemical plants yield $n \approx 0.6$ rather than 0.667, because:**

- Standardized sizes (vessel diameters come in discrete steps)
- Modular construction efficiencies
- Spare capacity over-design
- Auxiliary equipment (pumps, blowers) scaling more steeply

Hence the **Six-Tenths Rule** in final form:

$$
\boxed{C_B = C_A \left( \frac{S_B}{S_A} \right)^{0.6}}
$$

## 3.2 Worked Example — Six-Tenths Rule Application

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2023 Style Problem]**
> A 50,000 tonnes/year ammonia plant was built at a cost of ₹450 crores (2020 prices). Estimate the cost of a similar plant with capacity 1,50,000 tonnes/year using the Six-Tenths Rule. Also compute the updated cost in 2024 if the CEPCI was 596 in 2020 and 800 in 2024.

### Solution — Step by Step

**Step 1 — Identify given values:**

$$
C_A = ₹450 \text{ crores}, \quad S_A = 50{,}000 \text{ t/y}, \quad S_B = 1{,}50{,}000 \text{ t/y}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the capacity ratio:**

$$
\frac{S_B}{S_A} = \frac{1{,}50{,}000}{50{,}000} = 3.0
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the Six-Tenths Rule with $n = 0.6$:**

$$
C_B = 450 \times (3.0)^{0.6}
$$

Compute $(3.0)^{0.6}$:

$$
(3.0)^{0.6} = e^{0.6 \cdot \ln 3} = e^{0.6 \times 1.0986} = e^{0.6592} = 1.933
$$

Therefore:

$$
C_B = 450 \times 1.933 = ₹869.9 \text{ crores} \approx ₹870 \text{ crores (2020 prices)}
$$

**Step 4 — Escalate to 2024 using CEPCI:**

$$
C_{B,2024} = C_{B,2020} \times \frac{CEPCI_{2024}}{CEPCI_{2020}} = 869.9 \times \frac{800}{596}
$$

Compute the index ratio:

$$
\frac{800}{596} = 1.3423
$$

Therefore:

$$
C_{B,2024} = 869.9 \times 1.3423 = ₹1167.6 \text{ crores}
$$

$$
\boxed{C_{B,2024} \approx ₹1168 \text{ crores}}
$$

## 3.3 Worked Example — Lang Factor Method

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022 Style Problem]**
> The purchased cost of major process equipment for a fluid-processing chemical plant is ₹200 crores. The total module (Bare Module + Engineering + Contingency) cost of the plant is to be estimated. Also compute the TCI assuming Working Capital is 15% of FCI.

### Solution — Step by Step

**Step 1 — Select the correct Lang Factor:**

For a **fluid-processing plant** (e.g., refinery, distillation unit, lube plant):

$$
F_L = 4.74
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the Lang Factor equation:**

$$
C_{TM} = F_L \times C_E = 4.74 \times 200 = ₹948 \text{ crores}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Total Capital Investment (TCI):**

$$
WC = 0.15 \times FCI = 0.15 \times 948 = ₹142.2 \text{ crores}
$$

$$
TCI = FCI + WC = 948 + 142.2 = ₹1090.2 \text{ crores}
$$

$$
\boxed{TCI \approx ₹1090 \text{ crores}}
$$

**Step 4 — Cross-validate with Class 5 accuracy band:**

$$
\text{Range} = 1090.2 \pm 50\% = ₹545 \text{ crores to } ₹1635 \text{ crores}
$$

This wide band is precisely why a Study Estimate is **not suitable for final investment decisions** but only for feasibility screening.

## 3.4 Comparison Matrix — Study vs. Factored vs. Detailed Estimate

| Parameter | Study Estimate (Class 5) | Factored Estimate (Class 4) | Detailed Estimate (Class 3) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Accuracy | ±30% to ±50% | ±15% to ±30% | ±10% to ±15% |
| Engineering Effort | < 1% of project cost | 1% – 3% | 3% – 10% |
| Data Required | Capacity, rough equipment list | Equipment specs with material of construction | Full P&ID, vendor quotes |
| Lead Time | Days to weeks | Weeks to months | Months to year |
| Method | Six-Tenths, Lang Factor | Hand's Bare Module | Bottom-up line-item |
| Output | TCI range | Budgeted TCI | Committed TCI |
| Use | Feasibility, go/no-go | Capital approval | Project execution |

## 3.5 Step-by-Step Escalation Calculation Using Chemical Engineering Plant Cost Index (CEPCI)

For problems involving cost data from a different year, the **CEPCI** (published by *Chemical Engineering* magazine) is the standard deflator/inflator.

$$
C_{\text{present}} = C_{\text{old}} \times \frac{CEPCI_{\text{present}}}{CEPCI_{\text{old}}}
$$

**Step 1 — Identify reference year cost $C_{\text{old}}$ and the CEPCI of that year.**

**Step 2 — Identify the target year and its CEPCI.**

**Step 3 — Compute the ratio $(CEPCI_{\text{present}} / CEPCI_{\text{old}})$.**

**Step 4 — Multiply $C_{\text{old}}$ by this ratio to obtain the present-day cost.**

**Step 5 — Add region/location factor if the plant is in a different country:**

$$
C_{\text{India}} = C_{\text{US}} \times f_{\text{India/US}}
$$

where $f_{\text{India/US}} \approx 0.45$ to $0.60$ (Indian equipment and labour are cheaper than US Gulf Coast baseline).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Flowchart — Position of Study Estimate in Project Lifecycle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Stage 1: Idea Generation] --> nodeB[Stage 2: Order of Magnitude Study Estimate]
    nodeB --> nodeC{Go / No-Go Gate 1}
    nodeC -- Yes --> nodeD[Stage 3: Feasibility Study Report]
    nodeD --> nodeE[Stage 4: Factored / Budget Estimate]
    nodeE --> nodeF{Go / No-Go Gate 2}
    nodeF -- Yes --> nodeG[Stage 5: Front End Engineering Design]
    nodeG --> nodeH[Stage 6: Definitive Estimate Class 3]
    nodeH --> nodeI[Stage 7: Detailed Engineering Class 2]
    nodeI --> nodeJ[Stage 8: Construction and Commissioning]
```

**Reading the diagram:**

- The **Study Estimate occupies Gate 1** of the project gating process.
- It precedes the Factored Estimate, which precedes the Definitive Estimate.
- Each successive stage **narrows the accuracy band** (from ±50% to ±10%) but **increases engineering cost and lead time**.

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Inputs to a Study Estimate

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeInput1[Plant Capacity S] --> nodeEngine
    nodeInput2[Process Type Solid or Fluid] --> nodeEngine
    nodeInput3[Reference Plant Cost Cref] --> nodeEngine
    nodeInput4[Cost Index CEPCI] --> nodeEngine
    nodeInput5[Location Factor] --> nodeEngine
    nodeEngine[Study Estimate Engine] --> nodeOutput1[Estimated Plant Cost Cinstalled]
    nodeEngine --> nodeOutput2[Accuracy Band plus minus 30 to 50 percent]
    nodeEngine --> nodeOutput3[Total Capital Investment TCI]
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Three Methods of Study Estimate Generation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeStart[Known Information: Capacity and Reference Plant] --> nodeM1[Method 1: Six Tenths Rule]
    nodeStart --> nodeM2[Method 2: Lang Factor]
    nodeStart --> nodeM3[Method 3: Percentage of Equipment Cost]
    nodeM1 --> nodeOut[Output: Total Capital Investment Range]
    nodeM2 --> nodeOut
    nodeM3 --> nodeOut
```

## 4.4 Functional Architecture — Capital Cost Cascade

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    node1[Purchased Equipment Cost CE] --> node2[Bare Module Cost CBM]
    node2 --> node3[Total Module Cost CTM includes Contingency 18 percent]
    node3 --> node4[Grassroots Cost CGR includes Offsites 40 percent]
    node4 --> node5[Fixed Capital Investment FCI]
    node5 --> node6[Working Capital WC 10 to 20 percent of FCI]
    node6 --> node7[Total Capital Investment TCI]
```

**Cascade interpretation:**

- Each stage adds a multiplier (e.g., contingency multiplier 1.18, offsites multiplier 1.40) to the previous cost basis.
- A Study Estimate typically estimates only steps 1 through 5 and assumes step 6 to be a fixed fraction of step 5.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. [KTU University Exam - May 2023]

**Define a Study Estimate and state its accuracy range. Why is it called a "Class 5" estimate?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A Study Estimate, also known as an Order-of-Magnitude Estimate or Feasibility Estimate, is a preliminary capital cost projection prepared during the conceptual phase of a chemical plant project, using limited process data and historical cost analogies. It is classified as **Class 5** under the AACE Recommended Practice 18R-97 and carries an accuracy of **±30% to ±50%**. It is used to screen project alternatives and authorize further detailed engineering work. *(3 marks: definition 1.5 marks, accuracy 1 mark, purpose 0.5 mark)*

### Question 2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]

**State and explain the Six-Tenths Rule. What is the typical value of the exponent for chemical process plants?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The Six-Tenths Rule is an empirical cost-scaling law that relates the cost of a new plant to the cost of an existing reference plant via the ratio of their capacities raised to the power 0.6:

$$
C_B = C_A \left( \frac{S_B}{S_A} \right)^{0.6}
$$

For chemical process plants, the exponent **n = 0.6** is universally used because equipment cost is dominated by metal mass (surface area), which scales as the two-thirds power of capacity. *(3 marks: statement with formula 2 marks, physical justification 1 mark)*

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU ESE Module Internal Choice:** A single Part B question offers two alternatives, and the student must answer ONE.

---

### OPTION A (14 Marks)

#### **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**(a)** A 75,000 tonnes/year polyethylene plant was built in 2018 at a cost of ₹600 crores. Using the Six-Tenths Rule, estimate the cost of a similar 2,00,000 tonnes/year plant in 2024. Take the CEPCI for 2018 as 567 and 2024 as 800. (7 marks)

**(b)** Explain the Lang Factor method of preparing a Study Estimate. List the Lang Factor values for solid and fluid processing plants. (7 marks)

---

#### SOLUTION — PART (a)

**Step 1 — Identify data:**

$$
C_A = ₹600 \text{ crores}, \quad S_A = 75{,}000 \text{ t/y}, \quad S_B = 2{,}00{,}000 \text{ t/y}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the capacity ratio:**

$$
\frac{S_B}{S_A} = \frac{2{,}00{,}000}{75{,}000} = 2.667
$$

**Step 3 — Apply Six-Tenths Rule:**

$$
C_{B,2018} = 600 \times (2.667)^{0.6}
$$

Compute $(2.667)^{0.6}$:

$$
(2.667)^{0.6} = e^{0.6 \times \ln 2.667} = e^{0.6 \times 0.9808} = e^{0.5885} = 1.801
$$

Therefore:

$$
C_{B,2018} = 600 \times 1.801 = ₹1080.6 \text{ crores (2018 prices)}
$$

**Step 4 — Escalate from 2018 to 2024 using CEPCI:**

$$
C_{B,2024} = 1080.6 \times \frac{800}{567} = 1080.6 \times 1.4109 = ₹1524.6 \text{ crores}
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{Estimated cost in 2024} \approx ₹1525 \text{ crores}}
$$

---

#### SOLUTION — PART (b)

**Step 1 — Definition:**

The Lang Factor Method, proposed by H.J. Lang in 1948, estimates the **Total Module Cost (TMC)** of a chemical plant as a multiple of the **Purchased Equipment Cost (PEC)**. It is one of the most widely used techniques for a Study Estimate when detailed equipment lists are available but piping, instrumentation, and structural drawings are not yet prepared.

**Step 2 — Equation:**

$$
C_{TM} = F_L \times C_E
$$

**Step 3 — Lang Factor values (from Lang 1948):**

| Plant Type | Lang Factor $F_L$ |
|---|---|
| Solid Processing (e.g., cement, ore beneficiation) | 3.10 |
| Fluid Processing (e.g., petroleum refining, distillation) | 4.74 |
| Mixed Solid-Fluid | 3.63 (average) |

**Step 4 — Limitations:**

- Accuracy degrades for non-standard processes (e.g., cryogenic, biotech).
- Fails for plants with very high offsite investments (e.g., integrated refineries).
- Does not capture region-specific cost differences (India, Gulf, US).
- Hence, a Hand Factor or detailed factored estimate is preferred for Class 4 and beyond.

*(7 marks: definition 2 marks, equation 1 mark, factor values table 2 marks, limitations 2 marks)*

---

### OPTION B (14 Marks)

#### **[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

**(a)** With a neat flowchart, describe the project stages in which a Study Estimate is generated. State its accuracy and engineering effort required. (7 marks)

**(b)** The delivered cost of major equipment for a solid-processing plant is ₹150 crores. Compute the Total Module Cost using the Lang Factor method. Also estimate the Total Capital Investment assuming Working Capital = 15% of FCI. (7 marks)

---

#### SOLUTION — PART (a)

**Step 1 — Project Lifecycle Flowchart (reproduced for answer script):**

The project moves through: **Idea Generation → Study Estimate → Feasibility Study → Factored Estimate → FEED → Definitive Estimate → Detailed Engineering → Construction**.

**Step 2 — Position of Study Estimate:**

The Study Estimate is prepared immediately after idea generation and BEFORE detailed feasibility. It is used as the **Go / No-Go Gate 1** decision input.

**Step 3 — Accuracy:**

Class 5 estimate → **±30% to ±50%** accuracy per AACE 18R-97.

**Step 4 — Engineering Effort:**

Typically **0.1% to 1%** of the total project cost; data is taken from process flow diagrams, major equipment list, and historical cost data.

*(7 marks: flowchart 2 marks, position 2 marks, accuracy 1.5 marks, effort 1.5 marks)*

---

#### SOLUTION — PART (b)

**Step 1 — Identify plant type and select Lang Factor:**

For a **solid-processing plant** (e.g., cement, fertilizer granulation):

$$
F_L = 3.10
$$

**Step 2 — Compute Total Module Cost:**

$$
C_{TM} = F_L \times C_E = 3.10 \times 150 = ₹465 \text{ crores}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Working Capital:**

$$
WC = 0.15 \times 465 = ₹69.75 \text{ crores}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute Total Capital Investment:**

$$
TCI = FCI + WC = 465 + 69.75 = ₹534.75 \text{ crores}
$$

$$
\boxed{TCI \approx ₹534.75 \text{ crores}}
$$

*(7 marks: factor selection 1 mark, TMC 2 marks, WC 2 marks, TCI 2 marks)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Wrong Lang Factor** — Students frequently use $F_L = 4.74$ for solid plants or vice versa. Memorize the **plant type vs. factor mapping** explicitly.
> 2. **Forgetting to escalate** the cost using CEPCI when the reference plant is in a different year — this is a **3-mark loss** in typical KTU scripts.
> 3. **Mixing up FCI and TCI** — A study estimate gives FCI; TCI = FCI + Working Capital. KTU evaluators deduct 1 mark for this conflation.
> 4. **Wrong exponent in Six-Tenths Rule** — Always use **0.6** for chemical plants; some specialty equipment (e.g., crystallizers) may use 0.8.
> 5. **No units in final answer** — Always state the cost in the same unit as the input data (₹ crores, ₹ lakhs, $ million, etc.).

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Module 2 — Study Estimate)**

- Study Estimate = Class 5 estimate per AACE; accuracy = **±30% to ±50%**.
- It is prepared at the **conceptual / pre-feasibility** stage using only process capacity and major equipment list.
- **Six-Tenths Rule:** $C_B = C_A (S_B / S_A)^{0.6}$ — use it when only capacity and a reference plant cost are known.
- **Lang Factor Method:** $C_{TM} = F_L \cdot C_E$ — use it when the delivered equipment cost is known.
- **Lang Factors:** $F_L = 3.10$ (solid), $4.74$ (fluid), $3.63$ (mixed).
- **Hand's Method:** $C_{BM} = C_E (1 + \Sigma f_i)$ — use it for more granular Class 4 estimates.
- **Total Capital Investment:** $TCI = FCI + WC$, where $WC \approx 10\% - 20\%$ of FCI (typically 15%).
- **Cost escalation:** Use CEPCI ratio to deflate/inflate historical costs.
- **Engineering effort** for a Study Estimate is **0.1% – 1%** of the project cost.
- **Primary purpose:** Go/No-Go decision; **not** suitable for final investment sanction.
- A Study Estimate has **3 principal methods:** Six-Tenths, Lang Factor, and Hand's Percentage.
- The 0.6 exponent in the Six-Tenths Rule arises because **equipment cost scales with surface area**, not volume.
- For a fluid plant with $C_E = ₹100$ cr → $C_{TM} \approx ₹474$ cr (Lang) — remember this benchmark for quick mental checks in exams.
- Always present the **final cost with units and reference year** explicitly.
- For Indian chemical plants, apply **Location Factor ≈ 0.45 to 0.60** when scaling from US Gulf Coast baseline cost data.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
