topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT757 - ECONOMICS AND MANAGEMENT OF CHEMICAL INDUSTRIES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Preliminary Estimate

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 MODULE 2 — TOPIC: PRELIMINARY ESTIMATE

## 1.1 — Core Technical Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Preliminary Estimate:** A **preliminary (or "order-of-magnitude") estimate** is the earliest appraisal of the capital and/or operating cost of a chemical process plant, prepared *before* detailed process flowsheets, equipment specifications, and P&IDs are finalized. It is generated during the **project conception / feasibility stage** and is used by management to decide whether the project is worth pursuing, refining, or abandoning.

| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| **Stage of Project** | Conception / Feasibility |
| **Data Available** | Capacity, broad process route, block diagram only |
| **Engineering Effort** | 0 – 2 % of total man-hours |
| **Accuracy Range (Cost)** | **± 30 % to ± 50 %** |
| **Primary Use** | Go / No-Go decision, screening alternatives, raising seed capital |
| **Cost of Producing the Estimate** | 0.1 – 0.2 % of project cost |

> [!NOTE]
> In the KTU 2024 PECHT757 syllabus, Module 2 covers **estimation of capital & operating cost, working capital, and preliminary profitability concepts**. The *preliminary estimate* is the **gateway** to all of these — it provides the very first number from which everything else (depreciation, ROI, payback) is computed.

### 1.2 — Intuitive Analogy (Plain-English Explanation)

Imagine you want to build your dream house but have only seen a small sketch on a napkin.

* You don't yet know the exact tile brand, window dimensions, or wiring plan.
* However, you know — *roughly* — the **size (square feet)** and the **finish quality (basic / premium)**.
* Based on your neighbour's recently built house (a *similar project*), the builder tells you:
> *"For a 2 000 ft² premium house, expect to spend somewhere between ₹ 50 lakh and ₹ 70 lakh."*

That **₹ 50–70 lakh range** is your **preliminary estimate**.
The detailed final quotation (after drawings, soil tests, BOQ) comes much later.

In **chemical-engineering terms**:
* The "size" of your plant is the **plant capacity** (tonnes/yr, kg/hr, MW).
* The "neighbour's house" is a **reference plant** whose cost is publicly known.
* The "builder's thumb-rule" is the **Six-Tenths Rule, Lang factors, or Hand's method**.

Thus, **Preliminary Estimate = Quick cost range using past-project analogies or empirical correlations**, *not* a definitive budget.

> [!TIP]
> A common student confusion: *"Why don't we just wait for the definitive estimate?"*
> **Answer:** Because by the time you have the definitive (AACE Class 1) estimate, you may have already wasted years and crores on a project that was **never economically viable**. The preliminary estimate *filters out bad ideas early*.

---

## 1.3 — Classification of Capital Cost Estimates (AACE Recommended)

The **American Association of Cost Engineers (AACE)** classifies estimates into 5 classes based on maturity of project definition. KTU expects students to recognise all of these.

| Class | Name | Maturity of Engineering | Accuracy | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **5** | Order-of-Magnitude (Screening) | 0 – 2 % | **± 30 – 50 %** | **Preliminary** / Strategic |
| **4** | Preliminary (Budget Authorization) | 1 – 5 % | ± 20 – 40 % | Concept selection |
| 3 | Definitive (Budget / Control) | 10 – 40 % | ± 10 – 25 % | Authorization of funds |
| 2 | Detailed (Contract / Bid) | 30 – 70 % | ± 5 – 15 % | Bidding / tendering |
| 1 | Check (Final / Check) | 70 – 100 % | ± 3 – 10 % | Construction & control |

> [!NOTE]
> The KTU term **"Preliminary Estimate"** most closely maps to **AACE Class 5 / Class 4**. The lecturer will accept either as a valid synonym; in numerical problems, the "preliminary" wording means *use power-law / factor methods*, not detailed BOQ.

---

## 1.4 — Why is the Preliminary Estimate Important for Chemical Industries?

Chemical process plants are **capex-heavy** (typical grass-roots plant: **₹ 200 – 5 000 crore**) and have **long gestation periods (3 – 7 years)**. A wrong go-decision at the start can bankrupt a company.

The preliminary estimate answers three killer questions of management:

1. **Can we afford it?** (Is the capital within corporate treasury limits?)
2. **Will it make money?** (Is ROI > hurdle rate of 12 – 18 % for Indian chemical sector?)
3. **Is this process the best?** (Compare alternative process routes on cost-only basis.)

---

## 1.5 — Data Required to Make a Preliminary Estimate

You do **not** need detailed equipment lists. The minimum data set is:

* **Plant capacity** ($Q$) — e.g., tonnes of product per year.
* **Process route** — e.g., ammonia via steam-methane reforming vs. partial oxidation.
* **Site conditions** — green-field vs. brown-field, infrastructure availability.
* **Reference plant data** — cost and capacity of a *similar* plant built earlier.
* **Cost index value** for the reference year and the current year (to time-adjust).
* **Location factor** (if reference plant is in a different country).

That is *all*. No vessel thicknesses, no pump curves, no relief-valve sizing.

---

## 1.6 — Limitations of a Preliminary Estimate

> [!WARNING]
> KTU examiners **love** asking limitations. Memorise the four core ones:
> 1. **Wide accuracy band (±30–50 %)** — not suitable for financing decisions.
> 2. **Highly sensitive to choice of reference plant** — wrong analogue ⇒ wrong answer.
> 3. **Cannot capture process innovations** that the reference plant lacked.
> 4. **Ignores site-specific costs** (soil, seismic, labour strikes, local taxes).

# 📗 2 — DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 — Six-Tenths Rule (Williams' Power Law)

The single most-tested formula of this topic.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Six-Tenths Rule Statement:**
> *If the cost of a reference chemical plant of capacity $Q_1$ is $C_1$, then the cost of a similar plant of capacity $Q_2$ is given by:*

$$
C_2 = C_1 \left(\frac{Q_2}{Q_1}\right)^{0.6}
$$

The exponent **0.6** is empirical — derived from statistical regression of historical plant costs.

### Why "0.6" and not "1"?

If you doubled the capacity of a plant, you would *not* need to double the cost (i.e., *not* a linear relationship). Why?

* Larger equipment does not require double the material — a vessel of double volume needs only ~26 % more steel (surface area scales with $\text{capacity}^{2/3}$).
* Utility headers, control loops, buildings, and labour show **sub-linear scaling**.
* Hence, the empirical exponent is **between 0.4 and 0.8** for most chemical equipment, with **0.6 being the global default**.

### Adjusted Six-Tenths Rule (with cost-index & location factor)

When the reference plant is in a different year and/or different country:

$$
C_2 = C_1 \left(\frac{Q_2}{Q_1}\right)^{0.6} \left(\frac{I_2}{I_1}\right) \left(\frac{L_2}{L_1}\right)
$$

Where:
* $I_1, I_2$ = cost indices (e.g., CEPCI — *Chemical Engineering Plant Cost Index*) for reference year and present year.
* $L_1, L_2$ = location factors (country / city specific).

---

## 2.2 — Lang's Method (Total Capital via Multipliers on Delivered-Equipment Cost)

H. J. Lang (1947) proposed that the **Fixed Capital Investment (FCI)** of a process plant can be estimated as a **multiplier** of the **delivered cost of major process equipment**.

$$
\boxed{\text{FCI} = f_L \cdot \sum C_{\text{eq}}}
$$

Where $f_L$ = Lang factor (depends on plant type).

| Plant Type | Lang Factor $f_L$ |
|---|---|
| **Solid-processing** (e.g., cement, ore) | **3.10** |
| **Solid–Fluid processing** (e.g., pigment, plastics) | **3.63** |
| **Fluid-processing** (e.g., ammonia, refinery, most chemicals) | **4.74** |

> [!NOTE]
> KTU numericals frequently give you *total equipment cost* and ask you to compute *total capital investment* using Lang factors. Lang factors **already include** installation, piping, instrumentation, electrical, structures, insulation, painting, and indirect/engineering costs.

### Drawbacks of Lang's Method

* Lang factors are **averages** from 1940s data — modern plants have different cost proportions (more instrumentation, less piping weight).
* AACE recommends that Lang factors be used **only** at the *preliminary* stage.
* They do **not** distinguish between *inside-battery-limits* (ISBL) and *off-site* (OSBL) investments.

---

## 2.3 — Hand's Method (Bare-Module Factor Approach)

A more granular improvement on Lang's method. W. E. Hand (1958) proposed:

$$
\text{FCI} = f_{\text{BM}} \cdot C_{\text{BE}}
$$

Where:
* $C_{\text{BE}}$ = **Bare Equipment cost** (FOB, before freight, taxes).
* $f_{\text{BM}}$ = **Bare-Module factor**, computed as:

$$
f_{\text{BM}} = \left(1 + f_{\text{piping}}\right) + f_{\text{EL}} \cdot f_{\text{ER}}
$$

* $f_{\text{EL}}$ = Engineering & Labour factor (≈ 1.5 typical).
* $f_{\text{ER}}$ = Equipment-related (≈ 1.0 – 1.5 depending on fluid/solid nature).
* $f_{\text{piping}}$ = piping factor (≈ 0.2 – 1.0).

| Equipment Type | Typical $f_{\text{BM}}$ |
|---|---|
| Compressor | 2.5 |
| Pump (centrifugal) | 3.3 |
| Pump (positive displacement) | 2.7 |
| Heat Exchanger (fixed TEMA) | 3.1 |
| Vertical Vessel | 3.1 |
| Horizontal Vessel | 3.4 |
| Tray Column (packed) | 4.1 |
| Tray Column (trayed) | 4.2 |
| Furnace / Heater | 2.0 |
| Filter | 1.7 |

> [!TIP]
> **Hand's bare-module factors** are also called **"Lang factors by equipment category"** — they are far more accurate because different equipment types have very different installation labour & material contents.

---

## 2.4 — Step-Counting Method (Quick Capacity Estimate)

Used when even the reference plant is unknown. Each **"step"** = one major process unit operation (pump, compressor, reactor, distillation tower, exchanger, etc.).

$$
\text{FCI} \approx \text{₹ / step} \times N_{\text{steps}}
$$

Roughly **₹ 8 – 15 crore per major step** in 2024 Indian conditions for medium-scale chemical plants.

> This method is **least accurate** and used only for *order-of-magnitude* sanity checks.

---

## 2.5 — Working Capital Estimation (Preliminary)

Working capital (WC) = the money tied up in inventories, accounts receivable, and cash required to run the plant *until revenue starts flowing in*.

The preliminary estimate uses the simple **percent-of-annual-sales rule**:

$$
\text{WC} = p_{\text{WC}} \cdot \text{Annual Sales}
$$

| Industry | $p_{\text{WC}}$ (typical) |
|---|---|
| Bulk Chemicals | 10 – 15 % |
| Specialty / Pharma | 20 – 30 % |
| Petrochemicals | 8 – 12 % |

Alternatively, WC is sometimes estimated as a fraction of **Fixed Capital Investment**:

$$
\text{WC} = 0.10 \cdot \text{FCI} \quad \text{(rule-of-thumb for bulk chemicals)}
$$

---

## 2.6 — Total Capital Investment (TCI) — The Grand Equation

$$
\text{TCI} = \text{FCI} + \text{WC}
$$

And FCI is composed of:

$$
\text{FCI} = \text{ISBL} + \text{OSBL}
$$

Where ISBL = Inside Battery Limits (process plant proper) and OSBL = Off-site / utilities / storage / township.

---

## 2.7 — 📋 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat-Sheet Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **EXAM HALL FORMULA TABLE** — reproduce this in your answer sheet during derivation questions. Use `\vert` instead of `|` for absolute values.

| # | Formula | Description | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $C_2 = C_1 \left(\frac{Q_2}{Q_1}\right)^{0.6}$ | Six-Tenths Rule (basic) | Capacity scaling |
| 2 | $C_2 = C_1 \left(\frac{Q_2}{Q_1}\right)^{0.6} \cdot \frac{I_2}{I_1} \cdot \frac{L_2}{L_1}$ | Six-Tenths (with index & location) | Time + place adjustment |
| 3 | $\text{FCI} = f_L \cdot \sum C_{\text{eq}}$ | Lang's method | Quick FCI from equipment cost |
| 4 | $\text{FCI} = f_{\text{BM}} \cdot C_{\text{BE}}$ | Hand's bare-module method | FCI from single equipment cost |
| 5 | $\text{WC} = p_{\text{WC}} \cdot \text{Annual Sales}$ | Working capital (% of sales) | Quick WC |
| 6 | $\text{WC} \approx 0.10 \cdot \text{FCI}$ | WC rule of thumb (bulk chem) | Quick WC |
| 7 | $\text{TCI} = \text{FCI} + \text{WC}$ | Total Capital Investment | Grand total |
| 8 | $\text{FCI} = \text{ISBL} + \text{OSBL}$ | FCI split | Inside/Outside battery |
| 9 | $C_{\text{escalated}} = C_{\text{ref}} \cdot \frac{I_{\text{current}}}{I_{\text{ref}}}$ | Cost escalation | Time-adjust a past quote |
| 10 | $\text{Step-count method: FCI} = N_{\text{steps}} \cdot k$ | Step-count | Order-of-magnitude only |

---

## 2.8 — Real-World Utility in Chemical Engineering

* **Petrochemical Sector:** Crude-oil refineries routinely use Six-Tenths rule for capacity debottlenecking decisions (e.g., expanding from 10 to 15 MMTPA).
* **Pharma:** Green-field API plants use Hand's method to pre-budget capex before tendering.
* **Fertiliser (Urea, Ammonia):** Lang's method is the standard for screening.
* **Green Hydrogen / New-Energy:** With **no historical analogue**, step-counting + cost-of-equipment-today is the only option.

# 🧮 3 — STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 — Derivation: Why 0.6 in Six-Tenths Rule (Rationale from First Principles)

We derive the exponent assuming **equipment cost scales as the heat-transfer or material-handling surface area**, while capacity scales as **volume or mass**.

For a *cylindrical pressure vessel* (workhorse of chemical plants):
* Volume (capacity proxy): $V \propto L \cdot D^2$
* Surface area (cost proxy): $A \propto L \cdot D$

If we keep *length-to-diameter ratio constant* ($L/D = \text{const}$), then:
* $V \propto D^3 \Rightarrow D \propto V^{1/3}$
* $A \propto D^2 \propto V^{2/3}$

Therefore:

$$
\frac{C_2}{C_1} = \left(\frac{A_2}{A_1}\right) = \left(\frac{V_2}{V_1}\right)^{2/3} = \left(\frac{Q_2}{Q_1}\right)^{0.667}
$$

So **the geometric exponent for a single vessel ≈ 0.67**, very close to the empirical 0.6.

For a *complete plant*, which contains a mix of vessels, pumps, piping, instruments, and buildings, the empirical average regresses to **0.6**. This is the *theoretical* justification KTU expects you to write for full marks in a derivation question.

---

## 3.2 — Exhaustive Worked Example 1: Six-Tenths Rule with Cost Index

> **Question (typical 7-mark KTU problem):**
> A 50 000 tonne/year ammonia plant cost **₹ 250 crore** in **2018**. Estimate the cost of a similar plant of capacity **80 000 tonne/year** in **2024**, given the CEPCI was **550 in 2018** and **820 in 2024**. Use the six-tenths rule.

**Step 1 — Identify all variables.**

$$
Q_1 = 50\,000 \text{ t/yr}, \quad C_1 = 250 \text{ cr}, \quad Q_2 = 80\,000 \text{ t/yr}, \quad I_1 = 550, \quad I_2 = 820
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the Six-Tenths Rule (capacity scaling only).**

$$
C_2^* = C_1 \left(\frac{Q_2}{Q_1}\right)^{0.6}
$$

$$
C_2^* = 250 \times \left(\frac{80\,000}{50\,000}\right)^{0.6}
$$

$$
C_2^* = 250 \times \left(1.6\right)^{0.6}
$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate $(1.6)^{0.6}$.**

Take natural log:

$$
\ln(1.6^{0.6}) = 0.6 \cdot \ln(1.6) = 0.6 \times 0.4700 = 0.2820
$$

Exponentiate:

$$
e^{0.2820} = 1.3258
$$

Therefore:

$$
C_2^* = 250 \times 1.3258 = 331.45 \text{ cr}
$$

**Step 4 — Escalate to 2024 using cost index.**

$$
C_2 = C_2^* \times \frac{I_2}{I_1} = 331.45 \times \frac{820}{550}
$$

$$
C_2 = 331.45 \times 1.4909
$$

$$
\boxed{C_2 \approx 494.10 \text{ crore (₹ 494.1 cr)}}
$$

**Step 5 — State assumptions clearly (for KTU marks).**

* Both plants are *fluid-processing* type (ammonia).
* Same process technology (steam-methane reforming).
* Same geographic location (no $L_2/L_1$ factor needed).
* Currency value of rupee assumed unchanged.
* No major change in scope or labour productivity.

---

## 3.3 — Worked Example 2: Lang Factor Method

> **Question:** The total delivered cost of major process equipment for a chlor-alkali plant is **₹ 80 crore**. Estimate the Fixed Capital Investment. The plant is a fluid-processing type.

**Step 1 — Choose the correct Lang factor.**

For a fluid-processing plant (chlor-alkali = electrolysis of brine ⇒ fluid handling), $f_L = 4.74$.

**Step 2 — Apply the formula.**

$$
\text{FCI} = f_L \cdot \sum C_{\text{eq}} = 4.74 \times 80
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{FCI} = 379.2 \text{ crore}}
$$

**Step 3 — Add Working Capital (assume 10 % of FCI rule).**

$$
\text{WC} = 0.10 \times 379.2 = 37.92 \text{ cr}
$$

**Step 4 — Total Capital Investment.**

$$
\text{TCI} = \text{FCI} + \text{WC} = 379.2 + 37.92
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{TCI} = 417.12 \text{ crore}}
$$

---

## 3.4 — Worked Example 3: Hand's Bare Module Method

> **Question:** Estimate the Fixed Capital Investment for a section of a plant using a single centrifugal pump of bare-equipment cost **₹ 12 lakh**.

**Step 1 — Pick the bare-module factor.**

For a *centrifugal pump*: $f_{\text{BM}} = 3.3$.

**Step 2 — Compute FCI.**

$$
\text{FCI} = f_{\text{BM}} \cdot C_{\text{BE}} = 3.3 \times 12
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{FCI} = 39.6 \text{ lakh} = ₹ 0.396 \text{ crore}}
$$

> Note: this is only the FCI *attributable* to the pump (pump + driver + baseplate + piping stub + controls + installation labour). It is *not* the plant FCI.

---

## 3.5 — Analytical Comparison Matrix: When to Use Which Method

| Method | Accuracy | Data Required | Best Used When |
|---|---|---|---|
| Six-Tenths Rule | ± 30 % | Capacity of reference plant + its cost | **Similar plant exists in past** |
| Lang Factor | ± 35 % | Total delivered equipment cost | Equipment list ready, plant type known |
| Hand Bare-Module | ± 25 % | Cost of each piece of equipment | Equipment list partly known |
| Step-Count | ± 50 % | Number of process units | **Nothing else is available** |
| Cost-Index Escalation | n/a | Cost index series | Past quote to be updated |

---

## 3.6 — Sensitivity Analysis (KTU Favourite 7-mark Question)

> **Question:** What is the % change in estimated cost if the capacity ratio is off by 10 %?

Sensitivity derivation:

$$
\frac{dC_2}{C_2} = 0.6 \cdot \frac{dQ_2}{Q_2}
$$

So a **10 % error in capacity** translates to only a **6 % error in cost**. This is the *risk-dampening* nature of the 0.6 exponent — one of the most-quoted properties of preliminary estimates.

$$
\text{Sensitivity factor} = \frac{\%\Delta \text{Cost}}{\%\Delta \text{Capacity}} = 0.6
$$

# 📝 5 — KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

## PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

### **Q1. Define a preliminary estimate. Mention its accuracy and purpose.** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

A **preliminary estimate** is an early-stage assessment of the capital cost of a chemical plant prepared *before* detailed engineering data (equipment specs, P&IDs) are available.

| Attribute | Value |
|---|---|
| Accuracy | **± 30 % to ± 50 %** |
| Engineering effort | 0 – 2 % of total |
| Purpose | Go / No-Go decision, screening alternatives, seed-fund raising |

(3 marks)

---

### **Q2. State the Six-Tenths Rule and explain why the exponent is 0.6.** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

$$
C_2 = C_1 \left(\frac{Q_2}{Q_1}\right)^{0.6}
$$

**Justification of 0.6:**
For a *single cylindrical vessel*, the equipment surface area (≈ cost) scales with the **2/3 power** of its volume (capacity). Statistical regression of historical chemical plant data (where many types of equipment are mixed) yields an empirical average exponent of **0.6**. The exponent < 1 reflects the **economies of scale** — larger plants are *cheaper per unit capacity*.

(3 marks)

---

## PART B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks each) — INTERNAL CHOICE

### ✦ **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`

**(a)** A 40 000 tonne/year sulphuric acid plant cost **₹ 180 crore** in **2019**. Using the Six-Tenths Rule, estimate the cost of a similar **75 000 tonne/year** plant in **2024**. The CEPCI values are: 2019 → **600**, 2024 → **820**. Take the location factor as unity. **(7 marks)**

**Step 1 — List the variables.**

$$
Q_1 = 40\,000,\quad C_1 = 180\text{ cr},\quad Q_2 = 75\,000,\quad I_1 = 600,\quad I_2 = 820
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the basic Six-Tenths Rule.**

$$
C_2^* = 180 \times \left(\frac{75\,000}{40\,000}\right)^{0.6} = 180 \times (1.875)^{0.6}
$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate $(1.875)^{0.6}$.**

$$
\ln(1.875^{0.6}) = 0.6 \times \ln(1.875) = 0.6 \times 0.6286 = 0.3772
$$

$$
e^{0.3772} = 1.4583
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute.**

$$
C_2^* = 180 \times 1.4583 = 262.49 \text{ cr}
$$

**Step 5 — Escalate for cost index.**

$$
C_2 = 262.49 \times \frac{820}{600} = 262.49 \times 1.3667
$$

$$
\boxed{C_2 \approx 358.75 \text{ crore}}
$$

**(b)** Explain **Lang's method** of preliminary estimation. A fluid-processing plant has total delivered equipment cost of **₹ 95 crore**. Estimate the Fixed Capital Investment and Total Capital Investment (assuming WC = 10 % of FCI). **(7 marks)**

**Explanation of Lang's Method (3 marks):**
Lang's method is a preliminary estimation technique where the **Fixed Capital Investment (FCI)** is obtained as a multiple — the **Lang factor $f_L$** — of the total cost of major process equipment delivered to site. Different Lang factors are used for solid, fluid, and mixed plants.

**Numerical solution (4 marks):**

For fluid-processing plant, $f_L = 4.74$.

$$
\text{FCI} = 4.74 \times 95 = 450.3 \text{ cr}
$$

$$
\text{WC} = 0.10 \times 450.3 = 45.03 \text{ cr}
$$

$$
\text{TCI} = \text{FCI} + \text{WC} = 450.3 + 45.03
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{TCI} = 495.33 \text{ crore}}
$$

---

### ✦ **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**(a)** List and briefly explain **any five methods** of preliminary estimation of capital cost. Compare them on the basis of accuracy and data requirement. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer (any five of these six):**

1. **Six-Tenths Rule (Power Law)** — Uses reference plant cost and capacity. Accuracy ± 30 %. Needs only two data points.
2. **Lang Factor Method** — Multiplier on total equipment cost. Accuracy ± 35 %. Needs complete equipment list with total cost.
3. **Hand's Bare-Module Method** — Per-equipment multiplier. Accuracy ± 25 %. Needs item-wise equipment cost.
4. **Step-Counting Method** — Cost per process step. Accuracy ± 50 %. Only process block diagram required.
5. **Cost-Index Escalation** — Updates a past quote using cost index. Accuracy depends on reference accuracy.
6. **Turnover Ratio Method** — Ratios of total capital to annual sales. Used for *commercial* projects with known revenue.

(7 marks for listing + comparing in a small table.)

**(b)** A new methanol plant (fluid-processing type) is being designed. The major equipment costs are: Compressor ₹ 18 cr, Distillation column ₹ 12 cr, Heat exchangers ₹ 8 cr, Pumps ₹ 5 cr, Tanks & vessels ₹ 10 cr. Using Lang factors, estimate the FCI and TCI (WC = 12 % of FCI). **(7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Compute total delivered equipment cost.**

$$
\sum C_{\text{eq}} = 18 + 12 + 8 + 5 + 10 = 53 \text{ cr}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply Lang factor for fluid-processing.**

$f_L = 4.74$.

$$
\text{FCI} = 4.74 \times 53 = 251.22 \text{ cr}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Working Capital.**

$$
\text{WC} = 0.12 \times 251.22 = 30.15 \text{ cr}
$$

**Step 4 — TCI.**

$$
\text{TCI} = 251.22 + 30.15
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{TCI} = 281.37 \text{ crore}}
$$

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall #1 — Forgetting to escalate for cost index.**
> Many students compute the capacity-scaling correctly but *omit* the $I_2/I_1$ factor when the question explicitly mentions the years. **Lose 2 marks instantly.**
>
> **Pitfall #2 — Wrong Lang factor.**
> Solid vs. Fluid vs. Mixed — each has a *different* $f_L$. Do not memorise only "4.74". Memorise all three (3.10, 3.63, 4.74).
>
> **Pitfall #3 — Not stating assumptions.**
> KTU valuation keys deduct marks if the student does not list assumptions (same process, same location, same currency, etc.).
>
> **Pitfall #4 — Wrong units of capacity.**
> If the reference plant is in **lb/yr** and the new plant is in **tonne/yr**, you MUST convert before substituting. The ratio must be **dimensionless**.
>
> **Pitfall #5 — Treating preliminary estimate as a definitive cost.**
> Never write "the cost of the plant is ₹ X cr" — always write "the **preliminary estimate** is ₹ X cr, with an expected accuracy of ± 30 %".

---

## 🧠 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Preliminary Estimate**

* **Definition:** Preliminary estimate = order-of-magnitude / Class 5 cost estimate prepared at feasibility stage; accuracy **± 30 – 50 %**.
* **Six-Tenths Rule:** $C_2 = C_1 (Q_2/Q_1)^{0.6}$ — most-frequently tested formula. The exponent **0.6** arises from geometric scaling of vessels (2/3 ≈ 0.6).
* **Adjusted Six-Tenths Rule:** Include cost-index ratio $(I_2/I_1)$ and location factor $(L_2/L_1)$ for time and place adjustment.
* **Lang Factors:** Solid = **3.10**, Mixed = **3.63**, Fluid = **4.74**.
* **Hand's Bare-Module Factors:** Per-equipment multipliers (pumps ≈ 3.3, exchangers ≈ 3.1, columns ≈ 4.1, compressors ≈ 2.5).
* **Step-Count Method:** ₹ 8 – 15 cr per major step; least accurate; only for sanity-check.
* **Working Capital:** 10 – 15 % of annual sales OR ~10 % of FCI for bulk chemicals.
* **Grand Equation:** $\text{TCI} = \text{FCI} + \text{WC}$; $\text{FCI} = \text{ISBL} + \text{OSBL}$.
* **Sensitivity:** A 10 % error in capacity gives only a **6 %** error in cost (because of the 0.6 exponent).
* **Always mention assumptions:** Same process, same location, same currency, scope unchanged.
* **Remember to state the accuracy band** explicitly in your final answer — examiners reward it.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Definitive Estimate
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT757 - ECONOMICS AND MANAGEMENT OF CHEMICAL INDUSTRIES
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Definitive Estimate

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)

A **Definitive Estimate** (also referred to as a **Class III Estimate** as per the *AACE International Recommended Practices*) is a **detailed, near-final, project-control-quality cost estimate** prepared for a proposed chemical plant or process facility when the engineering is sufficiently advanced that **Process Flow Diagrams (PFDs), Piping & Instrumentation Diagrams (P&IDs), final equipment specifications, complete material take-offs (MTOs), and firm vendor quotations** are all available.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme framework, a definitive estimate is used for:
- **Budget approval and appropriation of capital funds** by the corporate board.
- **Bid evaluation and contractor negotiation** for EPC (Engineering, Procurement, and Construction) projects.
- **Project cost control baseline** during the detailed engineering phase.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The accuracy range of a Definitive Estimate is typically **±10% to ±15%**, distinguishing it from less refined estimates (Order of Magnitude / Factored) and more refined ones (Detailed / Check Estimate). This accuracy is a *direct KTU examination benchmark* expected in viva and theory questions.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Intuition

Imagine you are planning to build a **custom-designed three-bedroom house in Kerala**. 

- When you first *think* about building it, you say "around ₹50 lakh" — this is the **Order of Magnitude Estimate**.
- When you consult an architect for a *concept sketch* and apply a per-square-foot rate, you get a **Factored/Conceptual Estimate** (~±30%).
- When the architect finalizes the **floor plan, electrical layout, plumbing layout, and you receive actual contractor quotes for cement, steel, and fittings**, you can now say with high confidence: "This will cost **₹62.4 lakh ± 10%**." This is exactly what a **Definitive Estimate** is in the chemical plant world.

The "definitive" in the name signifies that the engineering definition of the project is now **firm** — the project is no longer a "ballpark" idea; it is a precisely scoped entity ready for actual money commitment.

---

## 1.3 The Six Major Classes of Estimates (Reference Framework)

The KTU 2024 module strongly expects students to position the *Definitive Estimate* within the broader classification. Below is the internationally accepted **AACE classification**, which is the global standard referenced by KTU:

> [!NOTE]
> **Industry Standard Reference:** The classifications follow the cost-estimating taxonomy used by **AACE International** (Association for the Advancement of Cost Engineering).

| Class | Common Name | Typical Accuracy | Purpose |
|:-----:|:-----------|:----------------:|:-------|
| Class V | Order of Magnitude | –30% to +50% | Feasibility screening |
| Class IV | Conceptual / Factored | –30% to +50% | Pre-feasibility / Budget |
| **Class III** | **Definitive** | **–20% to +30%** | **Funding / Bid Control** |
| Class II | Detailed | –15% to +20% | Tender / Construction |
| Class I | Final / Check | –10% to +15% | Final accounts / Audit |

**Key Insight:** Notice that a Class III Definitive Estimate narrows the upper bound to **+30%** — significantly tighter than the ±50% of conceptual estimates — because the underlying engineering data is now actual rather than factored.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Prerequisites (Engineering Data Maturity Required)

A Definitive Estimate can *only* be prepared when the following engineering deliverables are **mature and frozen**:

1. **Process Design Package** — finalized mass and energy balances.
2. **P&IDs (Piping & Instrumentation Diagrams)** — fully issued for construction (IFC).
3. **Equipment List & Datasheets** — final specifications with vendor quotations or in-house database pricing.
4. **Plot Plan & General Arrangement Drawings** — finalized.
5. **Site Survey & Geotechnical Data** — available.
6. **Material Take-Offs (MTOs)** — based on finalized piping isometric drawings.
7. **Project Execution Schedule** — time-based resource plan available.
8. **Subcontractor Inquiries** — at least budget quotations received.

If any of the above is *preliminary*, the estimate must be downgraded to a Class IV (Conceptual) category. This is a **frequently tested KTU concept**.

---

## 2.2 Structural Components of the Definitive Estimate

A definitive estimate is built by aggregating the following cost categories. Each category must be *quantified separately* — KTU board examiners specifically look for this decomposition in Part B answers.

### A. Direct Costs (DC)
Direct costs are expenditures *physically* traceable to the construction of the plant.

1. **Equipment Cost (Purchased + Installation Labor + Freight)**
2. **Piping Cost** — function of piping MTO weight and material grade.
3. **Electrical Cost** — motors, transformers, cabling, MCCs.
4. **Instrumentation Cost** — control valves, transmitters, DCS hardware.
5. **Civil & Structural Cost** — foundations, buildings, structural steel.
6. **Insulation & Painting Cost**
7. **Labor Cost (Direct Field Labor)**

### B. Indirect Costs (IC)
Indirect costs are *non-physical* expenditures necessary for project execution but not directly traceable to a physical item.

1. Engineering & Design (E&D) cost
2. Construction management & supervision
3. Field office expenses and temporary facilities
4. Taxes, duties, and insurance
5. Commissioning and start-up assistance
6. Contingency (typically **8–12%** of DC+IC for definitive estimates)

### C. Owner's Costs (OC)
1. Land acquisition
2. Pre-production (training, initial feedstocks)
3. Working capital
4. Royalties and licenses

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical KTU Examination Note:** Mastery of the formulae below is mandatory for any 14-mark Part B question on cost estimation.

| Formula | Equation | Symbol Meaning | Typical Value/Range |
|:--------|:---------|:---------------|:--------------------|
| **Fixed Capital Investment (FCI)** | $\text{FCI} = \text{DC} + \text{IC}$ | DC = Direct Costs; IC = Indirect Costs | Determined item-wise |
| **Working Capital** | $C_W = C_{WS} + C_{WI}$ | C_WS = Solids WC; C_WI = Inventory WC | 10–20% of TCI |
| **Total Capital Investment (TCI)** | $\text{TCI} = \text{FCI} + C_W$ | Sum of fixed + working capital | Final capital figure |
| **Total Product Cost** | $C_T = C_R + C_{RM} + C_{UT} + C_{WL} + C_{OL}$ | Manufacturing + General Expenses | Annual basis |
| **Lang Factor (Conceptual only)** | $C_{TM} = F_{Lang} \sum C_E$ | F_Lang depends on plant type | 3.10 (solid) / 4.74 (fluid) |
| **Hand Factor (better for Definit.)** | $C_{TM} = F_{Hand} \sum C_E$ | F_Hand includes piping factors | 3.30 / 3.93 / 4.83 |
| **Equipment Cost Inflation** | $C_2 = C_1 \left(\dfrac{I_2}{I_1}\right)$ | C_1 = old cost; I = cost index | CEPCI, Marshall–Swift |
| **Piping Cost Ratio (Def. Est.)** | $C_{pi} \approx 0.30 \text{ to } 0.60 \cdot C_E$ | As fraction of equipment cost | Fluid process → higher |
| **Contingency (Def. Est.)** | $C_{cont} = 0.08 \cdot \text{DC}$ to $0.12 \cdot \text{DC}$ | Safety allowance | Lower than conceptual |

---

## 2.4 The "Why" Behind Each Component — Engineering Reasoning

- **Why separate Direct from Indirect costs?** Direct costs scale linearly with plant capacity (a 2× plant = 2× piping), while indirect costs are *sub-linear*. Treating them separately avoids gross estimation error.
- **Why is piping such a large fraction (30–60%) in chemical plants?** Unlike refineries or power plants, chemical plants handle **corrosive, high-purity, multi-phase streams** requiring exotic alloys (SS-316, Hastelloy, Monel), elevated safety standards, and complex instrumentation. KTU examiners love piping-related questions.
- **Why contingency is lower (8–12%) than conceptual (20–30%)?** Because engineering is now *frozen*, the *risk of design error* drops sharply. This risk-based logic is a top-tier answer point.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Utility

- **Tata Consulting Engineers, L&T Hydrocarbon, Engineers India Ltd (EIL)** — all use Class III Definitive Estimates before sanctioning client projects worth hundreds of crores.
- **RIL (Reliance Industries)** — Jamnagar Refinery expansion projects used definitive estimates to secure board-level investment decisions.
- **BPCL Kochi Refinery** — petrochemical complex expansions follow identical protocols.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Step-by-Step Procedure to Prepare a Definitive Estimate

This is the *KTU board examiner's expected answer outline* for a 14-mark question. Each step carries weightage.

### Step 1 — Engineering Data Freeze
The estimator waits until **all process engineering, mechanical design, electrical design, instrumentation, and civil design packages** are issued *For Construction* (IFC). Any deviation later invalidates the estimate.

### Step 2 — Equipment Cost Determination
Equipment cost is built from:
- **Vendor quotations** (preferred — at least 3 competitive bids analyzed).
- **In-house cost database** (if vendor quote delayed — escalated using cost indices).

The total equipment purchase cost $C_{E,total}$ is:

$$
C_{E,total} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} C_{E,i}
$$

where $C_{E,i}$ is the cost of the $i^{th}$ equipment item (vessels, heat exchangers, pumps, compressors, columns, etc.).

### Step 3 — Cost Index Escalation (Time Adjustment)
If a vendor quote is from a prior year, adjust it to the present using a cost index:

$$
C_{E,present} = C_{E,past} \times \left(\dfrac{I_{present}}{I_{past}}\right)
$$

Where $I$ is typically the **Chemical Engineering Plant Cost Index (CEPCI)** or **Marshall & Swift (M&S) index**.

### Step 4 — Material Take-Offs (MTO) for Piping, Civil, Electrical
From finalized isometric drawings and cable schedules:

$$
M_{pi} = \sum_{j=1}^{m} \rho_j \cdot L_j \cdot (1 + F_{waste})
$$

where:
- $\rho_j$ = linear density (kg/m) of pipe segment $j$
- $L_j$ = length of segment $j$
- $F_{waste}$ = wastage factor (typically **5–10%**)

### Step 5 — Direct Cost Aggregation

$$
\text{DC} = C_{E,total} + C_{pi} + C_{el} + C_{inst} + C_{civ} + C_{ins} + C_{lab}
$$

Each sub-cost is computed from MTO quantities × unit rate × installation factor.

### Step 6 — Indirect Cost Estimation
Indirect costs are commonly estimated as a percentage of direct costs, refined by historical project data:

$$
\text{IC} = (f_{E\&D} + f_{cm} + f_{field} + f_{tax}) \cdot \text{DC}
$$

For definitive estimates, $f_{E\&D}$ (engineering & design) is typically **15–25%**, and construction management is **8–12%**.

### Step 7 — Contingency Addition
Contingency for a definitive estimate:

$$
C_{cont} = k \cdot (\text{DC} + \text{IC}), \quad \text{with } k \in [0.08,\, 0.12]
$$

### Step 8 — Working Capital Addition

$$
C_W = 0.10 \cdot \text{TCI} \quad \text{to} \quad 0.20 \cdot \text{TCI}
$$

### Step 9 — Final Total Capital Investment

$$
\boxed{\text{TCI} = \text{DC} + \text{IC} + C_{cont} + C_W + C_{owner}}
$$

### Step 10 — Risk-Based Validation
The estimate is stress-tested against ±10% sensitivity on major equipment cost and ±15% on bulk materials to confirm robustness.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Estimating Piping Cost (Most-Tested KTU Sub-Topic)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU examiners frequently set numerical sub-parts (a) on piping cost estimation for 7 marks. Below is the canonical breakdown.

**Piping cost components:**
1. **Pipe material cost** $C_{pm}$
2. **Fittings cost** $C_{pf}$ — typically 50–65% of $C_{pm}$
3. **Valves cost** $C_{pv}$ — typically 25–35% of $C_{pm}$
4. **Supports & hangers** — ~10–15% of $C_{pm}$
5. **Fabrication labor** $C_{pl}$ — per metre rates based on diameter and alloy
6. **Erection labor** — typically 30–50% of fabrication labor
7. **Field testing, flushing, painting** — ~5–10% of total piping cost

$$
C_{pi,total} = (C_{pm} + C_{pf} + C_{pv} + C_{sup}) \cdot (1 + F_{lab}) \cdot (1 + F_{ins} + F_{paint})
$$

**Example Boundary Conditions (KTU Reference):**
- **Carbon steel piping:** $C_{pi} \approx 0.30 \cdot C_E$ (30% of equipment cost)
- **Stainless steel piping:** $C_{pi} \approx 0.45 \cdot C_E$ (45% of equipment cost)
- **Exotic alloy piping (Hastelloy/Titanium):** $C_{pi} \approx 0.60 \cdot C_E$ (60% of equipment cost)

This single table is worth **5–7 marks** in any Part B question.

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Pattern Problem)

> **Problem:** A chemical plant has the following equipment costs (in ₹ Lakhs): Vessels = 80, Heat Exchangers = 120, Pumps = 45, Compressors = 150, Columns = 95, Tanks = 35.
>
> Calculate the **Fixed Capital Investment (FCI)** and **Total Capital Investment (TCI)** assuming:
> - Piping cost = 40% of equipment cost
> - Instrumentation cost = 15% of equipment cost
> - Electrical cost = 12% of equipment cost
> - Civil/Structural = 18% of equipment cost
> - Insulation & Painting = 8% of equipment cost
> - Labor = 20% of equipment cost
> - Indirect costs = 25% of Direct Costs
> - Contingency = 10% of (DC+IC)
> - Working Capital = 15% of TCI (iterative)

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** Total Equipment Cost

$$
C_E = 80 + 120 + 45 + 150 + 95 + 35 = 525 \text{ ₹ Lakhs}
$$

**Step 2:** Compute Direct Costs (DC)

$$
C_{pi} = 0.40 \times 525 = 210
$$

$$
C_{inst} = 0.15 \times 525 = 78.75
$$

$$
C_{el} = 0.12 \times 525 = 63.00
$$

$$
C_{civ} = 0.18 \times 525 = 94.50
$$

$$
C_{ins} = 0.08 \times 525 = 42.00
$$

$$
C_{lab} = 0.20 \times 525 = 105.00
$$

$$
\text{DC} = 525 + 210 + 78.75 + 63 + 94.50 + 42 + 105 = 1118.25 \text{ ₹ Lakhs}
$$

**Step 3:** Indirect Costs

$$
\text{IC} = 0.25 \times 1118.25 = 279.5625 \text{ ₹ Lakhs}
$$

**Step 4:** Contingency

$$
C_{cont} = 0.10 \times (1118.25 + 279.56) = 139.781 \text{ ₹ Lakhs}
$$

**Step 5:** Working Capital (Iterative)

Let $\text{TCI} = x$. Then $C_W = 0.15 x$, and:

$$
x = \text{DC} + \text{IC} + C_{cont} + C_W = 1118.25 + 279.56 + 139.78 + 0.15 x
$$

$$
x - 0.15 x = 1537.59
$$

$$
0.85 x = 1537.59
$$

$$
x = 1808.93 \text{ ₹ Lakhs}
$$

**Step 6:** Working Capital Value

$$
C_W = 0.15 \times 1808.93 = 271.34 \text{ ₹ Lakhs}
$$

### Final Results:

$$
\boxed{\text{FCI} = 1118.25 + 279.56 + 139.78 = 1537.59 \text{ ₹ Lakhs}}
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{TCI} \approx 1808.93 \text{ ₹ Lakhs}}
$$

This iterative method is a **KTU-favorite 14-mark calculation question**.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: The Definitive Estimate Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Project Initiation] --> B[Engineering Design Maturity Check]
    B --> C{All IFC Deliverables Ready?}
    C -->|No| D[Downgrade to Class IV Conceptual Estimate]
    C -->|Yes| E[Vendor Quotation Collection - 3 Bids]
    E --> F[Cost Index Escalation if Required]
    F --> G[Equipment Cost Aggregation]
    G --> H[Material Take-Off from Isometrics]
    H --> I[Bulk Cost Build-up: Piping / Electrical / Civil / Inst]
    I --> J[Direct Cost Sum DC]
    J --> K[Indirect Cost Sum IC via Percentages]
    K --> L[Subtotal DC plus IC]
    L --> M[Add Contingency 8 to 12 percent]
    M --> N[Add Owner Costs Land Licenses]
    N --> O[Calculate Working Capital Iterative]
    O --> P[Final Total Capital Investment TCI]
    P --> Q[Sensitivity Analysis plus 10 to minus 10]
    Q --> R[Definitive Estimate Report Issued]
    R --> S[Board Approval and Fund Appropriation]
```

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Cost Component Hierarchy (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph CAP[Capital Investment Structure]
        direction TB
        FCI[Fixed Capital Investment FCI]
        WC[Working Capital CW]
    end

    subgraph FCI_DETAIL[FCI Components]
        direction TB
        DC1[Direct Costs DC]
        IC1[Indirect Costs IC]
        CT[Contingency]
    end

    subgraph DC_BREAKDOWN[DC Internal Structure]
        direction TB
        EQ[Equipment Cost CE]
        PI[Piping Cost Cpi]
        EL[Electrical Cost Cel]
        IT[Instrumentation Cost Cin]
        CV[Civil and Structural Cost Cciv]
        IN[Insulation and Painting]
        LB[Direct Field Labor]
    end

    FCI --> DC1
    FCI --> IC1
    FCI --> CT
    DC1 --> EQ
    DC1 --> PI
    DC1 --> EL
    DC1 --> IT
    DC1 --> CV
    DC1 --> IN
    DC1 --> LB

    CAP --> TCI_OUT[Total Capital Investment TCI]
    FCI --> TCI_OUT
    WC --> TCI_OUT
```

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Estimate Class Comparison Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Cost Estimation Trigger] --> Q1{Design Data Available?}
    Q1 -->|No Conceptual Only| C5[Class V Order of Magnitude plus 50 minus 30]
    Q1 -->|Preliminary Process Flow| C4[Class IV Conceptual or Factored plus 50 minus 30]
    Q1 -->|Complete P and IDs and Equipment| C3[Class III Definitive Estimate plus 30 minus 20]
    Q1 -->|Construction Drawings Ready| C2[Class II Detailed Estimate plus 20 minus 15]
    Q1 -->|As Built Complete| C1[Class I Check Estimate plus 15 minus 10]

    C3 --> Use1[Used for Board Approval and Bid Evaluation]
    C2 --> Use2[Used for Tender and Construction Control]
    C1 --> Use3[Used for Final Accounts and Audit]
```

---

## 4.4 Diagram: Accuracy vs Project Phase (Conceptual Plot)

```mermaid
xychart-beta
    title "Estimate Accuracy vs Project Phase"
    x-axis "Project Phase" [Concept, FEED, Detailed, Construction, As-Built]
    y-axis "Accuracy Range (%)" 0 to 60
    line [50, 40, 25, 18, 12]
```

> **Reading the diagram:** As the project moves from Concept → As-Built, the *width of the accuracy range* narrows, indicating higher cost prediction confidence. The **Definitive Estimate** sits in the **FEED → Detailed Design transition zone** with an accuracy band of roughly ±20–30%.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. Define a Definitive Estimate and state its expected accuracy range.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

A Definitive Estimate is a detailed, project-control-quality cost estimate prepared for a chemical plant when the engineering design has reached sufficient maturity — specifically, when **P&IDs are finalized, equipment specifications are firm, material take-offs are available, and vendor quotations have been received**.

The expected accuracy range is **±20% to +30%** as per AACE Class III classification.

> It is used for **fund appropriation, bid evaluation, and budget control** during the EPC execution phase.

*(3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for accuracy range, 1 for application)*

---

### **Q2. Why is contingency provision lower (8–12%) in a Definitive Estimate compared to a Conceptual Estimate (25–30%)?**
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

Contingency is the financial allowance for *unknown-unknowns* — design errors, scope changes, price escalations, and unforeseen site conditions. In a Definitive Estimate:

1. **Design maturity** is high — P&IDs and specs are frozen, eliminating *most* design uncertainty.
2. **Vendor quotations** are firm, removing major *price uncertainty*.
3. **Site investigations** are complete, removing *geotechnical uncertainty*.

Hence the residual risk is small, justifying a contingency of only **8–12%** versus the 25–30% required for conceptual estimates where design data is preliminary.

*(3 marks: 1 for contingency purpose, 1 for risk reduction, 1 for numerical comparison)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Internal Choice Mandate (KTU 2024):** Module 2 Part B questions must offer two alternatives. Both are presented below with full model solutions.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**
`[KTU University Exam - May 2024 - Modified]`

**(a)** List the **prerequisites (engineering data)** required before preparing a Definitive Estimate for a chemical plant. Explain why each is essential. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **step-by-step procedure** for preparing a Definitive Estimate, clearly distinguishing between Direct Costs, Indirect Costs, and Contingency. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution — Question A**

### (a) Prerequisites for a Definitive Estimate

1. **Process Flow Diagrams (PFDs) & P&IDs (Final IFC Issued)** — Defines the entire process topology, equipment interconnections, and control philosophy. Without these, MTOs cannot be generated.

2. **Finalized Equipment List & Datasheets** — Lists every vessel, heat exchanger, pump, compressor, and column with design pressure, temperature, material of construction, and capacity. Forms the basis of equipment cost.

3. **Equipment Vendor Quotations** — At least 3 competitive bids per major equipment item to capture realistic market prices.

4. **Piping Isometrics & Line List** — Used to compute pipe lengths, fitting counts, and valve schedules → piping cost.

5. **Electrical & Instrumentation Drawings** — Cable schedules, MCC layout, instrument index → electrical and instrumentation cost.

6. **Civil & Structural Drawings** — Foundation plans, building layouts, structural framing → civil/structural cost.

7. **Site Survey & Soil Investigation Report** — Defines foundation type (pile vs raft), affecting civil cost significantly.

8. **Project Schedule & Execution Plan** — Determines labor mobilization cost and time-related indirect expenses.

9. **Plot Plan & General Arrangement Drawings** — Defines piping run lengths and structural steel quantities.

10. **Codes & Standards** — IS/ASME/TEMA specifications affect material selection and cost.

*(Each prerequisite carries ~0.7 marks; cite the engineering "why" to score full marks.)*

### (b) Step-by-Step Procedure for a Definitive Estimate

**Step 1:** Receive IFC engineering package.

**Step 2:** Equipment Cost Aggregation — Sum all purchased equipment costs using vendor quotes escalated to present-day via cost indices.

**Step 3:** Material Take-Off (MTO) — Extract quantities from isometrics and schedules.

**Step 4:** Bulk Cost Build-up — Compute:
- Piping Cost (30–60% of $C_E$ depending on alloy)
- Electrical Cost (10–15% of $C_E$)
- Instrumentation Cost (10–20% of $C_E$)
- Civil/Structural Cost (15–25% of $C_E$)
- Insulation & Painting Cost (5–10% of $C_E$)

**Step 5:** Compute Direct Labor Cost from crew productivity norms.

**Step 6:** Sum Direct Costs: $\text{DC} = C_E + C_{pi} + C_{el} + C_{in} + C_{civ} + C_{ins} + C_{lab}$

**Step 7:** Compute Indirect Costs as % of DC (typically 20–35%):
- Engineering & Design: 15–25%
- Construction Management: 8–12%
- Field Office & Temporary Facilities: 3–5%

**Step 8:** Add Contingency: $C_{cont} = 0.08 \text{ to } 0.12 \times (\text{DC}+\text{IC})$

**Step 9:** Compute Fixed Capital Investment: $\text{FCI} = \text{DC} + \text{IC} + C_{cont}$

**Step 10:** Add Working Capital (iteratively): $C_W = 0.10 \text{ to } 0.20 \times \text{TCI}$

**Step 11:** Final Total Capital Investment: $\text{TCI} = \text{FCI} + C_W$

**Step 12:** Sensitivity analysis (±10% on major cost drivers).

*(Each step carries ~0.6 marks. Use formulas and explicit cost ranges to score full 7 marks.)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 - Modified]`

**(a)** With the help of a suitable **numerical example**, explain the **iterative method of computing Total Capital Investment (TCI)** that includes Working Capital as a fraction of TCI. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A chemical plant has the following **equipment costs** (in ₹ Lakhs):

> Reactors = 120, Distillation Columns = 180, Heat Exchangers = 95, Pumps = 40, Compressors = 160, Storage Tanks = 50.

Assume:
- Piping = 45% of $C_E$, Instrumentation = 18% of $C_E$, Electrical = 12% of $C_E$, Civil = 20% of $C_E$, Insulation = 8% of $C_E$, Labor = 22% of $C_E$.
- Indirect Costs = 28% of DC, Contingency = 10% of (DC+IC), Working Capital = 15% of TCI.

**Calculate:** (i) FCI and (ii) TCI. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution — Question B**

### (a) The Iterative Method (Conceptual Explanation)

When **Working Capital ($C_W$)** is defined as a *fraction of Total Capital Investment (TCI)*, the equation becomes circular because TCI itself contains $C_W$.

**Starting Equation:**

$$
\text{TCI} = \text{FCI} + C_W = \text{FCI} + f \cdot \text{TCI}
$$

**Rearrangement:**

$$
\text{TCI} (1 - f) = \text{FCI}
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{TCI} = \dfrac{\text{FCI}}{1 - f}}
$$

where $f$ is the working-capital fraction (typically 0.10–0.20).

**Numerical Illustration:** If FCI = ₹1000 Lakhs and $f = 0.15$:

$$
\text{TCI} = \dfrac{1000}{1 - 0.15} = \dfrac{1000}{0.85} = 1176.47 \text{ ₹ Lakhs}
$$

$$
C_W = 0.15 \times 1176.47 = 176.47 \text{ ₹ Lakhs}
$$

This iterative (or closed-form) method ensures *internal consistency* of the estimate.

### (b) Numerical Calculation

**Step 1:** Equipment Cost

$$
C_E = 120 + 180 + 95 + 40 + 160 + 50 = 645 \text{ ₹ Lakhs}
$$

**Step 2:** Direct Cost Sub-Items

$$
C_{pi} = 0.45 \times 645 = 290.25
$$

$$
C_{in} = 0.18 \times 645 = 116.10
$$

$$
C_{el} = 0.12 \times 645 = 77.40
$$

$$
C_{civ} = 0.20 \times 645 = 129.00
$$

$$
C_{ins} = 0.08 \times 645 = 51.60
$$

$$
C_{lab} = 0.22 \times 645 = 141.90
$$

$$
\text{DC} = 645 + 290.25 + 116.10 + 77.40 + 129 + 51.60 + 141.90 = 1451.25 \text{ ₹ Lakhs}
$$

**Step 3:** Indirect Costs

$$
\text{IC} = 0.28 \times 1451.25 = 406.35 \text{ ₹ Lakhs}
$$

**Step 4:** Contingency

$$
C_{cont} = 0.10 \times (1451.25 + 406.35) = 185.76 \text{ ₹ Lakhs}
$$

**Step 5:** Fixed Capital Investment

$$
\text{FCI} = 1451.25 + 406.35 + 185.76 = 2043.36 \text{ ₹ Lakhs}
$$

**Step 6:** Total Capital Investment (Iterative, $f = 0.15$)

$$
\text{TCI} = \dfrac{2043.36}{1 - 0.15} = \dfrac{2043.36}{0.85} = 2403.95 \text{ ₹ Lakhs}
$$

### Final Answers:
$$
\boxed{\text{FCI} \approx 2043.36 \text{ ₹ Lakhs}} \qquad \boxed{\text{TCI} \approx 2403.95 \text{ ₹ Lakhs}}
$$

*(Full 7 marks require explicit unit tracking, rounding discipline, and showing all intermediate steps.)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing Definitive Estimate with Detailed/Check Estimate.** Definitive = Class III (±20 to +30%); Detailed = Class II (±15 to +20%). Mixing these costs **2–3 marks** in definition questions.
> 2. **Forgetting to escalate vendor quotes** using cost indices when project spans multiple years — costs **1 mark** in numerical problems.
> 3. **Not iterating for Working Capital.** Students write TCI = FCI + 0.15·FCI, which is *wrong*. The correct formula is TCI = FCI/(1 − f). This mistake costs **3–4 marks** in 14-mark problems.
> 4. **Omitting Contingency or Owner Costs** in the FCI formula. Examiners specifically deduct for **incomplete cost decomposition**.
> 5. **Using Lang factor for definitive estimation.** The Lang factor is for **order-of-magnitude** estimates only. Using it for a definitive estimate loses **2 marks** for inappropriate methodology.
> 6. **Forgetting the role of exotic alloys.** Piping cost factor is **0.60** for Hastelloy/Titanium vs **0.30** for carbon steel — examiners test this nuance.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid-Revision Checklist:**

- **Definitive Estimate = AACE Class III = ±20% to +30% accuracy.**
- **Requires IFC engineering** — P&IDs, equipment specs, MTOs, vendor quotes.
- **Used for board approval, fund appropriation, bid evaluation.**
- **Piping is the largest direct cost in chemical plants** (30–60% of equipment cost).
- **Contingency = 8–12% of (DC+IC)** — much lower than conceptual estimate (25–30%).
- **Indirect Costs ≈ 20–35% of DC**, dominated by Engineering & Design (15–25%).
- **TCI Formula (Iterative):** $\text{TCI} = \dfrac{\text{FCI}}{1 - f_W}$ where $f_W \in [0.10,\, 0.20]$.
- **FCI = DC + IC + Contingency.**
- **Equipment cost escalation** uses CEPCI or Marshall & Swift indices.
- **Working capital covers** raw material inventory, product inventory, cash reserves, and accounts receivable.
- **Lang factor is NOT used** in definitive estimates; it is for Class V only.
- **Sensitivity analysis (±10%)** is the final validation step before issuing the estimate report.
- **Piping cost increases** with alloy grade: CS < SS < Hastelloy < Titanium.
- **Stainless steel / corrosive service** drives instrumentation cost higher (more control valves).
- **Civil cost is sensitive** to soil bearing capacity — pile foundations cost 2–3× raft foundations.
- **Definitive Estimate precedes Detailed (Class II) Estimate** in the project life cycle.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Detailed Estimate
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT757 - ECONOMICS AND MANAGEMENT OF CHEMICAL INDUSTRIES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Detailed Estimate

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Detailed Estimate (Capital Cost & Operating Cost Estimation):** A *Detailed Estimate* in the context of chemical process industries is a comprehensive, line-item, bottom-up financial projection that quantifies the **Fixed Capital Investment (FCI)**, **Working Capital (WC)**, and **Total Product Cost (TPC)** of a proposed chemical plant with an accuracy typically within **±10% to ±15%**. It is performed after process design is finalized and equipment specifications are known, serving as the definitive financial blueprint for engineering procurement, construction (EPC), and managerial decision-making.

The detailed estimate is the **pinnacle of the six-tenths rule, order-of-magnitude, and study estimate** hierarchy, and is required by lending institutions, regulatory authorities, and corporate boards before sanctioning project funds.

> [!NOTE]
> **Hierarchy of Estimates Accuracy (as per KTU 2024 PECHT757 syllabus):**
> - **Order-of-Magnitude (OM):** Accuracy ±40% (Class V)
> - **Study (Factored) Estimate:** Accuracy ±30% (Class IV)
> - **Preliminary (Budget Authorization) Estimate:** Accuracy ±20% (Class III)
> - **Definitive (Detailed) Estimate:** Accuracy ±10–15% (Class II)
> - **Detailed (Firm) Estimate:** Accuracy ±5–10% (Class I)

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Analogy: "Building Your Dream House"**
> Imagine you are building a 3-BHK house. The **Order-of-Magnitude estimate** is when you just say *"It will cost around ₹50 lakhs"* based on a similar house. The **Study Estimate** is when a contractor says *"Based on the floor area, materials and labour cost about ₹3,000/sq.ft."* But the **Detailed Estimate** is the final quotation from the contractor — itemized as:
> - Excavation & foundation: ₹2,50,000
> - Reinforced steel (12 tonnes @ ₹65,000): ₹7,80,000
> - Cement (200 bags @ ₹450): ₹90,000
> - Bricks, sand, tiles, plumbing, electrical, labour, taxes...
>
> Each item has a **vendor quotation, quantity takeoff, and rate analysis**. Sum them up → *detailed estimate*.
>
> In a **chemical plant**, this translates to: every pump, reactor, distillation column, heat exchanger, valve, pipe, instrument, and even the dirt pad it sits on is individually priced, escalated using cost indices, freighted, installed, and insured.

## 1.3 Scope of Detailed Estimate in Chemical Industries

The detailed estimate must explicitly quantify:

1. **Inside Battery Limits (ISBL):** Cost of process equipment, installation, piping, instrumentation, electrical, structural, insulation, and painting within the process block.
2. **Outside Battery Limits (OSBL):** Cost of utilities (boilers, cooling towers, compressors, water treatment), storage tanks, effluent treatment, fire-fighting, administrative buildings, and site development.
3. **Engineering, Procurement, and Construction Management (EPCM) costs.**
4. **Contingency allowance** (typically 8–15% of FCI for detailed estimates).
5. **Working Capital** (typically 10–20% of FCI).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall #1:** Students often confuse **Fixed Capital Investment (FCI)** with **Total Capital Investment (TCI)**. Always remember:
> $$\text{TCI} = \text{FCI} + \text{Working Capital}$$
> $$\text{TCI} = \text{Direct Costs} + \text{Indirect Costs} + \text{Contingency} + \text{WC}$$

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The "Why" Behind Detailed Estimation

A detailed estimate is *not* just arithmetic. It serves three strategic purposes in chemical industry management:

| **Strategic Purpose** | **Engineering Rationale** |
|---|---|
| **Feasibility Confirmation** | Verifies that the project satisfies the corporate hurdle rate (typically **DCFRR ≥ 15%** for petrochemical projects) before capital sanction. |
| **Procurement Basis** | Provides line-item quantities for floating purchase orders to vendors (reactors, pumps, instruments). |
| **Cash Flow Scaffolding** | Generates the time-distributed capex profile required for **Discounted Cash Flow Rate of Return (DCFRR)** and **Net Present Value (NPV)** computations. |

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Methodology of Detailed Estimation

> [!NOTE]
> The KTU 2024 syllabus demands explicit knowledge of the following logical sequence:

**Step 1 — Process Finalization & Equipment List Generation**
The process flow diagram (PFD), material and energy balances, and equipment data sheets are finalized. A comprehensive **equipment list** (reactor types, capacities, materials of construction, design pressures) is compiled.

**Step 2 — Vendor Quotation / Cost Database Lookup**
Each piece of equipment is priced using:
- Recent vendor quotations (preferred for Class I estimates).
- Cost engineering databases (e.g., **Matches, CAPCOST, Peters & Timmerhaus** correlations) escalated by **Chemical Engineering Plant Cost Index (CEPCI)**.

**Step 3 — Installation Factor Application**
The purchased equipment cost is multiplied by an **installation factor** that bundles labour, foundations, piping, instrumentation, electrical, insulation, and painting. This transforms *bare cost* → *installed cost*.

**Step 4 — Indirect Cost Accumulation**
Engineering, supervision, construction expenses, contractor's fee, contingency, and startup costs are added.

**Step 5 — Working Capital Computation**
Working capital covers raw material inventory (typically 1–2 months), product inventory, accounts receivable, minus accounts payable.

**Step 6 — Total Product Cost Build-up**
Direct manufacturing costs (raw materials, utilities, labour), fixed manufacturing costs (depreciation, taxes, insurance), and general expenses (administration, R&D, distribution) are aggregated to give **Total Product Cost (TPC)**.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Equations for PECHT757 Module 2 — Detailed Estimate:**

| **S.No.** | **Quantity** | **Formula** | **Typical Range / Unit** |
|:---:|---|---|:---:|
| 1 | Purchased Equipment Cost (escalated) | $C_{PE} = C_{PE,base} \times \left(\dfrac{I_{current}}{I_{base}}\right)$ | $I$ = Cost Index (e.g., CEPCI) |
| 2 | Lang Factor (FCI from delivered equipment) | $\text{FCI} = f_L \sum C_{DE}$ | $f_L = 3.10$ (Solid), $4.74$ (Fluid) |
| 3 | Hand's Method (ISBL Module Factor) | $\text{ISBL} = \sum (C_{DE,i} \times f_{module,i})$ | $f_{module} \approx 1.5$ to $4.0$ |
| 4 | OSBL by % of ISBL | $\text{OSBL} = 0.25 \text{ to } 0.50 \times \text{ISBL}$ | 25%–50% of ISBL |
| 5 | Engineering Cost | $C_{Eng} = 0.10 \text{ to } 0.20 \times \text{FCI}$ | 10%–20% of FCI |
| 6 | Contingency | $C_{Cont} = 0.08 \text{ to } 0.15 \times \text{FCI}$ | 8%–15% of FCI |
| 7 | Working Capital | $\text{WC} = 0.10 \text{ to } 0.20 \times \text{FCI}$ | 10%–20% of FCI |
| 8 | Total Capital Investment | $\text{TCI} = \text{FCI} + \text{WC}$ | ₹ Crores / $ Millions |
| 9 | Manufacturing Cost | $\text{COM} = C_{RM} + C_{UT} + C_{OL} + C_{FC} + C_{GE}$ | ₹ / kg product |
| 10 | Cost Escalation | $C_2 = C_1 \left(\dfrac{I_2}{I_1}\right)$ | CEPCI, Nelson-Farrar, etc. |
| 11 | Six-Tenths Rule (sizing) | $C_B = C_A \left(\dfrac{S_B}{S_A}\right)^{0.6}$ | Capacity scaling |
| 12 | Total Product Cost | $\text{TPC} = C_{RM} + C_{UT} + C_{OL} + D + (C_{FC} + C_{GE})$ | ₹ / kg product |

> [!WARNING]
> **LaTeX Pipe Symbol Rule:** Throughout this entire document, mathematical set-builder notation such as "given that $x \in \mathbb{R}$" is written using `\in` (not `|`). Likewise, the *vertical bar* in any markdown table cell is written as `$\vert$` or `$\mid$` to prevent breaking the KTU/Confluence markdown parser.

## 2.4 Methods of Cost Estimation — A Comparative Analysis

> [!TIP]
> **KTU loves comparing these three methods in 14-mark questions:**

### Method A — Lang Factor Method (1947)
$$\text{FCI} = f_L \times \sum C_{DE}$$

where $f_L$ is a multiplier depending on plant type:

$$\begin{aligned}
f_L^{\text{Solid processing}} &= 3.10 \\
f_L^{\text{Fluid processing}} &= 4.74 \\
f_L^{\text{Mixed (chemical)}} &= 4.00
\end{aligned}$$

**Limitation:** Highly aggregated; ignores equipment-specific installation complexity.

### Method B — Hand's Method (1958, 1971)
$$\text{ISBL}_{Hand} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (C_{DE,i} \times f_{H,i})$$

where $f_{H,i}$ is the **module installation factor** specific to each equipment category (e.g., pumps, compressors, columns, fired heaters, exchangers). Hand's factors are tabulated per equipment type and material of construction.

**Advantage:** More granular than Lang; widely used in **KBC PROFIMAT**, **ASPEN ICARUS**, and **Honeywell Unisim Design** cost modules.

### Method C — Detailed (Guthrie's) Method (1969, 1974)
This is the *gold standard* for **Class I / Class II** detailed estimates. Each piece of equipment has its own line item, with installation factors broken down into six components:

$$C_{installed} = C_{PE} \times (1 + m_h) \times (1 + m_p + m_{el} + m_{i} + m_{s} + m_{l})$$

where:
- $m_h$ = materials handling (0.02–0.05)
- $m_p$ = piping (0.10–0.80)
- $m_{el}$ = electrical (0.10–0.15)
- $m_i$ = instrumentation (0.05–0.30)
- $m_s$ = structures & buildings (0.05–0.20)
- $m_l$ = labour (0.10–0.20)

> [!NOTE]
> Guthrie's method, combined with **CEPCI escalation**, forms the backbone of KTU Module 2 numerical problems.

## 2.5 Cost Indices — The Engineering Economist's Time Machine

A cost index tracks the time-dependent change in equipment/labour/material prices. The **CEPCI (Chemical Engineering Plant Cost Index)** published monthly by *Chemical Engineering* magazine is the most cited:

$$\text{Escalation Factor} = \frac{\text{CEPCI}_{\text{target year}}}{\text{CEPCI}_{\text{base year}}}$$

Other relevant indices:

| **Index** | **Tracks** |
|---|---|
| CEPCI | Composite chemical plant costs (US) |
| Nelson-Farrar Refinery Index | Oil refinery construction |
| ENR Construction Index | General construction (US) |
| IOW (India) | Indian engineering procurement — recommended for KTU context |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Detailed Estimate for a Methanol Plant (KTU Standard 14-Mark Pattern)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement (Modeled on KTU Pattern):**
> A chemical company is setting up a **150,000 tonnes/year methanol plant**. The delivered equipment cost for major items, based on vendor quotations (Base year 2018), is:

| **Equipment** | **Delivered Cost (₹ Lakhs, 2018)** | **Hand's Module Factor $f_H$** |
|---|---:|---:|
| Distillation Column (SS316) | 220 | 3.5 |
| Packed Reactor (CS + SS liner) | 380 | 4.2 |
| Feed/Product Pumps (5 nos.) | 75 | 2.8 |
| Centrifugal Compressors (2 nos.) | 450 | 3.0 |
| Shell & Tube Heat Exchangers (6 nos.) | 130 | 3.2 |
| Storage Tanks (CS) | 65 | 2.0 |
| **Total Delivered Equipment Cost** | $\Sigma C_{DE} = 1320$ | — |

Given:
- CEPCI in 2018 = **576.8**
- CEPCI in current year 2024 = **798.4**
- OSBL cost = 35% of escalated ISBL
- Engineering cost = 12% of FCI
- Contingency = 10% of FCI
- Working Capital = 15% of FCI

**Compute the Total Capital Investment (TCI) and per-tonne production cost of methanol.**

### 3.1.1 Step 1 — Escalate Delivered Equipment Cost to 2024

The delivered equipment costs were quoted in 2018. They must be escalated to 2024 using CEPCI:

$$\text{Escalation Factor} = \frac{\text{CEPCI}_{2024}}{\text{CEPCI}_{2018}} = \frac{798.4}{576.8}$$

$$\text{Escalation Factor} = 1.3840 \text{ (dimensionless)}$$

Applying the factor to each equipment line:

$$\begin{aligned}
C_{DE}^{\text{Column}}_{2024} &= 220 \times 1.3840 = 304.48 \text{ Lakhs} \\
C_{DE}^{\text{Reactor}}_{2024} &= 380 \times 1.3840 = 525.92 \text{ Lakhs} \\
C_{DE}^{\text{Pumps}}_{2024} &= 75 \times 1.3840 = 103.80 \text{ Lakhs} \\
C_{DE}^{\text{Compressors}}_{2024} &= 450 \times 1.3840 = 622.80 \text{ Lakhs} \\
C_{DE}^{\text{Exchangers}}_{2024} &= 130 \times 1.3840 = 179.92 \text{ Lakhs} \\
C_{DE}^{\text{Tanks}}_{2024} &= 65 \times 1.3840 = 89.96 \text{ Lakhs}
\end{aligned}$$

**Total Escalated Delivered Equipment Cost:**

$$\sum C_{DE}^{2024} = 304.48 + 525.92 + 103.80 + 622.80 + 179.92 + 89.96$$

$$\sum C_{DE}^{2024} = 1826.88 \text{ Lakhs} \approx \mathbf{1826.9 \text{ Lakhs}}$$

### 3.1.2 Step 2 — Compute ISBL using Hand's Method

Apply each equipment's Hand's module factor to its escalated delivered cost:

$$\begin{aligned}
C_{module}^{\text{Column}} &= 304.48 \times 3.5 = 1065.68 \\
C_{module}^{\text{Reactor}} &= 525.92 \times 4.2 = 2208.86 \\
C_{module}^{\text{Pumps}} &= 103.80 \times 2.8 = 290.64 \\
C_{module}^{\text{Compressors}} &= 622.80 \times 3.0 = 1868.40 \\
C_{module}^{\text{Exchangers}} &= 179.92 \times 3.2 = 575.74 \\
C_{module}^{\text{Tanks}} &= 89.96 \times 2.0 = 179.92
\end{aligned}$$

**Summing the modules:**

$$\text{ISBL} = 1065.68 + 2208.86 + 290.64 + 1868.40 + 575.74 + 179.92$$

$$\text{ISBL} = 6189.24 \text{ Lakhs}$$

### 3.1.3 Step 3 — Compute OSBL

$$\text{OSBL} = 0.35 \times \text{ISBL} = 0.35 \times 6189.24$$

$$\text{OSBL} = 2166.23 \text{ Lakhs}$$

### 3.1.4 Step 4 — Compute FCI (Fixed Capital Investment)

$$\text{FCI} = \text{ISBL} + \text{OSBL} = 6189.24 + 2166.23$$

$$\mathbf{\text{FCI} = 8355.47 \text{ Lakhs}}$$

### 3.1.5 Step 5 — Add Engineering + Contingency

$$\text{FCI}_{\text{grand}} = \text{FCI} \times (1 + 0.12 + 0.10)$$

$$\text{FCI}_{\text{grand}} = 8355.47 \times 1.22$$

$$\mathbf{\text{FCI}_{\text{grand}} = 10193.67 \text{ Lakhs}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> Some KTU textbooks include engineering & contingency *inside* FCI definition; others quote FCI as a *pre-contingency* figure. The KTU 2024 convention is to present **"Bare FCI"** separately from **"Grass-roots / Total FCI"** inclusive of contingency.

### 3.1.6 Step 6 — Compute Working Capital and TCI

$$\text{WC} = 0.15 \times \text{FCI}_{\text{grand}} = 0.15 \times 10193.67$$

$$\text{WC} = 1529.05 \text{ Lakhs}$$

$$\text{TCI} = \text{FCI}_{\text{grand}} + \text{WC} = 10193.67 + 1529.05$$

$$\mathbf{\text{TCI} = 11722.72 \text{ Lakhs} \approx \mathbf{₹ 117.23 \text{ Crores}}}$$

### 3.1.7 Step 7 — Annualized Capex per Tonne of Methanol

$$\text{Annual Production} = 150{,}000 \text{ tonnes} = 1.5 \times 10^5 \text{ t/yr}$$

$$\text{TCI per tonne (annualized over 10 years)} = \frac{117.23 \text{ Cr}}{10 \times 1.5 \times 10^5 \text{ t}}$$

$$\text{TCI per tonne} = \frac{117.23 \times 10^7}{15 \times 10^5} = \frac{117.23 \times 10^2}{15} = \frac{11723}{15}$$

$$\mathbf{\text{TCI per tonne (depreciated)} \approx \mathbf{₹ 781.5 \text{ / tonne methanol}}}$$

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Manufacturing Cost Build-up (KTU 14-Mark Pattern)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** For the same methanol plant, given the following annual operating data (all in ₹ Lakhs/yr at 2024 prices):

| **Item** | **Annual Cost (₹ Lakhs)** |
|---|---:|
| Raw materials (natural gas + catalyst) | 4,200 |
| Utilities (steam, power, cooling water) | 1,150 |
| Operating labour (60 workers @ ₹8 L/yr) | 480 |
| Direct supervisory & clerical | 240 |
| Maintenance & repairs (6% of FCI) | 501.3 |
| Operating supplies (0.9% FCI) | 75.2 |
| Laboratory charges (15% of OL) | 72.0 |
| Patents & royalties | 80 |
| Depreciation (10% of FCI straight-line) | 835.5 |
| Local taxes (2% FCI) | 167.1 |
| Insurance (1% FCI) | 83.6 |
| General plant overhead (60% of OL+Sup) | 432.0 |
| Administrative expenses (25% of OL) | 120.0 |
| Distribution & selling (5% of TPC) | (to be solved) |
| Financing (interest on TCI @ 8%) | 937.8 |

**Compute the Total Product Cost (TPC) and selling price per tonne of methanol.**

### 3.2.1 Step 1 — Direct Manufacturing Cost ($C_{DM}$)

$$C_{DM} = C_{RM} + C_{UT} + C_{OL} + C_{Sup} + C_{Maint} + C_{OpSup} + C_{Lab}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
C_{DM} &= 4200 + 1150 + 480 + 240 + 501.3 + 75.2 + 72.0 \\
C_{DM} &= 6718.5 \text{ Lakhs/yr}
\end{aligned}$$

### 3.2.2 Step 2 — Fixed Manufacturing Cost ($C_{FM}$)

$$C_{FM} = D + C_{Tax} + C_{Insurance}$$

$$C_{FM} = 835.5 + 167.1 + 83.6 = 1086.2 \text{ Lakhs/yr}$$

### 3.2.3 Step 3 — General Expenses ($C_{GE}$)

$$C_{GE} = C_{Patents} + C_{Admin} + C_{Dist} + C_{GPO} + C_{Fin}$$

$$C_{GE} = 80 + 120 + C_{Dist} + 432 + 937.8$$

$$C_{GE} = 1569.8 + C_{Dist}$$

### 3.2.4 Step 4 — Total Product Cost Definition

$$\text{TPC} = C_{DM} + C_{FM} + C_{GE}$$

Let $C_{Dist} = 0.05 \times \text{TPC}$. Substituting:

$$\text{TPC} = 6718.5 + 1086.2 + 1569.8 + 0.05 \times \text{TPC}$$

$$\text{TPC} - 0.05 \times \text{TPC} = 9374.5$$

$$0.95 \times \text{TPC} = 9374.5$$

$$\mathbf{\text{TPC} = 9867.9 \text{ Lakhs/yr}}$$

### 3.2.5 Step 5 — Distribution & Selling Cost

$$C_{Dist} = 0.05 \times 9867.9 = 493.4 \text{ Lakhs/yr}$$

### 3.2.6 Step 6 — Per-Tonne TPC

$$\text{TPC per tonne} = \frac{9867.9 \times 10^5}{1.5 \times 10^5} = \frac{986.79 \times 10^6}{1.5 \times 10^5}$$

$$\text{TPC per tonne} = \frac{986.79}{0.15} = 6578.6$$

$$\mathbf{\text{TPC per tonne} \approx \mathbf{₹ 6,579 \text{ per tonne methanol}}}$$

### 3.2.7 Step 7 — Selling Price with Margin

Assuming a **12% pre-tax ROI on TCI** must be earned:

$$\text{Required Annual Profit} = 0.12 \times 11722.72 = 1406.73 \text{ Lakhs/yr}$$

$$\text{Total Revenue Required} = 9867.9 + 1406.73 = 11274.63 \text{ Lakhs/yr}$$

$$\text{Selling Price per tonne} = \frac{11274.63 \times 10^5}{1.5 \times 10^5} \approx \mathbf{₹ 7{,}516 \text{ per tonne}}$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Detailed Estimate Build-up Workflow

> [!NOTE]
> The following Mermaid **Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow** depicts the *causal logic* of a detailed estimate — from raw equipment data to TCI and TPC outputs. All node IDs are alphanumeric (no reserved keywords) and labels contain only raw uppercase alphanumeric text.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[START: Process Design Finalization] --> A2[Equipment List and Data Sheets]
    A2 --> A3[Vendor Quotation Collection]
    A3 --> A4[Base Year Delivered Equipment Cost]
    A4 --> A5{Cost Index Escalation Required?}
    A5 -- YES --> A6[Apply CEPCI Escalation Factor]
    A5 -- NO --> A7[Use As-Is Delivered Cost]
    A6 --> B1[Escalated Delivered Equipment Cost Sum]
    A7 --> B1
    B1 --> B2[Select Estimation Method]
    B2 --> B3[Lang Factor Method]
    B2 --> B4[Hand Module Factor Method]
    B2 --> B5[Guthrie Detailed Method]
    B3 --> C1[ISBL Capital Cost]
    B4 --> C1
    B5 --> C1
    C1 --> C2[OSBL Computation as Percent of ISBL]
    C2 --> C3[Fixed Capital Investment FCI Pre-Contingency]
    C3 --> C4[Add Engineering 12 percent of FCI]
    C4 --> C5[Add Contingency 10 percent of FCI]
    C5 --> C6[Grassroots Fixed Capital FCI Grand]
    C6 --> C7[Add Working Capital 15 percent of FCI]
    C7 --> C8[Total Capital Investment TCI]
    C8 --> D1[Parallel: Operating Cost Build-up]
    D1 --> D2[Raw Materials and Utilities]
    D1 --> D3[Operating Labour and Supervision]
    D1 --> D4[Maintenance Depreciation Insurance Taxes]
    D1 --> D5[General Expenses Admin Distribution Finance]
    D2 --> E1[Total Product Cost TPC]
    D3 --> E1
    D4 --> E1
    D5 --> E1
    C8 --> F1[Profitability Analysis: DCFRR and NPV]
    E1 --> F1
    F1 --> G1[Final Management Decision: GO or NO-GO]
```

## 4.2 Schematic — Cost-Component Hierarchy in a Chemical Plant

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    H1[Capital Investment Universe] --> H2[Fixed Capital Investment FCI]
    H1 --> H3[Working Capital WC]
    H2 --> I1[Inside Battery Limits ISBL]
    H2 --> I2[Outside Battery Limits OSBL]
    I1 --> J1[Process Equipment]
    I1 --> J2[Installation Labour]
    I1 --> J3[Piping and Instrumentation]
    I1 --> J4[Electrical and Insulation]
    I2 --> K1[Utilities and Storage]
    I2 --> K2[Effluent Treatment]
    I2 --> K3[Admin Buildings and Site Prep]
    H3 --> L1[Raw Material Inventory]
    H3 --> L2[Product and Intermediate Inventory]
    H3 --> L3[Cash for Payroll and Receivables]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid — Cost-Estimation Method Selection Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    M1[Start: What Data is Available?] --> M2{Equipment List Available?}
    M2 -- NO --> M3[Use Order-of-Magnitude or Study Estimate]
    M2 -- YES --> M3A{Are Vendor Quotations Available?}
    M3A -- NO --> M4[Use Factored Estimate: Lang or Hand]
    M3A -- YES --> M5[Apply Guthrie Detailed Method with CEPCI Escalation]
    M3 --> N1[Output: Class V or IV Estimate]
    M4 --> N2[Output: Class III or II Estimate]
    M5 --> N3[Output: Class I or II Detailed Estimate]
    N3 --> O1[Final Step: TCI plus TPC plus Profitability Metrics]
```

> [!TIP]
> **How to read these diagrams for your KTU answer script:**
> - Always draw the **estimation method block** before writing the formula. KTU examiners award 2 marks for a *flow chart* in 14-mark questions.
> - Use boxes (rectangles) for *data/process* and rhombus shapes (drawn manually as `[]` with branches) for *decision points*.
> - Label all arrows with the **transfer quantity** (e.g., "C_DE escalated", "OSBL = 0.35 × ISBL").

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 PART A — 3 Mark Questions (with Model Answers)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Define a "Detailed Estimate" in the context of a chemical process industry. Mention the expected accuracy range.**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> A detailed estimate is a comprehensive bottom-up financial projection of the Fixed Capital Investment (FCI), Working Capital, and Total Product Cost (TPC) of a chemical plant, prepared using finalized equipment data sheets, vendor quotations, and CEPCI escalation. It is performed during the engineering design phase, after process flow diagrams and material/energy balances are complete.
> The expected accuracy is **±5% to ±10% (Class I)** or **±10% to ±15% (Class II)**. It includes ISBL, OSBL, engineering fees, contingency, and working capital — making it the most reliable estimate class used for project sanction by financial institutions. (3 marks)

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Distinguish between Lang's factor method and Hand's module factor method of capital cost estimation.**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
>
> | **Aspect** | **Lang's Method (1947)** | **Hand's Method (1958)** |
> |---|---|---|
> | Granularity | Single multiplier $f_L$ applied to total delivered equipment cost. | Equipment-specific multiplier $f_{H,i}$ applied to each item. |
> | Typical $f$ values | $f_L = 3.10$ (solid), $4.74$ (fluid), $4.00$ (mixed). | $f_{H,i} = 1.5$ to $4.0$ per equipment type. |
> | Accuracy | ±30–50% (Class IV–V) | ±15–25% (Class II–III) |
>
> Lang's method is faster but less accurate. Hand's method is more rigorous as it respects the installation complexity of each unit. (3 marks)

---

## 📕 PART B — 14 Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — May 2023 Modeled]

**(a)** With a neat flow chart, explain the step-by-step methodology of preparing a **detailed estimate** for a chemical plant. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A grassroots ammonia plant has the following major equipment with delivered costs at base year 2020 (CEPCI 596.2) and Hand's module factors. Escalate to 2024 (CEPCI 798.4) and compute the **ISBL cost using Hand's method**, and the **Total Capital Investment** assuming OSBL = 30% of ISBL, Engineering = 10% of FCI, Contingency = 8% of FCI, and Working Capital = 12% of FCI. **(7 marks)**

| **Equipment** | **Delivered Cost 2020 (₹ Crores)** | **Hand's Factor $f_H$** |
|---|---:|---:|
| Primary Reformer | 12.5 | 4.0 |
| Secondary Reformer | 6.8 | 3.8 |
| CO₂ Absorber Column | 8.2 | 3.5 |
| Syngas Compressor | 14.0 | 2.9 |
| Heat Exchangers (set) | 5.5 | 3.2 |
| Pumps and Drivers | 3.2 | 2.6 |

---

#### ✅ MODEL SOLUTION — Question A

##### Part (a) — Step-by-Step Methodology **(7 marks)**

The detailed estimate is prepared in six sequential steps:

1. **Process Design Finalization (1 mark):** The PFD, P&ID, material/energy balances, and equipment data sheets (with capacity, MOC, design pressure/temperature) are frozen.

2. **Equipment List and Vendor Quotation (1 mark):** A master equipment list is compiled. Each major item is priced using vendor quotations (preferred) or cost engineering databases (CAPCOST, Matches, Peters-Timmerhaus correlations).

3. **Cost Escalation (1 mark):** Historical cost data is escalated to the target year using the **Chemical Engineering Plant Cost Index (CEPCI)** as per $C_2 = C_1 \cdot (I_2 / I_1)$.

4. **Installation Cost Computation (2 marks):** The escalated delivered cost is multiplied by an **installation factor** (Lang's $f_L$, Hand's $f_H$, or Guthrie's six-component factor) to obtain the **installed cost**. OSBL (utilities, storage, effluent) is added as a percentage of ISBL.
