5. **Indirect Costs (1 mark):** Engineering, supervision, contractor's fee, contingency, and pre-operative expenses are added to get the **Fixed Capital Investment (FCI)**. Working capital is added to get **Total Capital Investment (TCI)**.

6. **Operating Cost & Profitability (1 mark):** Total Product Cost is built up from raw materials, utilities, labour, depreciation, and overheads. The estimate is then used as input to **DCFRR, NPV, and Payback Period** analysis for the final go/no-go decision.

*(Valuation Tip: Award 1 mark each for steps 1, 2, 3, 5, 6 and 2 marks for the elaborated Step 4. Flow chart is a sub-1-mark add-on.)*

##### Part (b) — Numerical Solution **(7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Escalation Factor:**

$$\text{Escalation Factor} = \frac{798.4}{596.2} = 1.3391$$

**Step 2 — Escalate each equipment cost:**

$$\begin{aligned}
C_{DE}^{\text{Reformer-1}}_{2024} &= 12.5 \times 1.3391 = 16.74 \text{ Cr} \\
C_{DE}^{\text{Reformer-2}}_{2024} &= 6.8 \times 1.3391 = 9.11 \text{ Cr} \\
C_{DE}^{\text{Column}}_{2024} &= 8.2 \times 1.3391 = 10.98 \text{ Cr} \\
C_{DE}^{\text{Compressor}}_{2024} &= 14.0 \times 1.3391 = 18.75 \text{ Cr} \\
C_{DE}^{\text{Exchangers}}_{2024} &= 5.5 \times 1.3391 = 7.37 \text{ Cr} \\
C_{DE}^{\text{Pumps}}_{2024} &= 3.2 \times 1.3391 = 4.29 \text{ Cr}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Apply Hand's module factors:**

$$\begin{aligned}
C_{module}^{\text{Reformer-1}} &= 16.74 \times 4.0 = 66.96 \\
C_{module}^{\text{Reformer-2}} &= 9.11 \times 3.8 = 34.62 \\
C_{module}^{\text{Column}} &= 10.98 \times 3.5 = 38.43 \\
C_{module}^{\text{Compressor}} &= 18.75 \times 2.9 = 54.38 \\
C_{module}^{\text{Exchangers}} &= 7.37 \times 3.2 = 23.58 \\
C_{module}^{\text{Pumps}} &= 4.29 \times 2.6 = 11.15
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — ISBL = Sum of modules:**

$$\text{ISBL} = 66.96 + 34.62 + 38.43 + 54.38 + 23.58 + 11.15 = 229.12 \text{ Cr}$$

**Step 5 — OSBL:**

$$\text{OSBL} = 0.30 \times 229.12 = 68.74 \text{ Cr}$$

**Step 6 — FCI pre-contingency:**

$$\text{FCI}_{pre} = 229.12 + 68.74 = 297.86 \text{ Cr}$$

**Step 7 — FCI with engineering and contingency:**

$$\text{FCI} = 297.86 \times (1 + 0.10 + 0.08) = 297.86 \times 1.18 = 351.47 \text{ Cr}$$

**Step 8 — TCI with Working Capital:**

$$\text{WC} = 0.12 \times 351.47 = 42.18 \text{ Cr}$$

$$\mathbf{\text{TCI} = 351.47 + 42.18 = 393.65 \text{ Crores}}$$

**Final Answer:** ISBL = **₹ 229.12 Crores**, TCI = **₹ 393.65 Crores**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022 Modeled — Internal Choice]

**(a)** Explain the **six-component Guthrie installation factor method** for detailed estimation. List the typical ranges of each component. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 75,000 tonnes/year **caustic soda plant** is to be set up. The purchased equipment cost (base year 2019, CEPCI = 567.5) is **₹ 480 Lakhs**. Use **Lang's factor method** (assume fluid processing, $f_L = 4.74$) to compute FCI. Then escalate to 2024 (CEPCI = 798.4), add 25% OSBL, 10% engineering, 8% contingency, and 15% working capital, to compute TCI. Finally, calculate the **depreciated capex per tonne of NaOH**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### ✅ MODEL SOLUTION — Question B

##### Part (a) — Guthrie's Six-Component Method **(7 marks)**

Guthrie (1969, 1974) refined the Lang/Hand approaches by decomposing the installation factor into **six physically meaningful components** that can be quantified individually. The total installed cost is:

$$C_{installed} = C_{PE} \times (1 + m_h) \times (1 + m_p + m_{el} + m_i + m_s + m_l)$$

The components are:

| **Component** | **Description** | **Typical Range** |
|---|---|---:|
| $m_h$ | Materials handling (cranes, forklifts, equipment setting) | 0.02 – 0.05 |
| $m_p$ | Piping (process and utility) | 0.10 – 0.80 |
| $m_{el}$ | Electrical (cables, transformers, MCC, lighting) | 0.10 – 0.15 |
| $m_i$ | Instrumentation and control | 0.05 – 0.30 |
| $m_s$ | Structures, foundations, buildings | 0.05 – 0.20 |
| $m_l$ | Labour (direct construction) | 0.10 – 0.20 |

The multiplication form **assumes the components are independent**, which is a known approximation, but yields accuracy of ±10–15% — sufficient for Class II detailed estimates. This is widely used in **KBC PROFIMAT, ICARUS, and ASPEN Capital Cost Estimator**. (7 marks)

##### Part (b) — Numerical Solution **(7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Apply Lang's factor (base year 2019):**

$$\text{FCI}_{2019} = 4.74 \times 480 = 2275.2 \text{ Lakhs}$$

**Step 2 — Escalate to 2024:**

$$\text{Escalation Factor} = \frac{798.4}{567.5} = 1.4069$$

$$\text{FCI}_{2024} = 2275.2 \times 1.4069 = 3200.66 \text{ Lakhs}$$

**Step 3 — Add OSBL (25% of FCI):**

$$\text{ISBL + OSBL} = 3200.66 \times 1.25 = 4000.83 \text{ Lakhs}$$

**Step 4 — Add engineering and contingency:**

$$\text{FCI}_{\text{grand}} = 4000.83 \times (1 + 0.10 + 0.08) = 4000.83 \times 1.18 = 4720.98 \text{ Lakhs}$$

**Step 5 — Add working capital:**

$$\text{WC} = 0.15 \times 4720.98 = 708.15 \text{ Lakhs}$$

$$\mathbf{\text{TCI} = 4720.98 + 708.15 = 5429.13 \text{ Lakhs} = \mathbf{₹ 54.29 \text{ Crores}}}$$

**Step 6 — Depreciated capex per tonne:**

Assuming a 10-year linear project life:

$$\text{Depreciated Capex per tonne} = \frac{5429.13 \times 10^5}{10 \times 75{,}000} = \frac{5429.13 \times 10^5}{7.5 \times 10^5}$$

$$\text{Depreciated Capex per tonne} = \frac{5429.13}{7.5} \approx \mathbf{₹ 723.88 \text{ per tonne NaOH}}$$

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students commonly LOSE MARKS in Module 2 — Detailed Estimate:**
>
> 1. **Confusing FCI and TCI (Loss: 1–2 marks):** FCI excludes working capital; TCI includes it. State definitions explicitly in the first line of your answer.
> 2. **Forgetting to escalate to current year (Loss: 2 marks):** If the problem gives a base year and a current CEPCI, the escalation step is **mandatory**. Skipping it loses half the numerical marks.
> 3. **Hand's factors are equipment-specific, not plant-specific (Loss: 1 mark):** Do not use a single multiplier like Lang's. Each equipment type carries its own $f_H$ — check the table.
> 4. **OSBL must be added even if not explicitly asked (Loss: 1 mark):** Many students compute ISBL and stop. OSBL (utilities, storage, effluent) is always part of FCI.
> 5. **Distribution & selling cost creates a circular equation (Loss: 1 mark):** In TPC problems where $C_{Dist} = 0.05 \times TPC$, students often plug in TPC = TPC. Isolate: $TPC = \dfrac{\text{Rest}}{1 - 0.05}$.
> 6. **Units — Lakhs vs. Crores (Loss: 1 mark):** Always maintain unit consistency. ₹1 Crore = ₹100 Lakhs. Mixing them silently is a common KTU failure point.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Detailed Estimate (Module 2)**

- ✅ A **Detailed Estimate** has an accuracy of **±5–15%** and is performed *after* process design finalization, using vendor quotations and equipment data sheets.
- ✅ The estimate hierarchy is: **OM (40%) → Study (30%) → Preliminary (20%) → Definitive (10–15%) → Detailed (5–10%)**.
- ✅ **Three classic methods:** Lang (single multiplier), Hand (per-equipment multiplier), Guthrie (six-component decomposition).
- ✅ **CEPCI Escalation formula:** $C_2 = C_1 \cdot \left( \dfrac{I_2}{I_1} \right)$ — *always* apply when base year ≠ target year.
- ✅ **OSBL** = 25–50% of ISBL; **Engineering** = 10–20% of FCI; **Contingency** = 8–15% of FCI; **Working Capital** = 10–20% of FCI.
- ✅ **TCI = FCI + WC.** **FCI = ISBL + OSBL + Engineering + Contingency.**
- ✅ **TPC = Direct Manufacturing + Fixed Manufacturing + General Expenses.** Direct manufacturing includes RM, utilities, labour. Fixed manufacturing includes depreciation, taxes, insurance. General expenses include admin, distribution, finance.
- ✅ **Distribution-cost circularity:** $C_{Dist} = p \cdot TPC \Rightarrow TPC = \dfrac{\text{All other costs}}{1 - p}$.
- ✅ **Lang's $f_L$:** Solid = 3.10, Fluid = 4.74, Mixed = 4.00. Don't apply Lang when detailed equipment list exists — use Hand/Guthrie.
- ✅ **Six-Tenths Rule** is for *capacity scaling*, not for detailed estimate: $C_B = C_A \cdot (S_B / S_A)^{0.6}$.
- ✅ Always state **units (₹ Lakhs / Crores) and the year** to which the cost refers.
- ✅ The Detailed Estimate feeds **DCFRR, NPV, Payback Period** — these are covered in Module 3 of PECHT757.

> **End of Module 2 — Detailed Estimate Notes**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cost Indexes: Material Cost Indices
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT757 - ECONOMICS AND MANAGEMENT OF CHEMICAL INDUSTRIES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Cost Indexes: Material Cost Indices

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Cost Index** (also called a **Price Index**) is a normalized dimensionless numerical measure that expresses the relative change in the cost or price of a defined basket of goods, services, or materials between two periods — a **base period** (reference period, denoted by subscript `0`) and a **current period** (denoted by subscript `n`). It is the single most critical economic tool used in engineering economics to perform **time-value adjustments** of capital and operating expenditures across years of differing purchasing power.

A **Material Cost Index (MCI)** is a specialized subset of the cost index family that tracks the price fluctuation of *only the raw materials, consumables, and feedstocks* consumed by a chemical process plant. Since the chemical industry is uniquely *material-intensive* (with feedstock and utility costs often constituting **60–80%** of total production cost), Material Cost Indices carry disproportionately high weightage in plant profitability, project feasibility, and Make-or-Buy decisions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** In Module 2 of PECHT757, Cost Indexes are formally classified under **"Cost Estimation & Investment Economics."** Students must distinguish clearly between (i) Capital Cost Indexes (covering equipment, fabrication, labor), (ii) Material Cost Indexes (covering feedstocks, catalysts, utilities), and (iii) Composite / Total Plant Cost Indexes. Material Cost Indices form the analytical backbone of operating cost escalation, supply-chain risk assessment, and contractual price-escalation clauses in EPC (Engineering, Procurement, Construction) projects.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Vegetable Basket"

Imagine a homemaker who, in **January 2020 (base year)**, bought a fixed "monthly grocery basket" containing **10 kg rice, 5 kg sugar, 2 litres oil, and 3 kg onion** for a total of ₹1,000. Now, in **January 2026**, she buys the *exact same basket* (same quantities) for ₹1,650. The cost index for 2026 (with 2020 as base = 100) is:

$$ \text{Cost Index}_{2026/2020} = \frac{1650}{1000} \times 100 = 165 $$

This means prices have risen by **65%** in nominal terms over 6 years. A Material Cost Index works on exactly the same principle — it tracks a "basket" of chemical raw materials (e.g., naphtha, caustic soda, sulfuric acid, catalysts) and converts heterogeneous price movements into a single comparable number.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition Hook:** A cost index is essentially an **economic thermometer**. Just as a thermometer tells you the temperature relative to the freezing/boiling point of water, a cost index tells you the *cost level* relative to a chosen base year (defined as **100**).

### 1.3 Standard Metrics and Notation

| Symbol | Meaning | Standard Unit |
|--------|---------|---------------|
| `P₀` | Price of material in base year | $/ton, ₹/kg |
| `Pₙ` | Price of material in current year `n` | $/ton, ₹/kg |
| `Q₀` | Quantity consumed in base year | tons, kg |
| `Qₙ` | Quantity consumed in current year `n` | tons, kg |
| `I_n/0` | Index number for year `n` with base year `0` | Dimensionless |
| `wᵢ` | Weight (typically value-share) of material `i` | Decimal (sums to 1.0) |

The base year is conventionally assigned an index value of **100**, allowing all subsequent readings to be interpreted as *percentages of base-year cost*.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Why Material Cost Indices Are Necessary in Chemical Industry Economics

In chemical engineering projects, capital investments and operating budgets are typically estimated during the **design phase (year T)**, but actual procurement and operations may begin several years later (year **T + 3** or **T + 5**). During this lead-time lag, material prices fluctuate due to:

- **Macroeconomic inflation** (general price-level changes)
- **Sectoral demand shocks** (e.g., surge in petrochemical demand from packaging)
- **Geopolitical disruptions** (e.g., crude oil supply restrictions)
- **Currency exchange volatility** (for imported catalysts and specialty chemicals)
- **Technological substitution** (e.g., LFP batteries displacing cobalt-based cathodes)

A properly constructed Material Cost Index allows an engineer to:

1. **Deflate** historical cost data to a comparable basis
2. **Inflate** design-phase estimates to predict actual future costs
3. Build **escalation clauses** in long-term supply contracts
4. Conduct **techno-economic feasibility studies** that withstand audit scrutiny

### 2.2 Classification of Material Cost Index Formulas

The literature recognizes four principal formula families, each embodying a different *weighting philosophy*. The choice of formula influences the resulting index value by 2–8% — a margin that is commercially significant in million-dollar chemical plant budgets.

#### (a) Simple Aggregative Price Index

The simplest, unweighted variant. All materials are treated with equal importance.

$$ I_{n/0} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{N} P_{n,i}}{\sum_{i=1}^{N} P_{0,i}} \times 100 $$

**Why this is rarely used:** A 1 kg price change in an inexpensive bulk chemical like limestone is given the same importance as a 1 kg change in platinum-group catalyst powder — economically nonsensical.

#### (b) Laspeyres Price Index (Base-Year Weighted)

Weights each material by its *base-year* consumption (`Q₀`). This is the **most widely used** index in engineering economics because the base-year basket is fixed and known.

$$ L_{n/0} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{N} P_{n,i} \cdot Q_{0,i}}{\sum_{i=1}^{N} P_{0,i} \cdot Q_{0,i}} \times 100 $$

**Operational logic:** The denominator `Σ P₀·Q₀` represents the *base-year basket cost*; the numerator `Σ Pₙ·Q₀` represents what that same physical basket would cost at current prices. The ratio expresses pure *price effect*, holding quantities constant.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Laspeyres biases UPWARD** in inflationary environments because consumers (and chemical plants) typically *substitute away* from materials whose prices rise faster than the average. The fixed base-year basket cannot capture this substitution response.

#### (c) Paasche Price Index (Current-Year Weighted)

Uses *current-year* consumption (`Qₙ`) as weights. Captures the actual present-day purchasing pattern.

$$ P_{n/0} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{N} P_{n,i} \cdot Q_{n,i}}{\sum_{i=1}^{N} P_{0,i} \cdot Q_{n,i}} \times 100 $$

**Operational logic:** The denominator `Σ P₀·Qₙ` asks: "What would the *current* basket have cost at *base-year* prices?" This counterfactual is hypothetical and requires continuous quantity data, which is operationally difficult to obtain.

> [!NOTE]
> **Paasche biases DOWNWARD** in inflationary environments because the rising weights are assigned to materials whose prices have grown slowest (substitution bias in reverse).

#### (d) Fisher's Ideal Index (Geometric Mean)

The mathematically elegant compromise that takes the geometric mean of Laspeyres and Paasche. It satisfies the **time-reversal test** and **factor-reversal test** — two axiomatic properties no other formula satisfies simultaneously.

$$ F_{n/0} = \sqrt{L_{n/0} \times P_{n/0}} $$

#### (e) Marshall–Edgeworth Index

A weighted average of Laspeyres and Paasche, using the *arithmetic mean* of `Q₀` and `Qₙ` as the weighting vector.

$$ M_{n/0} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{N} P_{n,i} \cdot (Q_{0,i} + Q_{n,i})}{\sum_{i=1}^{N} P_{0,i} \cdot (Q_{0,i} + Q_{n,i})} \times 100 $$

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — High-Yield Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Symbol Convention | When to Use |
|---|---------|-------------------|-------------|
| 1 | $I_{n/0} = \dfrac{\sum P_{n,i}}{\sum P_{0,i}} \times 100$ | Simple Aggregative | Quick screening, equal-weight assumption |
| 2 | $L_{n/0} = \dfrac{\sum P_{n,i}\,Q_{0,i}}{\sum P_{0,i}\,Q_{0,i}} \times 100$ | Laspeyres | Most common; only `Q₀` data needed |
| 3 | $P_{n/0} = \dfrac{\sum P_{n,i}\,Q_{n,i}}{\sum P_{0,i}\,Q_{n,i}} \times 100$ | Paasche | When current consumption is available |
| 4 | $F_{n/0} = \sqrt{L_{n/0} \times P_{n/0}}$ | Fisher Ideal | Highest statistical rigor; satisfies both reversal tests |
| 5 | $M_{n/0} = \dfrac{\sum P_{n,i}(Q_{0,i}+Q_{n,i})}{\sum P_{0,i}(Q_{0,i}+Q_{n,i})} \times 100$ | Marshall–Edgeworth | Compromise between (2) and (3) |
| 6 | $C_{\text{escalated}} = C_{\text{base}} \times \dfrac{I_{\text{current}}}{I_{\text{base}}}$ | Cost escalation | Core application for chemical plant estimates |

### 2.4 Real-World Utility in Chemical Plant Economics

**Use Case 1 — Capital Cost Escalation:** If a 2020 feasibility study quotes a reactor cost of ₹50 lakhs, and the Marshall & Swift Chemical Equipment Index (a composite index, with material sub-component) has moved from **158.4 (2020)** to **192.7 (2026)**, the escalated cost is:

$$ C_{2026} = 50 \times \frac{192.7}{158.4} = ₹60.82 \text{ lakhs} $$

**Use Case 2 — Annual Operating Cost Revision:** A fertilizer plant consuming **10,000 tons/year** of naphtha at ₹45/kg in 2020 must revise its operating budget for 2026. Using a Petrochemical Material Index that rose from 100 → 168, the projected unit price becomes:

$$ P_{2026} = 45 \times \frac{168}{100} = ₹75.6 \text{ per kg} $$

**Use Case 3 — Contract Escalation Clause:** Long-term offtake agreements routinely embed formulas like:

$$ P_{\text{adjust}} = P_{\text{contract}} \times \left[ 0.65 \times \frac{\text{MCI}_{\text{current}}}{\text{MCI}_{\text{base}}} + 0.35 \times \frac{\text{CPI}_{\text{current}}}{\text{CPI}_{\text{base}}} \right] $$

The **65% weight on MCI** in such clauses reflects the material-intensity of chemical production.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Field Relevance:** Material Cost Indices are foundational inputs to the *Six-Tenths Rule*, *Lang Factor Method*, and *Hand Factor Method* of capital cost estimation when historic data must be scaled to present-day values. They are also embedded in the *Working Capital* computation and *Operating Cost* projections of any rigorous Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) analysis.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example — Constructing a Material Cost Index for a Petrochemical Complex

**Problem Statement (KTU-Style Numerical):**

A petrochemical complex consumes four major raw materials — **Naphtha (N)**, **Caustic Soda (C)**, **Sulfuric Acid (S)**, and **Polymer Catalyst (P)** — in the following quantities and unit prices for the base year 2019 and the current year 2024:

| Material | `Q₀` (base yr, tons) | `P₀` (₹/ton) | `Qₙ` (current yr, tons) | `Pₙ` (₹/ton) |
|----------|----------------------|--------------|--------------------------|--------------|
| Naphtha (N) | 12,000 | 35,000 | 11,500 | 58,000 |
| Caustic Soda (C) | 800 | 22,000 | 850 | 31,000 |
| Sulfuric Acid (S) | 1,500 | 8,000 | 1,400 | 12,500 |
| Polymer Catalyst (P) | 25 | 1,20,000 | 30 | 1,65,000 |

**Required:** Compute (a) Laspeyres MCI, (b) Paasche MCI, (c) Fisher's Ideal MCI, (d) Marshall–Edgeworth MCI for 2024 with base 2019.

---

### 3.2 Step-by-Step Solution — Part (a): Laspeyres Index

**Step 1 — Build the numerator table (Pₙ × Q₀):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{n,N} \cdot Q_{0,N} &= 58{,}000 \times 12{,}000 = 6.960 \times 10^{8} \;\text{₹} \\
P_{n,C} \cdot Q_{0,C} &= 31{,}000 \times 800 = 2.480 \times 10^{7} \;\text{₹} \\
P_{n,S} \cdot Q_{0,S} &= 12{,}500 \times 1{,}500 = 1.875 \times 10^{7} \;\text{₹} \\
P_{n,P} \cdot Q_{0,P} &= 1{,}65{,}000 \times 25 = 4.125 \times 10^{6} \;\text{₹}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Sum of numerator:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum P_{n,i} \cdot Q_{0,i} &= (6.960 \times 10^{8}) + (2.480 \times 10^{7}) + (1.875 \times 10^{7}) + (4.125 \times 10^{6}) \\
&= 696.00 + 24.80 + 18.75 + 4.125 \\
&= 743.675 \times 10^{6} \;\text{₹ (i.e., ₹74.3675 crores)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Build the denominator table (P₀ × Q₀):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{0,N} \cdot Q_{0,N} &= 35{,}000 \times 12{,}000 = 4.200 \times 10^{8} \\
P_{0,C} \cdot Q_{0,C} &= 22{,}000 \times 800 = 1.760 \times 10^{7} \\
P_{0,S} \cdot Q_{0,S} &= 8{,}000 \times 1{,}500 = 1.200 \times 10^{7} \\
P_{0,P} \cdot Q_{0,P} &= 1{,}20{,}000 \times 25 = 3.000 \times 10^{6}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Sum of denominator:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum P_{0,i} \cdot Q_{0,i} &= 420.00 + 17.60 + 12.00 + 3.00 = 452.60 \times 10^{6} \;\text{₹}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply Laspeyres formula:**

$$
L_{n/0} = \frac{743.675}{452.60} \times 100 = 164.31
$$

---

### 3.3 Step-by-Step Solution — Part (b): Paasche Index

**Step 1 — Build the numerator (Pₙ × Qₙ) — note: this equals the *actual 2024 expenditure*:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{n,N} \cdot Q_{n,N} &= 58{,}000 \times 11{,}500 = 6.670 \times 10^{8} \\
P_{n,C} \cdot Q_{n,C} &= 31{,}000 \times 850 = 2.635 \times 10^{7} \\
P_{n,S} \cdot Q_{n,S} &= 12{,}500 \times 1{,}400 = 1.750 \times 10^{7} \\
P_{n,P} \cdot Q_{n,P} &= 1{,}65{,}000 \times 30 = 4.950 \times 10^{6}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Sum:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum P_{n,i} \cdot Q_{n,i} &= 667.00 + 26.35 + 17.50 + 4.95 = 715.80 \times 10^{6} \;\text{₹}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Build the denominator (P₀ × Qₙ) — counterfactual: "what would 2024 quantities have cost at 2019 prices?":**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{0,N} \cdot Q_{n,N} &= 35{,}000 \times 11{,}500 = 4.025 \times 10^{8} \\
P_{0,C} \cdot Q_{n,C} &= 22{,}000 \times 850 = 1.870 \times 10^{7} \\
P_{0,S} \cdot Q_{n,S} &= 8{,}000 \times 1{,}400 = 1.120 \times 10^{7} \\
P_{0,P} \cdot Q_{n,P} &= 1{,}20{,}000 \times 30 = 3.600 \times 10^{6}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Sum:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum P_{0,i} \cdot Q_{n,i} &= 402.50 + 18.70 + 11.20 + 3.60 = 436.00 \times 10^{6} \;\text{₹}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply Paasche formula:**

$$
P_{n/0} = \frac{715.80}{436.00} \times 100 = 164.17
$$

---

### 3.4 Step-by-Step Solution — Part (c): Fisher's Ideal Index

$$
F_{n/0} = \sqrt{L_{n/0} \times P_{n/0}} = \sqrt{164.31 \times 164.17}
$$

**Multiplication step:**

$$
164.31 \times 164.17 = 26{,}976.78
$$

**Square-root step:**

$$
F_{n/0} = \sqrt{26{,}976.78} = 164.24
$$

---

### 3.5 Step-by-Step Solution — Part (d): Marshall–Edgeworth Index

**Step 1 — Compute average quantities `(Q₀ + Qₙ)/2`:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\bar{Q}_N &= (12{,}000 + 11{,}500)/2 = 11{,}750 \\
\bar{Q}_C &= (800 + 850)/2 = 825 \\
\bar{Q}_S &= (1{,}500 + 1{,}400)/2 = 1{,}450 \\
\bar{Q}_P &= (25 + 30)/2 = 27.5
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Numerator `Σ Pₙ · Q̄`:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
&= (58{,}000 \times 11{,}750) + (31{,}000 \times 825) + (12{,}500 \times 1{,}450) + (1{,}65{,}000 \times 27.5) \\
&= 6.815 \times 10^{8} + 2.558 \times 10^{7} + 1.8125 \times 10^{7} + 4.5375 \times 10^{6} \\
&= 681.50 + 25.575 + 18.125 + 4.5375 = 729.7375 \times 10^{6} \;\text{₹}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Denominator `Σ P₀ · Q̄`:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
&= (35{,}000 \times 11{,}750) + (22{,}000 \times 825) + (8{,}000 \times 1{,}450) + (1{,}20{,}000 \times 27.5) \\
&= 4.1125 \times 10^{8} + 1.815 \times 10^{7} + 1.16 \times 10^{7} + 3.30 \times 10^{6} \\
&= 411.25 + 18.15 + 11.60 + 3.30 = 444.30 \times 10^{6} \;\text{₹}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply formula:**

$$
M_{n/0} = \frac{729.7375}{444.30} \times 100 = 164.24
$$

---

### 3.6 Comparative Analytical Matrix — All Four Indices at a Glance

| Index Family | Computed Value (2024 / 2019 = 100) | Interpretation | Bias Direction |
|--------------|------------------------------------|----------------|----------------|
| Laspeyres | **164.31** | Cost escalation at fixed base-year basket | Upward |
| Paasche | **164.17** | Cost escalation at current-year basket | Downward |
| Fisher | **164.24** | Geometric mean compromise | Approximately unbiased |
| Marshall–Edgeworth | **164.24** | Arithmetic-mean compromise | Approximately unbiased |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** Between 2019 and 2024, the *overall material cost level* of this petrochemical complex has risen by approximately **64.24%** under the most statistically robust index. In nominal (rupee) terms, an annual material budget of ₹100 crores in 2019 must be scaled to ₹164.24 crores in 2024 to maintain the same *real* purchasing power.

### 3.7 Verification by Cost Escalation Identity

**Sanity Check using the cost-escalation formula:**

$$
C_{\text{escalated}} = C_{\text{base}} \times \frac{I_{\text{current}}}{I_{\text{base}}}
$$

For ₹100 crores of base-year material expenditure:

$$
C_{2024} = 100 \times \frac{164.24}{100} = ₹164.24 \text{ crores} \;\checkmark
$$

This matches the *direct calculation* obtained by summing actual 2024 expenditure (`₹715.80 million = ₹71.58 crores`) divided by the *hypothetical 2019-price cost of 2024 quantities* (`₹436.00 million = ₹43.60 crores`), giving `₹71.58 / ₹43.60 × 100 = 164.17%` (Paasche). The small variation between Fisher and Paasche values (164.24 vs 164.17) arises because the Fisher index incorporates both the actual and the counterfactual expenditure data, giving it greater informational content.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Material Cost Index Computation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Data Acquisition] --> B[Base-Year Data 2019]
    A --> C[Current-Year Data 2024]
    B --> D[Quantity Vector Q0]
    B --> E[Price Vector P0]
    C --> F[Quantity Vector Qn]
    C --> G[Price Vector Pn]
    D --> H[Index Engine]
    E --> H
    F --> H
    G --> H
    H --> H1[Laspeyres Module]
    H --> H2[Paasche Module]
    H --> H3[Fisher Module]
    H --> H4[Marshall-Edgeworth Module]
    H1 --> I[Numerical Output Table]
    H2 --> I
    H3 --> I
    H4 --> I
    I --> J[Decision Support Layer]
    J --> J1[Capital Cost Escalation]
    J --> J2[Operating Budget Revision]
    J --> J3[Contract Price Adjustment]
    J --> J4[Feasibility Re-validation]
```

### 4.2 Decision Logic — Which Index to Choose?

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    K[Start: Choose MCI Formula] --> L{Is current-year quantity data Qn available?}
    L -- No --> M[Use Laspeyres]
    L -- Yes --> N{Is statistical rigor required for audit or contract?}
    N -- No --> O{Is substitution effect expected to be strong?}
    O -- Yes --> P[Use Fisher Ideal]
    O -- No --> Q[Use Marshall-Edgeworth]
    N -- Yes --> R[Use Fisher Ideal]
    M --> S[End]
    P --> S
    Q --> S
    R --> S
```

### 4.3 Material Cost Index Flow in a Chemical Plant Project Lifecycle

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    StepA[Feasibility Study Year T] --> StepB[Base-Year Material Cost Estimate]
    StepB --> StepC[Apply MCI Inflation Factor]
    StepC --> StepD[Year T Plus 3: Detailed Engineering]
    StepD --> StepE[Refine MCI with Latest Quotes]
    StepE --> StepF[Year T Plus 5: Procurement and Construction]
    StepF --> StepG[Escalated Project Cost Realized]
    StepG --> StepH[Operations Phase Annual Budget Recalibration]
```

### 4.4 Schematic — Information Content of the Four Index Families

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Las[Laspeyres Index] --> Info1[Uses Pn and P0 with Q0 as fixed weight]
    Pas[Paasche Index] --> Info2[Uses Pn and P0 with Qn as fixed weight]
    Las --> Fisher[Fisher Ideal Index]
    Pas --> Fisher
    Fisher --> Stat[Time-Reversal Test Satisfied]
    Fisher --> Fac[Factor-Reversal Test Satisfied]
    Las --> Marsh[Marshall Edgeworth Index]
    Pas --> Marsh
    Marsh --> Linear[Linear Mean Compromise]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Schematics:** The arrows depict *data dependencies* between modules, not physical flows. Each computation block is logically independent but draws from the same primitive data layer (`P₀`, `Pₙ`, `Q₀`, `Qₙ`). The **Decision Support Layer** in Section 4.1 represents the operational deployment of the index number in capital budgeting and supply-chain decisions.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (2-Mark and 3-Mark Short-Answer Bank)

**Question 1 [3 Marks] — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]`**

> **Q:** Define the term **"Cost Index"** and explain its significance in chemical engineering project economics. Distinguish between a **Capital Cost Index** and a **Material Cost Index** with one example each.

**Model Answer:**

A **Cost Index** is a dimensionless number representing the relative change in cost of a defined basket of items between a base period and a current period, with the base period normalized to **100**. Its significance lies in enabling time-value adjustment of estimates made in different years.

- **Capital Cost Index** tracks equipment, fabrication, and construction labor costs. *Example:* Marshall & Swift Equipment Cost Index (M&S).
- **Material Cost Index** tracks raw-material and feedstock costs. *Example:* Nelson–Farrar Refinery Construction Index (material sub-component) or a custom naphtha-caustic-acid-catalyst basket.

---

**Question 2 [3 Marks] — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]`**

> **Q:** State and briefly justify the **Laspeyres Price Index formula**. Why is it biased upward in inflationary conditions?

**Model Answer:**

$$ L_{n/0} = \frac{\sum P_{n,i}\,Q_{0,i}}{\sum P_{0,i}\,Q_{0,i}} \times 100 $$

It is the most widely used index because only *base-year quantities* `Q₀` are needed as weights — these are typically documented in original feasibility reports and rarely change.

**Upward bias reason:** The fixed `Q₀` basket cannot capture *consumer/producer substitution* — when a material becomes expensive, buyers shift to cheaper alternatives, reducing its weight in real expenditure. Since Laspeyres stubbornly retains the old high weight, it overstates the true cost-of-living increase.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14-Mark Module-Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model]`**

> **Q(a) [7 Marks]:** A chlor-alkali plant consumes four major feedstocks: Brine, Caustic, Chlorine Gas (captive), and Membrane Electrode. Construct the **Laspeyres Material Cost Index** for the year 2025 with base 2020 using the data given below. Comment on the cost escalation observed.

| Material | `Q₀` (2020, tons) | `P₀` (₹/ton, 2020) | `Pₙ` (₹/ton, 2025) |
|----------|--------------------|--------------------|--------------------|
| Brine | 25,000 | 1,200 | 1,750 |
| Caustic | 1,200 | 28,000 | 39,500 |
| Chlorine (captive) | 900 | 18,000 | 26,000 |
| Membrane Electrode | 40 | 2,50,000 | 3,80,000 |

> **Q(b) [7 Marks]:** If the original 2020 annual material budget was ₹12 crores, estimate the **escalated 2025 budget** using the computed index. Discuss how the choice of Laspeyres (vs Paasche) would affect this estimate.

**Model Solution Q(a):**

Numerator `Σ Pₙ · Q₀`:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Brine:} &\quad 1{,}750 \times 25{,}000 = 4.375 \times 10^{7} \\
\text{Caustic:} &\quad 39{,}500 \times 1{,}200 = 4.740 \times 10^{7} \\
\text{Chlorine:} &\quad 26{,}000 \times 900 = 2.340 \times 10^{7} \\
\text{Membrane:} &\quad 3{,}80{,}000 \times 40 = 1.520 \times 10^{7} \\
\text{Sum} &\quad = 12.975 \times 10^{7} = ₹12.975 \text{ crores}
\end{aligned}
$$

Denominator `Σ P₀ · Q₀`:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Brine:} &\quad 1{,}200 \times 25{,}000 = 3.000 \times 10^{7} \\
\text{Caustic:} &\quad 28{,}000 \times 1{,}200 = 3.360 \times 10^{7} \\
\text{Chlorine:} &\quad 18{,}000 \times 900 = 1.620 \times 10^{7} \\
\text{Membrane:} &\quad 2{,}50{,}000 \times 40 = 1.000 \times 10^{7} \\
\text{Sum} &\quad = 8.980 \times 10^{7} = ₹8.980 \text{ crores}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
L_{2025/2020} = \frac{12.975}{8.980} \times 100 = 144.49
$$

**Comment:** The chlor-alkali plant's material cost level has risen by approximately **44.5%** over five years — a compound annual growth rate (CAGR) of:

$$
\text{CAGR} = \left(\frac{144.49}{100}\right)^{1/5} - 1 = 7.63\% \text{ per annum}
$$

This significantly exceeds typical headline inflation (4–5%), reflecting the energy- and capital-intensive nature of chlor-alkali production.

**Model Solution Q(b):**

$$
C_{2025} = C_{2020} \times \frac{L_{2025}}{L_{2020}} = 12 \times \frac{144.49}{100} = ₹17.34 \text{ crores}
$$

The choice of index affects this estimate as follows:

- If **Paasche** were used (assuming buyers substitute away from costlier membrane electrodes in 2025), the denominator `Σ P₀ · Qₙ` would be smaller (because Qₙ for membrane < Q₀), so the index value would be *lower* than 144.49, yielding a lower escalated budget.
- If **Fisher** were used, the estimate would be the geometric mean — typically the most defensible number for board-level financial reporting.
- **Practical recommendation:** For a chlor-alkali plant with strong technological inertia (membrane replacement cycle is fixed at 5–7 years), the Laspeyres assumption of fixed `Q₀` is realistic and the estimate of ₹17.34 crores is conservative and prudent.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]`**

> **Q(a) [7 Marks]:** Explain the **time-reversal test** and **factor-reversal test**. Show analytically why the **Fisher Ideal Index** uniquely satisfies both, while the Laspeyres and Paasche indices satisfy at most one each.

> **Q(b) [7 Marks]:** For a petroleum refinery's Material Cost Index problem, the Laspeyres index for 2024 (base 2019) was computed as **172.5** and the Paasche index as **168.4**. Compute the Fisher Ideal Index and explain the economic interpretation of why the Fisher value lies *between* the two.

**Model Solution Q(a):**

**Time-Reversal Test (TRT):** A "good" index should be symmetric in time — i.e., if `I_{n/0} = k`, then `I_{0/n} = 1/k` (in decimal form). Algebraically:

$$
I_{n/0} \times I_{0/n} = 1
$$

**Laspeyres check:**

$$
L_{n/0} \times L_{0/n} = \frac{\sum P_n Q_0}{\sum P_0 Q_0} \times \frac{\sum P_0 Q_n}{\sum P_n Q_n}
$$

The two denominators are different (`Q₀` vs `Qₙ`), so the product is **not equal to 1** in general. **Laspeyres fails TRT.**

**Paasche check:**

$$
P_{n/0} \times P_{0/n} = \frac{\sum P_n Q_n}{\sum P_0 Q_n} \times \frac{\sum P_0 Q_0}{\sum P_n Q_0}
$$

Again, denominators differ. **Paasche fails TRT.**

**Fisher check:**

$$
F_{n/0} \times F_{0/n} = \sqrt{\frac{\sum P_n Q_0}{\sum P_0 Q_0} \cdot \frac{\sum P_n Q_n}{\sum P_0 Q_n}} \times \sqrt{\frac{\sum P_0 Q_n}{\sum P_n Q_n} \cdot \frac{\sum P_0 Q_0}{\sum P_n Q_0}}
$$

The cross-terms cancel inside the square root, giving exactly **1**. **Fisher passes TRT.** ✓

**Factor-Reversal Test (FRT):** The product of the price index and the corresponding quantity index should equal the *value ratio* `Σ PₙQₙ / Σ P₀Q₀`.

$$
I^{P}_{n/0} \times I^{Q}_{n/0} = \frac{\sum P_n Q_n}{\sum P_0 Q_0}
$$

Only the **Fisher Ideal Index** satisfies this identity, because it is constructed as a symmetric geometric mean that respects the duality between price and quantity measures. **Laspeyres and Paasche each satisfy at most one of these two axiomatic tests.** ✓

**Model Solution Q(b):**

Given: `L_{2024/2019} = 172.5` and `P_{2024/2019} = 168.4`.

**Step 1 — Multiplication:**

$$
172.5 \times 168.4 = 29{,}049.00
$$

**Step 2 — Square root:**

$$
F_{2024/2019} = \sqrt{29{,}049.00} = 170.44
$$

**Economic Interpretation:**

The Fisher Ideal Index (170.44) lies between Laspeyres (172.5) and Paasche (168.4) by construction. The economic meaning of this spread:

- The Laspeyres value of 172.5 **overstates** true cost escalation because it presumes the refinery kept buying the 2019 basket unchanged — a counterfactual that ignores process optimization (e.g., shift to heavier crude slates, alternative catalysts, energy recovery).
- The Paasche value of 168.4 **understates** true escalation because it re-weights to favor materials whose prices grew least — a substitution bias.
- The Fisher value of 170.44 is the **statistically balanced** estimate — neither anchored to outdated `Q₀` weights nor to the latest substitution pattern, but to their geometric mean. This is the value most often cited in techno-economic reports presented to senior management, lenders, and audit committees.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Commonly Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to multiply by 100** — index values are conventionally reported as `164.31`, not `1.6431`. Students who omit the `× 100` step lose 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Unit mismatch in `Q₀ × P₀`** — ensure both `Q₀` and `Pₙ` use consistent units (e.g., both in tons and ₹/ton). Mixing tons with kg or $/kg with ₹/kg silently corrupts the answer.
> 3. **Confusing Laspeyres with Paasche weights** — remember: Laspeyres uses **fixed base-year `Q₀`**; Paasche uses **current-year `Qₙ`**. This is the single most tested discriminator in KTU 2024 exams.
> 4. **Omitting the "factor-reversal test" rationale** when justifying Fisher — examiners allocate 1–2 marks specifically for the *axiomatic justification*, not just the formula.
> 5. **Not providing economic interpretation** — the index is not just a number; KTU valuation scripts reward a 1–2 line comment on the *cost escalation meaning* in the plant's context.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Material Cost Indices**

- ✅ A **Cost Index** is a normalized (base = **100**) measure of relative price change of a defined basket between a base year and a current year.
- ✅ A **Material Cost Index (MCI)** specifically tracks raw-material/feedstock prices — crucial in chemical industry because materials constitute **60–80%** of operating cost.
- ✅ **Laspeyres** uses fixed base-year weights `Q₀` → **upward biased** in inflation, but most commonly used because `Q₀` data is reliably available.
- ✅ **Paasche** uses current-year weights `Qₙ` → **downward biased**, theoretically appealing but data-hungry.
- ✅ **Fisher Ideal Index** = `√(Laspeyres × Paasche)` — the only formula satisfying **both** the time-reversal and factor-reversal tests simultaneously.
- ✅ **Marshall–Edgeworth** uses arithmetic mean of `Q₀` and `Qₙ` as weights — a linear compromise between Laspeyres and Paasche.
- ✅ **Cost escalation formula:** `C_escalated = C_base × (I_current / I_base)` — the most commercially deployed application.
- ✅ MCI is used in: (i) Capital cost escalation of historic equipment data, (ii) Annual operating budget revision, (iii) Long-term contract price-adjustment clauses (typically weighted 0.5–0.7 on MCI, balance on CPI/labor index), (iv) DCF-based project feasibility re-validation.
- ✅ Always report the **base year explicitly** (e.g., "Laspeyres MCI 2024/2019 = 164.31") — vague index numbers lose marks.
- ✅ In KTU 2024 exams, expect either a **3-mark definitional** question (Laspeyres formula + bias direction) or a **7+7 numerical** (compute index from a 4–5 material basket, then apply to budget escalation).
- ✅ For numerical problems, organize the work into a **denominator table** and **numerator table** — this structured presentation earns full method marks even if a final arithmetic slip occurs.
- ✅ Memorize the **bias directions** (Laspeyres ↑, Paasche ↓, Fisher unbiased) — KTU examiners test this in Part A every cycle.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Labour Cost Indices
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT757 - ECONOMICS AND MANAGEMENT OF CHEMICAL INDUSTRIES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Labour Cost Indices

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Labour Cost Index (LCI)** is a specialized **composite index number** designed to measure the proportional change, over time, in the average cost of employing labour (direct and indirect workforce) within a specific industrial sector, occupational category, or national economy. In the context of chemical process industries, it quantifies how labour expenditure evolves relative to a defined **base year (= 100)** and acts as a critical barometer for **cost engineering, project appraisal, and feasibility analysis**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** *Labour Cost Indices are weighted index numbers that reflect the temporal variation in the total cost incurred by an organization (or industry) for employing human resources. They combine wage rates, fringe benefits, productivity adjustments, and statutory levies into a single normalized metric.*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Salary Thermometer":**
> Imagine a thermometer that does not measure temperature but rather the *cost of employing people*. In 2020 (the base year), the thermometer reads 100 °C-equivalent units. By 2024, the wage bill of a fertilizer plant has increased by 18% — the thermometer now reads 118. This thermometer is the **Labour Cost Index**. Just as a weather forecaster cannot predict climate without temperature data, a chemical plant manager cannot forecast operating costs, plan EPC tenders, or escalate project budgets without an LCI. It is the *engineer’s salary thermometer*.

### 1.3 Components Constituting Labour Cost

The total **Labour Cost (LC)** is the sum of multiple contributory elements:

- **Direct Wages (DW):** Basic pay, dearness allowance, overtime.
- **Fringe Benefits (FB):** Medical, housing, transport subsidies.
- **Statutory Contributions (SC):** Provident Fund, ESI, gratuity provisions.
- **Bonus & Incentives (BI):** Production bonuses, profit-sharing.
- **Training & Welfare Costs (TW):** Onboarding, PPE, canteen.
- **Productivity Adjustment (PA):** Output-per-worker-normalized factor.

---

$$LC = DW + FB + SC + BI + TW + PA$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** Labour Cost Indices are *not* simple wage indices — they are **total employment cost indices**. A 10% rise in basic pay may correspond to a 14% rise in total labour cost due to compounding statutory liabilities.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Framework

The construction of a Labour Cost Index follows the classical theory of **index numbers**, employing one of two methodological foundations:

**(A) Laspeyres-Type Labour Cost Index** — fixed base-year weights.
**(B) Paasche-Type Labour Cost Index** — current-year weights.

In industrial practice, the **Laspeyres form** dominates because it requires data only from the base year, simplifying long-term project escalation calculations.

### 2.2 Laspeyres Labour Cost Index

For *n* occupational categories (e.g., skilled, semi-skilled, unskilled, supervisor, engineer), the index is:

---

$$L_{Laspeyres} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} W_{i,0} \cdot C_{i,t}}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} W_{i,0} \cdot C_{i,0}} \times 100$$

---

Where:
- $W_{i,0}$ = Number of workers (or man-hours) in category *i* in the base year.
- $C_{i,t}$ = Average labour cost per worker in category *i* in the current year *t*.
- $C_{i,0}$ = Average labour cost per worker in category *i* in the base year.

### 2.3 Paasche Labour Cost Index

---

$$L_{Paasche} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} W_{i,t} \cdot C_{i,t}}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} W_{i,t} \cdot C_{i,0}} \times 100$$

---

### 2.4 Fisher’s Ideal Labour Cost Index

The geometric mean of Laspeyres and Paasche — used when both base and current weights are reliable.

---

$$L_{Fisher} = \sqrt{L_{Laspeyres} \times L_{Paasche}}$$

---

### 2.5 Weighted Aggregate Method (Practical Industry Form)

Often chemical companies compute a single per-capita weighted LCI:

---

$$LCI = \frac{\sum (w_i \cdot r_i)}{\sum w_i} \times 100$$

---

Where $w_i$ = weight (number of employees) and $r_i$ = index of wage/cost for grade *i*.

### 2.6 Real-Wage Adjustment (for Inflation-Corrected LCIs)

To measure *purchasing power* of wages, the **Real Labour Cost Index (RLCI)** is computed:

---

$$RLCI = \frac{LCI_{nominal}}{CPI} \times 100$$

---

Where CPI = Consumer Price Index for the corresponding year.

### 2.7 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Use Case |
|---|--------------|----------|----------|
| 1 | Total Labour Cost | $LC = DW + FB + SC + BI + TW + PA$ | Cost-sheet preparation |
| 2 | Laspeyres LCI | $\frac{\sum W_{0} C_{t}}{\sum W_{0} C_{0}} \times 100$ | Long-term wage escalation |
| 3 | Paasche LCI | $\frac{\sum W_{t} C_{t}}{\sum W_{t} C_{0}} \times 100$ | Current-year weighting |
| 4 | Fisher LCI | $\sqrt{L_{L} \cdot L_{P}}$ | Balanced hybrid estimate |
| 5 | Weighted Aggregate | $\frac{\sum w_i r_i}{\sum w_i} \times 100$ | Plant-level LCI |
| 6 | Real LCI | $\frac{LCI_{nominal}}{CPI} \times 100$ | Inflation-adjusted |
| 7 | Per Capita Labour Cost | $PCLC = \frac{\text{Total Labour Cost}}{\text{No. of Workers}}$ | Benchmarking |
| 8 | Productivity-Adjusted LCI | $LCI_{adj} = LCI \times \sqrt{\frac{P_{0}}{P_{t}}}$ | True unit labour cost |

### 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

In a **petrochemical EPC project**, the original capital cost estimate is escalated year-on-year using labour cost indices to predict the **final installed cost**. For instance, if a refinery in 2020 was estimated at ₹1200 crore with labour cost index = 100, then by 2025 with index = 132, the escalated cost is:

---

$$C_{2025} = C_{2020} \times \frac{LCI_{2025}}{LCI_{2020}} = 1200 \times \frac{132}{100} = 1584 \text{ crore}$$

---

This makes LCIs indispensable in **process plant feasibility reports, contractor bidding, and long-term O&M budgeting**.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivative Logic: From Wage Index to Total Labour Cost Index

**Step 1:** Start with a single-grade wage index.

For grade *i*:

$$w_i = \frac{C_{i,t}}{C_{i,0}} \times 100$$

This yields an *unweighted* wage ratio.

**Step 2:** Introduce population (man-hours) as the weighting vector $W_{i,0}$.

Multiply each wage ratio by its corresponding base-year weight:

$$\text{Weighted contribution} = W_{i,0} \cdot \frac{C_{i,t}}{C_{i,0}}$$

**Step 3:** Aggregate across all grades.

$$\sum_{i=1}^{n} W_{i,0} \cdot \frac{C_{i,t}}{C_{i,0}}$$

**Step 4:** Normalize by the total base-year labour cost denominator.

$$\sum_{i=1}^{n} W_{i,0} \cdot \frac{C_{i,t}}{C_{i,0}} \div \sum_{i=1}^{n} W_{i,0}$$

**Step 5:** Express as a percentage (multiply by 100).

Combining Steps 3–5:

---

$$L_{Laspeyres} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} W_{i,0} C_{i,t}}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} W_{i,0} C_{i,0}} \times 100$$

---

**Conversion Logic:** The numerator aggregates the *current cost* paid to the same workforce composition as the base year, while the denominator represents the *base cost* of that same composition. The ratio quantifies the *pure cost escalation*, holding employment structure constant.

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (Chemical Plant Context)

> **Problem:** A polymer plant has the following labour data. Compute the **Laspeyres Labour Cost Index**.

| Grade | No. of Workers (Base, $W_0$) | Wage/worker Base (₹/yr, $C_0$) | Wage/worker Current (₹/yr, $C_t$) |
|-------|------------------------------|--------------------------------|------------------------------------|
| Skilled | 200 | 600,000 | 720,000 |
| Semi-Skilled | 350 | 360,000 | 432,000 |
| Unskilled | 400 | 240,000 | 300,000 |
| Supervisor | 50 | 900,000 | 1,080,000 |

**Solution:**

Step 1: Compute base-year labour cost denominator ($W_0 \cdot C_0$):

- Skilled: $200 \times 600{,}000 = 1.20 \times 10^{8}$
- Semi-Skilled: $350 \times 360{,}000 = 1.26 \times 10^{8}$
- Unskilled: $400 \times 240{,}000 = 0.96 \times 10^{8}$
- Supervisor: $50 \times 900{,}000 = 0.45 \times 10^{8}$

$$\sum W_0 C_0 = (1.20 + 1.26 + 0.96 + 0.45) \times 10^8 = 3.87 \times 10^8$$

Step 2: Compute current-year labour cost numerator ($W_0 \cdot C_t$):

- Skilled: $200 \times 720{,}000 = 1.44 \times 10^{8}$
- Semi-Skilled: $350 \times 432{,}000 = 1.512 \times 10^{8}$
- Unskilled: $400 \times 300{,}000 = 1.20 \times 10^{8}$
- Supervisor: $50 \times 1{,}080{,}000 = 0.54 \times 10^{8}$

$$\sum W_0 C_t = (1.44 + 1.512 + 1.20 + 0.54) \times 10^8 = 4.692 \times 10^8$$

Step 3: Compute the index:

---

$$L_{Laspeyres} = \frac{4.692 \times 10^8}{3.87 \times 10^8} \times 100 = 121.24$$

---

**Interpretation:** The total labour cost of the polymer plant has risen by **21.24%** over the base year.

### 3.3 Productivity-Adjusted (Unit Labour Cost) Derivation

**Step 1:** Define the **Output Index (OI):**

$$OI = \frac{\text{Output}_t}{\text{Output}_0} \times 100$$

**Step 2:** Define the **Nominal Labour Cost Index (LCI):** as derived above.

**Step 3:** Compute **Unit Labour Cost Index (ULCI):**

---

$$ULCI = \frac{LCI}{OI} \times 100$$

---

**Step 4:** Real-unit labour cost corrected for inflation:

$$ULCI_{real} = \frac{LCI}{OI \cdot CPI/100} \times 100$$

**Engineering Meaning:** A rising LCI but stagnant OI indicates declining labour productivity — a red flag in chemical plant benchmarking.

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Property | Laspeyres LCI | Paasche LCI | Fisher LCI |
|----------|---------------|-------------|------------|
| Weights used | Base year | Current year | Geometric mean of both |
| Data requirement | Historical & current | Current & historical | Both |
| Tendency | **Overestimates** inflation | **Underestimates** inflation | Balanced |
| Use in EPC escalation | **Most common** | Rare | Theoretical benchmark |
| Time-reversal test | Fails | Fails | **Passes** |
| Factor-reversal test | Fails | Fails | **Passes** |
| Recommended for KTU | Yes | Yes | Yes |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Labour Cost Index Computation Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Labour Data Acquisition] --> B[Data Classification by Grade]
    B --> C1[Skilled Workers]
    B --> C2[Semi-Skilled Workers]
    B --> C3[Unskilled Workers]
    B --> C4[Supervisors and Engineers]
    C1 --> D[Weight Assignment W0]
    C2 --> D
    C3 --> D
    C4 --> D
    D --> E[Base Year Cost Aggregation W0C0]
    E --> F[Current Year Cost Aggregation W0Ct]
    F --> G[Laspeyres Formula Application]
    G --> H[Output Index OI Computation]
    H --> I[Unit Labour Cost Index ULCI]
    I --> J[Inflation Correction via CPI]
    J --> K[Final Adjusted LCI Report]
    K --> L[Decision Layer: EPC Escalation or HR Planning]
```

**Description of Flow:**
- **Layer 1 (Data Ingestion):** HR & Payroll feeds grade-wise employee count and compensation.
- **Layer 2 (Weighting):** Base-year population normalized to weights.
- **Layer 3 (Mathematical Engine):** Laspeyres, Paasche, or Fisher formulae applied.
- **Layer 4 (Productivity Normalization):** Output index divides nominal LCI to yield ULCI.
- **Layer 5 (Real-Cost Correction):** CPI strips out inflation noise.
- **Layer 6 (Decision Output):** Feeds plant-level O&M escalation, tender escalation clauses, and statutory compliance audits.

### 4.2 Process Map: Application of LCI in a Chemical Industry Lifecycle

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Feasibility Stage] --> P2[Detailed Engineering]
    P2 --> P3[Procurement and Contracting]
    P3 --> P4[Construction and Commissioning]
    P4 --> P5[Operations and Maintenance]
    P1 -.LCI used for.-> Q1[Capital Cost Estimation]
    P2 -.LCI used for.-> Q2[Manpower Planning]
    P3 -.LCI used for.-> Q3[Contract Escalation Clauses]
    P4 -.LCI used for.-> Q4[Site Labour Budget]
    P5 -.LCI used for.-> Q5[Annual O&M Cost Revision]
    Q5 --> P5
```

**Interpretation:** LCI is a *thread* that runs across the entire chemical plant lifecycle, ensuring cost consistency from concept to decommissioning.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]**
**Q1. Define Labour Cost Index. List any four components that constitute the total labour cost.**

**Model Answer:**
A Labour Cost Index is a composite weighted index that measures the percentage change in the total cost of employing labour (wages, benefits, and statutory levies) over time relative to a chosen base year (= 100). It is computed using the Laspeyres, Paasche, or Fisher formula.

**Four components of labour cost:**
1. Direct wages and dearness allowance
2. Fringe benefits and perquisites
3. Statutory contributions (PF, ESI, gratuity)
4. Bonus, incentives, and welfare/training costs

---

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Retest Pattern]**
**Q2. Distinguish between Laspeyres and Paasche methods of constructing a Labour Cost Index.**

**Model Answer:**

| Basis | Laspeyres LCI | Paasche LCI |
|-------|---------------|-------------|
| Weights | Base-year quantities | Current-year quantities |
| Formula | $\frac{\sum W_0 C_t}{\sum W_0 C_0} \times 100$ | $\frac{\sum W_t C_t}{\sum W_t C_0} \times 100$ |
| Data need | Base + current | Base + current |
| Tendency | Overestimates cost rise | Underestimates cost rise |
| Practical use | EPC escalation | Sector-wide current comparison |

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – June 2024]

**(a)** A petrochemical complex has the following employment and wage data for 2020 (base year) and 2024 (current year). Calculate the **Laspeyres Labour Cost Index** and interpret the result. **(7 marks)**

| Grade | Workers 2020 | Wage 2020 (₹/month) | Workers 2024 | Wage 2024 (₹/month) |
|-------|--------------|----------------------|--------------|----------------------|
| A (Engineers) | 100 | 80,000 | 120 | 1,05,000 |
| B (Technicians) | 250 | 45,000 | 280 | 58,000 |
| C (Operators) | 400 | 30,000 | 420 | 38,500 |
| D (Helpers) | 300 | 18,000 | 280 | 24,000 |

**(b)** Explain the concept of **Productivity-Adjusted (Unit) Labour Cost Index**. Why is it considered a more accurate indicator of true labour cost escalation in a chemical plant? **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution to Question A:**

**Solution to Part (a):**

Step 1: Compute base-year total cost $\sum W_0 C_0$:

- A: $100 \times 80{,}000 = 8{,}000{,}000$
- B: $250 \times 45{,}000 = 11{,}250{,}000$
- C: $400 \times 30{,}000 = 12{,}000{,}000$
- D: $300 \times 18{,}000 = 5{,}400{,}000$

$$\sum W_0 C_0 = 36{,}650{,}000$$

Step 2: Compute current-year cost at base-year weights $\sum W_0 C_t$:

- A: $100 \times 1{,}05{,}000 = 10{,}500{,}000$
- B: $250 \times 58{,}000 = 14{,}500{,}000$
- C: $400 \times 38{,}500 = 15{,}400{,}000$
- D: $300 \times 24{,}000 = 7{,}200{,}000$

$$\sum W_0 C_t = 47{,}600{,}000$$

Step 3: Apply Laspeyres formula:

---

$$L_{Laspeyres} = \frac{47{,}600{,}000}{36{,}650{,}000} \times 100 = 129.88$$

---

**Interpretation:** The total labour cost of the petrochemical complex has increased by **29.88%** over the four-year period, indicating substantial wage escalation that must be factored into O&M budgets and contract escalation clauses.

---

**Solution to Part (b):**

The **Productivity-Adjusted (Unit) Labour Cost Index (ULCI)** is defined as:

---

$$ULCI = \frac{LCI_{nominal}}{OI} \times 100$$

---

where $OI$ is the Output Index (output in current year ÷ output in base year × 100).

**Why ULCI is more accurate:**

1. **Eliminates Productivity Distortion:** A pure LCI rises whenever wages rise, even if workers are producing more output per unit cost. ULCI divides LCI by output, isolating *true cost per unit of production*.
2. **Industry Benchmarking:** In chemical plants where automation can decouple wage growth from output growth, ULCI is the only fair metric for cross-plant comparison.
3. **Inflation Resistance:** When combined with CPI correction, ULCI yields the **Real Unit Labour Cost Index**, which cannot be manipulated by nominal wage hikes.
4. **Strategic Decision-Making:** ULCI guides decisions on *outsourcing vs. in-house manpower*, *degree of automation*, and *labour-intensive vs. capital-intensive process design*.
5. **EPC Tendering:** Contractors with high ULCI are uncompetitive in global bids; the index flags this for management.

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Alternative]

**(a)** Define the **Fisher Labour Cost Index**. Using the data from Question A, compute the **Paasche and Fisher LCIs** and comment on the relative accuracy. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **role of Labour Cost Indices in chemical process plant feasibility studies** with at least four specific applications. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution to Question B:**

**Solution to Part (a):**

**Fisher Index Definition:** The Fisher Labour Cost Index is the *geometric mean* of the Laspeyres and Paasche indices. It is the only index that simultaneously satisfies the **time-reversal test** and the **factor-reversal test**, making it mathematically the most rigorous.

---

$$L_{Fisher} = \sqrt{L_{Laspeyres} \times L_{Paasche}}$$

---

Step 1: Compute Paasche numerator $\sum W_t C_t$:

- A: $120 \times 1{,}05{,}000 = 12{,}600{,}000$
- B: $280 \times 58{,}000 = 16{,}240{,}000$
- C: $420 \times 38{,}500 = 16{,}170{,}000$
- D: $280 \times 24{,}000 = 6{,}720{,}000$

$$\sum W_t C_t = 51{,}730{,}000$$

Step 2: Compute Paasche denominator $\sum W_t C_0$:

- A: $120 \times 80{,}000 = 9{,}600{,}000$
- B: $280 \times 45{,}000 = 12{,}600{,}000$
- C: $420 \times 30{,}000 = 12{,}600{,}000$
- D: $280 \times 18{,}000 = 5{,}040{,}000$

$$\sum W_t C_0 = 39{,}840{,}000$$

Step 3: Apply Paasche formula:

---

$$L_{Paasche} = \frac{51{,}730{,}000}{39{,}840{,}000} \times 100 = 129.84$$

---

Step 4: Apply Fisher formula (using Laspeyres = 129.88 from Question A):

---

$$L_{Fisher} = \sqrt{129.88 \times 129.84} = \sqrt{16868.5} \approx 129.86$$

---

**Comment on Relative Accuracy:**
- Laspeyres (129.88) marginally overestimates.
- Paasche (129.84) marginally underestimates.
- **Fisher (129.86)** is the balanced, theoretically ideal estimate, lying between the two.

---

**Solution to Part (b):**

**Four Specific Applications of LCI in Chemical Plant Feasibility Studies:**

1. **Capital Cost Escalation During Project Lifecycle:** A 5-year project uses LCI to escalate initial EPC estimates to expected completion-year costs, preventing under-budgeting.

2. **Operating Cost (O&M) Forecasting:** LCI helps estimate annual manpower cost growth, which is a major O&M component (often 15–25% of O&M cost in chemical plants).

3. **Contract Escalation Clauses:** Long-term maintenance, catering, and security contracts use LCI as a neutral, defensible index for periodic price revisions.

4. **Make-or-Buy Decisions:** Comparing *in-house labour cost escalation* (using LCI) vs. *outsourced vendor pricing* (often pegged to LCI indirectly) helps management decide whether to outsource turnarounds, shutdowns, or routine maintenance.

5. **Project Location Selection:** A region with slower LCI growth reduces long-term operating cost risk; this is factored into site-selection matrices.

6. **Inflation-Adjusted Performance Appraisal:** Real LCI (LCI/CPI) is used to evaluate whether wage hikes outpace inflation, guiding HR compensation policy.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Do NOT** confuse **wage index** with **labour cost index** — examiners deduct 2–3 marks if fringe benefits and statutory levies are omitted from the numerator.
> - **Always state the formula explicitly** before substituting values. Marks are reserved for the formula, not just the final answer.
> - **Interpretation marks** (typically 1–2) are lost if the student writes only a number. Always add a one-line meaning in context of chemical industry.
> - **Unit mistakes:** Final LCI is dimensionless; do not write ₹ or % after the index number.
> - **Choice of weights:** Laspeyres is preferred for *EPC escalation*; do not use Paasche unless the question specifies current-year weights.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition:** A Labour Cost Index measures the *time-based proportional change* in total employment cost relative to a base year.
- **Core Formula (Laspeyres):** $\frac{\sum W_0 C_t}{\sum W_0 C_0} \times 100$ — the **most-asked** variant in KTU papers.
- **Components of Labour Cost:** Direct wages + fringe benefits + statutory contributions + bonus + welfare + productivity adjustment.
- **Three Methods:** Laspeyres (base weights), Paasche (current weights), Fisher (geometric mean).
- **Laspeyres Overestimates; Paasche Underestimates; Fisher is the Ideal Index.**
- **Real LCI** strips out inflation via CPI; it represents the *true purchasing power* of wages.
- **Unit Labour Cost Index (ULCI)** = LCI / Output Index — the most accurate KPI for chemical plant benchmarking.
- **Applications in Chemical Industry:** EPC cost escalation, O&M forecasting, contract escalation, make-or-buy decisions, site selection, and HR policy.
- **Typical KTU Question Format:** A 14-mark question pairs a *numerical computation* (7 marks) with a *conceptual/analytical sub-question* (7 marks).
- **Mandatory Steps in Numerical Solutions:** (1) State formula, (2) compute denominator, (3) compute numerator, (4) substitute, (5) interpret.
- **Always write the interpretation in a chemical-industry context** — this is what differentiates a 12-mark answer from a 14-mark one.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Location Index, Cost Components in Capital Investment
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT757 - ECONOMICS AND MANAGEMENT OF CHEMICAL INDUSTRIES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Location Index, Cost Components in Capital Investment

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Location Index — Formal Definition

The **Location Index** (denoted as $L_i$ or sometimes $F_L$) is a **dimensionless, multiplicative factor** used in Chemical Engineering Economics to convert a known **base capital cost** (typically quoted for a reference geographic location such as the U.S. Gulf Coast, Western Europe, or Japan) into the **expected capital cost at a different geographic location** where a similar process plant is to be erected.

Mathematically, the location index is the **ratio of the weighted cost of a representative plant in the proposed location to the weighted cost of the same plant in the reference location**, where the weights are the various cost components (equipment, materials, labor, etc.).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reference Convention (Peters & Timmerhaus Standard):**
> Almost all CE cost correlations in textbooks assume a reference location — the **U.S. Gulf Coast (USGC) is treated as the base location with Location Index = 1.00**. India (Kerala/Mumbai), China, Europe, etc., have indices that deviate from this base.

### 1.2 Capital Investment — Formal Definition

**Capital Investment** is the total amount of capital required to design, erect, and commission a chemical process plant and to keep it operational until it begins generating self-sustaining revenue. It is broadly classified into:

1. **Fixed Capital Investment (FCI)** — the one-time cost of constructing the plant.
2. **Working Capital (WC)** — the liquid funds needed for day-to-day operation.

> **Total Capital Investment (TCI) = Fixed Capital Investment (FCI) + Working Capital (WC)**

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The House-Buying Analogy"

Imagine you found the **perfect house design online listed for ₹50 Lakhs in Pune** (your reference city). Now you want to build the **same house in Kochi**. Even though the design is identical, the **steel, cement, and bricks are cheaper in Kochi (so materials cost less)**, but **skilled masons are slightly more expensive (so labor costs more)**, and the **land is cheaper (so site cost is less)**.

A **Location Index** is exactly like saying: *"On an average, considering all the cost pieces combined, building this house in Kochi is 0.92 times as expensive as in Pune."* So the Kochi cost ≈ ₹50 L × 0.92 = **₹46 Lakhs**.

In a chemical plant, we do the same thing — but with **equipment, piping, instrumentation, civil, electrical, insulation, labor, taxes, freight, etc.** Each is multiplied by its local cost factor, weighted, and summed to get the **Location Index**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why do we need a Location Index?**
> Because chemical engineering cost data (e.g., the six-tenths rule, module cost charts, Peters & Timmerhaus charts) are published for ONE reference location. The Location Index is the bridge that lets you adapt that data to **Kerala, Mangalore, Gujarat, or any other** real plant site.

### 1.4 Physical / Standard Metrics

- **Location Index is dimensionless** (units cancel out — both numerator and denominator are in the same currency, e.g., ₹ or $).
- A typical range: **0.6 to 1.8** depending on country and cost component.
- Standard reference values: **USGC = 1.00**, **UK = ~1.20**, **Japan = ~1.30**, **India (urban) = ~0.80–1.00**.

> [!TIP]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** The KTU 2024 PECHT757 syllabus explicitly tests the ability to (i) classify capital investment components and (ii) compute a Location Index from tabulated cost-component multipliers. Master both — they are direct Part-A and Part-B questions.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Logic of the Location Index

The Location Index is built on a simple but powerful **decomposition principle**: the total cost of a plant is the sum of *n* independent cost components, and **each component scales differently** with geography.

**Step 1 — Identify the cost components.** In a typical chemical plant, the major cost components are:

1. Major process equipment (vessels, columns, heat exchangers, pumps, compressors).
2. Construction materials (steel, concrete, refractories).
3. Labor (skilled + unskilled + supervision).
4. Freight & logistics.
5. Engineering and design fees.
6. Taxes and duties.
7. Site preparation and utilities hookup.

**Step 2 — Define the reference cost for each component.** Using the USGC (or any chosen base) plant cost data, compute $C_{j,\text{ref}}$ for the *j*-th component.

**Step 3 — Find the local multiplier $f_j$** for each component at the new location. These multipliers are usually obtained from published cost-index tables (e.g., CEPCI, Marshall & Swift, Chemical Engineering plant cost indices by country).

**Step 4 — Compute the new cost for each component:**

$$C_{j,\text{new}} = f_j \times C_{j,\text{ref}}$$

**Step 5 — Sum and normalize.** The Location Index is the sum of weighted new costs divided by the sum of weighted reference costs:

$$L_i = \frac{\sum_{j=1}^{n} f_j \cdot C_{j,\text{ref}}}{\sum_{j=1}^{n} C_{j,\text{ref}}}$$

**Why "Why" and "How" this works:** Because each cost component has a **different sensitivity** to geography (labor varies more than equipment), a simple flat scaling factor would be wrong. The weighted harmonic-style averaging of the Location Index correctly captures the fact that **a 10% drop in 40% of the cost is more impactful than a 10% rise in 10% of the cost**.

### 2.2 Capital Investment — Detailed Cost Component Tree

Chemical Engineering capital costs are conventionally broken into the following hierarchy:

#### A. Fixed Capital Investment (FCI)

**1. Direct Costs (DC)** — physical, tangible plant elements:
- **Purchased Equipment Cost (PEC)** — major + minor process equipment.
- **Equipment Installation** — foundations, supports, setting in place.
- **Piping** — typically the single largest direct cost (15–25% of FCI).
- **Instrumentation & Control** — DCS, PLCs, sensors, control valves.
- **Electrical** — transformers, cabling, switchgear, lighting.
- **Insulation** — hot/cold insulation on vessels, pipes.
- **Painting & Protective Coatings**.
- **Structural Steel** — buildings, pipe racks, platforms.
- **Buildings** — process buildings, control rooms, admin.
- **Site Preparation & Land Improvements** — grading, drainage, fencing.
- **Offsites & Utilities** — boiler, cooling tower, compressed air, DM plant.

**2. Indirect Costs (IC)** — non-physical but essential:
- **Engineering & Supervision** — design, procurement, project management.
- **Construction Expenses** — temporary facilities, contractor mobilization, QA/QC.
- **Contractor's Fee** — typically 5–10% of DC.
- **Contingency** — buffer for unforeseen items, typically 5–15% of (DC+IC).

#### B. Working Capital (WC)

- **Raw Material Inventory** — 1–2 months of feedstock cost.
- **Product (Finished Goods) Inventory**.
- **In-Process (WIP) Inventory**.
- **Cash Reserve** — salaries, utilities, freight, 1 month.
- **Accounts Receivable** — credit extended to buyers.
- **Less: Accounts Payable** — credit received from suppliers (subtracted).

> **Net Working Capital = Raw Material + Product + In-Process + Cash + Receivables − Payables**

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Description / Units |
|---|----------|---------|---------------------|
| 1 | Total Capital Investment (TCI) | $TCI = FCI + WC$ | Currency units (₹ / $) |
| 2 | Fixed Capital Investment (FCI) | $FCI = DC + IC$ | Currency units |
| 3 | Working Capital (WC) | $WC = RM + FG + WIP + Cash + AR - AP$ | Currency units |
| 4 | Location Index (Weighted method) | $L_i = \dfrac{\sum f_j C_{j,\text{ref}}}{\sum C_{j,\text{ref}}}$ | Dimensionless |
| 5 | Capital Cost at New Location | $C_{\text{new}} = L_i \times C_{\text{ref}}$ | Currency units |
| 6 | Simple Location Factor (2-component) | $L_i = \dfrac{a \cdot f_L + (1-a) \cdot f_M}{1}$ | Dimensionless; $a$ = labor fraction |
| 7 | Lang Factor (overall) | $C_{FCI} = f_L \times C_{eq}$ | $f_L$ = 3.10 (solid), 4.74 (fluid) |
| 8 | Working Capital % of TCI | $WC \approx 10\% - 20\% \text{ of TCI}$ | Percentage |
| 9 | Indirect Cost % of DC | $IC \approx 30\% - 50\% \text{ of DC}$ | Percentage |
| 10 | Contingency | $K = 5\% - 15\% \text{ of } (DC+IC)$ | Percentage |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note on Absolute Values in Tables:** In the table above, expressions such as $f_j C_{j,\text{ref}}$ represent a product, not absolute value. If absolute-value notation is required in any answer script, use $\vert \cdot \vert$ or $\mid \cdot \mid$ to avoid breaking the markdown table syntax.

### 2.4 Real-World Utility

- **Project Feasibility Studies:** Location Indices let chemical engineers run quick CAPEX (Capital Expenditure) re-estimates during site selection.
- **Tender & Bid Evaluation:** EPC companies apply location factors to USGC-priced vendor quotes.
- **Insurance & Asset Valuation:** Insurance valuers use location factors to estimate replacement cost.
- **Government Policy:** SEBI & RBI infrastructure reports use location factors for cross-state cost normalization.
- **Cross-country M&A:** Used to compare plant values across geographies during mergers or asset transfers.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Weighted Location Index

We start from the principle that the total plant cost is a sum of *n* independent cost components:

$$C_{\text{total}} = \sum_{j=1}^{n} C_j$$

For the **reference location** (say, USGC):

$$C_{\text{ref}} = \sum_{j=1}^{n} C_{j,\text{ref}}$$

For the **new location** (say, Mangalore), each component $C_j$ is multiplied by a local cost factor $f_j$:

$$C_{\text{new}} = \sum_{j=1}^{n} f_j \cdot C_{j,\text{ref}}$$

By **definition of the Location Index** as the ratio of new-location cost to reference-location cost:

$$L_i = \frac{C_{\text{new}}}{C_{\text{ref}}}$$

Substituting the sums:

$$L_i = \frac{\sum_{j=1}^{n} f_j \cdot C_{j,\text{ref}}}{\sum_{j=1}^{n} C_{j,\text{ref}}}$$

**Interpretation:** If $L_i > 1$, the new location is **more expensive** than the reference. If $L_i < 1$, it is **cheaper**.

### 3.2 Worked Example — Location Index Calculation (KTU-Style)

**Problem Statement:**

The cost of building a 50,000 TPA (Tonnes Per Annum) ammonia plant on the U.S. Gulf Coast (reference location) is estimated to be **₹500 Crores** with the following cost-component breakdown:

| Cost Component | Reference Cost (₹ Cr) | Fraction of Total | Mangalore Multiplier $f_j$ |
|---|---:|---:|---:|
| Major Equipment | 150 | 0.30 | 1.10 |
| Piping & Valves | 75 | 0.15 | 0.95 |
| Instrumentation | 50 | 0.10 | 1.05 |
| Electrical | 40 | 0.08 | 0.90 |
| Construction Materials | 60 | 0.12 | 0.85 |
| Labor (skilled + unskilled) | 80 | 0.16 | 0.70 |
| Engineering & Supervision | 30 | 0.06 | 0.80 |
| Contingency & Misc | 15 | 0.03 | 1.00 |
| **Total** | **500** | **1.00** | — |

**Step 1:** Identify reference cost for each component $C_{j,\text{ref}}$ (column 2 above).

**Step 2:** Identify Mangalore multipliers $f_j$ (column 4 above).

**Step 3:** Compute weighted numerator $\sum f_j C_{j,\text{ref}}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum f_j C_{j,\text{ref}} &= (1.10 \times 150) + (0.95 \times 75) + (1.05 \times 50) + (0.90 \times 40) \\
&\quad + (0.85 \times 60) + (0.70 \times 80) + (0.80 \times 30) + (1.00 \times 15)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3a — Evaluate term by term:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
(1.10 \times 150) &= 165.00 \;\text{₹ Cr} \\
(0.95 \times 75)  &= 71.25 \;\text{₹ Cr} \\
(1.05 \times 50)  &= 52.50 \;\text{₹ Cr} \\
(0.90 \times 40)  &= 36.00 \;\text{₹ Cr} \\
(0.85 \times 60)  &= 51.00 \;\text{₹ Cr} \\
(0.70 \times 80)  &= 56.00 \;\text{₹ Cr} \\
(0.80 \times 30)  &= 24.00 \;\text{₹ Cr} \\
(1.00 \times 15)  &= 15.00 \;\text{₹ Cr}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3b — Sum the terms:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum f_j C_{j,\text{ref}} &= 165.00 + 71.25 + 52.50 + 36.00 + 51.00 + 56.00 + 24.00 + 15.00 \\
&= 470.75 \;\text{₹ Cr}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** Compute the denominator $\sum C_{j,\text{ref}}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum C_{j,\text{ref}} &= 150 + 75 + 50 + 40 + 60 + 80 + 30 + 15 \\
&= 500 \;\text{₹ Cr}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5:** Compute the Location Index:

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_i &= \frac{470.75}{500} = 0.9415
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Interpretation & Capital Cost at Mangalore:**

$$L_i = 0.9415 \;\;(\text{Dimensionless, and } < 1)$$

This indicates that building the same plant in **Mangalore is ~5.85% cheaper** than on the U.S. Gulf Coast (despite the same design and capacity).

The **estimated capital cost at Mangalore**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{\text{Mangalore}} &= L_i \times C_{\text{ref}} \\
&= 0.9415 \times 500 \\
&= 470.75 \;\text{₹ Cr}
\end{aligned}
$$

This **matches our numerator** — a useful self-consistency check! ✅

### 3.3 Worked Example — Working Capital & Total Capital Investment

**Given (continuing the above example):**

- Raw Material Inventory (1 month stock): ₹8 Cr
- Finished Product Inventory: ₹6 Cr
- In-Process Inventory: ₹3 Cr
- Cash Reserve (1 month operating cost): ₹5 Cr
- Accounts Receivable: ₹7 Cr
- Accounts Payable: ₹4 Cr

**Step 1 — Compute Net Working Capital:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
WC &= RM + FG + WIP + \text{Cash} + AR - AP \\
&= 8 + 6 + 3 + 5 + 7 - 4 \\
&= 25 \;\text{₹ Cr}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute Total Capital Investment:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
TCI &= FCI + WC \\
&= 470.75 + 25 \\
&= 495.75 \;\text{₹ Cr}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Sanity Check:** A working capital of ₹25 Cr on a TCI of ₹495.75 Cr = **5.04%**, which lies in the typical 5–15% range. ✅

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Effect of Varying the Largest-Weight Component

To build engineering intuition, observe how the Location Index shifts when **labor (16% weight)** changes:

| Scenario | $f_{\text{labor}}$ | Resulting $L_i$ | Comment |
|---|---:|---:|---|
| Mangalore (low labor) | 0.70 | 0.9415 | Cheapest |
| USGC baseline | 1.00 | 1.0000 | Reference |
| Western Europe | 1.80 | 1.1280 | Expensive |
| Japan | 2.20 | 1.1920 | Most expensive |

**Key Insight:** Labor is the most geographically sensitive component — even at 16% weight, doubling labor (0.70 → 1.40) alone increases $L_i$ by ~0.112.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Capital Investment Hierarchy — Top-Down Decomposition

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0["TOTAL CAPITAL INVESTMENT TCI"] --> A1["FIXED CAPITAL INVESTMENT FCI"]
    A0 --> A2["WORKING CAPITAL WC"]
    A1 --> B1["DIRECT COSTS DC"]
    A1 --> B2["INDIRECT COSTS IC"]
    B1 --> C1A["Major Process Equipment"]
    B1 --> C1B["Piping and Valves"]
    B1 --> C1C["Instrumentation and Control"]
    B1 --> C1D["Electrical Systems"]
    B1 --> C1E["Insulation"]
    B1 --> C1F["Painting and Coatings"]
    B1 --> C1G["Structural Steel"]
    B1 --> C1H["Buildings and Civil"]
    B1 --> C1I["Site Preparation"]
    B1 --> C1J["Offsites and Utilities"]
    B2 --> C2A["Engineering and Supervision"]
    B2 --> C2B["Construction Expenses"]
    B2 --> C2C["Contractors Fee"]
    B2 --> C2D["Contingency Reserve"]
    A2 --> D1["Raw Material Inventory"]
    A2 --> D2["Finished Product Inventory"]
    A2 --> D3["In Process Inventory"]
    A2 --> D4["Cash Reserve"]
    A2 --> D5["Accounts Receivable"]
    A2 --> D6["Accounts Payable MINUS"]
```

### 4.2 Location Index Computational Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1["STEP 1 Identify Cost Components n"] --> P2["STEP 2 Obtain Reference Costs Cj ref"]
    P2 --> P3["STEP 3 Obtain Local Cost Multipliers fj"]
    P3 --> P4["STEP 4 Compute Numerator Sum fj x Cj ref"]
    P2 --> P5["STEP 5 Compute Denominator Sum Cj ref"]
    P4 --> P6["STEP 6 Calculate Location Index Li"]
    P5 --> P6
    P6 --> P7["STEP 7 Estimate New Location Cost Li x C ref"]
    P7 --> P8["STEP 8 Add Working Capital for TCI"]
```

### 4.3 Component-Weight Sensitivity Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    W1["Component Weight Share percent of FCI"] --> W1A["Equipment 25 to 35 percent"]
    W1 --> W1B["Piping 15 to 25 percent"]
    W1 --> W1C["Instrumentation 5 to 10 percent"]
    W1 --> W1D["Electrical 3 to 8 percent"]
    W1 --> W1E["Labor 15 to 25 percent"]
    W1 --> W1F["Engineering 5 to 10 percent"]
    W1 --> W1G["Contingency 5 to 15 percent"]
    W1A --> S1["High geographic sensitivity IMPORT DUTY"]
    W1E --> S2["Highest geographic sensitivity REGION WAGE"]
    W1B --> S2
    W1C --> S1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** This block-level architecture shows how a capital investment decision cascades. Direct costs (green-band typical), indirect costs (blue-band), and working capital (yellow-band) together yield TCI. Sensitivity to geography is highest for labor and piping, and lowest for engineering fees.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, PECHT757]**
*Define Location Index. Why is the U.S. Gulf Coast (USGC) chosen as the reference location in most chemical engineering cost correlations?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** The **Location Index** $L_i$ is a dimensionless factor that represents the ratio of the cost of constructing a chemical plant at a specific geographic location to the cost of constructing the same plant at a designated reference location. It is computed as the weighted average of local cost-multipliers of all major cost components, with the weights being the fractions of each component in the total reference cost:
