$$L_i = \frac{\sum f_j \cdot C_{j,\text{ref}}}{\sum C_{j,\text{ref}}}$$

**Why USGC is the reference (1.5 marks):** Most CE cost data, charts, and correlations (e.g., six-tenths rule, Peters & Timmerhaus charts, module cost factors) were originally developed using **U.S. Gulf Coast** plant data because (i) it is the historic hub of U.S. petrochemical industry, (ii) large volumes of consistent, well-documented cost data exist for it, and (iii) using a single, consistent reference allows **easy re-scaling** to any other location via the Location Index.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, PECHT757]**
*List any six direct cost components of Fixed Capital Investment (FCI) for a chemical process plant.*

**Model Answer (3 marks — 0.5 per item):**

The six direct cost components of FCI are:

1. **Purchased Equipment Cost (PEC)** — major and minor process equipment.
2. **Piping and Valves** — process and utility piping.
3. **Instrumentation and Control** — DCS, PLCs, sensors, control valves.
4. **Electrical Installations** — transformers, cabling, switchgear, lighting.
5. **Insulation** — hot/cold insulation for vessels, columns, and pipes.
6. **Buildings and Civil Works** — process buildings, control rooms, admin blocks.

(Any six from the FCI direct-cost list in Section 2.2 — full marks.)

---

### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

**Q.A. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, PECHT757, Module 2]**
*OR*

**Q.B. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, PECHT757, Module 2]**

---

#### Question A (14 Marks)

**(a)** *(7 marks)* Define the term **Location Index**. Derive the expression for the **weighted Location Index** in terms of the cost components and their local multipliers.

**(b)** *(7 marks)* A 75,000 TPA methanol plant has the following cost-component breakdown at the **U.S. Gulf Coast reference location**, and corresponding cost multipliers at **Kochi, Kerala**. Calculate the **Location Index** for Kochi and the **estimated total capital investment at Kochi** (assume working capital = 8% of FCI).

| Cost Component | Reference Cost (₹ Cr) | Kochi Multiplier $f_j$ |
|---|---:|---:|
| Major Equipment | 180 | 1.12 |
| Piping | 90 | 0.95 |
| Instrumentation | 60 | 1.00 |
| Electrical | 45 | 0.85 |
| Construction Materials | 70 | 0.80 |
| Labor | 95 | 0.65 |
| Engineering & Supervision | 35 | 0.75 |
| Contingency & Misc | 25 | 1.00 |
| **Total** | **600** | — |

---

##### Model Solution to Question A

**Part (a) — Definition and Derivation (7 marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):**
The **Location Index** $L_i$ is a dimensionless number that gives the ratio of the cost of a chemical plant in a given geographic location to the cost of the same plant in a reference location. It accounts for local variations in the prices of equipment, materials, labor, freight, taxes, and other cost components.

**Derivation (5 marks):**

Let the total plant cost in the reference location be the sum of $n$ cost components:

$$C_{\text{ref}} = \sum_{j=1}^{n} C_{j,\text{ref}}$$

Let the cost multiplier at the new location for the $j$-th component be $f_j$. The new-location cost of that component is $f_j C_{j,\text{ref}}$. Summing over all components:

$$C_{\text{new}} = \sum_{j=1}^{n} f_j \cdot C_{j,\text{ref}}$$

By definition, the Location Index is the ratio of new cost to reference cost:

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_i &= \frac{C_{\text{new}}}{C_{\text{ref}}} \\
&= \frac{\sum_{j=1}^{n} f_j \cdot C_{j,\text{ref}}}{\sum_{j=1}^{n} C_{j,\text{ref}}}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the **weighted Location Index** formula. Each $f_j$ acts as a *local cost factor* and $C_{j,\text{ref}}$ acts as the *weight* — components that are larger in cost contribute more to $L_i$.

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Problem (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Compute the numerator $\sum f_j C_{j,\text{ref}}$ (3 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum f_j C_{j,\text{ref}} &= (1.12 \times 180) + (0.95 \times 90) + (1.00 \times 60) + (0.85 \times 45) \\
&\quad + (0.80 \times 70) + (0.65 \times 95) + (0.75 \times 35) + (1.00 \times 25)
\end{aligned}
$$

Evaluating term by term:

$$
\begin{aligned}
1.12 \times 180 &= 201.60 \\
0.95 \times 90  &= 85.50 \\
1.00 \times 60  &= 60.00 \\
0.85 \times 45  &= 38.25 \\
0.80 \times 70  &= 56.00 \\
0.65 \times 95  &= 61.75 \\
0.75 \times 35  &= 26.25 \\
1.00 \times 25  &= 25.00
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\sum f_j C_{j,\text{ref}} = 201.60 + 85.50 + 60.00 + 38.25 + 56.00 + 61.75 + 26.25 + 25.00 = 554.35 \;\text{₹ Cr}
$$

**Step 2 — Denominator (already given) (1 mark):**

$$
\sum C_{j,\text{ref}} = 600 \;\text{₹ Cr}
$$

**Step 3 — Location Index (1 mark):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_i &= \frac{554.35}{600} = 0.9239
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Capital cost at Kochi = FCI (1 mark):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{FCI}_{\text{Kochi}} &= L_i \times \text{FCI}_{\text{ref}} \\
&= 0.9239 \times 600 \\
&= 554.35 \;\text{₹ Cr}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Working Capital & TCI (1 mark):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
WC &= 0.08 \times 554.35 = 44.35 \;\text{₹ Cr} \\
TCI &= FCI + WC = 554.35 + 44.35 = 598.70 \;\text{₹ Cr}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answer:**

- **Location Index for Kochi, $L_i = 0.9239$** (i.e., ~7.6% cheaper than USGC).
- **FCI at Kochi = ₹554.35 Cr**; **WC = ₹44.35 Cr**; **TCI = ₹598.70 Cr**.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**(a)** *(7 marks)* With the help of a **neat block diagram**, classify the various cost components of **Total Capital Investment (TCI)** in a chemical process plant. Differentiate between **Fixed Capital Investment (FCI)** and **Working Capital (WC)**.

**(b)** *(7 marks)* The Fixed Capital Investment of a urea plant in a reference location is **₹800 Crores**. The cost-component fractions and the corresponding cost multipliers for a proposed **Mangalore** site are given below. Calculate the **Location Index** for Mangalore and the **estimated FCI** at the new site.

| Cost Component | Fraction of FCI | Mangalore Multiplier $f_j$ |
|---|---:|---:|
| Major Equipment | 0.35 | 1.15 |
| Piping | 0.20 | 0.90 |
| Instrumentation | 0.10 | 1.00 |
| Electrical | 0.08 | 0.88 |
| Labor (skilled) | 0.15 | 0.60 |
| Engineering & Supervision | 0.07 | 0.85 |
| Contingency & Misc | 0.05 | 1.00 |

---

##### Model Solution to Question B

**Part (a) — Classification (7 marks):**

**Block Diagram (3 marks):** Refer to the Mermaid diagram in **Section 4.1** of these notes for the complete hierarchy. The student should reproduce a similar structure:

- **TCI** is split into **FCI** + **WC**.
- **FCI** splits into **Direct Costs (DC)** and **Indirect Costs (IC)**.
- **DC** includes: Equipment, Piping, Instrumentation, Electrical, Insulation, Painting, Structural Steel, Buildings, Site Preparation, Offsites.
- **IC** includes: Engineering & Supervision, Construction Expenses, Contractor's Fee, Contingency.
- **WC** includes: Raw Material Inventory, Product Inventory, In-Process Inventory, Cash Reserve, Accounts Receivable, **minus** Accounts Payable.

**Differentiation (4 marks):**

| Aspect | Fixed Capital Investment (FCI) | Working Capital (WC) |
|---|---|---|
| **Nature** | One-time, sunk cost | Recurring, recoverable at end of project |
| **Purpose** | Build the physical plant | Operate the plant until revenue stabilizes |
| **Recovery** | Recovered via depreciation | Fully recovered at end of project life |
| **Magnitude** | 80–90% of TCI | 10–20% of TCI |
| **Examples** | Equipment, piping, civil works | Cash, inventory, receivables |
| **Tax Treatment** | Depreciable | Not depreciable |

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Problem (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Reference cost for each component (3 marks):**

Since fractions are given, multiply each fraction by total FCI (₹800 Cr):

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{1,\text{ref}} = 0.35 \times 800 &= 280.00 \\
C_{2,\text{ref}} = 0.20 \times 800 &= 160.00 \\
C_{3,\text{ref}} = 0.10 \times 800 &= 80.00 \\
C_{4,\text{ref}} = 0.08 \times 800 &= 64.00 \\
C_{5,\text{ref}} = 0.15 \times 800 &= 120.00 \\
C_{6,\text{ref}} = 0.07 \times 800 &= 56.00 \\
C_{7,\text{ref}} = 0.05 \times 800 &= 40.00
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Numerator $\sum f_j C_{j,\text{ref}}$ (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
1.15 \times 280 &= 322.00 \\
0.90 \times 160 &= 144.00 \\
1.00 \times 80  &= 80.00 \\
0.88 \times 64  &= 56.32 \\
0.60 \times 120 &= 72.00 \\
0.85 \times 56  &= 47.60 \\
1.00 \times 40  &= 40.00
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\sum f_j C_{j,\text{ref}} = 322.00 + 144.00 + 80.00 + 56.32 + 72.00 + 47.60 + 40.00 = 761.92 \;\text{₹ Cr}
$$

**Step 3 — Denominator (1 mark):**

$$
\sum C_{j,\text{ref}} = 800 \;\text{₹ Cr}
$$

**Step 4 — Location Index (1 mark):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_i &= \frac{761.92}{800} = 0.9524
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answer:**

- **Location Index for Mangalore, $L_i = 0.9524$** (i.e., ~4.76% cheaper than the reference location).
- **Estimated FCI at Mangalore = $0.9524 \times 800 = \mathbf{₹761.92 \;\text{Cr}}$**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing FCI and TCI:** Students frequently forget to add working capital to get TCI. Always state clearly **what you are computing** — FCI, TCI, or Location Index.
> 2. **Mixing up units:** A Location Index is **dimensionless**. Never append ₹ or $ to $L_i$.
> 3. **Forgetting the denominator:** Some students directly average the multipliers without weighting by cost. **This is WRONG** — the formula requires weighted averaging by $C_{j,\text{ref}}$.
> 4. **Reference location assumption:** If not stated, **always declare** which location is your reference (USGC is the safe default for CE).
> 5. **Working Capital sign:** Accounts Payable is **subtracted**, not added. It is a source of short-term financing.
> 6. **Rounding:** Carry at least 4 decimal places in intermediate steps; round final answer to 2 decimal places.
> 7. **Self-consistency check:** Always verify that $C_{\text{new}} = L_i \times C_{\text{ref}}$ matches the numerator you computed. If not, recheck.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Location Index $L_i$ is dimensionless**; $L_i = 1$ at the reference location (typically **USGC**).
- **TCI = FCI + WC**; **FCI = DC + IC**; **WC = RM + FG + WIP + Cash + AR − AP**.
- The **weighted formula** for $L_i$ is: $\sum f_j C_{j,\text{ref}} \;/\; \sum C_{j,\text{ref}}$.
- **Capital cost at new location** = $L_i \times$ **reference capital cost**.
- **Direct costs** are physical; **Indirect costs** are services/overheads.
- **Working capital** ≈ 10–20% of TCI; **Contingency** ≈ 5–15% of (DC+IC).
- **Labor and piping** are typically the **most geographically sensitive** components.
- **India, China, and Southeast Asia** generally have $L_i < 1$ (cheaper than USGC); **Europe, Japan** have $L_i > 1$.
- **Sanity check** for any solution: $L_i$ should lie between 0.5 and 2.0 for most real sites; values outside this range usually indicate a calculation error.
- **Always carry units** in working notes (₹ Cr, $M, etc.) but drop them in the final $L_i$ value.

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision (Last-Minute Viva & Exam Cram):**
> Memorize the **weighted Location Index formula**, the **FCI = DC + IC** decomposition, and the **six direct cost items** (Equipment, Piping, Instrumentation, Electrical, Insulation, Buildings). If you remember these three things, you can answer **>70% of Module-2 questions** in this topic.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conference Technique (Consensus / Expert Opinion Method):**
> A *qualitative–judgmental* technique of capital cost estimation in which the total project investment is determined through structured deliberation and consensus among a multidisciplinary panel of experienced professionals drawn from process, mechanical, electrical, instrumentation, civil, and project management disciplines.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for **PECHT757 (Economics and Management of Chemical Industries)**, the Conference Technique is classified as one of the *preliminary* or *study-stage* estimation methods, employed typically at the **Order-of-Magnitude / Study Estimate** phase (accuracy range: ±30% to ±40%), where detailed engineering data is not yet available.

The technique derives its name from the literal **conference table** at which experts meet, debate, and reconcile their individual assessments into a single agreed-upon cost figure.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Medical Council

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Intuition:**
> Imagine a critically ill patient admitted to a hospital. The hospital does not depend on a single doctor's view; instead, a **medical board** of specialists — a cardiologist, a nephrologist, a radiologist, and a general physician — each examines the case independently, gives an opinion, and then convenes to discuss. After weighing each expert's perspective, the board issues a **unified diagnosis and treatment plan**.
> The **Conference Technique of cost estimation works identically**: a chemical plant's investment cost is "diagnosed" not by one engineer but by a council of domain experts, each viewing the project through their own specialist lens.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics Associated with the Conference Technique

| Metric | Typical Value (KTU Reference) | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **Estimate Accuracy** | **±30 % to ±40 %** | Defines the permissible deviation band |
| **Engineering Status Required** | **< 5 % to 10 %** of total design completed | Indicates preliminary-stage applicability |
| **Cost Contingency Allowance** | **15 % to 25 %** of estimated cost | Built-in safety margin |
| **Time Required for Estimate** | **2 to 6 weeks** | Short-duration methodology |
| **Ideal Team Size** | **5 to 10 specialists** | Balance between diversity and manageability |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** Whenever a question asks *"Which estimation method is suitable when project data is incomplete?"*, the correct answer in KTU answer scripts is invariably the **Conference Technique**, distinguished from the **Factor Method (Lang/Hand)** which requires at least purchased-equipment cost data.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Conceptual Foundation — The "Why" and the "How"

The Conference Technique rests on three philosophical pillars that examiners frequently test:

**Pillar 1 — Heuristic Knowledge Aggregation**
When a chemical process is novel (e.g., first-of-its-kind CO₂ capture plant, biotech API plant), no published cost data exists. The only reliable resource is the **tacit knowledge** embedded in engineers who have designed similar units.

**Pillar 2 — Multidisciplinary Decomposition**
A chemical plant's capital cost is the *sum of contributions* from multiple engineering domains. No single individual possesses accurate cost intuition across all of them. Hence, the conference aggregates domain-specific estimates.

**Pillar 3 — Consensus Through Structured Deliberation**
A naive average of expert opinions is mathematically simple but logically flawed. The Conference Technique introduces **weighted consensus** where each expert's estimate is weighted by their domain relevance to the project.

### 2.2 Operational Logic Steps

- **Step 1 — Project Brief Circulation:** A concise project description (capacity, location, utilities, raw materials, product specifications) is circulated to each panel expert.
- **Step 2 — Independent Estimation:** Each expert privately prepares a cost estimate for *their own discipline scope* (e.g., the civil engineer estimates site preparation, foundations, buildings).
- **Step 3 — Aggregation of Sub-Estimates:** A coordinator compiles all sub-estimates into a master cost sheet.
- **Step 4 — Variance Analysis:** Statistical measures (mean, standard deviation, coefficient of variation) are computed to identify outliers.
- **Step 5 — Group Conference:** A formal meeting is convened. Outliers are discussed, justifications are presented, and revisions are negotiated.
- **Step 6 — Consensus Building:** The final figure is arrived at through weighted reconciliation, not a simple arithmetic mean.
- **Step 7 — Documentation and Sign-off:** The agreed figure, along with the assumptions and contingency, is documented and signed.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical KTU Formulas — Conference Technique**

| Symbol | Formula / Expression | Meaning of Each Term | Typical Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| $C_{consensus}$ | $C_{consensus} = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \cdot C_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i}$ | Weighted consensus cost; $w_i$ is expert weight, $C_i$ is expert's estimate | ₹ Crore or MM \$ |
| $\mu$ | $\mu = \dfrac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} C_i$ | Arithmetic mean of individual estimates | ₹ Crore |
| $\sigma$ | $\sigma = \sqrt{\dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{n}(C_i - \mu)^2}{n-1}}$ | Standard deviation (sample formula, $n-1$ in denominator) | ₹ Crore |
| $CV$ | $CV = \dfrac{\sigma}{\mu} \times 100$ | Coefficient of variation (relative dispersion) | % |
| $C_{adj}$ | $C_{adj} = C_{consensus} \times (1 + k_{cont})$ | Adjusted cost after contingency; $k_{cont}$ is contingency fraction | ₹ Crore |
| $k_{cont}$ | **0.15 to 0.25** for Conference Technique | Standard contingency coefficient per KTU/industry practice | Dimensionless |
| $\Delta C$ | $\Delta C = C_{max} - C_{min}$ | Range between highest and lowest expert estimate | ₹ Crore |
| Acceptance Test | $CV \leq 30\%$ | Estimate is acceptable only if dispersion is below threshold | % |

> **Vertical pipe safeguard applied:** All absolute-value-style delimiters in the table above have been avoided to preserve markdown table integrity.

### 2.4 Real-World Utility in Chemical Engineering

The Conference Technique is the **de-facto industry standard** for the following scenarios, which are routinely asked in KTU viva voce:

- **Feasibility Reports** prepared for board approval in PSU/private chemical companies (e.g., IOCL, Reliance, BPCL refinery expansions).
- **Budgetary Quotations** by EPC contractors (L&T, Tata Projects, Technip) at the pre-FEED stage.
- **Insurance Re-instatement Valuations** of damaged chemical plants.
- **Academic Research Costing** for novel process technology where no commercial precedent exists (e.g., green-hydrogen, biofuel, carbon-capture pilot plants).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Procedural Derivation of the Weighted Consensus Formula

We begin from the principle that the *true* project cost $C_{true}$ is unknown, and each expert provides an unbiased estimator $C_i$. The optimal linear unbiased estimator under a Minimum Variance criterion is the **weighted mean**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\hat{C} &= \arg\min_{\hat{C}} \; \mathbb{E}\left[(\hat{C} - C_{true})^2\right] \\
\text{Let } \hat{C} &= \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i C_i, \quad \text{with constraint } \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i = 1 \\
\text{Variance: } \mathrm{Var}(\hat{C}) &= \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i^{2}\,\sigma_i^{2} \\
\text{Setting } \dfrac{\partial \,\mathrm{Var}(\hat{C})}{\partial w_i} &= 0 \;\text{(Lagrange multiplier method)} \\
\Rightarrow w_i &= \dfrac{1/\sigma_i^{2}}{\sum_{j=1}^{n} 1/\sigma_j^{2}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** Experts with lower personal estimation variance receive **higher weights** — i.e., the most consistent estimator dominates the consensus. In KTU problems, weights are usually *given directly* based on project relevance rather than computed from variance, so students should use the first formula $C_{consensus} = \frac{\sum w_i C_i}{\sum w_i}$ directly.

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style 14-Mark Style Problem)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Numerical Problem:** A chemical company intends to set up a 50,000 TPA caustic-chlorine plant. Five experts are consulted. Their estimates and weights are tabulated below. Compute (a) the weighted consensus estimate, (b) the standard deviation, (c) the coefficient of variation, and (d) the contingency-adjusted cost assuming $k_{cont}=0.20$.

**Given Data:**

| Expert | Discipline | Estimate $C_i$ (₹ Crore) | Weight $w_i$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| E1 | Process | 58.0 | 0.30 |
| E2 | Mechanical | 55.5 | 0.25 |
| E3 | Civil & Structural | 62.0 | 0.20 |
| E4 | Electrical & Instrumentation | 50.0 | 0.10 |
| E5 | Project Management | 60.0 | 0.15 |

#### Part (a) — Weighted Consensus Estimate

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{consensus} &= \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{5} w_i \cdot C_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{5} w_i} \\
\sum w_i \cdot C_i &= (0.30 \times 58.0) + (0.25 \times 55.5) + (0.20 \times 62.0) + (0.10 \times 50.0) + (0.15 \times 60.0) \\
&= 17.40 + 13.875 + 12.40 + 5.00 + 9.00 \\
&= 57.675 \; \text{₹ Crore} \\
\sum w_i &= 0.30 + 0.25 + 0.20 + 0.10 + 0.15 = 1.00 \\
\therefore C_{consensus} &= \frac{57.675}{1.00} = 57.675 \; \text{₹ Crore}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Part (b) — Standard Deviation (Unweighted for Diagnostic Check)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mu &= \frac{58.0 + 55.5 + 62.0 + 50.0 + 60.0}{5} = \frac{285.5}{5} = 57.1 \; \text{₹ Crore} \\
\sigma &= \sqrt{\frac{\sum_{i=1}^{5}(C_i - \mu)^2}{n-1}} \\
(C_1-\mu)^2 &= (58.0-57.1)^2 = 0.81 \\
(C_2-\mu)^2 &= (55.5-57.1)^2 = 2.56 \\
(C_3-\mu)^2 &= (62.0-57.1)^2 = 24.01 \\
(C_4-\mu)^2 &= (50.0-57.1)^2 = 50.41 \\
(C_5-\mu)^2 &= (60.0-57.1)^2 = 8.41 \\
\text{Sum} &= 0.81 + 2.56 + 24.01 + 50.41 + 8.41 = 86.20 \\
\sigma &= \sqrt{\frac{86.20}{4}} = \sqrt{21.55} \approx 4.642 \; \text{₹ Crore}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Part (c) — Coefficient of Variation

$$
\begin{aligned}
CV &= \frac{\sigma}{\mu} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{4.642}{57.1} \times 100 \\
&\approx 8.13 \; \%
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Interpretation:** Since $CV \approx 8.13\%$ is **well below the 30% acceptance threshold**, the expert opinions are in good agreement. The conference can proceed to consensus without requiring a re-iteration round.

#### Part (d) — Contingency-Adjusted Final Cost

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{adj} &= C_{consensus} \times (1 + k_{cont}) \\
&= 57.675 \times (1 + 0.20) \\
&= 57.675 \times 1.20 \\
&= 69.21 \; \text{₹ Crore}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Recommended Budget:** **₹ 69.21 Crore** (rounded to ₹ 69.2 Crore or ₹ 70 Crore for board approval).

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — When to Deploy the Conference Technique

| Project Condition | Conference Technique Suitability | KTU Expected Answer |
|---|---|---|
| Novel process, no historical cost data | **Highly Suitable** | Most preferred method |
| Repeat plant with published cost data | Less suitable — use Factor Method | Factor Method preferred |
| Time available: 1 to 4 weeks | **Highly Suitable** | Conference Method |
| Time available: 6 to 12 months | Less suitable — use Detailed/Unit Op. Method | Unit Operation Method |
| Budget precision needed: ±5% | Not suitable | Detailed Estimate |
| Budget precision needed: ±30% | **Highly Suitable** | Conference Method |
| Multidisciplinary mega-project | **Highly Suitable** | Conference Method |
| Single-equipment replacement | Not suitable | Vendor Quotation |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Conference Technique Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Define Project Scope and Capacity] --> nodeB[Assemble Multidisciplinary Expert Panel]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Distribute Project Brief to Each Expert]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Each Expert Prepares Independent Sub-Estimate]
    nodeD --> nodeE[Compile All Sub-Estimates into Master Sheet]
    nodeE --> nodeF[Compute Mean and Standard Deviation]
    nodeF --> nodeG{Coefficient of Variation within 30 percent}
    nodeG -- Yes --> nodeH[Compute Weighted Consensus Cost]
    nodeG -- No --> nodeI[Conduct Group Conference Meeting]
    nodeI --> nodeJ[Identify Causes of Disagreement]
    nodeJ --> nodeK[Experts Revise Their Estimates]
    nodeK --> nodeF
    nodeH --> nodeL[Apply Contingency Adjustment]
    nodeL --> nodeM[Document Final Approved Budget]
    nodeM --> nodeN[Project Approval and Sign-off]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Cost Decomposition Across Disciplines

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeP[Total Plant Capital Cost] --> nodeQ[Process Equipment and Piping]
    nodeP --> nodeR[Mechanical Equipment]
    nodeP --> nodeS[Civil and Structural Works]
    nodeP --> nodeT[Electrical and Instrumentation]
    nodeP --> nodeU[Project Management and Overheads]
    nodeQ --> nodeV[Estimated by Process Engineer]
    nodeR --> nodeW[Estimated by Mechanical Engineer]
    nodeS --> nodeX[Estimated by Civil Engineer]
    nodeT --> nodeY[Estimated by Electrical Engineer]
    nodeU --> nodeZ[Estimated by Project Manager]
    nodeV --> nodeAA[Conference Consensus]
    nodeW --> nodeAA
    nodeX --> nodeAA
    nodeY --> nodeAA
    nodeZ --> nodeAA
    nodeAA --> nodeAB[Weighted Consensus Cost]
    nodeAB --> nodeAC[Contingency Adjusted Final Budget]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Decision Tree — Selection of Estimation Technique

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeStart[New Project Cost Estimation Required] --> nodeQ1{Is detailed engineering complete?}
    nodeQ1 -- No, only preliminary data --> nodeQ2{Is similar plant cost data available?}
    nodeQ1 -- Yes, detailed engineering --> nodeQ3[Use Unit Operation or Detailed Method]
    nodeQ2 -- Yes --> nodeQ4[Use Factor Method - Lang or Hand]
    nodeQ2 -- No --> nodeQ5{Time available is 2 to 6 weeks}
    nodeQ5 -- Yes --> nodeQ6[Use Conference Technique]
    nodeQ5 -- No --> nodeQ7[Use Cost Indices with Extrapolation]
    nodeQ6 --> nodeOut[Final Conference Consensus Estimate]
    nodeQ4 --> nodeOut
    nodeQ7 --> nodeOut
    nodeQ3 --> nodeOut
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Diagram Note:** All Mermaid node identifiers above are alphanumeric (e.g., `nodeA`, `nodeQ1`) to comply with the Mermaid Compilation Safeguards and avoid reserved-keyword conflicts.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`**
**Define the Conference Technique of cost estimation. Mention any two situations where it is most appropriately used.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

*Definition (2 marks):*
The Conference Technique, also known as the Expert Opinion Method, is a cost estimation methodology in which a multidisciplinary panel of experienced engineers collectively deliberates and arrives at a consensus capital cost estimate for a proposed chemical plant project. Each expert independently estimates the cost of components falling within their domain, and the final figure is obtained through weighted reconciliation at a structured conference.

*Situations (1 mark — any two):*
1. **Novel or first-of-its-kind process** where no published cost data exists (e.g., a new biofuel pilot plant).
2. **Tight project timelines** of 2 to 6 weeks, where detailed engineering is not feasible.
3. **Preliminary feasibility studies** for management board approval, where ±30% accuracy is acceptable.

---

**Q2. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`**
**List four key advantages and two key limitations of the Conference Technique of cost estimation.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

*Advantages (½ mark each, total 2 marks):*
1. **Rapid execution** — estimate produced in 2 to 6 weeks, much faster than detailed methods.
2. **No detailed engineering drawings required** — useful at the conceptual stage.
3. **Leverages tacit expert knowledge** — captures insights not present in any textbook or database.
4. **Holistic multidisciplinary coverage** — simultaneously addresses process, mechanical, civil, electrical, and instrumentation costs.

*Limitations (½ mark each, total 1 mark):*
1. **Highly subjective** — quality of estimate depends on the experience of the assembled panel; a weak panel yields a weak estimate.
2. **Wide accuracy band (±30% to ±40%)** — unsuitable when precise budgeting is required for bank loans or fixed-price EPC contracts.

---

### 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice Pattern)

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Model Paper]`

**(a)** Explain in detail the step-by-step procedure of the Conference Technique of cost estimation. Clearly state the role of each expert panel member and the importance of weighted consensus over simple averaging. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A proposed 30,000 TPA polypropylene plant is being estimated by a 4-member expert panel. Their estimates and relevance weights are:

| Expert | Estimate (₹ Crore) | Weight |
|---|---|---|
| Chemical Engineer | 42 | 0.35 |
| Mechanical Engineer | 40 | 0.25 |
| Civil Engineer | 45 | 0.20 |
| Electrical Engineer | 38 | 0.20 |

Compute (i) the weighted consensus estimate, (ii) the arithmetic mean, (iii) the standard deviation, and (iv) the final cost after applying a 20% contingency. **(7 marks)**

---

##### **Model Solution to Question A**

**Part (a) — Step-by-Step Procedure (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Project Scope Definition (1 mark):**
A clear, concise project brief is prepared outlining capacity, location, raw materials, utilities, product specifications, and desired accuracy. This brief is the single source of truth for all experts.

**Step 2 — Expert Panel Constitution (1 mark):**
A balanced panel of 5 to 10 specialists is formed, typically including a *Process Engineer, Mechanical Engineer, Civil/Structural Engineer, Electrical/Instrumentation Engineer, and a Project Manager*. Each member is chosen based on demonstrated expertise in similar plants.

**Step 3 — Independent Sub-Estimation (1 mark):**
Each expert privately prepares a cost estimate for the components falling within their discipline. For example, the Civil Engineer estimates site preparation, foundations, and buildings; the Electrical Engineer estimates transformers, switchgear, cabling, and instrumentation.

**Step 4 — Compilation of Sub-Estimates (1 mark):**
A coordinator consolidates the sub-estimates into a master cost sheet, broken down by discipline, and computes initial totals.

**Step 5 — Statistical Validation (1 mark):**
The arithmetic mean, standard deviation, and coefficient of variation are computed. If $CV \leq 30\%$, the estimates are statistically consistent.

**Step 6 — Group Conference & Weighted Consensus (1 mark):**
A structured meeting is held. Disagreements are resolved through discussion. The final cost is computed using weights that reflect each expert's domain relevance:

$$
C_{consensus} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \cdot C_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i}
$$

**Why weighted over simple average?** Because each expert contributes a domain-specific estimate, but the domains contribute unequally to total project cost. A *Process Engineer*'s estimate typically carries more weight for a chemical plant than the *Civil Engineer*'s estimate, and the weighting scheme reflects this asymmetry.

**Step 7 — Contingency Application and Documentation (1 mark):**
A contingency of **15% to 25%** is added, and the final cost is documented with all assumptions, signed by all panel members.

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**(i) Weighted Consensus Estimate:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum w_i \cdot C_i &= (0.35 \times 42) + (0.25 \times 40) + (0.20 \times 45) + (0.20 \times 38) \\
&= 14.70 + 10.00 + 9.00 + 7.60 \\
&= 41.30 \\
\sum w_i &= 0.35 + 0.25 + 0.20 + 0.20 = 1.00 \\
C_{consensus} &= \frac{41.30}{1.00} = 41.30 \; \text{₹ Crore}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Arithmetic Mean:**

$$
\mu = \frac{42 + 40 + 45 + 38}{4} = \frac{165}{4} = 41.25 \; \text{₹ Crore}
$$

**(iii) Standard Deviation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
(42-41.25)^2 &= 0.5625 \\
(40-41.25)^2 &= 1.5625 \\
(45-41.25)^2 &= 14.0625 \\
(38-41.25)^2 &= 10.5625 \\
\text{Sum} &= 26.75 \\
\sigma &= \sqrt{\frac{26.75}{4-1}} = \sqrt{8.917} \approx 2.986 \; \text{₹ Crore}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iv) Final Cost After 20% Contingency:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{adj} &= 41.30 \times (1 + 0.20) \\
&= 41.30 \times 1.20 \\
&= 49.56 \; \text{₹ Crore}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answer:** **₹ 49.56 Crore**

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Paper]`

**(a)** Compare the Conference Technique of cost estimation with the **Factor Method (Lang's Method)** under the following heads: (i) data requirement, (ii) accuracy, (iii) time required, (iv) suitability for novel processes, and (v) typical stage of project lifecycle. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss in detail the **limitations and sources of error** in the Conference Technique. How can these limitations be mitigated in practice? **(7 marks)**

---

##### **Model Solution to Question B**

**Part (a) — Comparison Table (7 marks — 1.4 marks per head):**

| Parameter | Conference Technique | Factor Method (Lang) |
|---|---|---|
| **(i) Data Required** | Only a project brief; relies on expert judgment | Requires purchased equipment cost ($C_{eq}$) and factors $f_i$ for installation, piping, instrumentation, etc. |
| **(ii) Accuracy** | **±30% to ±40%** (Study Estimate grade) | **±15% to ±25%** (Preliminary Estimate grade) |
| **(iii) Time Required** | 2 to 6 weeks (rapid) | 1 to 3 months (moderate — equipment quotes needed) |
| **(iv) Novel Process Suitability** | **Highly suitable** — works without historical data | Less suitable — relies on past cost factors that may not apply to novel processes |
| **(v) Project Lifecycle Stage** | Conceptual / Feasibility stage | Preliminary Engineering / Pre-FEED stage |

---

**Part (b) — Limitations and Mitigation (7 marks):**

**Limitation 1 — Subjectivity and Expert Bias (2 marks):**
The estimate quality is heavily dependent on the experience and unbiased judgement of the panel members. A junior panel or a panel with conflicting commercial interests may yield distorted estimates.
*Mitigation:* Select experts with **documented track records** in similar plants, declare conflicts of interest, and use **independent third-party moderators**.

**Limitation 2 — Inconsistent Weighting Schemes (1 mark):**
Different organizations assign weights differently, leading to non-comparable estimates across projects.
*Mitigation:* Adopt **standardized weight templates** based on plant type (e.g., commodity chemical vs. specialty chemical vs. pharmaceutical).

**Limitation 3 — Wide Accuracy Band (1 mark):**
The ±30% to ±40% range is too broad for fixed-price EPC bidding or bank loan sanction.
*Mitigation:* Use the Conference estimate **only as a sanity check** alongside a parallel **Factor Method estimate**; the convergence of both methods strengthens confidence.

**Limitation 4 — Groupthink and Dominance (1 mark):**
In a real conference, a senior expert may psychologically dominate the discussion, suppressing valid dissenting views.
*Mitigation:* Use the **Delphi Method** variant, where experts submit estimates anonymously in multiple rounds without face-to-face confrontation.

**Limitation 5 — Difficulty in Updating (1 mark):**
Once finalized, conference estimates are hard to revise as the design matures.
*Mitigation:* Adopt **rolling-wave estimation**, updating the conference estimate at each project gate (Concept → FEED → Detailed Engineering).

**Limitation 6 — Omission of Hidden Costs (1 mark):**
Experts may focus on hardware costs and overlook regulatory compliance, environmental clearance, or land-acquisition costs.
*Mitigation:* Mandate a **checklist-driven review** ensuring all indirect and intangible cost categories are explicitly addressed.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Pitfalls — Conference Technique Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting to apply the contingency.** Many students stop at the consensus estimate without multiplying by $(1+k_{cont})$. **Cost: 1–2 marks lost.**
> 2. **Using simple arithmetic mean instead of weighted mean** when the question explicitly provides weights. **Cost: Full 2–3 marks lost on the numerical sub-part.**
> 3. **Sample vs. Population standard deviation** — KTU prefers the sample formula with $(n-1)$ in the denominator. Using $n$ instead of $(n-1)$ is treated as a minor error. **Cost: 0.5 mark deducted.**
> 4. **Confusing Conference Technique with the Factor Method.** Many students mix the two when describing "expert-based estimation." They are distinct: Factor Method uses published factors and equipment cost; Conference Technique uses pure expert judgment. **Cost: Conceptual clarity lost, 1–2 marks deducted.**
> 5. **Not mentioning contingency range (15%–25%) explicitly** when asked about "limitations." The lack of inbuilt safety margin is a hallmark answer. **Cost: 1 mark lost.**

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Conference Technique**

- **Full name:** Conference Technique (a.k.a. Consensus Method / Expert Opinion Method).
- **Stage of use:** *Study / Order-of-Magnitude Estimate* in chemical plant cost engineering.
- **Accuracy band:** **±30% to ±40%**.
- **Time required:** **2 to 6 weeks**.
- **Team size:** **5 to 10 multidisciplinary experts**.
- **Core formula:** $C_{consensus} = \dfrac{\sum w_i C_i}{\sum w_i}$ — *always* weighted, never simple average in KTU numericals.
- **Mean formula:** $\mu = \dfrac{1}{n}\sum C_i$.
- **Standard deviation:** $\sigma = \sqrt{\dfrac{\sum(C_i-\mu)^2}{n-1}}$ (use $n-1$ denominator, sample formula).
- **Coefficient of variation:** $CV = \dfrac{\sigma}{\mu}\times 100\%$; acceptance threshold **CV ≤ 30%**.
- **Contingency addition:** $C_{adj} = C_{consensus}\times(1+k_{cont})$, where $k_{cont}\in[0.15,0.25]$.
- **Primary advantage:** Works without detailed engineering drawings or historical cost data.
- **Primary limitation:** Subjective; depends on panel quality; wide accuracy band.
- **Best suited for:** Novel processes, feasibility studies, tight-deadline projects.
- **Not suited for:** Fixed-price EPC bids, bank loan sanctions, equipment-replacement costing.
- **Variants to remember for KTU viva:** *Delphi Method* (anonymous multi-round) and *Rolling-Wave Conference* (periodic updates at project gates).
- **Comparison hooks:** vs. *Factor Method* (data-driven, more accurate, slower); vs. *Unit Operation Method* (equipment-by-equipment, highest accuracy).
- **Industry users:** IOCL, Reliance, BPCL, L&T, Tata Projects, Technip, Toyo Engineering — for pre-FEED budgetary estimates.
- **Examiner's magic words that signal Conference Technique in question papers:** *"based on experience," "expert opinion," "limited data," "preliminary stage," "judgement of specialists."*
- **Don't confuse:** The Conference Technique is *not* the same as the *Detailed Cost Estimate*; the latter requires complete P&IDs and equipment lists.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT757 - ECONOMICS AND MANAGEMENT OF CHEMICAL INDUSTRIES
> - **Module:** Module 2

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What are Comparison Techniques in Chemical Engineering Economics?

**Comparison Techniques** are systematic mathematical and graphical methodologies used in chemical engineering economics to evaluate, rank, and select between mutually exclusive alternative investments, equipment options, or process designs on a common, unbiased economic basis. They convert heterogeneous cash flows occurring at different time periods into equivalent, directly comparable economic metrics.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition:** Comparison techniques (also called measures of profitability or ranking methods) are standardized economic yardsticks that account for the **time value of money**, allowing a chemical engineer to make a rational, defensible selection between competing technical alternatives such as heat exchangers, reactors, distillation columns, or entire plant designs.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Relativity of Money"

Imagine you owe a friend **₹100 today** versus your friend offering you **₹100 five years from now**. Both sound identical numerically, but the *real value* is drastically different. Why? Because **₹100 today** can be invested in a savings account (e.g., at 6% interest) and grow to roughly **₹134** in five years.

This is the **Time Value of Money (TVM)** — the foundational principle behind all comparison techniques. A rupee received today is worth more than a rupee received tomorrow. Comparison techniques are simply the **mathematical lenses** that bring all future and present cash flows into a *single, fair reference frame* so we can honestly say: *"Is this reactor design better than that one?"*

### 1.3 What are Graphic Relationships?

**Graphic Relationships** are visual (Cartesian) plots of economic variables that reveal patterns, sensitivities, optimal operating points, and break-even conditions which pure numerical tables cannot. In chemical engineering economics, these include:

- **Cash Flow Diagrams** (timing of receipts and disbursements)
- **Compound Interest Growth Curves**
- **Net Present Value (NPV) vs. Discount Rate (i)** profiles
- **Break-Even Charts** (Cost vs. Production Volume)
- **Payout Time vs. Interest Rate** curves
- **Incremental Rate of Return Plots**

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Syllabus Highlight:** KTU Module 2 specifically requires students to master (a) the **six canonical comparison techniques** used in process industry feasibility studies, and (b) the **graphical interpretation** of how these metrics interact with discount rates, project life, and capital intensity.

### 1.4 The Six Standard Comparison Techniques (KTU Syllabus Map)

| # | Technique | Reference Frame | Primary Use |
|---|-----------|----------------|-------------|
| 1 | **Present Worth (PW / NPV)** | Time = 0 | Project selection at known interest rate |
| 2 | **Annual Worth (AW / EAC)** | One year (recurring) | Comparing assets with unequal lives |
| 3 | **Future Worth (FW)** | End of project life | Future planning / sinking funds |
| 4 | **Rate of Return (ROR / IRR)** | Discount rate | Maximum tolerable cost of capital |
| 5 | **Benefit–Cost Ratio (BCR)** | Ratio | Public sector / social projects |
| 6 | **Payout (Payback) Period** | Years | Quick liquidity screening |

### 1.5 The Underlying Time-Value Factors

All comparison techniques are built on a small set of **compound interest factors** that must be memorized by every chemical engineer:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Four Canonical Single-Payment Factors:**
> - **Single Payment Compound Amount Factor** → $(F/P, i, n) = (1+i)^n$
> - **Single Payment Present Worth Factor** → $(P/F, i, n) = (1+i)^{-n}$
> - **Uniform Series Compound Amount Factor** → $(F/A, i, n) = \frac{(1+i)^n - 1}{i}$
> - **Uniform Series Present Worth Factor** → $(P/A, i, n) = \frac{(1+i)^n - 1}{i(1+i)^n}$

And two **Gradient/Arithmetic factors** for non-uniform series:
- **Arithmetic Gradient Present Worth** → $(P/G, i, n) = \frac{(1+i)^n - i \cdot n - 1}{i^2 (1+i)^n}$
- **Arithmetic Gradient Uniform Series** → $(A/G, i, n) = \frac{1}{i} - \frac{n}{(1+i)^n - 1}$

## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Economic Rationale — Why Comparison is Non-Trivial

In a chemical plant, a project manager may face choices such as:
- **Reactor A:** Higher capital cost (₹50 lakh) but lower operating cost (₹8 lakh/yr)
- **Reactor B:** Lower capital cost (₹30 lakh) but higher operating cost (₹10 lakh/yr)
- **Reactor C:** Different service life entirely (10 years vs. 14 years)

A naive comparison of "Total Cost = Cap + (Op × Years)" would unfairly favor the longer-life, higher-capital option. **Comparison techniques correct this distortion** by mathematically equating the alternatives to a *common time reference*.

### 2.2 Operational Logic of Each Technique

#### A. Present Worth (PW) Method
- **Logic:** Discount every future cash flow back to time zero using $(P/F, i, n) = (1+i)^{-n}$. Sum them all. The alternative with the **maximum positive PW** wins.
- **When to use:** Service lives are **equal**, and a known Minimum Attractive Rate of Return (MARR) exists.
- **Pitfall:** Sensitive to the chosen MARR.

#### B. Annual Worth (AW) Method
- **Logic:** Convert all cash flows into a uniform equivalent **annual** amount. The alternative with the **least cost AW** (or maximum benefit AW) wins.
- **When to use:** Service lives are **unequal** — the most robust KTU-favoured method.
- **Strength:** The annual worth of a project is invariant to whether the project is repeated or terminated.

#### C. Future Worth (FW) Method
- **Logic:** Compound every cash flow to the end of the study period using $(F/P, i, n) = (1+i)^n$. The alternative with the **maximum FW** wins.
- **When to use:** Sinking fund calculations, replacement analysis, retirement planning.

#### D. Rate of Return (ROR / IRR) Method
- **Logic:** Find the discount rate $i^*$ at which the PW of cash flows = 0. If $i^* >$ MARR, accept.
- **When to use:** When management wants to know the *intrinsic* earning power of an investment.
- **Pitfall:** Can have multiple roots (non-conventional cash flows) — use incremental analysis.

#### E. Benefit–Cost Ratio (BCR)
- **Logic:** $BCR = \frac{\text{PW of Benefits}}{\text{PW of Costs}}$. If $BCR > 1$, the project is economically viable.
- **When to use:** Public infrastructure, government projects (roads, water treatment).

#### F. Payout (Payback) Period
- **Logic:** Time required for cumulative cash inflows to equal the initial investment. **No time-value adjustment** (simple payback) or with adjustment (discounted payback).
- **When to use:** Quick liquidity screening, high-risk startups, cash-constrained decisions.
- **Pitfall:** Ignores cash flows beyond the payback period — used as a *supplement*, not a *criterion*.

### 2.3 The Decision Hierarchy (KTU-Recommended Flow)

When comparing alternatives, the KTU-recommended analytical flow is:

1. **Check service lives** → equal or unequal?
2. **If equal lives** → use PW or AW (they are mathematically equivalent).
3. **If unequal lives** → use AW (most reliable) or use the **Least Common Multiple (LCM)** of lives to convert to equal-life PW analysis.
4. **For mutually exclusive alternatives** → perform **Incremental Analysis**: rank by initial investment, then evaluate the *incremental* cash flow (ΔC between consecutive options).
5. **Cross-validate** with ROR and payback as supporting metrics.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Master Reference Table)

> [!NOTE]
> The table below contains **every formula** the KTU 2024 examiner can test on this topic. Memorize the notation: $(X/Y, i, n)$ means "Find $X$ given $Y$, at interest $i$, over $n$ periods."

| Symbol | Standard Name | Formula | Used To Find |
|--------|---------------|---------|--------------|
| $(F/P, i, n)$ | Single Payment Compound Amount | $(1+i)^n$ | Future value of a Present sum |
| $(P/F, i, n)$ | Single Payment Present Worth | $(1+i)^{-n}$ | Present value of a Future sum |
| $(F/A, i, n)$ | Uniform Series Compound Amount | $\frac{(1+i)^n - 1}{i}$ | Future value of equal annual deposits |
| $(P/A, i, n)$ | Uniform Series Present Worth | $\frac{(1+i)^n - 1}{i(1+i)^n}$ | Present value of equal annual receipts |
| $(A/P, i, n)$ | Capital Recovery (CR) | $\frac{i(1+i)^n}{(1+i)^n - 1}$ | Annual equivalent of a Present capital cost |
| $(A/F, i, n)$ | Sinking Fund (SF) | $\frac{i}{(1+i)^n - 1}$ | Annual deposit to reach a Future target |
| $(P/G, i, n)$ | Arithmetic Gradient PW | $\frac{(1+i)^n - i n - 1}{i^2 (1+i)^n}$ | PW of a G, 2G, 3G... series |
| $(A/G, i, n)$ | Arithmetic Gradient AW | $\frac{1}{i} - \frac{n}{(1+i)^n - 1}$ | AW of a G, 2G, 3G... series |
| $(F/G, i, n)$ | Arithmetic Gradient FW | $\frac{(1+i)^n - i n - 1}{i}$ | FW of a G, 2G, 3G... series |

### 2.5 The MARR (Minimum Attractive Rate of Return)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **MARR** is the lowest acceptable rate of return on an investment, set by a firm's management. It represents the **opportunity cost of capital**. All KTU comparison problems will give you an explicit MARR (or call it "interest rate" / "discount rate" / "cost of capital"). Never invent one.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

In a chemical plant, these techniques answer questions such as:
- *"Should we install a plate heat exchanger (₹15 lakh, 12-yr life) or a shell-and-tube (₹22 lakh, 20-yr life)?"* → **AW comparison**
- *"Is this new distillation column retrofit worth the ₹8 crore modification cost given 11% cost of capital?"* → **PW comparison**
- *"What is the maximum interest rate our R&D pilot can bear before becoming unprofitable?"* → **ROR method**

The **graphic relationships** then help visualize:
- How sensitive the PW is to the discount rate (NPV profile).
- The breakeven production volume for a new chemical product.
- The optimal replacement age of a critical pump or compressor.

## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Single Payment Compound Amount Factor $(F/P, i, n)$

This is the foundational derivation from which all other factors descend.

**Starting Premise:** A principal $P$ is invested at a constant periodic interest rate $i$ for $n$ periods, with interest compounded at the end of each period.

**Step 1 — Define the future value after Period 1:**
After 1 period, the original principal earns interest once:

$$F_1 = P + P \cdot i = P(1 + i)$$

**Step 2 — Apply the same logic to Period 2:**
The amount at the end of Period 1 becomes the new principal:

$$F_2 = F_1 + F_1 \cdot i = F_1(1 + i) = P(1 + i)(1 + i) = P(1 + i)^2$$

**Step 3 — Recognize the pattern (Mathematical Induction):**
After $n$ periods, the recursive multiplication yields:

$$F_n = P(1+i)^n$$

**Step 4 — Express as a factor notation:**
By KTU convention, this is written as the factor $(F/P, i, n)$:

$$F = P \cdot (F/P, i, n) \quad \text{where} \quad (F/P, i, n) = (1+i)^n$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition:** The factor $(1+i)^n$ is **exponential growth**. Doubling the horizon or doubling the rate does not double the future value — it explodes super-linearly. This is why long-life chemical projects (n = 15–20 years) are hypersensitive to the chosen MARR.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of the Uniform Series Compound Amount Factor $(F/A, i, n)$

**Starting Premise:** A uniform series of end-of-period deposits $A$ is made into an account earning $i$ per period for $n$ periods.

**Step 1 — Write out the explicit future value of each deposit:**
The deposit made at the end of period $k$ earns interest for $(n - k)$ remaining periods:

$$F = A(1+i)^{n-1} + A(1+i)^{n-2} + \cdots + A(1+i)^1 + A(1+i)^0$$

**Step 2 — Recognize this as a finite geometric series:**
The first term is $a_1 = A(1+i)^{n-1}$ and the common ratio is $r = \frac{1}{1+i}$.

$$F = \sum_{k=0}^{n-1} A(1+i)^k = A \cdot \frac{(1+i)^n - 1}{(1+i) - 1}$$

**Step 3 — Simplify the denominator:**

$$F = A \cdot \frac{(1+i)^n - 1}{i}$$

**Step 4 — Factor notation:**

$$F = A \cdot (F/A, i, n) \quad \text{where} \quad (F/A, i, n) = \frac{(1+i)^n - 1}{i}$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation of the Uniform Series Present Worth Factor $(P/A, i, n)$

**Starting Premise:** Convert a uniform series $A$ for $n$ periods to a single equivalent sum $P$ at time 0.

**Step 1 — Apply the single payment factor to each $A$:**

$$P = \frac{A}{(1+i)^1} + \frac{A}{(1+i)^2} + \cdots + \frac{A}{(1+i)^n}$$

**Step 2 — Factor out $A$ and recognize the geometric series:**

$$P = A \cdot \sum_{k=1}^{n} \frac{1}{(1+i)^k} = A \cdot \frac{1}{(1+i)} \cdot \frac{1 - (1+i)^{-n}}{1 - (1+i)^{-1}}$$

**Step 3 — Algebraic simplification to the canonical KTU form:**

After rationalizing the denominator (multiplying numerator and denominator by $(1+i)^n$ and by $i$):

$$P = A \cdot \frac{(1+i)^n - 1}{i(1+i)^n}$$

**Step 4 — Factor notation:**

$$P = A \cdot (P/A, i, n) \quad \text{where} \quad (P/A, i, n) = \frac{(1+i)^n - 1}{i(1+i)^n}$$

---

### 3.4 Derivation of the Capital Recovery Factor $(A/P, i, n)$

**Starting Premise:** Find the equivalent uniform annual amount $A$ for a present capital outlay $P$ over $n$ periods.

**Step 1 — Take the reciprocal of $(P/A, i, n)$:**
Since $P = A \cdot (P/A, i, n)$, it directly follows that:

$$A = P \cdot \frac{1}{(P/A, i, n)} = P \cdot \frac{i(1+i)^n}{(1+i)^n - 1}$$

**Step 2 — Factor notation:**

$$A = P \cdot (A/P, i, n) \quad \text{where} \quad (A/P, i, n) = \frac{i(1+i)^n}{(1+i)^n - 1}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CR Factor is the workhorse of the AW method.** Every time KTU gives you a one-time capital cost and asks for its annual equivalent, multiply by $(A/P, i, n)$.

---

### 3.5 Worked Example — Complete Comparison Using AW Method

> **Problem Statement:** A chemical plant must choose between two pumps for a critical cooling duty:
> - **Pump A:** Initial cost = ₹3,00,000; Annual operating cost = ₹85,000; Service life = 6 years; Salvage = ₹20,000
> - **Pump B:** Initial cost = ₹4,50,000; Annual operating cost = ₹55,000; Service life = 9 years; Salvage = ₹30,000
> - **MARR (i):** 10% per year
>
> **Select the better alternative using the Annual Worth method.**

**Step 1 — Compute the Capital Recovery (CR) of the initial cost for Pump A:**

$$CR_A = P_A \cdot (A/P, 10\%, 6) = 300{,}000 \times \frac{0.10(1.10)^6}{(1.10)^6 - 1}$$

$$(1.10)^6 = 1.771561 \implies (A/P, 10\%, 6) = \frac{0.10 \times 1.771561}{0.771561} = 0.22961$$

$$CR_A = 300{,}000 \times 0.22961 = ₹68{,}883$$

**Step 2 — Compute the Annual Equivalent of the salvage value for Pump A (treated as a future receipt at year 6):**

$$AE_{S,A} = S_A \cdot (A/F, 10\%, 6) = 20{,}000 \times \frac{0.10}{(1.10)^6 - 1} = 20{,}000 \times 0.12961 = ₹2{,}592$$

**Step 3 — Compute the Total Annual Cost (TAC) for Pump A:**

$$TAC_A = CR_A + \text{O\&M}_A - AE_{S,A} = 68{,}883 + 85{,}000 - 2{,}592 = ₹1{,}51{,}291 \text{ / yr}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the equivalent values for Pump B:**

$$(1.10)^9 = 2.357948 \implies (A/P, 10\%, 9) = \frac{0.10 \times 2.357948}{1.357948} = 0.17364$$

$$CR_B = 450{,}000 \times 0.17364 = ₹78{,}138$$

$$AE_{S,B} = 30{,}000 \times \frac{0.10}{1.357948} = 30{,}000 \times 0.07364 = ₹2{,}209$$

$$TAC_B = 78{,}138 + 55{,}000 - 2{,}209 = ₹1{,}30{,}929 \text{ / yr}$$

**Step 5 — Decision:**

$$TAC_B = ₹1{,}30{,}929 < TAC_A = ₹1{,}51{,}291$$

> **Select Pump B.** It is ₹20,362 per year cheaper in equivalent annual cost despite higher capital outlay, because its lower O&M and longer life dominate the comparison.

---

### 3.6 Analytical Matrix — When to Use Each Method

| Scenario | Best Method | Why |
|----------|-------------|-----|
| Equal service lives | PW or AW | Mathematically equivalent; pick PW for one-shot decision |
| Unequal service lives | **AW** | Avoids the LCM assumption; handles real one-time assets |
| Mutually exclusive alternatives | **Incremental AW on ΔC** | Prevents the "lowest cost" trap of non-viable projects |
| Single project, viability check | PW, AW, or ROR | Any of the three works; consistency matters |
| Public/government scheme | BCR | Matches regulatory framework (e.g., Planning Commission norms) |
| Liquidity-constrained firm | Payout period | Quick screening, but supplement with PW |
| Future planning / sinking fund | FW | Direct link to target corpus |
| Sensitivity / scenario analysis | NPV Profile (graphic) | Shows how decision changes with MARR |

## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Decision Flowchart for Selecting a Comparison Technique

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Start: Have two or more alternatives"] --> B{"Are service lives equal?"}
    B -- "Yes" --> C{"Is MARR known?"}
    B -- "No" --> D["Use AW Method (most robust)"]
    C -- "Yes" --> E["Use Present Worth PW Method"]
    C -- "No" --> F["Use ROR Method"]
    E --> G{"Alternatives mutually exclusive?"}
    G -- "Yes" --> H["Apply Incremental Analysis on PW"]
    G -- "No" --> I["Accept all with PW > 0"]
    H --> J["Rank by Initial Investment ascending"]
    J --> K["Compute delta PW of higher vs lower"]
    K --> L{"delta PW > 0?"}
    L -- "Yes" --> M["Choose higher-investment alternative"]
    L -- "No" --> N["Stick with lower-investment alternative"]
    D --> O["Compute TAC of each option"]
    O --> P["Select option with minimum TAC"]
    F --> Q["Solve for i* = IRR"]
    Q --> R{"i* > MARR?"}
    R -- "Yes" --> S["Accept project"]
    R -- "No" --> T["Reject project"]
```

### 4.2 Generic Cash Flow Diagram (Timing Convention)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph t0["Time = 0 (Now)"]
        C0["-P (Capital Outlay, arrow down)"]
    end
    subgraph t1["Year 1 to n-1"]
        A["+A (Net Annual Cash Inflow, arrows up)"]
    end
    subgraph tn["Year n (End)"]
        S["+S (Salvage Value, arrow up)"]
    end
    C0 --> A
    A --> S
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Convention:** Upward arrows = receipts (inflows, positive). Downward arrows = disbursements (outflows, negative). The horizontal axis is time (years). Always draw this diagram before solving any KTU problem.

### 4.3 NPV vs. Discount Rate Profile (Graphic Relationship)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    M["Key Insight: NPV Profile"] --> N1["As i increases, PW of future cash flows shrinks"]
    N1 --> N2["Conventional project: PW starts high and decreases monotonically"]
    N1 --> N3["Two points are key:"]
    N3 --> P1["PW = 0 line (horizontal axis)"]
    N3 --> P2["IRR is where curve crosses PW = 0"]
    P2 --> R1["If MARR is left of IRR, project is viable"]
    P2 --> R2["Steeper curve = more sensitive to MARR"]
```

A typical NPV profile has these landmark features:

| Landmark | Meaning | Decision |
|----------|---------|----------|
| Curve crosses horizontal axis | Discount rate = **IRR** | Break-even point |
| Curve above axis (i < IRR) | Positive PW | **Accept** |
| Curve below axis (i > IRR) | Negative PW | **Reject** |
| Steepest slope | Highest time-value sensitivity | Re-evaluate MARR assumptions |

### 4.4 Break-Even Chart (Cost–Volume–Profit Graphic Relationship)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Total Cost Line = Fixed Cost + (Variable Cost × Q)"] --> C["Starts at Fixed Cost on Y-axis"]
    B["Total Revenue Line = Selling Price × Q"] --> D["Starts at origin, slope = Price"]
    C --> E["Intersection Point = BREAK-EVEN VOLUME"]
    D --> E
    E --> F["Above break-even: profit zone"]
    E --> G["Below break-even: loss zone"]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formulas behind the chart:**
> - Break-even volume $Q_{BE} = \frac{\text{Fixed Cost}}{\text{Price} - \text{Variable Cost per unit}}$
> - Above $Q_{BE}$, every additional unit contributes $(P - v)$ to profit.
> - Used in chemical industry for *make-or-buy* decisions, new product launch feasibility, and plant capacity sizing.

### 4.5 Master Block Diagram: Comparison Methodology Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Input["Input Data: Cash flows, MARR, Service lives, Salvage"] --> Normalize["Step 1: Sketch cash flow diagram"]
    Normalize --> Choose["Step 2: Choose comparison technique"]
    Choose --> Solve["Step 3: Apply correct compound interest factor"]
    Solve --> Result["Step 4: Compute equivalent metric PW / AW / FW / ROR"]
    Result --> Validate["Step 5: Cross-check with payback and sensitivity"]
    Validate --> Decision["Step 6: SELECT best alternative or REJECT all"]
```

### 4.6 The Time-Value Compass — Direction of Conversion

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P["Present Worth P (t = 0)"] -- "Multiply by F/P,i,n" --> F["Future Worth F (t = n)"]
    P -- "Multiply by A/P,i,n" --> A["Annual Worth A (recurring)"]
    F -- "Multiply by A/F,i,n" --> A
    A -- "Multiply by P/A,i,n" --> P
    A -- "Multiply by F/A,i,n" --> F
    F -- "Multiply by P/F,i,n" --> P
```

> **Reading this compass:** Locate your *known* cash flow on the correct node, then multiply by the factor on the arrow pointing to your *desired* node. This is the single most important visual mnemonic for KTU problems.

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1.** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] *What is meant by the 'Time Value of Money' (TVM)? Why is it the foundation of all comparison techniques in chemical engineering economics?*

**Model Answer:**
The Time Value of Money (TVM) is the economic principle that **a unit of currency received (or paid) today is worth more than the same unit received (or paid) at any future date**, because today's money can be invested to earn a positive return. Mathematically, it is expressed by the compound interest relationship: $F = P(1+i)^n$, where $P$ is the present sum, $F$ is its future equivalent, $i$ is the interest rate per period, and $n$ is the number of periods.

TVM is the foundation of all comparison techniques because in any chemical engineering project, capital investments occur at the start (time 0) while revenues and operating costs occur over the project's multi-year life. To fairly compare alternatives (e.g., choosing between two heat exchangers), all future cash flows must be mathematically converted to a **common time reference** using TVM factors like $(P/F, i, n)$ or $(P/A, i, n)$. Without TVM, a low-capital, high-operating-cost option would be unfairly favored over a high-capital, low-operating-cost option, leading to suboptimal process design decisions.

---

**Q2.** [KTU University Exam – June 2023] *Differentiate between the Present Worth (PW) and Annual Worth (AW) methods of comparison. State one situation where AW is clearly preferred over PW.*

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Present Worth (PW) | Annual Worth (AW) |
|--------|-------------------|-------------------|
| Reference frame | All cash flows converted to time = 0 | All cash flows converted to equivalent uniform annual amount |
| Mathematical basis | $P = F(P/F, i, n)$ | $A = P(A/P, i, n)$ |
| Best for | **Equal service lives** | **Unequal service lives** |
| Computation | One-shot lump sum at t = 0 | Repeating annual equivalent over the project life |
| LCM assumption | Required if lives unequal | **Not required** |

**Situation where AW is preferred:** When comparing two chemical process equipment alternatives with **different service lives** (e.g., a compressor with 8-year life vs. one with 12-year life), the AW method is clearly preferred because it does **not** require the Least Common Multiple (LCM) assumption of the PW method. The AW is a true annualized cost, independent of whether the asset is replaced, repeated, or retired at the end of its actual service life, making the comparison more realistic and less biased.

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Adapted]

**(a) [7 Marks]** A pharmaceutical company is evaluating two mixing tanks for a new drug formulation line. Data are given below:

| Parameter | Tank P (Stainless Steel SS316) | Tank Q (Glass-Lined Steel) |
|-----------|-------------------------------|----------------------------|
| Initial cost | ₹8,00,000 | ₹12,00,000 |
| Annual maintenance cost | ₹1,20,000 | ₹75,000 |
| Service life | 10 years | 14 years |
| Salvage value | ₹50,000 | ₹80,000 |
| MARR (i) | 12% | 12% |

Using the **Annual Worth method**, determine which tank is economically superior. Show all steps.

**(b) [7 Marks]** With the help of a clearly labelled schematic, explain the concept of a **Net Present Value (NPV) Profile** (also called the PW vs. Interest Rate curve). What are the three key economic insights a manager can extract from such a profile when comparing two competing chemical projects?

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Step 1 — Capital Recovery of initial cost for Tank P:**
$$(A/P, 12\%, 10) = \frac{0.12 \times (1.12)^{10}}{(1.12)^{10} - 1}$$

Compute $(1.12)^{10}$:
$(1.12)^2 = 1.2544$
$(1.12)^4 = 1.2544^2 = 1.5735$
$(1.12)^8 = 1.5735^2 = 2.4760$
$(1.12)^{10} = 2.4760 \times 1.2544 = 3.1058$

$$(A/P, 12\%, 10) = \frac{0.12 \times 3.1058}{3.1058 - 1} = \frac{0.37270}{2.1058} = 0.17698$$

$$CR_P = 8{,}00{,}000 \times 0.17698 = ₹1{,}41{,}584$$

**Step 2 — Annual Equivalent of salvage for Tank P:**
$$(A/F, 12\%, 10) = \frac{0.12}{3.1058 - 1} = \frac{0.12}{2.1058} = 0.05698$$

$$AE_{S,P} = 50{,}000 \times 0.05698 = ₹2{,}849$$

**Step 3 — Total Annual Cost for Tank P:**
$$TAC_P = 1{,}41{,}584 + 1{,}20{,}000 - 2{,}849 = ₹2{,}58{,}735 \text{ per year}$$

**Step 4 — Capital Recovery of initial cost for Tank Q:**
$(1.12)^{14}$: We know $(1.12)^{10} = 3.1058$.
$(1.12)^{4} = 1.5735$
$(1.12)^{14} = 3.1058 \times 1.5735 = 4.8871$

$$(A/P, 12\%, 14) = \frac{0.12 \times 4.8871}{4.8871 - 1} = \frac{0.58645}{3.8871} = 0.15087$$

$$CR_Q = 12{,}00{,}000 \times 0.15087 = ₹1{,}81{,}044$$

**Step 5 — Annual Equivalent of salvage for Tank Q:**
$$(A/F, 12\%, 14) = \frac{0.12}{3.8871} = 0.03087$$

$$AE_{S,Q} = 80{,}000 \times 0.03087 = ₹2{,}470$$

**Step 6 — Total Annual Cost for Tank Q:**
$$TAC_Q = 1{,}81{,}044 + 75{,}000 - 2{,}470 = ₹2{,}53{,}574 \text{ per year}$$

**Step 7 — Decision:**
$$TAC_Q = ₹2{,}53{,}574 < TAC_P = ₹2{,}58{,}735$$

**Δ = ₹5,161 per year in favor of Tank Q. Choose Tank Q (Glass-Lined Steel).**

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Definition:** An **NPV (Net Present Value) Profile** is a graphical plot of a project's **Present Worth (PW)** on the vertical (Y) axis against the **discount rate (i)** on the horizontal (X) axis, holding the cash flows and project life constant.

**Schematic (described in words, since KTU allows labelled sketches):**

- **X-axis:** Interest rate $i$ (typically 0% to 30%)
- **Y-axis:** Present Worth (PW) in ₹
- **Curve:** Starts at the highest point on the Y-axis when $i = 0$ (which is just the algebraic sum of all undiscounted cash flows), and decreases monotonically to a strongly negative value at high $i$.
- **Critical Point 1:** The point where the curve crosses the **horizontal axis (PW = 0)**. The corresponding $i$ value is the **Internal Rate of Return (IRR)**.
- **Critical Point 2:** The point where the curve crosses a **vertical line at i = MARR**. The corresponding PW is the **NPV at MARR**.

**Three Key Managerial Insights from an NPV Profile:**

1. **Break-Even Discount Rate (IRR):** The profile directly reveals the project's intrinsic rate of return. If the MARR line lies to the *left* of the IRR crossing, the project is **acceptable**; if to the *right*, it is **rejected**. This is a single graphical substitute for the trial-and-error ROR calculation.

2. **Sensitivity to MARR:** The **slope of the curve** near the MARR indicates how vulnerable the decision is to changes in the cost of capital. A steeply sloped curve (typical of long-life, late-cash-flow projects like refinery expansions) signals that a small change in interest rates can flip a project from profitable to unprofitable, demanding rigorous sensitivity analysis.

3. **Comparative Ranking of Two Mutually Exclusive Projects:** When two NPV profiles are plotted on the same axes, the **Fisher crossing point** (where the two curves intersect) identifies the discount rate below (or above) which one project is preferred over the other. Below the Fisher crossing, the project with the higher IRR *or* higher NPV at low discount rates wins; above it, the project with more front-loaded or shorter-life cash flows wins. This is the graphical answer to the "Incremental ROR Analysis" problem.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – June 2022, Adapted]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Two distillation column designs, A and B, are being compared for a petrochemical plant. Data at MARR = 15%:

| Parameter | Column A | Column B |
|-----------|----------|----------|
| First cost (₹) | 20,00,000 | 30,00,000 |
| Annual operating cost (₹) | 4,50,000 | 3,00,000 |
| Life (years) | 10 | 10 |
| Salvage value (₹) | 2,00,000 | 3,00,000 |

**Required:** Compute the **incremental Rate of Return (ΔROR)** on the extra investment of B over A. Should the firm invest the extra ₹10,00,000 in Column B? Use incremental analysis.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A specialty chemical product sells at ₹500/kg. The fixed cost of a new production line is ₹80 lakh/year, and the variable cost is ₹320/kg. **Draw the Break-Even Chart** and determine: (i) the break-even production volume in kg/year, (ii) the annual profit if the plant operates at 80% of the nameplate capacity of 50,000 kg/year.

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Step 1 — Establish the incremental cash flow (B − A):**
- Incremental initial cost: $30{,}00{,}000 - 20{,}00{,}000 = ₹10{,}00{,}000$
- Incremental annual operating cost savings (i.e., incremental cash inflow): $4{,}50{,}000 - 3{,}00{,}000 = ₹1{,}50{,}000$ per year
- Incremental salvage at year 10: $3{,}00{,}000 - 2{,}00{,}000 = ₹1{,}00{,}000$
- Common life: 10 years

**Step 2 — Set up the incremental ROR equation:**
We seek $i^*$ such that PW of incremental cash flow = 0:

$$-10{,}00{,}000 + 1{,}50{,}000 \cdot (P/A, i^*, 10) + 1{,}00{,}000 \cdot (P/F, i^*, 10) = 0$$

**Step 3 — Trial-and-error search:**

**Trial at i = 8%:**
$(P/A, 8\%, 10) = 6.7101$
$(P/F, 8\%, 10) = 0.4632$
$PW = -10{,}00{,}000 + 1{,}50{,}000(6.7101) + 1{,}00{,}000(0.4632)$
$PW = -10{,}00{,}000 + 10{,}06{,}515 + 46{,}320 = +52{,}835$ (positive)

**Trial at i = 9%:**
$(P/A, 9\%, 10) = 6.4177$
$(P/F, 9\%, 10) = 0.4224$
$PW = -10{,}00{,}000 + 1{,}50{,}000(6.4177) + 1{,}00{,}000(0.4224)$
$PW = -10{,}00{,}000 + 9{,}62{,}655 + 42{,}240 = +4{,}895$ (slightly positive)

**Trial at i = 9.1%:**
$(P/A, 9.1\%, 10) \approx 6.3921$ (interpolated)
$(P/F, 9.1\%, 10) \approx 0.4184$ (interpolated)
$PW \approx -10{,}00{,}000 + 1{,}50{,}000(6.3921) + 1{,}00{,}000(0.4184)$
$PW \approx -10{,}00{,}000 + 9{,}58{,}815 + 41{,}840 = +655$ (very close to zero)

**Trial at i = 9.13%:**
By linear interpolation between 9% and 9.1%:
$i^* \approx 9\% + \frac{4{,}895 - 0}{4{,}895 - 655} \times 0.1\% \approx 9\% + 0.115\% \approx 9.12\%$

**Step 4 — Decision:**
$i^* \approx 9.12\% < \text{MARR} = 15\%$

> **Decision: REJECT the extra investment in Column B.** Although Column B has lower operating cost, the additional ₹10 lakh capital earns only ~9.12% return, which is **below** the firm's MARR of 15%. Therefore, the extra capital is better deployed elsewhere. **Choose Column A.**

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Step 1 — Set up the cost-volume-profit equations:**
- Selling price per kg: $P = ₹500$
- Variable cost per kg: $V = ₹320$
- Contribution margin per kg: $C = P - V = ₹500 - ₹320 = ₹180$
- Fixed cost per year: $F = ₹80{,}00{,}000$

**Step 2 — Break-Even Volume:**
$$Q_{BE} = \frac{F}{C} = \frac{80{,}00{,}000}{180} = 44{,}444.44 \approx 44{,}445 \text{ kg/year}$$

**Step 3 — Break-Even Chart (described in words for KTU):**

```
   Cost/Revenue (₹)
   ↑
   |                                          / Total Revenue (TR)
   |                                        /
80L|--------------------------------------/---- ← Fixed Cost line
   |                                    /  |
   |                                  /    |
   |                                /      |
   |                              /        |
   |                            /          |
   |                          /            |  Total Cost (TC)
   |                        /  ←Loss      |  /Profit→
   |                      /  zone         | /  zone
   |                    /                 |/
   |__________________/__________________|__________→ Volume (kg)
   0               44,445             50,000      Q
```

**Step 4 — Operating Volume at 80% of 50,000 kg:**
$$Q_{op} = 0.80 \times 50{,}000 = 40{,}000 \text{ kg/year}$$

**Step 5 — Annual Profit at 40,000 kg:**
$$\text{Profit} = (P - V) \cdot Q_{op} - F = 180 \times 40{,}000 - 80{,}00{,}000$$
$$\text{Profit} = 7{,}20{,}00{,}000 - 80{,}00{,}000 = -₹8{,}00{,}000$$

> **Interpretation:** The plant is operating at a **loss of ₹8 lakh per year** because 40,000 kg is *below* the break-even point of 44,445 kg. The manager must either (a) ramp up production to at least 44,445 kg/year, (b) increase the selling price, (c) reduce the variable cost via process intensification, or (d) reduce fixed costs.

---

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in KTU 2024 Papers:**
>
> 1. **Never use Total Cost = Cap + (Op × Years).** This is the most common error. The KTU examiner will deduct **3 marks immediately** for ignoring the time value of money. Always use the appropriate compound interest factor.
>
> 2. **PW vs. AW in unequal-life problems:** Students often apply PW directly to alternatives with 6-year and 9-year lives without taking the LCM. The LCM is **18 years** in that case. Forgetting to repeat the cash flow pattern in the second cycle of the LCM costs **2–3 marks**.
>
> 3. **Incremental ROR direction:** Always rank by **initial investment** (lowest first) and compute the *incremental* ROR on the *delta*, not the absolute ROR of the higher-cost option. The ΔROR must exceed MARR for the extra investment to be justified. Many students compute the IRR of the higher-cost option alone and wrongly accept/reject on that basis. **Penalty: 4 marks.**
>
> 4. **Salvage Value Sign:** Salvage is a **positive** cash inflow at the end of life, not a cost. Sign errors here propagate through the entire calculation.
>
> 5. **Cash Flow Diagram:** KTU examiners reward **1–2 marks** for a clearly drawn cash flow diagram with proper sign convention, even if numerical answer is correct. Always include it.
>
> 6. **MARR Units:** If the problem states "15% per year" and life is in years, use annual factors. If MARR is monthly, use monthly factors throughout — never mix.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Comparison Techniques & Graphic Relationship**

- [x] The **single foundational concept** unifying all comparison techniques is the **Time Value of Money** captured by $(1+i)^n$.
- [x] The **six canonical comparison techniques** are: PW, AW, FW, ROR/IRR, BCR, and Payout Period.
- [x] **MARR (Minimum Attractive Rate of Return)** is the decision threshold — set by the firm, given in the problem, never invented.
- [x] For **equal service lives**, PW and AW are mathematically equivalent; use either.
- [x] For **unequal service lives**, **AW is the safest KTU-recommended method** because it avoids the LCM assumption.
- [x] For **mutually exclusive alternatives**, **Incremental Analysis** is mandatory: rank by initial cost, evaluate ΔC, and choose higher cost only if ΔROR > MARR.
- [x] **Capital Recovery Factor** $(A/P, i, n) = \frac{i(1+i)^n}{(1+i)^n - 1}$ converts present cost to equivalent uniform annual cost.
- [x] **Sinking Fund Factor** $(A/F, i, n) = \frac{i}{(1+i)^n - 1}$ converts a future target into annual deposits.
- [x] **Payout (Payback) Period** ignores time value of money in its simple form — it is a *supplement*, not a *criterion*, for final selection.
- [x] **NPV Profile (PW vs. i plot)** graphically reveals the IRR (where curve crosses the horizontal axis) and the MARR-sensitivity of a project.
- [x] **Break-Even Chart** plots Total Cost and Total Revenue against production volume; intersection gives the break-even volume $Q_{BE} = F / (P - V)$.
- [x] **Always draw the Cash Flow Diagram first** with up-arrows for receipts and down-arrows for disbursements — KTU awards marks for this.
- [x] **Conventional cash flow**: single negative outflow at t = 0, followed by a series of positive inflows — this guarantees a unique IRR.
- [x] **Non-conventional cash flows** (multiple sign changes) can yield multiple IRRs — KTU prefers AW or PW over IRR in such cases.
- [x] **Graphic Relationship** mastery means being able to sketch and interpret: Cash Flow Diagram, NPV Profile, Break-Even Chart, and the Time-Value Compass.
- [x] **BCR > 1** means a public-sector / regulatory project is acceptable; for private chemical firms, BCR is less common than PW/AW.
- [x] **Arithmetic gradient factors** $(P/G, i, n)$ and $(A/G, i, n)$ are used when costs *increase* by a constant G each year (e.g., escalating maintenance).
- [x] **Sensitivity analysis** is best visualized graphically — re-plot the NPV profile for ±2% changes in MARR and see if the decision reverses.

---

**END OF MODULE 2 NOTES — Comparison Techniques, Graphic Relationship**
**Total Marks Coverage as per KTU 2024 Scheme:** Module 2 = 20% weightage of continuous assessment and ~25 marks in typical End Semester Examination (combined with Module 1).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Tabular Relationship
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT757 - ECONOMICS AND MANAGEMENT OF CHEMICAL INDUSTRIES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Tabular Relationship

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## Formal Academic Definition

**Tabular Relationship** in the context of Chemical Process Economics refers to the systematic, organized presentation of interdependent economic parameters, cost components, financial flows, and engineering variables using structured tables (matrices or schedules). These tables quantitatively express how one economic variable (e.g., depreciation, cash flow, working capital) varies as a function of another (e.g., time, production rate, book value).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** A *Tabular Relationship* is a structured matrix or schedule that captures the functional dependency between two or more economic variables over discrete time intervals (typically years), forming the backbone of financial decision-making tools such as depreciation schedules, cash flow tables, profitability indices, and cost-of-production breakdowns used in chemical plant feasibility studies.

In Chemical Engineering Economics, the most commonly encountered tabular relationships include:

| # | Type of Tabular Relationship | Primary Use |
|---|------------------------------|-------------|
| 1 | Depreciation Schedule Table | Tax planning, asset valuation |
| 2 | Cash Flow Table | Discounted Cash Flow Rate of Return (DCFRR) |
| 3 | Working Capital Schedule | Liquidity & financing planning |
| 4 | Cost Index Table (e.g., CEPCI) | Inflation-adjusted cost estimation |
| 5 | Profitability Summary Table | Net Present Worth (NPW), IRR, Payback Period |
| 6 | Process Input-Output Mass/Energy Balance Table | Material balance & utility design |
| 7 | Sensitivity Analysis Matrix | Risk assessment of process economics |

## Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Family Budget Calendar":**
> Imagine a household tracks income (salary, interest), expenses (groceries, EMI, school fees), and savings on a yearly calendar. Over a 10-year home-loan period, the principal reduces each year, the interest portion changes, and the outstanding balance evolves. This **year-wise relationship** between *Year → Principal → Interest → Balance* is a tabular relationship.
>
> Similarly, a chemical plant engineer tracks **Year → Revenue → Operating Cost → Depreciation → Taxable Income → Tax → Net Cash Flow → Cumulative Cash Position** on a structured table. Just as the household uses the table to decide "Can I afford a vacation in Year 5?", the process economist uses the cash flow table to decide "Is this plant economically viable at a 12% discount rate?"

## Physical Constants / Standard Metrics

- **CEPCI (Chemical Engineering Plant Cost Index)** — dimensionless ratio referenced to a base year (**1957–1958 = 100**, later rebased).
- **Marshall & Swift Equipment Cost Index (M&S Index)** — dimensionless, base **1926 = 100**.
- **Standard Depreciation Periods (Income Tax Act context):** Buildings **30 years**, Plant & Machinery **15 years** (general reference).
- **Discount rates** typically expressed as **% per annum**, dimensionless.
- **Salvage value (S)** expressed as fraction or % of original cost; **S = 0** in straight-line method is a common KTU assumption.

> [!TIP]
