> **KTU Board Examiner's Eye:** Always explicitly state assumptions (salvage value, tax rate, depreciation method, project life) *above* your table — partial credit is heavily awarded for clear tabulation headers.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## Structural Logic of a Tabular Relationship

Every tabular relationship in Chemical Process Economics follows a **3-layer architecture**:

1. **Input Layer (Independent Variable):** Usually *time (year)*, *production rate*, or *cost index year*.
2. **Transformation Layer (Mathematical Operator):** A formula or rule that converts the input into a derived output (e.g., depreciation formula, interest formula, discount factor).
3. **Output Layer (Dependent Variable):** The economic parameter of interest (book value, net cash flow, present worth factor).

### Step-by-Step Logic Construction

**Step 1 — Identify the Independent Axis**
The vertical (row) axis is almost always **time in years (0, 1, 2, …, n)**. Year 0 represents the construction/investment year; Year 1 onwards represents the operating phase.

**Step 2 — Identify the Dependent Columns**
Typical columns include:
- Gross Revenue / Sales
- Total Production Cost (Manufacturing + Fixed + Overhead)
- Depreciation (D)
- Taxable Income (TI)
- Income Tax (T = TI × tax rate)
- Net Profit After Tax (PAT)
- Add back Depreciation (non-cash)
- **Net Cash Flow (NCF)**
- Discount Factor (DF = 1/(1+i)ⁿ)
- Discounted Cash Flow (DCF = NCF × DF)
- Cumulative Discounted Cash Flow (ΣDCF)

**Step 3 — Apply the Governing Formulae Row-by-Row**
Each cell of the table is computed by substituting the row's year (n) into the appropriate formula.

**Step 4 — Compute the Decision Metric**
From the completed table, derive:
- **Net Present Worth (NPW)** = Σ DCF
- **Discounted Payback Period** = year when ΣDCF first becomes positive
- **DCFRR (IRR)** = i such that ΣDCF = 0 (interpolated)

## KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulae below are **high-yield** for KTU 2024 ESE — master the shaded ones first.

| # | Concept | Formula | Symbol Glossary | Unit |
|---|---------|---------|----------------|------|
| 1 | Straight-Line Depreciation | $D_t = \dfrac{C - S}{n}$ | C = Capital cost, S = Salvage, n = Life, t = Year | Currency/year |
| 2 | Book Value at year t | $BV_t = C - t \cdot D_t$ | BV declines linearly | Currency |
| 3 | Declining Balance Rate | $\alpha = \dfrac{1 - (S/C)^{1/n}}{}$ note: 200% DB → $D_t = 2 \cdot BV_{t-1} / n$ | α = depreciation fraction | dimensionless |
| 4 | Sum-of-Years-Digits (SOYD) | $D_t = \dfrac{(n - t + 1)}{S_n} \cdot (C - S)$, where $S_n = n(n+1)/2$ | Sₙ = sum of years | Currency/year |
| 5 | Net Cash Flow (after tax) | $NCF_t = (S_t - C_t)(1 - \tau) + D_t$ | Sₜ = Sales, Cₜ = Costs, τ = tax rate | Currency/year |
| 6 | Single Payment Present Worth Factor | $P/F = \dfrac{1}{(1+i)^n}$ | i = discount rate, n = year | dimensionless |
| 7 | Uniform Series Present Worth Factor | $P/A = \dfrac{(1+i)^n - 1}{i(1+i)^n}$ | Converts annuity to present value | dimensionless |
| 8 | Capital Recovery Factor | $A/P = \dfrac{i(1+i)^n}{(1+i)^n - 1}$ | Converts PV to annual equivalent | dimensionless |
| 9 | Future Worth Factor | $F/P = (1+i)^n$ | Compounding factor | dimensionless |
| 10 | CEPCI Cost Escalation | $C_{\text{present}} = C_{\text{past}} \times \dfrac{\text{CEPCI}_{\text{present}}}{\text{CEPCI}_{\text{past}}}$ | Dimensionless ratio | Currency |
| 11 | Net Present Worth | $NPW = \sum_{t=0}^{n} \dfrac{NCF_t}{(1+i)^t}$ | Sum of all discounted NCFs | Currency |
| 12 | Working Capital | $WC = C_{AR} + C_{INV} + C_{CASH} - C_{AP}$ | AR = Receivables, INV = Inventory, AP = Payables | Currency |

> [!WARNING]
> KTU students often confuse **Discount Factor (P/F)** with **Uniform Series Factor (P/A)**. The P/F is for a *single* future amount; P/A is for an *annuity* (equal payments every year). Mixing these up costs 4–5 marks instantly.

## Real-World Utility in Chemical Engineering

- **Feasibility Studies:** Chemical plant projects (e.g., a 1000 TPD ammonia plant) require tabulated cash flows over 10–15 years for bank loan appraisal.
- **Depreciation Planning:** Affects **Income Tax Act** liability, hence directly impacts net profit and dividends.
- **Make-or-Buy Decisions:** Tabular comparison of in-house production cost vs. outsourced purchase price.
- **Inflation Adjustments:** Cost engineers use CEPCI tables to update 2010 vendor quotes to 2024 prices.
- **Sensitivity Analysis:** Engineers build "what-if" tables varying selling price, raw material cost, and discount rate to test project robustness.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## Derivation 1: Straight-Line Depreciation Schedule (Year → Book Value)

### Problem Setup
A chemical reactor costs **C = ₹50,00,000** with salvage value **S = ₹5,00,000** and life **n = 5 years**. Construct the depreciation schedule table.

### Step-by-Step Construction

**Step 1 — Compute Annual Depreciation**

The straight-line depreciation formula states:

$$D_t = \frac{C - S}{n}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute Numerical Values**

$$D_t = \frac{50{,}00{,}000 - 5{,}00{,}000}{5} = \frac{45{,}00{,}000}{5} = 9{,}00{,}000 \text{ ₹/year}$$

**Step 3 — Compute Book Value for Each Year**

The book value at the end of year *t* is the original cost minus accumulated depreciation:

$$BV_t = C - t \cdot D_t$$

**Step 4 — Build the Table Row-by-Row**

For **Year 0 (start):**

$$BV_0 = 50{,}00{,}000 - 0 \cdot 9{,}00{,}000 = 50{,}00{,}000 \text{ ₹}$$

For **Year 1:**

$$BV_1 = 50{,}00{,}000 - 1 \cdot 9{,}00{,}000 = 41{,}00{,}000 \text{ ₹}$$

For **Year 2:**

$$BV_2 = 50{,}00{,}000 - 2 \cdot 9{,}00{,}000 = 32{,}00{,}000 \text{ ₹}$$

For **Year 3:**

$$BV_3 = 50{,}00{,}000 - 3 \cdot 9{,}00{,}000 = 23{,}00{,}000 \text{ ₹}$$

For **Year 4:**

$$BV_4 = 50{,}00{,}000 - 4 \cdot 9{,}00{,}000 = 14{,}00{,}000 \text{ ₹}$$

For **Year 5 (end):**

$$BV_5 = 50{,}00{,}000 - 5 \cdot 9{,}00{,}000 = 5{,}00{,}000 \text{ ₹} = S \checkmark$$

### Resulting Depreciation Schedule Table

| Year (t) | Depreciation (₹) | Accumulated Depreciation (₹) | Book Value (₹) |
|:--------:|:----------------:|:----------------------------:|:--------------:|
| 0        | —                | 0                            | 50,00,000      |
| 1        | 9,00,000         | 9,00,000                     | 41,00,000      |
| 2        | 9,00,000         | 18,00,000                    | 32,00,000      |
| 3        | 9,00,000         | 27,00,000                    | 23,00,000      |
| 4        | 9,00,000         | 36,00,000                    | 14,00,000      |
| 5        | 9,00,000         | 45,00,000                    | 5,00,000       |

> [!NOTE]
> The book value at year 5 exactly equals the salvage value (S), confirming internal consistency of the schedule.

---

## Derivation 2: Cash Flow Table with NPW Computation

### Problem Setup
A proposed chemical plant requires **initial investment = ₹1,00,00,000** at Year 0. The plant generates a uniform **annual net cash flow of ₹25,00,000** from Year 1 to Year 5. Compute the **Net Present Worth** at **i = 10%** and determine the **Discounted Payback Period**.

### Step-by-Step Construction

**Step 1 — Identify the Cash Flow Series**

Year 0: $NCF_0 = -1{,}00{,}00{,}000$ (cash outflow / investment)
Year 1 to 5: $NCF_t = +25{,}00{,}000$ (uniform inflow)

**Step 2 — Compute the Discount Factor for Each Year**

The single-payment present worth factor is:

$$DF_t = \frac{1}{(1 + i)^t} = \frac{1}{(1.10)^t}$$

**Step 3 — Calculate DF for Each Year**

$$DF_1 = \frac{1}{(1.10)^1} = \frac{1}{1.1000} = 0.9091$$

$$DF_2 = \frac{1}{(1.10)^2} = \frac{1}{1.2100} = 0.8264$$

$$DF_3 = \frac{1}{(1.10)^3} = \frac{1}{1.3310} = 0.7513$$

$$DF_4 = \frac{1}{(1.10)^4} = \frac{1}{1.4641} = 0.6830$$

$$DF_5 = \frac{1}{(1.10)^5} = \frac{1}{1.6105} = 0.6209$$

**Step 4 — Compute the Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) for Each Year**

$$DCF_t = NCF_t \times DF_t$$

For Year 0:

$$DCF_0 = -1{,}00{,}00{,}000 \times 1.0000 = -1{,}00{,}00{,}000$$

For Year 1:

$$DCF_1 = 25{,}00{,}000 \times 0.9091 = 22{,}72{,}727$$

For Year 2:

$$DCF_2 = 25{,}00{,}000 \times 0.8264 = 20{,}66{,}116$$

For Year 3:

$$DCF_3 = 25{,}00{,}000 \times 0.7513 = 18{,}78{,}288$$

For Year 4:

$$DCF_4 = 25{,}00{,}000 \times 0.6830 = 17{,}07{,}534$$

For Year 5:

$$DCF_5 = 25{,}00{,}000 \times 0.6209 = 15{,}52{,}304$$

**Step 5 — Compute Cumulative Discounted Cash Flow (ΣDCF)**

$$\Sigma DCF_0 = -1{,}00{,}00{,}000$$

$$\Sigma DCF_1 = -1{,}00{,}00{,}000 + 22{,}72{,}727 = -77{,}27{,}273$$

$$\Sigma DCF_2 = -77{,}27{,}273 + 20{,}66{,}116 = -56{,}61{,}157$$

$$\Sigma DCF_3 = -56{,}61{,}157 + 18{,}78{,}288 = -37{,}82{,}869$$

$$\Sigma DCF_4 = -37{,}82{,}869 + 17{,}07{,}534 = -20{,}75{,}335$$

$$\Sigma DCF_5 = -20{,}75{,}335 + 15{,}52{,}304 = -5{,}23{,}031$$

**Step 6 — Compute Net Present Worth (NPW)**

$$NPW = \sum_{t=0}^{5} DCF_t = -5{,}23{,}031 \text{ ₹}$$

### Resulting Cash Flow Table

| Year (t) | NCF (₹)        | DF @ 10% | DCF (₹)       | Cumulative DCF (₹) |
|:--------:|:--------------:|:--------:|:-------------:|:------------------:|
| 0        | -1,00,00,000   | 1.0000   | -1,00,00,000  | -1,00,00,000       |
| 1        | +25,00,000     | 0.9091   | +22,72,727    | -77,27,273         |
| 2        | +25,00,000     | 0.8264   | +20,66,116    | -56,61,157         |
| 3        | +25,00,000     | 0.7513   | +18,78,288    | -37,82,869         |
| 4        | +25,00,000     | 0.6830   | +17,07,534    | -20,75,335         |
| 5        | +25,00,000     | 0.6209   | +15,52,304    | **-5,23,031**      |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** Since **NPW = -5,23,031 ₹ < 0**, the project **fails the 10% NPW test** — the invested capital is not recovered at a 10% discount rate over 5 years. Decision: **Reject** the project at i = 10%.

### Discounted Payback Period (Linear Interpolation)

Since ΣDCF remains negative throughout Year 5, the **discounted payback period exceeds 5 years**, so the project does not pay back within its operating life at the chosen discount rate.

---

## Derivation 3: Cost Index Escalation Table

### Problem Setup
A vendor quoted a **distillation column in 2015** for **₹45,00,000**. The CEPCI values are: **CEPCI(2015) = 556.8** and **CEPCI(2024) = 803.0**. Compute the **escalated cost in 2024**.

### Step-by-Step Construction

**Step 1 — State the Cost Index Escalation Formula**

$$C_{2024} = C_{2015} \times \frac{\text{CEPCI}_{2024}}{\text{CEPCI}_{2015}}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the Numerical Values**

$$C_{2024} = 45{,}00{,}000 \times \frac{803.0}{556.8}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the Ratio**

$$\frac{803.0}{556.8} = 1.4422$$

**Step 4 — Compute the Final Cost**

$$C_{2024} = 45{,}00{,}000 \times 1.4422 = 64{,}89{,}900 \text{ ₹}$$

### Cost Escalation Summary Table

| Year  | CEPCI   | Equipment Cost (₹)  | Inflation-Adjusted Cost (₹) |
|:-----:|:-------:|:-------------------:|:---------------------------:|
| 2015  | 556.8   | 45,00,000           | 45,00,000 (base)            |
| 2024  | 803.0   | —                   | **64,89,900**               |

> [!TIP]
> **Shortcut Validation:** Inflation over 9 years = (803.0/556.8) - 1 = 44.22% cumulative. This matches general Indian chemical plant inflation trends (~4–5% per year compounded).

---

## Analytical Matrix: Choosing the Right Tabular Relationship

| Decision Goal                          | Required Tabular Relationship        | Key Output Column          |
|----------------------------------------|--------------------------------------|----------------------------|
| Tax-shield optimization                | Depreciation schedule                | Book Value / Depreciation  |
| Bank loan appraisal                    | Cash flow table                      | Net Cash Flow / ΣDCF       |
| Equipment price update                 | Cost index table                     | Escalated Capital Cost     |
| Working capital management             | Working capital schedule             | Net Working Capital        |
| Profitability comparison (alternatives)| Cumulative cash flow table           | NPW, IRR, BCR              |
| Breakeven analysis                     | Production cost vs. sales revenue    | Breakeven point (units/₹)  |
| Risk assessment                        | Sensitivity matrix (input × output)  | Tornado chart data         |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## Diagram 1: Architecture of a Tabular Relationship (Generic)

This block diagram shows how inputs, transformation formulae, and outputs are linked inside any economic tabular relationship in chemical plant design.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Axis: Time / Production Rate / Cost Index Year] --> B[Row Index Generator: t = 0, 1, 2 ... n]
    B --> C[Transformation Layer: Economic Formulae]
    C --> D1[Depreciation Cell: D_t]
    C --> D2[Book Value Cell: BV_t]
    C --> D3[Net Cash Flow Cell: NCF_t]
    C --> D4[Discount Factor Cell: P/F or P/A]
    C --> D5[Discounted Cash Flow: DCF_t]
    D1 --> E[Tabular Output Matrix]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    D4 --> E
    D5 --> E
    E --> F[Decision Metrics: NPW / IRR / Payback / BCR]
    F --> G{Investment Decision}
    G -->|Accept| H[Proceed with Project]
    G -->|Reject| I[Abandon / Reconfigure Project]
```

## Diagram 2: Cash Flow Table Generation Pipeline

This diagram illustrates the sequential flow used to construct a cash flow table from raw plant data, a critical skill tested in KTU ESE.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Plant Cost Data: Capital, Working Capital, Salvage] --> B[Revenue Forecast Table]
    A --> C[Operating Cost Forecast Table]
    B --> D[Gross Profit Table]
    C --> D
    D --> E[Depreciation Schedule]
    E --> F[Taxable Income Table]
    F --> G[Tax Computation Table]
    G --> H[Net Profit After Tax Table]
    H --> I[Add Back Non-Cash Depreciation]
    I --> J[Net Cash Flow Table]
    J --> K[Apply Discount Factor P/F]
    K --> L[Discounted Cash Flow Table]
    L --> M[Cumulative DCF Curve]
    M --> N[Decision: NPW and Payback]
```

## Diagram 3: Inter-Relationship Among Tabular Outputs

This diagram shows how the columns of a complete economic table are mathematically linked. Each node is a column, and each edge is a formulaic dependency.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    CAP[Capital Investment] --> DEP[Depreciation]
    DEP --> TI[Taxable Income]
    TI --> TAX[Income Tax]
    TAX --> PAT[Net Profit After Tax]
    PAT --> NCF[Net Cash Flow]
    DEP --> NCF
    NCF --> DCF[Discounted Cash Flow]
    DCF --> NPW[Net Present Worth]
    DCF --> PAY[Payback Period]
    NPW --> DEC{Decision}
    PAY --> DEC
    DEC -->|NPW > 0| ACC[Accept]
    DEC -->|NPW < 0| REJ[Reject]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The **book value** flows downward through depreciation → taxable income → tax → net profit → net cash flow, while the **discount factor** applies horizontally to convert each annual NCF into its present value.

## Diagram 4: Cost Index Table Usage (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Vendor Quotation: Year X, Cost CX] --> B[Locate CEPCI for Year X]
    C[Target Year Y] --> D[Locate CEPCI for Year Y]
    B --> E[Compute Escalation Ratio: CEPCIY / CEPCIX]
    D --> E
    E --> F[Apply Ratio: CY = CX * Escalation Ratio]
    F --> G[Updated Equipment Cost for Year Y]
    G --> H[Add Installation, Piping, Instrumentation Factors]
    H --> I[Total Plant Capital Cost Estimate]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define a Tabular Relationship as used in Chemical Process Economics. List any three types of such tables with their primary purpose.**

**Model Answer:**

A **Tabular Relationship** is a structured matrix that quantifies the dependency of one or more economic variables on another (typically time), enabling engineers to compute financial decision metrics for a chemical plant project.

The three principal types of tables are:

1. **Depreciation Schedule Table** — Used to compute annual depreciation, accumulated depreciation, and book value of equipment for tax and accounting purposes.
2. **Cash Flow Table** — Used to track annual revenue, cost, tax, and net cash flow for NPW, IRR, and payback period calculations.
3. **Cost Index Table (e.g., CEPCI)** — Used to escalate historical equipment costs to present-day values for capital cost estimation.

*(3 marks: 1 for definition + 0.5 × 2 marks for each table type with purpose)*

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Write the general format of a cash flow table for a chemical plant with a uniform annual net cash flow. State the formula for Net Present Worth (NPW).**

**Model Answer:**

The general format of a cash flow table contains the following columns:

| Year (t) | Capital Investment | Revenue | Operating Cost | Depreciation | Taxable Income | Tax | Net Profit After Tax | Net Cash Flow | Discount Factor (P/F) | Discounted Cash Flow | Cumulative DCF |
|:--------:|:------------------:|:-------:|:--------------:|:------------:|:--------------:|:---:|:--------------------:|:-------------:|:---------------------:|:--------------------:|:--------------:|
| 0        | X                  | —       | —              | —            | —              | —   | —                    | -X            | 1.0000                | -X                   | -X             |
| 1        | —                  | R₁      | OC₁            | D₁           | TI₁            | T₁  | PAT₁                 | NCF₁          | 1/(1+i)               | DCF₁                 | ΣDCF₁          |
| ...      | ...                | ...     | ...            | ...          | ...            | ... | ...                  | ...           | ...                   | ...                  | ...            |
| n        | —                  | Rₙ      | OCₙ            | Dₙ           | TIₙ            | Tₙ  | PATₙ                 | NCFₙ          | 1/(1+i)ⁿ              | DCFₙ                 | ΣDCFₙ          |

The **Net Present Worth** is computed as:

$$NPW = \sum_{t=0}^{n} \frac{NCF_t}{(1+i)^t}$$

where *NCFₜ* is the net cash flow in year *t*, *i* is the discount rate, and *n* is the project life.

*(3 marks: 1.5 for table format + 1.5 for NPW formula)*

---

## Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Module 2 Choice 1]**

**(a)** A chemical plant equipment costs **₹20,00,000** with a salvage value of **₹2,00,000** and a useful life of **5 years**. Construct the **straight-line depreciation schedule table** showing year, depreciation, accumulated depreciation, and book value. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** Using the above data, construct a **cash flow table** assuming a uniform annual revenue of **₹8,00,000**, uniform operating cost of **₹3,00,000** (excluding depreciation), and a tax rate of **30%**. Compute the **Net Present Worth** at **i = 12%** and comment on project viability. **[7 marks]**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Depreciation Schedule [7 marks]**

**Step 1: Compute Annual Depreciation**

$$D = \frac{C - S}{n} = \frac{20{,}00{,}000 - 2{,}00{,}000}{5} = \frac{18{,}00{,}000}{5} = 3{,}60{,}000 \text{ ₹/year}$$

**Step 2: Compute Book Value Year-Wise**

$$BV_t = C - t \cdot D = 20{,}00{,}000 - t \times 3{,}60{,}000$$

**Depreciation Schedule Table:**

| Year (t) | Depreciation D (₹) | Accumulated Depreciation (₹) | Book Value BV (₹) |
|:--------:|:-------------------:|:-----------------------------:|:------------------:|
| 0        | —                   | 0                             | 20,00,000          |
| 1        | 3,60,000            | 3,60,000                      | 16,40,000          |
| 2        | 3,60,000            | 7,20,000                      | 12,80,000          |
| 3        | 3,60,000            | 10,80,000                     | 9,20,000           |
| 4        | 3,60,000            | 14,40,000                     | 5,60,000           |
| 5        | 3,60,000            | 18,00,000                     | 2,00,000 (= S) ✓   |

*(1 mark: formula identification, 2 marks: numerical substitution, 2 marks: table population, 2 marks: validation of BV at year n = S)*

---

**Part (b) — Cash Flow Table and NPW [7 marks]**

**Step 1: Compute Taxable Income Per Year**

For each operating year (Year 1 to Year 5):

$$TI_t = \text{Revenue} - \text{Operating Cost} - D_t$$

$$TI_t = 8{,}00{,}000 - 3{,}00{,}000 - 3{,}60{,}000 = 1{,}40{,}000 \text{ ₹/year}$$

**Step 2: Compute Tax**

$$T_t = TI_t \times 0.30 = 1{,}40{,}000 \times 0.30 = 42{,}000 \text{ ₹/year}$$

**Step 3: Compute Net Cash Flow**

$$NCF_t = (TI_t - T_t) + D_t = (1{,}40{,}000 - 42{,}000) + 3{,}60{,}000 = 4{,}58{,}000 \text{ ₹/year}$$

**Step 4: Compute Discount Factors at i = 12%**

$$DF_t = \frac{1}{(1.12)^t}$$

**Step 5: Compute Discounted Cash Flow**

$$DCF_t = NCF_t \times DF_t$$

**Cash Flow Table:**

| Year (t) | Revenue (₹) | Op. Cost (₹) | Dep. (₹) | Taxable Income (₹) | Tax @ 30% (₹) | Net Cash Flow (₹) | DF @ 12% | DCF (₹)       |
|:--------:|:-----------:|:------------:|:--------:|:------------------:|:-------------:|:-----------------:|:--------:|:-------------:|
| 0        | —           | —            | —        | —                  | —             | —                 | 1.0000   | —             |
| 1        | 8,00,000    | 3,00,000     | 3,60,000 | 1,40,000           | 42,000        | 4,58,000          | 0.8929   | 4,08,946      |
| 2        | 8,00,000    | 3,00,000     | 3,60,000 | 1,40,000           | 42,000        | 4,58,000          | 0.7972   | 3,65,131      |
| 3        | 8,00,000    | 3,00,000     | 3,60,000 | 1,40,000           | 42,000        | 4,58,000          | 0.7118   | 3,26,011      |
| 4        | 8,00,000    | 3,00,000     | 3,60,000 | 1,40,000           | 42,000        | 4,58,000          | 0.6355   | 2,91,082      |
| 5        | 8,00,000    | 3,00,000     | 3,60,000 | 1,40,000           | 42,000        | 4,58,000          | 0.5674   | 2,59,894      |

**Step 6: Sum All DCFs (NPW) [Assuming Year 0 investment is already covered, i.e., the table shows operating-phase NCF only. Add Year 0 = 0 for operating cash flow analysis.]**

$$NPW = 4{,}08{,}946 + 3{,}65{,}131 + 3{,}26{,}011 + 2{,}91{,}082 + 2{,}59{,}894 = 16{,}51{,}064 \text{ ₹}$$

**Step 7: Comment on Viability**

Since **NPW = ₹16,51,064 > 0** at i = 12%, the project **generates positive present value** and is **economically viable / acceptable** at the 12% discount rate.

*(2 marks: NCF derivation, 2 marks: discount factor computation, 2 marks: table + summation, 1 mark: economic interpretation)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Module 2 Choice 2]**

**(a)** Explain the **CEPCI cost index method** for updating historical equipment costs. A heat exchanger was quoted in **2010** for **₹18,00,000** when **CEPCI(2010) = 550.8**. If **CEPCI(2024) = 803.0**, compute the **escalated cost in 2024**. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** Define **Working Capital**. Construct a working capital schedule for a chemical plant with the following data: Raw material inventory = ₹6,00,000; Finished goods inventory = ₹9,00,000; Accounts receivable = ₹4,50,000; Cash balance = ₹1,50,000; Accounts payable = ₹3,00,000. Comment on the working capital position. **[7 marks]**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — CEPCI Cost Index Method [7 marks]**

**Step 1: Concept Explanation (3 marks)**

The **Chemical Engineering Plant Cost Index (CEPCI)** is a dimensionless number published monthly in *Chemical Engineering* magazine that tracks inflation in the process equipment market. It is referenced to a base year (1957–58 = 100). The cost of any process equipment at any future year can be estimated from a past quote using:

$$C_{\text{present}} = C_{\text{past}} \times \frac{\text{CEPCI}_{\text{present}}}{\text{CEPCI}_{\text{past}}}$$

**Step 2: Numerical Computation (4 marks)**

$$C_{2024} = 18{,}00{,}000 \times \frac{803.0}{550.8}$$

Compute the ratio:

$$\frac{803.0}{550.8} = 1.4578$$

Therefore:

$$C_{2024} = 18{,}00{,}000 \times 1.4578 = 26{,}24{,}040 \text{ ₹}$$

**Conclusion:** The heat exchanger that cost ₹18,00,000 in 2010 would cost approximately **₹26,24,040** in 2024 due to inflation in process equipment markets (a **~45.78% increase** over 14 years, or ~2.74% CAGR).

---

**Part (b) — Working Capital Schedule [7 marks]**

**Step 1: Definition (2 marks)**

**Working Capital (WC)** is the capital required for day-to-day operations of a firm. It represents the difference between current assets and current liabilities:

$$WC = C_{\text{current assets}} - C_{\text{current liabilities}}$$

Current assets include raw materials inventory, finished goods inventory, accounts receivable, and cash. Current liabilities include accounts payable and short-term loans.

**Step 2: Working Capital Schedule Table (3 marks)**

| S.No. | Component                              | Type            | Amount (₹) |
|:-----:|:--------------------------------------:|:---------------:|:----------:|
| 1     | Raw Material Inventory                 | Current Asset   | 6,00,000   |
| 2     | Finished Goods Inventory               | Current Asset   | 9,00,000   |
| 3     | Accounts Receivable                    | Current Asset   | 4,50,000   |
| 4     | Cash Balance                           | Current Asset   | 1,50,000   |
|       | **Total Current Assets (TCA)**         |                 | **21,00,000** |
| 5     | Accounts Payable                       | Current Liability | 3,00,000 |
|       | **Total Current Liabilities (TCL)**    |                 | **3,00,000**  |
|       | **Net Working Capital (NWC = TCA - TCL)** |              | **18,00,000** |

**Step 3: Comment on Working Capital Position (2 marks)**

The plant has a **Net Working Capital of ₹18,00,000**, which is **positive and substantial**. This indicates:

- **Strength:** The plant has sufficient liquid resources to meet short-term obligations and operational expenses.
- **Concern:** A very high working capital tied up in inventory (raw materials + finished goods = ₹15,00,000, ~71% of TCA) suggests possible **overstocking**, which increases holding cost and ties up capital. The plant should consider **just-in-time (JIT) inventory** practices to optimize.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Tabular Relationship Questions**
> 1. **Missing Table Headers:** Always label every column clearly (₹, units, year). A missing header costs 1 mark even if numbers are correct.
> 2. **Year 0 vs Year 1 Confusion:** Year 0 is the *investment year* (cash outflow). Operations start in Year 1. Mixing these up reverses the NPW sign.
> 3. **Forgetting to Add Back Depreciation:** Net Cash Flow = (Revenue – Cost – Dep) × (1 – τ) + Dep. Many students stop at Net Profit and forget the add-back, losing 2 marks.
> 4. **No Validation Step:** Always verify that BV at year *n* = S (for SLN) or that ΣDCF matches expected magnitude. Examiners award bonus marks for validation.
> 5. **Discount Factor Rounding:** Use **4-decimal precision** (e.g., 0.8929) — do not round to 2 decimals (0.89) as it causes significant cumulative error in NPW.
> 6. **Unit Inconsistency:** Mixing lakhs and crores within the same table confuses evaluators — use **one unit system throughout** (preferably ₹ or ₹ lakhs).

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Quick-Reference Summary for KTU ESE**

- ✅ A **Tabular Relationship** quantifies how one economic variable (e.g., cash flow, book value) depends on another (e.g., time) using a structured table.
- ✅ The **straight-line depreciation** is constant each year: $D = (C - S)/n$.
- ✅ The **book value at year n** must equal the salvage value S — this is your validation check.
- ✅ **Net Cash Flow (NCF)** = (Revenue – Cost – Dep)(1 – τ) + Dep. **Always add back depreciation** since it is a non-cash expense.
- ✅ The **discount factor** is $P/F = 1/(1+i)^n$. Use 4-decimal precision.
- ✅ **NPW > 0** ⇒ Accept the project; **NPW < 0** ⇒ Reject.
- ✅ **CEPCI escalation formula:** $C_{\text{new}} = C_{\text{old}} \times (\text{CEPCI}_{\text{new}} / \text{CEPCI}_{\text{old}})$.
- ✅ **Working Capital** = Current Assets – Current Liabilities. Positive WC = healthy liquidity.
- ✅ Common tables: Depreciation Schedule, Cash Flow, Cost Index, Working Capital, Profitability Summary.
- ✅ Always **state assumptions** (salvage, tax rate, project life, discount rate) *above* the table.
- ✅ Use a **single, consistent currency unit** throughout the entire table.
- ✅ In KTU ESE, the **table itself carries 2–3 marks** — draw it neatly with ruled lines; do not use plain text lists.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Detailed Item Estimate
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT757 - ECONOMICS AND MANAGEMENT OF CHEMICAL INDUSTRIES
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Detailed Item Estimate

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Detailed Item Estimate (also called Detailed or Quasi-Detailed Estimate):**
> A bottom-up engineering cost estimate in which every major piece of process equipment (reactors, columns, heat exchangers, pumps, compressors, vessels, etc.) is individually sized, priced, and itemized, after which the installed, auxiliary, and indirect costs are calculated using well-defined multiplying factors (factorial or "Lang" / "Hand" methods). The accuracy expected is typically within **±5 % to ±15 %** of the final plant cost, and it is prepared at the end of the **Process Design / Basic Engineering** stage, just before detailed engineering.

In the standard AACE (American Association of Cost Engineers) classification this corresponds to a **Class 3 / Class 2 estimate**. It is the level at which a project receives "funding approval" in real chemical industry practice.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain-English Intuition)

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world analogy — "Building a custom house":**
> Think of constructing a house. An **order-of-magnitude** estimate is saying, "A house costs roughly ₹x per square foot" — a wild guess. A **study (factored) estimate** is "₹5,000 per sq.ft × 2,000 sq.ft = ₹1 Crore" — still rough. A **detailed item estimate**, however, is asking: *the kitchen needs 30 cabinets at ₹4,000 each, 12 windows, 200 m of wiring, 8 light fixtures, 1 geyser, 2 days of plumber's labour* — you list every nut and bolt (well, every meaningful item) and add them up. Only then can you confidently tell the client the bill.

The same logic applies to a chemical plant: every reactor, every pump, every column is "itemized" and aggregated, instead of using a single global multiplier on the whole plant.

### 1.3 Position of Detailed Item Estimate in the Hierarchy

| Estimate Type | Typical Accuracy | When Prepared | Data Required |
|---|---|---|---|
| Order-of-Magnitude (Ratio) | $\pm 30 \,\% \text{ to } \pm 50 \,\%$ | Initial screening | Plant capacity only |
| Study / Factored (Lang/Hand) | $\pm 25 \,\% \text{ to } \pm 40 \,\%$ | Preliminary feasibility | Major equipment list |
| **Detailed Item Estimate** | $\pm 5 \,\% \text{ to } \pm 15 \,\%$ | After process design | Complete PFD, P&ID, equipment specs |
| Definitive / Check | $\pm 1 \,\% \text{ to } \pm 5 \,\%$ | After detailed engineering | Bids, vendor quotes |

### 1.4 Standard Metrics Used

- **CEPCI** — Chemical Engineering Plant Cost Index (annual)
- **Marshall & Swift (M&S) Equipment Cost Index**
- **Nelson–Farrar Refinery Construction Index**
- **Scaling exponent $n$** — typically **0.6 (six-tenths rule)** for chemical equipment
- **Lang factor $f_L$** — typically **3.10 (solids) / 4.74 (fluids) / 3.63 (mixed)**
- **Hand factor $f_H$** — typically **3.30 (solids) / 4.40 (fluids) / 3.90 (mixed)**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Conceptual Steps of a Detailed Item Estimate

A KTU-style answer must follow this logical order:

1. **Obtain the equipment list** from the Process Flow Diagram (PFD) and material/energy balance.
2. **Size every item** of equipment (vessel, column, heat exchanger, pump, compressor, etc.).
3. **Purchase (bare) cost of each equipment item** ($C_{p,i}$) — obtained from vendor data, in-house correlations, or published charts (e.g., Peters & Timmerhaus, Turton, Couper, Sinnott-Towler).
4. **Update purchase cost to present day** using a cost index:
   $$C_{p,\text{now}} = C_{p,\text{ref}} \times \frac{I_{\text{now}}}{I_{\text{ref}}}$$
5. **Compute the bare module cost** of each item (or its installed cost) using the factorial approach.
6. **Add the cost of all items** to get the **Total Module Cost (TMC)** or **Total Bare Module Cost (TBMC)**.
7. **Add auxiliary facilities** (boiler house, cooling tower, water treatment, tankage, utilities off-sites) — typically $20\text{–}40 \,\%$ of TMC.
8. **Add field indirects** (construction, freight, insurance) and **engineering costs**.
9. **Add contingency** (typically $8\text{–}15 \,\%$ of the total).
10. **Add working capital** (typically $10\text{–}20 \,\%$ of the fixed capital investment, FCI) and **start-up cost** if required.
11. **Obtain Total Capital Investment (TCI)** = Fixed Capital + Working Capital.

### 2.2 The "Why" Behind the Detailed Approach

> [!NOTE]
> The detailed approach is preferred over a single global Lang factor because:
> - Different materials of construction (carbon steel vs. stainless steel vs. special alloys) carry very different multipliers.
> - The factorial breakdown lets the project manager see **where the money is going** and identify cost-reduction targets (value engineering).
> - Tax depreciation schedules require equipment-level data.
> - Replacement-cost and insurance valuations require itemized data.
> - It permits **sensitivity analysis** on individual items.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **complete formula bank** required for KTU 2024 Scheme problems on this topic. All symbols are KTU-conventional.

| # | Formula | Description | Boundary / Typical Value |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $C_B = C_A \, (S_B \vert S_A)^n$ | Six-tenths rule cost scaling by capacity/size | $n = 0.6$ (most chemical equipment) |
| 2 | $C_{\text{now}} = C_{\text{ref}} \, (I_{\text{now}} \vert I_{\text{ref}})$ | Time-update of cost using cost index | CEPCI, M&S, Nelson–Farrar |
| 3 | $C_{\text{TM}} = f_L \cdot \sum C_p$ | Lang factor method (Total Module) | $f_L = 3.10$ solids, $4.74$ fluids, $3.63$ mixed |
| 4 | $C_{\text{TM}} = f_H \cdot \sum C_p$ | Hand factor method (Total Module) | $f_H = 3.30$ solids, $4.40$ fluids, $3.90$ mixed |
| 5 | $C_{\text{BM},i} = C_{p,i} \left(1 + f_{\text{er}} + f_p + f_{\text{el}} + f_i + f_c + f_s + f_l\right)$ | Bare Module (installed) cost of item $i$ | Uses module-factors |
| 6 | $C_{\text{TM}} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} C_{\text{BM},i}$ | Total Bare Module (Total Module) cost | Sum over all items |
| 7 | $C_{\text{aux}} = 0.20 \text{ to } 0.40 \, C_{\text{TM}}$ | Auxiliary / offsite facilities | For new plant sites |
| 8 | $C_{\text{FCI}} = 1.18 \left( C_{\text{TM}} + C_{\text{aux}} \right)$ | Fixed Capital Investment (incl. 18 % contingency & indirects) | 1.18 is the standard Guth–Couper multiplier |
| 9 | $C_{\text{WC}} = 0.10 \text{ to } 0.20 \, C_{\text{FCI}}$ | Working Capital | Material + product inventories, cash, receivables |
| 10 | $C_{\text{TCI}} = C_{\text{FCI}} + C_{\text{WC}}$ | Total Capital Investment | Final result required in problems |
| 11 | $f_{\text{er}}$ | Equipment–material–labour ratio (erection factor) | $\approx 0.20$ to $0.30$ |
| 12 | $f_p$ | Piping factor | $\approx 0.30$ to $0.80$ |
| 13 | $f_{\text{el}}$ | Electrical factor | $\approx 0.10$ to $0.20$ |
| 14 | $f_i$ | Instrumentation & control factor | $\approx 0.10$ to $0.30$ |
| 15 | $f_c$ | Civil / structural factor | $\approx 0.15$ to $0.30$ |
| 16 | $f_s$ | Insulation factor | $\approx 0.02$ to $0.10$ |
| 17 | $f_l$ | Lagging / paint / scaffolding factor | $\approx 0.05$ to $0.15$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Quick check:** The sum of all module factors $(1 + f_{\text{er}} + f_p + f_{\text{el}} + f_i + f_c + f_s + f_l)$ for a typical CS-equipment fluid-processing plant is $\approx 2.5\text{–}3.5$. Multiplying by $\sum C_p$ should approach the Lang-factor result, which is the **internal consistency test** KTU examiners often use.

### 2.6 Real-World Utility of Detailed Item Estimation

- **Feasibility reports** of petrochemical, refinery, fertilizer, polymer, and specialty chemical plants.
- **Insurance and replacement valuations** for old plants.
- **Tax depreciation schedules** (each item depreciates differently).
- **Capital-vs-operating-cost trade-off studies** (e.g., buy a larger pump vs. lower utility cost).
- **Build-Own-Operate (BOO) / EPC contract bid preparation**.
- **Annual budgeting** of capex for corporate planning.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation — Six-Tenths Rule

The six-tenths rule arises from a logarithmic regression of historical cost-vs-capacity data for a large population of chemical equipment:

$$
\log C = \log a + n \, \log S
$$

where $C$ is the purchase cost, $S$ is the size/capacity parameter, and $n$ is the slope of the $\log$–$\log$ regression. For a broad set of equipment, **$n \approx 0.6$**, hence the name "six-tenths rule".

For two equipment items of the same type but different sizes $S_A$ and $S_B$:

$$
\frac{C_B}{C_A} = \left(\frac{S_B}{S_A}\right)^{n}
$$

$$
\therefore \quad C_B = C_A \, \left(\frac{S_B}{S_A}\right)^{n}
$$

**Sanity check:** Doubling the capacity increases the cost by a factor of $2^{0.6} \approx 1.516$, i.e., only ~52 % more — this is the well-known **economy of scale** in chemical plants.

### 3.2 Derivation — Cost Index Updating

If an equipment cost $C_{\text{ref}}$ is known in the reference year, its current cost is:

$$
C_{\text{now}} = C_{\text{ref}} \times \frac{I_{\text{now}}}{I_{\text{ref}}}
$$

**Why this works:** Cost indices are basket-weighted averages of labour, material, and energy costs. The price of an item has changed in proportion to the index between the two years. This is **linear in the index** by construction.

### 3.3 Derivation — Bare Module Cost from Module Factors

For an equipment item of purchase cost $C_{p,i}$ the bare (installed) module cost is the sum of (a) the equipment itself, (b) installation labour, (c) piping, (d) electrical, (e) instrumentation, (f) civil, (g) insulation, and (h) lagging:

$$
C_{\text{BM},i} = C_{p,i} + C_{\text{erection}} + C_{\text{piping}} + C_{\text{electrical}} + C_{\text{instr}} + C_{\text{civil}} + C_{\text{insul}} + C_{\text{lag}}
$$

Each term is empirically a fixed fraction of $C_{p,i}$ for a given equipment class:

$$
C_{\text{erection}} = f_{\text{er}} \, C_{p,i}, \quad C_{\text{piping}} = f_p \, C_{p,i}, \quad \ldots
$$

Substituting all eight terms:

$$
C_{\text{BM},i} = C_{p,i}\left(1 + f_{\text{er}} + f_p + f_{\text{el}} + f_i + f_c + f_s + f_l\right)
$$

Summing over $N$ equipment items:

$$
C_{\text{TM}} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} C_{\text{BM},i}
$$

### 3.4 Derivation — Fixed Capital Investment (Guth & Couper Multiplier)

The Total Module Cost $C_{\text{TM}}$ is the sum of all "onsite" erected modules. To it we must add:

- **Auxiliary facilities** (boiler house, cooling tower, tankage, environmental) = $A \cdot C_{\text{TM}}$
- **Field indirects** (construction supervision, freight, insurance, taxes) = $0.05 \cdot (C_{\text{TM}} + C_{\text{aux}})$
- **Engineering & home office** = $0.10 \cdot (C_{\text{TM}} + C_{\text{aux}})$
- **Contingency** = $0.10 \cdot (C_{\text{TM}} + C_{\text{aux}})$

Adding them and grouping gives the **1.18 multiplier** (Guth & Couper):

$$
C_{\text{FCI}} = 1.18 \, C_{\text{TM,total}} \quad \text{(standard textbook value)}
$$

### 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU 14-Mark Standard)

**Problem:** A fluid-processing plant has four major items with purchase costs ₹12, 18, 24, 30 lakh in 2018. Estimate the **Total Capital Investment in 2024** using the Lang factor for fluids ($f_L = 4.74$). Use CEPCI: 2018 = 603.1, 2024 = 800.0. Working capital = 15 % of FCI. Auxiliary = 25 % of $C_{\text{TM}}$.

**Step 1 — Cost index update to 2024 (each item):**

$$
C_{p,\text{now}} = C_{p,2018} \times \frac{800.0}{603.1}
$$

Multiplier $M = 800.0 \div 603.1 = 1.3265$

- Item 1: $12 \times 1.3265 = 15.918$ lakh
- Item 2: $18 \times 1.3265 = 23.877$ lakh
- Item 3: $24 \times 1.3265 = 31.836$ lakh
- Item 4: $30 \times 1.3265 = 39.795$ lakh

**Step 2 — Sum of purchase costs:**

$$
\sum C_p = 15.918 + 23.877 + 31.836 + 39.795 = 111.426 \text{ lakh}
$$

**Step 3 — Total Module Cost using Lang factor (fluids):**

$$
C_{\text{TM}} = 4.74 \times 111.426 = 528.16 \text{ lakh}
$$

**Step 4 — Auxiliary facilities (25 % of $C_{\text{TM}}$):**

$$
C_{\text{aux}} = 0.25 \times 528.16 = 132.04 \text{ lakh}
$$

**Step 5 — Fixed Capital Investment (Guth & Couper, 1.18):**

$$
C_{\text{FCI}} = 1.18 \times (528.16 + 132.04) = 1.18 \times 660.20 = 778.04 \text{ lakh}
$$

**Step 6 — Working Capital (15 % of FCI):**

$$
C_{\text{WC}} = 0.15 \times 778.04 = 116.71 \text{ lakh}
$$

**Step 7 — Total Capital Investment:**

$$
\boxed{C_{\text{TCI}} = 778.04 + 116.71 = 894.75 \text{ lakh} \approx \text{₹ 8.95 Crore}}
$$

### 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Choice of Method

| Situation | Recommended Method | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Few items, all fluid, same material of construction | **Lang factor** | Quick, classic KTU approach |
| Mixed materials (CS + SS + alloy) | **Module-factor method** | Captures material-specific multipliers |
| Very early stage, only capacity known | **Power-law scaling** (six-tenths) | Needs only one known reference cost |
| Many items, audit-grade required | **Hand factor** (refined) | Better than Lang for fluid + solids mix |
| Corporate valuation, insurance | **Itemized + CEPCI** | Traceable, defensible |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Flowchart — Step-by-Step Process of a Detailed Item Estimate

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Start: PFD and M&E Balance available] --> A1[Step 1: Generate complete equipment list from PFD]
    A1 --> A2[Step 2: Size every item: V, A, kW, m2, etc.]
    A2 --> A3[Step 3: Look up purchase cost Cp_i from chart or vendor quote]
    A3 --> A4{Step 4: Is the cost data on present date?}
    A4 -- No --> A5[Apply cost index update: Cp_now = Cp_ref x I_now / I_ref]
    A5 --> A6[Step 5: Compute bare module cost C_BM_i using module factors]
    A4 -- Yes --> A6
    A6 --> A7[Step 6: Sum all C_BM_i to obtain Total Module Cost C_TM]
    A7 --> A8[Step 7: Add auxiliary facilities C_aux = 0.20 to 0.40 x C_TM]
    A8 --> A9[Step 8: Apply Guth-Couper 1.18 multiplier for FCI = 1.18 x C_TM_total]
    A9 --> A10[Step 9: Compute Working Capital WC = 0.10 to 0.20 x FCI]
    A10 --> A11[Step 10: Total Capital Investment TCI = FCI + WC]
    A11 --> A12[End: Deliver Class 3 estimate to management]
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of the Cost Build-up

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[PFD + M and E Balance] --> B1[Equipment List]
    B1 --> C1[Purchase Cost Cp_i]
    C1 --> D1[Bare Module Cost C_BM_i per item]
    D1 --> E1[Total Module Cost C_TM]
    E1 --> F1[Auxiliary Facilities]
    F1 --> G1[Field Indirects]
    G1 --> H1[Engineering and Home Office]
    H1 --> I1[Contingency]
    I1 --> J1[Fixed Capital Investment FCI]
    J1 --> K1[Working Capital WC]
    K1 --> L1[Total Capital Investment TCI]
```

### 4.3 Cost-Sankey-Style Tree — Where the Money Goes

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P0[Total Capital Investment TCI] --> P1[Fixed Capital Investment FCI ~ 85%]
    P0 --> P2[Working Capital WC ~ 15%]
    P1 --> Q1[Total Module Cost C_TM]
    P1 --> Q2[Auxiliary Facilities]
    P1 --> Q3[Field Indirects 5%]
    P1 --> Q4[Engineering 10%]
    P1 --> Q5[Contingency 10%]
    Q1 --> R1[Sum of Bare Module Costs C_BM_i]
    R1 --> S1[Equipment Erection]
    R1 --> S2[Piping]
    R1 --> S3[Electrical]
    R1 --> S4[Instrumentation]
    R1 --> S5[Civil and Structural]
    R1 --> S6[Insulation]
    R1 --> S7[Lagging and Paint]
```

### 4.4 Decision Tree — Which Multiplier to Use

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    D0[Choose Estimation Method] --> D1{Are equipment materials different?}
    D1 -- Yes --> D2[Use Module Factor Method]
    D1 -- No --> D3{Is it fluid or solid processing?}
    D3 -- Fluid --> D4[Use Lang factor f_L = 4.74]
    D3 -- Solid --> D5[Use Lang factor f_L = 3.10]
    D3 -- Mixed --> D6[Use Hand factor f_H = 3.90]
    D2 --> D7[Compute per-item C_BM_i and sum]
    D4 --> D8[Apply C_TM = f_L x sum Cp]
    D5 --> D8
    D6 --> D9[Apply C_TM = f_H x sum Cp]
    D7 --> D10[Add Aux then 1.18 multiplier for FCI]
    D8 --> D10
    D9 --> D10
    D10 --> D11[Add Working Capital 10 to 20 percent of FCI]
    D11 --> D12[Final: Total Capital Investment TCI]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. Define "Detailed Item Estimate" and state its expected accuracy.**
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

> **Model Answer:**
> A *Detailed Item Estimate* is a bottom-up cost estimate in which every major piece of process equipment is individually sized, priced, and listed, and the installed cost of each is built up using module factors (Lang / Hand / factorial). It is prepared at the close of basic/process engineering. The expected accuracy is **$\pm 5 \,\%$ to $\pm 15 \,\%$** of the actual project cost, corresponding to AACE Class 3.

**Q2. What is the six-tenths rule? Why is the exponent 0.6?**
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

> **Model Answer:**
> The six-tenths rule states that for two pieces of similar equipment differing only in size $S$, the cost scales as
> $C_B = C_A \, (S_B / S_A)^{0.6}$.
> The exponent 0.6 arises empirically: a logarithmic regression of the purchase cost versus capacity for thousands of chemical equipment items yields a slope close to 0.6. It reflects the well-known **economy of scale** — capacity grows faster than cost (in the limit, $C \propto S^{2/3}$ for geometrically similar vessels).

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer (14 Marks; Internal Choice)

#### Question A (14 Marks) — Comprehensive Calculation

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

A fluid-processing chemical plant has the following six major equipment items with purchase costs in 2020 (CEPCI 2020 = 596.2):

| Item | Cost in 2020 (₹ lakh) |
|---|---|
| Reactor | 50 |
| Distillation column | 75 |
| Heat exchanger | 25 |
| Pump | 8 |
| Compressor | 40 |
| Storage tank | 12 |

Estimate the **Total Capital Investment in 2024** (CEPCI 2024 = 800.0) using:

- (a) Lang factor method for fluids ($f_L = 4.74$) and (b) the Guth–Couper 1.18 multiplier, with working capital = 15 % of FCI and auxiliary facilities = 30 % of $C_{\text{TM}}$. **(7 marks)**
- (a) Hand factor method ($f_H = 4.40$ for fluids) and (b) comment on which result is more conservative and why. **(7 marks)**

---

**Solution:**

**Part (a) — Lang Factor Method (7 marks)**

Step 1: Cost-index update multiplier:

$$
M = \frac{800.0}{596.2} = 1.3418
$$

Step 2: Updated 2024 purchase costs (each = 2020 cost × M):

| Item | Cost 2020 (₹ lakh) | Cost 2024 (₹ lakh) |
|---|---|---|
| Reactor | 50 | 67.09 |
| Column | 75 | 100.64 |
| HX | 25 | 33.55 |
| Pump | 8 | 10.73 |
| Compressor | 40 | 53.67 |
| Tank | 12 | 16.10 |
| **Sum $\sum C_p$** | — | **281.78** |

Step 3: Total Module Cost:

$$
C_{\text{TM}} = 4.74 \times 281.78 = 1335.6 \text{ lakh}
$$

Step 4: Auxiliary facilities (30 % of $C_{\text{TM}}$):

$$
C_{\text{aux}} = 0.30 \times 1335.6 = 400.7 \text{ lakh}
$$

Step 5: Fixed Capital Investment:

$$
C_{\text{FCI}} = 1.18 \times (1335.6 + 400.7) = 1.18 \times 1736.3 = 2048.8 \text{ lakh}
$$

Step 6: Working Capital (15 % of FCI):

$$
C_{\text{WC}} = 0.15 \times 2048.8 = 307.3 \text{ lakh}
$$

Step 7: Total Capital Investment:

$$
\boxed{C_{\text{TCI}} = 2048.8 + 307.3 = 2356.1 \text{ lakh} \approx \text{₹ 23.56 Crore}}
$$

**Part (b) — Hand Factor Method & Comparison (7 marks)**

Step 1: Sum of purchase costs = ₹ 281.78 lakh (same as above).

Step 2: Total Module Cost (Hand factor, fluids, $f_H = 4.40$):

$$
C_{\text{TM,Hand}} = 4.40 \times 281.78 = 1239.8 \text{ lakh}
$$

Step 3: Auxiliary (30 %):

$$
C_{\text{aux}} = 0.30 \times 1239.8 = 371.9 \text{ lakh}
$$

Step 4: FCI:

$$
C_{\text{FCI}} = 1.18 \times (1239.8 + 371.9) = 1.18 \times 1611.7 = 1901.8 \text{ lakh}
$$

Step 5: Working capital (15 %):

$$
C_{\text{WC}} = 0.15 \times 1901.8 = 285.3 \text{ lakh}
$$

Step 6: Total Capital Investment:

$$
\boxed{C_{\text{TCI,Hand}} = 1901.8 + 285.3 = 2187.1 \text{ lakh} \approx \text{₹ 21.87 Crore}}
$$

**Comparison comment (model answer):**
The Lang factor ($4.74$) gives a higher TCI than the Hand factor ($4.40$) for the same fluid plant. The Lang factor is therefore **more conservative** (i.e., gives a higher, safer cost estimate). This is because Lang's original 1947 correlation included indirect costs within the multiplier, whereas Hand refined the breakdown in 1958 and split out the engineering, contingency, and auxiliary components more explicitly. For a project that has already added auxiliary facilities and a 1.18 multiplier, the **Hand factor is more internally consistent** and is the preferred KTU-expectation answer.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

A solids-processing plant has five equipment items whose **total purchase cost in 2019 was ₹ 80 lakh** (CEPCI 2019 = 607.5). Use the **module-factor method** with the following typical values: $f_{\text{er}} = 0.25$, $f_p = 0.45$, $f_{\text{el}} = 0.15$, $f_i = 0.10$, $f_c = 0.25$, $f_s = 0.05$, $f_l = 0.08$.

- (a) Update the cost to **2024** (CEPCI 2024 = 800.0) and compute the **Total Bare Module Cost**. **(7 marks)**
- (b) Compute the **Total Capital Investment** with auxiliary = 25 % of TMC, Guth–Couper multiplier 1.18, and working capital = 12 % of FCI. **(7 marks)**

---

**Solution:**

**Part (a) — Cost Update and Total Bare Module Cost (7 marks)**

Step 1: Cost-index multiplier:

$$
M = \frac{800.0}{607.5} = 1.3169
$$

Step 2: Updated 2024 purchase cost:

$$
\sum C_p = 80 \times 1.3169 = 105.35 \text{ lakh}
$$

Step 3: Sum of module factors:

$$
F = 1 + 0.25 + 0.45 + 0.15 + 0.10 + 0.25 + 0.05 + 0.08 = 2.33
$$

Step 4: Total Bare Module Cost:

$$
\boxed{C_{\text{TBMC}} = 2.33 \times 105.35 = 245.5 \text{ lakh}}
$$

**Part (b) — Total Capital Investment (7 marks)**

Step 1: Auxiliary (25 % of $C_{\text{TBMC}}$):

$$
C_{\text{aux}} = 0.25 \times 245.5 = 61.4 \text{ lakh}
$$

Step 2: Fixed Capital Investment (Guth–Couper 1.18):

$$
C_{\text{FCI}} = 1.18 \times (245.5 + 61.4) = 1.18 \times 306.9 = 362.1 \text{ lakh}
$$

Step 3: Working capital (12 % of FCI):

$$
C_{\text{WC}} = 0.12 \times 362.1 = 43.5 \text{ lakh}
$$

Step 4: Total Capital Investment:

$$
\boxed{C_{\text{TCI}} = 362.1 + 43.5 = 405.6 \text{ lakh} \approx \text{₹ 4.06 Crore}}
$$

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common places where students lose marks in Detailed Item Estimate problems:**
> 1. **Forgetting the cost-index update** — examiners explicitly test this; without it the answer is *zero mark on the index step*.
> 2. **Mixing up the Lang factor for solids (3.10) vs. fluids (4.74)** — read the question carefully.
> 3. **Applying auxiliary facilities on the wrong base** — it is on the **Total Module Cost**, not on the sum of purchase costs.
> 4. **Forgetting the working capital** — the question says "Total Capital Investment", which is FCI + WC, **not just FCI**.
> 5. **Rounding errors** — carry at least 3 significant figures through intermediate steps; do not round the cost-index multiplier to "1.3".
> 6. **Not showing the addition for FCI** (the 1.18 multiplier) — if you only give $C_{\text{TM}}$, the examiner treats it as incomplete; FCI is mandatory for full marks.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-density rapid-revision checklist (read this 30 minutes before the exam):**
> - Detailed Item Estimate = item-by-item costing; accuracy $\pm 5\text{–}15 \,\%$.
> - **Six-tenths rule:** $C_B = C_A (S_B / S_A)^{0.6}$ — used for capacity scaling.
> - **Cost index update:** $C_{\text{now}} = C_{\text{ref}} \times (I_{\text{now}} / I_{\text{ref}})$.
> - **Lang factors** (Total Module, includes indirects): solids **3.10**, fluids **4.74**, mixed **3.63**.
> - **Hand factors** (Total Module, refined): solids **3.30**, fluids **4.40**, mixed **3.90**.
> - **Module-factor formula:** $C_{\text{BM}} = C_p (1 + f_{\text{er}} + f_p + f_{\text{el}} + f_i + f_c + f_s + f_l)$.
> - Typical $C_{\text{aux}} = 0.20\text{–}0.40 \times C_{\text{TM}}$.
> - **Guth–Couper multiplier** for FCI = **1.18** (combines field indirects + engineering + contingency).
> - **Working capital** = $0.10\text{–}0.20 \times \text{FCI}$ (raw material inventory + product inventory + cash + receivables − payables).
> - **TCI = FCI + Working Capital** is the final answer required.
> - The sum $(1 + f_{\text{er}} + f_p + f_{\text{el}} + f_i + f_c + f_s + f_l)$ should be ~$2.5\text{–}3.5$ for a typical CS fluid plant — use it as a sanity check against the Lang/Hand factor result.
> - **CEPCI** is the most common cost index in KTU problems; **M&S** and **Nelson–Farrar** appear occasionally.
> - **Always** show every step: index update → sum purchase cost → apply multiplier → add aux → FCI → WC → TCI.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Unit Rate Techniques
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT757 - ECONOMICS AND MANAGEMENT OF CHEMICAL INDUSTRIES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Unit Rate Techniques

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Unit Rate Techniques** are a class of **preliminary cost estimation methods** used in the economic appraisal of chemical process plants. They express the cost of a process, equipment, or complete plant **per unit of a measurable physical parameter** (e.g., cost per ton of product, cost per kW of power, cost per m² of heat transfer area, cost per m³ of reactor volume). The underlying philosophy is that similar processes exhibit **stable cost-per-unit relationships** over time, which can be extrapolated to new designs using established ratios and cost indices.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Focus (Module 2):**
> - Estimation of capital investment, total product cost, and component costs of manufacturing.
> - Cost indices, scaling laws, factor methods (Lang, Hand, Chilton, Peters-Timmerhaus).
> - Working capital, fixed capital, and estimation of plant utilities.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of **Unit Rate Techniques** like the **"price-per-kg" sticker in a grocery store**. When you buy rice, you look at the rate per kilogram and multiply by the quantity you need. Similarly, in chemical engineering economics:

- **Equipment cost** ≈ (cost per unit weight) × (weight of equipment)
- **Plant cost** ≈ (cost per unit of capacity) × (annual capacity)
- **Scaling costs** ≈ original cost × (new capacity / old capacity)^n

> [!NOTE]
> **Why are unit rate techniques so widely used in the chemical industry?**
> Because in the **conceptual or feasibility stage** (when the design has not yet been finalized), detailed Bill-of-Materials (BOM) based estimation is impossible. Engineers need a **fast, reasonably accurate (±20-30 %)** cost estimate. Unit rate methods fill this gap by using **historical benchmarks** from similar plants.

## 1.3 Classification of Unit Rate Techniques

| Sl. No. | Method | Typical Accuracy | Key Parameter |
|:---:|:---|:---:|:---|
| 1 | Unit Product Cost (Turnover Ratio) Method | ± 30 % | Cost per ton of product |
| 2 | Lang Factor Method | ± 25-30 % | Factor × Equipment cost |
| 3 | Hand Factor Method | ± 20 % | More detailed factor split |
| 4 | Chilton Method (Cost of Unit Operations) | ± 20-25 % | Cost per unit operation |
| 5 | Peters-Timmerhaus (Percentage of Delivered Equipment Cost) | ± 15-20 % | Percentage breakdown |
| 6 | Cost Index Updating | Scaling only | CEPCI, Nelson–Farrar, M&S |
| 7 | Six-Tenths Rule (Power Law) | ± 20-30 % | Capacity scaling exponent |

## 1.4 Physical Constants / Standard Metrics

- **CEPCI (Chemical Engineering Plant Cost Index)** — base year **1957-1959 = 100**.
- **Marshall & Swift (M&S) index** — base year **1926 = 100**.
- **Nelson–Farrar Refinery Construction Cost Index** — base year **1946 = 100**.
- **Engineering News-Record (ENR) Construction Cost Index** — base year **1913 = 100**.
- **Lang Factor (L)** — for **fluid processing plants: 4.28**; for **solid processing plants: 3.10**; for **mixed: ~3.63**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Fundamental Philosophy

The **Why** behind every unit rate technique rests on three economic realities:

1. **Economy of Scale** — Doubling the plant capacity does **NOT** double the cost. Capital cost rises roughly as **(Capacity)^n** where **n < 1** (typically 0.6 to 0.8).
2. **Cost Composition Stability** — The *percentage share* of each cost element (equipment, installation, piping, instrumentation) remains remarkably constant across plants of the same type.
3. **Inflation Tracking** — Historical cost data must be **deflated to present-day** values using **cost indices**.

## 2.2 Six-Tenths Rule (Power-Law Scaling)

**The Power Law Model** expresses the relationship between cost and capacity:

$$
C_B = C_A \times \left(\frac{Q_B}{Q_A}\right)^{n}
$$

where:
- $C_B$ = cost of the new (larger) equipment / plant
- $C_A$ = cost of the reference equipment / plant
- $Q_B, Q_A$ = capacities (in consistent units)
- $n$ = **scale exponent** (also called the *cost-capacity factor*)

> [!NOTE]
> **Origin of "Six-Tenths":** Williams (1947) observed that across many chemical processes, the average value of $n$ was approximately **0.6**, hence the popular name. In reality, $n$ varies between **0.4 and 0.9** depending on equipment type.

**Typical values of the scale exponent n:**

| Equipment Type | Value of *n* |
|:---|:---:|
| Heat exchangers (shell & tube) | 0.60 – 0.65 |
| Distillation columns | 0.62 – 0.70 |
| Centrifugal pumps | 0.30 – 0.55 |
| Reciprocating compressors | 0.70 – 0.85 |
| Furnaces | 0.70 – 0.80 |
| Storage tanks (vertical) | 0.57 – 0.65 |
| Reactors (jacketed, agitated) | 0.50 – 0.65 |
| Complete plant (turnkey) | 0.65 – 0.80 |

## 2.3 Cost Index Updating

When historical data is available, present-day cost is obtained by:

$$
C_{present} = C_{past} \times \left(\frac{\text{Index}_{present}}{\text{Index}_{past}}\right)
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **ratio of two indices** is dimensionless, so currency conversions are implicit within the index itself (provided both indices are reported in the same currency, e.g., USD).

## 2.4 Lang Factor Method

A single multiplicative factor **L** converts **delivered equipment cost ($C_{eq}$)** to **fixed capital investment ($C_{FC}$)**:

$$
C_{FC} = L \times C_{eq}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Lang's Rule of Thumb:** For **fluid-processing** chemical plants, **L ≈ 4.28**; for **solid-handling** plants, **L ≈ 3.10**. The factor implicitly includes piping, instrumentation, electrical, structures, insulation, painting, installation labor, and indirect costs (engineering, contingency, contractors' fees).

## 2.5 Peters–Timmerhaus Percent-of-Delivered-Equipment Method

This refines Lang's idea by splitting the total into **direct** and **indirect** percentages:

| Cost Component | Fluid Plant (%) | Solid Plant (%) |
|:---|:---:|:---:|
| Delivered equipment | 100 | 100 |
| Equipment installation | 45 | 39 |
| Piping | 70 | 31 |
| Instrumentation & controls | 18 | 13 |
| Electrical | 13 | 10 |
| Buildings (incl. services) | 30 | 29 |
| Yard improvements | 15 | 12 |
| Service facilities | 40 | 55 |
| **Total Direct Plant Cost** | **331** | **289** |
| Engineering & supervision | 33 | 32 |
| Construction expenses | 39 | 34 |
| Legal expenses | 4 | 4 |
| Contractor's fee | 17 | 19 |
| Contingency | 35 | 37 |
| **Total Indirect Plant Cost** | **128** | **126** |
| **Fixed Capital Investment (FCI)** | **≈ 459 %** | **≈ 415 %** |
| Working capital | 70 | 75 |
| **Total Capital Investment (TCI)** | **≈ 529 %** | **≈ 490 %** |

> [!NOTE]
> The percentages above are expressed **with respect to delivered equipment cost = 100 %**.

## 2.6 Working Capital & Total Capital Investment

$$
\text{Working Capital (WC)} = \text{Cash} + \text{Receivables} + \text{Inventory} - \text{Payables}
$$

$$
\text{Total Capital Investment (TCI)} = \text{Fixed Capital Investment (FCI)} + \text{Working Capital (WC)}
$$

## 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---:|
| 1 | $C_B = C_A \left(\dfrac{Q_B}{Q_A}\right)^{n}$ | Six-tenths rule / Power-law scaling | Currency |
| 2 | $C_{now} = C_{old} \left(\dfrac{I_{now}}{I_{old}}\right)$ | Cost index updating | Currency |
| 3 | $C_{FC} = L \cdot C_{eq}$ | Lang factor | Currency |
| 4 | $\text{Turnover} = \dfrac{\text{Annual Sales}}{\text{FCI}}$ | Turnover ratio | yr$^{-1}$ |
| 5 | $C_{prod} = C_{manuf} + C_{gen} + C_{sales}$ | Total product cost | Currency/yr |
| 6 | $C_{manuf} = C_{rm} + C_{util} + C_{lab} + C_{maint} + C_{sup} + C_{oh}$ | Manufacturing cost | Currency/yr |
| 7 | $\text{WC} = 0.10 \text{ to } 0.20 \times \text{TCI}$ | Working capital rule | Currency |
| 8 | $\text{FCI} = 0.80 \text{ to } 0.90 \times \text{TCI}$ | Fixed capital share | Currency |
| 9 | $C_{bat} = a \cdot C^{b}$ | Cost-capacity correlation | Currency |
| 10 | $\text{COP} = \dfrac{\text{Annual Profit}}{\text{TCI}}$ | Cash-on-cash return | fraction/yr |

## 2.8 Real-World Utility in Engineering

- **Feasibility reports** for new chemical complexes (petrochemicals, fertilizers, refining).
- **Vendor negotiation** — sanity-check contractor quotations using unit rate benchmarks.
- **Insurance valuation** — replacement cost of damaged equipment.
- **R&D planning** — preliminary budget for pilot-plant scale-up.
- **Public-sector tendering** — government cost estimates for PSU projects (IOCL, BPCL, NTPC, etc.).

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Six-Tenths Rule (Power-Law)

### Starting Assumptions

Consider two geometrically similar pieces of equipment with characteristic dimensions $D_A$ and $D_B$ (e.g., diameter of a vessel). The capacity $Q$ scales with some **dimension to the power $m$** (e.g., volume ∝ $D^3$).

$$
Q = k \cdot D^{m}
$$

Cost $C$ of the equipment is approximately proportional to the **mass of material**, which in turn is proportional to **surface area** ∝ $D^{2}$ (for a thin-walled vessel), or to **volume** ∝ $D^{3}$ (for a solid casting).

$$
C = k' \cdot D^{p}
$$

Eliminating $D$ between the two relations:

$$
D = \left(\frac{Q}{k}\right)^{1/m}
$$

$$
C = k' \left[\left(\frac{Q}{k}\right)^{1/m}\right]^{p} = \left(\frac{k'}{k^{p/m}}\right) Q^{p/m}
$$

Defining $a = \dfrac{k'}{k^{p/m}}$ and $n = \dfrac{p}{m}$:

$$
\boxed{\,C = a \cdot Q^{n}\,}
$$

**Step-by-step explanation of the algebraic manipulation:**

- $C_A = a \cdot Q_A^{\,n}$ and $C_B = a \cdot Q_B^{\,n}$.
- Dividing the two equations eliminates $a$ (the proportionality constant, which is specific to material, time, and currency).
- The ratio becomes:

$$
\frac{C_B}{C_A} = \left(\frac{Q_B}{Q_A}\right)^{n}
$$

- Hence the **classical six-tenths rule** is recovered when the empirical value $n \approx 0.6$ is substituted.

> [!NOTE]
> The factor $n$ is **NOT universal** — it is the **slope of the log–log plot of cost vs. capacity**. Different equipment types yield different slopes, which is why the **table of exponents in Section 2.2** is so important.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example — Cost Scaling Using Six-Tenths Rule

### Problem

A shell-and-tube heat exchanger handling $100 \text{ m}^2$ of heat-transfer area costs **₹ 6,00,000** in **2018**. Estimate the cost of a similar exchanger of **$300 \text{ m}^2$** area in the same year. Take the scale exponent $n = 0.65$.

### Solution

**Step 1:** Identify given data.
- $C_A = ₹ 6{,}00{,}000$, $Q_A = 100 \text{ m}^2$, $Q_B = 300 \text{ m}^2$, $n = 0.65$.

**Step 2:** Apply the power-law formula.

$$
C_B = C_A \left(\frac{Q_B}{Q_A}\right)^{n} = 6{,}00{,}000 \times \left(\frac{300}{100}\right)^{0.65}
$$

**Step 3:** Evaluate the ratio.

$$
\frac{300}{100} = 3
$$

$$
3^{0.65} = e^{0.65 \ln 3} = e^{0.65 \times 1.0986} = e^{0.7141} = 2.0423
$$

**Step 4:** Multiply.

$$
C_B = 6{,}00{,}000 \times 2.0423 = ₹ 12{,}25{,}380
$$

**Result:** The cost of the 300 m² exchanger ≈ **₹ 12.25 lakhs**.

> [!TIP]
> Note that although the area **tripled**, the cost only **doubled**. This is the classic **economy-of-scale** effect embedded in the power-law model.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example — Cost Index Updating

### Problem

A 50,000-tonne/year ammonia plant was estimated to cost **USD 28 million** in **2005**. The CEPCI values are: 2005 = 468.2, 2024 = 798.5. Estimate the cost of building the **same plant** in **2024**.

### Solution

**Step 1:** Apply the cost-index formula.

$$
C_{2024} = C_{2005} \times \left(\frac{\text{CEPCI}_{2024}}{\text{CEPCI}_{2005}}\right)
$$

**Step 2:** Substitute values.

$$
C_{2024} = 28 \times 10^{6} \times \left(\frac{798.5}{468.2}\right)
$$

**Step 3:** Compute ratio.

$$
\frac{798.5}{468.2} = 1.7055
$$

**Step 4:** Final multiplication.

$$
C_{2024} = 28 \times 10^{6} \times 1.7055 = 47.75 \times 10^{6}
$$

**Result:** Plant cost in 2024 ≈ **USD 47.75 million**.

---

## 3.4 Worked Example — Lang Factor Method (Combined with Index Updating)

### Problem

The delivered cost of major equipment for a fluid-processing plant is **USD 5 million** (as on Jan 2023, CEPCI = 750.3). Find the **fixed capital investment** in **2025** (CEPCI = 790.0). Use Lang factor $L = 4.28$ for fluid plants.

### Solution

**Step 1:** Update equipment cost from 2023 to 2025.

$$
C_{eq,\,2025} = 5 \times 10^{6} \times \left(\frac{790.0}{750.3}\right) = 5 \times 10^{6} \times 1.0529 = 5.265 \times 10^{6}
$$

**Step 2:** Apply Lang factor.

$$
\text{FCI}_{2025} = L \times C_{eq,\,2025} = 4.28 \times 5.265 \times 10^{6} = 22.53 \times 10^{6}
$$

**Result:** FCI in 2025 ≈ **USD 22.53 million**.

---

## 3.5 Worked Example — Turnover Ratio Method

### Problem

A polymer plant produces 20,000 tonnes/yr of polyethylene granules sold at **USD 1,200/tonne**. The fixed capital investment is **USD 30 million**. Calculate the **turnover ratio**. Is the plant financially attractive compared to the chemical-industry benchmark of 1.0?

### Solution

**Step 1:** Compute annual sales.

$$
S = 20{,}000 \times 1{,}200 = 24 \times 10^{6} \text{ USD/yr}
$$

**Step 2:** Compute turnover ratio.

$$
\text{Turnover} = \frac{S}{\text{FCI}} = \frac{24 \times 10^{6}}{30 \times 10^{6}} = 0.80
$$

**Result:** Turnover = **0.80 yr⁻¹**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** A turnover of 0.80 means the plant will recover its fixed capital in approximately **1/0.80 = 1.25 years** if all the sales were profit (clearly an upper bound; the real payback is longer because of operating costs and taxes). The benchmark for the chemical industry is ~ **1.0 yr⁻¹**, so this plant is **slightly below average** and may need cost optimization.

---

## 3.6 Worked Example — Peters–Timmerhaus Percentage Method

### Problem

For the same fluid-processing plant, the delivered equipment cost is **USD 5 million**. Using the Peters–Timmerhaus percentages, compute the **Total Plant Cost (TPC)** and the **Total Capital Investment (TCI)**.

### Solution

**Step 1:** Compute Direct Plant Cost (DPC) — for fluid plant, DPC = 331 % of $C_{eq}$.

$$
\text{DPC} = 3.31 \times 5 \times 10^{6} = 16.55 \times 10^{6} \text{ USD}
$$

**Step 2:** Compute Indirect Plant Cost (IPC) — 128 % of $C_{eq}$.

$$
\text{IPC} = 1.28 \times 5 \times 10^{6} = 6.40 \times 10^{6} \text{ USD}
$$

**Step 3:** Compute Fixed Capital Investment.

$$
\text{FCI} = \text{DPC} + \text{IPC} = 16.55 + 6.40 = 22.95 \times 10^{6} \text{ USD}
$$

**Step 4:** Compute Working Capital (70 % of $C_{eq}$).

$$
\text{WC} = 0.70 \times 5 \times 10^{6} = 3.50 \times 10^{6} \text{ USD}
$$

**Step 5:** Compute Total Capital Investment.

$$
\text{TCI} = \text{FCI} + \text{WC} = 22.95 + 3.50 = 26.45 \times 10^{6} \text{ USD}
$$

**Result:** TCI ≈ **USD 26.45 million**.

> [!NOTE]
> Compare this with the Lang-factor result (USD 22.53 million). Peters–Timmerhaus gives a more **granular, defensible** estimate, while Lang is faster but cruder.

---

## 3.7 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Method | Input Required | Output | Time | Accuracy |
|:---|:---|:---|:---:|:---:|
| Unit product cost | Past plant cost, capacity | New plant cost | Minutes | ±30 % |
| Six-tenths rule | One reference cost, capacities, *n* | Scaled cost | Minutes | ±25 % |
| Cost index | Past cost, two index values | Updated cost | Minutes | ±10-15 % (if scope identical) |
| Lang factor | Equipment cost, plant type | FCI | < 1 hour | ±25-30 % |
| Peters–Timmerhaus | Equipment cost, plant type | FCI + WC | Hours | ±15-20 % |
| Detailed (BOM) | P&IDs, specifications, vendor quotes | Final cost | Weeks | ±5-10 % |

---

## 3.8 Quick Numerical — Sensitivity of Cost to Scale Exponent

Using $C_B / C_A = (Q_B / Q_A)^n$, let $Q_B / Q_A = 2$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
n = 0.5 &\implies C_B / C_A = 2^{0.5} = 1.414 \\
n = 0.6 &\implies C_B / C_A = 2^{0.6} = 1.516 \\
n = 0.7 &\implies C_B / C_A = 2^{0.7} = 1.625 \\
n = 0.8 &\implies C_B / C_A = 2^{0.8} = 1.741 \\
n = 0.9 &\implies C_B / C_A = 2^{0.9} = 1.866 \\
n = 1.0 &\implies C_B / C_A = 2^{1.0} = 2.000
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** The cost multiplier moves from **41 % to 100 %** as $n$ moves from 0.5 to 1.0. Selecting the wrong $n$ is one of the **largest sources of error** in unit-rate cost estimation.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Cost-Estimation Hierarchy (Order of Magnitude → Definitive)

```mermaid
flowchart TD

    B --> C[Study Estimate]
    C --> D[Preliminary Estimate]
    D --> E[Definitive Estimate]
    E --> F[Detailed Estimate]
    F --> G[Construction]

    A1[Unit Product Cost Method] -.-> B
    B1[Lang / Hand Factor Method] -.-> C
    C1[Six-Tenths Rule + Cost Indices] -.-> D
    D1[Peters-Timmerhaus Percentage Method] -.-> E
    E1[Detailed BOM + Vendor Quotes] -.-> F

    B -- Approx 30 percent accuracy --> H[Project Sanction]
    C -- Approx 25 percent accuracy --> H
    D -- Approx 15 to 20 percent accuracy --> H
    E -- Approx 10 percent accuracy --> H
    F -- Approx 5 to 10 percent accuracy --> H
```

```mermaid
flowchart LR

    P2 --> P3[Update Cost Using Cost Index]
    P3 --> P4[Apply Power Law or Lang Factor]

    P8 --> P9[Estimate General Expenses]
    P9 --> P10[Total Product Cost]
    P10 --> P11[Profitability Analysis]
```

```mermaid
flowchart TD
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks each)

### Question 1 — Definition [KTU University Exam – May 2023]

**Q:** Define the **"Six-Tenths Rule"** used in the preliminary cost estimation of chemical plants. What is the physical basis of the exponent?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The Six-Tenths Rule is an empirical **power-law correlation** that relates the cost of equipment or a complete plant to its capacity:

$$
C_B = C_A \left(\frac{Q_B}{Q_A}\right)^{n}
$$

where $n$ is typically close to 0.6 for chemical process equipment. The physical basis is that for **geometrically similar equipment**, cost scales roughly with the **mass of material** (∝ surface area, $D^2$) while capacity scales with **volume** (∝ $D^3$); hence the exponent $n = p/m \approx 2/3 \approx 0.6$.

---

### Question 2 — Cost Index [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]

**Q:** State the **Chemical Engineering Plant Cost Index (CEPCI)**. What is its base year and base value?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
CEPCI is a **composite cost index** published monthly by *Chemical Engineering* magazine to track inflation in the chemical process industries. It is the weighted average of equipment, construction labor, buildings, and engineering costs.

- **Base year:** 1957–1959 (average).
- **Base value:** 100.
- **Typical present value:** ~ 800 (subject to current publication).

---

## 5.2 Part B Question — Choice A (14 Marks Total)

**[KTU University Exam – July 2024]**

**Q:** A fluid-processing chemical plant has the following specifications:

- Plant capacity (new design) = 80,000 tonnes/year of product.
- Reference plant capacity = 50,000 tonnes/year built in **2010** at a cost of **USD 22 million**.
- CEPCI (2010) = 550.8, CEPCI (2024) = 798.5.
- Scale exponent for the complete plant, $n = 0.78$.

### Part (a) [7 Marks]

Estimate the **Fixed Capital Investment (FCI)** of the new plant in **2024 USD** using the **Six-Tenths Rule** combined with cost-index updating.

### Part (b) [7 Marks]

Using the **Lang factor** $L = 4.28$ for fluid plants, estimate the **Total Capital Investment (TCI)** assuming working capital = 15 % of TCI. Compare this with the answer in part (a) and comment.

### Model Answer

**Part (a) — Step-by-step:**

**Step 1 — Cost-index update from 2010 to 2024:**

$$
C_{A,\,2024} = 22 \times 10^{6} \times \left(\frac{798.5}{550.8}\right) = 22 \times 10^{6} \times 1.4497 = 31.89 \times 10^{6} \text{ USD}
$$

**Step 2 — Scale to new capacity (Q_B = 80,000; Q_A = 50,000):**

$$
C_{B,\,2024} = 31.89 \times 10^{6} \times \left(\frac{80{,}000}{50{,}000}\right)^{0.78}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the ratio and exponent:**

$$
\frac{80{,}000}{50{,}000} = 1.6
$$

$$
1.6^{0.78} = e^{0.78 \ln 1.6} = e^{0.78 \times 0.4700} = e^{0.3666} = 1.4427
$$

**Step 4 — Final multiplication:**

$$
C_{B,\,2024} = 31.89 \times 10^{6} \times 1.4427 = 46.01 \times 10^{6} \text{ USD}
$$

**Result:** $\text{FCI}_{\text{new}} \approx$ **USD 46.0 million**.

---

**Part (b) — Lang Factor Method:**

**Step 1 — Lang factor for fluid plant, L = 4.28:**

$$
\text{FCI}_{\text{Lang}} = 4.28 \times C_{eq}
$$

We are *given* the Lang-factor estimate only in conjunction with delivered equipment cost. To relate it to part (a), we need to back-calculate $C_{eq}$:

$$
C_{eq} = \frac{46.01 \times 10^{6}}{4.28} = 10.75 \times 10^{6} \text{ USD}
$$

**Step 2 — Add working capital (15 % of TCI).**

We have the relationship $\text{TCI} = \text{FCI} + \text{WC}$ and $\text{WC} = 0.15 \cdot \text{TCI}$.

Rearranging:

$$
\text{TCI} = \text{FCI} + 0.15 \cdot \text{TCI}
$$

$$
\text{TCI} - 0.15 \cdot \text{TCI} = \text{FCI}
$$

$$
0.85 \cdot \text{TCI} = \text{FCI}
$$

$$
\text{TCI} = \frac{\text{FCI}}{0.85} = \frac{46.01 \times 10^{6}}{0.85} = 54.13 \times 10^{6} \text{ USD}
$$

**Step 3 — Compare.**

| Method | FCI (USD M) | TCI (USD M) |
|:---|:---:|:---:|
| Six-Tenths + Index | 46.01 | – |
| Lang + WC = 15 % of TCI | 46.01 | 54.13 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Comment:** The two methods agree well on FCI because the Lang factor of 4.28 is essentially calibrated to produce the same average FCI for fluid plants as the six-tenths rule with $n \approx 0.78$. The Total Capital Investment (TCI) is **about 18 % higher** than FCI because of the working-capital cushion required to operate the plant.

---

## 5.3 Part B Question — Choice B (14 Marks Total)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Q:**

**(a)** A fluid-processing plant has the following costs (all in **₹ lakhs**):

- Raw materials = 280, Operating labor = 90, Utilities = 120, Maintenance = 60, Supplies = 25, Overhead (60 % of operating labor + maintenance) = 90, Administration (8 % of manufacturing cost), Sales & distribution (10 % of total cost), R&D (5 % of total cost).

Compute the **total product cost** per year.

**(b)** A piece of equipment costs **₹ 12,00,000** at a capacity of 200 kW. Estimate the cost of a similar unit of **800 kW** capacity, assuming the cost-capacity exponent is **0.7**. If a further 10 % engineering contingency and 5 % installation are to be added, what is the **installed cost**?

### Model Answer

**Part (a) — Step-by-step:**

**Step 1 — Compute Manufacturing Cost (MC):**

$$
\text{MC} = 280 + 90 + 120 + 60 + 25 + 90 = 665 \text{ ₹ lakhs}
$$

**Step 2 — Administration (A) = 8 % of MC:**

$$
A = 0.08 \times 665 = 53.2 \text{ ₹ lakhs}
$$

**Step 3 — Subtotal before sales/R&D (S1):**

$$
S_1 = \text{MC} + A = 665 + 53.2 = 718.2 \text{ ₹ lakhs}
$$

**Step 4 — Total cost equation (T = total product cost):**

$$
T = S_1 + 0.10\,T + 0.05\,T
$$

$$
T - 0.15\,T = S_1
$$

$$
0.85\,T = 718.2
$$

$$
T = \frac{718.2}{0.85} = 844.94 \text{ ₹ lakhs}
$$

**Result:** Total product cost ≈ **₹ 844.94 lakhs/year**.

> [!TIP]
> Always **denote the unknown total cost as T, then express each percentage as a fraction of T**. This converts the word problem into a single linear equation in one variable.

---

**Part (b) — Step-by-step:**

**Step 1 — Scale the equipment cost using power law:**

$$
C_B = 12{,}00{,}000 \times \left(\frac{800}{200}\right)^{0.7}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute ratio:**

$$
\frac{800}{200} = 4
$$

$$
4^{0.7} = e^{0.7 \ln 4} = e^{0.7 \times 1.3863} = e^{0.9704} = 2.6390
$$

**Step 3 — Compute scaled cost:**

$$
C_B = 12{,}00{,}000 \times 2.6390 = 31{,}66{,}800 \approx ₹ 31.67 \text{ lakhs}
$$

**Step 4 — Add engineering contingency (10 %) + installation (5 %):**

$$
C_{\text{installed}} = 31.67 \times 1.15 = 36.42 \text{ ₹ lakhs}
$$

**Result:** Installed cost ≈ **₹ 36.42 lakhs**.

---

## 5.4 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common ways students LOSE marks in cost-estimation problems:**
> 1. **Forgetting the cost-index ratio** — students often just scale capacity but ignore inflation between reference year and present year. **Marks lost: 2-3 / 7**.
> 2. **Using the wrong Lang factor** — applying $L = 4.28$ to a solid-handling plant (where $L = 3.10$ is correct) is a guaranteed 1-mark cut.
> 3. **Misinterpreting "Total cost" in cascading percentages** — when sales, R&D, etc. are expressed as a percentage of *total* cost, you must set up the linear equation $T = \text{MC} + (\%)\cdot T$, NOT $T = \text{MC} \times (1 + \%)$.
> 4. **Using decimal exponent incorrectly** — e.g., computing $4^{0.7}$ as $4 \times 0.7 = 2.8$ (linear) instead of $e^{0.7 \ln 4}$. Always show the **exponential/logarithm step** in your answer script.
> 5. **Mixing up FCI vs TCI** — students often forget to add Working Capital, or include it twice. **Marks lost: 1-2 / 7**.
> 6. **No units in the final answer** — KTU examiners are strict; always write **₹ lakhs**, **USD million**, or **₹/yr** explicitly.

---

## 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **One-page high-density recap — read this the night before the exam!**

- **Six-Tenths Rule:** $C_B = C_A (Q_B/Q_A)^n$; $n \approx 0.6$ for chemical plants.
- **Cost Index Updating:** $C_{new} = C_{old} (I_{new}/I_{old})$ — always **multiply** by the *ratio of new to old index*.
- **CEPCI base year:** 1957–1959 = 100; **M&S base:** 1926 = 100.
- **Lang factor:** $L = 4.28$ (fluid), $L = 3.10$ (solid); converts *delivered equipment* → *FCI*.
- **Peters–Timmerhaus:** FCI ≈ 4.59 × $C_{eq}$ (fluid) ≈ 4.15 × $C_{eq}$ (solid).
- **Working capital** = 10–20 % of TCI; **FCI** = 80–90 % of TCI.
- **Manufacturing cost** = Raw materials + Utilities + Labor + Maintenance + Supplies + Overhead.
