- **Total product cost** = Manufacturing cost + General expenses (Admin, Sales, R&D, Finance).
- **Turnover ratio** = Annual sales / FCI; benchmark ≈ 1.0 yr⁻¹ for the chemical industry.
- **Cost-capacity exponent $n$** depends on equipment type; the **same $n$** is used in the six-tenths rule for *similar* equipment only.
- **Always check units** — currency, time (annual), and physical quantity (tonnes/yr, m², kW).
- **Show all intermediate steps** — KTU examiners reward working, not just the final number.
- **Sensitivity to $n$:** a 10 % error in $n$ can give 5–10 % error in cost — choose $n$ carefully.
- **Economy of scale:** doubling capacity does NOT double the cost — because $n < 1$.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Percentage of Delivered Equipment Cost
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT757 - ECONOMICS AND MANAGEMENT OF CHEMICAL INDUSTRIES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Percentage of Delivered Equipment Cost

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Percentage of Delivered Equipment Cost Method** (also called the **Lang Percentage Method** or **Factor Method of Cost Estimation**) is a preliminary capital cost estimation technique in which the total fixed capital investment (FCI) of a chemical process plant is estimated by summing the cost of delivered equipment with the costs of all auxiliary items (installation, piping, instrumentation, electrical, buildings, etc.) expressed as **fixed percentage factors** multiplied by the **delivered equipment cost**.

Formally, if $C_{DE}$ is the **delivered equipment cost**, then:

$$\text{Fixed Capital Investment (FCI)} = C_{DE} \times \left[ 1 + \sum_{i=1}^{n} f_i \right]$$

where each $f_i$ is the fractional percentage cost of the $i^{th}$ component (piping, electrical, instrumentation, etc.) relative to $C_{DE}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Delivered Equipment Cost ($C_{DE}$)** is defined as the **Purchased Equipment Cost ($C_{PE}$)** plus **freight, insurance, handling, and taxes** required to bring the equipment to the plant site.
> Typically, $C_{DE} = (1.05 \text{ to } 1.10) \times C_{PE}$. This is the **basis (100%)** against which all other costs are measured.

> [!NOTE]
> **Classification of Cost Estimation Methods by Accuracy (KTU standard):**
> - **Order of Magnitude Estimate** (Accuracy $\pm$ 40%): Turnover ratio, capacity factored.
> - **Study/Factored Estimate** (Accuracy $\pm$ 25%): Percentage of delivered equipment cost.
> - **Preliminary/Scope Estimate** (Accuracy $\pm$ 12%): Equipment factored.
> - **Detailed/Definitive Estimate** (Accuracy $\pm$ 5%): Quoted, requires P&ID and specs.

The percentage method falls under the **Study Estimate (Class IV)** category and is the most commonly used preliminary tool in KTU board problems.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine you are renovating a house. You know the exact price of the major appliances (refrigerator, oven, washing machine, water heater) — the **purchased equipment cost**. To get them to your home, the shop charges you a small delivery and installation handling charge — this is the **freight** component, giving you the **delivered equipment cost** (appliances safely at your doorstep).

Now, the appliances are useless without:
- **Pipes & plumbing** to connect water (piping in a plant),
- **Wiring & switches** to power them (electrical),
- **A control panel** to operate them (instrumentation),
- **A kitchen counter & shelving** to place them (buildings),
- **A plumber & electrician's labour** (installation),
- **Architect's drawings & municipal approval fees** (engineering and supervision),
- **An "unexpected extras" buffer** (contingency).

In renovation, experience tells us these add up to roughly **2 to 4 times** the appliance cost. The percentage-of-delivered-equipment method is **exactly this logic** applied to a chemical plant — it converts knowledge of just the equipment cost into a full plant capital estimate using empirically derived percentage factors.

### 1.3 Why This Method Matters for KTU

- Requires only the **purchased equipment cost** (obtainable from vendor quotations or cost databases like Matches, CAPCOST, or Peters-Timmerhaus charts).
- Provides an estimate of the **entire FCI** without needing detailed engineering drawings, P&IDs, or material take-offs.
- **Speed**: A complete FCI estimate can be done in **30–60 minutes** for a 20–30 equipment list, making it ideal for project screening and feasibility studies.
- Used as a **sanity check** for detailed bottom-up estimates.

### 1.4 Physical Constants, Standard Metrics & Source Reference Ranges

- **Freight factor** (for converting purchased → delivered): typically **5% to 10%** of $C_{PE}$, depending on equipment weight, origin, and transport mode. A working benchmark is **8%**.
- **Lang factor ($f_L$)**: a single multiplicative factor that lumps all components together.
  - Solid processing plant: $f_L = 3.10$
  - Fluid processing plant: $f_L = 4.74$
  - Mixed solid–fluid plant: $f_L = 3.63$
- Standard reference: derived from statistical data in **Peters, Timmerhaus & West, "Plant Design and Economics for Chemical Engineers"** and consolidated in **Sinnott & Towler, "Chemical Engineering Design"**.

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner Insight:** KTU problems in PECHT757 almost always assume the **fluid-processing factor of 4.74** unless the problem statement explicitly says "solid processing" or "mixed". Always read the plant-type descriptor carefully.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Hierarchical Cost Structure

The capital cost of a chemical plant has a **strict hierarchy** that the student must internalize. Each level builds upon the previous one. The chain is:

**Purchased Equipment Cost ($C_{PE}$)** → **Delivered Equipment Cost ($C_{DE}$)** → **Total Physical Plant Cost (PPC)** → **Fixed Capital Investment (FCI)** → **Total Capital Investment (TCI)**

#### 2.1.1 Step 1 — Purchased to Delivered

$$C_{DE} = C_{PE} \times (1 + f_{\text{freight}})$$

where $f_{\text{freight}} \approx 0.05$ to $0.10$ (typical: **0.08**).

#### 2.1.2 Step 2 — Delivered Equipment to Total Physical Plant Cost (PPC)

The PPC includes the delivered equipment plus all **direct costs** (the cost of physically constructing and installing the plant):

| Direct Cost Component | Description | Typical % of $C_{DE}$ (Solid) | Typical % of $C_{DE}$ (Fluid) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Equipment + Installation | Foundations, supports, insulation, painting | 39 | 40 |
| Piping | Process and utility piping, fittings, valves | 49 | 70 |
| Instrumentation | Control valves, sensors, DCS, panels | 13 | 18 |
| Electrical | Transformers, MCC, cabling, lighting | 18 | 15 |
| Buildings | Process buildings, admin offices, control rooms | 22 | 20 |
| Yard Improvements | Fencing, roads, parking, drainage | 9 | 10 |
| Service Facilities | Steam, water, air, effluent treatment utilities | 49 | 70 |
| **Sum of direct factors** | | **199** | **233** |

$$\text{PPC} = C_{DE} \times (1 + \sum f_{\text{direct}}) = C_{DE} \times (1 + 1.99)_{\text{solid}} \text{ or } C_{DE} \times (1 + 2.33)_{\text{fluid}}$$

#### 2.1.3 Step 3 — PPC to Fixed Capital Investment (FCI)

FCI adds the **indirect costs** (engineering and overhead):

| Indirect Cost Component | Description | Typical % of $C_{DE}$ (Solid) | Typical % of $C_{DE}$ (Fluid) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Engineering & Supervision | Design, drafting, project engineering | 9 | 15 |
| Construction Expenses | Construction supervision, temporary facilities | 9 | 18 |
| Legal Expenses | Permits, contracts, patent fees | 1 | 2 |
| Contractor's Fee | General contractor's overhead and profit | 3 | 6 |
| Contingency | Allowance for unforeseen items | 9 | 15 |
| **Sum of indirect factors** | | **31** | **56** |

$$\text{FCI} = C_{DE} \times (1 + \sum f_{\text{direct}} + \sum f_{\text{indirect}})$$

Combining both sums, for the **fluid processing** case (the KTU default):

$$\text{FCI} = C_{DE} \times (1 + 2.33 + 0.56) = C_{DE} \times 3.89 \approx 4.74 \times C_{DE} \text{ (Lang factor)}$$

The slight numerical difference arises because the Lang factor was derived from broader historical data and includes minor additional items (taxes, interest during construction, startup costs) embedded statistically.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the Lang factor differs slightly from the sum-of-percentages total:**
> The Lang factor (3.10 solid, 4.74 fluid) is an **empirical statistical factor** that captures average plant behaviour across many projects. The percentage breakdown above is the **deterministic build-up**. In a KTU exam, the student is expected to use **whichever the problem provides**. If the problem gives Lang factors, use them. If it gives a percentage table, use the table.

#### 2.1.4 Step 4 — FCI to Total Capital Investment (TCI)

$$\text{TCI} = \text{FCI} + \text{Working Capital (WC)}$$

$$\text{WC} = \alpha \times \text{FCI} \quad \text{where } \alpha \approx 0.10 \text{ to } 0.20 \text{ (typical: 0.15)}$$

Therefore:

$$\text{TCI} = \text{FCI} \times (1 + \alpha) = \text{FCI} \times (1.10 \text{ to } 1.20)$$

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $C_{DE} = C_{PE} \times (1 + f_{\text{fright}})$ | Purchased → Delivered equipment cost; $f_{\text{fright}} = 0.05$ to $0.10$ |
| 2 | $\text{PPC} = C_{DE} \times (1 + \sum f_{\text{direct}})$ | Physical plant cost from direct cost percentages |
| 3 | $\text{FCI} = \text{PPC} \times (1 + \sum f_{\text{indirect}}) = C_{DE} \times (1 + \sum f_{\text{all}})$ | Final fixed capital investment |
| 4 | $\text{FCI} = f_L \times C_{DE}$ | Lang factor shortcut; $f_L = 3.10$ (solid), $4.74$ (fluid), $3.63$ (mixed) |
| 5 | $\text{WC} = \alpha \times \text{FCI}$ | Working capital; $\alpha = 0.10$ to $0.20$ |
| 6 | $\text{TCI} = \text{FCI} + \text{WC} = \text{FCI} \times (1 + \alpha)$ | Total capital investment |
| 7 | $C_{\text{aux}} = f_i \times C_{DE}$ | Cost of any individual auxiliary item ($i$-th component) |

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Feasibility & Front-End Engineering Design (FEED)**: When a chemical company evaluates a new product line, dozens of process alternatives may be possible. The percentage method enables a **comparative ranking** of these alternatives with minimal data.
- **Insurance valuation**: Insurers use these factors to estimate the **replacement value** of an existing plant.
- **Academic & KTU context**: This method appears in PECHT757 Module 2 problems almost every year — typically asking students to compute FCI, TCI, or individual component costs given $C_{PE}$ and a plant type.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Rule:** When numerical values for both the percentage table and the Lang factor are provided, **use the data given in the problem** — do not mix-and-match. The examiner checks against the specific values provided.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Numerical Example 1 — Fluid Processing Plant (KTU-Style)

**Problem Statement:**
A proposed fluid processing chemical plant has the following data:
- Purchased cost of all major equipment: $C_{PE}$ = Rs. 1,20,00,000 (1.2 Crore)
- Freight, insurance, and taxes: **8%** of $C_{PE}$
- Plant type: **Fluid processing**
- Working capital: **15%** of FCI
- Use the **Lang factor** method with $f_L = 4.74$.

**Compute:** (a) Delivered Equipment Cost, (b) Fixed Capital Investment, (c) Total Capital Investment.

---

#### Step 1 — Calculate Delivered Equipment Cost $C_{DE}$

The freight factor is 8% = 0.08.

$$C_{DE} = C_{PE} \times (1 + f_{\text{fright}})$$

$$C_{DE} = 1{,}20{,}00{,}000 \times (1 + 0.08)$$

$$C_{DE} = 1{,}20{,}00{,}000 \times 1.08$$

$$C_{DE} = \text{Rs. } 1{,}29{,}60{,}000$$

**Reasoning:** The freight/insurance/tax component adds 8% on top of the FOB (free-on-board) factory cost of the equipment. The result represents the equipment cost at the plant gate, fully insured and taxes paid.

---

#### Step 2 — Calculate Fixed Capital Investment (FCI) using Lang Factor

For a fluid processing plant, $f_L = 4.74$:

$$\text{FCI} = f_L \times C_{DE}$$

$$\text{FCI} = 4.74 \times 1{,}29{,}60{,}000$$

$$\text{FCI} = \text{Rs. } 6{,}14{,}30{,}400$$

**Reasoning:** The Lang factor of 4.74 is an empirically derived multiplier that, when applied to delivered equipment cost, gives the total fixed capital investment including all direct and indirect costs. It accounts for installation, piping, instrumentation, electrical, buildings, engineering, contingency, etc.

---

#### Step 3 — Calculate Working Capital and Total Capital Investment

Working capital fraction $\alpha = 0.15$:

$$\text{WC} = \alpha \times \text{FCI} = 0.15 \times 6{,}14{,}30{,}400 = \text{Rs. } 92{,}14{,}560$$

$$\text{TCI} = \text{FCI} + \text{WC} = 6{,}14{,}30{,}400 + 92{,}14{,}560$$

$$\text{TCI} = \text{Rs. } 7{,}06{,}44{,}960$$

---

#### Step 4 — Verification by Component Build-Up Method (Fluid Plant)

Let us verify by computing the percentage build-up to the same FCI.

Direct cost factors for fluid plant: $\sum f_{\text{direct}} = 2.33$
Indirect cost factors for fluid plant: $\sum f_{\text{indirect}} = 0.56$

$$\text{FCI}_{\text{buildup}} = C_{DE} \times (1 + 2.33 + 0.56) = C_{DE} \times 3.89$$

$$\text{FCI}_{\text{buildup}} = 1{,}29{,}60{,}000 \times 3.89 = \text{Rs. } 5{,}04{,}14{,}400$$

The Lang factor (4.74 × $C_{DE}$ = Rs. 6,14,30,400) is **higher** than the build-up factor (3.89 × $C_{DE}$ = Rs. 5,04,14,400). The difference (Rs. 1,10,16,000) represents **allowances not captured in the standard percentage table** — including interest during construction, taxes, startup costs, and plant-offsite facilities. This is why the Lang factor is treated as a "complete" empirical multiplier.

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example 2 — Solid Plant Component Cost Calculation

**Problem Statement:**
For a **solid processing plant**, the purchased equipment cost is Rs. 80,00,000. The freight factor is 7%. The plant uses the percentage build-up method with the following component percentages (relative to $C_{DE}$):

| Component | % of $C_{DE}$ |
|---|---|
| Installation | 39 |
| Piping | 49 |
| Instrumentation | 13 |
| Electrical | 18 |
| Buildings | 22 |
| Yard improvements | 9 |
| Service facilities | 49 |
| Engineering & supervision | 9 |
| Construction expenses | 9 |
| Contractor's fee | 3 |
| Contingency | 9 |

**Compute:** (a) Delivered equipment cost, (b) Piping cost, (c) FCI, (d) TCI (with WC = 18% of FCI).

---

#### Step (a) — Delivered Equipment Cost

$$C_{DE} = 80{,}00{,}000 \times (1 + 0.07) = 80{,}00{,}000 \times 1.07$$

$$C_{DE} = \text{Rs. } 85{,}60{,}000$$

#### Step (b) — Piping Cost (Individual Component)

Piping is 49% of $C_{DE}$:

$$C_{\text{piping}} = 0.49 \times C_{DE} = 0.49 \times 85{,}60{,}000$$

$$C_{\text{piping}} = \text{Rs. } 41{,}94{,}400$$

#### Step (c) — Fixed Capital Investment (Build-Up)

Sum of all percentages = $39 + 49 + 13 + 18 + 22 + 9 + 49 + 9 + 9 + 3 + 9 = 229\%$

$$\text{FCI} = C_{DE} \times (1 + 2.29) = C_{DE} \times 3.29$$

$$\text{FCI} = 85{,}60{,}000 \times 3.29$$

$$\text{FCI} = \text{Rs. } 2{,}81{,}62{,}400$$

#### Step (d) — Total Capital Investment

$$\text{WC} = 0.18 \times 2{,}81{,}62{,}400 = \text{Rs. } 50{,}69{,}232$$

$$\text{TCI} = 2{,}81{,}62{,}400 + 50{,}69{,}232 = \text{Rs. } 3{,}32{,}31{,}632$$

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Plant Types

| Parameter | Solid Plant | Fluid Plant | Mixed Plant |
|---|---|---|---|
| Lang factor $f_L$ | 3.10 | 4.74 | 3.63 |
| $\sum f_{\text{direct}}$ typical | 1.99 | 2.33 | 2.16 |
| $\sum f_{\text{indirect}}$ typical | 0.31 | 0.56 | 0.43 |
| FCI / $C_{PE}$ ratio (with 8% freight) | $3.10 \times 1.08 = 3.35$ | $4.74 \times 1.08 = 5.12$ | $3.63 \times 1.08 = 3.92$ |
| Dominant cost driver | Buildings & equipment | Piping & service facilities | Piping & buildings |
| Examples | Cement, ore crushing, fertilizers | Distillation, reactors with fluids | Pharma, food processing |

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for KTU:** "**Fluids = Fancy & Fragile = 4.74**" — fluid plants cost more because they need more piping, more instrumentation, more utility service, and stricter safety/containment. Solids are simpler (conveyors, bins) so the multiplier is lower (3.10).

### 3.4 Key Conceptual Derivations

#### 3.4.1 Why is "Delivered" Equipment the Basis, not "Purchased"?

The $C_{DE}$ is used as the basis because:
1. It is the cost at a **defined reference point** (the plant site) — all subsequent installation labour and material are relative to this.
2. Freight, taxes, and insurance are **proportional to equipment cost and weight** — they are absorbed into the basis.
3. If we used $C_{PE}$ as the basis, every other percentage would have to be inflated by $(1 + f_{\text{fright}})$, complicating the table.

#### 3.4.2 Working Capital — What does it include?

$$\text{WC} = \text{Raw material inventory} + \text{Product inventory} + \text{In-process inventory} + \text{Cash reserves} - \text{Accounts payable}$$

For preliminary estimation, KTU problems use the shortcut $\text{WC} = \alpha \times \text{FCI}$ with $\alpha = 0.10$ to $0.20$.

#### 3.4.3 When does the Percentage Method Fail?

- For **brand-new technology** where historical percentage data does not apply.
- For **modular or skid-mounted** plants (different cost distribution).
- For plants with **extreme off-sites** (e.g., captive power plants) where the cost distribution deviates from the statistical mean.
- For **high-pressure / cryogenic / hazardous** plants where safety and instrumentation costs are disproportionately high.

In such cases, a **detailed estimate (Class I)** must be used.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Cost Hierarchy Flowchart — From Purchased Equipment to Total Capital Investment

This diagram captures the **logical flow** of the percentage-of-delivered-equipment-cost method, including every major transformation step.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[PURCHASED EQUIPMENT COST CPE] --> B[ADD FREIGHT 5 TO 10 PERCENT]
    B --> C[DELIVERED EQUIPMENT COST CDE 100 PERCENT BASIS]
    C --> D[DIRECT COSTS APPLY PERCENTAGE FACTORS]
    D --> D1[INSTALLATION 25 TO 55 PERCENT]
    D --> D2[PIPING 30 TO 80 PERCENT]
    D --> D3[INSTRUMENTATION 6 TO 30 PERCENT]
    D --> D4[ELECTRICAL 10 TO 40 PERCENT]
    D --> D5[BUILDINGS 15 TO 35 PERCENT]
    D --> D6[YARD IMPROVEMENTS 5 TO 15 PERCENT]
    D --> D7[SERVICE FACILITIES 30 TO 80 PERCENT]
    D1 --> E[TOTAL PHYSICAL PLANT COST PPC]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    D4 --> E
    D5 --> E
    D6 --> E
    D7 --> E
    E --> F[INDIRECT COSTS APPLY PERCENTAGE FACTORS]
    F --> F1[ENGINEERING AND SUPERVISION 5 TO 15 PERCENT]
    F --> F2[CONSTRUCTION EXPENSES 5 TO 15 PERCENT]
    F --> F3[CONTRACTOR FEE 1 TO 5 PERCENT]
    F --> F4[CONTINGENCY 5 TO 15 PERCENT]
    F1 --> G[FIXED CAPITAL INVESTMENT FCI]
    F2 --> G
    F3 --> G
    F4 --> G
    G --> H[ADD WORKING CAPITAL ALPHA TIMES FCI]
    H --> I[TOTAL CAPITAL INVESTMENT TCI]
    J[ALTERNATE PATH LANG FACTOR] -.-> G
    C -.-> J
    K[SOLID PLANT FL = 3.10] -.-> J
    L[FLUID PLANT FL = 4.74] -.-> J
    M[MIXED PLANT FL = 3.63] -.-> J
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Mapping Cost Interactions

For a deeper structural view, the diagram below shows the **functional architecture** of how the cost components interact with the equipment base and with each other.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    BASE[BASE COST LAYER CPE PLUS CDE] --> DC[DIRECT COST LAYER]
    DC --> IC[INDIRECT COST LAYER]
    IC --> FCI[FCI LAYER]
    FCI --> WC[WORKING CAPITAL LAYER]
    WC --> TCI[FINAL OUTPUT TCI]
    DC --> DCN1[CONSTRUCTION MATERIALS]
    DC --> DCN2[LABOUR FOR INSTALLATION]
    DC --> DCN3[CONSTRUCTION EQUIPMENT]
    IC --> ICN1[DESIGN ENGINEERING EFFORT]
    IC --> ICN2[PROJECT MANAGEMENT]
    IC --> ICN3[REGULATORY AND PERMITTING]
    WC --> WCN1[INVENTORY FINANCING]
    WC --> WCN2[CASH FLOW BUFFER]
    WCN2 --> TCI
    DCN1 --> TCI
    DCN2 --> TCI
    DCN3 --> TCI
    ICN1 --> TCI
    ICN2 --> TCI
    ICN3 --> TCI
    WCN1 --> TCI
```

### 4.3 Decision Tree — Choosing the Right Lang Factor

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{IS THE PROCESS PRIMARILY SOLID HANDLING} -->|YES| S1[USE LANG FACTOR 3.10]
    Q1 -->|NO| Q2{IS THE PROCESS PRIMARILY FLUID HANDLING}
    Q2 -->|YES| F1[USE LANG FACTOR 4.74]
    Q2 -->|NO| M1[USE LANG FACTOR 3.63 MIXED PLANT]
    S1 --> RES[APPLY FCI = FL TIMES CDE]
    F1 --> RES
    M1 --> RES
    RES --> OUT[TCI = FCI TIMES 1 PLUS ALPHA]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Readability Note:** In the Mermaid block, all node labels are written in **plain uppercase alphanumeric text** with no markdown bold, italics, or HTML tags — this ensures flawless rendering across all Mermaid-compatible viewers (GitHub, VSCode, Obsidian, and the KTU e-learning portal).
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**Define the term "Delivered Equipment Cost". How is it different from "Purchased Equipment Cost"? Mention the typical range of the freight factor used to convert one to the other.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Purchased Equipment Cost ($C_{PE}$)** is the **factory-gate (FOB) price** quoted by the equipment vendor for the bare equipment, without any transport, insurance, or tax charges.

The **Delivered Equipment Cost ($C_{DE}$)** is the total cost of the equipment **once it has reached the plant site**, inclusive of freight, insurance, handling, and applicable taxes.

Mathematically:

$$C_{DE} = C_{PE} \times (1 + f_{\text{fright}})$$

The **freight factor $f_{\text{fright}}$** typically ranges between **5% and 10%** of $C_{PE}$, with **8%** being a common working value. The delivered cost is the standard basis (100%) against which all other plant cost components are factored in the percentage-of-delivered-equipment method.

> *(Examiner's note: 1 mark for each definition, 1 mark for the freight factor range — full marks.)*

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam — June 2023]`
**Differentiate between Fixed Capital Investment (FCI) and Total Capital Investment (TCI). What percentage of FCI does working capital usually represent?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Fixed Capital Investment (FCI) | Total Capital Investment (TCI) |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | Capital tied up in **physical, permanent** plant assets (equipment, buildings, installations, engineering). | FCI **plus** the capital required for **day-to-day operation** during the initial period (inventories, cash, receivables). |
| Formula | $\text{FCI} = C_{DE} \times f_L$ or build-up from percentages | $\text{TCI} = \text{FCI} + \text{WC}$ |
| Nature | Long-term, depreciable | Tied up in current assets; partially recoverable at end-of-life |
| Computation path | End of the percentage build-up | One step beyond FCI |

$$\text{WC} \approx (10\% \text{ to } 20\%) \times \text{FCI}; \text{ typical: } 15\%$$

$$\text{TCI} = \text{FCI} \times (1 + 0.15) = 1.15 \times \text{FCI}$$

> *(Examiner's note: 2 marks for the difference table, 1 mark for the WC percentage range.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice Module Pattern)

---

### **OPTION A — Question A (14 Marks)**

`[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]`

**(a)** Explain the **percentage of delivered equipment cost method** for the estimation of fixed capital investment of a chemical plant. List the **direct cost** and **indirect cost** components with their typical percentage ranges for a **fluid processing plant**. (7 marks)

**(b)** A fluid processing chemical plant has a **purchased equipment cost of Rs. 2,00,00,000**. The freight and insurance factor is **8%**. The total physical plant cost works out to be **2.95 times** the delivered equipment cost, and the indirect costs amount to **20%** of the physical plant cost. The working capital is estimated at **18%** of the FCI. Calculate: (i) Delivered Equipment Cost, (ii) Fixed Capital Investment, (iii) Total Capital Investment. (7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

The **percentage of delivered equipment cost method** (also called the **factored estimate method** or **Lang percentage method**) is a preliminary capital cost estimation technique in which the Fixed Capital Investment (FCI) of a chemical plant is obtained by multiplying the **delivered equipment cost ($C_{DE}$)** by a series of percentage factors that account for all other plant costs.

**Procedure:**

1. **Compute Delivered Equipment Cost:** Add freight, insurance, and taxes (typically 5–10% of purchased cost) to the purchased equipment cost.

$$C_{DE} = C_{PE} \times (1 + f_{\text{fright}})$$

2. **Apply Direct Cost Factors:** Multiply $C_{DE}$ by the sum of direct cost percentages (installation, piping, instrumentation, electrical, buildings, yard improvements, service facilities).

3. **Apply Indirect Cost Factors:** Add indirect cost percentages (engineering, construction, contractor's fee, contingency) to obtain FCI.

**Direct Cost Components for a Fluid Processing Plant:**

| Component | Range (% of $C_{DE}$) |
|---|---|
| Equipment installation (incl. foundations, insulation, painting) | 25 – 55 |
| Piping (process and utility) | 30 – 80 |
| Instrumentation (control valves, sensors, DCS) | 6 – 30 |
| Electrical (transformers, cabling, lighting) | 10 – 40 |
| Buildings (process buildings, control rooms) | 15 – 35 |
| Yard improvements (roads, fencing, drainage) | 5 – 15 |
| Service facilities (steam, water, air, effluent) | 30 – 80 |

**Indirect Cost Components for a Fluid Processing Plant:**

| Component | Range (% of $C_{DE}$) |
|---|---|
| Engineering and supervision | 5 – 15 |
| Construction expenses | 5 – 15 |
| Legal expenses | 1 – 3 |
| Contractor's fee | 1 – 5 |
| Contingency | 5 – 15 |

This method belongs to the **Study Estimate (Class IV)** category with an accuracy of approximately $\pm 25\%$. It is widely used in feasibility and front-end engineering design (FEED) studies.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Given:**
- $C_{PE}$ = Rs. 2,00,00,000
- $f_{\text{fright}}$ = 0.08
- PPC = $2.95 \times C_{DE}$
- Indirect costs = 20% of PPC
- WC = 18% of FCI

**(i) Delivered Equipment Cost:**

$$C_{DE} = C_{PE} \times (1 + f_{\text{fright}}) = 2{,}00{,}00{,}000 \times 1.08$$

$$C_{DE} = \text{Rs. } 2{,}16{,}00{,}000$$

**(ii) Fixed Capital Investment:**

$$\text{PPC} = 2.95 \times C_{DE} = 2.95 \times 2{,}16{,}00{,}000 = \text{Rs. } 6{,}37{,}20{,}000$$

Indirect costs = 20% of PPC:

$$C_{\text{indirect}} = 0.20 \times 6{,}37{,}20{,}000 = \text{Rs. } 1{,}27{,}44{,}000$$

$$\text{FCI} = \text{PPC} + C_{\text{indirect}} = 6{,}37{,}20{,}000 + 1{,}27{,}44{,}000$$

$$\boxed{\text{FCI} = \text{Rs. } 7{,}64{,}64{,}000}$$

**(iii) Total Capital Investment:**

$$\text{WC} = 0.18 \times 7{,}64{,}64{,}000 = \text{Rs. } 1{,}37{,}63{,}520$$

$$\text{TCI} = \text{FCI} + \text{WC} = 7{,}64{,}64{,}000 + 1{,}37{,}63{,}520$$

$$\boxed{\text{TCI} = \text{Rs. } 9{,}02{,}27{,}520}$$

---

### **OPTION B — Question B (14 Marks)**

`[KTU University Exam — June 2022]`

**(a)** Discuss the **Lang factor method** of capital cost estimation. What are the typical Lang factors for **solid, fluid, and mixed** processing plants? Under what circumstances is the Lang factor method preferred over the detailed percentage build-up? (7 marks)

**(b)** The purchased equipment cost for a proposed **mixed processing plant** is **Rs. 3,50,00,000**. The freight is **7%** of the purchased cost. Using the **Lang factor approach**, compute the (i) Delivered Equipment Cost, (ii) Fixed Capital Investment, (iii) Total Capital Investment, assuming working capital is **16%** of FCI. (7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

The **Lang factor method** is a **single-multiplier shortcut** version of the percentage-of-delivered-equipment-cost method, developed by **H.J. Lang** from statistical analysis of historical chemical plant construction data. The method relates the **Fixed Capital Investment (FCI)** directly to the **Delivered Equipment Cost ($C_{DE}$)** through a single empirically determined factor, $f_L$:

$$\text{FCI} = f_L \times C_{DE}$$

**Typical Lang Factors:**

| Plant Type | Lang Factor $f_L$ | Remarks |
|---|---|---|
| **Solid processing** | **3.10** | Lower because solids handling needs less piping and instrumentation; conveyors, bins, crushers dominate. |
| **Fluid processing** | **4.74** | Higher because fluid plants require extensive piping, complex instrumentation, more service utilities, and stricter safety/containment. |
| **Mixed (solid + fluid)** | **3.63** | Intermediate; reflects hybrid cost distribution. |

**When the Lang factor method is preferred:**

1. When a **very early-stage** order-of-magnitude estimate is required (screening many alternatives).
2. When the **equipment list is known** but the detailed P&ID, instrument index, and material take-off are not yet available.
3. As a **cross-check** against a more detailed build-up estimate.
4. When **historical plant data** is not available for project-specific percentage factors.
5. For **academic and competitive exam** problems where a quick, single-step calculation is required.

**Limitations:**
- The factors are **averages**; they do not capture project-specific deviations (e.g., exotic materials, hazardous service, modular construction).
- They are **not applicable to brand-new technologies** where statistical data is sparse.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Given:**
- $C_{PE}$ = Rs. 3,50,00,000
- $f_{\text{fright}}$ = 0.07
- Plant type: **Mixed processing** → $f_L = 3.63$
- WC = 16% of FCI → $\alpha = 0.16$

**(i) Delivered Equipment Cost:**

$$C_{DE} = C_{PE} \times (1 + f_{\text{fright}}) = 3{,}50{,}00{,}000 \times 1.07$$

$$C_{DE} = \text{Rs. } 3{,}74{,}50{,}000$$

**(ii) Fixed Capital Investment:**

$$\text{FCI} = f_L \times C_{DE} = 3.63 \times 3{,}74{,}50{,}000$$

$$\text{FCI} = \text{Rs. } 13{,}59{,}43{,}500$$

**(iii) Total Capital Investment:**

$$\text{WC} = \alpha \times \text{FCI} = 0.16 \times 13{,}59{,}43{,}500 = \text{Rs. } 2{,}17{,}50{,}960$$

$$\text{TCI} = \text{FCI} + \text{WC} = 13{,}59{,}43{,}500 + 2{,}17{,}50{,}960$$

$$\text{TCI} = \text{Rs. } 15{,}76{,}94{,}460$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Wrong plant type → wrong Lang factor:** The single most common error. If the problem says "fluid", use 4.74; "solid" → 3.10; "mixed" → 3.63. **Do NOT default to 4.74 for everything.**
> 2. **Forgetting the freight step:** Many students compute $\text{FCI} = f_L \times C_{PE}$ directly. This is **wrong**. The Lang factor is applied to **delivered** cost, not purchased cost. Always compute $C_{DE}$ first.
> 3. **Mixing WC bases:** Working capital is a percentage of **FCI**, not of $C_{DE}$ or $C_{PE}$. Get the FCI first, then apply WC.
> 4. **Unit errors in cost values:** Lakhs vs. Crores vs. Rupees. KTU problems often use lakhs. Write "Rs. 50 lakhs" or "Rs. 50,00,000" consistently.
> 5. **Confusing TCI with FCI in the final answer:** The 14-mark question always asks for TCI separately if WC is given. Do not stop at FCI.
> 6. **Rounding off mid-calculation:** Carry at least 4–5 significant figures through intermediate steps to avoid compounding errors.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [ ] **Definition:** Percentage-of-delivered-equipment-cost is a Class IV (Study) estimate method giving $\pm 25\%$ accuracy.
- [ ] **Cost chain:** $C_{PE} \rightarrow C_{DE} \rightarrow \text{PPC} \rightarrow \text{FCI} \rightarrow \text{TCI}$ — memorize this hierarchy.
- [ ] **Freight factor:** 5–10% (typical 8%) converts $C_{PE} \rightarrow C_{DE}$.
- [ ] **Lang factors:** Solid = 3.10, Fluid = 4.74, Mixed = 3.63. Applied to $C_{DE}$, NOT $C_{PE}$.
- [ ] **Direct cost components (7):** Installation, Piping, Instrumentation, Electrical, Buildings, Yard improvements, Service facilities.
- [ ] **Indirect cost components (5):** Engineering & supervision, Construction expenses, Legal expenses, Contractor's fee, Contingency.
- [ ] **Working Capital:** 10–20% of FCI (typical 15%). Added to FCI to get TCI.
- [ ] **Mnemonic:** "**Fluids need Fancy plumbing**" → 4.74; "**Solids are Simpler**" → 3.10.
- [ ] **Pitfall check:** Never skip the freight step. Never apply Lang factor to $C_{PE}$. Never compute WC on wrong base.
- [ ] **Verification tip:** Build-up from percentages and Lang factor should give similar order-of-magnitude FCI values. Large discrepancy implies either wrong plant type or wrong percentage values.
- [ ] **Master formula for KTU:**
$$\text{TCI} = f_L \times C_{PE} \times (1 + f_{\text{fright}}) \times (1 + \alpha)$$
- [ ] **Alternative single-line formula (build-up version):**
$$\text{TCI} = C_{DE} \times \left( 1 + \sum f_{\text{direct}} + \sum f_{\text{indirect}} \right) \times (1 + \alpha)$$
- [ ] **Always read the problem carefully:** Identify plant type (solid/fluid/mixed) before picking the Lang factor.
- [ ] **Always state assumptions:** Freight factor, WC percentage, plant type, Lang factor version — write them down before substituting numbers.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: William's Six Tenth Factor
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT757 - ECONOMICS AND MANAGEMENT OF CHEMICAL INDUSTRIES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** William's Six Tenth Factor

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 📘 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

**Williams' Six-Tenths Factor Rule** is an empirical *preliminary cost estimation* technique used in chemical process economics to relate the **purchase cost of a piece of process equipment to its capacity (size)**. The rule was formalized by **Williams (1947)** and states that:

> *If the cost of a given equipment at capacity $S_A$ is $C_A$, then the cost of a geometrically similar unit of capacity $S_B$ is given by:*

$$C_B = C_A \left(\frac{S_B}{S_A}\right)^{0.6}$$

The exponent **0.6** is termed the **"Six-Tenths Factor"** (also called the *cost-capacity exponent* or *scale-up exponent*, commonly denoted as $n$).

## 💡 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition (Plain English)

Imagine you walk into a bakery to buy a cake:
- A **500 g cake** costs ₹300.
- Will a **1000 g (1 kg) cake** cost exactly ₹600? 

**No!** The baker will charge you around ₹300 × (1000/500)^0.6 ≈ **₹436** — because the oven, labor, decoration, and electricity don't double when the cake size doubles. There are *economies of scale*.

Williams' Six-Tenths Rule works the **same way** for chemical equipment:
- A **reactor that is 10× bigger** does NOT cost 10× more.
- It costs roughly **10^0.6 ≈ 3.98×** more (almost 4×).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The exponent 0.6 is the "magic number"** — it captures the fact that for most chemical process equipment, costs grow **slower than capacity** (a phenomenon called *economy of scale*).

## 🎯 Key Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Value | Physical Meaning |
|-----------|--------|---------------|------------------|
| **Six-Tenths Exponent** | $n$ | **0.6** (standard) | Captures economy of scale |
| Equipment Capacity | $S_A, S_B$ | Variable | Size metric (volume, throughput, etc.) |
| Equipment Cost | $C_A, C_B$ | Variable | Purchase cost in monetary units |
| Validity Range | — | **0.5 to 10×** capacity | Reliability window for the rule |
| Expected Accuracy | — | **± 30 %** | Preliminary estimate grade |

> [!NOTE]
> The exponent $n$ is **NOT always 0.6**! It varies by equipment type:
> - Heat exchangers → $n \approx 0.6$–$0.8$  
> - Compressors/pumps → $n \approx 0.7$–$0.9$  
> - Fractionating columns → $n \approx 0.6$–$0.7$  
> - Storage tanks → $n \approx 0.5$–$0.7$
> 
> The "0.6" is a **rough industry default**, hence the name "Six-Tenths Rule."
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 🧠 The "Why" Behind the Rule — Underlying Logic

Williams' rule is derived from the **General Power Law Model** of cost-capacity relationships observed across chemical process industries:

**Step 1 — Power Law Cost Model:**  
For a family of geometrically similar equipment, historical purchase-cost data empirically fits a power-law curve:

$$C = K \cdot S^{n}$$

where $K$ is a constant (depends on material, pressure rating, year, currency) and $S$ is a characteristic capacity parameter.

**Step 2 — Eliminate the Constant:**  
Since $K$ is unknown (and depends on year, location, material), we form the **ratio** of two similar units to cancel it out:

$$\frac{C_B}{C_A} = \left(\frac{S_B}{S_A}\right)^{n}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the Empirical Exponent:**  
Williams' statistical survey of chemical process equipment showed that the **average value** of $n$ across most equipment families clusters around **0.6**. This led to the famous rule.

## 📋 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units / Notes |
|---|---------|-------------|----------------|
| 1 | $C_B = C_A \left(\dfrac{S_B}{S_A}\right)^{0.6}$ | **Williams' Six-Tenths Rule** | $C$ in ₹/\$, $S$ in chosen capacity unit |
| 2 | $C = K \cdot S^{n}$ | General power-law cost model | Empirical fit; $n = 0.4$ to $0.9$ |
| 3 | $C_B = C_A \cdot (R)^{0.6}$ | Compact form with ratio $R = S_B/S_A$ | $R$ is dimensionless |
| 4 | $\ln C = \ln K + n \cdot \ln S$ | Linearized form (for regression) | Plot $\ln C$ vs $\ln S$; slope = $n$ |
| 5 | $\dfrac{C_B}{C_A} = R^{n}$ | Cost ratio form | Use to compute scale-up factor |
| 6 | $\text{Scale-up factor} = R^{0.6}$ | Multiplier applied to base cost | For $R=2$: factor = **1.516** |
| 7 | $R = 2 \Rightarrow \text{factor} \approx 1.52$ | Quick lookup: doubling capacity | Useful for exam short-cuts |
| 8 | $R = 10 \Rightarrow \text{factor} \approx 3.98$ | Quick lookup: 10× capacity | "Rule of four" for 10× scale |

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Shortcut — Memorize these two values:**
> - Doubling capacity ($R=2$) → cost increases by **1.52×**
> - Tenfold capacity ($R=10$) → cost increases by **3.98× ≈ 4×**

## ⚙️ Real-World Engineering Utility

1. **Feasibility Studies** — Quickly estimate capital cost of new plants before detailed engineering begins.
2. **Vendor Negotiation** — Cross-check vendor quotations by computing expected cost for a given size.
3. **Inflation Updates** — Combine with cost indices (e.g., Chemical Engineering Plant Cost Index, CEPCI) to update old cost data to current year.
4. **Scale-Up Decisions** — Compare costs of building one large unit vs. multiple smaller units (e.g., 1×10,000 L vs 2×5,000 L).
5. **Greenfield Project Budgeting** — Generate order-of-magnitude capital estimates for board approvals.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 🔬 Derivation 1 — From Power Law to Williams' Equation

### Starting Postulate
Assume equipment cost $C$ varies with capacity $S$ according to the empirical power law:

$$C = K \cdot S^{n}$$

**Step-by-step derivation:**

**Step 1:** Write the cost equation for equipment A (known size and cost):

$$C_A = K \cdot S_A^{n}$$

**Step 2:** Write the cost equation for equipment B (different size, unknown cost):

$$C_B = K \cdot S_B^{n}$$

**Step 3:** Form the ratio $C_B / C_A$ to **eliminate the unknown constant $K$**:

$$\frac{C_B}{C_A} = \frac{K \cdot S_B^{n}}{K \cdot S_A^{n}}$$

**Step 4:** Simplify — $K$ cancels, and the powers combine via the law of exponents $\dfrac{a^{n}}{b^{n}} = \left(\dfrac{a}{b}\right)^{n}$:

$$\frac{C_B}{C_A} = \left(\frac{S_B}{S_A}\right)^{n}$$

**Step 5:** Rearrange to solve for the unknown cost $C_B$:

$$C_B = C_A \left(\frac{S_B}{S_A}\right)^{n}$$

**Step 6:** Substitute the Williams empirical value $n = 0.6$:

$$\boxed{C_B = C_A \left(\frac{S_B}{S_A}\right)^{0.6}}$$

> **Logic of conversion (Step 4 explanation):** The constant $K$ is the same for both pieces of equipment *because* they belong to the same equipment family (same type, same material, same design code). The only variable is size $S$. Dividing cancels $K$ cleanly, leaving a pure ratio of powers — which the index law collapses into a single power of the ratio.

---

## 🧮 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Standard Problem)

### Problem Statement
A **stainless-steel storage tank** with capacity **5,000 L** costs **₹ 2,50,000**. Using Williams' Six-Tenths Rule, estimate the cost of a **similar tank** with capacity **20,000 L**. Also find the cost if the capacity is **doubled** from 5,000 L to 10,000 L.

### Given Data
- $C_A = 2{,}50{,}000$ ₹
- $S_A = 5{,}000$ L (base case)
- $n = 0.6$ (Williams' factor)
- $S_B = 20{,}000$ L (Part 1) and $S_B = 10{,}000$ L (Part 2)

---

### Part (a): Scaling from 5,000 L → 20,000 L

**Step 1:** Identify the capacity ratio:

$$R = \frac{S_B}{S_A} = \frac{20{,}000}{5{,}000} = 4$$

**Step 2:** Apply the Williams' rule formula:

$$C_B = C_A \cdot (R)^{0.6}$$

**Step 3:** Substitute the values:

$$C_B = 2{,}50{,}000 \times (4)^{0.6}$$

**Step 4:** Evaluate $4^{0.6}$ using logarithms:

$$\ln(4^{0.6}) = 0.6 \times \ln 4 = 0.6 \times 1.3863 = 0.8318$$

$$4^{0.6} = e^{0.8318} = 2.2974$$

**Step 5:** Compute final cost:

$$C_B = 2{,}50{,}000 \times 2.2974 = 5{,}74{,}350 \text{ ₹}$$

$$\boxed{C_B \approx ₹\,5{,}74{,}350}$$

**Interpretation:** A tank **4× larger** costs only **~2.3× more**, not 4× more. This is the *economy of scale* in action.

---

### Part (b): Scaling from 5,000 L → 10,000 L (Doubling)

**Step 1:** Capacity ratio:

$$R = \frac{10{,}000}{5{,}000} = 2$$

**Step 2:** Apply the rule:

$$C_B = 2{,}50{,}000 \times (2)^{0.6}$$

**Step 3:** Evaluate $2^{0.6}$:

$$\ln(2^{0.6}) = 0.6 \times \ln 2 = 0.6 \times 0.6931 = 0.4159$$

$$2^{0.6} = e^{0.4159} = 1.5157$$

**Step 4:** Compute final cost:

$$C_B = 2{,}50{,}000 \times 1.5157 = 3{,}78{,}925 \text{ ₹}$$

$$\boxed{C_B \approx ₹\,3{,}78{,}925}$$

**Interpretation:** Doubling the capacity increases the cost by only **~52 %**, not 100 %.

---

### Analytical Matrix — Sensitivity of Cost to Capacity

Using $C_B/C_A = R^{0.6}$, the matrix below shows how cost scales:

| Capacity Ratio $R$ | $R^{0.6}$ | Cost Multiplier | Comment |
|---------------------|-----------|-----------------|---------|
| 0.5 (half-size) | 0.660 | 0.66× | 50 % size → 66 % cost |
| 1.0 (same size) | 1.000 | 1.00× | Baseline |
| 2.0 (double) | **1.516** | 1.52× | Memorize this! |
| 3.0 (triple) | 1.933 | 1.93× | |
| 4.0 (quadruple) | 2.297 | 2.30× | From worked example |
| 5.0 | 2.626 | 2.63× | |
| 10.0 | **3.981** | 3.98× | "Rule of four" |
| 100.0 | 15.849 | 15.85× | 100× size → ~16× cost |

> [!NOTE]
> **Strong economy of scale:** A 100× capacity increase only requires a ~16× cost increase (≈ 84 % savings vs. linear scaling).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 🗺️ Diagram 1: Block-Level Functional Architecture of Williams' Rule Application

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Input: Known Equipment Data"] --> B["Identify Base Cost CA and Base Capacity SA"]
    B --> C{"Is Equipment Geometrically Similar?"}
    C -- "No" --> D["STOP: Rule Not Applicable"]
    C -- "Yes" --> E["Identify New Capacity SB"]
    E --> F["Compute Capacity Ratio R = SB / SA"]
    F --> G["Check Validity: 0.5 less than R less than 10"]
    G -- "Out of Range" --> H["Apply Caution: Use Detailed Estimate"]
    G -- "In Range" --> I["Apply Williams Formula: CB = CA times R power 0.6"]
    I --> J["Compute Numerical Value of R power 0.6"]
    J --> K["Multiply: CB = CA times Factor"]
    K --> L["Output: Estimated Cost CB plus minus 30 percent accuracy"]
    H --> M["Consider Cost Index Update if Year Differs"]
    L --> M
    M --> N["Final Adjusted Cost Estimate for Engineering Decision"]
```

---

## 🗺️ Diagram 2: Decision Flow for Selecting Cost Estimation Method

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1["Start: Need Cost Estimate"] --> P2["What is the Project Stage?"]
    P2 -- "Research / Order of Magnitude" --> P3["Use Capacity-Levered Models like Williams"]
    P2 -- "Study / Feasibility" --> P4["Use Williams Six Tenth Rule or Factor Methods"]
    P2 -- "Engineering / Design" --> P5["Use Detailed Vendor Quotations"]
    P2 -- "Construction" --> P6["Use As-Bid or Actual Cost Records"]
    P3 --> Q1["Exponent 0.6 Default unless Known Otherwise"]
    P4 --> Q2["Determine Specific n for Equipment Type from Data"]
    Q1 --> R1["Apply Formula: CB = CA times SB/SA power 0.6"]
    Q2 --> R1
    R1 --> S1["Cross-validate with Multiple Equipment Types"]
    S1 --> T1["Final Preliminary Cost Estimate Ready"]
```

---

## 🗺️ Diagram 3: Cost vs. Capacity Relationship Curve

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    X["Capacity S axis"] --> Y["Cost C axis"]
    Y2["Linear Scaling n equals 1.0"] --> L1["Cost grows 1 to 1 with size"]
    Y3["Williams Six Tenths n equals 0.6"] --> L2["Cost grows slower than size Economy of Scale"]
    Y4["n less than 0.4 Rarely Realistic"] --> L3["Approaches constant cost regardless of size"]
    L1 --> CMP["Comparison: Williams curve is BELOW the linear line"]
    L2 --> CMP
    L3 --> CMP
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation of Diagram 3:** Williams' Six-Tenths curve (n = 0.6) always lies *below* the 45° linear line. The further below, the stronger the economy of scale. This visual is a classic KTU board question — *be prepared to sketch and label it.*
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 📝 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]** 
*State Williams' Six-Tenths Rule. What does the exponent 0.6 represent?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Williams' Six-Tenths Rule is an empirical preliminary cost estimation technique used to estimate the cost of process equipment at a different capacity. It states that:

$$C_B = C_A \left(\frac{S_B}{S_A}\right)^{0.6}$$

where $C_A, C_B$ are the costs of the existing and new equipment, and $S_A, S_B$ are their respective capacities. The exponent **0.6** is the **cost-capacity exponent (n)**, which represents the *economy of scale* — the fact that cost grows **slower than capacity** for most chemical process equipment. A value of 0.6 is the empirical industry average across equipment families.

*(1 mark — definition, 1 mark — formula, 1 mark — explanation of 0.6)*

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
*What are the limitations of Williams' Six-Tenths Rule?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Equipment similarity required** — Applicable only to geometrically similar equipment of the same type, material, and design. **(1 mark)**
2. **Limited capacity range** — Valid only for $0.5 \leq S_B/S_A \leq 10$. Beyond this range, accuracy drops sharply. **(1 mark)**
3. **Low accuracy (±30 %)** — It is a *preliminary/Class 5 estimate* and is unsuitable for budgeting final project costs; detailed vendor quotes must replace it. **(1 mark)**

---

## 📝 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Original Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 2]**

(a) **Derive Williams' Six-Tenths Rule** from the general power-law cost model. List **three assumptions** under which the rule is valid. **(7 marks)**

(b) A **shell-and-tube heat exchanger** with heat transfer area **20 m²** costs **₹ 4,00,000**. Using Williams' Six-Tenths Rule, estimate the cost of a **similar exchanger** with area **80 m²**. Comment on the cost multiplier and the **economy of scale** observed. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Derivation (7 marks):**

1. Assume the cost-capacity relationship follows the empirical power law: $C = K \cdot S^{n}$ **(1 mark)**
2. For two similar units A and B: $C_A = K \cdot S_A^{n}$ and $C_B = K \cdot S_B^{n}$ **(1 mark)**
3. Dividing: $\dfrac{C_B}{C_A} = \dfrac{S_B^{n}}{S_A^{n}} = \left(\dfrac{S_B}{S_A}\right)^{n}$ **(1 mark)**
4. Rearranging: $C_B = C_A \left(\dfrac{S_B}{S_A}\right)^{n}$ **(1 mark)**
5. Substituting Williams' empirical exponent $n = 0.6$: $C_B = C_A \left(\dfrac{S_B}{S_A}\right)^{0.6}$ **(1 mark)**

**Assumptions (any 3, 1 mark each):**
- The two equipment units are **geometrically similar** (same type, shape, proportions).
- The **cost-capacity exponent $n$ is constant** over the range considered.
- Both units are made of the **same material** and operate under the **same design pressure/temperature**.
- Cost data is referenced to the **same year and currency** (otherwise cost index adjustment is needed).
- Capacity ratio is within the **reliable range (0.5 to 10)**. **(3 marks)**

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:** $C_A = 4{,}00{,}000$ ₹, $S_A = 20$ m², $S_B = 80$ m², $n = 0.6$

**Step 1:** Capacity ratio:
$$R = \frac{S_B}{S_A} = \frac{80}{20} = 4 \quad \textbf{(1 mark)}$$

**Step 2:** Apply Williams' formula:
$$C_B = 4{,}00{,}000 \times (4)^{0.6} \quad \textbf{(1 mark)}$$

**Step 3:** Compute $4^{0.6}$:
$$4^{0.6} = e^{0.6 \times \ln 4} = e^{0.6 \times 1.3863} = e^{0.8318} = 2.297 \quad \textbf{(2 marks)}$$

**Step 4:** Final cost:
$$C_B = 4{,}00{,}000 \times 2.297 = 9{,}18{,}800 \text{ ₹} \quad \textbf{(1 mark)}$$

**Step 5 — Comments (2 marks):**
- **Cost multiplier:** A **4× larger** exchanger costs only **~2.3× more**, not 4× more.  
- **Economy of scale:** This sub-linear cost growth (exponent < 1) reflects the *economy of scale* — larger equipment benefits from lower per-unit fixed costs (foundations, instrumentation, piping) and more efficient material utilization. Engineers should consider building **one large unit** rather than multiple small ones, but must balance this against **transport, maintenance, and operational risk** constraints.

$$\boxed{C_B \approx ₹\,9{,}18{,}800}$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 2]**

(a) Explain the concept of the **cost-capacity exponent ($n$)** in Williams' rule. Discuss how the value of $n$ **varies for different chemical process equipment** with suitable examples. **(7 marks)**

(b) A **centrifugal pump** handling **50 m³/h** costs **₹ 1,50,000**. The plant needs a similar pump of capacity **200 m³/h**. Using Williams' rule (assume $n = 0.8$ for pumps), estimate the new cost. If the vendor offers a quote of **₹ 6,50,000**, what is the **percentage deviation** from the Williams estimate? Comment on the result. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Theory (7 marks):**

The **cost-capacity exponent $n$** is the power to which the capacity ratio is raised in the cost-estimation equation. It quantifies how steeply cost grows with size. **(2 marks)**

Williams' analysis revealed that $n$ is **not universal** but depends on the equipment type:

| Equipment | Typical $n$ | Reason |
|-----------|-------------|--------|
| Heat exchangers, columns | 0.6 – 0.7 | Moderate economy of scale |
| Centrifugal pumps, compressors | 0.7 – 0.9 | High specific power; cost scales strongly with size |
| Storage tanks | 0.5 – 0.7 | Very strong economy of scale (surface ∝ D²) |
| Furnaces, reactors | 0.6 – 0.8 | Moderate |

**(3 marks for table and explanation)**

The exponent $n$ is determined empirically by **plotting $\ln C$ vs $\ln S$** for historical data; the slope of the best-fit line gives $n$. Values **below 1** indicate economy of scale, **above 1** indicate diseconomy (rare, but seen in some very large custom equipment). The standard 0.6 is a **conservative default**. **(2 marks)**

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:** $C_A = 1{,}50{,}000$ ₹, $S_A = 50$ m³/h, $S_B = 200$ m³/h, $n = 0.8$ (pumps)

**Step 1:** Capacity ratio:
$$R = \frac{200}{50} = 4 \quad \textbf{(1 mark)}$$

**Step 2:** Apply Williams' formula with $n = 0.8$:
$$C_B = 1{,}50{,}000 \times (4)^{0.8} \quad \textbf{(1 mark)}$$

**Step 3:** Compute $4^{0.8}$:
$$4^{0.8} = e^{0.8 \times \ln 4} = e^{0.8 \times 1.3863} = e^{1.1090} = 3.031 \quad \textbf{(2 marks)}$$

**Step 4:** Williams estimate:
$$C_{B,\text{Williams}} = 1{,}50{,}000 \times 3.031 = 4{,}54{,}650 \text{ ₹} \quad \textbf{(1 mark)}$$

**Step 5:** Vendor quote given = ₹ 6,50,000.

**Step 6:** Percentage deviation:
$$\%\text{Deviation} = \frac{C_{\text{vendor}} - C_{\text{Williams}}}{C_{\text{Williams}}} \times 100$$

$$= \frac{6{,}50{,}000 - 4{,}54{,}650}{4{,}54{,}650} \times 100 = \frac{1{,}95{,}350}{4{,}54{,}650} \times 100$$

$$= 42.97 \% \quad \textbf{(1 mark)}$$

**Step 7 — Comments (1 mark):**

The vendor's quote is **~43 % higher** than the Williams estimate. Since Williams' rule is only a **preliminary estimate (±30 % accuracy)**, a 43 % deviation is **beyond the expected error margin**. Possible reasons: special material (stainless steel vs. carbon steel), higher pressure rating, vendor-specific design premium, or a different hydraulic class. **The estimator should investigate the cause before accepting the quote.**

$$\boxed{C_{B,\text{Williams}} \approx ₹\,4{,}54{,}650;\ \text{Deviation} \approx +43\,\%}$$

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
> 
> 1. **Forgetting to convert to ratio form** — Students write $C_B = C_A \times 0.6 \times (S_B/S_A)$. This is **WRONG**! The 0.6 is an **exponent**, not a multiplier. Always write $C_B = C_A \times (S_B/S_A)^{0.6}$. **(Lose 1–2 marks)**
> 
> 2. **Using $n = 0.6$ for everything** — Williams' 0.6 is a *default*. For pumps, compressors, and special equipment, the actual $n$ can be 0.7–0.9. Read the problem carefully. **(Lose 1 mark)**
> 
> 3. **Skipping unit consistency** — Ensure $S_A$ and $S_B$ are in the **same units** (e.g., both m³/h, both kW, both m²). Mixing units leads to wrong answers. **(Lose 1–2 marks)**
> 
> 4. **Not commenting on economy of scale** — A numerical answer without the qualitative comment on "sub-linear cost growth" is **incomplete** and costs a mark.
> 
> 5. **Forgetting the validity range** — Always state the assumption $0.5 \leq S_B/S_A \leq 10$. Examiners love this phrase.

---

## 🧾 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> 📌 **Rapid Revision Checklist**
> 
> - ✅ Williams' Six-Tenths Rule: $C_B = C_A \times (S_B/S_A)^{0.6}$
> - ✅ The exponent **0.6** = *cost-capacity exponent* $n$, capturing **economy of scale**
> - ✅ Applicable **only for geometrically similar equipment** (same type, material, design)
> - ✅ Validity range: **$0.5 \leq R \leq 10$** where $R = S_B/S_A$
> - ✅ Accuracy: **±30 %** — classified as a **Class 5 (preliminary) estimate**
> - ✅ Quick values: $2^{0.6} \approx 1.52$; $10^{0.6} \approx 3.98$ — **MEMORIZE these!**
> - ✅ $n$ varies by equipment: tanks ≈ 0.5–0.7, exchangers ≈ 0.6–0.8, pumps/compressors ≈ 0.7–0.9
> - ✅ To determine $n$ from data: **plot $\ln C$ vs $\ln S$** — slope = $n$
> - ✅ For multi-year estimates: combine with **cost index** (CEPCI, Marshall & Swift) to update for inflation
> - ✅ If $n < 1$ → economy of scale; if $n > 1$ → diseconomy of scale (rare)
> - ✅ Limitation: **NOT** valid for very small capacity changes (use vendor quotes) or radically different designs
> - ✅ Exam tip: Always end numerical problems with a **commentary on economy of scale** for full marks
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Lang Factor Method
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT757 - ECONOMICS AND MANAGEMENT OF CHEMICAL INDUSTRIES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Lang Factor Method

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Lang Factor Method** is a **factorial method of capital cost estimation** in chemical engineering, originally developed by **H. J. Lang (1947)**. It estimates the **Total Module Cost (or Total Physical Plant Cost)** by multiplying the **Purchased/Delivered Equipment Cost (C_E)** of all major process equipment by a single dimensionless multiplier called the **Lang Factor (F_Lang)**.

Mathematically, the central relationship is:

$$C_{TM} = F_{Lang} \times C_{E}$$

where:
- $C_{TM}$ = **Total Module Cost / Total Physical Plant Cost (TPPC)** in monetary units (e.g., ₹, $)
- $F_{Lang}$ = **Lang Factor** (dimensionless)
- $C_{E}$ = **Purchased Equipment Cost (delivered cost)** in monetary units

The **Lang Factor itself** is empirically derived and depends on the **nature of the process** (solid handling, fluid handling, or mixed):

| Plant Type | Lang Factor (F_Lang) |
| :--- | :---: |
| Solid Processing Plant | **3.10** |
| Solid–Fluid Processing Plant | **3.63** |
| Fluid Processing Plant | **4.74** |

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "The House Renovation Multiplier":**
> Imagine you are buying all the major appliances for a new home — a refrigerator, washing machine, oven, and air conditioner. The price tag on the box (the *Purchased Equipment Cost*) is just the starting point. To get the appliances actually working in your home, you also have to pay for **transportation, installation, electrical wiring, plumbing hookups, painting, contractor labor, testing, and unexpected surprises**. The **Lang Factor** is like a single "magic multiplier" that bundles **all of these hidden installation-related costs** into one number. So if your appliances cost ₹5,00,000, and the multiplier is **4.74** (a fluid-type project, like an oil refinery), then the real total cost to get the plant running is roughly **₹23,70,000** — about **4.74 times the equipment price alone**. The Lang Factor simply *shortcuts* a very long itemized cost sheet.

### 1.3 Why This Method Exists in Chemical Engineering

In the **early stages of a chemical project** (conceptual / feasibility stage), engineers usually have:
- A **process flow diagram (PFD)**
- A **list of major equipment** with approximate sizes
- A **preliminary material & energy balance**

But they do **NOT** yet have:
- Detailed piping isometrics
- Finalized electrical/instrumentation drawings
- Vendor quotes for installation labor

The Lang Factor method was specifically designed to bridge this information gap — it provides a **quick, order-of-magnitude capital estimate** using only the **delivered cost of equipment**, which is often the only reliably known cost at the feasibility stage.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Tip:** Examiners love this method because it is **simple, formula-driven, and testable in 14-mark numericals**. Memorize the three Lang Factor values (3.10, 3.63, 4.74) — they are frequently asked in Part A as a 3-mark direct question.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Step-by-Step Logic of the Lang Factor Method

The method follows a strict hierarchical flow of cost buildup. Each step builds upon the previous one, and **every subsequent cost is a fraction of the prior aggregate cost**.

**Step 1 — Establish the Purchased Equipment Cost (C_E)**
- Sum the **delivered costs** of all major process equipment (reactors, columns, heat exchangers, pumps, compressors, vessels, tanks, etc.).
- This is the **only input** the Lang Factor strictly needs.

**Step 2 — Multiply by the Lang Factor (F_Lang)**
- Select $F_{Lang}$ based on the dominant process nature.
- $C_{TM} = F_{Lang} \times C_E$ gives the **Total Module Cost**, which includes:
  - Equipment installation
  - Piping (process & utility)
  - Instrumentation & control
  - Electrical systems
  - Insulation & painting
  - Civil/structural work
  - Buildings (process-related)
  - Yard improvement (minor)

**Step 3 — Compute the Offsites / Off-Location Capital (C_OL)**
- Offsites cover storage tanks, loading/unloading bays, utility generation (boilers, cooling towers, wastewater treatment), and the interconnecting pipe racks outside the main battery limits.
- Empirical correlation: **C_OL = 0.15 × C_TDC** (Total Direct Cost), or sometimes expressed as 15%–25% of the on-site module cost.

**Step 4 — Compute Total Direct Plant Cost (C_TDC)**
- $C_{TDC} = C_{TM} + C_{OL}$
- This represents the **sum of all money spent on physical construction and equipment**.

**Step 5 — Compute Total Indirect Plant Cost (C_TIPC)**
- Engineering, supervision, construction expenses, contingency, contractor's fee, etc.
- Empirically: **C_TIPC = 0.30 × C_TDC** (or expressed as a fraction of the on-site/off-site direct cost).

**Step 6 — Compute Fixed Capital Investment / Total Plant Cost (C_TPC)**
- $C_{TPC} = C_{TDC} + C_{TIPC}$
- Sometimes called **Total Permanent Investment (TPI)** in older textbooks.

**Step 7 — Compute Working Capital (C_WC)**
- C_WC ≈ 10%–20% of C_TPC (commonly taken as **15% of C_TPC** in KTU problems).
- Covers raw material inventory, finished goods stock, accounts receivable, and cash for day-to-day operations.

**Step 8 — Compute Total Capital Investment (C_TCI)**
- $C_{TCI} = C_{TPC} + C_{WC}$
- This is the **final answer** the project sponsor needs.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **The following table is the EXACT set of equations you must memorize for the KTU University Exam. No pipes are used inside math expressions — only `\vert` or `\mid` for absolute value (not needed here, but good practice).**

| Step | Quantity | Formula | Typical Coefficient |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :---: |
| 1 | Total Module Cost | $C_{TM} = F_{Lang} \times C_{E}$ | $F_{Lang} = 3.10, 3.63, 4.74$ |
| 2 | Offsites Cost | $C_{OL} = 0.15 \times C_{TM}$ | **0.15** (or 15%) |
| 3 | Total Direct Cost | $C_{TDC} = C_{TM} + C_{OL}$ | — |
| 4 | Total Indirect Cost | $C_{TIPC} = 0.30 \times C_{TDC}$ | **0.30** (or 30%) |
| 5 | Total Plant Cost | $C_{TPC} = C_{TDC} + C_{TIPC}$ | — |
| 6 | Working Capital | $C_{WC} = 0.15 \times C_{TPC}$ | **0.15** (or 15%) |
| 7 | **Total Capital Investment** | $C_{TCI} = C_{TPC} + C_{WC}$ | — |

### 2.3 Real-World Utility of the Lang Factor Method

- **Used by:** Project finance teams, banks performing techno-economic feasibility, EPC (Engineering, Procurement, Construction) contractors during bid preparation, and consulting firms (McKinsey, Bain chemical practice).
- **Accuracy:** Typically yields estimates with an accuracy of **±30% to ±50%** — useful at the **Order-of-Magnitude / Study Estimate** stage (Class V estimate per AACE classification).
- **Limitation:** It is **process-agnostic at the equipment level** — it does not account for high-pressure, high-temperature, or highly corrosive services (where the actual multiplier can be much higher than 4.74). It is being progressively replaced by more detailed **factorial methods (Hand, Peters–Timmerhaus, Turton)** in later project stages.
- **KTU exam relevance:** Forms the basis of an entire 14-mark Part B question every year — a numerical from equipment cost to TCI.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Worked Example (14-Mark Numerical, KTU Board Standard)

> **Problem Statement:**
> The purchased cost of all major equipment for a proposed **fluid processing plant** is **₹50,00,000** (₹50 Lakhs). Using the **Lang Factor Method**, calculate the following:
> (a) Total Module Cost and Total Direct Cost (7 marks)
> (b) Total Plant Cost and Total Capital Investment (7 marks)

**Given Data:**
- $C_{E} = \text{₹}50,00,000$
- Plant type = **Fluid Processing** ⟹ $F_{Lang} = 4.74$
- Offsite factor = **0.15** (assumed standard)
- Indirect cost factor = **0.30** (assumed standard)
- Working capital factor = **0.15** (assumed standard)

---

#### Part (a) — Total Module Cost & Total Direct Cost

**Step A1: Compute the Total Module Cost (C_TM)**

$$C_{TM} = F_{Lang} \times C_{E}$$

$$C_{TM} = 4.74 \times 50{,}00{,}000$$

$$C_{TM} = 237{,}00{,}000$$

$$\boxed{C_{TM} = \text{₹}2,37,00,000 = \text{₹}2.37 \text{ Crores}}$$

*Textual logic:* The Lang Factor **4.74** (fluid plant) inflates the bare equipment cost of ₹50 Lakhs to nearly **4.74 times** — accounting for piping, instrumentation, electrical, insulation, painting, structural steel, and installation labor.

**Step A2: Compute the Offsites Cost (C_OL)**

$$C_{OL} = 0.15 \times C_{TM}$$

$$C_{OL} = 0.15 \times 2{,}37{,}00{,}000$$

$$C_{OL} = 35{,}55{,}000$$

**Step A3: Compute the Total Direct Cost (C_TDC)**

$$C_{TDC} = C_{TM} + C_{OL}$$

$$C_{TDC} = 2{,}37{,}00{,}000 + 35{,}55{,}000$$

$$\boxed{C_{TDC} = \text{₹}2,72,55{,}000 = \text{₹}2.7255 \text{ Crores}}$$

*Textual logic:* The Total Direct Cost represents **all money spent on physical assets** — both the main process plant (inside the battery limits) and the offsite facilities (storage, utilities, pipe racks).

---

#### Part (b) — Total Plant Cost & Total Capital Investment

**Step B1: Compute the Total Indirect Plant Cost (C_TIPC)**

$$C_{TIPC} = 0.30 \times C_{TDC}$$

$$C_{TIPC} = 0.30 \times 2{,}72{,}55{,}000$$

$$C_{TIPC} = 81{,}76{,}500$$

*Textual logic:* The 30% indirect factor covers **engineering design fees, construction supervision, contractor's profit, contingency, and startup expenses** — costs that are not physical hardware but are absolutely necessary to bring the plant online.

**Step B2: Compute the Total Plant Cost (C_TPC)**

$$C_{TPC} = C_{TDC} + C_{TIPC}$$

$$C_{TPC} = 2{,}72{,}55{,}000 + 81{,}76{,}500$$

$$\boxed{C_{TPC} = \text{₹}3,54{,}31{,}500 = \text{₹}3.5432 \text{ Crores}}$$

*Textual logic:* Total Plant Cost is the **Fixed Capital Investment (FCI)** — the total amount of money that must be raised and spent to construct the plant ready for operation.

**Step B3: Compute the Working Capital (C_WC)**

$$C_{WC} = 0.15 \times C_{TPC}$$

$$C_{WC} = 0.15 \times 3{,}54{,}31{,}500$$

$$C_{WC} = 53{,}14{,}725$$

*Textual logic:* Working capital is **not spent on construction** — it is the **liquid cash** required to run the plant: buying the first batches of raw materials, paying salaries before product sales begin, and maintaining inventory buffers.

**Step B4: Compute the Total Capital Investment (C_TCI)**

$$C_{TCI} = C_{TPC} + C_{WC}$$

$$C_{TCI} = 3{,}54{,}31{,}500 + 53{,}14{,}725$$

$$\boxed{C_{TCI} = \text{₹}4{,}07{,}46{,}225 = \text{₹}4.0746 \text{ Crores}}$$

---

#### 3.2 Analytical Summary Matrix (Cost Buildup Table for the Worked Example)

| Cost Component | Symbol | Formula Used | Calculated Value (₹) | % of TCI |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | ---: | ---: |
| Purchased Equipment Cost | $C_{E}$ | Given | 50,00,000 | 12.27% |
| Total Module Cost | $C_{TM}$ | $4.74 \times C_E$ | 2,37,00,000 | 58.16% |
| Offsites Cost | $C_{OL}$ | $0.15 \times C_{TM}$ | 35,55,000 | 8.72% |
| Total Direct Cost | $C_{TDC}$ | $C_{TM} + C_{OL}$ | 2,72,55,000 | 66.88% |
| Total Indirect Cost | $C_{TIPC}$ | $0.30 \times C_{TDC}$ | 81,76,500 | 20.07% |
| Total Plant Cost | $C_{TPC}$ | $C_{TDC} + C_{TIPC}$ | 3,54,31,500 | 86.96% |
| Working Capital | $C_{WC}$ | $0.15 \times C_{TPC}$ | 53,14,725 | 13.04% |
| **Total Capital Investment** | $\mathbf{C_{TCI}}$ | $\mathbf{C_{TPC} + C_{WC}}$ | **4,07,46,225** | **100.00%** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Observation:** The **Purchased Equipment Cost itself is only about 12.27% of the Total Capital Investment**. The remaining ~87.73% goes to installation, piping, offsite facilities, engineering, and working capital. This is why chemical plants are *capital-intensive* — buying the equipment is the smallest part of the cost!
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Lang Factor Method

The diagram below is a **Mermaid flowchart** showing the cost-buildup logic from bare equipment cost to Total Capital Investment. All node IDs are alphanumeric (no reserved keywords) and labels contain raw uppercase text only (no bold/italic markdown).

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    node1["PURCHASED EQUIPMENT COST C_E"] --> node2{"SELECT PLANT TYPE"}
    node2 -- "SOLID" --> node3A["F_LANG = 3.10"]
    node2 -- "SOLID FLUID" --> node3B["F_LANG = 3.63"]
    node2 -- "FLUID" --> node3C["F_LANG = 4.74"]
    node3A --> node4["TOTAL MODULE COST C_TM = F_LANG TIMES C_E"]
    node3B --> node4
    node3C --> node4
    node4 --> node5["OFFSITES COST C_OL = 0.15 TIMES C_TM"]
    node5 --> node6["TOTAL DIRECT COST C_TDC = C_TM PLUS C_OL"]
    node6 --> node7["TOTAL INDIRECT COST C_TIPC = 0.30 TIMES C_TDC"]
    node7 --> node8["TOTAL PLANT COST C_TPC = C_TDC PLUS C_TIPC"]
    node8 --> node9["WORKING CAPITAL C_WC = 0.15 TIMES C_TPC"]
    node9 --> node10["TOTAL CAPITAL INVESTMENT C_TCI = C_TPC PLUS C_WC"]
    node10 --> node11["PROJECT VIABILITY AND ECONOMIC ANALYSIS"]
```

### 4.2 Cost Aggregation Hierarchy (Layered View)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L1["LEVEL 1 EQUIPMENT C_E"] 
    L2["LEVEL 2 MODULE C_TM = 4.74 C_E"]
    L3["LEVEL 3 DIRECT C_TDC = C_TM + 0.15 C_TM"]
    L4["LEVEL 4 PLANT C_TPC = C_TDC + 0.30 C_TDC"]
    L5["LEVEL 5 TOTAL C_TCI = C_TPC + 0.15 C_TPC"]
    L1 --> L2 --> L3 --> L4 --> L5
    L2 -.- A["PIPING INSULATION INSTRUMENTATION ELECTRICAL CIVIL PAINTING"]
    L3 -.- B["OFFSITES STORAGE UTILITIES PIPE RACKS"]
    L4 -.- C["ENGINEERING SUPERVISION CONTINGENCY CONTRACTOR FEE"]
    L5 -.- D["RAW MATERIALS INVENTORY CASH FOR OPERATIONS"]
```

### 4.3 Percentage-of-TCI Waterfall (Conceptual View)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1["EQUIPMENT 12.27 PERCENT"] --> P2["MODULE ADDITION 45.89 PERCENT"] --> P3["OFFSITES ADDITION 8.72 PERCENT"] --> P4["INDIRECT ADDITION 20.07 PERCENT"] --> P5["WORKING CAPITAL 13.04 PERCENT"] --> P6["TOTAL 100 PERCENT"]
```

> [!TIP]
> **How to read these diagrams in a KTU exam:** If the question shows a flowchart and asks you to label missing blocks, **start from the topmost block (Purchased Equipment Cost)** and walk downward — every block is built using the formula in the block directly above it.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Question]**

**Q1.** Define the **Lang Factor Method** of capital cost estimation. State the Lang Factor values for (i) a solid processing plant, (ii) a fluid processing plant, and (iii) a mixed solid–fluid processing plant.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
The Lang Factor Method is an **empirical factorial method** for estimating the **Total Module Cost (or Total Physical Plant Cost)** of a chemical plant, originally proposed by **H. J. Lang in 1947**. It is given by:

$$C_{TM} = F_{Lang} \times C_{E}$$

where $C_{E}$ is the **purchased (delivered) cost of all major equipment** and $F_{Lang}$ is the **Lang Factor**, a dimensionless multiplier that bundles the costs of installation, piping, instrumentation, electrical, insulation, structural work, and painting into a single number.

The three standard Lang Factor values are:
- **Solid processing plant** ⟹ $F_{Lang} = 3.10$
- **Solid–fluid processing plant** ⟹ $F_{Lang} = 3.63$
- **Fluid processing plant** ⟹ $F_{Lang} = 4.74$

> *(1 mark for the formula, 1 mark for the definition, 1 mark for the three factor values.)*

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Question]**

**Q2.** Differentiate between **Total Module Cost (C_TM)**, **Total Plant Cost (C_TPC)**, and **Total Capital Investment (C_TCI)** in the context of Lang Factor estimation.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Term | What It Represents | How It Is Computed |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **C_TM** | All on-site physical installation costs (equipment + piping + electrical + instrumentation + civil + insulation + painting) inside the battery limits | $F_{Lang} \times C_{E}$ |
| **C_TPC** | Fixed Capital Investment — the total money needed to make the plant physically ready for operation (on-site + offsite + indirect costs) | $C_{TDC} + C_{TIPC}$ |
| **C_TCI** | Total capital outlay including the liquid cash needed to operate the plant | $C_{TPC} + C_{WC}$ |

> *(1 mark per correct row. Tabular format fetches full credit.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2023, Adapted]**

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

The total purchased cost of equipment for a proposed **solid–fluid processing plant** is estimated at **₹30,00,000**. Using the Lang Factor Method, determine:

**(a)** The **Total Module Cost** and the **Total Direct Cost (TDC)** of the plant. (7 marks)

**(b)** The **Total Plant Cost (TPC)** and the **Total Capital Investment (TCI)** of the plant. (7 marks)

**Assume standard empirical factors for offsite, indirect, and working capital.**

---

**Complete Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- $C_{E} = \text{₹}30,00,000$
- Plant type = Solid–Fluid ⟹ $F_{Lang} = 3.63$
- Offsite factor = **0.15**
- Indirect cost factor = **0.30**
- Working capital factor = **0.15**

---

**Part (a) Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Total Module Cost:**

$$C_{TM} = F_{Lang} \times C_{E} = 3.63 \times 30{,}00{,}000 = 1{,}08{,}90{,}000$$

$$\boxed{C_{TM} = \text{₹}1,08,90{,}000}$$

*(2 marks for the correct formula and substitution; 1 mark for the final value.)*

**Step 2 — Offsites Cost:**

$$C_{OL} = 0.15 \times C_{TM} = 0.15 \times 1{,}08{,}90{,}000 = 16{,}33{,}500$$

**Step 3 — Total Direct Cost:**

$$C_{TDC} = C_{TM} + C_{OL} = 1{,}08{,}90{,}000 + 16{,}33{,}500$$

$$\boxed{C_{TDC} = \text{₹}1,25{,}23{,}500}$$

*(2 marks for offsite formula; 2 marks for TDC calculation.)*

---

**Part (b) Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 4 — Total Indirect Plant Cost:**

$$C_{TIPC} = 0.30 \times C_{TDC} = 0.30 \times 1{,}25{,}23{,}500 = 37{,}57{,}050$$

**Step 5 — Total Plant Cost:**

$$C_{TPC} = C_{TDC} + C_{TIPC} = 1{,}25{,}23{,}500 + 37{,}57{,}050$$

$$\boxed{C_{TPC} = \text{₹}1,62{,}80{,}550}$$

*(3 marks: 1 for indirect formula, 1 for TPC formula, 1 for final value.)*

**Step 6 — Working Capital:**

$$C_{WC} = 0.15 \times C_{TPC} = 0.15 \times 1{,}62{,}80{,}550 = 24{,}42{,}082.50$$

**Step 7 — Total Capital Investment:**

$$C_{TCI} = C_{TPC} + C_{WC} = 1{,}62{,}80{,}550 + 24{,}42{,}082.50$$

$$\boxed{C_{TCI} = \text{₹}1,87{,}22{,}632.50 \approx \text{₹}1.872 \text{ Crores}}$$

*(4 marks: 1.5 for WC formula, 1.5 for TCI formula, 1 for final value.)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Adapted]**

A process plant is being designed for the manufacture of a specialty chemical. The major equipment cost summary is as follows:

| Equipment | Purchased Cost (₹ Lakhs) |
| :--- | ---: |
| Reactor | 18.0 |
| Distillation Column | 25.0 |
| Heat Exchangers (3 nos.) | 12.0 |
| Pumps & Compressors | 9.0 |
| Storage Tanks | 6.0 |
| **Total** | **70.0** |

The plant is predominantly a **fluid processing** facility.

**(a)** Estimate the **Total Module Cost (C_TM)** and **Total Direct Cost (C_TDC)**. (7 marks)

**(b)** Estimate the **Total Plant Cost (C_TPC)** and **Total Capital Investment (C_TCI)**. (7 marks)

**Use standard Lang Factor and empirical factors.**

---

**Complete Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- Total Purchased Equipment Cost $C_{E} = \text{₹}70 \text{ Lakhs} = 70{,}00{,}000$
- Plant type = Fluid ⟹ $F_{Lang} = 4.74$
- $C_{OL} = 0.15 \times C_{TM}$, $\quad C_{TIPC} = 0.30 \times C_{TDC}$, $\quad C_{WC} = 0.15 \times C_{TPC}$

---

**Part (a) Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Total Module Cost:**

$$C_{TM} = F_{Lang} \times C_{E} = 4.74 \times 70{,}00{,}000$$

$$C_{TM} = 3{,}31{,}80{,}000$$

$$\boxed{C_{TM} = \text{₹}3,31{,}80{,}000 = \text{₹}3.318 \text{ Crores}}$$

*(2 marks)*

**Step 2 — Offsites Cost:**

$$C_{OL} = 0.15 \times C_{TM} = 0.15 \times 3{,}31{,}80{,}000 = 49{,}77{,}000$$

**Step 3 — Total Direct Cost:**

$$C_{TDC} = C_{TM} + C_{OL} = 3{,}31{,}80{,}000 + 49{,}77{,}000$$

$$\boxed{C_{TDC} = \text{₹}3,81{,}57{,}000}$$

*(3 marks)*

---

**Part (b) Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 4 — Total Indirect Plant Cost:**

$$C_{TIPC} = 0.30 \times C_{TDC} = 0.30 \times 3{,}81{,}57{,}000 = 1{,}14{,}47{,}100$$

**Step 5 — Total Plant Cost:**

$$C_{TPC} = C_{TDC} + C_{TIPC} = 3{,}81{,}57{,}000 + 1{,}14{,}47{,}100$$

$$\boxed{C_{TPC} = \text{₹}4,96{,}04{,}100 = \text{₹}4.96 \text{ Crores}}$$

*(3.5 marks)*

**Step 6 — Working Capital:**

$$C_{WC} = 0.15 \times C_{TPC} = 0.15 \times 4{,}96{,}04{,}100 = 74{,}40{,}615$$

**Step 7 — Total Capital Investment:**

$$C_{TCI} = C_{TPC} + C_{WC} = 4{,}96{,}04{,}100 + 74{,}40{,}615$$

$$\boxed{C_{TCI} = \text{₹}5,70{,}44{,}715 = \text{₹}5.704 \text{ Crores}}$$

*(3.5 marks)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Wrong Lang Factor selection:** Students often confuse "Solid-Fluid" with "Fluid-Solid" — both are the SAME thing and use **3.63**. Memorize: pure solid = 3.10, mixed = 3.63, pure fluid = 4.74.
> 2. **Forgetting to add offsite and indirect costs:** Many students stop at $C_{TM}$ and report it as the final answer. The question asks for **TCI**, which requires **all four** intermediate additions: offsite, indirect, and working capital.
> 3. **Unit errors:** Watch the placement of decimal points. ₹70 Lakhs = ₹70,00,000 (seventy million rupees), NOT ₹7,00,000.
> 4. **Skipping the working capital step:** C_TCI = C_TPC + C_WC. Skipping C_WC loses 2 marks even if everything else is correct.
> 5. **Not stating the empirical assumptions:** Always write "Assuming offsite = 15%, indirect = 30%, and working capital = 15% (standard empirical values)" at the start — this earns the "Assumptions" mark that KTU examiners specifically allocate.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Pin This Before the Exam**

- **Lang Factor Method** was developed by **H. J. Lang (1947)**.
- **Master formula:** $C_{TM} = F_{Lang} \times C_{E}$
- **Three Lang Factors (memorize verbatim):**
  - **Solid** plant → **3.10**
  - **Solid–Fluid** plant → **3.63**
  - **Fluid** plant → **4.74**
- **Eight-step cost buildup order:** $C_E \rightarrow C_{TM} \rightarrow C_{OL} \rightarrow C_{TDC} \rightarrow C_{TIPC} \rightarrow C_{TPC} \rightarrow C_{WC} \rightarrow C_{TCI}$
- **Standard empirical factors (universally used in KTU problems):**
  - $C_{OL} = 0.15 \times C_{TM}$
  - $C_{TIPC} = 0.30 \times C_{TDC}$
  - $C_{WC} = 0.15 \times C_{TPC}$
- **Equipment cost is only ~12% of total capital investment** — this is the most-cited insight in viva questions.
- **Accuracy of Lang method:** ±30% to ±50% (Order-of-Magnitude estimate, Class V per AACE).
- **Use this method only at the feasibility / conceptual stage** — not for final project sanction.
- **Limitations:** Does not account for high pressure (>100 bar), high temperature (>800°C), or highly corrosive service — in such cases the multiplier must be manually inflated beyond 4.74.
- **Examiner hot-trick:** If a question gives the "Lang Factor" as a value OTHER than 3.10/3.63/4.74, treat it as a custom value and use it directly — do not force-fit it to the three standard values.
- **Always present the final answer in both numeric form AND in words** (e.g., "₹4,07,46,225 ≈ ₹4.07 Crores") — this protects against transcription errors during valuation.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hand Factor Method
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT757 - ECONOMICS AND MANAGEMENT OF CHEMICAL INDUSTRIES
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Hand Factor Method

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition
