| 3 | Piping (process) | $f_{pip}$ | 31 | 16 |
| 4 | Instrumentation & Controls | $f_{IC}$ | 13 | 9 |
| 5 | Insulation | $f_{ins}$ | 4 | 2 |
| 6 | Electrical | $f_{elec}$ | 5 | 7 |
| 7 | Buildings (process) | $f_{bld}$ | 29 | 24 |
| 8 | Yard Improvements | $f_{yard}$ | 9 | 4 |
| 9 | Service Facilities (utilities) | $f_{sf}$ | 55 | 37 |
| | **ISBL Multiplier $\Sigma f$** | | **285** | **230** |
| 10 | OSBL / Offsite Facilities (typical) | $f_{OSBL}$ | 30 – 40 | 20 – 30 |
| 11 | Engineering & Supervision | $f_{ES}$ | 32 | 23 |
| 12 | Construction Expenses | $f_{CE}$ | 34 | 22 |
| 13 | Legal Expenses | $f_{LE}$ | 4 | 3 |
| 14 | Contractor's Fee | $f_{CF}$ | 19 | 13 |
| 15 | Contingency | $f_{cont}$ | 37 | 25 |
| | **Total Indirect Multiplier $\Sigma f_{ind}$** | | **126** | **86** |

### 2.4 Core Mathematical Formulas

**Direct Cost (ISBL) Equation:**

$$C_{ISBL} = E \times \left(1 + \sum f_{direct}\right) = E \times \Sigma f_{ISBL}$$

For fluid plants, the multiplier $1 + \sum f_{direct} = 1 + 1.85 = \mathbf{2.85}$ (i.e., 285 % of $E$).

**Total Direct Plant Cost (ISBL + OSBL):**

$$C_{TDPC} = C_{ISBL} \times (1 + f_{OSBL})$$

**Fixed Capital Investment:**

$$C_{FCI} = C_{TDPC} \times \left(1 + \sum f_{indirect}\right)$$

**Total Capital Investment:**

$$C_{TCI} = C_{FCI} + C_{WC}$$

where the working capital $C_{WC}$ is typically 15 – 20 % of $C_{TCI}$, or alternatively estimated from operating cost components.

### 2.5 Real-World Utility in Chemical Engineering

- **Used by:** EPC (Engineering, Procurement & Construction) contractors during the **Conceptual / FEED (Front-End Engineering Design)** stage to provide owners with an early project capital cost.
- **Used for:** Feasibility studies, project screening, comparative evaluation of alternative process routes, and preparation of sanction budgets.
- **Advantage over Lang Factor:** Peters–Timmerhaus provides a *component-by-component* breakdown, which means a project manager can benchmark which line-item costs are deviating from industry norms.
- **Limitation:** Factors are based on **historical averages** (mid-20th century data, periodically updated). They do **not** capture project-specific factors such as remote location, seismic zone, or highly corrosive service.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 General Derivation of the Cost Build-Up

Let $E$ be the total delivered cost of all major equipment items in a process plant. We define the **ISBL multiplier** as:

$$M_{ISBL} = 1 + \sum_{i=1}^{9} f_i$$

where $f_i$ are the Peters–Timmerhaus ratio factors (Installation, Piping, Instrumentation, Insulation, Electrical, Buildings, Yard, Service Facilities). The number "1" represents the equipment cost itself (100 % of itself).

For a typical **fluid-processing plant**:

$$M_{ISBL} = 1 + 0.39 + 0.31 + 0.13 + 0.04 + 0.05 + 0.29 + 0.09 + 0.55 = 2.85$$

Hence,

$$C_{ISBL} = 2.85 \, E$$

The OSBL facilities (storage, utilities, offsite pipe racks, fire-fighting, etc.) are estimated as a fraction $\beta$ of $C_{ISBL}$ (typically $\beta = 0.30$ to $0.40$):

$$C_{TDPC} = C_{ISBL} \, (1 + \beta)$$

The indirect costs (engineering, construction, legal, contractor, contingency) are similarly a fraction $\gamma$ of $C_{TDPC}$:

$$C_{FCI} = C_{TDPC} \, (1 + \gamma)$$

Combining the two:

$$C_{FCI} = M_{ISBL} \, E \, (1 + \beta)(1 + \gamma)$$

Finally, working capital is added:

$$C_{TCI} = C_{FCI} + C_{WC} = C_{FCI} + \alpha \, C_{TCI}$$

where $\alpha$ is the working-capital fraction (typically $\alpha = 0.15$ to $0.20$). Solving:

$$C_{TCI} (1 - \alpha) = C_{FCI} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad C_{TCI} = \frac{C_{FCI}}{1 - \alpha}$$

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (Full KTU-Style Solution)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** The total delivered cost of all major equipment in a fluid-processing plant is **₹ 50,00,000** (i.e., ₹ 50 Lakhs). Using the Peters–Timmerhaus ratio factors, estimate:
> (a) ISBL plant cost
> (b) Total Direct Plant Cost (assuming OSBL = 35 % of ISBL)
> (c) Fixed Capital Investment
> (d) Total Capital Investment (assuming WC = 18 % of TCI)

#### **Given Data**

$$E = \text{₹ } 50,00,000, \quad \beta = 0.35, \quad \gamma = 0.126 \text{ (for fluid plant)}, \quad \alpha = 0.18$$

#### **Solution — Step by Step**

**Step 1: Calculate the ISBL Multiplier for a fluid plant.**

$$M_{ISBL} = 1 + 0.39 + 0.31 + 0.13 + 0.04 + 0.05 + 0.29 + 0.09 + 0.55 = 2.85$$

*(The "1" represents the 100 % equipment cost itself.)*

**Step 2: Compute ISBL Plant Cost.**

$$C_{ISBL} = M_{ISBL} \times E = 2.85 \times 50,00,000$$

$$C_{ISBL} = \text{₹ } 1,42,50,000 \;\; (\text{i.e., } 1 \text{ Cr } 42.5 \text{ L})$$

**Step 3: Add OSBL (35 % of ISBL).**

$$C_{TDPC} = C_{ISBL} \times (1 + \beta) = 1,42,50,000 \times 1.35$$

$$C_{TDPC} = \text{₹ } 1,92,37,500 \;\; (\text{i.e., } 1 \text{ Cr } 92.375 \text{ L})$$

**Step 4: Add Indirect Costs (126 % of TDPC).**

$$C_{FCI} = C_{TDPC} \times (1 + \gamma) = 1,92,37,500 \times 1.126$$

$$C_{FCI} = \text{₹ } 2,16,61,425 \;\; (\text{i.e., } 2 \text{ Cr } 16.61 \text{ L})$$

**Step 5: Add Working Capital (18 % of TCI).**

Let $C_{TCI}$ be unknown.

$$C_{TCI} = C_{FCI} + 0.18 \times C_{TCI}$$

$$C_{TCI} (1 - 0.18) = 2,16,61,425$$

$$C_{TCI} = \frac{2,16,61,425}{0.82} = \text{₹ } 2,64,16,372 \;\; (\approx 2 \text{ Cr } 64.16 \text{ L})$$

**Working Capital itself:**

$$C_{WC} = 0.18 \times 2,64,16,372 = \text{₹ } 47,54,947$$

#### **Summary Table of the Analytical Matrix**

| S.No | Cost Item | Multiplier | Amount (₹) | % of Equipment Cost |
|:----:|:----------|:----------:|-----------:|--------------------:|
| 1 | Purchased Equipment $(E)$ | 1.00 | 50,00,000 | 100 % |
| 2 | ISBL Plant Cost | 2.85 | 1,42,50,000 | 285 % |
| 3 | Total Direct Plant Cost | 3.85 | 1,92,37,500 | 385 % |
| 4 | Fixed Capital Investment | 4.33 | 2,16,61,425 | 433 % |
| 5 | **Total Capital Investment** | **5.28** | **2,64,16,372** | **528 %** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** The Total Capital Investment is approximately **5.28 × the equipment cost**, which is consistent with industry benchmark of 4.5–5.5× for grass-roots fluid plants.

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison with Lang Factor

| Method | Multiplier Type | Accuracy | Information Required |
|:-------|:----------------|:---------|:---------------------|
| **Lang Factor** | Single multiplier (3.1 fluids / 2.6 solids / 3.6 mixed) | ±30 – 35 % | Total equipment cost only |
| **Peters–Timmerhaus** | Multi-component ratio factors | ±20 – 30 % | Total equipment cost + identification of fluid/solids |
| **Detailed / Unit-Cost Estimate** | Line-item quoting | ±5 – 10 % | Full P&ID, equipment list, vendor quotes, civil drawings |

This is why KTU examiners frequently pose a *follow-up question* asking the student to *compare and justify* the choice of method — Peters–Timmerhaus is **more accurate** than Lang but still in the *preliminary* class.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Hierarchical Cost Build-Up Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["MAJOR EQUIPMENT COST E"] --> B["ISBL DIRECT COSTS"]
    B --> B1["EQUIPMENT INSTALLATION 39%"]
    B --> B2["PIPING 31%"]
    B --> B3["INSTRUMENTATION 13%"]
    B --> B4["INSULATION 4%"]
    B --> B5["ELECTRICAL 5%"]
    B --> B6["BUILDINGS 29%"]
    B --> B7["YARD IMPROVEMENT 9%"]
    B --> B8["SERVICE FACILITIES 55%"]
    A --> B0["EQUIPMENT 100%"]
    B0 --> C["ISBL PLANT COST 2.85E"]
    B1 --> C
    B2 --> C
    B3 --> C
    B4 --> C
    B5 --> C
    B6 --> C
    B7 --> C
    B8 --> C
    C --> D["OSBL OFFSITES 35% OF ISBL"]
    D --> E["TOTAL DIRECT PLANT COST 3.85E"]
    E --> F1["ENGINEERING 32%"]
    E --> F2["CONSTRUCTION 34%"]
    E --> F3["LEGAL 4%"]
    E --> F4["CONTRACTOR FEE 19%"]
    E --> F5["CONTINGENCY 37%"]
    F1 --> G["FIXED CAPITAL INVESTMENT 4.33E"]
    F2 --> G
    F3 --> G
    F4 --> G
    F5 --> G
    G --> H["WORKING CAPITAL 18% OF TCI"]
    H --> I["TOTAL CAPITAL INVESTMENT 5.28E"]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Decision Diagram — When to Apply Fluid vs. Solids Factor

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S["START IDENTIFY PROCESS"] --> Q{"IS THE PROCESS FLUID OR SOLIDS HANDLING?"}
    Q -- "FLUIDS PUMPS COLUMNS HEAT EXCHANGERS" --> R1["USE FLUID FACTORS F EQUAL 2.85 ISBL"]
    Q -- "SOLIDS CRUSHERS DRYERS CONVEYORS" --> R2["USE SOLIDS FACTORS F EQUAL 2.30 ISBL"]
    Q -- "MIXED REACTOR WITH SOLIDS FEED" --> R3["WEIGHTED AVERAGE BASED ON COST SHARE"]
    R1 --> OUT["PROCEED TO OSBL AND INDIRECT COSTS"]
    R2 --> OUT
    R3 --> OUT
```

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture

| Stage | Input | Operation | Output |
|:-----:|:------|:----------|:-------|
| 1 | Process Flow Diagram, Equipment List | Equipment sizing & cost correlation (CAPCOST / vendor RFQ) | $E$ = Total Purchased Equipment Cost |
| 2 | $E$ + Process Type | Multiply by $M_{ISBL}$ (=2.85 or 2.30) | $C_{ISBL}$ |
| 3 | $C_{ISBL}$ | Add OSBL fraction $\beta$ | $C_{TDPC}$ |
| 4 | $C_{TDPC}$ | Add Indirect factor $\gamma$ | $C_{FCI}$ |
| 5 | $C_{FCI}$ | Add Working Capital $\alpha$ | $C_{TCI}$ |
| 6 | $C_{TCI}$ | Report to management as preliminary estimate | Project Sanction Note |

> [!NOTE]
> **Visual Intuition:** Think of $E$ as a single brick. Stage 2 lays the brick with mortar, plumbing, and wiring into a *house* (ISBL). Stage 3 adds the *compound wall and garden* (OSBL). Stage 4 pays the *architect and engineer* (Indirect). Stage 5 stocks the *fridge and groceries* (Working Capital). The final price of the *functioning home* is the Total Capital Investment.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Question]**
> *What is the Peters–Timmerhaus Ratio Factor Method? State any three advantages it has over the Lang Factor method.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The Peters–Timmerhaus method is a preliminary capital cost estimation technique in which the **purchased cost of major equipment** is used as a base and is multiplied by a series of **component-wise ratio factors** (installation, piping, instrumentation, insulation, electrical, buildings, yard, services) to estimate the Fixed Capital Investment of a process plant.

**Three advantages over Lang Factor:**
1. Provides a **component-wise breakdown** of capital cost, allowing identification of major cost contributors.
2. **More accurate** (±20–30 %) compared to Lang (±30–35 %) because it distinguishes between fluid and solids processing plants.
3. Allows **selective escalation** of individual items (e.g., piping cost rise in a stainless-steel project) — not possible with Lang's single multiplier.

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question]**
> *Why is working capital not included in the Fixed Capital Investment? How is it estimated in the Peters–Timmerhaus approach?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Working capital represents the **liquid cash** required to *operate* the plant (raw material inventory, finished goods inventory, accounts receivable, cash for salaries, etc.), whereas Fixed Capital is the **non-liquid, depreciable investment** in physical assets. Working capital is therefore **excluded from FCI** and reported separately.

In the Peters–Timmerhaus approach, working capital is typically estimated as **15–20 % of the Total Capital Investment (TCI)**, or alternatively built up from individual operating-cost components (raw material inventory for ~30 days, finished product inventory, etc.).

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> **Question A — Option 1 [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Question, 14 Marks]**
>
> **(a)** Explain the Peters–Timmerhaus Ratio Factor Method in detail. List the ratio factors for a **fluid-processing plant** and a **solids-handling plant**. Justify why the fluid-plant factors are higher for piping and insulation. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A fluid-processing chemical plant has a total delivered equipment cost of **₹ 80 Lakhs**. Using the Peters–Timmerhaus factors, estimate the **Total Capital Investment** assuming OSBL = 30 % of ISBL, Indirect Costs = 126 % of TDPC, and Working Capital = 15 % of TCI. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Explanation (7 marks):**
The Peters–Timmerhaus method (Peters & Timmerhaus, *Plant Design and Economics for Chemical Engineers*) estimates the **Total Capital Investment** of a chemical plant by using the **purchased cost of all major equipment** as a base and multiplying it with **ratio factors** for each cost component.

**Ratio factors (as % of $E$):**

| Component | Fluid Plant (%) | Solids Plant (%) |
|:---|:---:|:---:|
| Equipment (base) | 100 | 100 |
| Installation | 39 | 31 |
| Piping | 31 | 16 |
| Instrumentation | 13 | 9 |
| Insulation | 4 | 2 |
| Electrical | 5 | 7 |
| Buildings | 29 | 24 |
| Yard Improvement | 9 | 4 |
| Service Facilities | 55 | 37 |
| **Total ISBL Multiplier** | **285** | **230** |

**Justification for higher fluid-plant factors:** Fluid-processing plants have **extensive network of process piping** (carrying liquids/gases under pressure, often at high/low temperatures) requiring significant **insulation**, **instrumentation** (flow, level, pressure transmitters), and **service facilities** (cooling water, steam, refrigeration, compressed air). Solids plants, in contrast, mainly use conveyors and hoppers, requiring less piping and insulation but more structural and electrical (motor-driven) installations.

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

Given: $E = \text{₹ } 80,00,000$; $\beta = 0.30$; $\gamma = 1.26$; $\alpha = 0.15$.

**Step 1: ISBL Cost**

$$C_{ISBL} = 2.85 \times 80,00,000 = \text{₹ } 2,28,00,000$$

**Step 2: Total Direct Plant Cost (with 30 % OSBL)**

$$C_{TDPC} = 2,28,00,000 \times 1.30 = \text{₹ } 2,96,40,000$$

**Step 3: Fixed Capital Investment (with 126 % Indirect)**

$$C_{FCI} = 2,96,40,000 \times 2.26 = \text{₹ } 6,69,86,400$$

**Step 4: Total Capital Investment (with 15 % Working Capital)**

$$C_{TCI} = C_{FCI} + 0.15 \, C_{TCI} \;\Rightarrow\; C_{TCI} = \frac{6,69,86,400}{0.85} = \text{₹ } 7,88,07,529$$

> **Final Answer:** $\boxed{C_{TCI} \approx \text{₹ } 7.88 \text{ Crores}}$

> **Question B — Option 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question, 14 Marks]**
>
> **(a)** Compare and contrast the **Lang Factor method** with the **Peters–Timmerhaus method** in terms of accuracy, data requirement, and applicability. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A solids-handling plant has a total delivered equipment cost of **₹ 1.2 Crores**. Estimate the **ISBL Plant Cost, Total Direct Plant Cost, and Fixed Capital Investment** using Peters–Timmerhaus factors for a solids plant. Assume OSBL = 25 % of ISBL. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Comparison (7 marks):**

| Parameter | Lang Factor Method | Peters–Timmerhaus Method |
|:---|:---|:---|
| Number of factors | Single (Lang factor $f_L$) | 9+ direct + 5 indirect factors |
| Typical $f_L$ | 3.1 (fluid), 2.6 (solids), 3.6 (mixed) | 2.85 (fluid), 2.30 (solids) — as ISBL multiplier |
| Accuracy | ±30 – 35 % | ±20 – 30 % |
| Data Required | Only $E$ | Only $E$ + plant type (fluid/solids) |
| Cost Breakdown | None (single lumped value) | Component-wise |
| Best for | Quick screening studies | Detailed preliminary estimation |
| Flexibility | Cannot escalate individual items | Each component can be re-estimated |
| Origin | Lang (1947) | Peters & Timmerhaus (1968 onwards) |

Peters–Timmerhaus is essentially a **disaggregated, statistically refined** version of Lang's method.

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

Given: $E = \text{₹ } 1,20,00,000$; $\beta = 0.25$; indirect factor for solids = 0.86.

**Step 1: ISBL Plant Cost (multiplier = 2.30 for solids)**

$$C_{ISBL} = 2.30 \times 1,20,00,000 = \text{₹ } 2,76,00,000$$

**Step 2: Total Direct Plant Cost (25 % OSBL)**

$$C_{TDPC} = 2,76,00,000 \times 1.25 = \text{₹ } 3,45,00,000$$

**Step 3: Fixed Capital Investment (86 % Indirect for solids)**

$$C_{FCI} = 3,45,00,000 \times 1.86 = \text{₹ } 6,41,70,000$$

> **Final Answer:** $C_{ISBL} = \text{₹ } 2.76$ Cr; $C_{TDPC} = \text{₹ } 3.45$ Cr; $C_{FCI} = \text{₹ } 6.42$ Cr.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — How Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the "+1" in the ISBL multiplier** — students write $1.85$ instead of $2.85$ (1 mark deduction each time it occurs).
> 2. **Applying fluid factors to a solids problem** (or vice versa) — *check the question stem carefully*. This is the single biggest error.
> 3. **Adding Working Capital as a percentage of FCI** instead of TCI — this leads to an algebraic loop. Always write $C_{TCI} = C_{FCI} / (1 - \alpha)$.
> 4. **Reporting the final answer without units** (Lakhs / Crores) — at least **1 mark** is reserved for "appropriate units and significant figures".
> 5. **Ignoring the OSBL component** — the question may say "estimate ISBL" or "estimate TCI"; do not skip OSBL/Indirect/WC when asked for TCI.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Peters–Timmerhaus Method — One-Page Summary:**

- **Type:** Preliminary / Order-of-magnitude capital cost estimation (±20–30 %).
- **Base:** Purchased cost of major equipment $E$ (the only "primary" number; everything else is derived).
- **ISBL Multiplier:** **2.85** for fluid plants, **2.30** for solids plants.
- **Hierarchy:** $E \;\rightarrow\; C_{ISBL} \;\rightarrow\; C_{TDPC} \;\rightarrow\; C_{FCI} \;\rightarrow\; C_{TCI}$.
- **Key Components (Direct, fluid):** Installation 39 %, Piping 31 %, Instrumentation 13 %, Insulation 4 %, Electrical 5 %, Buildings 29 %, Yard 9 %, Services 55 %.
- **Key Components (Indirect, fluid):** Engineering 32 %, Construction 34 %, Legal 4 %, Contractor Fee 19 %, Contingency 37 %.
- **OSBL:** Typically **30 – 40 %** of ISBL.
- **Working Capital:** Typically **15 – 20 % of TCI**, *excluded* from FCI.
- **OSBL/Indirect/WC are independent multiplicative layers** — multiply them all to get the final multiplier on $E$.
- **Total Capital Investment Multiplier:** $\approx 5.0 \pm 0.5 \times E$ for a typical grass-roots fluid plant.
- **Limitation:** Averages only — use with caution for highly corrosive, cryogenic, or high-pressure plants where individual factors deviate significantly.
- **Comparison:** More accurate than Lang factor; less accurate than detailed unit-cost (factored) estimate.
- **Examiner's Favourite:** Numericals of the type "Given $E$, estimate ISBL / TDPC / FCI / TCI" appear in **almost every KTU end-semester paper**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Estimation of Revenue
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT757 - ECONOMICS AND MANAGEMENT OF CHEMICAL INDUSTRIES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Estimation of Revenue

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Chemical Process Economics** under the KTU 2024 Scheme, **Estimation of Revenue** refers to the systematic, quantitative procedure of forecasting the gross income (sales turnover) that a proposed chemical manufacturing project is expected to generate over its operating life. Revenue is the primary credit side entry in any **Cash Flow Statement** and serves as the financial foundation upon which **Profitability Analysis** (ROI, NPV, IRR, Payback Period) is built.

Mathematically, the **Annual Gross Revenue (R)** is defined as the aggregate monetary value realized from the sale of the principal product, co-products, and by-products of the chemical plant, evaluated at their respective **selling prices (Sᵢ)** and **production volumes (Qᵢ)**:

$$\text{R} \;=\; \sum_{i=1}^{n} S_{i} \cdot Q_{i}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Revenue estimation is a **Module 2 (Process Economics & Investment Decisions)** core competency. Students must demonstrate the ability to project revenue over the project horizon, incorporate plant capacity utilization, and adjust for working capital tied up in receivables.

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition – Net Revenue vs. Gross Revenue:**
> - **Gross Revenue** = Total value of products sold (before any deductions).
> - **Net Revenue** = Gross Revenue − Sales Tax − Excise Duty − Trade Discounts − Returns.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

Imagine you plan to open a small **handmade-soap manufacturing unit** in Kerala.

You would not just randomly decide how much money you'll make each month. Instead, you would:
1. Estimate how many soaps you can physically produce per day (capacity).
2. Multiply that by the days the factory will actually run (operating days).
3. Multiply by the price at which each soap sells in the market.

That gives you your **estimated revenue**. In a chemical plant (say, an Ammonia or Urea plant), the same logic applies — only the numbers are in **lakhs of tonnes per annum** and **crores of rupees**.

> [!TIP]
> **Intuitive Hook:** Think of revenue as the *"fuel tank"* of a project. Just as a car cannot run without fuel, a project cannot be evaluated for profitability without a reliable revenue estimate. If the fuel estimate is wrong, the entire journey (project) fails.

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants, Standard Metrics & Key Parameters

The following standard industrial parameters govern revenue estimation in chemical projects:

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Industrial Range | Significance |
| :--- | :---: | :--- | :--- |
| Plant Capacity Utilization | CU | **70% – 90%** (Year 1 lower, ramps up) | Fraction of nameplate capacity actually produced |
| Plant On-Stream Factor / Availability | OSF | **90% – 95%** (≈330 days/year) | Mechanical uptime of plant |
| Selling Price (Market) | Sᵢ | Quoted in ₹/tonne or \$/kg | Determined by market dynamics |
| Production Rate | Qᵢ | tonnes/day or kg/hr | Engineering design output |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake:** Many students assume 100% capacity utilization in Year 1. In reality, a chemical plant starts at **~50–70%** of capacity in the first year due to commissioning, operator training, and process stabilization.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Operational Framework of Revenue Estimation

Revenue estimation in a chemical process plant follows a structured, hierarchical methodology. The estimation procedure cascades from engineering design parameters up to financial projections:

### Step 1: Establish Plant Nameplate Capacity
The **design capacity** is the maximum guaranteed output under optimal conditions, derived from material and energy balances.

### Step 2: Apply On-Stream Factor (OSF)
$$\text{Operating Hours per Year} \;=\; 8760 \times \text{OSF}$$

For a typical chemical plant with OSF = 0.91, operating hours ≈ **7970 hours/year**.

### Step 3: Apply Capacity Utilization (CU)
$$\text{Actual Annual Production} \;=\; \text{Nameplate Capacity} \times 8760 \times \text{OSF} \times \text{CU}$$

### Step 4: Multiply by Selling Price
$$\text{Annual Revenue} \;=\; \text{Actual Annual Production} \times \text{Selling Price}$$

### Step 5: Account for Co-products and By-products
For a multi-product plant (e.g., a Chlor-Alkali plant producing NaOH, Cl₂, H₂), all revenue streams are summed.

### Step 6: Project Across Project Life (typically 10–15 years)
Apply **escalation factors** to both price and volume.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Board-Ready)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize the following table** — these formulas appear repeatedly in KTU ESE Module 2 questions.

| # | Concept | Formula | Units | Boundary / Typical Value |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :---: | :--- |
| 1 | Gross Revenue (single product) | $R = S \times Q$ | ₹/year | — |
| 2 | Gross Revenue (multi-product) | $R = \sum S_{i} Q_{i}$ | ₹/year | $i = 1, 2, \dots n$ |
| 3 | Actual Production | $Q = C_{\text{nom}} \times 8760 \times \text{OSF} \times \text{CU}$ | tonnes/year | OSF ≤ 1, CU ≤ 1 |
| 4 | Operating Hours | $t_{\text{op}} = 8760 \times \text{OSF}$ | hours/year | 8760 = total hours |
| 5 | Revenue Escalation (year n) | $R_{n} = R_{0}(1+e)^{n}$ | ₹/year | $e$ = price escalation |
| 6 | Average Revenue (lifetime) | $\bar{R} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{n=1}^{N} R_{n}$ | ₹/year | $N$ = project life |
| 7 | Revenue with Discounting | $PV(R) = \sum_{n=1}^{N}\frac{R_{n}}{(1+i)^{n}}$ | ₹ | $i$ = discount rate |
| 8 | Selling Price (Cost-Plus) | $S = \text{Unit Production Cost} \times (1 + \text{Markup})$ | ₹/tonne | Markup = 10–25% |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Note on Table Symbols:** In any KTU answer script or online exam, write **CU** and **OSF** as dimensionless fractions (e.g., 0.85), not as percentages (85%), to avoid dimensional ambiguity.

---

## 2.3 Why is Revenue Estimation Critical in Engineering?

In chemical engineering economics, revenue estimation bridges the gap between **process design** and **financial viability**:

1. **Profitability Indicators Depend on Revenue:** NPV, IRR, Payback Period — all require accurate revenue projections.
2. **Loan Repayment Schedules:** Banks (the financial source of capital investment) demand realistic revenue forecasts before sanctioning term loans.
3. **Tax Planning:** Income tax is calculated on net profit (Revenue − Costs). An overestimated revenue invites inflated tax liabilities.
4. **Market Strategy:** Forecasted revenue guides decisions on capacity expansion, product mix, and pricing strategy.
5. **Working Capital:** Estimated revenue determines the receivables component of working capital (typically 30–45 days of revenue).

---

## 2.4 Sources of Revenue in a Chemical Plant

A chemical plant typically has **three categories** of revenue-generating streams:

| Category | Definition | Example |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Main Product** | Primary product for which the plant was designed | Urea in a fertilizer plant |
| **Co-product** | Valued product produced in stoichiometric ratio | Ammonium Nitrate from Urea plant |
| **By-product** | Incidental output with market value | Glycerol from Biodiesel plant |

> [!TIP]
> **Valuation Rule for By-products:** By-products are credited at **Net Realizable Value (NRV)** in revenue estimation — i.e., selling price minus the cost of any further processing or separation required.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Annual Revenue for a Single-Product Plant

### Problem Setup
Consider a Sulphuric Acid (H₂SO₄) plant with the following data:
- Nameplate Capacity: $C_{\text{nom}} = 100{,}000$ tonnes/year
- On-Stream Factor: $\text{OSF} = 0.91$
- Capacity Utilization (Year 1): $\text{CU}_{1} = 0.70$, ramping to $\text{CU}_{5} = 0.95$ by Year 5
- Selling Price: $S = \text{₹}15{,}000$/tonne (constant for simplicity)
- Project Life: $N = 10$ years

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1: Convert nameplate capacity to daily production**
$$C_{\text{daily}} = \frac{C_{\text{nom}}}{365} = \frac{100{,}000}{365} \approx 273.97 \text{ tonnes/day}$$

**Step 2: Compute actual operating hours per year**
$$t_{\text{op}} = 8760 \times \text{OSF} = 8760 \times 0.91 = 7971.6 \text{ hours/year}$$

**Step 3: Compute Year 1 actual production (using CU₁ = 0.70)**
$$Q_{1} = C_{\text{nom}} \times \text{OSF} \times \text{CU}_{1}$$

$$Q_{1} = 100{,}000 \times 0.91 \times 0.70 = 63{,}700 \text{ tonnes/year}$$

**Step 4: Compute Year 1 revenue**
$$R_{1} = Q_{1} \times S = 63{,}700 \times 15{,}000$$

$$R_{1} = \text{₹}95{,}55{,}00{,}000 \;=\; \text{₹}95.55 \text{ Crores}$$

**Step 5: Compute Year 5 revenue (CU₅ = 0.95)**
$$Q_{5} = 100{,}000 \times 0.91 \times 0.95 = 86{,}450 \text{ tonnes/year}$$

$$R_{5} = 86{,}450 \times 15{,}000 = \text{₹}129.675 \text{ Crores}$$

**Step 6: Compute revenue ramp-up profile (assuming linear CU increase)**
$$\text{CU}_{n} = 0.70 + 0.05(n-1) \quad \text{for } n = 1 \text{ to } 6$$

$$\text{CU}_{1} = 0.70,\; \text{CU}_{2} = 0.75,\; \text{CU}_{3} = 0.80, \ldots$$

---

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: Multi-Product Chlor-Alkali Plant

A Chlor-Alkali plant produces three products with the following stoichiometric ratio per tonne of NaOH:

| Stream | Quantity (tonnes/yr) | Selling Price (₹/tonne) |
| :--- | :---: | :---: |
| NaOH (Main) | 100,000 | 25,000 |
| Cl₂ (Co-product) | 88,750 | 18,000 |
| H₂ (By-product) | 2,500 | 30,000 |

Apply OSF = 0.92, CU = 0.85 for Year 1.

### Step-by-Step Computation

**Step 1: Actual production of NaOH**
$$Q_{\text{NaOH}} = 100{,}000 \times 0.92 \times 0.85 = 78{,}200 \text{ tonnes/year}$$

**Step 2: Scale co-product and by-product quantities proportionally**
$$Q_{\text{Cl}_{2}} = 78{,}200 \times \frac{88{,}750}{100{,}000} = 69{,}402.5 \text{ tonnes/year}$$

$$Q_{\text{H}_{2}} = 78{,}200 \times \frac{2{,}500}{100{,}000} = 1{,}955 \text{ tonnes/year}$$

**Step 3: Compute individual revenue streams**
$$R_{\text{NaOH}} = 78{,}200 \times 25{,}000 = \text{₹}195.50 \text{ Cr}$$

$$R_{\text{Cl}_{2}} = 69{,}402.5 \times 18{,}000 = \text{₹}124.92 \text{ Cr}$$

$$R_{\text{H}_{2}} = 1{,}955 \times 30{,}000 = \text{₹}5.87 \text{ Cr}$$

**Step 4: Total Annual Revenue**
$$R_{\text{total}} = R_{\text{NaOH}} + R_{\text{Cl}_{2}} + R_{\text{H}_{2}}$$

$$R_{\text{total}} = 195.50 + 124.92 + 5.87 = \text{₹}326.29 \text{ Crores per year}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Revenue Escalation Over Project Life

If the selling price escalates at a constant rate **e** = 5% per annum:

$$\boxed{R_{n} = R_{1} \cdot (1+e)^{n-1}}$$

For the Sulphuric Acid example (R₁ = ₹95.55 Cr, e = 0.05):

| Year (n) | Calculation | Revenue (₹ Crores) |
| :---: | :--- | :---: |
| 1 | $95.55 \times (1.05)^{0}$ | 95.55 |
| 2 | $95.55 \times (1.05)^{1}$ | 100.33 |
| 3 | $95.55 \times (1.05)^{2}$ | 105.34 |
| 5 | $95.55 \times (1.05)^{4}$ | 116.15 |
| 10 | $95.55 \times (1.05)^{9}$ | 148.30 |

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Revenue Estimation Checklist

| Checkpoint | Engineering Input | Financial Output |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Is OSF realistic for the process? | Mechanical reliability data | Operating hours |
| Is CU Year 1 conservative? | Commissioning plan | Conservative revenue |
| Are by-products credited? | Material balance | Additional revenue |
| Is escalation applied to price? | Inflation forecasts | Future revenue |
| Are taxes/duties deducted? | Statutory data | Net revenue |
| Is working capital estimated? | Receivable days | Cash flow timing |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart: Revenue Estimation Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Project Basis] --> B[Define Nameplate Capacity]
    B --> C[Apply On-Stream Factor OSF]
    C --> D[Apply Capacity Utilization CU Year-wise]
    D --> E[Compute Annual Production Volume Q]
    E --> F[Identify Main Product Co-products By-products]
    F --> G[Determine Selling Prices S_i per Tonne]
    G --> H[Compute Individual Revenue Streams S_i × Q_i]
    H --> I[Apply Price Escalation Factor e over N Years]
    I --> J[Deduct Excise Sales Tax Trade Discounts]
    J --> K[Net Annual Revenue R_net]
    K --> L{Is Plant Multi-year?}
    L -- Yes --> M[Build Revenue Profile R_1 to R_N]
    L -- No --> N[Single-Year Revenue Used]
    M --> O[Apply Discount Factor for NPV]
    N --> O
    O --> P[Final Revenue Stream for Cash Flow]
```

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: Revenue Component Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Total Revenue Stream] --> B[Main Product Revenue]
    A --> C[Co-product Revenue]
    A --> D[By-product Revenue]
    B --> B1[Selling Price × Q_main]
    C --> C1[Selling Price × Q_coproduct]
    D --> D1[NRV × Q_byproduct]
    A --> E[Less: Tax Deductions]
    E --> E1[Excise Duty]
    E --> E2[Sales Tax]
    E --> E3[Trade Discounts]
    A --> F[Net Revenue]
```

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Timeline: Capacity Utilization Ramp-Up

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Y1[Year 1: CU = 70 percent] --> Y2[Year 2: CU = 75 percent]
    Y2 --> Y3[Year 3: CU = 82 percent]
    Y3 --> Y4[Year 4: CU = 88 percent]
    Y4 --> Y5[Year 5 to N: CU = 92 to 95 percent]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** The non-linear ramp-up reflects real commissioning behaviour. Students should NOT assume constant CU across all years — this is a frequent KTU board question trap.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define "Gross Revenue" and "Net Revenue" in the context of a chemical industry project. List any two deductions required to obtain Net Revenue from Gross Revenue.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Gross Revenue** is the total monetary value of products sold by a chemical plant during a given period, computed as the sum of (Selling Price × Quantity Sold) for all main products, co-products, and by-products.

**Net Revenue** is the actual revenue retained by the company after statutory and commercial deductions from the Gross Revenue.

**Two mandatory deductions:**
1. **Excise Duty** — Central government tax on manufacture.
2. **Sales Tax / GST** — Tax on sale of goods.

*(Trade discounts and product returns are also acceptable deductions.)*

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**What is meant by "Capacity Utilization" in revenue estimation? Why is the Year 1 capacity utilization assumed lower than the design capacity?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Capacity Utilization (CU)** is the ratio of actual production achieved in a given year to the nameplate (maximum) design capacity of the plant. It is expressed as a dimensionless fraction between 0 and 1.

In **Year 1**, CU is typically lower (50–70%) due to:
1. **Plant commissioning** and stabilization runs.
2. **Operator training** and process familiarization.
3. **Equipment teething issues** and quality ramp-up.

---

## 5.2 Part B — ESE Module Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

### **QUESTION A** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]` — (14 Marks)

A proposed **Ammonia (NH₃) plant** has a nameplate capacity of **150,000 tonnes/year**. The plant is expected to operate at:
- OSF = 0.92
- Year 1: CU = 0.65, Year 2: CU = 0.80, Year 3 to Year 10: CU = 0.92
- Selling price of NH₃: ₹22,000/tonne (Year 1)
- Price escalation: 4% per annum

**(a)** Compute the **Year 1 annual revenue** and **Year 3 annual revenue** of the plant. (7 Marks)

**(b)** Compute the **total cumulative revenue** over the 10-year project life, considering escalation. (7 Marks)

---

#### Model Solution for Question A

**Part (a) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1: Year 1 production volume**
$$Q_{1} = 150{,}000 \times 0.92 \times 0.65 = 89{,}700 \text{ tonnes/year}$$

**Step 2: Year 1 revenue (base price)**
$$R_{1} = 89{,}700 \times 22{,}000 = \text{₹}197.34 \text{ Crores}$$

**Step 3: Year 3 production volume (CU₃ = 0.92)**
$$Q_{3} = 150{,}000 \times 0.92 \times 0.92 = 126{,}960 \text{ tonnes/year}$$

**Step 4: Year 3 selling price (with 2 years of escalation)**
$$S_{3} = 22{,}000 \times (1.04)^{2} = 22{,}000 \times 1.0816 = \text{₹}23{,}795.2/\text{tonne}$$

**Step 5: Year 3 revenue**
$$R_{3} = 126{,}960 \times 23{,}795.2 = \text{₹}302.10 \text{ Crores}$$

---

**Part (b) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1: Year-wise CU table**
| Year | CU | Production (tonnes) | Selling Price (₹/tonne) | Revenue (₹ Crores) |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 1 | 0.65 | 89,700 | 22,000.00 | 197.34 |
| 2 | 0.80 | 110,400 | 22,880.00 | 252.59 |
| 3 | 0.92 | 126,960 | 23,795.20 | 302.10 |
| 4 | 0.92 | 126,960 | 24,747.01 | 314.18 |
| 5 | 0.92 | 126,960 | 25,736.89 | 326.75 |
| 6 | 0.92 | 126,960 | 26,766.36 | 339.82 |
| 7 | 0.92 | 126,960 | 27,837.02 | 353.41 |
| 8 | 0.92 | 126,960 | 28,950.50 | 367.55 |
| 9 | 0.92 | 126,960 | 30,108.52 | 382.25 |
| 10 | 0.92 | 126,960 | 31,312.86 | 397.54 |

**Step 2: Summing all revenues**
$$\sum R_{n} = 197.34 + 252.59 + 302.10 + 314.18 + 326.75 + 339.82 + 353.41 + 367.55 + 382.25 + 397.54$$

$$\sum R_{n} \approx \text{₹}3{,}233.53 \text{ Crores (cumulative over 10 years)}$$

---

### **QUESTION B** `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]` — (14 Marks) — **ALTERNATIVE**

A **Urea Fertilizer Plant** produces Urea (main product) and Ammonium Nitrate (co-product) in a stoichiometric mass ratio of **3.5 : 1**.

- Urea nameplate capacity: 200,000 tonnes/year
- OSF = 0.90, CU (steady state) = 0.88
- Selling price of Urea: ₹18,000/tonne
- Selling price of Ammonium Nitrate: ₹14,000/tonne
- Price escalation: 3% per annum

**(a)** Compute the **annual revenue** from both products at steady state (Year 3). (7 Marks)

**(b)** If the plant operates for **8 years** with CU = 0.70 (Year 1), 0.80 (Year 2), and 0.88 (Years 3–8), compute the **total undiscounted revenue** over 8 years. (7 Marks)

---

#### Model Solution for Question B

**Part (a) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1: Steady-state Urea production**
$$Q_{\text{Urea}} = 200{,}000 \times 0.90 \times 0.88 = 158{,}400 \text{ tonnes/year}$$

**Step 2: Ammonium Nitrate production (mass ratio 3.5:1)**
$$Q_{\text{AN}} = \frac{158{,}400}{3.5} = 45{,}257.14 \text{ tonnes/year}$$

**Step 3: Year 3 escalated selling prices (after 2 years)**
$$S_{\text{Urea},3} = 18{,}000 \times (1.03)^{2} = \text{₹}19{,}090.2/\text{tonne}$$

$$S_{\text{AN},3} = 14{,}000 \times (1.03)^{2} = \text{₹}14{,}847.8/\text{tonne}$$

**Step 4: Year 3 revenues**
$$R_{\text{Urea},3} = 158{,}400 \times 19{,}090.2 = \text{₹}302.39 \text{ Cr}$$

$$R_{\text{AN},3} = 45{,}257.14 \times 14{,}847.8 = \text{₹}67.19 \text{ Cr}$$

**Step 5: Total Year 3 revenue**
$$R_{\text{total},3} = 302.39 + 67.19 = \text{₹}369.58 \text{ Crores}$$

---

**Part (b) — 7 Marks**

**Revenue table with CU and escalation:**

| Year | CU | Urea Q (tonnes) | AN Q (tonnes) | $S_{\text{Urea}}$ (₹) | $S_{\text{AN}}$ (₹) | Revenue (₹ Cr) |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 1 | 0.70 | 126,000 | 36,000 | 18,000 | 14,000 | 277.20 |
| 2 | 0.80 | 144,000 | 41,143 | 18,540 | 14,420 | 326.20 |
| 3 | 0.88 | 158,400 | 45,257 | 19,090.2 | 14,847.8 | 369.58 |
| 4 | 0.88 | 158,400 | 45,257 | 19,662.9 | 15,293.2 | 380.66 |
| 5 | 0.88 | 158,400 | 45,257 | 20,252.8 | 15,752.0 | 392.10 |
| 6 | 0.88 | 158,400 | 45,257 | 20,860.4 | 16,224.6 | 403.86 |
| 7 | 0.88 | 158,400 | 45,257 | 21,486.2 | 16,711.3 | 415.96 |
| 8 | 0.88 | 158,400 | 45,257 | 22,130.8 | 17,212.7 | 428.45 |

**Total 8-year revenue ≈ ₹2,994.01 Crores**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in Revenue Estimation Questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to multiply by BOTH OSF and CU.** Many students write $Q = C_{\text{nom}} \times \text{CU}$ only, missing the OSF. (–2 marks)
> 2. **Assuming CU = 1 for all years.** A plant NEVER runs at 100% from Day 1. (–1 to –2 marks)
> 3. **Forgetting price escalation** in multi-year problems. (–2 marks)
> 4. **Confusing Gross Revenue with Net Revenue.** Deductions for excise/GST are often ignored. (–1 mark)
> 5. **Scaling co-products without re-checking stoichiometry.** Always re-verify mass ratios after applying OSF × CU. (–1 mark)
> 6. **Unit inconsistency** — mixing tonnes/day and tonnes/year without proper conversion. (–1 mark)

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Revision Checklist for KTU Module 2 — Estimation of Revenue:**

- ☐ Revenue = Sum of (Selling Price × Quantity) for all saleable products.
- ☐ Always apply **BOTH** On-Stream Factor (OSF) and Capacity Utilization (CU) when computing production volume.
- ☐ Year 1 CU is conservative (typically 60–70%); steady-state CU is 85–95%.
- ☐ **8760 hours/year** is the universal conversion factor for time.
- ☐ Co-products scale stoichiometrically with the main product.
- ☐ By-products are valued at **Net Realizable Value (NRV)**.
- ☐ Apply **price escalation** for multi-year revenue projections.
- ☐ **Net Revenue** = Gross Revenue − Excise Duty − Sales Tax − Trade Discounts.
- ☐ Revenue is the *primary credit* entry in the cash flow statement.
- ☐ Overestimated revenue → Inflated NPV/IRR → Inaccurate payback period.
- ☐ **Capacity utilization ramp-up** is non-linear in practice — reflect this in tables.
- ☐ A **multi-product plant** has multiple revenue streams — sum them all.
- ☐ Revenue estimation is closely tied to **working capital** (receivables = 30–45 days of sales).
- ☐ For discounted cash flow analysis, **discount each year's revenue** before summing.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Estimation of Product Cost
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT757 - ECONOMICS AND MANAGEMENT OF CHEMICAL INDUSTRIES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Estimation of Product Cost

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Product Cost Estimation** in chemical engineering economics is the systematic, quantitative procedure of predicting the **total manufacturing cost** required to produce a specified quantity of a chemical product over a defined time period (typically one year) at a given production rate, before any profit or selling expense markup is applied.

According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme** outcome mapping for PECHT757, the formal definition is:

> **Product Cost** = The sum of all *direct manufacturing expenses*, *fixed manufacturing expenses*, and *general expenses* incurred to convert raw materials into a finished, saleable product at the plant gate.

It is formally represented as:

$$C_{product} = C_{RM} + C_{L} + C_{U} + C_{M} + C_{D} + C_{T} + C_{I} + C_{OH} + C_{GE}$$

where every term is calculated on a **per-year basis** and converted to a **per-unit cost** by dividing by annual production.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Examiner's Emphasis:** Students frequently confuse *Product Cost* with *Total Capital Investment*. Capital investment is a **one-time upfront expenditure** (paid before the plant starts), whereas product cost is a **recurring annual operating expense**. This distinction carries **2 to 3 marks** in every KTU exam paper.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Tea Stall Economics" Model

Imagine you (the chemical engineer) decide to open a small roadside **tea stall** instead of designing a petrochemical plant. To estimate the **per-cup cost of tea**, you must account for:

| Tea Stall Component | Chemical Plant Equivalent | Nature of Cost |
|---|---|---|
| Milk, tea powder, sugar, water | Raw materials | **Variable** (scales with cups sold) |
| Tea stall helper's salary | Operating labor | **Variable/Semi-variable** |
| Gas cylinder refills | Utilities (steam, electricity, cooling water) | **Variable** |
| Repainting stall, fixing kettle | Maintenance & repairs | **Fixed** |
| Buying the kettle, glasses, stove | Depreciation of equipment | **Fixed** |
| Stall license fees | Property taxes | **Fixed** |
| Stall rent (owner's opportunity cost) | Plant overhead | **Fixed** |
| Your own time managing inventory | General expenses (Admin, R&D, Distribution) | **Fixed** |

> [!NOTE]
> **The Intuition:** Notice how some costs (milk, gas) **grow** when you sell more tea, while others (stall rent, kettle depreciation) **stay constant** no matter how many cups you sell. Product cost estimation is the chemical engineer's rigorous, mathematical version of this everyday bookkeeping — but applied to reactors, distillation columns, and heat exchangers worth crores of rupees.

## 1.3 The Two Major Categories of Capital & Cost

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A chemical plant requires **two distinct pools of money** before it can make even one kilogram of product:

1. **Total Capital Investment (TCI)** — A *one-time* lump sum paid *before* the first drop of product leaves the plant.
   $$TCI = FCI + WC$$
   where $FCI$ = Fixed Capital Investment, $WC$ = Working Capital.
2. **Total Product Cost (Manufacturing Cost)** — A *recurring annual expense* paid *every year* to keep the plant running.

The estimation of product cost is, therefore, the bridge between **design-phase investment decisions** and **operational-phase profitability analysis**.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Hierarchical Decomposition of Product Cost

The KTU 2024 syllabus mandates a **three-tier decomposition** of product cost, following the classic Peters & Timmerhaus textbook framework (which is the de-facto reference style used by KTU examiners):

### **Tier 1 — Direct Manufacturing Costs (Variable / Proportional)**

These costs scale **directly and almost linearly** with production rate.

- **Raw Materials ($C_{RM}$):** Cost of all feedstocks consumed per year. Calculated as:
$$C_{RM} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left( M_i \times P_i \times 1.05 \right)$$
  where the factor **1.05** accounts for a **5% storage/handling loss** (a KTU-favorite assumption).

- **Operating Labor ($C_L$):** Wages of operators directly running the equipment. Obtained from:
$$C_L = (\text{Operators/shift}) \times (\text{Shifts/day}) \times (\text{Days/yr}) \times (\text{Hourly wage})$$
  KTU examiners typically assume **3 shifts/day × 365 days/yr = 1095 operator-shifts/yr/operator**.

- **Utilities ($C_U$):** Steam, electricity, cooling water, process water, refrigeration, fuel, compressed air.
$$C_U = \sum_{j} (Q_j \times C_j)$$
  where $Q_j$ = annual quantity of utility $j$, $C_j$ = unit cost.

- **Maintenance & Repairs ($C_{MR}$):** Typically **2–10%** of Fixed Capital Investment per year (depends on process corrosiveness).

- **Operating Supplies ($C_{OS}$):** Spare parts, lubricants, catalysts — typically **10–20%** of maintenance cost.

- **Laboratory Charges ($C_{Lab}$):** Quality control — typically **10–20%** of operating labor cost.

### **Tier 2 — Fixed Manufacturing Costs (Independent of Production Rate)**

These costs are incurred **even if the plant produces zero output** (assuming it remains on standby).

- **Depreciation ($C_D$):** The *systematic allocation* of the plant's tangible asset cost over its useful life. The KTU-preferred methods are:
  - **Straight-Line Method (SLN):**
$$C_D = \frac{FCI - S_V}{n}$$
  where $S_V$ = salvage value, $n$ = useful life in years (typically **10 years** for chemical plants in KTU problems).

  - **Declining Balance Method (DB):**
$$C_D^{(k)} = \alpha \times (BV)_{k-1}$$
  where $\alpha$ = depreciation fraction (typically $1/n$ for double-declining balance), $BV$ = book value at end of previous year.

  - **Sum-of-Years-Digits Method (SOYD):**
$$C_D^{(k)} = \frac{n - k + 1}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} i} \times (FCI - S_V)$$

- **Property Taxes & Insurance ($C_{T+I}$):** Typically **1–3%** of FCI per year combined.

- **Plant Overhead ($C_{OH}$):** Indirect plant costs (supervision, safety, plant cafeteria, lighting). Typically **50–70%** of $(C_L + C_{MR})$ or **5–10%** of FCI.

### **Tier 3 — General Expenses (Non-Manufacturing / Corporate-Level)**

- **Administration ($C_{Admin}$):** Office salaries, legal, accounting. Typically **15–25%** of $C_L$ or **2–5%** of FCI.
- **Distribution & Selling ($C_{DS}$):** Marketing, shipping. Typically **2–20%** of $C_{RM}$.
- **Research & Development ($C_{R\&D}$):** Typically **~5%** of total sales (not relevant for cost-of-product estimation per KTU convention, but mentioned for completeness).

---

## 2.2 The KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Parameter | Formula | Typical KTU Assumed Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Total Capital Investment | $TCI = FCI + WC$ | — | ₹ or \$ |
| 2 | Working Capital | $WC = 0.10 \text{ to } 0.20 \times TCI$ | $WC \approx 0.15 \times FCI$ | ₹ or \$ |
| 3 | Fixed Capital Investment | $FCI = C_{equip} + C_{installation} + C_{piping} + \dots$ | — | ₹ or \$ |
| 4 | Lang Factor (Conceptual) | $FCI = f_L \times C_{equip}$ | $f_L = 3.10$ (solid), $4.74$ (fluid) | — |
| 5 | Cost Index Update | $C_{present} = C_{past} \times \dfrac{I_{present}}{I_{past}}$ | CEPCI 2019 ≈ **607.5**, 2024 ≈ **800+** | Ratio |
| 6 | Raw Material Cost | $C_{RM} = 1.05 \times \sum M_i P_i$ | 5% loss factor | ₹/yr |
| 7 | Operating Labor | $C_L = N_{op} \times 3 \times 365 \times w$ | 3 shifts, 365 days | ₹/yr |
| 8 | Maintenance & Repairs | $C_{MR} = 0.02 \text{ to } 0.10 \times FCI$ | Avg **6%** | ₹/yr |
| 9 | Operating Supplies | $C_{OS} = 0.10 \text{ to } 0.20 \times C_{MR}$ | Avg **15%** | ₹/yr |
| 10 | Laboratory | $C_{Lab} = 0.10 \text{ to } 0.20 \times C_L$ | Avg **15%** | ₹/yr |
| 11 | Depreciation (SLN) | $C_D = (FCI - S_V)/n$ | $n = 10$ yrs, $S_V = 0$ | ₹/yr |
| 12 | Taxes & Insurance | $C_{T+I} = 0.01 \text{ to } 0.03 \times FCI$ | Avg **2%** | ₹/yr |
| 13 | Plant Overhead | $C_{OH} = 0.50 \text{ to } 0.70 \times (C_L + C_{MR})$ | Avg **60%** | ₹/yr |
| 14 | Administration | $C_{Adm} = 0.15 \text{ to } 0.25 \times C_L$ | Avg **20%** | ₹/yr |
| 15 | Distribution & Selling | $C_{DS} = 0.02 \text{ to } 0.20 \times C_{RM}$ | Avg **10%** | ₹/yr |
| 16 | Total Manufacturing Cost | $C_{Mfg} = \text{Direct} + \text{Fixed}$ | — | ₹/yr |
| 17 | Total Product Cost | $C_{prod} = C_{Mfg} + C_{GE}$ | — | ₹/yr |
| 18 | Unit Product Cost | $c_{unit} = C_{prod}/Q_{annual}$ | — | ₹/kg or ₹/ton |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "60-30-10" Rule of Thumb (KTU Memory Aid):**
> In a typical **fluid-processing** chemical plant, the breakdown of total product cost is approximately:
> - **Raw Materials ≈ 60%**
> - **Utilities + Labor ≈ 30%**
> - **Overheads + Depreciation ≈ 10%**
> For a **solid-processing** plant, the raw material share can rise to **70–80%**.
> Memorize this — KTU examiners award **partial marks** for stating this rule even without full calculation.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

The estimation of product cost is the **single most important deliverable** during the **BFD → PFD → P&ID** design progression in any EPC (Engineering, Procurement, Construction) project. It enables engineers to:

1. **Compare Alternative Processes** — e.g., air oxidation vs. catalytic oxidation of a hydrocarbon.
2. **Justify Capital Investment** — by computing payback period, NPV, IRR.
3. **Set Selling Price** — using *Selling Price = Product Cost × (1 + Markup)*.
4. **Optimize Capacity** — to find the *economic production rate* that minimizes unit cost (the classic U-shaped cost curve).
5. **Perform Sensitivity Analysis** — identifying whether the cost is most sensitive to feedstock price or utility price.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Master Equation — The Generalized Manufacturing Cost Build-Up

The **KTU-recommended master equation** for total annual product cost, as derived from the hierarchical decomposition in Section 2, is:

$$C_{prod} = \underbrace{C_{RM} + C_L + C_U + C_{MR} + C_{OS} + C_{Lab}}_{\text{Direct Manufacturing Cost}} + \underbrace{C_D + C_{T+I} + C_{OH}}_{\text{Fixed Manufacturing Cost}} + \underbrace{C_{Adm} + C_{DS}}_{\text{General Expenses}}$$

This is expanded in full as:

$$C_{prod} = 1.05\sum_{i} M_i P_i + C_L + \sum_j Q_j C_j + (0.02 \text{ to } 0.10)FCI + 0.15 C_{MR} + 0.15 C_L$$
$$+ \frac{FCI - S_V}{n} + 0.02 FCI + 0.60(C_L + C_{MR}) + 0.20 C_L + 0.10 C_{RM}$$

The **per-unit cost** is then:

$$c_{unit} = \frac{C_{prod}}{Q_{annual}} \quad [\text{\₹/kg or \₹/ton}]$$

---

## 3.2 Worked Derivations — The Three KTU High-Yield Derivation Patterns

### **Derivation 1: Cost Index Adjustment (Six-tenths Rule + CEPCI Indexing)**

**Step 1 — The Problem Context:**
A pump cost **₹2,00,000** in 2018 (when CEPCI = 568.3). Estimate the cost of an identical pump in **2024** if CEPCI = 803.4. The pump's capacity has scaled from **Q₁ = 50 m³/hr** to **Q₂ = 100 m³/hr**.

**Step 2 — State the Cost Index Formula:**
$$\frac{C_2}{C_1} = \frac{I_2}{I_1}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the Six-Tenths Rule for Capacity Scaling:**
$$\frac{C_2}{C_1} = \left(\frac{Q_2}{Q_1}\right)^{0.6}$$

**Step 4 — Combine Both Effects:**
$$\frac{C_{2024}}{C_{2018}} = \frac{I_{2024}}{I_{2018}} \times \left(\frac{Q_{2024}}{Q_{2018}}\right)^{0.6}$$

**Step 5 — Substitute Numerical Values:**
$$\frac{C_{2024}}{C_{2018}} = \frac{803.4}{568.3} \times \left(\frac{100}{50}\right)^{0.6}$$

**Step 6 — Evaluate Each Term:**
$$\frac{803.4}{568.3} = 1.4135$$

$$\left(\frac{100}{50}\right)^{0.6} = (2)^{0.6} = e^{0.6 \ln 2} = e^{0.6 \times 0.6931} = e^{0.4159} = 1.5157$$

**Step 7 — Compute the Final Cost:**
$$C_{2024} = 2{,}00{,}000 \times 1.4135 \times 1.5157$$

$$C_{2024} = 2{,}00{,}000 \times 2.1421$$

$$\boxed{C_{2024} = \text{₹}4{,}28{,}420}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Six-Tenths Rule"** is a KTU-favorite heuristic. The exponent **0.6** represents the empirical *capacity economy of scale* — doubling the capacity does *not* double the cost; it only increases cost by a factor of $\approx 1.52$.

---

### **Derivation 2: The Lang Factor Method for FCI Estimation**

**Step 1 — Statement:**
For *fluid-processing* plants (the most common in KTU problems), the Lang Factor is empirically determined to be:
$$f_L = \frac{FCI}{C_{total\ equipment}} = 4.74$$

**Step 2 — Equipment Cost Compilation:**
Suppose a process requires:
- 2 Reactors @ ₹15,00,000 each
- 1 Distillation Column @ ₹40,00,000
- 3 Heat Exchangers @ ₹8,00,000 each
- 5 Pumps @ ₹2,00,000 each
- 1 Compressor @ ₹25,00,000

**Step 3 — Sum the Equipment Cost:**
$$C_{equip} = 2 \times 15{,}00{,}000 + 40{,}00{,}000 + 3 \times 8{,}00{,}000 + 5 \times 2{,}00{,}000 + 25{,}00{,}000$$

$$C_{equip} = 30{,}00{,}000 + 40{,}00{,}000 + 24{,}00{,}000 + 10{,}00{,}000 + 25{,}00{,}000$$

$$C_{equip} = 1{,}29{,}00{,}000 \ \text{(₹ 1.29 Crore)}$$

**Step 4 — Apply Lang Factor for Fluid Process:**
$$FCI = f_L \times C_{equip} = 4.74 \times 1{,}29{,}00{,}000$$

$$\boxed{FCI = \text{₹ }6{,}11{,}46{,}000 \approx \text{₹ }6.11 \text{ Crore}}$$

**Step 5 — Compute Working Capital:**
$$WC = 0.15 \times FCI = 0.15 \times 6.11 = \text{₹ }91.7 \text{ Lakh}$$

**Step 6 — Total Capital Investment:**
$$TCI = FCI + WC = 6.11 + 0.917 \approx \text{₹ }7.03 \text{ Crore}$$

---

### **Derivation 3: Straight-Line Depreciation Schedule**

**Step 1 — Setup:**
$FCI = \text{₹ }6.11 \text{ Crore}$, Salvage Value $S_V = \text{₹ }11 \text{ Lakh}$, Useful Life $n = 10$ years, $S_V$ at end.

**Step 2 — Annual Depreciation:**
$$C_D = \frac{FCI - S_V}{n} = \frac{6{,}11{,}46{,}000 - 11{,}00{,}000}{10}$$

$$C_D = \frac{6{,}00{,}46{,}000}{10} = \text{₹ }60{,}04{,}600 \text{ / year}$$

**Step 3 — Book Value Trajectory (end-of-year values):**

| Year $k$ | Opening BV | Depreciation | Closing BV |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 6,11,46,000 | 60,04,600 | 5,51,41,400 |
| 2 | 5,51,41,400 | 60,04,600 | 4,91,36,800 |
| 3 | 4,91,36,800 | 60,04,600 | 4,31,32,200 |
| ... | ... | ... | ... |
| 10 | 71,04,600 | 60,04,600 | 11,00,000 (= $S_V$) ✓ |

**Step 4 — Cumulative Depreciation Check:**
$$\sum_{k=1}^{10} C_D^{(k)} = 10 \times 60{,}04{,}600 = \text{₹ }6{,}00{,}46{,}000 = FCI - S_V \ \checkmark$$

---

### **Derivation 4: Complete Annual Product Cost Build-Up (Composite Example)**

Assume: Annual production $Q = 10{,}000$ tonnes, Raw material cost = ₹50,000/tonne (with 5% loss), Operating labor = 4 operators × 3 shifts × 365 days × ₹500/day, Utilities = ₹15 Lakh/yr, $FCI = \text{₹ }6.11 \text{ Crore}$.

**Step 1 — Direct Manufacturing Costs:**
$$C_{RM} = 1.05 \times 10{,}000 \times 50{,}000 = \text{₹ }52.5 \text{ Crore}$$

$$C_L = 4 \times 3 \times 365 \times 500 = \text{₹ }21.9 \text{ Lakh}$$

$$C_U = \text{₹ }15.00 \text{ Lakh}$$

$$C_{MR} = 0.06 \times 6.11 = \text{₹ }36.66 \text{ Lakh}$$

$$C_{OS} = 0.15 \times 36.66 = \text{₹ }5.50 \text{ Lakh}$$

$$C_{Lab} = 0.15 \times 21.9 = \text{₹ }3.29 \text{ Lakh}$$

**Step 2 — Fixed Manufacturing Costs:**
$$C_D = 60.05 \text{ Lakh (from Derivation 3)}$$

$$C_{T+I} = 0.02 \times 6.11 = \text{₹ }12.22 \text{ Lakh}$$

$$C_{OH} = 0.60 \times (21.9 + 36.66) = \text{₹ }35.14 \text{ Lakh}$$

**Step 3 — General Expenses:**
$$C_{Adm} = 0.20 \times 21.9 = \text{₹ }4.38 \text{ Lakh}$$

$$C_{DS} = 0.10 \times 5250 = \text{₹ }525.0 \text{ Lakh}$$

**Step 4 — Total Annual Product Cost:**
$$C_{prod} = (5250 + 21.9 + 15 + 36.66 + 5.50 + 3.29) + (60.05 + 12.22 + 35.14) + (4.38 + 525.0) \ \text{ Lakh}$$

$$C_{prod} = 5332.35 + 107.41 + 529.38 = \text{₹ }5969.14 \text{ Lakh} \approx \text{₹ }59.69 \text{ Crore/yr}$$

**Step 5 — Unit Cost:**
$$c_{unit} = \frac{59.69 \text{ Crore}}{10{,}000 \text{ tonnes}} = \text{₹ }59{,}690 / \text{tonne} \approx \text{₹ }59.69 / \text{kg}$$

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Cost Behavior vs. Production Rate

| Cost Element | Variable / Fixed / Semi-Variable | % of FCI (typical) | KTU Hint |
|---|---|---|---|
| Raw Materials | Variable | — | "Scales linearly with Q" |
| Operating Labor | Semi-variable | — | Step-wise increase at shift change |
| Utilities | Variable | — | Proportional to throughput |
| Maintenance | Fixed + Variable | **2–10%** | "Higher for corrosive service" |
| Depreciation | Fixed | — | "Independent of Q (for SLN)" |
| Taxes & Insurance | Fixed | **1–3%** | "Pay even if plant is idle" |
| Plant Overhead | Fixed | **5–10%** | — |
| Administration | Fixed | **2–5%** | "Corporate-level, not plant" |
| Distribution | Variable | **2–20%** | "Scales with sales volume" |

---

## 4.1 Hierarchical Block Diagram of Total Product Cost

    C --> C2[Taxes and Insurance 2 percent FCI]

    D --> D2[Distribution and Selling 10 percent RM]
