<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2023]**
**Distinguish clearly between Fixed Capital Investment (FCI) and Total Product Cost. Why is this distinction critical in chemical plant economics?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | FCI | Total Product Cost |
|---|---|---|
| Nature | One-time, upfront | Recurring, annual |
| Paid to | Equipment vendors, contractors | Suppliers, employees, utilities |
| Recovery | Through depreciation over 10–20 years | Through product sales revenue |
| Magnitude | ₹ Crores | ₹ Crores/year |

**Significance (1 Mark):** This distinction is critical because FCI determines the *capital at risk* and payback period, while product cost determines the *breakeven selling price* and operating profitability. Confusing them leads to erroneous cost-of-product estimates and flawed investment decisions.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]**
**State the Lang Factor method for estimating Fixed Capital Investment. What are the typical Lang Factor values for solid, fluid, and mixed processing plants?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The **Lang Factor method** estimates FCI from the total purchased equipment cost using an empirically-derived multiplier:

$$FCI = f_L \times C_{equip,total}$$

**Typical Lang Factors (1 Mark each):**

| Plant Type | $f_L$ |
|---|---|
| Solid processing | **3.10** |
| Fluid processing | **4.74** |
| Mixed (solid + fluid) | **3.63** |

This method is used during the *preliminary / order-of-magnitude estimate* stage when detailed component-wise FCI breakdown is unavailable.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting the 5% storage loss factor in raw materials** — costs 1 mark every time.
> 2. **Using depreciation as a percentage of FCI instead of computing SLN formula** — examiners specifically test whether you know the depreciation schedule.
> 3. **Reporting "Product Cost" without dividing by annual production** to give *per-unit cost* — this is the **single most common mistake** in KTU answer sheets, costing 2 marks.
> 4. **Mixing up Working Capital % basis** — WC is typically 15% of FCI, **not** 15% of TCI (which would over-constrain).
> 5. **Not clearly separating Direct / Fixed / General expenses** in tabular form — examiners award marks for the **tabular structure**, not just final numbers.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Comprehensive Product Cost Estimation**

**A newly proposed chemical plant is to produce 8,000 tonnes/year of a specialty chemical. The following data are available:**

- Raw material consumption: 1.05 tonnes/tonne of product @ ₹35,000/tonne
- Operating labor: 6 operators per shift, working in 3 shifts, 365 days/year, wage = ₹600/day
- Utilities cost: ₹22,00,000/year
- Fixed Capital Investment: ₹8,50,00,000
- Salvage value: ₹50,00,000
- Plant useful life: 10 years (straight-line depreciation)
- Maintenance & repairs: 6% of FCI/year
- Operating supplies: 15% of maintenance cost
- Laboratory charges: 15% of operating labor cost
- Taxes & insurance: 2% of FCI/year
- Plant overhead: 60% of (Operating Labor + Maintenance)
- Administration: 20% of Operating Labor
- Distribution & selling: 10% of Raw Material cost

**Compute:**
**(a)** The total annual manufacturing cost (7 marks)
**(b)** The total annual product cost and the per-unit product cost in ₹/kg (7 marks)

---

### **Model Solution — Question A**

#### **Part (a): Total Annual Manufacturing Cost (7 Marks)**

**Step 1 — Raw Material Cost ($C_{RM}$):**
$$C_{RM} = 1.05 \times 8{,}000 \times 35{,}000$$
$$C_{RM} = 1.05 \times 28{,}00{,}00{,}000 = \text{₹ }29{,}40{,}00{,}000 / \text{yr}$$

**Step 2 — Operating Labor ($C_L$):**
$$C_L = 6 \times 3 \times 365 \times 600$$
$$C_L = 39{,}42{,}000 / \text{yr}$$

**Step 3 — Utilities ($C_U$):**
$$C_U = \text{₹ }22{,}00{,}000 / \text{yr}$$

**Step 4 — Maintenance & Repairs ($C_{MR}$):**
$$C_{MR} = 0.06 \times 8{,}50{,}00{,}000 = \text{₹ }51{,}00{,}000 / \text{yr}$$

**Step 5 — Operating Supplies ($C_{OS}$):**
$$C_{OS} = 0.15 \times 51{,}00{,}000 = \text{₹ }7{,}65{,}000 / \text{yr}$$

**Step 6 — Laboratory Charges ($C_{Lab}$):**
$$C_{Lab} = 0.15 \times 39{,}42{,}000 = \text{₹ }5{,}91{,}300 / \text{yr}$$

**Step 7 — Direct Manufacturing Cost:**
$$C_{DMC} = 29{,}40{,}00{,}000 + 39{,}42{,}000 + 22{,}00{,}000 + 51{,}00{,}000 + 7{,}65{,}000 + 5{,}91{,}300$$
$$C_{DMC} = \text{₹ }30{,}65{,}98{,}300 / \text{yr}$$

**Step 8 — Depreciation (SLN):**
$$C_D = \frac{FCI - S_V}{n} = \frac{8{,}50{,}00{,}000 - 50{,}00{,}000}{10} = \frac{8{,}00{,}00{,}000}{10}$$
$$C_D = \text{₹ }80{,}00{,}000 / \text{yr}$$

**Step 9 — Taxes & Insurance:**
$$C_{T+I} = 0.02 \times 8{,}50{,}00{,}000 = \text{₹ }17{,}00{,}000 / \text{yr}$$

**Step 10 — Plant Overhead:**
$$C_{OH} = 0.60 \times (C_L + C_{MR}) = 0.60 \times (39{,}42{,}000 + 51{,}00{,}000)$$
$$C_{OH} = 0.60 \times 90{,}42{,}000 = \text{₹ }54{,}25{,}200 / \text{yr}$$

**Step 11 — Fixed Manufacturing Cost:**
$$C_{FMC} = 80{,}00{,}000 + 17{,}00{,}000 + 54{,}25{,}200 = \text{₹ }1{,}51{,}25{,}200 / \text{yr}$$

**Step 12 — Total Annual Manufacturing Cost:**
$$C_{Mfg} = C_{DMC} + C_{FMC} = 30{,}65{,}98{,}300 + 1{,}51{,}25{,}200$$
$$\boxed{C_{Mfg} = \text{₹ }32{,}17{,}23{,}500 / \text{yr}}$$

---

#### **Part (b): Total Product Cost & Per-Unit Cost (7 Marks)**

**Step 13 — Administration Cost:**
$$C_{Adm} = 0.20 \times C_L = 0.20 \times 39{,}42{,}000 = \text{₹ }7{,}88{,}400 / \text{yr}$$

**Step 14 — Distribution & Selling Cost:**
$$C_{DS} = 0.10 \times C_{RM} = 0.10 \times 29{,}40{,}00{,}000 = \text{₹ }2{,}94{,}00{,}000 / \text{yr}$$

**Step 15 — General Expenses:**
$$C_{GE} = 7{,}88{,}400 + 2{,}94{,}00{,}000 = \text{₹ }3{,}01{,}88{,}400 / \text{yr}$$

**Step 16 — Total Annual Product Cost:**
$$C_{prod} = C_{Mfg} + C_{GE} = 32{,}17{,}23{,}500 + 3{,}01{,}88{,}400$$
$$\boxed{C_{prod} = \text{₹ }35{,}19{,}11{,}900 / \text{yr} \approx \text{₹ }35.19 \text{ Crore/yr}}$$

**Step 17 — Per-Unit Product Cost:**

Convert annual production to kg: $8{,}000 \text{ tonnes} = 8 \times 10^6 \text{ kg}$

$$c_{unit} = \frac{35{,}19{,}11{,}900}{8 \times 10^6} = \frac{35.191}{8}$$

$$\boxed{c_{unit} \approx \text{₹ }439.89 / \text{kg} \approx \text{₹ }4.40 \text{ lakh/tonne}}$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Comprehensive Problem**

**A process plant requires the following purchased equipment (all costs in ₹):**

| Equipment | Qty | Unit Cost (2018) | Capacity per unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Reactor | 2 | 25,00,000 | 5 m³ |
| Heat Exchanger | 4 | 12,00,000 | 50 m² |
| Distillation Column | 1 | 60,00,000 | 1000 kg/hr |
| Pump | 6 | 3,50,000 | 20 m³/hr |
| Compressor | 1 | 35,00,000 | 500 Nm³/hr |

The plant is to be **doubled in capacity** (Qₙₑw = 2 × Qₒₗd). The CEPCI for 2018 was **568.3** and the current CEPCI is **803.4**.

**(a)** Estimate the **current Fixed Capital Investment** using the **Lang Factor** (assume fluid processing) and **Six-Tenths Rule** for capacity scaling (7 marks).
**(b)** If the annual production is 12,000 tonnes and operating labor is ₹48 Lakh/yr, compute the **unit product cost in ₹/kg** using the following assumptions:
- Raw materials = 60% of total product cost
- Utilities = 12% of total product cost
- Depreciation (SLN, n=10 yrs, SV=0) = 8% of total product cost
- Remaining 20% = all other costs combined
(7 marks)

---

### **Model Solution — Question B**

#### **Part (a): Current FCI via Lang Factor + Six-Tenths Rule (7 Marks)**

**Step 1 — Compute Original Equipment Cost (2018, base capacity):**
$$C_{equip,old} = (2 \times 25L) + (4 \times 12L) + (1 \times 60L) + (6 \times 3.5L) + (1 \times 35L)$$
$$C_{equip,old} = 50L + 48L + 60L + 21L + 35L = \text{₹ }214 \text{ Lakh}$$

**Step 2 — Apply Six-Tenths Rule + CEPCI Update for Doubled Capacity:**
$$C_{equip,new} = C_{equip,old} \times \frac{I_{current}}{I_{2018}} \times \left(\frac{Q_{new}}{Q_{old}}\right)^{0.6}$$

$$C_{equip,new} = 214L \times \frac{803.4}{568.3} \times (2)^{0.6}$$

$$C_{equip,new} = 214L \times 1.4135 \times 1.5157$$

$$C_{equip,new} = 214L \times 2.1421 = \text{₹ }458.41 \text{ Lakh}$$

**Step 3 — Apply Lang Factor for Fluid Processing ($f_L = 4.74$):**
$$FCI_{new} = 4.74 \times 458.41 = \text{₹ }2172.86 \text{ Lakh}$$

$$\boxed{FCI_{new} \approx \text{₹ }21.73 \text{ Crore}}$$

---

#### **Part (b): Unit Product Cost from Percent Breakdown (7 Marks)**

**Step 1 — Let Total Product Cost per year = $X$ ₹.**

Given:
- Raw materials = 0.60 $X$
- Utilities = 0.12 $X$
- Depreciation = 0.08 $X$
- Others = 0.20 $X$ (which includes $C_L = \text{₹}48$ Lakh as part)

**Step 2 — Compute Depreciation to Back-Calculate $X$:**
$$C_D = 0.08 X = \frac{FCI}{n} = \frac{2172.86}{10} = \text{₹ }217.286 \text{ Lakh}$$

$$\therefore X = \frac{217.286}{0.08} = \text{₹ }2716.08 \text{ Lakh / yr}$$

**Step 3 — Cost Component Verification:**

| Component | % of X | Value (₹ Lakh/yr) |
|---|---|---|
| Raw Materials | 60% | 1629.65 |
| Utilities | 12% | 325.93 |
| Depreciation | 8% | 217.29 |
| Others (incl. labor) | 20% | 543.22 |
| **Total $X$** | **100%** | **2716.08** |

(Note: Operating labor ₹48 Lakh is absorbed within the "Others" 20% category.)

**Step 4 — Unit Product Cost:**
Annual production $Q = 12{,}000 \text{ tonnes} = 12 \times 10^6 \text{ kg}$

$$c_{unit} = \frac{2716.08 \times 10^5}{12 \times 10^6} = \frac{2716.08}{120}$$

$$\boxed{c_{unit} \approx \text{₹ }226.34 / \text{kg} \approx \text{₹ }2.26 \text{ lakh/tonne}}$$

---

## TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Before walking into the KTU exam hall, ensure you can confidently state, derive, and apply each of the following:**

- ✅ **The three-tier decomposition** of product cost: Direct Manufacturing + Fixed Manufacturing + General Expenses.
- ✅ **The 5% raw material loss factor** ($1.05 \times \sum M_i P_i$) is non-negotiable — KTU examiners assume it by default.
- ✅ **Operating labor formula**: $C_L = N_{op} \times \text{shifts/day} \times \text{days/yr} \times \text{daily wage}$. For 24×7 operation, use **3 shifts × 365 days**.
- ✅ **Lang Factors**: Solid = **3.10**, Fluid = **4.74**, Mixed = **3.63** — memorize these verbatim.
- ✅ **Six-Tenths Rule**: $C_2/C_1 = (Q_2/Q_1)^{0.6}$. Combined with CEPCI index for time and capacity scaling.
- ✅ **Depreciation (SLN)**: $C_D = (FCI - S_V)/n$. KTU default: $n = 10$ years, $S_V$ often zero or stated.
- ✅ **Working Capital**: $WC = 0.15 \times FCI$ (typical KTU assumption).
- ✅ **TCI** = FCI + WC.
- ✅ **Per-unit product cost** = Total annual product cost ÷ Annual production in kg (NOT tonnes — conversion trap).
- ✅ **The "60-30-10" rule of thumb**: Raw Materials ≈ 60%, Utilities + Labor ≈ 30%, Overheads ≈ 10% for fluid plants.
- ✅ **Cost Index Update Formula**: $C_{new} = C_{old} \times (I_{new}/I_{old}) \times (Q_{new}/Q_{old})^{0.6}$ — the **single most repeated formula** in KTU Module 2.
- ✅ **Fixed vs. Variable cost behavior** — know which cost components move with production rate.
- ✅ **Maintenance & Repairs range**: 2–10% of FCI (corrosive service → higher end; mild service → lower end).

> **End of Topic: Estimation of Product Cost — Module 2**
> *Aligned with KTU 2024 Scheme, PECHT757 — Economics and Management of Chemical Industries.*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Direct and Indirect Production Cost
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT757 - ECONOMICS AND MANAGEMENT OF CHEMICAL INDUSTRIES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Direct and Indirect Production Cost

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal KTU Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Production Cost** in a chemical plant refers to the total monetary expenditure incurred in converting raw materials into finished products at a specified production rate. It is the sum of **Direct Production Cost (DPC)** and **Indirect Production Cost (IPC)**, often expressed as the **Total Production Cost (TPC)**.

The **Cost of Production** is the cornerstone of chemical engineering economics. Every design decision — reactor volume, heat exchanger area, utility line sizing — is ultimately justified by its impact on the cost of producing a kilogram of product.

Mathematically, the **Gross Annual Cost of Production** is given by:

$$
\text{COG} = \text{DPC} + \text{IPC} + \text{General Expenses (GE)}
$$

Where:
- **DPC** = Direct Production Cost
- **IPC** = Indirect Production Cost  
- **GE** = General Expenses (administrative, distribution, R&D, financing)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Mapping:** This topic falls under **Module 2 – Cost Estimation**, and directly supports the Course Outcome **CO2**: *Estimate the total capital and operating cost of a chemical process plant.*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Bakery Cost Model**
> 
> Imagine you run a bakery producing bread:
> 
> **Direct Costs** are like the **flour, sugar, yeast, and oven gas** — you only buy them *because* you are baking bread today. Stop baking, stop buying. They vary **proportionally** with how much bread you bake.
> 
> **Indirect Costs** are like the **rent of the shop, salary of the security guard, and annual fire insurance** — you pay these even if you don't bake a single loaf. They exist *because the bakery exists*, not because of any specific batch.
> 
> **General Expenses** are like **advertising on TV and the accountant's annual fee** — they keep the business running, but are not tied to the kitchen itself.

In a chemical plant, the **flour** is **raw materials**, the **oven gas** is **utilities**, the **baker** is **operating labor**, the **shop rent** is **depreciation + property tax**, and the **TV ad** is **selling expense**.

## 1.3 Classification Overview

| Cost Type | Also Known As | Variability with Production |
|---|---|---|
| Direct Production Cost (DPC) | Variable / Manufacturing Cost | Proportional to output |
| Indirect Production Cost (IPC) | Fixed / Overhead Cost | Largely **independent** of output |
| General Expenses (GE) | Period Expenses | Fixed |

---

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Definition to Memorize:**
> *"Direct production costs are those expenses that vary in direct proportion to the rate of production and are attributable to the manufacturing operation itself. Indirect production costs are fixed expenses incurred regardless of production rate, arising from the existence and maintenance of the plant infrastructure."*

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Direct Production Cost (DPC) — Item-by-Item Breakdown

The Direct Production Cost has **nine standard components** as per KTU and Peters & Timmerhaus convention. Each must be estimated carefully because errors in DPC compound across the plant life (typically 10–15 years for chemical plants).

### 2.1.1 Raw Materials (CR)

$$
C_{RM} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (Q_i \times P_i)
$$

Where $Q_i$ = annual quantity of raw material $i$ (kg/yr), $P_i$ = unit price (₹/kg).

- Based on **material balance** at design production rate.
- Include stoichiometric excess if the reaction requires it.
- Use **delivered cost** (includes freight, insurance, taxes) — NOT list price.
- Storage life and degradation losses must be considered.

### 2.1.2 Catalysts and Solvents (CCS)

- Often the **most overlooked** line item.
- Catalyst life is given in **kg catalyst / kg product** or in operating hours.
- Solvent losses occur via evaporation, entrainment, and degradation.

$$
\text{Annual Catalyst Cost} = \frac{\text{Annual Production}}{\text{Catalyst Life (kg product/kg cat.)}} \times P_{cat}
$$

### 2.1.3 Utilities (CU) — The "Big Four"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Utilities** are the **second-largest** direct cost in most chemical plants, often **15–25% of DPC**.

| Utility | Source | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Steam** | Boiler | Heating, reboiler duty |
| **Electricity** | Grid / captive power | Pumps, compressors, lighting |
| **Cooling Water** | Cooling tower | Condensers, coolers |
| **Refrigeration** | Chiller unit | Sub-ambient cooling |
| Compressed Air | Compressor | Instrumentation, pneumatic tools |
| Inert Gas (N₂) | ASU / cylinders | Blanketing, purging |
| Process Water | DM/RO plant | Reaction medium, washing |

Formulas (per kg or per ton of product):

$$
\text{Steam cost} = \frac{\text{Steam enthalpy delivered} \times \text{Biomass or fuel cost}}{\eta_{boiler}}
$$

$$
\text{Cooling water cost} = \dot{m}_{cw} \times C_{cw} \times t_{op} \times P_{cw}
$$

Where $\dot{m}_{cw}$ = cooling water circulation rate (kg/h), $t_{op}$ = annual operating hours, $P_{cw}$ = ₹/kg.

### 2.1.4 Operating Labor (COL)

$$
C_{OL} = N_{ops} \times S_{avg} \times t_{op}
$$

- $N_{ops}$ = number of operators per shift
- $S_{avg}$ = average annual salary including benefits
- **Rule of thumb (Peters):** 1 operator per 3–5 equipment items for moderate complexity.

### 2.1.5 Direct Supervisory and Clerical Labor (CSC)

- Typically **15–20% of COL**.
- Foremen, shift supervisors, time-keepers, plant clerks.

### 2.1.6 Maintenance and Repairs (CMR)

$$
C_{MR} = 0.02 \text{ to } 0.10 \times FCI
$$

> **Where FCI = Fixed Capital Investment.**

- 2% for solid-handling, robust plants.
- 10% for highly corrosive, high-pressure processes.

### 2.1.7 Operating Supplies (COS)

$$
C_{OS} = 0.10 \text{ to } 0.20 \times C_{MR}
$$

Includes lubricants, gaskets, charts, cleaning chemicals, gloves, safety gear.

### 2.1.8 Laboratory Charges (CLC)

$$
C_{LC} = 0.10 \text{ to } 0.20 \times C_{OL}
$$

QC/QA testing, in-process sampling, environmental monitoring.

### 2.1.9 Patents and Royalties (CPR)

- License fees, know-how charges, technology transfer fees.
- Often a **fixed annual payment** plus a **royalty per kg produced**.

---

## 2.2 Indirect Production Cost (IPC) — The Fixed Cost Layer

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **IPC = Fixed Costs.** They are paid whether you produce 1 ton or 100,000 tons per year. They exist because the plant exists.

### 2.2.1 Depreciation (CD) — Most Important

Depreciation is the **systematic allocation of the FCI over the useful life** of the asset. KTU expects familiarity with three methods:

**(a) Straight-Line Method (SLM)**

$$
C_D = \frac{FCI - S_v}{n}
$$

Where $S_v$ = salvage value, $n$ = project life (years).

**(b) Declining Balance Method (DBM)**

$$
C_D^{(k)} = F \times (FCI - \sum_{i=1}^{k-1} C_D^{(i)})
$$

For **double-declining balance**, $F = 2/n$.

**(c) Sum-of-Years-Digits (SOYD)**

$$
C_D^{(k)} = \frac{n - k + 1}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} i} \times (FCI - S_v)
$$

The **service life** for depreciation in chemical plants is typically **10 years** (for SLM in tax calculations) or **5–7 years** under accelerated taxation laws.

### 2.2.2 Property Taxes and Insurance (CTI)

$$
C_{TI} = 0.01 \text{ to } 0.04 \times FCI
$$

### 2.2.3 Factory Overhead (CFO)

This is a **catch-all** that includes:

$$
C_{FO} = 0.05 \text{ to } 0.15 \times (C_{OL} + C_{MR} + C_{OS} + C_{TI} + C_D)
$$

Includes: safety equipment, plant medical facilities, cafeteria, plant protection, payroll overhead.

### 2.2.4 General Plant Overhead (CGO)

$$
C_{GO} = 0.05 \text{ to } 0.10 \times C_{OL}
$$

- Accounting, personnel, plant administration.

### 2.2.5 Administrative Expenses (CAE)

$$
C_{AE} = 0.01 \text{ to } 0.08 \times COG
$$

- Corporate office, executive salaries, legal, audit.

### 2.2.6 Distribution and Selling Expenses (CDS)

$$
C_{DS} = 0.02 \text{ to } 0.20 \times COG
$$

- Marketing, sales force, freight-out, warehousing, advertising.

### 2.2.7 Research and Development (CRD)

$$
C_{RD} = 0.01 \text{ to } 0.05 \times COG
$$

- Process improvement, new product development, technical services.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| Symbol | Item | Formula / Range | Type |
|---|---|---|---|
| $C_{RM}$ | Raw Materials | $\sum Q_i P_i$ | Variable |
| $C_{CS}$ | Catalysts & Solvents | Catalyst life-based | Variable |
| $C_U$ | Utilities | Based on material & energy balance | Variable |
| $C_{OL}$ | Operating Labor | $N_{ops} \times S_{avg} \times t_{op}$ | Semi-variable |
| $C_{SC}$ | Supervisory/Clerical | $0.15$ to $0.20 \times C_{OL}$ | Fixed |
| $C_{MR}$ | Maintenance & Repairs | $0.02$ to $0.10 \times FCI$ | Fixed |
| $C_{OS}$ | Operating Supplies | $0.10$ to $0.20 \times C_{MR}$ | Fixed |
| $C_{LC}$ | Laboratory Charges | $0.10$ to $0.20 \times C_{OL}$ | Fixed |
| $C_{PR}$ | Patents & Royalties | Contract-based | Mixed |
| $C_D$ | Depreciation | SLM / DBM / SOYD | Fixed |
| $C_{TI}$ | Taxes & Insurance | $0.01$ to $0.04 \times FCI$ | Fixed |
| $C_{FO}$ | Factory Overhead | $0.05$ to $0.15 \times (C_{OL}+C_{MR}+C_{OS}+C_{TI}+C_D)$ | Fixed |
| $C_{GO}$ | General Plant Overhead | $0.05$ to $0.10 \times C_{OL}$ | Fixed |
| $C_{AE}$ | Administrative Expenses | $0.01$ to $0.08 \times COG$ | Fixed |
| $C_{DS}$ | Distribution & Selling | $0.02$ to $0.20 \times COG$ | Fixed |
| $C_{RD}$ | R&D | $0.01$ to $0.05 \times COG$ | Fixed |

### 2.3.1 Total Production Cost — Master Equation

$$
\boxed{
COG = DPC + IPC + GE
}
$$

Where:

$$
DPC = C_{RM} + C_{CS} + C_U + C_{OL} + C_{SC} + C_{MR} + C_{OS} + C_{LC} + C_{PR}
$$

$$
IPC = C_D + C_{TI} + C_{FO} + C_{GO}
$$

$$
GE = C_{AE} + C_{DS} + C_{RD}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Cost of Goods (COG)** and **Cost of Production (COP)** are used interchangeably in KTU textbooks. Both refer to the **annual total cost** to produce and deliver the product.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

1. **Design Optimization:** The unit production cost ₹/kg is the objective function minimized during process optimization (e.g., optimizing reflux ratio, conversion, recycle ratio).
2. **Break-Even Analysis:** Fixed costs (IPC + GE) determine the break-even production rate.
3. **Pricing Strategy:** A minimum selling price must exceed the unit cost by a margin equal to desired ROI.
4. **Make-or-Buy Decisions:** Comparison of in-house DPC vs. outsourced purchase price.
5. **Plant Debottlenecking:** Identifying which cost component is most sensitive to throughput changes.
6. **Inflation Forecasting:** Each component has a different inflation index (electricity vs. labor), requiring differential escalation.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Unit Cost of Production

The **cost per unit mass of product** is the most reported economic metric.

### 3.1.1 Starting Point

Annual production rate of main product: $P_{rate}$ (kg/yr).

### 3.1.2 Derivation Steps

$$
\text{Unit Cost} = \frac{\text{Total Annual Cost of Production}}{\text{Annual Production}}
$$

$$
\boxed{
c_{unit} = \frac{COG}{P_{rate}} \quad \left[ \frac{\text{₹}}{\text{kg}} \right]
}
$$

If the plant produces **multiple products**, by-products must be **credited** at their market value:

$$
c_{unit,net} = \frac{COG - \sum (\text{By-product revenue})}{P_{rate,main}}
$$

### 3.1.3 Worked Example

A plant produces 50,000 tonnes/yr of sulphuric acid. Annual DPC = ₹60 crore, IPC = ₹20 crore, GE = ₹10 crore. By-product revenue (steam export) = ₹8 crore/yr.

$$
COG_{gross} = 60 + 20 + 10 = 90 \text{ crores}
$$

$$
COG_{net} = 90 - 8 = 82 \text{ crores}
$$

$$
c_{unit,net} = \frac{82 \times 10^7}{50,000 \times 10^3} = \frac{820,000,000}{50,000,000} = 16.4 \text{ ₹/kg}
$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Fixed Capital Investment from Cost Factors

Often the reverse problem: given a Total Production Cost, back-calculate FCI using the percentage relationships.

### 3.2.1 Define

Let $\alpha = 0.06$ (typical average for $C_{MR} + C_{TI} + C_{D} + C_{FO}$ as fraction of FCI).

Then:

$$
C_{MR} + C_{TI} + C_D + C_{FO} = \alpha \cdot FCI
$$

And $C_{FO}$ itself contains a fraction of $C_{OL} + C_{MR} + C_{OS} + C_{TI} + C_D$, leading to a **recursive** relationship.

### 3.2.2 Solve Iteratively

Substituting $C_{OS} = 0.15 \times C_{MR}$, $C_{SC} = 0.18 \times C_{OL}$, etc., and letting $C_{OL} = L$ (a known quantity from staffing), the total fixed cost can be expressed as:

$$
C_{Fixed} = a \cdot L + b \cdot FCI
$$

Where $a$ and $b$ are known lumped coefficients.

For example, with $a = 1.5$ and $b = 0.18$:

$$
C_{Fixed} = 1.5L + 0.18 \, FCI
$$

If $L$ = ₹50 lakh/yr and $C_{Fixed}$ = ₹5 crore/yr:

$$
5 \times 10^7 = 1.5 \times 5 \times 10^6 + 0.18 \times FCI
$$

$$
5 \times 10^7 = 7.5 \times 10^6 + 0.18 \, FCI
$$

$$
0.18 \, FCI = 4.25 \times 10^7
$$

$$
\boxed{FCI = \frac{4.25 \times 10^7}{0.18} = 2.36 \times 10^8 \text{ ₹} = 23.6 \text{ crores}}
$$

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Variable vs. Fixed Cost Sensitivity

| Cost Component | Behavior if Production ↑ 10% | Behavior if Production ↓ 10% |
|---|---|---|
| Raw Materials | ↑ 10% (linear) | ↓ 10% (linear) |
| Utilities | ↑ 8–10% (slight sub-linear) | ↓ 8–10% |
| Operating Labor | Unchanged (step function) | Unchanged |
| Maintenance | Unchanged | Unchanged |
| Depreciation | Unchanged | Unchanged |
| Taxes/Insurance | Unchanged | Unchanged |
| Selling Expense | ↑ 5–8% | ↓ 5–8% |
| **Unit Cost** | **↓ ~3–5%** (economies of scale) | **↑ ~5–8%** (diseconomies) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** Doubling the plant capacity does NOT double unit cost. Fixed costs (IPC) are spread over more units, lowering unit cost. This is the **economy of scale** in chemical plants.

---

## 3.4 Comprehensive Numerical Worked-Out Problem

### Problem
A fertilizer plant has the following data:
- Annual production: 100,000 tonnes/yr of urea.
- Raw material cost (NH₃ + CO₂): ₹3,200/tonne urea.
- Catalyst cost: ₹40/tonne urea.
- Utilities: ₹800/tonne urea.
- Operating labor: 30 operators × 2 shifts × ₹6 lakh/yr each = ₹360 lakh/yr.
- Supervisory: 18% of OL.
- FCI = ₹120 crores.
- Maintenance: 6% of FCI.
- Operating supplies: 15% of maintenance.
- Laboratory: 18% of OL.
- Depreciation (SLM, n = 10, $S_v$ = 0): 10% of FCI.
- Taxes & Insurance: 2% of FCI.
- Factory overhead: 10% of (OL + MR + OS + TI + D).
- General plant overhead: 8% of OL.
- Administrative: 4% of COG.
- Distribution & Selling: 6% of COG.
- R&D: 2% of COG.

Find: **COG, DPC, IPC, GE, and unit cost of urea.**

### Solution (All in ₹ lakhs/yr)

**Step 1: DPC Components**

$$
C_{RM} = 100{,}000 \times 3200 = 32{,}000 \text{ lakh/yr} = 320 \text{ cr}
$$

$$
C_{CS} = 100{,}000 \times 40 = 4{,}00{,}000 \text{ lakh/yr} = 40 \text{ cr}
$$

$$
C_U = 100{,}000 \times 800 = 80 \text{ cr}
$$

$$
C_{OL} = 360 \text{ lakh} = 3.6 \text{ cr}
$$

$$
C_{SC} = 0.18 \times 3.6 = 0.648 \text{ cr}
$$

$$
C_{MR} = 0.06 \times 120 = 7.2 \text{ cr}
$$

$$
C_{OS} = 0.15 \times 7.2 = 1.08 \text{ cr}
$$

$$
C_{LC} = 0.18 \times 3.6 = 0.648 \text{ cr}
$$

$$
C_{PR} = 0 \text{ (assumed nil)}
$$

$$
\boxed{DPC = 320 + 40 + 80 + 3.6 + 0.648 + 7.2 + 1.08 + 0.648 + 0 = 453.176 \text{ cr}}
$$

**Step 2: IPC Components**

$$
C_D = 0.10 \times 120 = 12 \text{ cr}
$$

$$
C_{TI} = 0.02 \times 120 = 2.4 \text{ cr}
$$

$$
C_{FO} = 0.10 \times (3.6 + 7.2 + 1.08 + 2.4 + 12) = 0.10 \times 26.28 = 2.628 \text{ cr}
$$

$$
C_{GO} = 0.08 \times 3.6 = 0.288 \text{ cr}
$$

$$
\boxed{IPC = 12 + 2.4 + 2.628 + 0.288 = 17.316 \text{ cr}}
$$

**Step 3: General Expenses**

GE depends on COG, so we set up an equation:

$$
GE = 0.04 \, COG + 0.06 \, COG + 0.02 \, COG = 0.12 \, COG
$$

**Step 4: Solve for COG**

$$
COG = DPC + IPC + GE = 453.176 + 17.316 + 0.12 \, COG
$$

$$
0.88 \, COG = 470.492
$$

$$
\boxed{COG = 534.65 \text{ cr/yr}}
$$

**Step 5: General Expenses and Unit Cost**

$$
GE = 0.12 \times 534.65 = 64.16 \text{ cr/yr}
$$

$$
\text{Unit cost} = \frac{534.65 \times 10^7}{100{,}000 \times 10^3} = \frac{5.3465 \times 10^9}{10^8} = 53.47 \text{ ₹/kg}
$$

### Verification

| Component | Value (₹ cr) | % of COG |
|---|---|---|
| DPC | 453.18 | 84.76% |
| IPC | 17.32 | 3.24% |
| GE | 64.16 | 12.00% |
| **COG** | **534.65** | **100%** |

✅ DPC dominates (~85%) as expected in a bulk chemicals plant. ✔ Matches the Peters–Timmerhaus rule of thumb.

---

## 3.5 The Percentage-of-COG Pie (Industry Benchmarks)

| Plant Type | DPC % | IPC % | GE % |
|---|---|---|---|
| Bulk chemicals (urea, sulphuric acid) | 80–90% | 3–8% | 8–12% |
| Specialty chemicals (pharma, dyes) | 60–75% | 8–15% | 15–25% |
| Petrochemicals (refinery) | 85–92% | 2–6% | 6–10% |
| Food processing | 75–85% | 5–10% | 10–15% |

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2023]
**Distinguish between Direct Production Cost (DPC) and Indirect Production Cost (IPC) with two examples each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | DPC | IPC |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Costs directly attributable to manufacturing | Costs not directly tied to production rate |
| **Variability** | Varies proportionally with output | Largely independent of output |
| **Examples** | (i) Raw materials, (ii) Utilities, (iii) Operating labor | (i) Depreciation, (ii) Property taxes, (iii) Insurance |

**Marking scheme:** 1 mark for definition distinction + 1 mark for variability + 1 mark for correct examples. **(3 marks)**

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]
**List the components included in the Indirect Production Cost (IPC) of a chemical plant.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The four standard components of IPC are:

1. **Depreciation** ($C_D$): Systematic allocation of FCI over asset life.
2. **Property Taxes and Insurance** ($C_{TI}$): 1–4% of FCI annually.
3. **Factory Overhead** ($C_{FO}$): 5–15% of (OL + MR + OS + TI + D) — covers safety, plant protection, payroll burden.
4. **General Plant Overhead** ($C_{GO}$): 5–10% of operating labor — covers administration at plant level.

**Marking scheme:** 1 mark each for the four items with a one-line explanation. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B: 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – June 2024]

**(a)** Define the term **Cost of Goods (COG)**. List all **nine components of Direct Production Cost (DPC)** with a brief description of each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A chemical plant has Fixed Capital Investment (FCI) of ₹500 crores. Estimate the **Depreciation** using (i) Straight-Line Method and (ii) Double-Declining Balance Method for a project life of **10 years** with a salvage value of ₹50 crores. Also calculate the **Maintenance and Repair cost** at 6% of FCI and **Taxes and Insurance** at 2% of FCI. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Q.A(a) — (7 marks)

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (2 marks):**
> The **Cost of Goods (COG)** or **Total Cost of Production** is the annual monetary expenditure required to manufacture and deliver the finished product, including all manufacturing, overhead, administrative, distribution, and R&D expenses.
> 
> $$\text{COG} = \text{DPC} + \text{IPC} + \text{GE}$$

**Nine DPC Components (5 marks — 0.5 mark per item + 0.05 for clarity):**

1. **Raw Materials ($C_{RM}$):** Annual cost of feedstocks based on material balance × delivered price.
2. **Catalysts and Solvents ($C_{CS}$):** Cost of catalyst makeup and solvent replacement based on lifetime data.
3. **Utilities ($C_U$):** Cost of steam, electricity, cooling water, refrigeration, compressed air, inert gases.
4. **Operating Labor ($C_{OL}$):** Wages of operators directly running the process.
5. **Direct Supervisory and Clerical Labor ($C_{SC}$):** Foremen, shift supervisors, plant clerks — typically 15–20% of $C_{OL}$.
6. **Maintenance and Repairs ($C_{MR}$):** Cost of routine and breakdown maintenance — 2–10% of FCI.
7. **Operating Supplies ($C_{OS}$):** Lubricants, gaskets, safety gear — 10–20% of $C_{MR}$.
8. **Laboratory Charges ($C_{LC}$):** QC/QA and in-process testing — 10–20% of $C_{OL}$.
9. **Patents and Royalties ($C_{PR}$):** License fees, know-how, technology transfer.

---

#### Model Solution to Q.A(b) — (7 marks)

**Given:** FCI = ₹500 cr, $n = 10$ yr, $S_v$ = ₹50 cr, $C_{MR} = 0.06 \times 500$ = ₹30 cr/yr, $C_{TI} = 0.02 \times 500$ = ₹10 cr/yr.

**(i) Straight-Line Method (SLM):**

$$
C_D = \frac{FCI - S_v}{n} = \frac{500 - 50}{10} = \frac{450}{10}
$$

$$
\boxed{C_D = \text{₹45 crores/yr}}
$$

**Depreciable base = FCI − Salvage = ₹450 crores**, spread evenly over 10 years.

**(ii) Double-Declining Balance Method (DDBM):**

$$
F = \frac{2}{n} = \frac{2}{10} = 0.20
$$

| Year $k$ | Book Value at Start (₹ cr) | $C_D^{(k)} = F \times BV$ (₹ cr) |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 500.00 | $0.20 \times 500 = 100.00$ |
| 2 | 400.00 | $0.20 \times 400 = 80.00$ |
| 3 | 320.00 | $0.20 \times 320 = 64.00$ |
| 4 | 256.00 | $0.20 \times 256 = 51.20$ |
| 5 | 204.80 | $0.20 \times 204.80 = 40.96$ |
| 6 | 163.84 | $0.20 \times 163.84 = 32.77$ |
| 7 | 131.07 | $0.20 \times 131.07 = 26.21$ |
| 8 | 104.86 | $0.20 \times 104.86 = 20.97$ |
| 9 | 83.89 | $0.20 \times 83.89 = 16.78$ |
| 10 | 67.11 | $0.20 \times 67.11 = 13.42$ |

**Total depreciation over 10 years under DDBM = ₹446.31 crores** (slightly less than the depreciable base of ₹450 cr due to floor at salvage).

**Year 1 SLM = ₹45 cr/yr; Year 1 DDBM = ₹100 cr/yr.** DDBM front-loads depreciation, providing larger tax shields early in the project life.

**Maintenance and Repair:** $C_{MR} = 0.06 \times 500 = \text{₹30 cr/yr}$. **(1 mark)**
**Taxes and Insurance:** $C_{TI} = 0.02 \times 500 = \text{₹10 cr/yr}$. **(1 mark)**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**(a)** Explain the term **Depreciation**. Describe the **Straight-Line Method (SLM)** and **Sum-of-Years-Digits (SOYD)** methods with suitable formulas. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A pharmaceutical plant produces 5,000 tonnes/yr of a drug intermediate. The annual costs are:
- Raw materials: ₹12,000/tonne
- Utilities: ₹3,500/tonne
- Catalysts: ₹800/tonne
- Operating labor: ₹80 lakh/yr
- Supervisory labor: 18% of OL
- FCI = ₹40 crores; Maintenance: 5% of FCI; Operating supplies: 12% of MR; Laboratory: 20% of OL
- Depreciation: 10% of FCI; Taxes & Insurance: 2% of FCI
- Factory overhead: 8% of (OL + MR + OS + TI + D); General plant overhead: 7% of OL
- Administrative: 5% of COG; Distribution & Selling: 8% of COG; R&D: 3% of COG.

**Compute the total Cost of Goods (COG) and the unit cost of production.** **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Q.B(a) — (7 marks)

> [!NOTE]
> **Depreciation (2 marks):**
> Depreciation is the **systematic allocation of the Fixed Capital Investment (FCI) of a tangible asset over its useful economic life**, representing the loss in value due to wear, tear, obsolescence, or passage of time. It is a **non-cash expense** used for tax and accounting purposes, allowing recovery of capital.

**Straight-Line Method (SLM) — 2.5 marks:**

$$
C_D = \frac{FCI - S_v}{n}
$$

- $FCI$ = Original fixed capital investment.
- $S_v$ = Salvage value at end of life.
- $n$ = Useful life in years.
- **Constant annual depreciation** throughout the asset life.
- Simple, easy to compute, but does not reflect actual wear pattern (most wear happens in early years).

**Sum-of-Years-Digits Method (SOYD) — 2.5 marks:**

$$
C_D^{(k)} = \frac{n - k + 1}{\frac{n(n+1)}{2}} \times (FCI - S_v)
$$

- The fraction $\frac{n - k + 1}{n(n+1)/2}$ decreases linearly with year $k$.
- For $n = 10$, sum of years = $10 + 9 + 8 + \cdots + 1 = 55$.
- Year 1 fraction = 10/55 = 0.1818.
- Year 10 fraction = 1/55 = 0.0182.
- **Accelerated depreciation** — heavier charge in early years, similar in philosophy to DDBM.
- Reflects the empirical observation that assets lose value faster in early years.

---

#### Model Solution to Q.B(b) — (7 marks)

**Step 1: Variable Costs (₹/yr)**

$$
C_{RM} = 5000 \times 12000 = 6 \times 10^7 = \text{₹6.00 cr}
$$

$$
C_U = 5000 \times 3500 = 1.75 \times 10^7 = \text{₹1.75 cr}
$$

$$
C_{CS} = 5000 \times 800 = 4 \times 10^6 = \text{₹0.40 cr}
$$

$$
C_{OL} = 0.80 \text{ cr}
$$

$$
C_{SC} = 0.18 \times 0.80 = 0.144 \text{ cr}
$$

**Step 2: FCI-based costs**

$$
C_{MR} = 0.05 \times 40 = 2.00 \text{ cr}
$$

$$
C_{OS} = 0.12 \times 2.00 = 0.24 \text{ cr}
$$

$$
C_{LC} = 0.20 \times 0.80 = 0.16 \text{ cr}
$$

$$
C_D = 0.10 \times 40 = 4.00 \text{ cr}
$$

$$
C_{TI} = 0.02 \times 40 = 0.80 \text{ cr}
$$

$$
C_{FO} = 0.08 \times (0.80 + 2.00 + 0.24 + 0.80 + 4.00) = 0.08 \times 7.84 = 0.6272 \text{ cr}
$$

$$
C_{GO} = 0.07 \times 0.80 = 0.056 \text{ cr}
$$

**Step 3: DPC and IPC**

$$
DPC = 6.00 + 1.75 + 0.40 + 0.80 + 0.144 + 2.00 + 0.24 + 0.16 + 0 = 11.534 \text{ cr}
$$

$$
IPC = 4.00 + 0.80 + 0.6272 + 0.056 = 5.4832 \text{ cr}
$$

**Step 4: GE expressed as fraction of COG**

$$
GE = (0.05 + 0.08 + 0.03) \times COG = 0.16 \times COG
$$

**Step 5: Solve for COG**

$$
COG = DPC + IPC + GE = 11.534 + 5.4832 + 0.16 \times COG
$$

$$
0.84 \times COG = 17.0172
$$

$$
\boxed{COG = \text{₹20.259 cr/yr}}
$$

**Step 6: GE and Unit Cost**

$$
GE = 0.16 \times 20.259 = 3.2414 \text{ cr}
$$

$$
\text{Unit Cost} = \frac{20.259 \times 10^7}{5000 \times 10^3} = \frac{2.0259 \times 10^8}{5 \times 10^6} = 40.52 \text{ ₹/kg}
$$

| Component | Value (₹ cr) | % of COG |
|---|---|---|
| DPC | 11.534 | 56.9% |
| IPC | 5.483 | 27.1% |
| GE | 3.241 | 16.0% |
| **COG** | **20.259** | **100%** |

✔ Higher IPC% and GE% than bulk chemicals — typical of pharma/specialty plants.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 
> 1. **Forgetting the GE feedback loop** (10–15% of students): When GE is a % of COG, you must solve the **implicit equation** $COG = DPC + IPC + 0.15 \times COG$ explicitly. Writing $GE = 0.15 \times (DPC + IPC)$ is WRONG and will lose 2–3 marks.
> 
> 2. **Confusing depreciation methods**: SLM gives *constant* annual depreciation; SOYD and DDBM give *accelerated* depreciation. Do not compute DDBM like SLM.
> 
> 3. **Confusing DPC with TPC (Total Product Cost)**: TPC also includes packaging, storage, and freight-out, but these are NOT in DPC. Always stick to the standard 9-component list for DPC.
> 
> 4. **Using FCI when you should use WCI (Working Capital Investment)** for some components — KTU problems often mix them. Read carefully whether the cost is "of FCI" or "of capital."
> 
> 5. **Not crediting by-product revenue** in unit-cost problems — costs inflate by 5–20% if missed.
> 
> 6. **Unit conversion errors** in utility costs (e.g., kg/s vs. kg/yr vs. tonnes/day) — convert ALL rates to a common annual basis before multiplying.
> 
> 7. **Forgetting depreciation salvage value**: If $S_v > 0$, the depreciable base is $FCI - S_v$, not $FCI$.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist:**
> 
> ✅ **DPC has 9 components**: CR, CCS, CU, COL, CSC, CMR, COS, CLC, CPR.
> 
> ✅ **IPC has 4 components**: CD, CTI, CFO, CGO.
> 
> ✅ **GE has 3 components**: CAE, CDS, CRD.
> 
> ✅ **COG = DPC + IPC + GE** — the master annual cost equation.
> 
> ✅ **Variable costs** (raw materials, utilities, catalysts) scale linearly with production.
> 
> ✅ **Fixed costs** (depreciation, taxes, insurance, R&D) are independent of production.
> 
> ✅ **Depreciation formulas**:
> - SLM: $C_D = (FCI - S_v)/n$
> - DDBM: $C_D^{(k)} = (2/n) \times BV_{k-1}$
> - SOYD: $C_D^{(k)} = (n-k+1) \times (FCI - S_v) / [n(n+1)/2]$
> 
> ✅ **Key percentages to memorize**:
> - $C_{MR}$: 2–10% of FCI
> - $C_{TI}$: 1–4% of FCI
> - $C_{OS}$: 10–20% of $C_{MR}$
> - $C_{SC}$: 15–20% of $C_{OL}$
> - $C_{LC}$: 10–20% of $C_{OL}$
> - $C_{FO}$: 5–15% of (OL + MR + OS + TI + D)
> - $C_{GO}$: 5–10% of $C_{OL}$
> 
> ✅ **Unit cost** = $COG / \text{Annual Production (in same mass units)}$.
> 
> ✅ **Bulk chemicals**: DPC ≈ 80–90% of COG. **Specialty chemicals**: DPC ≈ 60–75% of COG.
> 
> ✅ **By-products** must be credited at market value to obtain **net unit cost**.
> 
> ✅ **Economy of scale**: As production ↑, unit cost ↓ because fixed costs spread over more units.
> 
> ✅ **When GE is a % of COG**, always solve the **implicit equation** algebraically; do not assume GE is independent.
> 
> ✅ **Operating labor is semi-variable** (step function with throughput) — often modeled as fixed for short-term analysis.
> 
> ✅ **Utility cost estimation** requires completing the material and energy balance first — it is NOT a percentage of FCI.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Variable Production Costs
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECHT757 - ECONOMICS AND MANAGEMENT OF CHEMICAL INDUSTRIES > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Variable Production Costs ## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Variable Production Costs (VPC)** are those manufacturing expenses whose **total magnitude varies directly and proportionately with the volume of production** within the relevant range, while the **variable cost per unit remains approximately constant** over the same operating window. In the context of the **chemical process industries (CPI)**, variable production costs are the costs incurred on inputs that are physically consumed, transformed, or degraded during the act of converting raw feedstocks into finished products. By the **KTU 2024 Scheme** cost-classification framework, these are operational expenditures that disappear the moment the plant shuts down and reappear the moment the plant restarts. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Kitchen Mixer" Model Imagine a chemical plant as a giant industrial mixer making tomato ketchup: - The **reactor vessel**, **agitator motor housing**, **insulation**, and **plant management salaries** are **fixed** — whether you make 10 bottles or 10,000 bottles of ketchup a day, you still need them. - The **tomatoes, sugar, vinegar, salt, glass bottles, the electricity to spin the mixer, and the steam to cook the sauce** are **variable** — you consume them *proportional* to how much ketchup you actually produce. > [!NOTE] > **Intuitive Rule of Thumb:** If shutting down the plant for one week *eliminates* the cost, it is **variable**. If the cost *continues* regardless, it is **fixed**. ### 1.3 Standard Boundary Metrics in Chemical Engineering Economics - **Relevant Range** — the production volume band within which the linear cost behavior assumption holds. - **Unit Cost (₹/kg or ₹/tonne)** — the normalized variable cost per kilogram of product. - **Specific Consumption (kg/kg product)** — the engineering quantity of input consumed per unit of output, e.g., **steam at 2.5 tonnes/tonne of product**. - **Operating Days (typically 330 days/year or 8000 hours/year)** — the standard uptime assumed for cost normalization in KTU problems. ## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Classification of Variable Production Costs in a Chemical Plant Variable costs in chemical manufacturing can be decomposed into four functional buckets: 1. **Raw Material Costs (RM)** — feedstocks, reagents, solvents, catalysts (consumable portion). 2. **Utility Costs (U)** — steam, cooling water, chilled water, process water, electricity, fuel oil, compressed air, inert gases (N₂, Ar). 3. **Direct (Operating) Labor — variable portion** — hourly wages, shift allowances, overtime premiums tied to production tonnage. 4. **Consumables & Miscellaneous** — packaging materials, lubricants, laboratory reagents for in-process QC, royalties tied to production volume. ### 2.2 Cost Behavior Pattern — The Linear Model Within the **relevant range**, the total variable cost function is linear in output quantity $Q$: $$TVC(Q) = v \cdot Q$$ where $v$ is the variable cost per unit (₹/kg). For a chemical plant producing multiple products or using multiple feedstocks, this generalizes to: $$TVC = \sum_{i=1}^{n} P_i \cdot M_i(Q)$$ Here $P_i$ is the unit price of input $i$, and $M_i(Q)$ is the mass consumption of input $i$ as a function of output $Q$. ### 2.3 Why Variable Costs Matter — Engineering Economic Utility - **Pricing decisions** — minimum selling price must at least cover variable cost to avoid negative cash margin. - **Shutdown vs. Continue decision** — if product selling price drops below *average variable cost*, immediate shutdown is economically optimal (since fixed costs become sunk in the short run). - **Break-even and Contribution Margin analysis** — variable cost is the subtractant that creates the contribution toward recovering fixed costs and generating profit. - **Process selection** — comparing the variable cost profile of alternative reaction pathways (e.g., catalytic vs. thermal cracking). ### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula / Relationship | Symbol Legend | Typical KTU Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $TVC = v \cdot Q$ | Total Variable Cost, unit variable cost, output | ₹, ₹/kg, kg | | 2 | $AVC = \dfrac{TVC}{Q} = v$ | Average Variable Cost | ₹/kg | | 3 | $MC = \dfrac{d(TVC)}{dQ}$ | Marginal Cost (slope of TVC curve) | ₹/kg | | 4 | $CM = P - v$ | Contribution Margin per unit (P = selling price) | ₹/kg | | 5 | $CM_{ratio} = \dfrac{P - v}{P}$ | Contribution Margin Ratio (dimensionless) | — | | 6 | $Q_{BE} = \dfrac{FC}{P - v}$ | Break-even Quantity | kg or tonnes | | 7 | $RM_{cost} = \sum P_i \cdot s_i$ | Raw Material Cost ( $s_i$ = specific consumption of input $i$ ) | ₹/kg product | | 8 | $U_{cost} = \sum P_j \cdot u_j$ | Utility Cost ( $u_j$ = specific utility consumption ) | ₹/kg product | | 9 | $VPC_{total} = RM + U + VL + C$ | Total Variable Production Cost | ₹/yr | | 10 | $SR_{shutdown} = P < AVC$ | Shutdown Rule (Short Run) | ₹/kg vs ₹/kg | > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Reality Check:** In a real chemical plant, marginal cost is **not exactly constant**. It curves upward at very high throughput due to *yield degradation*, *fouling*, and *utility bottlenecks*. However, **KTU exam problems universally assume the linear approximation** within the relevant range unless explicitly stated otherwise. ## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation I — Marginal Cost from Total Variable Cost Function **Given:** A chemical plant's total variable cost behaves as: $$TVC(Q) = v \cdot Q$$ where $v$ is constant in the relevant range. **Step 1:** Apply the definition of marginal cost as the rate of change of total cost with respect to output. $$MC \equiv \frac{d(TVC)}{dQ}$$ **Step 2:** Substitute the TVC expression. $$MC = \frac{d(v \cdot Q)}{dQ}$$ **Step 3:** Since $v$ is a constant coefficient, the derivative reduces to: $$MC = v \cdot \frac{d(Q)}{dQ} = v \cdot 1$$ $$\boxed{MC = v = AVC}$$ **Interpretation:** When the variable cost function is strictly linear, the marginal cost equals the average variable cost equals the unit variable cost — all three collapse to a single constant. This is the **linear cost-behavior assumption** that KTU 2024 Scheme problems consistently use. --- ### 3.2 Derivation II — Break-Even Quantity **Given:** Total Revenue $TR = P \cdot Q$, Total Cost $TC = FC + v \cdot Q$, where $FC$ = Fixed Cost. **Step 1:** Set up the break-even condition where profit = 0, i.e., $TR = TC$. $$P \cdot Q_{BE} = FC + v \cdot Q_{BE}$$ **Step 2:** Collect the $Q_{BE}$ terms on the left-hand side. $$P \cdot Q_{BE} - v \cdot Q_{BE} = FC$$ **Step 3:** Factor out $Q_{BE}$. $$Q_{BE} \cdot (P - v) = FC$$ **Step 4:** Solve for $Q_{BE}$ by dividing both sides by $(P - v)$. $$Q_{BE} = \frac{FC}{P - v}$$ $$\boxed{Q_{BE} = \frac{\text{Fixed Cost}}{\text{Contribution Margin per unit}}}$$ **Physical Meaning in a Chemical Plant:** $Q_{BE}$ is the *minimum annual tonnage* the plant must sell just to cover both its fixed costs (salaries, depreciation, insurance) and its variable costs (raw materials, utilities). Anything sold above $Q_{BE}$ contributes to **profit** at the rate $(P - v)$ per kg. --- ### 3.3 Derivation III — Total Variable Cost for a Multi-Component Chemical Process **Given:** A process consumes $n$ raw materials and $m$ utilities. Let: - $P_i$ = market price of raw material $i$ (₹/kg), $i = 1, 2, \dots, n$ - $s_i$ = specific consumption of raw material $i$ (kg/kg product) - $Q_j$ = market price of utility $j$ (₹/kg or ₹/kWh), $j = 1, 2, \dots, m$ - $u_j$ = specific consumption of utility $j$ per kg product **Step 1:** The raw material component of variable cost is: $$RM = \sum_{i=1}^{n} P_i \cdot s_i$$ **Step 2:** The utility component of variable cost is: $$U = \sum_{j=1}^{m} Q_j \cdot u_j$$ **Step 3:** Add variable labor $VL$ (₹/kg product) and consumables $C$ (₹/kg product): $$v = RM + U + VL + C = \sum_{i=1}^{n} P_i \cdot s_i + \sum_{j=1}^{m} Q_j \cdot u_j + VL + C$$ **Step 4:** The total variable cost for annual production $M$ tonnes is: $$TVC_{annual} = v \cdot M$$ ### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style) A sulphuric acid plant has the following data: | Item | Specific Consumption | Unit Price | |---|---|---| | Sulphur feed | 0.34 kg/kg H₂SO₄ | ₹ 30/kg | | Process water | 0.72 kg/kg H₂SO₄ | ₹ 5/kg | | Cooling water | 50 L/kg H₂SO₄ | ₹ 0.50/L | | Steam (low pressure) | 0.20 kg/kg H₂SO₄ | ₹ 4/kg | | Electricity | 0.06 kWh/kg H₂SO₄ | ₹ 7/kWh | | Variable labor + consumables | — | ₹ 0.80/kg H₂SO₄ | **Calculate the total variable production cost per kg of H₂SO₄.** **Solution:** $$\text{RM} = (0.34 \times 30) + (0.72 \times 5) = 10.20 + 3.60 = 13.80 \; \text{₹/kg}$$ $$\text{Utilities} = (50 \times 0.50) + (0.20 \times 4) + (0.06 \times 7) = 25.00 + 0.80 + 0.42 = 26.22 \; \text{₹/kg}$$ $$v = RM + U + VL + C = 13.80 + 26.22 + 0.80 = 40.82 \; \text{₹/kg H}_2\text{SO}_4$$ If annual production $M = 50{,}000$ tonnes: $$TVC_{annual} = 40.82 \times 50{,}000 \times 1000 = ₹ 2{,}041{,}000{,}000 = ₹ 204.1 \; \text{crores/year}$$ ## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block Diagram — Variable Cost Flow Through a Chemical Plant ```mermaid flowchart LR A[RM Storage
Feedstock Inventory] --> B[Reactor Section
Conversion Stage] B --> C[Separation Section
Distillation Extraction] C --> D[Utilities Network
Steam Water Power] D --> B C --> E[Finished Product
Tank Farm] E --> F[Packaging and Dispatch] F --> G[Revenue Realisation
Customer Billing] G -.-> H[Contribution Margin
P minus v] B -.-> I[Variable Cost Stream
RM plus U plus VL plus C] C -.-> I D -.-> I F -.-> I I --> J[Profit and Loss
Statement] H --> J ``` ### 4.2 Cost Classification Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph KTU["Total Production Cost TPC"] A[Fixed Costs FC] B[Variable Costs VPC] end subgraph FC_DETAIL["Fixed Cost Components"] A1[Depreciation] A2[Plant Salary and Wages Permanent] A3[Insurance and Taxes] A4[Maintenance Fixed Allocation] A5[Rent and Overhead] end subgraph VC_DETAIL["Variable Cost Components"] B1[Raw Materials RM] B2[Process Utilities U] B3[Variable Labor VL] B4[Consumables and Packaging C] end A --> A1 A --> A2 A --> A3 A --> A4 A --> A5 B --> B1 B --> B2 B --> B3 B --> B4 B1 --> B1A[Sulphur Feedstock] B1 --> B1B[Catalyst Consumables] B1 --> B1C[Solvents and Reagents] B2 --> B2A[Steam and Boiler Feed Water] B2 --> B2B[Cooling Tower Water] B2 --> B2C[Electricity from Grid] B2 --> B2D[Compressed Air and N2] ``` ### 4.3 Decision Logic — Continue vs. Shutdown (Short-Run Rule) ```mermaid flowchart TD Start([Market Price P for Product]) --> Compare{P versus AVC} Compare -- P greater than AVC --> Continue[Continue Production
Positive Contribution] Compare -- P equals AVC --> Indifferent[Shutdown or Continue
Zero Marginal Benefit] Compare -- P less than AVC --> Shutdown[Immediate Shutdown
Avoid Variable Loss] Continue --> CalcCM[Compute Contribution CM per kg] CalcCM --> Profit[Profit equals CM times Q minus FC] Shutdown --> Loss[Loss equals only FC
Sunk in Short Run] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Diagram Interpretation:** The flowcharts trace how every kilogram of feedstock and every utility joule eventually translates into a rupee figure inside the Profit & Loss statement. In a KTU examination, the examiner expects you to be able to **sketch such a block diagram** and *label each cost stream* with its formula. ## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** **Define variable production costs. Give two examples specific to a chemical process plant.** **Model Answer:** Variable production costs are those manufacturing costs whose **total amount changes in direct proportion** to the volume of production within the relevant range, while the per-unit variable cost remains approximately constant. Examples specific to chemical plants: 1. **Raw materials** — e.g., sulphur feedstock consumed in sulphuric acid manufacture; naphtha feed in a petrochemical cracker. 2. **Process utilities** — e.g., steam consumed in reboilers; cooling water circulated through heat exchangers. > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often wrongly classify **catalyst** entirely as fixed cost. Clarify — the *catalyst inventory charged to the reactor* is a **capital fixed cost** (depreciated), while *make-up catalyst* consumed over time is **variable**. Distinguish clearly. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **Distinguish between average variable cost (AVC) and marginal cost (MC). Under what condition do they become numerically equal?** **Model Answer:** - **AVC** is the total variable cost divided by the quantity produced, $AVC = TVC / Q$. It represents the *average burden* per unit. - **MC** is the additional cost incurred by producing *one more* unit, $MC = d(TVC)/dQ$. It represents the *incremental burden*. - **Equality condition:** When the total variable cost function is **strictly linear** in $Q$ (i.e., $TVC = vQ$ with $v$ constant), then $AVC = MC = v$. --- ### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks each — Internal Choice) #### QUESTION A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024] A chemical plant manufactures **50,000 tonnes/year** of caustic soda (NaOH) by the membrane cell process. The selling price is **₹ 38/kg**. The annual fixed cost is **₹ 18 crores**. The variable cost data per kg of NaOH is tabulated below: | Input | Specific Consumption | Unit Price | |---|---|---| | Brine (as NaCl equivalent) | 1.55 kg | ₹ 4.50/kg | | Electricity | 2.30 kWh | ₹ 6.00/kWh | | Process steam | 0.85 kg | ₹ 3.20/kg | | Cooling water | 28 L | ₹ 0.40/L | | Variable labor + maintenance consumables | — | ₹ 1.10/kg | **(a) [7 Marks]** Calculate the (i) total variable cost per kg of NaOH, (ii) total annual variable cost, and (iii) contribution margin per kg. **(b) [7 Marks]** Determine the (i) annual break-even quantity in tonnes, (ii) annual profit at the current production level of 50,000 tonnes, and (iii) whether the plant should shut down if the market price drops to ₹ 22/kg. --- **Model Solution:** **Part (a):** Step 1 — Raw material component: $$RM = (1.55 \times 4.50) = ₹ 6.975 \text{ per kg}$$ Step 2 — Utility component: $$U = (2.30 \times 6.00) + (0.85 \times 3.20) + (28 \times 0.40) = 13.80 + 2.72 + 11.20 = ₹ 27.72 \text{ per kg}$$ Step 3 — Variable labor and consumables: $$VL + C = ₹ 1.10 \text{ per kg}$$ Step 4 — Total variable cost per kg: $$v = RM + U + VL + C = 6.975 + 27.72 + 1.10 = ₹ 35.795 \text{ per kg}$$ Step 5 — Annual variable cost: $$TVC = v \times M = 35.795 \times 50{,}000 \times 1000 = ₹ 1{,}789.75 \text{ crores/year}$$ Step 6 — Contribution margin per kg: $$CM = P - v = 38 - 35.795 = ₹ 2.205 \text{ per kg}$$ --- **Part (b):** Step 1 — Break-even quantity: $$Q_{BE} = \frac{FC}{CM} = \frac{18 \times 10^7}{2.205 \times 1000} = 81{,}632.65 \text{ tonnes} \approx 81{,}633 \text{ tonnes/year}$$ Since the current production of 50,000 tonnes is *below* the break-even point, the plant is currently operating at a **loss**. Step 2 — Annual profit (or loss) at 50,000 tonnes: $$\text{Profit} = CM \times M - FC = (2.205 \times 50{,}000 \times 1000) - (18 \times 10^7)$$ $$\text{Profit} = ₹ 11.025 \text{ crores} - ₹ 18 \text{ crores} = -₹ 6.975 \text{ crores (Loss)}$$ Step 3 — Shutdown decision at ₹ 22/kg: $$P_{new} = 22 \text{ ₹/kg}, \quad AVC = 35.795 \text{ ₹/kg}$$ Since $P_{new} < AVC$, the short-run shutdown rule dictates **immediate shutdown**. Producing would add to the loss by an amount equal to $(AVC - P_{new}) = 13.795$ ₹/kg. > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Valuation Warning (Q A):** > - **Common Mistake 1 (–2 marks):** Forgetting to convert tonnes to kg when computing total revenue or total cost. Always write down units. > - **Common Mistake 2 (–2 marks):** Reporting the break-even in kg instead of tonnes, or vice-versa. Match units on both sides of the equation. > - **Common Mistake 3 (–3 marks):** Failing to apply the short-run shutdown rule correctly. Many students answer "continue since price > 0". The correct criterion is **$P$ vs. $AVC$**, not $P$ vs. 0. > - **Common Mistake 4 (–2 marks):** Reporting the loss as positive instead of negative. Always sign-check. --- #### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – June 2024] A pharmaceutical company produces **two formulations, P and Q**, from the same multiproduct facility. The joint variable cost data for an operating day is as follows: | Cost Item | Formulation P (₹/kg) | Formulation Q (₹/kg) | |---|---|---| | Active Pharmaceutical Ingredient (API) | 320 | 510 | | Excipients and binders | 80 | 60 | | Solvent (recovered 90%) | 45 | 70 | | Clean-in-place utilities | 25 | 35 | | Packaging (blister + carton) | 40 | 55 | | Direct labor (variable) | 30 | 25 | The selling price is **₹ 700/kg for P** and **₹ 950/kg for Q**. The plant operates **300 days/year** and incurs a **fixed cost of ₹ 6 crores/year**. Daily production capacity is **800 kg of P or 1000 kg of Q or any mix** (linear programming constraint: total kg ≤ 800 weighted by time allocation). **(a) [7 Marks]** Compute the unit contribution margin, contribution margin ratio, and rank the products by profitability per kg. **(b) [7 Marks]** If the company produces **500 kg/day of P** and **600 kg/day of Q** for 300 days, calculate the annual profit and the break-even production mix ratio (assuming mix is maintained). --- **Model Solution:** **Part (a):** Step 1 — Variable cost per kg: - For P: $v_P = 320 + 80 + 45 + 25 + 40 + 30 = ₹ 540/\text{kg}$ - For Q: $v_Q = 510 + 60 + 70 + 35 + 55 + 25 = ₹ 755/\text{kg}$ Step 2 — Contribution margin per kg: - $CM_P = 700 - 540 = ₹ 160/\text{kg}$ - $CM_Q = 950 - 755 = ₹ 195/\text{kg}$ Step 3 — Contribution margin ratio: - $CMR_P = 160 / 700 = 0.2286 = 22.86\%$ - $CMR_Q = 195 / 950 = 0.2053 = 20.53\%$ Step 4 — Profitability ranking per kg: **Q is more profitable per kg (₹ 195 > ₹ 160)**, but **P has a higher margin ratio**. The "best" product depends on whether the constraint is kg of capacity or revenue of capacity. KTU students should comment on both. --- **Part (b):** Step 1 — Annual production: - $M_P = 500 \times 300 = 1{,}50{,}000$ kg/year - $M_Q = 600 \times 300 = 1{,}80{,}000$ kg/year Step 2 — Annual contribution: - $CM_{annual} = (160 \times 1{,}50{,}000) + (195 \times 1{,}80{,}000)$ - $CM_{annual} = 2.40 \text{ cr} + 3.51 \text{ cr} = ₹ 5.91 \text{ crores}$ Step 3 — Annual profit: $$\text{Profit} = CM_{annual} - FC = 5.91 - 6.00 = -₹ 0.09 \text{ crores (Loss)}$$ Step 4 — Break-even condition: $$160 \cdot M_P + 195 \cdot M_Q = 6 \times 10^7$$ With mix ratio $M_P / M_Q = 500 / 600 = 5/6$, let $M_P = 5k$ and $M_Q = 6k$: $$160(5k) + 195(6k) = 6 \times 10^7$$ $$800k + 1170k = 6 \times 10^7$$ $$1970k = 6 \times 10^7$$ $$k = 30{,}457.97$$ So $M_P^{BE} \approx 1{,}52{,}290$ kg/year and $M_Q^{BE} \approx 1{,}82{,}748$ kg/year. > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Valuation Warning (Q B):** > - **Common Mistake 1 (–2 marks):** Using sales revenue instead of contribution margin in the break-even formula. Always subtract variable cost first. > - **Common Mistake 2 (–2 marks):** Ignoring the production mix constraint when computing break-even for multi-product firms. The break-even depends on the *product mix* chosen. > - **Common Mistake 3 (–1 mark):** Reporting a negative profit without explicitly calling it a "loss". Examiners reward precise language. --- ### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Variable Production Costs (KTU 2024 Scheme)** - **Definition:** Total cost varies with output; per-unit cost remains approximately constant within the relevant range. - **Four Buckets:** Raw Materials + Utilities + Variable Labor + Consumables/Packaging. - **Core Formula:** $TVC = v \cdot Q$; hence $AVC = MC = v$ under linear assumption. - **Contribution Margin:** $CM = P - v$; the per-unit revenue available to cover fixed costs and contribute to profit. - **Break-Even:** $Q_{BE} = FC / (P - v)$; the minimum output to avoid loss. - **Short-Run Shutdown Rule:** Shut down if $P < AVC$; continue if $P \geq AVC$ and $P \cdot Q \geq FC$. - **Multi-product extension:** $TVC = \sum_i v_i \cdot Q_i$; break-even is mix-dependent. - **Engineering reality (mention in answers):** Linearity is an *approximation*; in practice, MC curves upward at high throughput due to yield drop, fouling, and utility bottlenecks. - **Always show units** (₹/kg, tonnes/year) — KTU examiners deduct marks for unit mismatch. - **Always specify the relevant range** when applying the linear model.

topics.title: Administration Expenses
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT757 - ECONOMICS AND MANAGEMENT OF CHEMICAL INDUSTRIES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Administration Expenses

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Administration Expenses** (often abbreviated as *G&A — General & Administrative Expenses*) constitute the category of **indirect, non-manufacturing overheads** that are essential for the overall governance, regulation, and strategic management of a chemical processing enterprise. In the context of chemical engineering economics, these expenses form a critical sub-component of the **Fixed Costs** or **General Expenses** in the master **Cost Sheet** of an industrial project.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PECHT757):**
> *Administration expenses are non-manufacturing indirect costs incurred for the management, supervision, and administrative support of the overall enterprise. They are NOT directly assignable to the production of a specific chemical product but are necessary for the legal, financial, and organizational continuity of the plant.*

Unlike *Manufacturing Expenses* (raw materials, operating labor, utilities), administration expenses are incurred **regardless of whether the plant produces one tonne or one thousand tonnes** of chemical product, making them predominantly **fixed in nature**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The "Head Office vs. Factory Floor" Model**

Imagine a major restaurant chain in Kerala — say, a chain of "Sadhya" outlets:

- **The Kitchen** (where the actual food is cooked) is your **Manufacturing/Production Unit**. The rice, vegetables, cooks, gas, vessels → these are *Manufacturing Costs*.
- **The Head Office in Kochi** (where the accountants tally daily sales, the HR manager hires new cooks, the legal team handles FSSAI licenses, the CEO plans expansion to Thrissur) → these are **Administration Expenses**.

Even if every kitchen is closed on a Sunday, the head office still pays:
- The accountant's salary
- The office rent
- The telephone bill
- The CA's audit fees
- The depreciation of the office computer

**Administration Expenses are exactly this — the "head office" cost of running a chemical plant.**

## 1.3 Position in the KTU Cost Sheet Hierarchy

In the standard KTU Chemical Engineering Economics framework, the **Total Product Cost** is structured as:

1. **Direct Manufacturing Costs** (raw materials, operating labor, utilities, etc.)
2. **Indirect Manufacturing Costs** (depreciation, taxes, insurance, plant overheads)
3. **General Expenses** ← *Administration Expenses live here*
   - Administration Expenses
   - Distribution & Selling Expenses
   - Research & Development Expenses
   - Financing (Interest on loans)

> [!TIP]
> **Board Exam Tip:** A student who writes administration expenses under "Manufacturing Cost" loses full marks. Administration is **NEVER** part of manufacturing cost — it sits in the *General Expenses* tier.

## 1.4 Key Distinguishing Metrics

The standard KTU benchmark values for administration expenses as a percentage of **Total Product Cost** or **Total Sales Revenue** in chemical industries are:

- Typically **3% to 8%** of the **Total Product Cost**
- Roughly **2% to 5%** of the **Total Sales Value**

These percentages are explicitly emphasized in KTU valuation keys and must be committed to memory.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Taxonomy of Administration Expenses

Administration expenses in a chemical plant can be broadly classified into **five functional clusters**, each with its own line items. Below is the exhaustive breakdown as per standard KTU-accepted chemical engineering cost estimation literature.

### 2.1.1 Cluster 1: Administrative Salaries & Wages
These are the largest single component and typically dominate administration cost.

- Salary of the **Plant Manager / General Manager**
- Salary of **Departmental Heads** (Production Manager, Maintenance Manager — *administrative share only*)
- Salary of **Accounts & Finance Officers**
- Salary of **HR & Payroll Officers**
- Salary of **Clerical & Stenographic Staff**
- Salary of **Receptionists and Office Boys**

### 2.1.2 Cluster 2: Office Infrastructure Costs

- Office **Rent** (if office is rented; usually owned in large plants)
- Office **Lighting, Air-conditioning, and Utilities** (the non-process share)
- **Stationery**, printing, photocopying, postage stamps
- **Furniture & Fixtures** depreciation
- **Computers and IT infrastructure** depreciation

### 2.1.3 Cluster 3: Legal, Compliance & Professional Fees

- **Audit Fees** (Statutory, Internal, Cost Audit)
- **Legal Consultancy Fees**
- **Company Secretarial Fees**
- **Tax Filing and Compliance Fees** (GST consultant, etc.)
- **Licensing and Renewal Fees** (Pollution Control Board, Factory License, etc.)

### 2.1.4 Cluster 4: Communication & Travel

- **Telephone, Mobile, and Internet Bills** (non-production)
- **Postage, Courier, and Telegram**
- **Travel Allowances** for Directors and Senior Management
- **Board Meeting Expenses**, AGMs, EGMs

### 2.1.5 Cluster 5: Miscellaneous Administrative Overheads

- **Insurance Premiums** (for office buildings, vehicles for managers)
- **Depreciation of Office Vehicles** (cars for directors)
- **Subscriptions and Memberships** (industry associations, technical bodies)
- **Donations and CSR activities** (within regulatory limits)
- **Bank Charges** (non-financing related)

## 2.2 Why Administration Expenses Matter — The "Why" Behind Each Element

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Logic:**

| Why This Expense Exists | Engineering / Business Rationale |
|---|---|
| Administrative Salaries | Without management, no plant can legally or operationally run — strategic decisions need humans. |
| Office Rent/Utilities | The administrative function requires a physical workspace separated from the noisy, hazardous process area. |
| Legal/Audit Fees | Statutory compliance under Indian Companies Act, Factories Act, and Environmental Regulations is non-negotiable. |
| Communication | Modern chemical plants have remote corporate offices — coordination with vendors, regulators, and customers is essential. |
| Insurance | Office buildings, vehicles, and equipment must be protected against fire, theft, and natural disasters. |

## 2.3 The "How" — How Administration Expenses Are Estimated

In a real KTU board exam problem, you will NOT be given every line item. Instead, you will be given a **plant capacity, equipment cost, or number of employees**, and asked to estimate administration expenses using **empirical percentage factors**. The three most common estimation methodologies are:

### Methodology A: Percentage of Total Product Cost

$$\text{Administration Expenses} = (0.03 \text{ to } 0.08) \times \text{Total Product Cost}$$

### Methodology B: Percentage of Operating Labor Cost

$$\text{Administration Expenses} = (0.15 \text{ to } 0.30) \times \text{Operating Labor Cost}$$

### Methodology C: Percentage of Sales Value

$$\text{Administration Expenses} = (0.02 \text{ to } 0.05) \times \text{Annual Sales Value}$$

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master Formula Table for KTU 2024 — Administration Expenses Module**

| # | Formula Description | Mathematical Expression | Typical Range / Units | Used In |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Administration as % of Total Product Cost | $A = f_A \times C_{TP}$ where $f_A \approx 0.05$ | $f_A = 0.03$ to $0.08$ (dimensionless) | Most common in KTU problems |
| 2 | Administration as % of Operating Labor | $A = f_A \times C_{OL}$ where $f_A \approx 0.20$ | $f_A = 0.15$ to $0.30$ (dimensionless) | When labor data is given |
| 3 | Administration as % of Sales | $A = f_A \times S$ where $f_A \approx 0.03$ | $f_A = 0.02$ to $0.05$ (dimensionless) | Profitability analysis module |
| 4 | Total General Expenses | $G = A + D + R + F$ | Currency units (₹ or \$) | Cost sheet preparation |
| 5 | Total Product Cost | $C_{TP} = C_{DM} + C_{IM} + G$ | Currency units (₹ or \$) | Master cost equation |
| 6 | Sales minus Total Cost = Gross Profit | $P_g = S - C_{TP}$ | Currency units (₹ or \$) | Final profitability |
| 7 | Net Profit (after tax) | $P_n = P_g \times (1 - t)$ | $t =$ tax rate | Corporate finance |

> [!TIP]
> **Critical KTU Convention:** Always express $f_A$ (the factor) as a **decimal** (e.g., 0.05) and **never** as a percentage (e.g., 5%) inside the mathematical equation. Marks are deducted for using 5 instead of 0.05.

## 2.5 Real-World Utility in Chemical Engineering

In an actual **Chemical Industry setting**, administration expenses are vital for:

1. **Project Appraisal & DPR Preparation** — Detailed Project Reports submitted to banks and financial institutions MUST include a realistic estimation of administration expenses for the loan to be sanctioned.
2. **Budgetary Control** — Chemical plants prepare annual budgets segregating manufacturing vs. administration costs.
3. **Variance Analysis** — Actual vs. Budgeted administration costs are compared to identify cost overruns.
4. **Transfer Pricing** — For multinational chemical companies (e.g., BPCL Kochi Refinery, Hindustan Unilever), administration cost allocation is critical for inter-company pricing.
5. **Strategic Decision-Making** — Whether to outsource legal/IT work or maintain in-house depends on this cost analysis.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Pitfall:** Students often confuse *Administration Expenses* with *Plant Overhead* or *Indirect Manufacturing Cost*. Plant Overheads (like supervisor's salary in the production area, factory office rent) belong to **Indirect Manufacturing Cost**, NOT to Administration Expenses. Only corporate-office-level expenses qualify as Administration.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 The Master Cost Equation — Step-by-Step Derivation

The position of Administration Expenses in the cost sheet must be derived before any numerical problem is solved. Below is the complete logical chain.

**Step 1: Define Total Capital Investment**

The **Fixed Capital Investment** ($C_{FC}$) and **Working Capital** ($C_{WC}$) together constitute the **Total Capital Investment**.

$$\begin{aligned}
C_{TC} &= C_{FC} + C_{WC}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2: Define Direct Manufacturing Cost**

The **Direct Manufacturing Cost** ($C_{DM}$) includes all costs directly traceable to production.

$$\begin{aligned}
C_{DM} &= C_{RM} + C_{OL} + C_{U} + C_{M\&R} + C_{Misc}
\end{aligned}$$

where:

- $C_{RM}$ = Cost of Raw Materials
- $C_{OL}$ = Cost of Operating Labor (direct wages of plant operators)
- $C_{U}$ = Cost of Utilities (steam, electricity, cooling water, refrigeration)
- $C_{M\&R}$ = Cost of Maintenance and Repairs
- $C_{Misc}$ = Miscellaneous direct supplies (lubricants, catalysts, etc.)

**Step 3: Define Indirect Manufacturing Cost**

$$\begin{aligned}
C_{IM} &= C_{Dep} + C_{Tax} + C_{Ins} + C_{PO}
\end{aligned}$$

where:

- $C_{Dep}$ = Depreciation of plant and equipment
- $C_{Tax}$ = Local property taxes and factory licenses
- $C_{Ins}$ = Insurance of plant, machinery, and buildings
- $C_{PO}$ = Plant Overhead (factory office, safety, security, plant-level supervision)

**Step 4: Define General Expenses**

$$\begin{aligned}
G &= A + C_{DS} + C_{R\&D} + C_{Fin}
\end{aligned}$$

where:

- $A$ = **Administration Expenses** (our topic)
- $C_{DS}$ = Distribution & Selling Expenses
- $C_{R\&D}$ = Research & Development Expenses
- $C_{Fin}$ = Financing Costs (interest on loans)

**Step 5: Total Manufacturing Cost**

$$\begin{aligned}
C_{TM} &= C_{DM} + C_{IM}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 6: Total Product Cost (Master Equation)**

$$\begin{aligned}
C_{TP} &= C_{TM} + G \\
C_{TP} &= C_{DM} + C_{IM} + A + C_{DS} + C_{R\&D} + C_{Fin}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 7: Profitability**

$$\begin{aligned}
S &= \text{Annual Sales Revenue} \\
P_g &= S - C_{TP} \\
P_n &= P_g - \text{Tax} - \text{Dividends}
\end{aligned}$$

## 3.2 Fully Worked Example — KTU Standard Problem

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Example 1: Estimating Administration Expenses Using the Percentage Method**

**Problem Statement (KTU-Style):**
A chemical plant producing **caustic soda** has the following annual cost data. The plant manager wants to estimate the **annual administration expenses** and prepare a complete cost sheet.

**Given Data:**
- Cost of Raw Materials: ₹ 75,00,000 per year
- Operating Labor Cost: ₹ 12,00,000 per year
- Utilities Cost: ₹ 18,00,000 per year
- Maintenance & Repairs: ₹ 6,00,000 per year
- Depreciation: ₹ 9,00,000 per year
- Local Taxes & Insurance: ₹ 4,50,000 per year
- Plant Overheads: ₹ 5,50,000 per year
- Distribution & Selling Expenses: ₹ 8,00,000 per year
- Research & Development: ₹ 3,00,000 per year
- Financing Cost (Interest): ₹ 6,00,000 per year
- Annual Sales Revenue: ₹ 2,20,00,000 per year

**Step 1: Calculate Direct Manufacturing Cost ($C_{DM}$)**

$$\begin{aligned}
C_{DM} &= C_{RM} + C_{OL} + C_{U} + C_{M\&R} \\
&= 75{,}00{,}000 + 12{,}00{,}000 + 18{,}00{,}000 + 6{,}00{,}000 \\
&= 1{,}11{,}00{,}000 \text{ ₹/year}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2: Calculate Indirect Manufacturing Cost ($C_{IM}$)**

$$\begin{aligned}
C_{IM} &= C_{Dep} + C_{Tax\&Ins} + C_{PO} \\
&= 9{,}00{,}000 + 4{,}50{,}000 + 5{,}50{,}000 \\
&= 19{,}00{,}000 \text{ ₹/year}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3: Calculate Total Manufacturing Cost ($C_{TM}$)**

$$\begin{aligned}
C_{TM} &= C_{DM} + C_{IM} \\
&= 1{,}11{,}00{,}000 + 19{,}00{,}000 \\
&= 1{,}30{,}00{,}000 \text{ ₹/year}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4: Estimate Administration Expenses (Using 5% of Total Product Cost — assumed iterative)**

Using the empirical factor $f_A = 0.05$ of **Total Product Cost** ($C_{TP}$):

$$\begin{aligned}
A &= 0.05 \times C_{TP}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5: Calculate Total Product Cost ($C_{TP}$) in terms of $A$**

Let us first sum all the *known* components:

$$\begin{aligned}
C_{TM} &= 1{,}30{,}00{,}000 \\
C_{DS} &= 8{,}00{,}000 \\
C_{R\&D} &= 3{,}00{,}000 \\
C_{Fin} &= 6{,}00{,}000 \\
A &= ? \text{ (unknown)}
\end{aligned}$$

Therefore:

$$\begin{aligned}
C_{TP} &= C_{TM} + C_{DS} + C_{R\&D} + C_{Fin} + A \\
C_{TP} &= 1{,}30{,}00{,}000 + 8{,}00{,}000 + 3{,}00{,}000 + 6{,}00{,}000 + A \\
C_{TP} &= 1{,}47{,}00{,}000 + A
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 6: Substitute into the Administration Expense Formula**

$$\begin{aligned}
A &= 0.05 \times C_{TP} \\
A &= 0.05 \times (1{,}47{,}00{,}000 + A) \\
A &= 7{,}35{,}000 + 0.05 A \\
A - 0.05 A &= 7{,}35{,}000 \\
0.95 A &= 7{,}35{,}000 \\
A &= \frac{7{,}35{,}000}{0.95} \\
A &= 7{,}73{,}684.21 \text{ ₹/year}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 7: Back-Calculate the Total Product Cost**

$$\begin{aligned}
C_{TP} &= 1{,}47{,}00{,}000 + 7{,}73{,}684.21 \\
C_{TP} &= 1{,}54{,}73{,}684.21 \text{ ₹/year}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 8: Verify Administration as a Percentage**

$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{A}{C_{TP}} \times 100 &= \frac{7{,}73{,}684.21}{1{,}54{,}73{,}684.21} \times 100 \\
&= 5.00 \% \checkmark
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 9: Calculate Gross Profit**

$$\begin{aligned}
P_g &= S - C_{TP} \\
&= 2{,}20{,}00{,}000 - 1{,}54{,}73{,}684.21 \\
&= 65{,}26{,}315.79 \text{ ₹/year}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 10: Net Profit (Assuming 30% Tax)**

$$\begin{aligned}
P_n &= P_g \times (1 - 0.30) \\
&= 65{,}26{,}315.79 \times 0.70 \\
&= 45{,}68{,}421.05 \text{ ₹/year}
\end{aligned}$$

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — The Decision Tree

| If the Problem Gives You... | Use This Formula | Typical Factor $f_A$ |
|---|---|---|
| Total Product Cost (with $A$ as unknown) | Iterative: $A = f_A \times C_{TP}$ | $0.05$ |
| Operating Labor Cost only | Direct: $A = f_A \times C_{OL}$ | $0.20$ |
| Annual Sales only | Direct: $A = f_A \times S$ | $0.03$ |
| Number of administrative employees | Direct: $A = N_{admin} \times (\text{avg salary} + \text{per capita overhead})$ | Variable |
| Explicit line-item data (salaries + rent + fees...) | Direct sum: $A = \sum_i A_i$ | N/A |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Pattern Recognition:** In 90% of KTU board problems, the iterative method using $f_A = 0.05$ of Total Product Cost is tested. Always solve it algebraically, not by trial and error.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Cost Sheet Hierarchy

The diagram below illustrates the structural position of Administration Expenses within the complete KTU-2024 cost sheet architecture. This is the most commonly tested diagrammatic representation.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Total Capital Investment] --> A1[Fixed Capital Investment]
    A0 --> A2[Working Capital]
    B0[Total Product Cost C_TP] --> B1[Direct Manufacturing Cost C_DM]
    B0 --> B2[Indirect Manufacturing Cost C_IM]
    B0 --> B3[General Expenses G]
    B1 --> B1a[Raw Materials C_RM]
    B1 --> B1b[Operating Labor C_OL]
    B1 --> B1c[Utilities C_U]
    B1 --> B1d[Maintenance and Repairs C_MR]
    B2 --> B2a[Depreciation C_Dep]
    B2 --> B2b[Taxes and Insurance C_TI]
    B2 --> B2c[Plant Overhead C_PO]
    B3 --> B3a[Administration Expenses A]
    B3 --> B3b[Distribution and Selling C_DS]
    B3 --> B3c[Research and Development C_RD]
    B3 --> B3d[Financing Cost C_Fin]
    C0[Annual Sales S] --> D0[Gross Profit P_g]
    B0 --> D0
    D0 --> E0[Net Profit P_n after Tax]
```

## 4.2 Administration Expenses — Internal Component Breakdown

The following block diagram maps the **internal hierarchy** of Administration Expenses itself, showing how the major clusters cascade into specific line items.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    AD0[Administration Expenses A] --> AD1[Cluster 1 Admin Salaries]
    AD0 --> AD2[Cluster 2 Office Infrastructure]
    AD0 --> AD3[Cluster 3 Legal and Professional Fees]
    AD0 --> AD4[Cluster 4 Communication and Travel]
    AD0 --> AD5[Cluster 5 Miscellaneous Overheads]
    AD1 --> AD1a[Plant Manager Salary]
    AD1 --> AD1b[Departmental Heads]
    AD1 --> AD1c[Accounts and Finance Officers]
    AD1 --> AD1d[Clerical and HR Staff]
    AD2 --> AD2a[Office Rent]
    AD2 --> AD2b[Stationery and Printing]
    AD2 --> AD2c[Furniture and Computer Depreciation]
    AD2 --> AD2d[Office Utilities]
    AD3 --> AD3a[Statutory Audit Fees]
    AD3 --> AD3b[Legal Consultancy]
    AD3 --> AD3c[Tax Filing Fees]
    AD3 --> AD3d[Licensing and Renewals]
    AD4 --> AD4a[Telephone and Internet]
    AD4 --> AD4b[Postage and Courier]
    AD4 --> AD4c[Travel Allowances]
    AD4 --> AD4d[Board Meeting Costs]
    AD5 --> AD5a[Insurance Premiums]
    AD5 --> AD5b[CSR and Donations]
    AD5 --> AD5c[Subscriptions]
    AD5 --> AD5d[Bank Charges]
```
