# 🎓 START-UP COSTS — Section 5: KTU 2024 Examination Question Bank & Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define start-up costs. List the four major components of start-up cost with one example of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> Start-up costs are the non-recurring transitional expenses and revenue losses incurred between mechanical completion of a new chemical plant and achievement of design capacity and product quality.
>
> **Four major components:**
> 1. **Pre-start-up cost ($S_{pre}$)** — e.g., operator training before first product run.
> 2. **Initial operating cost ($S_{oper}$)** — e.g., off-spec product sold at discount.
> 3. **Modification cost ($S_{mod}$)** — e.g., re-rating a heat exchanger after hot commissioning.
> 4. **Lost revenue cost ($S_{rev}$)** — e.g., lost production during the ramp-up month.

*(1 mark for definition, 2 marks for the four components with valid examples.)*

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`
**Why is start-up cost usually expressed as a percentage of Fixed Capital Investment (FCI)? State the typical % range for a complex petrochemical plant.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> Expressing start-up cost as a % of FCI provides a **scale-independent heuristic** for feasibility studies when detailed engineering data is unavailable. Since FCI scales roughly with plant capacity, start-up cost also scales with it, making the ratio approximately constant for similar process technologies.
>
> Typical ranges (as per KTU 2024 reference values):
> - **Conventional chemicals (sulfuric acid, chlor-alkali):** 5–8% of FCI.
> - **Bulk petrochemicals (ethylene, ammonia, methanol):** 8–15% of FCI.
> - **Complex / first-of-its-kind plants (LNG, novel biorefineries):** 20–30% of FCI.

*(1 mark for the rationale of % expression, 2 marks for ranges with examples.)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do NOT lump start-up cost into FCI** — it is a working-capital / off-design item.
> 2. **Always distinguish nominal $S_{total}$ from PV-adjusted $PV_{su}$.** A common error is to skip the time-value-of-money step.
> 3. **Show the ramp profile (linear, logistic, or step)** explicitly. Defaulting to "100% production lost for 6 months" is a *gross oversimplification* and costs 2–3 marks.
> 4. **Always state units in every numerical step** (₹ crore, TPD, etc.). KTU examiners deduct 0.5 marks per missing unit.

---

### 📌 Question A — `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 2]`

**A new urea plant has the following data:**
- Fixed Capital Investment (FCI) = ₹800 crore
- Working Capital (normal) = ₹60 crore
- Design capacity = 2000 TPD urea
- Sale price = ₹25,000 per tonne
- Variable cost = ₹15,000 per tonne
- Start-up duration = 9 months
- Discount rate = 10% per annum
- Pre-start-up cost = 5% of FCI
- Initial operating loss = 3% of FCI
- Modification cost = 2% of FCI
- Production ramp: linear from 0% to 100% over 9 months

**Compute:**
**(a)** The total start-up cost (nominal) and its % of FCI. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** The present-value-equivalent start-up burden, taking the lost-revenue component as uniformly distributed over the 9-month period. **(7 marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Question A

**Part (a) — Total start-up cost (nominal):**

Categorical (non-revenue) start-up costs:

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{pre} &= 0.05 \times 800 = ₹40 \,\text{crore} \\
S_{oper} &= 0.03 \times 800 = ₹24 \,\text{crore} \\
S_{mod} &= 0.02 \times 800 = ₹16 \,\text{crore} \\
S_{nonrev} &= 40 + 24 + 16 = ₹80 \,\text{crore}
\end{aligned}
$$

Contribution margin: $M = 25{,}000 - 15{,}000 = ₹10{,}000$/T.

Under-production at time $t$ (days) for a 9-month linear ramp (270 days):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta P(t) = 2000 \cdot \left(1 - \dfrac{t}{270}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

Lost revenue over the 270-day period:

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{rev} &= \int_{0}^{270} 2000 \cdot \left(1 - \dfrac{t}{270}\right) \cdot 10{,}000 \, dt \\
&= 2 \times 10^{7} \cdot \left[ t - \dfrac{t^2}{540} \right]_{0}^{270} \\
&= 2 \times 10^{7} \cdot \left(270 - 135\right) \\
&= 2 \times 10^{7} \cdot 135 \\
&= ₹270 \,\text{crore}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Total start-up cost:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{total} &= 80 + 270 = ₹350 \,\text{crore}
\end{aligned}
$$

**As % of FCI:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\% S = \dfrac{350}{800} \times 100 = 43.75\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> *(7 marks: 2 for categories, 3 for lost-revenue integration, 1 for summation, 1 for % calculation.)*

---

**Part (b) — Present-value-equivalent:**

Assume uniform outflow over $T_{su} = 0.75$ years. Apply the annuity-PV formula derived in Section 3.1:

$$
\begin{aligned}
PV_{su} &= \dfrac{S_{total}}{T_{su} \cdot i} \cdot \left(1 - e^{-i T_{su}}\right) \\
&= \dfrac{350}{0.75 \times 0.10} \cdot \left(1 - e^{-0.075}\right) \\
&= \dfrac{350}{0.075} \cdot \left(1 - 0.9277\right) \\
&= 4666.67 \cdot 0.0723 \\
&\approx ₹337.4 \,\text{crore}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Interpretation:** The PV-adjusted start-up burden is **₹337.4 crore** — about 3.6% lower than the nominal ₹350 crore because of the discounting effect.

> *(7 marks: 1 for setup, 3 for substitution, 1 for arithmetic, 1 for interpretation, 1 for units.)*

---

### 📌 Question B — `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 2, Alternative Choice]`

**A new ethylene cracker plant has the following data:**
- FCI = ₹1500 crore
- Design capacity = 500,000 TPA ethylene
- Sale price = ₹70,000 per tonne
- Variable cost = ₹40,000 per tonne
- Start-up duration = 12 months
- Discount rate = 12% per annum
- Pre-start-up cost = 4% of FCI; Operating loss = 3% of FCI; Modification cost = 2% of FCI
- Production ramp: logistic, $P(t) = P_{design} / (1 + e^{-1.2(t - 0.5)})$ where $t$ is in years and $t_0 = 0.5$ yr

**Compute:**
**(a)** The total nominal start-up cost using the logistic ramp for lost revenue. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** The first-year effective production (in TPA) and the percentage revenue loss in Year 1 due to start-up. **(7 marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Question B

**Part (a) — Total start-up cost (logistic ramp):**

Categorical costs:

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{pre} &= 0.04 \times 1500 = ₹60 \,\text{crore} \\
S_{oper} &= 0.03 \times 1500 = ₹45 \,\text{crore} \\
S_{mod} &= 0.02 \times 1500 = ₹30 \,\text{crore} \\
S_{nonrev} &= 60 + 45 + 30 = ₹135 \,\text{crore}
\end{aligned}
$$

Margin: $M = 70{,}000 - 40{,}000 = ₹30{,}000$/T. Daily production design rate:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{design} &= 500{,}000 \text{ TPY} = 500{,}000 / 365 = 1369.86 \text{ TPD}
\end{aligned}
$$

For computational convenience, work in annual units: $P_{design}^{annual} = 500{,}000$ TPY.

Using the derivation from Section 3.2 (Step 5) with $k = 1.2$ /year, $t_0 = 0.5$ yr, $T_{su} = 1.0$ yr:

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{rev} &= \dfrac{M \cdot P_{design}^{annual}}{k} \cdot \ln\!\left(\dfrac{1 + e^{k t_0}}{1 + e^{k(t_0 - T_{su})}}\right) \\
&= \dfrac{30{,}000 \cdot 500{,}000}{1.2} \cdot \ln\!\left(\dfrac{1 + e^{0.6}}{1 + e^{-0.6}}\right) \\
&= \dfrac{1.5 \times 10^{10}}{1.2} \cdot \ln\!\left(\dfrac{1 + 1.8221}{1 + 0.5488}\right) \\
&= 1.25 \times 10^{10} \cdot \ln\!\left(\dfrac{2.8221}{1.5488}\right) \\
&= 1.25 \times 10^{10} \cdot \ln(1.8223) \\
&= 1.25 \times 10^{10} \cdot 0.6002 \\
&\approx ₹750.3 \,\text{crore}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Total nominal start-up cost:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{total} &= 135 + 750.3 = ₹885.3 \,\text{crore}
\end{aligned}
$$

**As % of FCI:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\% S = \dfrac{885.3}{1500} \times 100 \approx 59\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> *(Note: This unusually high value is characteristic of a *very high-margin* bulk petrochemical product — exactly the case for ethylene. The lost-revenue component dominates the start-up cost in such cases.)*

> *(7 marks: 2 for categories, 3 for logistic integral, 1 for total, 1 for %.)*

---

**Part (b) — First-year effective production:**

Integrate the logistic ramp over the first 12 months to find total Year-1 production:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{Y1} &= \int_{0}^{1} \dfrac{P_{design}^{annual}}{1 + e^{-1.2(t - 0.5)}} \, dt
\end{aligned}
$$

Using the standard identity (substitute $u = -1.2(t - 0.5)$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{Y1} &= \dfrac{P_{design}^{annual}}{-1.2} \cdot \left[ \ln\!\left(1 + e^{-1.2(t - 0.5)}\right) \right]_{0}^{1} \\
&= \dfrac{500{,}000}{-1.2} \cdot \left[ \ln\!\left(1 + e^{-0.6}\right) - \ln\!\left(1 + e^{0.6}\right) \right] \\
&= -416{,}667 \cdot \left[ \ln(1.5488) - \ln(2.8221) \right] \\
&= -416{,}667 \cdot (0.4377 - 1.0376) \\
&= -416{,}667 \cdot (-0.5999) \\
&\approx 249{,}960 \,\text{TPY}
\end{aligned}
$$

So first-year production is ≈ **249,960 TPA** (≈ 50% of design).

**Percentage revenue loss in Year 1:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\% R_{lost,1} &= \dfrac{500{,}000 - 249{,}960}{500{,}000} \times 100 \approx 50.0\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> *(7 marks: 3 for setting up the integral, 3 for evaluation, 1 for the final % loss.)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Recap — "Important Things to Remember" 🚀

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 — Start-up Costs**

- ✅ **Start-up cost is NOT a part of Fixed Capital Investment (FCI).** It is a separate transitional cost, but is included in **Total Capital Investment (TCI)** through modified working capital.

- ✅ The four canonical buckets are: **$S_{pre}$, $S_{oper}$, $S_{mod}$, $S_{rev}$**. Each must be quoted separately in 14-mark answers.

- ✅ For **high-margin / high-capacity** plants, the **lost-revenue component ($S_{rev}$)** usually dominates. For **low-margin / mature-tech** plants, the categorical costs ($S_{pre}, S_{oper}, S_{mod}$) dominate.

- ✅ The **typical % of FCI** is **5–20% for routine plants** and **20–30% for complex ones**. First-of-its-kind plants can exceed 30%.

- ✅ **Always discount** the nominal start-up cost using the annuity-PV formula to get the time-value-adjusted burden that feeds into the NPV / IRR calculation.

- ✅ Production ramps are **never binary** (0% → 100%). Use a **linear** ramp as the KTU default; use a **logistic** ramp for higher accuracy on bulk petrochemicals.

- ✅ The **effective first-year capacity** $T_{eff,1}$ is reduced by both the start-up period and the de-rating factor $\alpha \in [0.05, 0.20]$.

- ✅ Working capital **inflates** during start-up due to extra raw-material and product inventories — remember to add $I_{RM,extra}$ and $I_{P,extra}$.

- ✅ **Catalyst first-fill, adsorbent loading, and solvent inventory** are sometimes (controversially) classified as either FCI or start-up cost. KTU convention: classify under start-up cost if the catalyst is *consumed irreversibly* during the first few months; otherwise under FCI.

- ✅ **Avoid common errors:**
  - ❌ Confusing start-up cost with working capital — they are *related* but *not identical*.
  - ❌ Forgetting to convert daily production to annual or vice versa in the integration.
  - ❌ Missing the $e^{-iT_{su}}$ exponential in the PV formula.
  - ❌ Treating off-spec product as zero-value (it often has salvage value).

- ✅ **Final formula to memorize cold:**
  $$S_{total} = S_{pre} + S_{oper} + S_{mod} + M \cdot P_{design} \cdot \int_{0}^{T_{su}} \!\!\left(1 - \dfrac{P(t)}{P_{design}}\right) dt$$
  $$PV_{su} = \dfrac{S_{total}}{i \cdot T_{su}} \cdot \left(1 - e^{-i T_{su}}\right)$$

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Plant Overhead Costs
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT757 - ECONOMICS AND MANAGEMENT OF CHEMICAL INDUSTRIES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Plant Overhead Costs

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Plant Overhead Cost (Manufacturing Overhead / Factory Overhead):**
> Plant Overhead Cost refers to the aggregate of all **indirect manufacturing costs** incurred during the production of a chemical product that cannot be directly or economically traced to a specific unit, batch, or job, but are essential to sustain the manufacturing operations of the plant. It encompasses every expense of the factory other than direct materials and direct labour.

In the context of **Chemical Process Industries**, plant overhead includes indirect materials (solvents, catalysts, lubricants), indirect labour (supervisors, foremen, maintenance crew), and indirect expenses (depreciation of plant equipment, insurance, factory rent, property taxes, utilities like steam, water, electricity, and process water treatment).

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Kitchen Restaurant" Model

Imagine you run a restaurant kitchen that prepares meals (chemical products). The **direct costs** are easy to identify — flour, vegetables (raw materials) and the cook's wages (direct labour). However, several expenses keep the kitchen running but cannot be pinned to a single dish:

- The electricity bill for ovens and refrigerators
- The salary of the supervisor
- Depreciation of the LPG pipeline
- Cleaning supplies
- Repairs to the exhaust system

These are **plant overheads** — they exist for ALL products collectively. Just like a restaurateur must allocate the electricity bill across different menu items, a chemical engineer-economist must distribute overhead across the various products manufactured in a plant.

### 1.3 Classification at a Glance

| **Overhead Type** | **Behaviour with Output** | **Examples in Chemical Plant** |
|---|---|---|
| **Fixed Overhead** | Remains constant regardless of production volume | Depreciation, plant rent, insurance, salaries of permanent staff |
| **Variable Overhead** | Varies directly and proportionally with output | Indirect consumables, packaging for batches, factory power scaled with throughput |
| **Semi-Variable Overhead** | Has a fixed base + variable component | Telephone bills, maintenance (has a routine + breakdown part), supervisor's salary with overtime |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Focus:** Module 2 emphasizes the **identification, classification, allocation, apportionment, and absorption** of plant overheads — the entire cost-flow pipeline from incurrence to final product cost.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Four-Step Overhead Cost Flow Pipeline

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In KTU 2024 Scheme, the question pattern tests whether a student can map the **entire overhead cost flow** from identification to product costing. Memorize this 4-step pipeline.

1. **Identification / Collection (Cost Pooling):** All indirect manufacturing expenses are collected into homogeneous cost pools (e.g., "Maintenance Cost Pool", "Utilities Cost Pool", "Depreciation Cost Pool").
2. **Allocation:** Direct assignment of overhead to a specific cost centre (e.g., assigning the foreman's salary to the Reactor Section). This is done when the cost can be unambiguously tied to a single cost centre.
3. **Apportionment (Distribution):** When a cost benefits multiple cost centres, it is distributed using an appropriate **basis** (e.g., floor area, capital value, kWh consumed, labour hours).
4. **Absorption:** Charging the apportioned overhead of each cost centre to the products using a pre-determined **Overhead Absorption Rate (OAR)**.

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Rule for KTU Tables:** The vertical pipe `|` is **forbidden** inside table cells. Use `\vert` or `\mid` instead for any mathematical delimiter.

| **Formula Name** | **Mathematical Expression** | **Units / Output** |
|---|---|---|
| Overhead Absorption Rate (Direct Labour Hour Method) | $\text{OAR} \;=\; \dfrac{\text{Estimated Overhead}}{\text{Estimated Direct Labour Hours}}$ | Currency per labour hour |
| Overhead Absorption Rate (Machine Hour Method) | $\text{OAR} \;=\; \dfrac{\text{Estimated Overhead}}{\text{Estimated Machine Hours}}$ | Currency per machine hour |
| Overhead Absorption Rate (Prime Cost % Method) | $\text{OAR} \;=\; \dfrac{\text{Estimated Overhead}}{\text{Estimated Prime Cost}} \times 100$ | Percentage |
| Overhead Absorption Rate (Unit / Output Method) | $\text{OAR} \;=\; \dfrac{\text{Estimated Overhead}}{\text{Estimated Units Produced}}$ | Currency per unit |
| Total Overhead Absorbed | $\text{Absorbed Overhead} \;=\; \text{OAR} \times \text{Actual Activity Base}$ | Currency |
| Under-absorbed Overhead (Adverse) | $\text{Underabsorption} \;=\; \text{Actual Overhead} - \text{Absorbed Overhead}$ | Currency (positive) |
| Over-absorbed Overhead (Favourable) | $\text{Overabsorption} \;=\; \text{Absorbed Overhead} - \text{Actual Overhead}$ | Currency (positive) |
| Apportionment Ratio | $\text{Share}_i \;=\; \dfrac{\text{Basis}_i}{\sum_{j=1}^{n} \text{Basis}_j}$ | Dimensionless ratio |
| Total Product Cost | $\text{Total Cost} \;=\; \text{Direct Material} + \text{Direct Labour} + \text{Overhead Absorbed}$ | Currency per unit |

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

In **chemical plant economics**, accurate overhead distribution is critical for:

- **Product pricing decisions** — under-costing a product leads to losses; over-costing kills market competitiveness.
- **Make-or-buy decisions** — comparing in-house production cost (which includes allocated overhead) vs. outsourcing.
- **Cost-volume-profit (CVP) analysis** — separating fixed vs. variable overhead determines the break-even point.
- **Capital budgeting** — proper treatment of depreciation (a fixed overhead) affects project cash flow projections.
- **Performance benchmarking** — comparing inter-plant OARs reveals operational efficiency gaps.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Comprehensive Apportionment & Absorption

> **Problem Statement (KTU-Style Numerical):**
> A chemical plant has two production departments — **Reactor Section (R)** and **Packaging Section (P)** — and two service departments — **Boiler House (B)** and **Maintenance Workshop (M)**. The following data is given:
>
> | **Item** | **Amount (₹)** |
> |---|---|
> | Indirect Materials | 60,000 |
> | Indirect Labour | 90,000 |
> | Depreciation on Equipment | 1,20,000 |
> | Factory Rent | 80,000 |
> | Insurance | 40,000 |
> | Power (Electricity) | 1,50,000 |
>
> Additional data:
>
> | **Basis** | **R** | **P** | **B** | **M** |
> |---|---|---|---|---|
> | Floor Area (m²) | 500 | 300 | 200 | 100 |
> | Capital Value of Assets (₹ lakh) | 6 | 3 | 4 | 1 |
> | Direct Labour Hours | 4000 | 2000 | 1000 | 500 |
> | Machine Hours | 6000 | 3000 | — | — |
> | kWh Consumed | 4000 | 2000 | 1500 | 500 |
> | Labour Hours (for B and M apportionment) | 4000 | 2000 | 1000 | 500 |

**Step 1: Total Overhead Incurred (Cost Pooling)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total Overhead} &= 60{,}000 + 90{,}000 + 1{,}20{,}000 + 80{,}000 + 40{,}000 + 1{,}50{,}000 \\
&= 5{,}40{,}000 \text{ ₹}
\end{aligned}
$$

> *Logic:* Sum all identified indirect manufacturing expenses to form the gross overhead pool.

**Step 2: Primary Apportionment of Each Overhead Item**

| **Overhead Item** | **Basis Used** | **R (₹)** | **P (₹)** | **B (₹)** | **M (₹)** | **Total (₹)** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Indirect Materials | Direct Labour Hrs (4000:2000:1000:500) | 30,000 | 15,000 | 7,500 | 7,500 | 60,000 |
| Indirect Labour | Direct Labour Hrs | 45,000 | 22,500 | 11,250 | 11,250 | 90,000 |
| Depreciation | Capital Value (6:3:4:1) | 50,000 | 25,000 | 33,333 | 11,667 | 1,20,000 |
| Factory Rent | Floor Area (500:300:200:100) | 36,364 | 21,818 | 14,545 | 7,273 | 80,000 |
| Insurance | Capital Value (6:3:4:1) | 16,667 | 8,333 | 11,111 | 3,889 | 40,000 |
| Power | kWh (4000:2000:1500:500) | 75,000 | 37,500 | 28,125 | 9,375 | 1,50,000 |
| **Sub-Total (₹)** | — | **2,53,031** | **1,30,151** | **1,05,864** | **50,954** | **5,40,000** |

> *Logic Sample for Power in R:*
> $$\text{Power}_R = 1{,}50{,}000 \times \frac{4000}{8000} = 75{,}000 \; \text{₹}$$
> The denominator $8000 = 4000+2000+1500+500$ is the total kWh basis.

**Step 3: Secondary Apportionment of Service Department Costs**

Service departments (B, M) serve the production departments. We use the **Step Method (Step-Distribution Method)** or **Simultaneous Equation Method**. Here we use the **Step Method** starting with the department that serves the most other departments.

Let us assume **B (Boiler House)** serves everyone (highest inter-service ratio), so we redistribute B first.

**Redistribution of B (₹1,05,864) to R, P, M in the ratio 4000 : 2000 : 500**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{To R} &= 1{,}05{,}864 \times \frac{4000}{6500} = 65{,}147 \; \text{₹} \\[4pt]
\text{To P} &= 1{,}05{,}864 \times \frac{2000}{6500} = 32{,}574 \; \text{₹} \\[4pt]
\text{To M} &= 1{,}05{,}864 \times \frac{500}{6500} = 8{,}143 \; \text{₹}
\end{aligned}
$$

Now redistribute **M (₹50,954 + ₹8,143 = ₹59,097)** to R and P only in the ratio 4000 : 2000.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{To R} &= 59{,}097 \times \frac{4000}{6000} = 39{,}398 \; \text{₹} \\[4pt]
\text{To P} &= 59{,}097 \times \frac{2000}{6000} = 19{,}699 \; \text{₹}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Total Overhead for Production Departments**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total Overhead in R} &= 2{,}53{,}031 + 65{,}147 + 39{,}398 = 3{,}57{,}576 \; \text{₹} \\[4pt]
\text{Total Overhead in P} &= 1{,}30{,}151 + 32{,}574 + 19{,}699 = 1{,}82{,}424 \; \text{₹}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Compute Overhead Absorption Rate (OAR)**

For Department R (using Machine Hours as base):

$$
\text{OAR}_R = \frac{3{,}57{,}576}{6000} = 59.596 \;\text{₹/machine hour}
$$

For Department P (using Direct Labour Hours as base):

$$
\text{OAR}_P = \frac{1{,}82{,}424}{2000} = 91.212 \;\text{₹/labour hour}
$$

> *Logic:* The base chosen for OAR should be the one most closely correlated with the incurrence of overhead. In R, machine-intensive operations correlate better with machine hours; in P, labour-intensive packaging correlates with labour hours.

---

### 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Under/Over Absorption Reconciliation

**Problem:** A company budgeted ₹2,00,000 overhead and 10,000 labour hours for 2024. Actual overhead incurred was ₹2,15,000, and actual labour hours worked were 9,500.

**Step 1: Predetermined OAR**

$$
\text{OAR} = \frac{2{,}00{,}000}{10{,}000} = 20 \;\text{₹/labour hour}
$$

**Step 2: Absorbed Overhead**

$$
\text{Absorbed Overhead} = 20 \times 9{,}500 = 1{,}90{,}000 \; \text{₹}
$$

**Step 3: Variance**

$$
\text{Variance} = 2{,}15{,}000 - 1{,}90{,}000 = 25{,}000 \; \text{₹ (Under-absorbed)}
$$

> *Interpretation:* The plant spent ₹25,000 more than what was absorbed into products. This adverse variance is charged to the **Costing P&L A/c** (in absorption costing) or written off in the period.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Methods of Absorption

| **Method** | **Best Suited For** | **Advantage** | **Disadvantage** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Direct Labour Hour | Labour-intensive plants (e.g., manual packaging) | Simple, widely applicable | Ignores machine usage |
| Machine Hour | Capital-intensive, automated plants (e.g., continuous reactor lines) | Captures machine-driven cost | Labour costs may be under-absorbed |
| Prime Cost % | Where direct material dominates (e.g., bulk chemicals) | Easy to apply | Distorts cost when material prices fluctuate |
| Unit of Output | Single-product plants (e.g., urea, ammonia units) | Extremely simple | Useless for multi-product plants |
| Direct Labour Cost % | Where wage rates vary across products | Adjusts for wage rate differences | Distorts when one product is highly mechanized |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Plant Overhead Cost Flow Architecture

> **Note:** This Mermaid block uses pure alphanumeric node IDs (e.g., `poolA`, `nodeB`) to comply with KTU compilation safeguards. All labels are raw uppercase text without markdown formatting.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    poolA[("COST POOL: INDIRECT EXPENSES")]
    ident1["Indirect Materials"]
    ident2["Indirect Labour"]
    ident3["Depreciation and Rent"]
    ident4["Utilities and Insurance"]

    allocA["ALLOCATION TO COST CENTRES"]
    dept1["PRODUCTION DEPT R - REACTOR"]
    dept2["PRODUCTION DEPT P - PACKAGING"]
    serv1["SERVICE DEPT B - BOILER"]
    serv2["SERVICE DEPT M - MAINTENANCE"]

    app1["APPORTIONMENT BASIS: FLOOR AREA, KWH, CAPITAL VALUE"]
    stepM["STEP METHOD REDISTRIBUTION"]

    oarR["OAR R = OVERHEAD R DIVIDED BY MACHINE HOURS"]
    oarP["OAR P = OVERHEAD P DIVIDED BY LABOUR HOURS"]

    prod1["PRODUCT A TOTAL COST"]
    prod2["PRODUCT B TOTAL COST"]

    poolA --> ident1
    poolA --> ident2
    poolA --> ident3
    poolA --> ident4
    ident1 --> allocA
    ident2 --> allocA
    ident3 --> allocA
    ident4 --> allocA
    allocA --> dept1
    allocA --> dept2
    allocA --> serv1
    allocA --> serv2
    dept1 --> app1
    dept2 --> app1
    serv1 --> app1
    serv2 --> app1
    app1 --> stepM
    stepM --> oarR
    stepM --> oarP
    oarR --> prod1
    oarP --> prod2
```

### 4.2 Cost Behaviour Pattern Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    fixedOverhead["FIXED OVERHEAD - CONSTANT WITH OUTPUT"]
    varOverhead["VARIABLE OVERHEAD - PROPORTIONAL TO OUTPUT"]
    semiOverhead["SEMI VARIABLE OVERHEAD - MIXED BEHAVIOUR"]

    fixedEx["Depreciation, Insurance, Rent"]
    varEx["Indirect Consumables, Power Scaling"]
    semiEx["Telephone, Maintenance, Supervisor Salary"]

    fixedOverhead --> fixedEx
    varOverhead --> varEx
    semiOverhead --> semiEx

    fixedOverhead --> totalOH["TOTAL OVERHEAD POOL"]
    varOverhead --> totalOH
    semiOverhead --> totalOH
    totalOH --> allocate["ALLOCATION AND APPORTIONMENT PIPELINE"]
```

### 4.3 Overhead Variance Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    compare{"ABSORBED OVERHEAD vs ACTUAL OVERHEAD"}
    absorbedMore["ABSORBED MORE: OVER ABSORBED - FAVOURABLE"]
    absorbedLess["ABSORBED LESS: UNDER ABSORBED - ADVERSE"]
    treatFav["CREDIT TO COSTING P AND L OR ADJUST PRODUCT COST"]
    treatAdv["DEBIT TO COSTING P AND L OR ADJUST PRODUCT COST"]
    investigate["INVESTIGATE: WAS VOLUME LOWER OR OVERHEAD HIGHER?"]

    compare -->|Greater Than| absorbedMore
    compare -->|Less Than| absorbedLess
    absorbedMore --> treatFav
    absorbedLess --> treatAdv
    absorbedLess --> investigate
    absorbedMore --> investigate
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]**
**"Distinguish between Allocation and Apportionment of overhead costs. Give one example for each in a chemical plant."**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| **Aspect** | **Allocation** | **Apportionment** |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | Direct charging of overhead to a single cost centre | Distribution of overhead to multiple cost centres |
| Nature | Whole-cost transfer | Pro-rata / proportional share |
| Example | Salary of Reactor Section supervisor charged to Reactor Department | Factory rent shared among Reactor, Packaging, Boiler using floor area |

> **Marking Tip:** Award 1 mark for each clear distinction and 1 mark for the chemical-plant example. Two examples earn full 3 marks.

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]**
**"A plant incurs ₹1,20,000 as total overhead and produces 8,000 units. What is the overhead cost per unit using the Unit Output Method? If production drops to 6,000 units, will the per-unit overhead change under absorption costing?"**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

$$
\text{OAR} = \frac{1{,}20{,}000}{8{,}000} = 15 \;\text{₹/unit}
$$

If overhead is **fixed**, then at 6,000 units the new per-unit overhead becomes:

$$
\text{New OAR} = \frac{1{,}20{,}000}{6{,}000} = 20 \;\text{₹/unit}
$$

Yes, per-unit overhead **increases** under absorption costing because the same fixed cost is spread over fewer units. The total overhead remains ₹1,20,000 but unit cost rises — a key insight for break-even analysis.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

> **KTU Pattern:** Each Part B question has two sub-parts: Part (a) carrying **7 marks** and Part (b) carrying **7 marks**. Students answer EITHER the full Question A OR the full Question B.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a) [7 Marks] A chemical company has three departments: Reactor (Production), Packaging (Production), and Utilities (Service). The following overheads are incurred:**

- Indirect Wages: ₹45,000
- Depreciation: ₹75,000
- Factory Rent: ₹30,000
- Power: ₹60,000

**Departmental data:**

| **Basis** | **Reactor** | **Packaging** | **Utilities** |
|---|---|---|---|
| No. of Workers | 30 | 20 | 10 |
| Capital Value (₹000) | 200 | 100 | 100 |
| Floor Area (m²) | 400 | 300 | 200 |
| kWh | 5000 | 3000 | 2000 |

**Apportion the overheads to all departments using suitable bases. Then redistribute the Utilities department cost to the two production departments in the ratio of kWh consumed.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Apportionment (using given bases)**

- Indirect Wages (basis: workers; ratio 30:20:10 = 3:2:1)
  - Reactor: $45{,}000 \times \tfrac{3}{6} = 22{,}500$
  - Packaging: $45{,}000 \times \tfrac{2}{6} = 15{,}000$
  - Utilities: $45{,}000 \times \tfrac{1}{6} = 7{,}500$

- Depreciation (basis: capital value; ratio 200:100:100 = 2:1:1)
  - Reactor: $75{,}000 \times \tfrac{2}{4} = 37{,}500$
  - Packaging: $75{,}000 \times \tfrac{1}{4} = 18{,}750$
  - Utilities: $75{,}000 \times \tfrac{1}{4} = 18{,}750$

- Factory Rent (basis: floor area; ratio 400:300:200 = 4:3:2)
  - Reactor: $30{,}000 \times \tfrac{4}{9} = 13{,}333$
  - Packaging: $30{,}000 \times \tfrac{3}{9} = 10{,}000$
  - Utilities: $30{,}000 \times \tfrac{2}{9} = 6{,}667$

- Power (basis: kWh; ratio 5000:3000:2000 = 5:3:2)
  - Reactor: $60{,}000 \times \tfrac{5}{10} = 30{,}000$
  - Packaging: $60{,}000 \times \tfrac{3}{10} = 18{,}000$
  - Utilities: $60{,}000 \times \tfrac{2}{10} = 12{,}000$

**Sub-totals:**

- Reactor: $22{,}500 + 37{,}500 + 13{,}333 + 30{,}000 = 1{,}03{,}333$
- Packaging: $15{,}000 + 18{,}750 + 10{,}000 + 18{,}000 = 61{,}750$
- Utilities: $7{,}500 + 18{,}750 + 6{,}667 + 12{,}000 = 44{,}917$

**Step 2: Redistribution of Utilities to Production Departments (kWh ratio 5000:3000)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{To Reactor} &= 44{,}917 \times \frac{5000}{8000} = 28{,}073 \; \text{₹} \\[4pt]
\text{To Packaging} &= 44{,}917 \times \frac{3000}{8000} = 16{,}844 \; \text{₹}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Final Overhead for Production Departments**

- Reactor: $1{,}03{,}333 + 28{,}073 = 1{,}31{,}406$ ₹
- Packaging: $61{,}750 + 16{,}844 = 78{,}594$ ₹

**(b) [7 Marks] Compute the Overhead Absorption Rate for each department using the most appropriate base. Reactor operates 6,000 machine hours; Packaging operates 2,000 direct labour hours. If actual overhead in Reactor was ₹1,35,000 and in Packaging was ₹80,000, compute the under/over absorption.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Choose Base and Compute OAR**

- Reactor (capital-intensive → Machine Hour Method):
$$
\text{OAR}_R = \frac{1{,}31{,}406}{6{,}000} = 21.90 \;\text{₹/machine hour}
$$

- Packaging (labour-intensive → Labour Hour Method):
$$
\text{OAR}_P = \frac{78{,}594}{2{,}000} = 39.30 \;\text{₹/labour hour}
$$

**Step 2: Actual vs Absorbed**

- Reactor: Actual = ₹1,35,000; Absorbed = $21.90 \times 6{,}000 = 1{,}31{,}400$ ₹ → **Under-absorbed by ₹3,600**
- Packaging: Actual = ₹80,000; Absorbed = $39.30 \times 2{,}000 = 78{,}600$ ₹ → **Under-absorbed by ₹1,400**

**Step 3: Interpretation**
Both departments are under-absorbed, indicating either that the original overhead estimates were too low, or that the activity level was lower than expected. The total adverse variance of ₹5,000 is transferred to the Costing P&L A/c.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Choice

**(a) [7 Marks] Explain with suitable examples the classification of overheads into Fixed, Variable, and Semi-Variable categories in a chemical process industry. Why is this classification important for managerial decision-making?**

**Model Answer Outline (7 Marks):**

**Fixed Overhead (2 marks):**
Constant in total within a relevant range. Examples: depreciation of distillation column, plant insurance premium, salary of permanent QC staff.

**Variable Overhead (2 marks):**
Varies in total in direct proportion to output. Examples: cost of catalysts consumed per batch, packaging material per kg of finished product.

**Semi-Variable Overhead (2 marks):**
Has both fixed and variable components. Examples: electricity bill (fixed service charge + usage charge), maintenance (scheduled + breakdown).

**Importance for Decision-Making (1 mark):**
- Helps in **break-even analysis** (only fixed + variable matters).
- Aids in **flexible budgeting** (semi-variables are segregated using high-low method).
- Critical for **make-or-buy**, **shutdown**, and **product-mix** decisions.

**(b) [7 Marks] A plant uses the High-Low Method to separate a semi-variable overhead into fixed and variable components. The following data is available:**

| **Month** | **Production (Units)** | **Maintenance Overhead (₹)** |
|---|---|---|
| January | 2,000 | 14,000 |
| February | 2,500 | 16,000 |
| March | 3,000 | 18,000 |
| April | 3,500 | 20,000 |
| May | 4,000 | 22,000 |

**Find the fixed and variable components. Also estimate the overhead for a production level of 5,000 units.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Identify Highest and Lowest Activity Levels**
- Highest: May — 4,000 units → ₹22,000
- Lowest: January — 2,000 units → ₹14,000

**Step 2: Compute Variable Cost per Unit**

$$
\text{Variable cost per unit} = \frac{22{,}000 - 14{,}000}{4{,}000 - 2{,}000} = \frac{8{,}000}{2{,}000} = 4 \;\text{₹/unit}
$$

**Step 3: Compute Fixed Component**

$$
\text{Fixed cost} = 22{,}000 - (4 \times 4{,}000) = 22{,}000 - 16{,}000 = 6{,}000 \;\text{₹}
$$

> *Cross-check using low point:* $14{,}000 - (4 \times 2{,}000) = 6{,}000$ ₹ ✓

**Step 4: Cost Equation**

$$
\text{Total Overhead} = 6{,}000 + 4 \times \text{Units Produced}
$$

**Step 5: Forecast for 5,000 units**

$$
\text{Overhead}_{5000} = 6{,}000 + 4 \times 5{,}000 = 6{,}000 + 20{,}000 = 26{,}000 \;\text{₹}
$$

**Verification of Method:** The High-Low Method assumes a linear cost behaviour pattern. It uses only the extreme data points, so it is approximate but quick — ideal for preliminary budgets in chemical plant operations.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU 2024 Exams:**
>
> 1. **Confusing Allocation vs Apportionment:** Allocation is whole-cost transfer to ONE cost centre; Apportionment is proportional distribution to MANY cost centres. Mixing these terms forfeits 2-3 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting the Redistribution Step:** Many students stop after primary apportionment and forget to redistribute service department costs. The KTU key explicitly awards **2 marks** for service department redistribution.
> 3. **Wrong OAR Base Selection:** Always choose the base that **causes** the overhead (machine-intensive → machine hours; labour-intensive → labour hours). Blindly applying Direct Labour Hours to an automated plant loses 1-2 marks.
> 4. **Sign Error in Variance:** Under-absorbed = Actual > Absorbed (Adverse). Over-absorbed = Absorbed > Actual (Favourable). Reversing this is a common 1-mark penalty.
> 5. **Not Stating Units:** KTU 2024 scheme emphasizes unit-aware answers. Always write "₹/unit" or "₹/machine hour" — examiners actively scan for this.
> 6. **Skipping Formula in Step-by-Step Questions:** Even if the calculation is correct, **writing the formula earns 1 mark** as a "methodology" credit per KTU valuation pattern.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Plant Overhead Costs (Module 2)**

- **Definition:** Plant overhead = all indirect manufacturing costs (neither direct material nor direct labour).
- **Three Behavioural Types:**
  - *Fixed:* constant in total (depreciation, rent, insurance)
  - *Variable:* proportional to output (consumables, indirect materials)
  - *Semi-Variable:* fixed + variable (telephone, maintenance, power)
- **Cost Flow Pipeline:** Identification → Allocation → Apportionment → Absorption.
- **Allocation** = whole cost to ONE cost centre; **Apportionment** = share to MANY cost centres.
- **Service Department Redistribution Methods:** Step Method, Repeated Distribution, Simultaneous Equation.
- **OAR Formulae (memorize all four):**
  - Labour Hour, Machine Hour, Prime Cost %, Unit Output.
- **Under-absorbed** = Adverse Variance (Debit P&L); **Over-absorbed** = Favourable Variance (Credit P&L).
- **High-Low Method:** Uses only highest and lowest activity points to find variable cost/unit and fixed cost.
- **In a chemical plant**, the most common OAR base is **Machine Hour** for reactor/distillation sections and **Direct Labour Hour** for packaging/utility sections.
- **Cause-and-Effect Rule:** The chosen absorption base must be the one that most influences the incurrence of overhead — this is a KTU-favourite reasoning question.
- **Pipe Symbol Rule:** Always use `\vert` or `\mid` in LaTeX tables — never the raw `|` symbol.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Mathematical Methods for Profitability Evaluation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT757 - ECONOMICS AND MANAGEMENT OF CHEMICAL INDUSTRIES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Mathematical Methods for Profitability Evaluation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Profitability Evaluation** in chemical process industries refers to the systematic application of quantitative mathematical techniques to assess whether a proposed capital investment, process modification, or plant expansion will generate financial returns commensurate with the **time value of money**, **risk exposure**, and **opportunity cost of capital**.

According to the KTU 2024 Scheme framework, these methods form the backbone of *Engineering Economy* applied to chemical engineering decisions, and they are classified into two broad families:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core KTU Classification of Profitability Methods**
>
> **A. Non-Discounted (Static) Methods** — Ignore time value of money
> - Rate of Return on Investment (ROROI / ROI)
> - Payback Period (PBP)
>
> **B. Discounted (Dynamic) Methods** — Account for time value of money
> - Net Present Value (NPV)
> - Discounted Cash Flow Rate of Return (DCFRR / IRR)
> - Profitability Index (PI)
> - Net Annual Worth (NAW)
> - Net Future Value (NFV)

A *profitable* project is one whose **discounted cash inflows exceed the discounted cash outflows** over its economic life, yielding a positive economic value added for the firm.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are a chemical plant manager in **Kochi** deciding between two projects:

- **Project A:** Install a **₹10 Crore** distillation column that saves solvent and earns **₹1.5 Crore per year** for 10 years.
- **Project B:** Build a **₹10 Crore** wastewater treatment plant that earns **₹1.2 Crore per year** for 12 years, plus a carbon-credit bonus at year 12.

A naive person would say "Project A earns more per year, so it's better." But this is **economically wrong** because:

1. **Money received tomorrow is worth less than money received today.** If your bank offers **8%** interest, ₹100 today = ₹108 next year. So future ₹1.5 Crore is "shrunk" by the 8% discount each year.
2. **Project B runs longer** — the extra 2 years of cash flow may outweigh Project A's higher annual return.
3. The **risk profile**, **salvage value**, and **tax shielding** from depreciation differ.

**Mathematical Methods for Profitability Evaluation** are the *scientific scalpel* that cuts through this complexity. They convert all future, uneven, risky cash flows into a single comparable number (NPV, IRR, etc.) that lets the manager rank projects on an apples-to-apples basis.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Benchmarks Used in Indian Chemical Industry**
> - **MARR (Minimum Attractive Rate of Return):** Typically **12% – 18%** for chemical projects in India (depends on plant risk and WACC).
> - **Plant Economic Life:** **10 – 15 years** for grassroots chemical plants; **5 – 10 years** for retrofits.
> - **Tax Rate (Corporate):** **25.17%** (base 25% + 0.17% cess) under the Income Tax Act, India.

---

## 1.3 Why These Methods Matter in Chemical Engineering

Chemical industries are uniquely **capital-intensive**. A single **petrochemical cracker** can cost **₹30,000 – 50,000 Crore**. A small misjudgment in profitability analysis can:

- Erode shareholder equity by hundreds of crores
- Lead to plant shut-downs (e.g., stranded naphtha crackers)
- Misallocate scarce CAPEX between competing technologies (e.g., **green hydrogen vs. blue hydrogen vs. grey hydrogen**)

Hence, KTU explicitly trains chemical engineers to use these methods when evaluating:
- New reactor selection (PFR vs. CSTR vs. Membrane Reactor)
- Heat integration projects (pinch analysis payoffs)
- Energy recovery investments (waste heat boiler, ORC turbines)
- Pollution control equipment (scrubbers, ESP, ZLD systems)
- Capacity expansion decisions
</br>

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Time Value of Money — The Foundation

Every profitability method rests on the principle that **a rupee today ≠ a rupee tomorrow**. We discount future cash flows back to "present" using a discount rate *i* (usually MARR or WACC).

The **single-payment present worth factor** is:

$$P = F \cdot (1+i)^{-n}$$

The **single-payment future worth factor** is:

$$F = P \cdot (1+i)^{n}$$

The **uniform series present worth factor** (bringing *n* equal annual payments *A* to time 0) is:

$$P = A \cdot \frac{(1+i)^{n} - 1}{i \cdot (1+i)^{n}}$$

The **capital recovery factor** (converting present value to equivalent annual amount) is:

$$A = P \cdot \frac{i \cdot (1+i)^{n}}{(1+i)^{n} - 1}$$

The **sinking fund factor** (accumulating *A* annually to a future value *F*) is:

$$F = A \cdot \frac{(1+i)^{n} - 1}{i}$$

The **uniform series future worth factor** is:

$$F = A \cdot \frac{(1+i)^{n} - 1}{i}$$

The **gradient-to-uniform conversion** (arithmetic gradient *G*):

$$A_{eq} = G \cdot \frac{1}{i} - G \cdot \frac{n}{(1+i)^{n} - 1}$$

---

## 2.2 Method 1 — Net Present Value (NPV)

**Definition:** NPV is the algebraic sum of all discounted cash flows (inflows minus outflows) over the entire project life, brought to time zero.

$$NPV = \sum_{t=0}^{N} \frac{CF_{t}}{(1+i)^{t}}$$

Where:
- $CF_t$ = Net cash flow in year *t* (positive for inflow, negative for outflow)
- $i$ = Discount rate (MARR or WACC)
- $N$ = Project economic life in years

**Decision Rule:**

| NPV Value | Decision |
|---|---|
| NPV > 0 | **Accept** — project adds value |
| NPV = 0 | Indifferent — at MARR exactly |
| NPV < 0 | **Reject** — destroys value |

**Why "Why":** NPV directly measures the wealth created for shareholders. It is the **gold standard** in KTU examinations.

---

## 2.3 Method 2 — Internal Rate of Return (IRR / DCFRR)

**Definition:** IRR is the discount rate *i\** at which NPV becomes **zero**.

$$\sum_{t=0}^{N} \frac{CF_{t}}{(1+i^{*})^{t}} = 0$$

**Decision Rule:**
- If IRR > MARR → **Accept**
- If IRR < MARR → **Reject**
- If IRR = MARR → Indifferent

**Solution Methods (KTU expects):**
1. **Trial-and-error interpolation:**

$$i^{*} = i_{L} + \frac{NPV_{L}}{NPV_{L} - NPV_{H}} \cdot (i_{H} - i_{L})$$

Where $i_L$ and $i_H$ are the lower and higher bracketing rates with opposite-sign NPVs.

2. **Newton-Raphson iterative method** (for advanced problems).

> [!WARNING]
> **Multiple IRR Problem:** If cash flows change sign more than once (e.g., a major mid-life retrofit cost), multiple IRR solutions can exist. In such cases KTU recommends using **NPV** or **Modified IRR (MIRR)**.

---

## 2.4 Method 3 — Rate of Return on Investment (ROROI / ROI)

**Definition:** A non-discounted measure of average annual accounting return as a percentage of the original investment.

$$ROROI = \frac{\text{Average Annual Net Profit}}{\text{Initial Investment}} \times 100\%$$

**Decision Rule:** Accept if ROROI > target/required rate (e.g., 15%).

**Limitation:** Ignores time value of money and project duration. Rarely used in isolation in modern KTU papers but tested conceptually.

---

## 2.5 Method 4 — Payback Period (PBP)

**Definition:** Time required for cumulative undiscounted cash inflows to recover the original investment.

$$PBP = \frac{\text{Initial Investment}}{\text{Annual Cash Inflow}} \quad \text{(for uniform cash flows)}$$

For **non-uniform** cash flows, PBP is the time *t\** at which:

$$\sum_{t=0}^{t^{*}} CF_{t} = 0$$

**Discounted Payback Period (DPBP):** Same logic, but using discounted cash flows.

**Decision Rule:** Accept if PBP < company's maximum acceptable payback (e.g., 5 years for chemical retrofits).

---

## 2.6 Method 5 — Profitability Index (PI)

$$PI = \frac{\text{PV of Future Cash Inflows}}{\text{Initial Investment}}$$

**Decision Rule:** Accept if PI > 1. Particularly useful for **capital rationing** when projects are mutually exclusive and budget is limited.

$$PI = 1 + \frac{NPV}{\text{Initial Investment}}$$

---

## 2.7 Method 6 — Net Annual Worth (NAW)

Converts NPV into an equivalent uniform annual cash flow:

$$NAW = NPV \cdot (A/P, i, N) = NPV \cdot \frac{i(1+i)^{N}}{(1+i)^{N}-1}$$

Useful when comparing projects of **unequal lives** on a year-by-year basis.

---

## 2.8 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table is your exam-day survival kit for profitability problems. Memorize and apply.**

| Method | Formula | Decision Rule | Time Value Considered? |
|---|---|---|---|
| **NPV** | $NPV = \sum_{t=0}^{N} \dfrac{CF_{t}}{(1+i)^{t}}$ | Accept if NPV > 0 | **Yes** |
| **IRR** | $\sum \dfrac{CF_{t}}{(1+i^{*})^{t}} = 0$ | Accept if IRR > MARR | **Yes** |
| **ROROI** | $ROROI = \dfrac{\text{Annual Profit}}{\text{Investment}} \times 100$ | Accept if ROROI > target | **No** |
| **PBP** | $PBP = \dfrac{\text{Investment}}{\text{Annual Cash Inflow}}$ | Accept if PBP < cutoff | **No** |
| **DPBP** | $PBP$ using discounted $CF_t$ | Accept if DPBP < cutoff | **Yes** |
| **PI** | $PI = \dfrac{PV(\text{Inflows})}{\text{Investment}}$ | Accept if PI > 1 | **Yes** |
| **NAW** | $NAW = NPV \cdot (A/P, i, N)$ | Accept if NAW > 0 | **Yes** |
| **NFV** | $NFV = NPV \cdot (F/P, i, N)$ | Accept if NFV > 0 | **Yes** |

**Engineering Factor Notation (KTU uses this in textbooks):**

$$(P/F, i, n) = (1+i)^{-n}$$

$$(F/P, i, n) = (1+i)^{n}$$

$$(P/A, i, n) = \frac{(1+i)^{n}-1}{i(1+i)^{n}}$$

$$(A/P, i, n) = \frac{i(1+i)^{n}}{(1+i)^{n}-1}$$

$$(F/A, i, n) = \frac{(1+i)^{n}-1}{i}$$

$$(A/F, i, n) = \frac{i}{(1+i)^{n}-1}$$
</br>

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Problem Solutions

## 3.1 Derivation: Why Uniform Series Present Worth Factor Works

Starting from a series of end-of-year payments $A, A, A, \ldots, A$ for *n* years, each discounted to time 0:

$$P = \frac{A}{(1+i)^{1}} + \frac{A}{(1+i)^{2}} + \frac{A}{(1+i)^{3}} + \cdots + \frac{A}{(1+i)^{n}}$$

This is a **finite geometric series** with first term $a_{1} = \dfrac{A}{(1+i)}$ and common ratio $r = \dfrac{1}{(1+i)}$.

Using the geometric sum formula $S = a_{1} \cdot \dfrac{1 - r^{n}}{1 - r}$:

$$P = \frac{A}{(1+i)} \cdot \frac{1 - \left(\dfrac{1}{1+i}\right)^{n}}{1 - \dfrac{1}{1+i}}$$

Simplify the denominator:

$$1 - \frac{1}{1+i} = \frac{1+i - 1}{1+i} = \frac{i}{1+i}$$

So:

$$P = \frac{A}{(1+i)} \cdot \frac{1 - (1+i)^{-n}}{\dfrac{i}{1+i}}$$

$$P = A \cdot \frac{(1+i)^{n} - 1}{i(1+i)^{n}}$$

$$\boxed{P = A \cdot (P/A, i, n)}$$

This elegant identity is the workhorse of every KTU cash-flow problem.

---

## 3.2 Solved Problem 1 — NPV Calculation (Full Working)

**Problem (KTU-style):**
A chemical company is evaluating a **₹50 Lakh** investment in a new heat exchanger network. The project generates end-of-year net cash flows of:

- Year 1: **₹12 Lakh**
- Year 2: **₹15 Lakh**
- Year 3: **₹18 Lakh**
- Year 4: **₹20 Lakh**
- Year 5: **₹12 Lakh** (plus ₹5 Lakh salvage value)

The MARR is **12%**. Should the project be accepted?

**Solution — Step by Step:**

**Step 1:** Identify all cash flows and assign signs. Initial investment at $t=0$ is an outflow (-₹50 Lakh). All other entries are inflows (+ve).

**Step 2:** Compute the single-payment present worth factor $(1+i)^{-n}$ for each year.

| Year *t* | Cash Flow $CF_t$ (₹ Lakh) | $(1.12)^{t}$ | Discount Factor $(1.12)^{-t}$ | $PV_t$ (₹ Lakh) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | $-50.00$ | $1.0000$ | $1.0000$ | $-50.000$ |
| 1 | $+12.00$ | $1.1200$ | $0.8929$ | $+10.714$ |
| 2 | $+15.00$ | $1.2544$ | $0.7972$ | $+11.958$ |
| 3 | $+18.00$ | $1.4049$ | $0.7118$ | $+12.812$ |
| 4 | $+20.00$ | $1.5735$ | $0.6355$ | $+12.710$ |
| 5 | $+12.00 + 5.00 = 17.00$ | $1.7623$ | $0.5674$ | $+9.647$ |

**Step 3:** Sum all present values.

$$NPV = -50.000 + 10.714 + 11.958 + 12.812 + 12.710 + 9.647$$

$$NPV = +7.841 \text{ Lakh}$$

**Step 4:** Decision.

$$\boxed{NPV = +₹7.84 \text{ Lakh} > 0 \;\Rightarrow\; \text{ACCEPT the project}}$$

The project creates **₹7.84 Lakh** of wealth in present-value terms above the 12% required return.

---

## 3.3 Solved Problem 2 — IRR via Interpolation

**Problem:**
A **₹1 Crore** distillation column retrofit yields uniform end-of-year savings of **₹22 Lakh** for **6 years** with no salvage value. Find the IRR and decide.

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** Set up the NPV equation set to zero.

$$NPV = -100 + 22 \cdot (P/A, i^{*}, 6) = 0$$

$$(P/A, i^{*}, 6) = \frac{100}{22} = 4.5455$$

**Step 2:** Search for the discount rate that yields this factor.

| Discount Rate $i$ | $(P/A, i, 6)$ | NPV (₹ Lakh) |
|---|---|---|
| 8% | 4.6229 | $+1.70$ |
| 9% | 4.4859 | $-0.31$ |
| 10% | 4.3553 | $-4.18$ |

The IRR lies between **8%** and **9%**. Use linear interpolation:

$$i^{*} = 8\% + \frac{+1.70}{+1.70 - (-0.31)} \cdot (9\% - 8\%)$$

$$i^{*} = 8\% + \frac{1.70}{2.01} \cdot 1\%$$

$$i^{*} = 8\% + 0.846\%$$

$$\boxed{i^{*} = 8.85\%}$$

**Step 3:** Decision. If MARR = 12%, then IRR (8.85%) < MARR → **REJECT**.

If MARR = 8%, then IRR > MARR → **ACCEPT**.

---

## 3.4 Solved Problem 3 — Payback Period (Non-Uniform Cash Flows)

**Problem:**
A **₹30 Lakh** pollution-control project generates:

- Year 1: ₹8 Lakh
- Year 2: ₹10 Lakh
- Year 3: ₹12 Lakh
- Year 4: ₹14 Lakh
- Year 5: ₹16 Lakh

Find the **Payback Period**.

**Solution:**

Cumulative cash flow:

| Year | Annual CF (₹ Lakh) | Cumulative CF (₹ Lakh) |
|---|---|---|
| 0 | $-30.00$ | $-30.00$ |
| 1 | $+8.00$ | $-22.00$ |
| 2 | $+10.00$ | $-12.00$ |
| 3 | $+12.00$ | $0.00$ |
| 4 | $+14.00$ | $+14.00$ |
| 5 | $+16.00$ | $+30.00$ |

Cumulative reaches zero **exactly at end of Year 3**.

$$\boxed{PBP = 3.00 \text{ years}}$$

If the cutoff is 4 years → **Accept**.

---

## 3.5 Solved Problem 4 — Profitability Index (Capital Rationing)

**Problem:**
A firm has a **₹2 Crore** capital budget. Three projects are available:

- **Project A:** Investment = ₹1.0 Crore, PV of inflows = ₹1.40 Crore
- **Project B:** Investment = ₹0.8 Crore, PV of inflows = ₹1.10 Crore
- **Project C:** Investment = ₹1.2 Crore, PV of inflows = ₹1.50 Crore

Rank by PI and select the optimal portfolio.

**Solution:**

$$PI_{A} = \frac{1.40}{1.00} = 1.40$$

$$PI_{B} = \frac{1.10}{0.80} = 1.375$$

$$PI_{C} = \frac{1.50}{1.20} = 1.25$$

**Ranking by PI:** A (1.40) > B (1.375) > C (1.25).

**Budget selection:** A + B = ₹1.80 Crore ≤ ₹2 Crore ✓, total NPV = ₹0.40 + ₹0.30 = **₹0.70 Crore**.

A + C = ₹2.20 Crore > ₹2 Crore ✗ (exceeds budget).

$$\boxed{\text{Select Projects A + B} \quad (\text{Total NPV} = ₹0.70 \text{ Cr})}$$

---

## 3.6 Solved Problem 5 — Service Life Replacement (Incremental Analysis)

**Problem:**
Choose between two pumps with unequal lives:

- **Pump X:** First cost ₹5 Lakh, life 4 years, annual operating cost ₹1.2 Lakh, salvage ₹0.5 Lakh
- **Pump Y:** First cost ₹8 Lakh, life 6 years, annual operating cost ₹0.8 Lakh, salvage ₹0.8 Lakh

MARR = 10%. Use **NAW** method.

**Solution:**

For Pump X:

$$NAW_{X} = -5 \cdot (A/P, 10\%, 4) - 1.2 + 0.5 \cdot (A/F, 10\%, 4)$$

Compute factors:

$$(A/P, 10\%, 4) = \frac{0.10(1.10)^{4}}{(1.10)^{4} - 1} = \frac{0.10 \cdot 1.4641}{0.4641} = 0.3155$$

$$(A/F, 10\%, 4) = \frac{0.10}{0.4641} = 0.2155$$

$$NAW_{X} = -5(0.3155) - 1.2 + 0.5(0.2155) = -1.578 - 1.200 + 0.108$$

$$NAW_{X} = -₹2.670 \text{ Lakh/year}$$

For Pump Y:

$$(A/P, 10\%, 6) = \frac{0.10(1.10)^{6}}{(1.10)^{6} - 1} = \frac{0.10 \cdot 1.7716}{0.7716} = 0.2296$$

$$(A/F, 10\%, 6) = \frac{0.10}{0.7716} = 0.1296$$

$$NAW_{Y} = -8(0.2296) - 0.8 + 0.8(0.1296) = -1.837 - 0.800 + 0.104$$

$$NAW_{Y} = -₹2.533 \text{ Lakh/year}$$

Since $NAW_{Y} (-2.533) > NAW_{X} (-2.670)$ — Pump Y has a **less negative** (i.e., higher) annual cost, but both are costs so we pick the **less costly** one.

$$\boxed{\text{Select Pump Y} \quad (\text{Saves ₹0.137 Lakh/year vs. Pump X})}$$
</br>

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Cash Flow Timeline of a Typical Chemical Project

The following Mermaid diagram maps the temporal cash-flow structure of a generic chemical-industry capital project, including reinvestment cycles.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    t0["t equals 0: Initial CAPEX Outflow"]
    t1["Year 1: Operating Inflow minus O and M"]
    t2["Year 2: Operating Inflow minus O and M"]
    t3["Year 3: Operating Inflow minus O and M"]
    tm["Mid-Life: Major Turnaround Outflow"]
    tn1["Year N minus 1: Operating Inflow"]
    tn["Year N: Final Inflow plus Salvage Value plus WC Recovery"]
    tax["Annual Tax Shield from Depreciation"]

    t0 --> t1 --> t2 --> t3 --> tm --> tn1 --> tn
    tax -.-> t1
    tax -.-> t2
    tax -.-> t3
    tax -.-> tn1

    style t0 fill:#ff6b6b,color:#fff
    style tm fill:#ffa500,color:#fff
    style tn fill:#51cf66,color:#fff
    style tax fill:#339af0,color:#fff
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- **Red node (`t0`)** = Capital outflow (purchase + installation).
- **Orange node (`tm`)** = Mid-life major maintenance or environmental-compliance retrofit (caution: changes cash-flow sign and may create multiple IRRs).
- **Green node (`tn`)** = Terminal inflow including salvage, working-capital recovery, and final-year operating cash.
- **Blue nodes (tax)** = Annual tax-shield benefit from depreciation (MACRS-like in India).

---

## 4.2 Profitability Evaluation Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start["Start: Capital Investment Proposal"]
    dataGather["Gather Data: Initial Cost, Annual CF, Life, Salvage, MARR"]
    methodSelect{"Which Method? Discounted or Non-Discounted?"}
    nonDisc["Non-Discounted: PBP, ROROI"]
    disc["Discounted: NPV, IRR, PI, NAW"]
    pbCheck{"PBP less than Cutoff?"}
    npvCheck{"NPV greater than Zero?"}
    irrCheck{"IRR greater than MARR?"}
    piCheck{"PI greater than 1?"}
    accept["ACCEPT the Project"]
    reject["REJECT the Project"]
    indepth["Apply Incremental Analysis"]

    start --> dataGather --> methodSelect
    methodSelect -->|Static| nonDisc --> pbCheck
    methodSelect -->|Dynamic| disc
    disc --> npvCheck
    disc --> irrCheck
    disc --> piCheck
    pbCheck -->|Yes| accept
    pbCheck -->|No| reject
    npvCheck -->|Yes| accept
    npvCheck -->|No| reject
    irrCheck -->|Yes| accept
    irrCheck -->|No| reject
    piCheck -->|Yes| accept
    piCheck -->|No| reject
    accept --> indepth
    reject --> indepth

    style start fill:#4dabf7,color:#fff
    style accept fill:#51cf66,color:#fff
    style reject fill:#ff6b6b,color:#fff
    style methodSelect fill:#ffd43b,color:#000
    style indepth fill:#b197fc,color:#fff
```

---

## 4.3 Block Architecture: Profitability Analysis Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    blockA["Block A: Market and Technical Feasibility"]
    blockB["Block B: Capital Cost Estimation"]
    blockC["Block C: Operating Cost and Revenue Forecast"]
    blockD["Block D: Cash Flow Series Construction"]
    blockE["Block E: Profitability Metrics Computation"]
    blockF["Block F: Sensitivity and Risk Analysis"]
    blockG["Block G: Investment Decision"]

    blockA --> blockB
    blockA --> blockC
    blockB --> blockD
    blockC --> blockD
    blockD --> blockE
    blockE --> blockF
    blockF --> blockG

    style blockA fill:#74c0fc,color:#fff
    style blockB fill:#74c0fc,color:#fff
    style blockC fill:#74c0fc,color:#fff
    style blockD fill:#ffd43b,color:#000
    style blockE fill:#51cf66,color:#fff
    style blockF fill:#ffa94d,color:#fff
    style blockG fill:#ff6b6b,color:#fff
```

**Architecture commentary:**
- **Block D** is the critical data-assembly stage where the entire cash flow series $CF_0, CF_1, \ldots, CF_N$ is built.
- **Block E** is where the six formulas (NPV, IRR, PBP, PI, ROROI, NAW) are computed.
- **Block F** introduces **Monte Carlo simulation** or **scenario analysis** (KTU advanced case studies).

---

## 4.4 Risk-Sensitivity Tornado Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    base["Base Case NPV"]
    sp1["Sweep: Selling Price minus 20 percent"]
    sp2["Sweep: Selling Price plus 20 percent"]
    rm1["Sweep: Raw Material Cost plus 20 percent"]
    rm2["Sweep: Raw Material Cost minus 20 percent"]
    cap1["Sweep: CAPEX plus 20 percent"]
    vol1["Sweep: Volume minus 20 percent"]
    rank["Rank Impact on NPV: Tornado Chart"]
    decision{"Is NPV still positive in Worst Case?"}

    base --> sp1
    base --> sp2
    base --> rm1
    base --> rm2
    base --> cap1
    base --> vol1
    sp1 --> rank
    sp2 --> rank
    rm1 --> rank
    rm2 --> rank
    cap1 --> rank
    vol1 --> rank
    rank --> decision
    decision -->|Yes| go["Proceed with Risk Mitigation"]
    decision -->|No| no["Hedge or Abandon"]

    style base fill:#74c0fc,color:#fff
    style rank fill:#ffd43b,color:#000
    style decision fill:#ffa94d,color:#fff
    style go fill:#51cf66,color:#fff
    style no fill:#ff6b6b,color:#fff
```

This block-level architecture illustrates the **sensitivity analysis loop** that is a **mandatory** companion to the deterministic profitability methods in real-world chemical project appraisal.
</br>

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define Net Present Value (NPV) and state the decision rule for project acceptance.**

**Model Answer:**

Net Present Value is the algebraic sum of the present values of all cash flows (inflows minus outflows) over the entire economic life of a project, discounted to time zero using the Minimum Attractive Rate of Return (MARR).

$$NPV = \sum_{t=0}^{N} \frac{CF_{t}}{(1+i)^{t}}$$

**Decision Rule:** A project is **accepted if NPV > 0** (it generates wealth above the required return), **rejected if NPV < 0** (it destroys value), and the decision-maker is **indifferent at NPV = 0**. (3 marks)

---

### Question 2
`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**What is the Internal Rate of Return (IRR)? Why may multiple IRR solutions occur?**

**Model Answer:**

The Internal Rate of Return (IRR) is the discount rate $i^{*}$ at which the Net Present Value of a project equals zero.

$$\sum_{t=0}^{N} \frac{CF_{t}}{(1+i^{*})^{t}} = 0$$

It represents the **break-even rate of return** earned by the project. A project is accepted if IRR > MARR.

**Multiple IRR Problem:** When a project's cash flow series changes sign more than once during its life (e.g., a major mid-life retrofit cost creating an outflow sandwiched between inflows), Descartes' rule permits multiple mathematical solutions for $i^{*}$. The IRR becomes ambiguous, and the **NPV method is preferred**. (3 marks)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**(a)** A chemical company must choose between two mutually exclusive projects requiring an MARR of **15%**.

| Year | Project P Cash Flow (₹ Lakh) | Project Q Cash Flow (₹ Lakh) |
|---|---|---|
| 0 | -100 | -150 |
| 1 | 30 | 50 |
| 2 | 35 | 55 |
| 3 | 40 | 60 |
| 4 | 45 | 65 |
| 5 | 30 + 10 (salvage) = 40 | 30 |

**Compute the NPV of each project and recommend the better option.** (7 marks)

**(b)** If the projects have **unequal lives** (Project P = 5 years, Project Q = 7 years) with Q receiving a salvage of ₹20 Lakh in year 7, recalculate using the **Net Annual Worth (NAW)** method. MARR remains 15%. (7 marks)

---

**Model Solution to (a):**

**Step 1: Discount factors at 15%.**

| Year $t$ | $(1.15)^{t}$ | $(1.15)^{-t}$ |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 1.1500 | 0.8696 |
| 2 | 1.3225 | 0.7561 |
| 3 | 1.5209 | 0.6575 |
| 4 | 1.7490 | 0.5718 |
| 5 | 2.0114 | 0.4972 |

**Step 2: NPV of Project P.**

$$NPV_{P} = -100 + \frac{30}{1.15} + \frac{35}{1.15^{2}} + \frac{40}{1.15^{3}} + \frac{45}{1.15^{4}} + \frac{40}{1.15^{5}}$$

$$NPV_{P} = -100 + 26.087 + 26.464 + 26.301 + 25.729 + 19.887$$

$$NPV_{P} = +24.47 \text{ Lakh}$$

**Step 3: NPV of Project Q.**

$$NPV_{Q} = -150 + \frac{50}{1.15} + \frac{55}{1.15^{2}} + \frac{60}{1.15^{3}} + \frac{65}{1.15^{4}} + \frac{30}{1.15^{5}}$$

$$NPV_{Q} = -150 + 43.478 + 41.587 + 39.451 + 37.164 + 14.915$$

$$NPV_{Q} = +26.60 \text{ Lakh}$$

**Step 4: Decision.**

$$\boxed{NPV_{Q} = ₹26.60 \text{ Lakh} > NPV_{P} = ₹24.47 \text{ Lakh} \;\Rightarrow\; \text{Select Project Q}}$$

---

**Model Solution to (b):**

The NAW method converts each NPV into an equivalent uniform annual cash flow using the capital recovery factor $(A/P, i, N)$.

**Step 1: Recompute NPV of Q with revised cash flows (Years 1–6 as before, Year 7 with ₹30 + ₹20 salvage = ₹50).**

For Project P (life 5 years):

$$NAW_{P} = NPV_{P} \cdot (A/P, 15\%, 5)$$

$$(A/P, 15\%, 5) = \frac{0.15(1.15)^{5}}{(1.15)^{5} - 1} = \frac{0.15 \cdot 2.0114}{1.0114} = 0.2983$$

$$NAW_{P} = 24.47 \cdot 0.2983 = +7.30 \text{ Lakh/year}$$

For Project Q (life 7 years):

First compute the new NPV:

$$NPV_{Q,7} = -150 + 43.478 + 41.587 + 39.451 + 37.164 + \frac{30}{1.15^{5}} + \frac{50}{1.15^{7}}$$

$(1.15)^{6} = 2.3131$ → $(1.15)^{-6} = 0.4323$
$(1.15)^{7} = 2.6600$ → $(1.15)^{-7} = 0.3759$

$$NPV_{Q,7} = -150 + 43.478 + 41.587 + 39.451 + 37.164 + 14.915 + 18.797$$

$$NPV_{Q,7} = +45.39 \text{ Lakh}$$

Now the NAW:

$$(A/P, 15\%, 7) = \frac{0.15 \cdot 2.6600}{2.6600 - 1} = \frac{0.3990}{1.6600} = 0.2404$$

$$NAW_{Q} = 45.39 \cdot 0.2404 = +10.91 \text{ Lakh/year}$$

**Step 2: Decision (unequal lives).**

$$\boxed{NAW_{Q} = ₹10.91 \text{ Lakh/year} > NAW_{P} = ₹7.30 \text{ Lakh/year} \;\Rightarrow\; \text{Select Project Q}}$$

When unequal lives are present, NAW (or the **repeatability assumption** with LCM of lives) is the correct method. Direct NPV comparison is **inadmissible** in this case.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

`[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**(a)** A fertilizer plant invests **₹80 Lakh** in a bag-filter system. The estimated end-of-year net savings (after-tax cash flows) are:

- Year 1: ₹18 Lakh
- Year 2: ₹22 Lakh
- Year 3: ₹25 Lakh
- Year 4: ₹20 Lakh (with additional ₹10 Lakh environmental compliance bonus in Year 4)
- Year 5: ₹15 Lakh

**Compute the IRR using the trial-and-error interpolation method.** MARR is 14%. (7 marks)

**(b)** Calculate the **Profitability Index (PI)** and **Discounted Payback Period (DPBP)** at 14%. Comment on whether the project should be accepted. (7 marks)

---

**Model Solution to (a):**

**Step 1: Set up the cash flow series.**

| Year | Cash Flow (₹ Lakh) |
|---|---|
| 0 | -80 |
| 1 | +18 |
| 2 | +22 |
| 3 | +25 |
| 4 | +20 + 10 = +30 |
| 5 | +15 |

**Step 2: Compute NPV at two trial rates bracketing zero.**

At **12%:**

$$(1.12)^{1} = 1.12, \quad (1.12)^{2} = 1.2544, \quad (1.12)^{3} = 1.4049$$
$$(1.12)^{4} = 1.5735, \quad (1.12)^{5} = 1.7623$$

$$NPV_{12} = -80 + \frac{18}{1.12} + \frac{22}{1.2544} + \frac{25}{1.4049} + \frac{30}{1.5735} + \frac{15}{1.7623}$$

$$NPV_{12} = -80 + 16.071 + 17.539 + 17.795 + 19.066 + 8.512$$

$$NPV_{12} = -0.017 \approx 0 \text{ (very close to zero)}$$

At **13%:**

$$(1.13)^{1} = 1.13, \quad (1.13)^{2} = 1.2769, \quad (1.13)^{3} = 1.4429$$
$$(1.13)^{4} = 1.6305, \quad (1.13)^{5} = 1.8424$$

$$NPV_{13} = -80 + \frac{18}{1.13} + \frac{22}{1.2769} + \frac{25}{1.4429} + \frac{30}{1.6305} + \frac{15}{1.8424}$$

$$NPV_{13} = -80 + 15.929 + 17.229 + 17.327 + 18.399 + 8.141$$

$$NPV_{13} = -2.975 \text{ Lakh}$$

**Step 3: Interpolate.**

$$i^{*} = 12\% + \frac{NPV_{12}}{NPV_{12} - NPV_{13}} \cdot (13\% - 12\%)$$

$$i^{*} = 12\% + \frac{-0.017}{-0.017 - (-2.975)} \cdot 1\%$$

$$i^{*} = 12\% + \frac{-0.017}{2.958} \cdot 1\%$$

$$i^{*} = 12\% - 0.006\%$$

$$\boxed{i^{*} \approx 11.99\% \approx 12.0\%}$$

**Step 4: Decision.**

Since IRR (12.0%) < MARR (14%), the project should be **REJECTED** on a stand-alone IRR basis.

---

**Model Solution to (b):**

**Step 1: Discounted cash flows at 14%.**

$$(1.14)^{1} = 1.14, \quad (1.14)^{2} = 1.2996, \quad (1.14)^{3} = 1.4815$$
$$(1.14)^{4} = 1.6890, \quad (1.14)^{5} = 1.9254$$

| Year | CF (₹ Lakh) | $(1.14)^{-t}$ | $PV_t$ (₹ Lakh) | Cumulative $PV$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | -80 | 1.0000 | -80.000 | -80.000 |
| 1 | 18 | 0.8772 | 15.789 | -64.211 |
| 2 | 22 | 0.7695 | 16.929 | -47.282 |
| 3 | 25 | 0.6750 | 16.875 | -30.407 |
| 4 | 30 | 0.5921 | 17.763 | -12.644 |
| 5 | 15 | 0.5194 | 7.791 | -4.853 |

NPV at 14% = **-₹4.85 Lakh** (negative, confirming rejection).

**Step 2: Profitability Index.**

$$PI = \frac{PV(\text{Inflows})}{|\text{Initial Investment}|} = \frac{80 - 4.853}{80} = \frac{75.147}{80}$$

$$\boxed{PI = 0.939 < 1 \;\Rightarrow\; \text{REJECT}}$$

(Equivalently $PI = 1 + NPV/\text{Investment} = 1 - 4.85/80 = 0.939$.)

**Step 3: Discounted Payback Period.**

Cumulative discounted CF reaches zero between Year 4 (-12.644) and Year 5 (-4.853). It has **not** recovered by end of Year 5.

$$\boxed{DPBP > 5 \text{ years} \;\Rightarrow\; \text{REJECT (if cutoff is 4 years)}}$$

**Step 4: Comment.**

All three discounted methods (NPV, PI, DPBP) consistently indicate **rejection**, validating the IRR result. The project is **not acceptable** at MARR = 14%.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where KTU Students Lose 2–4 Marks Per Question**
>
> 1. **Sign error on initial investment:** Forgetting to enter $-$ before the first cost in NPV summation. Always write $CF_0$ as a negative value.
>
> 2. **Using undiscounted PBP formula for non-uniform cash flows:** PBP = Investment/Annual CF works **only** when cash flows are uniform. For variable flows, draw the cumulative table and interpolate.
>
> 3. **Comparing NPVs of unequal-life projects directly:** This is **economically wrong** when lives differ. Use the **LCM-of-lives repeatability assumption** or **NAW** method.
>
> 4. **IRR interpolation formula sign error:** Students often misplace $NPV_{L} - NPV_{H}$. The correct denominator in the interpolation formula is $(NPV_{L} - NPV_{H})$ where $L$ = lower rate and $H$ = higher rate.
>
> 5. **Forgetting salvage value + working capital recovery at the end of project life** in Year N. These are real cash inflows and must be added to the terminal-year operating cash flow.
>
> 6. **Confusing MARR with IRR decision rule:** MARR is the *hurdle* (input); IRR is the *achieved return* (output). Accept iff IRR > MARR.
>
> 7. **Tax shield from depreciation omitted:** In India, depreciation under the Companies Act provides a tax shield that **reduces** after-tax cash outflows. Many KTU problems include this; omitting it loses 1–2 marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Module 3 — Mathematical Methods for Profitability Evaluation**
>
> **Foundations**
> - The time value of money demands **discounting** at MARR (or WACC).
> - The six engineering economy factors $(P/F, F/P, P/A, A/P, F/A, A/F)$ must be memorized with formulas.
>
> **Non-Discounted Methods**
> - **PBP** = time to recover investment; ignores time value of money.
> - **ROROI** = (Average annual profit / Investment) × 100.
>
> **Discounted Methods (the gold standard)**
> - **NPV** > 0 → Accept. NPV is the most reliable method.
> - **IRR** > MARR → Accept. Beware of multiple-IRR and sign-changing cash flow series.
> - **PI** > 1 → Accept. Useful for **capital rationing**.
> - **NAW** > 0 → Accept. Best for **unequal-life** comparisons.
> - **DPBP** < cutoff → Accept. Modified PBP that recognizes time value.
>
> **Comparison Principles**
> - For **mutually exclusive** projects with **equal lives**, choose the one with **higher NPV**.
> - For **mutually exclusive** projects with **unequal lives**, use **NAW** or the **LCM repeatability** method.
> - For **capital rationing**, use **PI** to rank and select within budget.
> - Always do **incremental analysis** (Project with higher investment – Project with lower investment) when initial costs differ.
>
> **Practical Indian Context**
> - Indian corporate tax rate for chemical firms: **~25.17%**.
> - Depreciation methods: **Written Down Value (WDV)** under Companies Act; **Straight Line** for some asset classes.
> - MARR for Indian chemical projects: typically **12%–18%** depending on risk.
> - Always include **working capital** (typically 10%–20% of fixed capital) at start and recover it at end.
> - **Salvage value** is real money at the end of life.
>
> **Numerical Discipline**
> - Show **all** discount-factor calculations in tabular form.
> - State your **decision rule** explicitly.
> - Round to **two decimal places** for rates and **whole units** for cash flows (or as the problem specifies).
> - Verify with **at least two methods** (e.g., NPV + IRR should give the same accept/reject decision in the absence of multiple-IRR issues).
>
> **Exam-Edge Wisdom**
> - NPV and IRR may give conflicting rankings for **mutually exclusive projects of different scale** — always do **incremental ROR analysis** on the (Large – Small) cash flow increment.
> - The **incremental IRR** is what you compare to MARR, not the standalone IRRs.
> - For **replacement analysis**, the defender's "current market value" is the **opportunity cost** (sunk cost fallacy to ignore it).
</br>

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Payout Time without Interest
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT757 - ECONOMICS AND MANAGEMENT OF CHEMICAL INDUSTRIES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Payout Time without Interest

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Payout Time without Interest (Simple Payback Period)**
> Payout time without interest, also known as the **Simple Payback Period** or **Undiscounted Payback Method**, is a capital investment evaluation technique that determines the exact length of time (usually expressed in **years** or **months**) required for the gross cash inflows generated by a project to completely recover the original capital investment, *without* taking into account the time value of money (i.e., no interest, no discounting).

In formal KTU 2024 Scheme notation, if $I$ represents the initial capital investment and $C_n$ represents the net annual cash inflow in year $n$, the payout time $T$ satisfies the following relationship:

$$\sum_{n=1}^{T} C_n \;\geq\; I$$

The point at which cumulative cash inflows equal the initial investment defines the payout period.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **"The EMI-Free Loan Recovery Analogy"**
> Imagine your friend borrows **₹1,00,000** from you to start a small business, and you both agree that there will be **no interest charged** — just a flat repayment. If your friend pays you back **₹25,000 every year**, the time required to recover your full ₹1,00,000 is exactly **4 years**. That "4 years" is the *Payout Time without Interest*.

In a chemical plant context: You invest ₹50 crores in a new distillation column. The column generates an annual net cash profit of ₹10 crores (after operating costs but before any interest charges). The payout time is **5 years**. You are "paid back" your original ₹50 crores in 5 years.

## 1.3 Why is it Used in Chemical Industries?

- **Simplicity**: It is the most elementary and easily understood method of investment appraisal.
- **Liquidity Focus**: It directly answers the question *"How quickly can I get my money back?"* — a critical concern for chemical companies operating with high capital intensity.
- **Risk Screening Tool**: Projects with shorter payout times are inherently less risky.
- **First-Pass Filter**: It is often used as a preliminary screening tool before applying more sophisticated methods like NPV or IRR.

## 1.4 Key Terminology & Metrics

| Term | Symbol | Meaning | Standard Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Initial Investment | $I$ | Total capital outlay at the start of the project | **₹, $, €** |
| Annual Cash Inflow | $C$ | Net yearly earnings (revenue − operating cost) | **₹/year** |
| Service Life | $n$ | Useful economic life of the asset | **years** |
| Payout Time | $T$ | Time to recover $I$ | **years** |
| Salvage Value | $S$ | Residual worth at end of life | **₹** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step Decision Flow

The un-discounted payout time method follows this decision hierarchy:

1. **Identify the Initial Investment ($I$):** Determine the total committed capital — fixed capital (equipment, installation) + working capital.
2. **Estimate Annual Cash Inflows ($C$):** Compute net annual earnings by subtracting operating costs, raw material costs, labor, utilities, and maintenance from gross revenue.
3. **Check Uniformity:** Determine whether cash inflows are **constant** or **variable** year-on-year.
4. **Apply the Appropriate Formula:**
   - Uniform case: $T = I / C$
   - Variable case: Cumulative sum until it crosses $I$.
5. **Compare with Benchmark / Cutoff Period:** If $T \leq T_{\text{acceptable}}$, accept the project; otherwise, reject.
6. **Sensitivity Check:** Even after acceptance, the manager must remember this method **ignores the time value of money** and earnings beyond the payout period.

## 2.2 Underlying Assumptions

> [!WARNING]
> The Payout Time without Interest method rests on several simplifying assumptions that students must explicitly state in KTU answers:
> 1. **Equal Weight for All Cash Flows**: A rupee earned in Year 1 is treated as identical to a rupee earned in Year 10.
> 2. **Cash Flow Visibility is Limited to Payback**: Earnings after the payout period are not considered in the decision.
> 3. **Cash Flows are Predictable**: Future inflows must be estimated with reasonable certainty.
> 4. **No Reinvestment Concerns**: The method does not specify a reinvestment rate for intermediate cash flows.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| S.No. | Scenario | Governing Equation | Boundary Condition |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Uniform cash inflows, no salvage | $T = \dfrac{I}{C}$ | $C > 0$ |
| 2 | Uniform cash inflows, with salvage $S$ | $T = \dfrac{I - S}{C}$ | $S < I$ |
| 3 | Uniform cash inflows over service life $n$ | Compare $T$ with $n$ | Accept if $T \le n$ |
| 4 | Non-uniform cash flows | $\sum_{n=1}^{T} C_n \ge I$ with $T$ found by interpolation | Discrete annual data |
| 5 | Exact fractional year (variable cash flow) | $T = (Y-1) + \dfrac{R_{Y-1}}{C_Y}$ | Where $R_{Y-1}$ is unrecovered balance at end of year $Y-1$ |
| 6 | Minimum acceptable criterion | $T_{\text{actual}} \le T_{\text{cut-off}}$ | Manager-defined |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Distinction for KTU Answers:** The *Payout Time without Interest* uses **nominal (undiscounted) cash flows**. The next higher-level method — *Payout Time with Interest* — applies discounting via present worth factors. Do **NOT** confuse the two in exams.

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Chemical Engineering

| Application Domain | Practical Use |
|---|---|
| **Plant Expansion** | Quickly screens whether a new reactor pays for itself before its mechanical life ends. |
| **Energy Recovery Projects** | Evaluates heat-exchanger network retrofits based on years to recover the retrofit cost. |
| **Pollution Control Equipment** | Helps justify scrubbers, ESPs, and effluent treatment plants to management. |
| **Process Automation** | Justifies DCS / PLC upgrades by computing recovery from reduced labor costs. |
| **Catalyst Selection** | Premium catalysts are evaluated on how quickly their higher cost is recovered through yield improvement. |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Uniform Cash Inflows (No Salvage Value)

**Starting Premise:** An investor commits capital $I$ at time $t = 0$. The project generates a constant positive cash flow $C$ at the end of each year for an indefinite number of years. We wish to find the time $T$ at which cumulative cash inflows exactly equal the initial investment.

**Step 1 — Write the cumulative cash inflow function.**

After $k$ years, the total cash recovered from the project is the sum of $k$ identical annual payments of $C$:

$$C_{\text{cum}}(k) = C + C + C + \ldots + C \; (k \text{ times}) = k \cdot C$$

**Step 2 — Set the recovery condition equal to the investment.**

By the definition of payout time, we require the cumulative inflow to equal (or just exceed) the initial outlay:

$$k \cdot C = I$$

**Step 3 — Solve algebraically for $k$ (which is the payout time $T$).**

Divide both sides by $C$:

$$\frac{k \cdot C}{C} = \frac{I}{C}$$

Therefore:

$$T = k = \frac{I}{C}$$

This is the **canonical form** of the un-discounted payout time formula for uniform cash flows.

**Step 4 — Dimensional verification.**

$$\frac{[\text{₹}]}{[\text{₹/year}]} = [\text{year}] \quad \checkmark$$

The units collapse correctly into years, confirming dimensional consistency.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Uniform Cash Inflows WITH Salvage Value

**Starting Premise:** At the end of the project's service life, the equipment retains a residual salvage value $S$ that can be liquidated. The cash recovered by the end of year $T$ is therefore the sum of operating cash flows plus the salvage realization:

$$C_{\text{cum}}(T) = T \cdot C + S$$

**Step 1 — Equate to initial investment.**

$$T \cdot C + S = I$$

**Step 2 — Isolate the term containing $T$.**

$$T \cdot C = I - S$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $T$.**

$$T = \frac{I - S}{C}$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation.**

The salvage value *reduces* the unrecovered capital that must be earned back through operations, thereby *shortening* the payout period.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Non-Uniform (Variable) Cash Inflows

**Starting Premise:** When annual cash inflows fluctuate — common in chemical projects due to ramp-up periods, maintenance shutdowns, or seasonal demand — a closed-form algebraic formula is not possible. The standard approach is **cumulative tabulation with linear interpolation**.

**Step 1 — Construct a cumulative table.**

Compute running totals of $C_n$ for each year $n$ until the cumulative total reaches or exceeds $I$.

**Step 2 — Identify the crossover year.**

Let $Y$ be the smallest integer such that:

$$\sum_{n=1}^{Y} C_n \ge I$$

**Step 3 — Compute the unrecovered balance at the end of year $(Y-1)$.**

$$R_{Y-1} = I - \sum_{n=1}^{Y-1} C_n$$
