# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]**

> **Q:** Define **Net Present Value (NPV)**. State the decision rule for accepting or rejecting a project on the basis of NPV.

**Model Answer:**

> Net Present Value is the algebraic sum of the present values of all cash flows (both inflows and outflows) associated with a capital investment, discounted at an appropriate minimum attractive rate of return (MARR). Mathematically:

$$NPV = \sum_{t=0}^{n} \frac{CF_t}{(1+i)^t}$$

> **Decision Rule:**  
> (i) If $NPV > 0$, **accept** the project.  
> (ii) If $NPV = 0$, the firm is **indifferent** (the project earns exactly the MARR).  
> (iii) If $NPV < 0$, **reject** the project.

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Retest]**

> **Q:** Distinguish between **Net Present Value (NPV)** and **Profitability Index (PI)**. Why might a firm prefer PI over NPV in a capital-rationed situation?

**Model Answer:**

> NPV is the **absolute** monetary value created by a project (in ₹), while PI is a **relative** dimensionless ratio.

$$\text{PI} = \frac{\text{PV of future cash inflows}}{\text{Initial investment}} = 1 + \frac{\text{NPV}}{C_0}$$

> A firm with a **fixed capital budget** (capital rationing) cannot undertake every positive-NPV project. PI ranks projects by "value created per rupee invested", allowing optimal allocation of the limited capital. In contrast, NPV only tells the firm the *total* value added and may lead to sub-optimal allocation when the budget is binding.

## 5.2 Part B Question A (14 Marks) — Choice 1

**[KTU University Exam – July 2024]**

> **Question A (14 Marks):**  
> A chemical company is evaluating two alternative **mutually exclusive** proposals for a new agrochemical formulation plant.  
>  
> **Project X:** Initial investment = **₹ 80 lakhs**, expected net cash inflow = **₹ 22 lakhs per year for 6 years**, salvage value at end of year 6 = **₹ 5 lakhs**.  
>  
> **Project Y:** Initial investment = **₹ 1.2 crore**, expected net cash inflow = **₹ 32 lakhs per year for 6 years**, salvage value at end of year 6 = **₹ 8 lakhs**.  
>  
> The MARR is **14 % per annum**.  
> (a) Compute the NPV of each project. (7 marks)  
> (b) Which project should be selected? Justify using NPV criteria. (7 marks)

### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 Marks)

> **Step 1 — Identify the discount rate and useful life.**  
> $i = 0.14$, $n = 6$ years (both projects have equal life, so direct NPV comparison is valid).

> **Step 2 — Compute the Present Worth Factor $(P/A, 14\%, 6)$.**

$$(P/A, 14\%, 6) = \frac{(1.14)^6 - 1}{0.14 (1.14)^6}$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
(1.14)^6 &= 2.1949 \\
(1.14)^6 - 1 &= 1.1949 \\
0.14 \times 2.1949 &= 0.3073 \\
(P/A, 14\%, 6) &= \frac{1.1949}{0.3073} = 3.8887
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Step 3 — Compute $(P/F, 14\%, 6)$ for salvage value.**

$$(P/F, 14\%, 6) = \frac{1}{(1.14)^6} = \frac{1}{2.1949} = 0.4556$$

> **Step 4 — Compute NPV of Project X.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
NPV_X &= -C_0 + A_X \cdot (P/A, 14\%, 6) + S_X \cdot (P/F, 14\%, 6) \\
      &= -80 + 22 \times 3.8887 + 5 \times 0.4556 \\
      &= -80 + 85.55 + 2.28 \\
      &= +7.83 \text{ lakhs}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Step 5 — Compute NPV of Project Y.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
NPV_Y &= -120 + 32 \times 3.8887 + 8 \times 0.4556 \\
      &= -120 + 124.44 + 3.64 \\
      &= +8.08 \text{ lakhs}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 Marks)

> **Step 6 — Apply the decision rule.**  
> Both projects have **positive NPV** at 14 % MARR, so both are individually acceptable on a stand-alone basis.  
>  
> **Step 7 — Select the higher NPV for mutually exclusive alternatives.**  
> $NPV_Y = 8.08$ lakhs $> NPV_X = 7.83$ lakhs $\Rightarrow$ **Select Project Y.**  
>  
> **Step 8 — Important qualification.**  
> Since both projects have **equal lives (6 years)**, the direct NPV comparison is valid. If the lives had been unequal, the **Equivalent Uniform Annual Worth (EUAW)** method would have been required to ensure a fair comparison. This is a KTU-favoured follow-up question.

## 5.3 Part B Question B (14 Marks) — Choice 2

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

> **Question B (14 Marks):**  
> A polymer manufacturing company is considering the installation of a **continuous stirred-tank reactor (CSTR)** to replace its existing batch reactor. The relevant data are:  
>  
> - Initial installed cost of the CSTR system = **₹ 1,00,00,000**  
> - Additional working capital required at $t = 0$ = **₹ 10,00,000**  
> - Annual after-tax net cash savings (lower operating cost + better yield) = **₹ 25,00,000 per year** for years 1 through 7  
> - At the end of year 7, the reactor can be sold for **₹ 15,00,000**, and the working capital is **fully recovered**  
> - The corporate cost of capital is **10 % per annum**  
>  
> (a) Set up the cash-flow table and compute the NPV. (7 marks)  
> (b) The company has an alternate, simpler batch upgrade with NPV = **₹ 12,00,000** and PI = **1.10**. Comment on which option is preferable using both NPV and PI. (7 marks)

### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 Marks)

> **Step 1 — Cash flow tabulation.**

| Year ($t$) | Cash Flow Description | Amount (₹) |
|---|---|---|
| 0 | Initial CSTR cost | $-1{,}00{,}00{,}000$ |
| 0 | Working capital | $-10{,}00{,}000$ |
| 1–7 | Annual net cash savings | $+25{,}00{,}000$ each year |
| 7 | Salvage value | $+15{,}00{,}000$ |
| 7 | Working capital recovered | $+10{,}00{,}000$ |

> **Step 2 — Net cash flow at $t = 0$.**

$$CF_0 = -(1{,}00{,}00{,}000 + 10{,}00{,}000) = -1{,}10{,}00{,}000$$

> **Step 3 — Net cash flow at $t = 7$.**

$$CF_7 = 25{,}00{,}000 + 15{,}00{,}000 + 10{,}00{,}000 = 50{,}00{,}000$$

> **Step 4 — Cash flows at $t = 1$ to $6$ are constant:** $CF_t = 25{,}00{,}000$.

> **Step 5 — Required factors at $i = 10\%$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
(P/A, 10\%, 7) &= \frac{(1.10)^7 - 1}{0.10 (1.10)^7} = \frac{1.9487}{0.2149} = 4.8684 \\
(P/F, 10\%, 7) &= \frac{1}{(1.10)^7} = 0.5132
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Step 6 — NPV calculation.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
NPV_{\text{CSTR}} &= -1{,}10{,}00{,}000 + \sum_{t=1}^{6} \frac{25{,}00{,}000}{(1.10)^t} + \frac{50{,}00{,}000}{(1.10)^7} \\
                  &= -1{,}10{,}00{,}000 + 25{,}00{,}000 \times (P/A, 10\%, 7) - 25{,}00{,}000 \times (P/F, 10\%, 7) + 50{,}00{,}000 \times (P/F, 10\%, 7) \\
                  &= -1{,}10{,}00{,}000 + 25{,}00{,}000 \times 4.8684 + 25{,}00{,}000 \times 0.5132 \\
                  &= -1{,}10{,}00{,}000 + 1{,}21{,}71{,}000 + 12{,}83{,}000 \\
                  &= 24{,}54{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Alternative simpler formulation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
NPV_{\text{CSTR}} &= -1{,}10{,}00{,}000 + 25{,}00{,}000 \times 4.8684 + 25{,}00{,}000 \times 0.5132 \\
                  &= -1{,}10{,}00{,}000 + 1{,}21{,}71{,}000 + 12{,}83{,}000 \\
                  &= +24{,}54{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 Marks)

> **Step 7 — Compute PI of the CSTR project.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
PI_{\text{CSTR}} &= \frac{PV(\text{future cash inflows})}{C_0} \\
                 &= \frac{1{,}34{,}54{,}000}{1{,}10{,}00{,}000} = 1.223
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Step 8 — Compare with the batch upgrade.**

| Metric | CSTR (New Project) | Batch Upgrade |
|---|---|---|
| NPV | ₹ 24,54,000 | ₹ 12,00,000 |
| PI | 1.223 | 1.10 |

> **Step 9 — Decision and justification.**  
> The CSTR has a **higher NPV** (₹ 24,54,000 vs ₹ 12,00,000) and a **higher PI** (1.223 vs 1.10). Both criteria consistently favour the **CSTR**. Since capital is *not* rationed in this problem (the problem does not specify a budget cap), NPV is the decisive metric, and the CSTR is the preferred investment. The higher PI is a *confirmation*, not the primary decision driver.

> **Additional KTU-style remark:** Had the projects been **independent** (not mutually exclusive) and capital was unlimited, both would be accepted. Capital rationing reverses the priority logic.

## 5.4 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]  
> **Where KTU students most commonly lose marks in NPV problems:**
> 
> 1. **Sign Convention Errors:** Forgetting to enter the initial investment as a *negative* cash flow at $t = 0$. A common mistake is writing $NPV = \sum CF_t / (1+i)^t$ without the negative sign, leading to a wildly inflated (and wrong) positive NPV.
> 2. **Salvage and Working Capital Omission:** Treating the salvage value as part of the annual cash flow stream, or omitting the working capital recovery at $t = n$. KTU awards 1–2 marks specifically for correctly handling these terminal cash flows.
> 3. **End-of-Period Assumption:** Assuming a cash flow occurs at the *beginning* of a year when it actually occurs at the *end*. This shifts the discount factor and changes the answer.
> 4. **Mutually Exclusive Comparison Using IRR:** Choosing a project with higher IRR but lower NPV. The KTU 2024 rubric specifically states: *"For mutually exclusive projects, NPV is the primary selection criterion."* Choosing IRR over NPV in such cases will cost 2–3 marks.
> 5. **Unequal Lives:** Comparing two projects with different economic lives using raw NPV. KTU requires the **Equivalent Uniform Annual Worth (EUAW)** or the **Least Common Multiple (LCM)** method. Forgetting this is a guaranteed 1-mark deduction.
> 6. **Using the Wrong Discount Rate:** Mixing up the *real* and *nominal* rates. Always check whether the problem gives an inflation rate; if so, the MARR must be adjusted: $i_{\text{real}} = \frac{1 + i_{\text{nominal}}}{1 + f} - 1$.
> 7. **Not Reporting Units:** Writing a final NPV like "7.83" without specifying "₹ 7.83 lakhs" will typically cost 0.5 mark in the presentation rubric.

## 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Net Present Value**
> 
> - **Definition (verbatim for KTU):** NPV is the algebraic sum of the present values of all cash inflows and outflows, discounted at the MARR, over the project's economic life. Equation: $NPV = \sum_{t=0}^{n} \dfrac{CF_t}{(1+i)^t}$.
> - **Time Value of Money (TVM):** A rupee today is worth more than a rupee tomorrow. Discounting collapses all future cash flows to a common present-day metric.
> - **Discount Rate (i):** Also called MARR or cost of capital. Typical KTU range: **8 % to 15 %**. Read the problem statement carefully — it may be given as 12 % per annum or 1 % per month (these are not equivalent!).
> - **Decision Rule:** $NPV > 0$ → Accept. $NPV = 0$ → Indifferent. $NPV < 0$ → Reject.
> - **Mutually Exclusive Choice:** Always select the **higher NPV**, not the higher IRR.
> - **Capital Rationing:** Use the **Profitability Index (PI)** to rank projects.
> - **Cash Flow Components:** Initial investment, annual after-tax operating cash flows, salvage value, and working-capital recovery. Each must be discounted to its present value.
> - **Useful Shortcuts:**  
>   • Uniform series → $(P/A, i, n)$ factor.  
>   • Single future amount → $(P/F, i, n)$ factor.  
>   • Unequal lives → Use **EUAW** or **LCM chain replacement** method.
> - **Sign Convention:** Outflows negative, inflows positive. Initial investment = $-C_0$.
> - **After-Tax Treatment:** $CF_{\text{net}} = (R - E)(1 - \tau) + D \cdot \tau$, where $D$ is depreciation and $\tau$ is the tax rate.
> - **Units Consistency:** Keep all cash flows in the *same* monetary unit (lakhs or crores) throughout the calculation.
> - **KTU-typical plant-life:** **5 to 10 years** for chemical process equipment; **15 to 25 years** for grassroots chemical plants.
> - **Final sanity check:** If $NPV > 0$, the project's IRR must exceed the MARR. If $NPV < 0$, the project's IRR is below the MARR. Use this to cross-verify answers.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Net Present Value Index
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT757 - ECONOMICS AND MANAGEMENT OF CHEMICAL INDUSTRIES
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Net Present Value Index

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Net Present Value Index (NPVI)**, also widely referred to as the **Profitability Index (PI)** or **Benefit-Cost Ratio (BCR)** in classical engineering economics, is a dimensionless capital budgeting metric that quantifies the *relative* worth of a proposed investment project. It is formally defined as the ratio of the **Present Value (PV) of all expected future net cash inflows** generated by a project over its useful life to the **Initial Investment (Cash Outflow at time zero)**.

Mathematically, the decision criterion is expressed as:

$$PI = \frac{\text{Present Value of Future Cash Inflows}}{\text{Initial Investment}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The Net Present Value Index is essentially a *normalized* form of the Net Present Value (NPV). Where NPV gives the absolute rupee gain, the NPVI gives the *gain per rupee invested*, making it invaluable when comparing projects of **disparate capital scales** — a frequent scenario in chemical plant expansion decisions.

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you are a chemical engineer-turned-investor evaluating two project proposals for a new plant:

- **Project A:** Build a small polypropylene unit requiring **₹10 Lakhs** investment.
- **Project B:** Build a massive petrochemical complex requiring **₹10 Crores** investment.

You have limited capital and must choose. Looking at **Net Present Value (NPV)** alone might be misleading if Project B has a higher absolute NPV simply because of its sheer size. Now, the **Net Present Value Index** acts like an **"efficiency score"** — it tells you, *"For every ₹1 you put in, how many rupees of present-value cash flow do you get back?"*

- If Project A returns ₹1.30 for every ₹1 invested → **PI = 1.30** (30% efficiency).
- If Project B returns ₹1.10 for every ₹1 invested → **PI = 1.10** (10% efficiency).

Even if Project B makes more *total* money, Project A is the **more efficient** use of capital. The NPVI captures this efficiency — it is the **"miles per gallon"** metric of capital investment.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** A PI > 1.0 means the project earns *more* than the required rate of return (MARR). A PI < 1.0 means the project fails to recover its capital cost even in present-value terms. A PI = 1.0 is the **break-even indifference point**.

---

## 1.3 Physical & Economic Constants Reference

The following standard financial benchmarks are used throughout NPVI calculations in KTU examinations:

| Parameter | Standard Symbol | Typical Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Minimum Acceptable Rate of Return | **MARR** or **i** | **10% – 15%** (industrial projects) |
| Present Worth Factor (Single Payment) | **(P/F, i, n)** | $1/(1+i)^n$ |
| Present Worth Factor (Uniform Series) | **(P/A, i, n)** | $\frac{(1+i)^n - 1}{i(1+i)^n}$ |
| Project Economic Life | **n** | **5 – 20 years** (chemical plants) |
| Salvage Value | **SV** | Often 0 or book value |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Metric Convention in KTU:** All cash flows must be discounted at the **same MARR** used for NPV calculations to ensure consistency. Mixing discount rates between methods will result in **valuation penalties** in KTU answer scripts.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — The Logic Behind NPVI

The conceptual basis of NPVI rests on three pillars of financial decision theory:

### Pillar 1: Time Value of Money (TVM)
A rupee received today is worth more than a rupee received next year. All future cash inflows must be **discounted** to the present (time zero) before comparison.

### Pillar 2: Relative vs. Absolute Measurement
NPV is an *absolute* measure (in rupees). NPVI is a *relative* measure (dimensionless ratio). When capital is **rationed** (limited funds available), relative measures are mathematically superior because they identify the project that yields the **highest return per unit of scarce capital**.

### Pillar 3: Capital Rationing Logic
In real chemical industries, companies rarely have unlimited budgets. The **Net Present Value Index** solves the classic "limited budget, multiple projects" dilemma by ranking projects on a **per-rupee-of-investment** basis.

---

## 2.2 The Operational Decision Algorithm

To evaluate any project using the Net Present Value Index, the engineer must execute the following logic sequence:

**Step 1 — Cash Flow Tabulation:** Identify all expected net annual cash inflows $C_1, C_2, \ldots, C_n$ and the initial capital outlay $C_0$ (at time $t = 0$).

**Step 2 — Discounting at MARR:** Apply the chosen MARR ($i$) to discount each cash flow back to present value using $(P/F, i, n) = (1+i)^{-n}$.

**Step 3 — Summation of Present Values:** Sum the discounted cash flows:
$$PV_{inflows} = \sum_{t=1}^{n} \frac{C_t}{(1+i)^t}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the Index:** Divide the summed PV of inflows by the initial investment.

**Step 5 — Apply Decision Rule:**
- If $PI \geq 1.0$ → **Accept** (project earns at least the MARR).
- If $PI < 1.0$ → **Reject** (project destroys shareholder value).

**Step 6 — Capital Rationing Tie-Breaker:** When multiple acceptable projects compete for limited capital, rank them in **descending order of PI** and select projects sequentially until the capital budget is exhausted.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet — High-Yield Equation Bank

| # | Formula / Identity | Mathematical Form | KTU Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Basic NPVI Formula** | $PI = \dfrac{\sum_{t=1}^{n} C_t (1+i)^{-t}}{C_0}$ | General project evaluation |
| 2 | **NPVI for Uniform Cash Flows** | $PI = \dfrac{A \cdot (P/A, i, n)}{C_0}$ | Annuity-type chemical plant cash flows |
| 3 | **NPVI in terms of NPV** | $PI = 1 + \dfrac{NPV}{C_0}$ | Quick conversion from NPV to PI |
| 4 | **Single Payment Future Factor** | $(F/P, i, n) = (1+i)^n$ | Compounding forward |
| 5 | **Single Payment Present Factor** | $(P/F, i, n) = (1+i)^{-n}$ | Discounting future to present |
| 6 | **Uniform Series Present Factor** | $(P/A, i, n) = \dfrac{(1+i)^n - 1}{i(1+i)^n}$ | Annuity PV calculation |
| 7 | **Uniform Series Future Factor** | $(F/A, i, n) = \dfrac{(1+i)^n - 1}{i}$ | Annuity FV calculation |
| 8 | **Decision Threshold** | $PI \geq 1.0$ | Acceptance rule |
| 9 | **NPVI Ranking for Capital Rationing** | Rank by $PI$ descending | Choose best projects first |
| 10 | **Incremental PI (Mutually Exclusive)** | $\Delta PI = \dfrac{\Delta PV_{inflows}}{\Delta C_0}$ | Compare alternatives properly |

> [!NOTE]
> **Avoiding Markdown Errors:** In all KTU answer scripts, never write absolute values inside table cells using the pipe `|` character — use $\vert$ or $\mid$ in LaTeX mode to prevent LaTeX rendering breakage.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Chemical Engineering

The NPVI is extensively used in the **chemical process industry** for:

- **Petrochemical Plant Expansion Decisions** — Comparing debottlenecking vs. greenfield expansion.
- **Pollution Control Equipment ROI** — Evaluating wet scrubbers vs. electrostatic precipitators under environmental compliance mandates.
- **Catalyst Replacement Projects** — Comparing platinum vs. zeolite catalyst retrofits in reformers.
- **Energy Recovery Systems** — Heat exchanger network retrofits where capital costs vary by an order of magnitude.
- **R&D Project Selection** — Where multiple research proposals compete for a fixed R&D budget.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the NPVI Formula

### Derivation from First Principles (Discounted Cash Flow Theory)

Let $C_0$ be the initial capital investment at time $t = 0$. Let $C_1, C_2, \ldots, C_n$ be the expected net cash inflows at the end of years 1 through $n$. Let $i$ be the Minimum Acceptable Rate of Return (MARR).

**Step 1 — Discount each future cash flow to its present value at time $t = 0$.**

The present value of a single cash flow $C_t$ received at the end of year $t$ is given by:

$$PV_t = \frac{C_t}{(1+i)^t}$$

**Step 2 — Sum all discounted cash inflows to obtain the total present value of inflows.**

$$PV_{inflows} = \sum_{t=1}^{n} \frac{C_t}{(1+i)^t}$$

This can be expanded as:

$$\begin{aligned}
PV_{inflows} &= \frac{C_1}{(1+i)^1} + \frac{C_2}{(1+i)^2} + \frac{C_3}{(1+i)^3} + \cdots + \frac{C_n}{(1+i)^n}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Form the ratio with the initial investment.**

By definition, the Net Present Value Index is:

$$\begin{aligned}
PI &= \frac{PV_{inflows}}{C_0} \\
  &= \frac{\displaystyle\sum_{t=1}^{n} \frac{C_t}{(1+i)^t}}{C_0}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Special case: Uniform annual cash flow $A$ over $n$ years.**

If $C_1 = C_2 = \cdots = C_n = A$ (a uniform annuity, which is the most common KTU problem format), the geometric series sum yields the famous annuity present worth factor:

$$(P/A, i, n) = \frac{(1+i)^n - 1}{i(1+i)^n}$$

Therefore the NPVI simplifies to:

$$\begin{aligned}
PI &= \frac{A \cdot (P/A, i, n)}{C_0} \\
  &= \frac{A}{C_0} \cdot \frac{(1+i)^n - 1}{i(1+i)^n}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Derive the NPV-to-PI conversion identity.**

Since $NPV = PV_{inflows} - C_0$, we can write $PV_{inflows} = NPV + C_0$. Substituting:

$$\begin{aligned}
PI &= \frac{NPV + C_0}{C_0} \\
  &= \frac{NPV}{C_0} + 1
\end{aligned}$$

This identity is **extremely useful** in KTU exams because it allows you to compute PI directly from an already-calculated NPV without re-discounting.

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (Standard KTU Pattern)

### Problem Statement
A chemical company is evaluating a proposal to install a new distillation column. The data are as follows:

- **Initial Investment ($C_0$):** ₹ 5,00,000
- **Expected Annual Net Cash Inflow ($A$):** ₹ 1,20,000 (uniform for 5 years)
- **Salvage Value:** ₹ 50,000 at end of year 5
- **MARR ($i$):** 12% per annum

**Required:** Calculate the Net Present Value Index and decide whether the project should be accepted.

### Exhaustive Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Identify the cash flow components.**

$$\begin{aligned}
C_0 &= 5{,}00{,}000 \\
A &= 1{,}20{,}000 \text{ per year for } n = 5 \text{ years} \\
SV &= 50{,}000 \text{ at } t = 5 \\
i &= 0.12
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Calculate the Present Worth Factor for Uniform Series.**

$$(P/A, 12\%, 5) = \frac{(1+0.12)^5 - 1}{0.12 \times (1+0.12)^5}$$

Computing $(1.12)^5$:

$$\begin{aligned}
(1.12)^1 &= 1.1200 \\
(1.12)^2 &= 1.2544 \\
(1.12)^3 &= 1.4049 \\
(1.12)^4 &= 1.5735 \\
(1.12)^5 &= 1.7623
\end{aligned}$$

Therefore:

$$\begin{aligned}
(P/A, 12\%, 5) &= \frac{1.7623 - 1}{0.12 \times 1.7623} \\
               &= \frac{0.7623}{0.2115} \\
               &= 3.6048
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Calculate the Present Worth Factor for Single Payment (Salvage Value).**

$$(P/F, 12\%, 5) = \frac{1}{(1.12)^5} = \frac{1}{1.7623} = 0.5674$$

**Step 4 — Compute the Total Present Value of Cash Inflows.**

$$\begin{aligned}
PV_{inflows} &= A \cdot (P/A, 12\%, 5) + SV \cdot (P/F, 12\%, 5) \\
             &= 1{,}20{,}000 \times 3.6048 + 50{,}000 \times 0.5674 \\
             &= 4{,}32{,}576 + 28{,}370 \\
             &= 4{,}60{,}946
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Compute the Net Present Value Index.**

$$\begin{aligned}
PI &= \frac{PV_{inflows}}{C_0} \\
   &= \frac{4{,}60{,}946}{5{,}00{,}000} \\
   &= 0.9219
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 6 — Apply the Decision Rule.**

Since $PI = 0.9219 < 1.0$, the project **does not meet the required MARR** and must be **REJECTED**.

**Verification using NPV-to-PI identity:**

$$\begin{aligned}
NPV &= PV_{inflows} - C_0 = 4{,}60{,}946 - 5{,}00{,}000 = -39{,}054 \\
PI &= 1 + \frac{NPV}{C_0} = 1 + \frac{-39{,}054}{5{,}00{,}000} = 1 - 0.0781 = 0.9219 \;\;\checkmark
\end{aligned}$$

Both methods agree — confirming the project should be **rejected**.

---

## 3.3 Capital Rationing Analytical Matrix

Suppose a chemical firm has a **capital budget constraint of ₹ 30 Lakhs** and four viable independent projects:

| Project | Initial Cost (₹) | PV of Inflows (₹) | NPV (₹) | PI | Rank by PI |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| P1 (Catalyst change) | 5,00,000 | 7,50,000 | 2,50,000 | **1.50** | 1st |
| P2 (Heat exchanger) | 12,00,000 | 16,80,000 | 4,80,000 | **1.40** | 2nd |
| P3 (New reactor) | 18,00,000 | 23,40,000 | 5,40,000 | **1.30** | 3rd |
| P4 (Storage tank) | 8,00,000 | 9,60,000 | 1,60,000 | **1.20** | 4th |

**Optimal selection (greedy by PI):**
- Select P1 (₹ 5L), remaining budget ₹ 25L.
- Select P2 (₹ 12L), remaining ₹ 13L.
- Select P4 (₹ 8L), remaining ₹ 5L. P3 requires ₹ 18L — **cannot fit**.
- **Total NPV captured = 2,50,000 + 4,80,000 + 1,60,000 = ₹ 8,90,000.**

---

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 NPVI Decision-Making Flowchart

The following Mermaid flowchart illustrates the complete decision logic an engineer must follow when evaluating a project using the Net Present Value Index, including the critical capital-rationing branch.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Start: Project Proposal Received"] --> B["Tabulate Cash Flows: C0 and C1 through Cn"]
    B --> C["Identify MARR i and Project Life n"]
    C --> D["Discount All Future Inflows to Present Value"]
    D --> E["Sum PV of Inflows: PV_inflows"]
    E --> F["Compute PI = PV_inflows / C0"]
    F --> G{"Is PI greater than or equal to 1.0?"}
    G -- "No" --> H["REJECT Project: Fails to Meet MARR"]
    G -- "Yes" --> I["Project is Individually Acceptable"]
    I --> J{"Is Capital Rationed (Limited Budget)?"}
    J -- "No" --> K["ACCEPT Project: Sufficient Capital Available"]
    J -- "Yes" --> L["Rank All Acceptable Projects by PI Descending"]
    L --> M["Select Projects Sequentially Until Budget Exhausted"]
    M --> N["Final Portfolio Selected"]
    K --> N
    H --> O["End: Decision Communicated to Management"]
    N --> O
```

---

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of NPVI Computation

For complex multi-project evaluations, the NPVI calculation pipeline can be visualized as a four-stage functional architecture that maps how raw cash flow data flows through discounting, indexing, ranking, and selection modules.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT["Stage 1: Data Acquisition Module"]
        A1["Initial Cost C0"] --> M1
        A2["Annual Inflows C1 to Cn"] --> M1
        A3["MARR i and Life n"] --> M1
        A4["Salvage Value SV"] --> M1
    end
    M1["Raw Cash Flow Vector"] --> M2
    subgraph PROCESS["Stage 2: Discounting Engine"]
        M2 --> B1["Apply P/F Factor: 1 / 1 plus i to power t"]
        B1 --> B2["Sum PV_inflows"]
        B2 --> B3["Compute PI = PV_inflows / C0"]
    end
    B3 --> M3
    subgraph DECISION["Stage 3: Decision Logic Gate"]
        M3 --> C1["Compare PI to Threshold 1.0"]
        C1 --> C2{"PI greater than or equal to 1.0?"}
        C2 -- "Yes" --> C3["Acceptable Flag"]
        C2 -- "No" --> C4["Reject Flag"]
    end
    C3 --> M4
    subgraph OUTPUT["Stage 4: Ranking and Selection"]
        M4 --> D1["Sort Acceptable Projects by PI Descending"]
        D1 --> D2["Allocate Capital Within Budget Cap"]
        D2 --> D3["Final Investment Portfolio"]
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Architectural Note for KTU Students:** This block-level abstraction mirrors how enterprise-scale capital budgeting software (e.g., SAP PS, Oracle Hyperion) internally processes investment portfolios. Understanding this architecture helps engineers debug valuation mismatches in real plant ERP systems.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Q: Define the Net Present Value Index. State the decision rule for accepting or rejecting a project based on it.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Net Present Value Index (NPVI)**, also called the **Profitability Index (PI)**, is the ratio of the present value of future cash inflows to the initial investment of a project.

$$\text{PI} = \frac{\text{PV of Future Cash Inflows}}{\text{Initial Investment}}$$

**Decision Rule:**
- If **PI ≥ 1** → **Accept** the project (the project earns at least the required rate of return).
- If **PI < 1** → **Reject** the project (the project fails to recover its capital cost in present value terms).
- If **PI = 1** → The project is at the indifference point; it exactly recovers the MARR.

*(Award 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for the formula, 1 mark for the decision rule.)*

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Q: How does the Net Present Value Index differ from the Net Present Value? In which situation is NPVI preferred over NPV?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | NPV | NPVI |
|---|---|---|
| Nature | Absolute measure (in ₹) | Relative measure (dimensionless ratio) |
| Formula | $PV_{inflows} - C_0$ | $PV_{inflows} / C_0$ |
| Unit | Rupees | Ratio |
| Use | Measures total wealth gain | Measures efficiency per rupee invested |

The **NPVI is preferred over NPV** when **capital is rationed** (i.e., the firm has a limited capital budget and must choose among multiple acceptable projects of differing scales). Under capital rationing, ranking by PI identifies the projects that give the **highest return per rupee of scarce capital**, leading to optimal portfolio selection.

*(Award 1 mark each for two differences and 1 mark for the capital rationing application.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024]

A chemical company is evaluating two mutually exclusive proposals for setting up a new polymerization unit. The cash flow details are:

| Parameter | Proposal X | Proposal Y |
|---|---|---|
| Initial Investment | ₹ 8,00,000 | ₹ 12,00,000 |
| Annual Net Cash Inflow | ₹ 2,50,000 | ₹ 3,40,000 |
| Project Life | 5 years | 5 years |
| Salvage Value | ₹ 1,00,000 | ₹ 2,00,000 |
| MARR | 12% | 12% |

**Sub-part (a) [7 marks]:** Calculate the Net Present Value Index for both proposals and comment on their individual acceptability.

**Sub-part (b) [7 marks]:** Using the incremental analysis approach, determine which mutually exclusive proposal should be selected.

---

### Model Solution for Question A

#### Solution to Sub-part (a) — Individual NPVI Calculation

**Common Factors (MARR = 12%, n = 5):**
From the table, $(P/A, 12\%, 5) = 3.6048$ and $(P/F, 12\%, 5) = 0.5674$.

**Proposal X:**

PV of inflows:
$$\begin{aligned}
PV_X &= 2{,}50{,}000 \times 3.6048 + 1{,}00{,}000 \times 0.5674 \\
     &= 9{,}01{,}200 + 56{,}740 \\
     &= 9{,}57{,}940
\end{aligned}$$

NPVI for X:
$$\begin{aligned}
PI_X &= \frac{9{,}57{,}940}{8{,}00{,}000} = 1.1974
\end{aligned}$$

Since $PI_X = 1.1974 > 1.0$, Proposal X is **individually acceptable**.

**Proposal Y:**

PV of inflows:
$$\begin{aligned}
PV_Y &= 3{,}40{,}000 \times 3.6048 + 2{,}00{,}000 \times 0.5674 \\
     &= 12{,}25{,}632 + 1{,}13{,}480 \\
     &= 13{,}39{,}112
\end{aligned}$$

NPVI for Y:
$$\begin{aligned}
PI_Y &= \frac{13{,}39{,}112}{12{,}00{,}000} = 1.1159
\end{aligned}$$

Since $PI_Y = 1.1159 > 1.0$, Proposal Y is also **individually acceptable**.

However, both projects cannot be chosen since they are **mutually exclusive**. A direct PI comparison is **incorrect** for mutually exclusive projects — we must perform incremental analysis.

---

#### Solution to Sub-part (b) — Incremental Analysis

Since Proposal Y has the higher initial investment, we compute the **incremental cash flows** (Y − X):

$$\begin{aligned}
\Delta C_0 &= 12{,}00{,}000 - 8{,}00{,}000 = 4{,}00{,}000 \\
\Delta A &= 3{,}40{,}000 - 2{,}50{,}000 = 90{,}000 \\
\Delta SV &= 2{,}00{,}000 - 1{,}00{,}000 = 1{,}00{,}000
\end{aligned}$$

PV of incremental inflows:
$$\begin{aligned}
\Delta PV &= 90{,}000 \times 3.6048 + 1{,}00{,}000 \times 0.5674 \\
          &= 3{,}24{,}432 + 56{,}740 \\
          &= 3{,}81{,}172
\end{aligned}$$

Incremental NPVI:
$$\begin{aligned}
\Delta PI &= \frac{\Delta PV}{\Delta C_0} = \frac{3{,}81{,}172}{4{,}00{,}000} = 0.9529
\end{aligned}$$

Since $\Delta PI = 0.9529 < 1.0$, the **extra investment of ₹ 4,00,000** in Proposal Y does **not earn** the required MARR of 12%. Therefore, Proposal Y should be **rejected** in favor of Proposal X.

**Final Recommendation: Select Proposal X.**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

A chemical plant has a capital budget of **₹ 50 Lakhs** and the following four independent projects under consideration:

| Project | Initial Cost | Annual Cash Inflow | Life (years) |
|---|---|---|---|
| P1 | ₹ 10,00,000 | ₹ 3,00,000 | 6 |
| P2 | ₹ 15,00,000 | ₹ 4,00,000 | 6 |
| P3 | ₹ 20,00,000 | ₹ 5,50,000 | 6 |
| P4 | ₹ 12,00,000 | ₹ 3,20,000 | 6 |

The MARR is **10%** per annum. Assume uniform annual cash flows and no salvage value.

**Sub-part (a) [7 marks]:** Calculate the Net Present Value Index for each project and rank them in descending order.

**Sub-part (b) [7 marks]:** Using the PI ranking, determine the optimal set of projects the firm should undertake within its ₹ 50 Lakh budget.

---

### Model Solution for Question B

#### Solution to Sub-part (a) — Individual PI Calculation

**Common Factor at MARR = 10%, n = 6:**

$$(P/A, 10\%, 6) = \frac{(1.10)^6 - 1}{0.10 \times (1.10)^6}$$

Computing $(1.10)^6 = 1.7716$:

$$(P/A, 10\%, 6) = \frac{1.7716 - 1}{0.10 \times 1.7716} = \frac{0.7716}{0.17716} = 4.3553$$

**Project P1:**

$$\begin{aligned}
PV_1 &= 3{,}00{,}000 \times 4.3553 = 13{,}06{,}590 \\
PI_1 &= \frac{13{,}06{,}590}{10{,}00{,}000} = 1.3066
\end{aligned}$$

**Project P2:**

$$\begin{aligned}
PV_2 &= 4{,}00{,}000 \times 4.3553 = 17{,}42{,}120 \\
PI_2 &= \frac{17{,}42{,}120}{15{,}00{,}000} = 1.1614
\end{aligned}$$

**Project P3:**

$$\begin{aligned}
PV_3 &= 5{,}50{,}000 \times 4.3553 = 23{,}95{,}415 \\
PI_3 &= \frac{23{,}95{,}415}{20{,}00{,}000} = 1.1977
\end{aligned}$$

**Project P4:**

$$\begin{aligned}
PV_4 &= 3{,}20{,}000 \times 4.3553 = 13{,}93{,}696 \\
PI_4 &= \frac{13{,}93{,}696}{12{,}00{,}000} = 1.1614
\end{aligned}$$

**Ranking Table:**

| Rank | Project | PI | Initial Cost (₹) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | P1 | **1.3066** | 10,00,000 |
| 2 | P3 | **1.1977** | 20,00,000 |
| 3 | P2 | **1.1614** | 15,00,000 |
| 4 | P4 | **1.1614** | 12,00,000 |

---

#### Solution to Sub-part (b) — Optimal Portfolio Selection

Apply the greedy selection algorithm in descending PI order until the **₹ 50 Lakh** budget is exhausted:

| Step | Project Selected | Cost (₹) | Cumulative Cost (₹) | Budget Remaining (₹) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | P1 | 10,00,000 | 10,00,000 | 40,00,000 |
| 2 | P3 | 20,00,000 | 30,00,000 | 20,00,000 |
| 3 | P2 | 15,00,000 | 45,00,000 | 5,00,000 |
| 4 | P4 | 12,00,000 | **Cannot fit** | 5,00,000 (insufficient) |

**Final Selection: Projects P1 + P3 + P2 = ₹ 45,00,000 (₹ 5,00,000 unused).**

Total NPV of selected portfolio:
$$\begin{aligned}
NPV_{total} &= (13{,}06{,}590 - 10{,}00{,}000) + (23{,}95{,}415 - 20{,}00{,}000) + (17{,}42{,}120 - 15{,}00{,}000) \\
            &= 3{,}06{,}590 + 3{,}95{,}415 + 2{,}42{,}120 \\
            &= ₹ 9{,}44{,}125
\end{aligned}$$

P4 is rejected because its required capital (₹ 12 Lakh) exceeds the remaining budget (₹ 5 Lakh).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
>
> 1. **Direct PI Comparison for Mutually Exclusive Projects:** Students often simply compare $PI_X$ vs. $PI_Y$ and pick the higher one. This is **WRONG** for mutually exclusive projects of differing scales — you MUST perform **incremental analysis** ($\Delta PI$). Direct PI ranking is valid **only for independent projects under capital rationing**.
>
> 2. **Forgetting to Discount the Salvage Value:** Salvage value, if any, must be discounted back to time zero using $(P/F, i, n)$. Omitting this step causes **3–5 mark deductions** in KTU scripts.
>
> 3. **Misinterpreting PI < 1 as Always Bad:** A PI < 1 only means the project fails to earn the **specified** MARR. If a project has very low risk and a low MARR is acceptable, a PI just below 1 may still be the best available alternative.
>
> 4. **Mixing Up P/A and P/F Factors:** Always verify whether the cash flow is a **uniform series** (use $P/A$) or a **single future payment** (use $P/F$). Mixing these is a classic 2-mark trap.
>
> 5. **Unit Inconsistency:** Ensure MARR is expressed as a decimal (0.12, not 12) in all calculations. Cash flow values must be in **consistent units** (e.g., all in Lakhs or all in Rupees).

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Net Present Value Index**

- **Definition:** NPVI = PV of Future Cash Inflows ÷ Initial Investment.
- **Decision Rule:** Accept if $PI \geq 1$; Reject if $PI < 1$.
- **Identity:** $PI = 1 + \dfrac{NPV}{C_0}$ — use this to convert between NPV and PI quickly.
- **Capital Rationing Use:** Rank independent projects by **descending PI** and select greedily until budget is exhausted.
- **Mutually Exclusive Projects:** **NEVER** compare PI directly — always use **incremental analysis** ($\Delta PI$ on the cost difference).
- **Uniform Annuity Shortcut:** $PI = \dfrac{A \cdot (P/A, i, n)}{C_0}$ for level cash flows.
- **Salvage Handling:** Always discount salvage value separately using $(P/F, i, n)$ and add it to the annuity PV.
- **Decimals Over Percentages:** Always substitute MARR as decimal ($i = 0.12$) in formulas.
- **Dimensionless Output:** PI has no units — it is a pure ratio.
- **PI vs. NPV Trade-off:** PI is superior for capital rationing; NPV is superior for absolute wealth maximization without budget constraints.
- **Common KTU Distractors:** Watch for problems where salvage is given but ignored, or where MARR is mixed up between methods.
- **Mermaid Diagram Symbol Convention:** All flowcharts use standardized gate symbols for decisions and rectangle blocks for processing — memorize this for structured answers.

---

*End of Module 3 — Net Present Value Index Notes*
*Prepared as per KTU 2024 Scheme B.Tech Examination Standards*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: DCF Rate of Return
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECHT757 - ECONOMICS AND MANAGEMENT OF CHEMICAL INDUSTRIES > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** DCF Rate of Return # 💰 DCF Rate of Return (DCFROR) — KTU Module 3 Premium Notes --- ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview > [!IMPORTANT] > **DCF Rate of Return (DCFROR)** is defined as the discount rate at which the **Net Present Value (NPV)** of all cash flows generated by a project over its entire economic life becomes exactly **equal to ZERO**. Formally known in engineering economics texts as the **Internal Rate of Return (IRR)**, the DCFROR represents the **intrinsic earning rate** of a proposed chemical plant investment, expressed as a **percentage per annum (%)**. In KTU parlance, this metric answers the question: > *"At what effective annual interest rate (i*) does the project exactly recover its initial capital outlay, neither making a profit nor a loss in present-value terms?"* ### 🎯 Conceptual Analogy (The "Savings Account" Intuition) Imagine you deposit your entire investment capital into a **fictional magic savings account** at an unknown interest rate *i\*%* per year. Simultaneously, you withdraw every cash inflow from the project at the end of each year. At the end of the project's life, your savings account balance is exactly **zero**. That magic interest rate — the one that empties your account precisely at project closure — is the **DCF Rate of Return**. > [!NOTE] > **Key Distinction:** Unlike the *Rate of Return on Investment (ROI)*, which uses **book values** (accounting depreciation), the DCFROR uses **actual cash flows** discounted at their true time value. It is therefore considered the **most rigorous profitability metric** for capital-intensive chemical process plants. ### 📚 Standard Metric Convention Used in KTU Valuation | Symbol | Meaning | Typical Units | |---|---|---| | $C_t$ | Net cash flow in year *t* (after-tax, plus depreciation add-back) | ₹ Lakhs / Crores | | $I_0$ | Initial fixed-capital investment (FCI) at time $t = 0$ | ₹ Lakhs / Crores | | $i^*$ | DCF Rate of Return (DCFROR) | **% per year** | | $n$ | Project economic life | years | | $S_n$ | Salvage value at end of life $n$ | ₹ Lakhs / Crores | | $W$ | Working capital returned at end | ₹ Lakhs / Crores | --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 🔬 The Governing Economic Principle The DCFROR is rooted in the **time-value-of-money** axiom: a Rupee today is worth more than a Rupee ten years from now because today's Rupee can be reinvested at the prevailing cost of capital. The DCFROR seeks the discount rate that **annihilates the time-value premium** across all cash streams. ### 📐 Master DCFROR Equation (The Fundamental Balance) For a project spanning $n$ years with cash flow $C_t$ in each year, the DCFROR $i^*$ satisfies: $$\boxed{\text{NPV}(i^*) \;=\; 0 \;=\; \sum_{t=1}^{n} \frac{C_t}{(1+i^*)^{t}} \;-\; I_0}$$ A more complete form including terminal-year recoveries: $$0 \;=\; \sum_{t=1}^{n-1} \frac{C_t}{(1+i^*)^{t}} \;+\; \frac{C_n + S_n + W}{(1+i^*)^{n}} \;-\; I_0 \;-\; W_0$$ where: - $W_0$ = initial working capital invested at $t=0$ - $W$ = working capital recovered at $t=n$ - $S_n$ = salvage value at end of year $n$ ### 🧭 Step-by-Step Logic for Finding DCFROR 1. **Construct the cash-flow table** — list all after-tax cash inflows (revenue − operating cost − tax + depreciation). 2. **Compute the NPV function** $\text{NPV}(i)$ as a function of an assumed discount rate $i$. 3. **Apply the Trial-and-Error (Interpolation) Method**: - Guess two bracketing discount rates $i_L$ (low) and $i_H$ (high) such that $\text{NPV}(i_L) > 0$ and $\text{NPV}(i_H) < 0$. - Apply linear interpolation: $$i^* \;\approx\; i_L \;+\; \frac{\text{NPV}(i_L)}{\text{NPV}(i_L) - \text{NPV}(i_H)} \times (i_H - i_L)$$ 4. **Decision Criterion (KTU Standard)**: | Condition | KTU Verdict | |---|---| | $i^* \geq i_{\text{MARR}}$ | **Accept** the project | | $i^* < i_{\text{MARR}}$ | **Reject** the project | | $i^* = i_{\text{MARR}}$ | **Indifferent** (boundary case) | > MARR = **Minimum Acceptable Rate of Return**, set by company policy (typically 10–20% in Indian chemical industry). ### 📊 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (High-Yield) | # | Formula / Relation | Meaning / Use | |---|---|---| | 1 | $\sum_{t=1}^{n} \dfrac{C_t}{(1+i^*)^t} = I_0$ | Defining equation of DCFROR | | 2 | $i^* \approx i_L + \dfrac{\text{NPV}_L}{\text{NPV}_L - \text{NPV}_H}(i_H - i_L)$ | Linear interpolation formula | | 3 | $C_t = (\text{Profit})_t (1 - \tau) + D_t$ | After-tax cash flow (profit + depreciation add-back); $\tau$ = tax rate | | 4 | $i^*_{\text{incremental}} = $ IRR of $(\text{Alt B}) - (\text{Alt A})$ | Used for **mutually exclusive** alternative ranking | | 5 | $\text{NPV} = -I_0 + \sum_{t=1}^{n} \dfrac{C_t}{(1+i)^t}$ | General NPV at arbitrary discount rate $i$ | | 6 | $\text{PWFA} = C \cdot \dfrac{(1+i)^n - 1}{i(1+i)^n}$ | Present Worth Factor for Annuity (uniform $C$) | | 7 | $\text{FWFA} = C \cdot \dfrac{(1+i)^n - 1}{i}$ | Future Worth Factor for Annuity | > [!WARNING] > **Critical Pitfall — Units & Absolute Values:** When writing NPV values inside markdown tables, ALWAYS use the LaTeX vertical bar $\vert$ instead of ASCII pipe $\vert$ — otherwise the table will break. Use $\lvert \text{NPV}_L \rvert$ if you mean absolute value. ### 🛢️ Real-World Utility in Chemical Engineering In a **petrochemical complex** (e.g., an ethylene cracker unit requiring ₹2,500 Cr investment), DCFROR is the **primary decision driver** because: - It gives a **percentage return** comparable across projects of vastly different scales (a ₹100 Cr dyes plant vs. a ₹5,000 Cr refinery). - It incorporates the entire project horizon (15–25 years typical for process plants) and **declining cash-flow patterns**. - It is **decision-rule independent** of capital size — directly comparable to the firm's weighted average cost of capital (WACC). --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 🧪 Derivation of the DCFROR Interpolation Formula We know the NPV at any discount rate $i$ is: $$\text{NPV}(i) \;=\; -I_0 \;+\; \sum_{t=1}^{n} \frac{C_t}{(1+i)^t}$$ Because $\text{NPV}(i)$ is a **monotonically decreasing continuous function** of $i$ (for typical positive-investment projects), there exists exactly one root $i^*$ where $\text{NPV}(i^*) = 0$. **Linearization step:** Assume $\text{NPV}(i)$ is approximately linear in the small interval $[i_L, i_H]$ where the sign change occurs. Using the two-point form of a straight line: $$\frac{\text{NPV}(i) - \text{NPV}(i_L)}{i - i_L} \;=\; \frac{\text{NPV}(i_H) - \text{NPV}(i_L)}{i_H - i_L}$$ Setting $\text{NPV}(i^*) = 0$ and solving for $i^*$: $$i^* - i_L \;=\; \frac{-\text{NPV}(i_L)}{\text{NPV}(i_H) - \text{NPV}(i_L)} \cdot (i_H - i_L)$$ $$\boxed{\,i^* \;\approx\; i_L \;+\; \frac{\text{NPV}(i_L)}{\text{NPV}(i_L) - \text{NPV}(i_H)} \cdot (i_H - i_L)\,}$$ > [!NOTE] > This is a **first-order Newton-type approximation**. Accuracy improves as $i_L$ and $i_H$ are chosen closer together (typically $|i_H - i_L| \leq 5\%$). --- ### 📘 Worked Example (KTU 2024 Pattern — 14-Mark Style) **Problem Statement:** A chemical company is evaluating a new **sulphuric acid plant**. The following data is provided: - Initial fixed-capital investment $I_0$ = **₹ 800 Lakhs** - Annual after-tax net cash flow $C$ (uniform, years 1 to 8) = **₹ 220 Lakhs** - Salvage value $S_8$ = **₹ 80 Lakhs** - Project life $n$ = **8 years** - MARR = **12% per year** **Find the DCF Rate of Return using the interpolation method. State accept/reject decision.** --- #### 🔢 STEP 1 — Build the Discounted Cash-Flow Table | Year $t$ | Cash Flow $C_t$ (₹ Lakhs) | Discount Factor @ 15% | PV @ 15% (₹ Lakhs) | Discount Factor @ 20% | PV @ 20% (₹ Lakhs) | |:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:| | 1 | 220 | 0.8696 | 191.31 | 0.8333 | 183.33 | | 2 | 220 | 0.7561 | 166.34 | 0.6944 | 152.77 | | 3 | 220 | 0.6575 | 144.65 | 0.5787 | 127.31 | | 4 | 220 | 0.5718 | 125.80 | 0.4823 | 106.11 | | 5 | 220 | 0.4972 | 109.38 | 0.4019 | 88.42 | | 6 | 220 | 0.4323 | 95.11 | 0.3349 | 73.68 | | 7 | 220 | 0.3759 | 82.70 | 0.2791 | 61.40 | | 8 | 300 (220 + 80) | 0.3269 | 98.07 | 0.2326 | 69.78 | | **Σ PV** | | | **1013.36** | | **862.80** | > *(Values computed using present-worth annuity factors: PWFA(15%,8) = 4.4873; PWFA(20%,8) = 3.8372)* --- #### 🔢 STEP 2 — Compute NPV at Two Trial Rates **At $i_L = 15\%$:** $$\text{NPV}_{15\%} \;=\; 1013.36 \;-\; 800 \;=\; +213.36 \;\; \text{₹ Lakhs} \quad (\text{Positive})$$ **At $i_H = 20\%$:** $$\text{NPV}_{20\%} \;=\; 862.80 \;-\; 800 \;=\; +62.80 \;\; \text{₹ Lakhs} \quad (\text{Still positive, need higher rate})$$ > ⚠️ Both NPVs are positive → the DCFROR is **above 20%**. We must re-trial with a higher rate. --- #### 🔢 STEP 3 — Re-Trial at $i_H = 25\%$ Compute PWFA(25%, 8): $$\text{PWFA}(25\%, 8) \;=\; \frac{(1.25)^8 - 1}{0.25 \times (1.25)^8}$$ $$(1.25)^8 \;=\; 4.7684$$ $$\text{PWFA}(25\%, 8) \;=\; \frac{4.7684 - 1}{0.25 \times 4.7684} \;=\; \frac{3.7684}{1.1921} \;=\; 3.1614$$ PV of annuity (years 1–7, $C=220$): $\;220 \times (\text{PWFA at 7 yrs})$: $$\text{PWFA}(25\%,7) \;=\; \frac{(1.25)^7 - 1}{0.25 \times (1.25)^7} \;=\; \frac{3.8147 - 1}{0.25 \times 3.8147} \;=\; \frac{2.8147}{0.9537} \;=\; 2.9514$$ $$220 \times 2.9514 \;=\; 649.31 \;\; \text{₹ Lakhs (PV of years 1–7)}$$ PV of year-8 cash flow (220 + 80 = 300): $$\frac{300}{(1.25)^8} \;=\; \frac{300}{4.7684} \;=\; 62.92 \;\; \text{₹ Lakhs}$$ **Total PV at 25%:** $$\sum \text{PV} \;=\; 649.31 \;+\; 62.92 \;=\; 712.23 \;\; \text{₹ Lakhs}$$ **NPV at 25%:** $$\text{NPV}_{25\%} \;=\; 712.23 \;-\; 800 \;=\; -87.77 \;\; \text{₹ Lakhs} \quad (\text{Negative ✓})$$ --- #### 🔢 STEP 4 — Apply Interpolation Between 20% and 25% - $i_L = 20\%$, $\text{NPV}_L = +62.80$ - $i_H = 25\%$, $\text{NPV}_H = -87.77$ $$i^* \;\approx\; 20 \;+\; \frac{62.80}{62.80 - (-87.77)} \times (25 - 20)$$ $$i^* \;\approx\; 20 \;+\; \frac{62.80}{150.57} \times 5$$ $$i^* \;\approx\; 20 \;+\; 0.4171 \times 5 \;=\; 20 \;+\; 2.09$$ $$\boxed{\,i^* \;\approx\; 22.09\% \text{ per annum}\,}$$ --- #### 🔢 STEP 5 — KTU Decision Since $i^* = 22.09\% \;>\; \text{MARR} = 12\%$, the project is **ACCEPTED** ✅. The sulphuric acid plant earns ~22% per year on its capital — comfortably above the firm's hurdle rate. --- ### 🔁 Verification (Optional Sanity Check at 22%) $$\text{PWFA}(22\%,8) \;\approx\; 3.6193$$ $$\sum \text{PV (annuity 220, yrs 1–8)} \;=\; 220 \times 3.6193 \;=\; 796.25$$ Add PV of extra salvage 80 at year 8: $$\frac{80}{(1.22)^8} \;=\; \frac{80}{5.0664} \;=\; 15.79$$ $$\sum \text{PV}_{\text{total}} \;\approx\; 796.25 + 15.79 \;=\; 812.04 \;\; \text{(approx., close to 800)}$$ NPV ≈ +12 → within rounding tolerance ✓. **DCFROR ≈ 22% confirmed.** --- ### 📋 Analytical Matrix: When to Use Which Method | Scenario | Preferred Tool | Why | |---|---|---| | Single project, accept/reject | **DCFROR vs MARR** | Quick percentage-based screening | | Two mutually exclusive projects (different scales) | **Incremental DCFROR** on $\Delta C_t$ | Avoids scale-bias of standalone IRR | | Two mutually exclusive projects (different lives) | **NPV at MARR** + Annual Worth | DCFROR cannot directly handle unequal lives | | Capital rationing / budget caps | **Profitability Index (PI)** | DCFROR ignores absolute Rupee magnitude | | High-precision financial modelling | Newton-Raphson iteration on NPV($i$) | Avoids interpolation error | --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 📊 Diagram A — Block-Level Functional Architecture of DCFROR Computation ```mermaid flowchart TD A["Define Project Boundaries
n, I0, CFt, Sn, W"] --> B["Construct
Cash Flow Table"] B --> C{"Uniform
Cash Flows?"} C -- "Yes" --> D["Use PWFA
Shortcut"] C -- "No" --> E["Use Year-by-Year
Discounting"] D --> F["Compute NPV at i_L"] E --> F F --> G{"NPV >= 0?"} G -- "Yes" --> H["Try Higher Rate
i_H = i_L + Delta i"] G -- "No" --> I["Try Lower Rate
i_L = i_H - Delta i"] H --> F I --> F F --> J{"Sign Change
NPV_L > 0 and NPV_H < 0?"} J -- "Yes" --> K["Apply Linear
Interpolation"] K --> L["Obtain i_star
(DCFROR Estimate)"] L --> M{"i_star vs MARR"} M -- "i_star >= MARR" --> N["ACCEPT
Project"] M -- "i_star < MARR" --> O["REJECT
Project"] J -- "No" --> P["Re-Bracket
Try Wider Range"] P --> F ``` ### 📈 Diagram B — NPV vs Discount Rate Curve (Canonical Shape) ```mermaid flowchart LR A["i = 0%
NPV = +Max"] -->|"Discount Rate Increases"| B["i = i_star
NPV = 0 (Root)"] B -->|"Higher Rate"| C["i = MARR
NPV may be + or -"] C -->|"Even Higher"| D["i = very large
NPV approaches -I0"] style A fill:#d4f4dd style B fill:#ffe599 style C fill:#cfe2f3 style D fill:#f4cccc ``` ### 🧭 Diagram C — Decision Logic Map for KTU Valuation ```mermaid flowchart TD Start["Compute i_star"] --> Q1{"Is i_star
Real and Unique?"} Q1 -- "No (Multiple Roots)" --> R1["Use NPV or PI
Method Instead"] Q1 -- "Yes" --> Q2{"i_star >
MARR?"} Q2 -- "Greater" --> Accept["ACCEPT
Full Marks"] Q2 -- "Smaller" --> Reject["REJECT
Document Reason"] Q2 -- "Equal" --> Border["Indifference
Borderline Case"] style Accept fill:#b6d7a8 style Reject fill:#ea9999 style Border fill:#ffd966 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Why unique?** For *conventional* cash flows (one sign change from negative to positive), Descartes' rule of signs guarantees exactly **one** real positive DCFROR. *Non-conventional* flows (e.g., mining or environmental cleanup operations with mid-life major outlays) can yield **multiple IRRs** — KTU expects students to recognize and flag this. --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ### 📝 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) #### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Define the term "DCF Rate of Return" as applied to chemical plant investment analysis. State the decision rule.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The **DCF Rate of Return (DCFROR)**, also called the **Internal Rate of Return (IRR)**, is defined as the discount rate $i^*$ at which the Net Present Value (NPV) of all cash flows over the project life equals **zero**. Mathematically: $$\sum_{t=1}^{n} \frac{C_t}{(1+i^*)^{t}} \;=\; I_0$$ **Decision Rule (1 mark):** The project is accepted if $i^* \geq i_{\text{MARR}}$ (Minimum Acceptable Rate of Return); otherwise it is rejected. > *Mark split: 2 marks for definition + 1 mark for decision rule.* --- #### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Distinguish between Rate of Return on Investment (ROI) and DCF Rate of Return (DCFROR).** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Aspect | ROI | DCFROR | |---|---|---| | **Basis** | Accounting (book) values | Time-adjusted cash flows | | **Depreciation** | Uses straight-line depreciation | No depreciation; actual cash flows | | **Time value of money** | **Not** considered | **Fully** considered via discounting | | **Typical Magnitude** | Lower than DCFROR | Generally higher & more accurate | | **Use** | Historical accounting | Forward-looking project appraisal | > *Mark split: 1 mark for each valid contrast, totaling 3.* --- ### 📚 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice) --- #### **Question A** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` — 14 Marks A pharmaceutical company is considering the installation of a new **paracetamol API plant**. The following data is supplied: - Fixed-capital investment $I_0$ = **₹ 1,200 Lakhs** - Working capital = **₹ 150 Lakhs** (released at end of life) - Annual gross profit (before tax & depreciation) = **₹ 450 Lakhs** - Income tax rate $\tau$ = **30%** - Plant life = **10 years**, straight-line depreciation to zero salvage - MARR = **15%** Compute the **DCF Rate of Return** by interpolation. Should the project be accepted? **(a) Setup and Cash-Flow Construction — 7 marks** **(b) DCFROR Calculation and Decision — 7 marks** --- ### ✅ Model Solution — Question A #### Part (a): Setup & Cash-Flow Construction **Step 1 — Identify depreciation:** (1 mark) $$D \;=\; \frac{I_0 - S_n}{n} \;=\; \frac{1200 - 0}{10} \;=\; \text{₹ 120 Lakhs/year}$$ **Step 2 — Taxable income per year:** (1 mark) $$\text{Taxable Income} \;=\; \text{Gross Profit} - D \;=\; 450 - 120 \;=\; \text{₹ 330 Lakhs}$$ **Step 3 — Tax amount:** (1 mark) $$\text{Tax} \;=\; 0.30 \times 330 \;=\; \text{₹ 99 Lakhs}$$ **Step 4 — Net profit after tax:** (1 mark) $$\text{Net Profit} \;=\; 330 - 99 \;=\; \text{₹ 231 Lakhs}$$ **Step 5 — After-tax cash flow $C_t$ (years 1–10):** (1 mark) $$C_t \;=\; \text{Net Profit} + D \;=\; 231 + 120 \;=\; \text{₹ 351 Lakhs (uniform)}$$ **Step 6 — Terminal cash flow (year 10):** (1 mark) $$C_{10} \;=\; 351 \;+\; 0 \;+\; 150 \;\;(\text{working capital return}) \;=\; \text{₹ 501 Lakhs}$$ **Step 7 — Total initial outlay at $t = 0$:** (1 mark) $$\text{Outlay} \;=\; I_0 + W_0 \;=\; 1200 + 150 \;=\; \text{₹ 1,350 Lakhs}$$ > *(All steps explicitly written for 7 marks)* --- #### Part (b): DCFROR Calculation by Interpolation **Step 1 — Compute Present Worth at $i_L = 20\%$:** (2 marks) $$\text{PWFA}(20\%, 10) \;=\; \frac{(1.2)^{10} - 1}{0.2 \times (1.2)^{10}} \;=\; \frac{6.1917 - 1}{1.2383} \;=\; 4.1925$$ PV of years 1–9 (annuity 351): $$351 \times \big[\text{PWFA}(20\%,10) - \text{DF}(20\%,10)\big] \;=\; 351 \times (4.1925 - 0.1615) \;=\; 351 \times 4.0310 \;=\; 1414.88$$ PV of year 10 ($C_{10} = 501$): $$\frac{501}{(1.2)^{10}} \;=\; \frac{501}{6.1917} \;=\; 80.92$$ $$\sum \text{PV}_{20\%} \;=\; 1414.88 \;+\; 80.92 \;=\; 1495.80 \;\; \text{₹ Lakhs}$$ $$\text{NPV}_{20\%} \;=\; 1495.80 - 1350 \;=\; +145.80 \;\; \text{₹ Lakhs} \quad (\text{Positive ✓})$$ **Step 2 — Compute Present Worth at $i_H = 30\%$:** (2 marks) $$\text{PWFA}(30\%, 10) \;=\; \frac{(1.3)^{10} - 1}{0.3 \times (1.3)^{10}} \;=\; \frac{13.7858 - 1}{4.1357} \;=\; 3.0915$$ PV of years 1–9 (annuity 351): $$351 \times (3.0915 - 0.0728) \;=\; 351 \times 3.0187 \;=\; 1059.56$$ PV of year 10: $$\frac{501}{(1.3)^{10}} \;=\; \frac{501}{13.7858} \;=\; 36.34$$ $$\sum \text{PV}_{30\%} \;=\; 1059.56 + 36.34 \;=\; 1095.90$$ $$\text{NPV}_{30\%} \;=\; 1095.90 - 1350 \;=\; -254.10 \;\; \text{₹ Lakhs} \quad (\text{Negative ✓})$$ **Step 3 — Linear Interpolation:** (2 marks) $$i^* \;\approx\; 20 \;+\; \frac{+145.80}{145.80 - (-254.10)} \times (30 - 20)$$ $$i^* \;\approx\; 20 \;+\; \frac{145.80}{399.90} \times 10$$ $$i^* \;\approx\; 20 \;+\; 0.3646 \times 10 \;=\; 20 \;+\; 3.65$$ $$\boxed{\,i^* \;\approx\; 23.65\% \text{ per annum}\,}$$ **Step 4 — Decision:** (1 mark) Since $i^* = 23.65\% \;>\; \text{MARR} = 15\%$, the **paracetamol plant is ACCEPTED** ✅. > *(7 marks fully allocated as above)* --- ### ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Loss Traps in DCFROR Problems:** > 1. **Forgetting depreciation add-back** — students often use Net Profit instead of Net Profit + Depreciation as cash flow. Result: wrong DCFROR by 2–4%. → Lose **2 marks**. > 2. **Excluding working-capital recovery** at project end (year $n$). Always include $W$ in terminal $C_n$. → Lose **1 mark**. > 3. **Using wrong annuity factor** — confusing PWFA with FWFA or SPPWF. Verify: $\text{PWFA} = \frac{(1+i)^n - 1}{i(1+i)^n}$, NOT $\frac{(1+i)^n - 1}{i}$ (that is FWFA). > 4. **Reporting DCFROR without units** as "23" instead of "23% per year". → Lose **0.5 mark** for unit formality. > 5. **Not stating the decision explicitly** ("project is accepted since $i^* > \text{MARR}$"). → Lose **1 mark**. > 6. **Bracketing error** — both trial NPVs must be of **opposite signs** for interpolation to be valid. Document your sign-check. --- #### **Question B** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice) A fertilizer company is evaluating a **urea prilling tower upgrade**. Two design alternatives are available: | Parameter | Alternative P | Alternative Q | |---|---|---| | Fixed Capital $I_0$ | ₹ 600 Lakhs | ₹ 900 Lakhs | | Annual net cash flow (uniform) | ₹ 175 Lakhs | ₹ 240 Lakhs | | Salvage value | ₹ 60 Lakhs | ₹ 100 Lakhs | | Life | 6 years | 6 years | | MARR | 12% | 12% | **(a)** Compute the DCF Rate of Return for **both alternatives** using interpolation. — **7 marks** **(b)** Perform an **incremental DCFROR analysis** on (Q − P) and recommend the best alternative. — **7 marks** --- ### ✅ Model Solution — Question B #### Part (a): DCFROR for Both Alternatives — 7 marks ##### Alternative P Cash flows: $C_{1\text{–}5} = 175$; $C_6 = 175 + 60 = 235$; Outlay = 600. **At $i = 20\%$:** (1 mark) $$\text{PWFA}(20\%,6) = 3.3255, \quad \text{DF}(20\%,6) = 0.3349$$ $$\text{PV}_{20\%} = 175 \times (3.3255 - 0.3349) + \frac{235}{(1.2)^6}$$ $$= 175 \times 2.9906 + \frac{235}{2.9860} = 523.36 + 78.70 = 602.06$$ $$\text{NPV}_{20\%} = 602.06 - 600 = +2.06 \;\; (\text{Near zero ✓})$$ **At $i = 21\%$:** (1 mark) $$\text{PWFA}(21\%,6) \approx 3.2477, \quad \text{DF}(21\%,6) \approx 0.3114$$ $$\text{PV}_{21\%} = 175 \times (3.2477 - 0.3114) + \frac{235}{(1.21)^6}$$ $$= 175 \times 2.9363 + \frac{235}{3.1384} = 513.85 + 74.88 = 588.73$$ $$\text{NPV}_{21\%} = 588.73 - 600 = -11.27$$ **Interpolation:** (1 mark) $$i^*_P \approx 20 + \frac{2.06}{2.06 + 11.27} \times 1 = 20 + 0.155 = 20.16\%$$ ##### Alternative Q Cash flows: $C_{1\text{–}5} = 240$; $C_6 = 240 + 100 = 340$; Outlay = 900. **At $i = 15\%$:** (1 mark) $$\text{PWFA}(15\%,6) = 3.7845, \quad \text{DF}(15\%,6) = 0.4323$$ $$\text{PV}_{15\%} = 240 \times (3.7845 - 0.4323) + \frac{340}{(1.15)^6}$$ $$= 240 \times 3.3522 + \frac{340}{2.3131} = 804.53 + 146.99 = 951.52$$ $$\text{NPV}_{15\%} = 951.52 - 900 = +51.52$$ **At $i = 20\%$:** (1 mark) $$\text{PV}_{20\%} = 240 \times 2.9906 + \frac{340}{2.9860} = 717.74 + 113.86 = 831.60$$ $$\text{NPV}_{20\%} = 831.60 - 900 = -68.40$$ **Interpolation:** (1 mark) $$i^*_Q \approx 15 + \frac{51.52}{51.52 + 68.40} \times 5 = 15 + \frac{51.52}{119.92} \times 5 = 15 + 2.15 = 17.15\%$$ ##### Summary (1 mark) | Alternative | DCFROR $i^*$ | vs MARR (12%) | Verdict | |---|---|---|---| | P | ≈ 20.16% | > MARR | Accept individually | | Q | ≈ 17.15% | > MARR | Accept individually | --- #### Part (b): Incremental DCFROR (Q − P) — 7 marks **Incremental cash flows:** - $\Delta I_0 = 900 - 600 = $ **₹ 300 Lakhs** - $\Delta C_{1\text{–}5} = 240 - 175 = $ **₹ 65 Lakhs/year** - $\Delta C_6 = (340) - (235) = $ **₹ 105 Lakhs/year** - $\Delta n = 6$ years **At $i = 10\%$:** (2 marks) $$\text{PWFA}(10\%,6) = 4.3553, \quad \text{DF}(10\%,6) = 0.5645$$ $$\text{PV}_{10\%} = 65 \times (4.3553 - 0.5645) + \frac{105}{(1.1)^6}$$ $$= 65 \times 3.7908 + \frac{105}{1.7716} = 246.40 + 59.27 = 305.67$$ $$\text{NPV}_{10\%} = 305.67 - 300 = +5.67$$ **At $i = 12\%$:** (2 marks) $$\text{PWFA}(12\%,6) = 4.1114, \quad \text{DF}(12\%,6) = 0.5066$$ $$\text{PV}_{12\%} = 65 \times (4.1114 - 0.5066) + \frac{105}{(1.12)^6}$$ $$= 65 \times 3.6048 + \frac{105}{1.9738} = 234.31 + 53.20 = 287.51$$ $$\text{NPV}_{12\%} = 287.51 - 300 = -12.49$$ **Interpolation:** (2 marks) $$\Delta i^* \approx 10 + \frac{5.67}{5.67 + 12.49} \times 2 = 10 + \frac{5.67}{18.16} \times 2 = 10 + 0.624 = 10.62\%$$ **Decision Logic:** - The lower-cost alternative (P) is the **initial defender**. - $\Delta i^* = 10.62\% < \text{MARR} = 12\%$, so the **extra ₹300 Lakhs investment in Q is NOT justified**. - The incremental capital earns only 10.62%, below the hurdle rate. **Recommendation:** Choose **Alternative P** (the cheaper option) — it gives higher return on capital deployed. > *(7 marks allocated as: 2 (cash flow setup) + 4 (trial calculations) + 1 (recommendation))* --- ### 🧾 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — DCF Rate of Return** - ✅ **DCFROR = $i^*$** such that $\text{NPV}(i^*) = 0$. - ✅ Computed by **interpolation** between two bracketing rates where NPV changes sign. - ✅ Decision: **Accept if $i^* \geq \text{MARR}$**. - ✅ Always include **depreciation add-back** in cash flow: $C_t = \text{Net Profit} + D$. - ✅ Always include **working-capital return** and **salvage value** in the terminal year. - ✅ For **mutually exclusive** alternatives, use **incremental DCFROR**, not standalone DCFROR. - ✅ DCFROR assumes cash flows are **reinvested at $i^*$** (a weakness vs. NPV's reinvestment at MARR). - ✅ Watch for **non-conventional flows** → may yield multiple IRRs (use NPV instead). - ✅ Linear interpolation is **approximate**; error reduces as bracketing interval narrows. - ✅ Higher DCFROR ≠ better project if **scale and life** differ — NPV is the tie-breaker. - ✅ MARR for Indian process industries: typically **12–18%**; refine per company policy. - ✅ KTU expects **units (% per year)**, **explicit decision statement**, and **sign-checked bracketing** — missing any of these costs marks. ---

topics.title: Incremental Analysis, Breakeven and Minimum Cost Analysis: Variable Cost and Fixed Cost
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT757 - ECONOMICS AND MANAGEMENT OF CHEMICAL INDUSTRIES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Incremental Analysis, Breakeven and Minimum Cost Analysis: Variable Cost and Fixed Cost

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Fixed Cost (FC)
A **Fixed Cost** is an expenditure that does **NOT** vary with the level of production or sales volume within a relevant range. It is incurred regardless of whether the plant operates at 0%, 50%, or 100% capacity.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU Definition:** *Fixed costs are the costs that remain constant in total magnitude irrespective of the variations in the output, within a specific capacity range, over a defined period of time.*

**Examples in a Chemical Plant:**
- Depreciation of reactors, distillation columns, and heat exchangers
- Insurance premium and property tax
- Salaries of permanent staff (plant manager, supervisors)
- Lease rental of land and building
- Interest on long-term loans

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight for KTU Exams:** Per-unit fixed cost DECREASES as production volume INCREASES. This is the famous "spreading the overhead" effect.

### 1.2 Variable Cost (VC)
A **Variable Cost** is an expenditure that **varies directly and proportionally** with the level of production. If you produce twice as much, you pay (approximately) twice as much in variable cost.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU Definition:** *Variable costs are the costs that vary in direct proportion to the volume of production. The total variable cost changes with output, but the variable cost per unit remains constant.*

**Examples in a Chemical Plant:**
- Raw materials (feedstock, catalysts, solvents)
- Process utilities (steam, cooling water, electricity for pumps)
- Direct labor wages (piece-rate or hourly production workers)
- Packaging and consumables
- Royalty and excise duty on output

### 1.3 Semi-Variable (Mixed) Cost
Contains both a fixed and a variable component (e.g., electricity bill with a fixed demand charge + variable consumption charge).

### 1.4 Incremental Analysis
**Incremental Analysis** (also called *Differential Analysis* or *Marginal Analysis*) is the decision-making technique that compares the **additional costs and additional revenues** between two alternative courses of action, *ignoring* the costs that remain unchanged (sunk or common costs).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Definition:** *Incremental analysis is a method of identifying the additional revenue and additional costs (and hence additional profit) that result from selecting one alternative over another.*

**Conceptual Analogy — "Should I Hire a Cab or Take the Bus?"**
You are going from college to the airport (40 km away).
- **Bus:** ₹60 fixed ticket, 90 minutes, shared with strangers.
- **Cab:** ₹800 fixed, 50 minutes, private.

The **incremental cost** of the cab = ₹800 − ₹60 = **₹740**. The **incremental benefit** = time saved (40 min) + comfort + privacy.

If you value the 40 minutes saved at more than ₹740, you choose the cab. If not, you choose the bus. The cost of your college fees, hostel rent, etc. are **irrelevant** (sunk/common) — they occur either way. This is the heart of incremental analysis.

### 1.5 Breakeven Analysis
**Breakeven Analysis** determines the production volume or sales revenue at which **Total Revenue = Total Cost** (i.e., profit = 0). Below this point the company incurs a loss; above it, the company makes a profit.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition:** *The breakeven point is the level of operation at which the total revenue is just equal to the total cost, resulting in neither profit nor loss.*

**Conceptual Analogy — "The Tiffin Shop Owner"**
A small tiffin shop has a daily rent of ₹500 (fixed). Each tiffin costs ₹40 to make (variable — raw materials, gas) and sells for ₹80. Every tiffin sold contributes ₹80 − ₹40 = ₹40 toward rent. To pay the rent alone, he must sell **₹500 / ₹40 = 12.5 → 13 tiffins**. Below 13, loss; above 13, profit. That 13th tiffin is the **breakeven point**.

### 1.6 Minimum Cost Analysis
**Minimum Cost Analysis** determines the production rate or batch size at which the **Total Cost is minimized** (or equivalently, profit is maximized for a given selling price).

**Conceptual Analogy — "The Idli Batter Batch"**
A catering lady makes idli batter. Making 1 kg/day means she shops daily (high transport + time cost = high variable cost). Making 100 kg/day means she must buy a fridge and a wet grinder (high fixed cost). The **optimum batch** that minimizes total cost per day lies somewhere in between — governed by the famous *square-root law* (Economic Order Quantity, EOQ).

### 1.7 Summary of Cost Behavior

| Behavior Type | Total Cost | Cost per Unit |
|---|---|---|
| **Pure Fixed** | Constant as Q changes | Decreases as Q increases |
| **Pure Variable** | Increases linearly with Q | Constant as Q changes |
| **Semi-Variable (Mixed)** | Increases but not in 1:1 ratio | Decreases (asymptotically approaches variable rate) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Mathematical Modeling of Costs

**Linear Cost Model (most common in KTU problems):**

$$TC(Q) = FC + v \cdot Q$$

where:
- $TC(Q)$ = Total Cost at production volume $Q$ (₹ or $)
- $FC$ = Total Fixed Cost per period
- $v$ = Variable cost per unit (₹/unit)
- $Q$ = Quantity produced (units)

**Revenue Model:**

$$TR(Q) = S \cdot Q$$

where $S$ = Selling price per unit.

**Profit Model:**

$$\pi(Q) = TR(Q) - TC(Q) = (S - v)Q - FC$$

The term $(S - v)$ is the **Contribution Margin per unit (CM)** — the amount each unit contributes toward recovering fixed cost and then toward profit.

### 2.2 Breakeven Point Derivation Logic

At breakeven: $TR = TC$

$$S \cdot Q_{BE} = FC + v \cdot Q_{BE}$$

Rearranging:

$$Q_{BE} = \frac{FC}{S - v}$$

**Breakeven Sales Revenue:**

$$R_{BE} = S \cdot Q_{BE} = \frac{FC}{1 - \dfrac{v}{S}} = \frac{FC}{\text{Contribution Margin Ratio}}$$

where the **Contribution Margin Ratio (CMR)** is:

$$CMR = \frac{S - v}{S} = 1 - \frac{v}{S}$$

### 2.3 Incremental Analysis Logic

When choosing between two alternatives A and B:

**Incremental Fixed Cost:**

$$\Delta FC = FC_B - FC_A$$

**Incremental Variable Cost (per unit):**

$$\Delta v = v_B - v_A$$

**Incremental Profit (choosing B over A):**

$$\Delta \pi(Q) = (S_B - S_A) \cdot Q - \Delta FC - \Delta v \cdot Q$$

Setting $\Delta \pi = 0$ gives the **incremental breakeven** between the two alternatives:

$$Q_{inc} = \frac{\Delta FC}{(\Delta S) - (\Delta v)} = \frac{\Delta FC}{\Delta CM}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rule of Thumb for KTU Valuation:**
> - If actual $Q > Q_{inc}$, choose the alternative with the **higher contribution margin** (cheaper variable cost, but more capital-intensive).
> - If actual $Q < Q_{inc}$, choose the alternative with the **lower fixed cost** (lower capital commitment).

### 2.4 Minimum Cost Analysis — The General (Non-Linear) Case

Real processes have variable costs that are **not strictly linear** because of economies of scale, learning curves, and non-linear utility consumption. A standard quadratic model is:

$$TC(Q) = FC + aQ + bQ^{2}$$

where $a$ = coefficient of linear variable cost, $b$ = coefficient of quadratic (scale-related) cost, $b > 0$.

**Necessary condition for minimum:**

$$\frac{d(TC)}{dQ} = a + 2bQ = 0$$

This gives the optimum production rate:

$$Q^{*} = \frac{-a}{2b}$$

For economic realism, the linear term $a$ may be expressed differently. In many KTU problems, the total cost is written as:

$$TC = FC + bQ^{2} \quad (a = 0)$$

giving:

$$Q^{*} = \sqrt{\frac{FC}{b}} \quad ; \quad TC_{min} = 2 \cdot FC$$

### 2.5 Economic Batch Size (EOQ-type) Derivation

When production occurs in discrete batches, total annual cost is:

$$TC = \underbrace{\frac{D}{Q} \cdot S}_{\text{Setup cost}} + \underbrace{\frac{Q}{2} \cdot H}_{\text{Carrying cost}}$$

where:
- $D$ = annual demand (units/yr)
- $Q$ = batch size
- $S$ = setup cost per batch (₹/batch)
- $H$ = holding/carrying cost per unit per year (₹/unit·yr)

Differentiating and setting to zero:

$$\frac{d(TC)}{dQ} = -\frac{DS}{Q^{2}} + \frac{H}{2} = 0$$

$$\boxed{Q_{EOQ} = \sqrt{\frac{2DS}{H}}}$$

Minimum total cost:

$$TC_{min} = \sqrt{2DSH}$$

### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> **This table covers ≥ 90% of numerical problems asked in KTU exams on this topic.**

| # | Concept | Formula | Variables / Meaning | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Total Cost (Linear) | $TC = FC + vQ$ | $FC$: Fixed Cost, $v$: Var. cost/unit, $Q$: Quantity | ₹ |
| 2 | Total Revenue | $TR = S \cdot Q$ | $S$: Selling price/unit | ₹ |
| 3 | Profit | $\pi = (S-v)Q - FC$ | $(S-v)$ = Contribution margin/unit | ₹ |
| 4 | Breakeven Quantity | $Q_{BE} = \dfrac{FC}{S - v}$ | Production units at zero profit | units |
| 5 | Breakeven Revenue | $R_{BE} = \dfrac{FC}{1 - v/S}$ | Sales value at zero profit | ₹ |
| 6 | Contribution Margin Ratio | $CMR = 1 - v/S$ | Fraction of revenue available for FC + profit | dimensionless |
| 7 | Incremental Breakeven | $Q_{inc} = \dfrac{\Delta FC}{\Delta (S-v)}$ | Switchover point between two alternatives | units |
| 8 | Min-Cost Production (quadratic) | $Q^{*} = -a/(2b)$ for $TC = FC + aQ + bQ^{2}$ | Optimum output for minimum total cost | units |
| 9 | Min-Cost (no linear term) | $Q^{*} = \sqrt{FC/b}$ for $TC = FC + bQ^{2}$ | Optimum output | units |
| 10 | Economic Batch Size (EOQ) | $Q_{EOQ} = \sqrt{2DS/H}$ | $D$: demand, $S$: setup cost, $H$: holding cost | units |
| 11 | Min TC at EOQ | $TC_{min} = \sqrt{2DSH}$ | Minimum total annual cost | ₹ |
| 12 | Capacity Utilization | $CU = Q_{BE} / Q_{max} \times 100\%$ | % of capacity at breakeven | % |
| 13 | Margin of Safety | $MoS = Q_{actual} - Q_{BE}$ | Production volume above breakeven | units |
| 14 | Profit at any Q | $\pi = (S-v)(Q - Q_{BE})$ | Quick computation given breakeven known | ₹ |

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

In a **chemical plant** these tools drive decisions like:
1. **Plant capacity sizing** — "Should I install a 50,000 TPA or 1,00,000 TPA plant?" → Incremental analysis.
2. **Make or buy decision** — "Should I manufacture H₂SO₄ in-house or purchase from a neighbouring plant?" → Incremental analysis.
3. **Pricing policy** — "What selling price is needed to break even on this new specialty chemical?" → Breakeven analysis.
4. **Inventory & batch scheduling** — "What is the optimal batch size to minimize the sum of ordering and holding costs?" → Minimum cost / EOQ.
5. **Process debottlenecking** — "At what throughput does our compressor upgrade pay for itself?" → Incremental breakeven.
