### QUESTION B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)

**`[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`**

**(a)** Explain the concept of whistle-blowing in engineering practice. Under what conditions is whistle-blowing morally justified? (7 marks — Bloom: Understand/Apply)

**(b)** "Public safety overrides all other obligations of an engineer." Critically evaluate this statement with two industrial case examples (excluding Bhopal). (7 marks — Bloom: Evaluate/Analyze)

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Whistle-blowing in Engineering (7 marks)**

**Definition (1 mark):**  
Whistle-blowing is the formal disclosure by an employee of illegal, unethical, or unsafe practices of the employer to an external authority capable of initiating corrective action.

**Why it is necessary (2 marks):**  
Internal channels sometimes fail when senior management is complicit, when commercial pressure overrides safety, or when regulatory oversight is weak. Whistle-blowing bypasses these internal failures.

**Conditions justifying whistle-blowing (4 marks):**

1. **Serious harm** is involved (death, severe injury, environmental damage).
2. **Internal channels** have been exhausted without remedy.
3. **Documented evidence** supports the claim.
4. **Reasonable belief** exists that disclosure will prevent harm.
5. The disclosure is made to an **appropriate authority** (regulator, law enforcement).

**Priority Equation:**
$$\text{Whistle-blow if:} \quad H_{\text{harm}} > L_{\text{loyalty}}$$
where $H_{\text{harm}}$ is the magnitude of potential harm and $L_{\text{loyalty}}$ is the obligation to the employer. In chemical engineering, $H_{\text{harm}}$ is usually very large due to toxic/explosive hazards, so whistle-blowing is frequently justified.

**(b) Critical Evaluation of the Statement (7 marks)**

The statement *"Public safety overrides all other obligations of an engineer"* is a direct enunciation of Canon 1 and is **ethically correct, legally enforceable, and professionally non-negotiable**.

**Supporting Argument (3 marks):**

Engineers hold a unique position of public trust. Unlike other professions, engineering decisions can affect thousands of lives simultaneously. Therefore, the ethical hierarchy places public safety above commercial profit, contractual loyalty, and even personal career advancement. This is enshrined in:
- Section 304 BNS (culpable homicide)
- Environment Protection Act 1986
- Public Liability Insurance Act 1991
- National Disaster Management Act 2005

**Case Example 1: Texas City Refinery Explosion, BP, 2005 (2 marks)**

A lack of safety culture at the BP Texas City refinery led to a hydrocarbon vapor cloud explosion that killed 15 workers and injured 180. Investigators found that safety valves (RVs) on a raffinate splitter had been disabled to avoid false alarms during turnaround. Engineers had raised concerns but management pressured them to ignore these warnings for production continuity. **This is a clear violation of Canon 1, where production overrides overrode public/worker safety.**

**Case Example 2: Boeing 737 MAX Crashes, Lion Air & Ethiopian Airlines, 2018-2019 (2 marks)**

Two Boeing 737 MAX aircraft crashed within 5 months, killing 346 people. The Maneuvering Characteristics Augmentation System (MCAS) relied on a single Angle-of-Attack sensor. Engineers had warned about single-point-of-failure risks, but cost pressure and schedule commitments led to its deployment. Boeing engineers who raised safety concerns were reportedly sidelined. **Again, this is a Canon 1 violation: schedule and cost prioritized over passenger safety.**

**Conclusion (bonus):** Both cases reinforce that the engineer's paramount duty to public safety is not just an abstract ideal but a binding professional obligation with real-world consequences.

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in KTU Valuation:**
> 1. **Confusing "Canon" with "Law"**: Students often write "it is illegal to..." instead of "it is a violation of Canon 1 to..." — marks deducted for misframing.
> 2. **Omitting the Priority Hierarchy**: When asked about conflicts between canons, students forget to mention that **Canon 1 always overrides**. Marks reserved for this directly.
> 3. **Wrong Case Studies**: Avoid using generic case studies (e.g., the Titanic). For PECHT757, KTU examiners prefer **chemical industry** case studies — Bhopal, Flixborough, Texas City, Vizag gas leak (2020).
> 4. **Mixing Ethics with Morals**: Ethics refers to professional conduct codes; morals are personal beliefs. Examiners look for this distinction.
> 5. **One-line answers for 14-mark questions**: A 14-mark question expects **structured tables, diagrams, and ~400–500 words minimum**. Skipping the table or diagram costs 2–3 marks outright.
> 6. **Forgetting the legal anchor**: Linking the canon to BNS Section 304 / EPA 1986 shows legal awareness and fetches bonus marks.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Rapid-Revision Sheet — Canons of Ethics of Engineers**

- ☐ **Definition**: Canons = codified fundamental moral principles governing engineering conduct.
- ☐ **Why Canons?** Engineering is a profession with public trust; canons protect this trust.
- ☐ **Number of Canons**: Seven fundamental canons (K1–K7), grouped under four domains.
- ☐ **Domain 1 — Public**: Canon 1 (Safety paramount) + Canon 2 (Competence).
- ☐ **Domain 2 — Employer**: Canon 3 (Faithful agency) + Canon 4 (Confidentiality).
- ☐ **Domain 3 — Profession**: Canon 5 (Reputation) + Canon 6 (Continuing development).
- ☐ **Domain 4 — Self**: Canon 7 (Honest public statements).
- ☐ **Priority Order**: Canon 1 > All other canons (always).
- ☐ **Whistle-blowing**: Justified when harm > loyalty; required when public safety at stake.
- ☐ **Conflict of Interest**: Disclose, recuse, or divest.
- ☐ **Bhopal Disaster (1984)**: Textbook case study violating Canons 1, 2, 6, and 7.
- ☐ **Legal Backing**: BNS §304, Environment Protection Act 1986, Factories Act 1948.
- ☐ **Enforcement**: IEI, IIChE (professional bodies) + civil/criminal courts (legal).
- ☐ **Professional Body**: Institution of Engineers (India) — IEI — is the principal Indian body for chemical engineers (alongside IIChE).
- ☐ **Mnemonic to Remember the Seven Canons**: **"Public Safety Comes First; Competence Confirms It; Faithful Agency Follows; Confidentiality Counts; Colleagues' Character Cannot Be Maligned; Continuous Competence Cultivation; Candor Concludes All."**
- ☐ **Always link Canons to Chemical Engineering Examples** in KTU answers (pressure vessels, HAZOP, MSDS, plant safety) for higher marks.
